questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The major products $U$ and $V$ in the following reaction are
questions.solution:
The given reactions involve the alkylation of phenol under different solvent conditions, which dictate whether O-alkylation or C-alkylation occurs.

In the first reaction to form U, phenol reacts with 1-bromo-3-methylbut-2-ene in the presence of $K_2CO_3$ and acetone. $K_2CO_3$ acts as a base to form the phenoxide ion. Acetone is a polar aprotic solvent. In polar aprotic solvents, the phenoxide ion is weakly solvated and exists as a relatively free anion. Because oxygen is more electronegative and a harder nucleophilic center than carbon, it preferentially attacks the alkyl halide via an $S_N2$ mechanism. This leads to O-alkylation, forming an ether. Therefore, U is phenyl 3-methylbut-2-enyl ether.

In the second reaction to form V, phenol first reacts with KOH to form potassium phenoxide. This salt is then reacted with allyl bromide in benzene. Benzene is a non-polar solvent. In non-polar solvents, the potassium phenoxide exists as a tight ion pair. The potassium cation coordinates strongly with the oxygen atom, sterically hindering it and reducing its nucleophilicity. Consequently, the electrophilic attack occurs preferentially at the less hindered ortho-carbon of the aromatic ring, leading to C-alkylation. Therefore, V is 2-allylphenol.

Comparing these products with the given options, Option (C) correctly shows U as the O-alkylated product and V as the C-alkylated product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following does not lose water even in conc. $H_2SO_4$?
questions.solution:
Dehydration of alcohols in the presence of concentrated $H_2SO_4$ proceeds via an E1 mechanism, which involves the formation of a carbocation intermediate. The feasibility of the dehydration reaction depends on the stability of this carbocation.

Let us analyze the carbocations formed by the loss of a water molecule from each compound:

1. Compound 1 (Cyclopent-2-en-1-ol): Forms an allylic carbocation, which is resonance stabilized. Dehydration yields cyclopentadiene, a stable conjugated diene.

2. Compound 2 (Cyclopenta-2,4-dien-1-ol): Forms the cyclopentadienyl cation. This cation is cyclic, planar, completely conjugated, and possesses $4 \pi$ electrons. According to Hückel's rule ($4n \pi$ electrons, where $n=1$), it is anti-aromatic. Because anti-aromatic species are exceptionally unstable, this carbocation will not form, and the compound resists dehydration even in concentrated $H_2SO_4$.

3. Compound 3 (Cyclohepta-2,4,6-trien-1-ol): Forms the cycloheptatrienyl (tropylium) cation. It is cyclic, planar, completely conjugated, and has $6 \pi$ electrons ($4n+2 \pi$ electrons, where $n=1$). It is aromatic and highly stable, so dehydration occurs very readily.

4. Compound 4 (Cyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-ol): Forms a doubly allylic, resonance-stabilized carbocation. It easily loses a proton to form benzene, which is highly stable due to aromaticity.

Since the intermediate formed from compound 2 is highly unstable (anti-aromatic), it does not lose water.

Answer: $2$

questions.q: Amongst the following compounds, the one which would not respond to iodoform test is
questions.solution:
Acetic acid, \(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\) would not respond to iodoform test. In the mechanism of haloform reaction, in the first step, base deprotonates most acidic alpha \(\mathrm{H}\) atom (of methyl keto group).
In acetic acid, the most acidic proton is attached to \(\mathrm{O}\) atom. So deprotonation of \(\alpha-\) hydrogen does not occur and hence no haloform reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of reactivity for the addition reaction of the following carbonyl compounds with ethylmagnesium iodide is
questions.solution: The correct order of reactivity for the addition reaction of the given carbonyl compounds with ethylmagnesium iodide is $1>111>11>\mathrm{IV}$ This order can be explained on the basis of following two factors: (i) Inductive effect Greater the number of alkyl (electron releasing) groups attached to carbonyl group, greater will be the electron density on carbonyl carbon. Thus, it lowers the attack of nucleophile and hence, reactivity decreases. (ii) Steric effect As the number of alkyl group attached to carbonyl carbon increases, the attack of nucleophile on carbonyl group becomes more and more difficult due to steric hinderance.

questions.q: Amongst the following compounds, the one that will not respond to Cannizzaro reaction upon treatment with alkali is
questions.solution: For a carbonyl group to give Cannizaro reaction, it should not have an $\alpha$ -hydrogen atom. Among the given compound $\mathrm{Ne}_{3} \mathrm{CCHO}, \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CHO}$ and HCHO respond to Cannizaro reaction but $\mathrm{Cl}_{3} \mathrm{CCHO}$ do not due to the following reason. When hydroxyl group attacks the carbonyl group a tetrahedral intermediate forms. This tetrahedral intermediate will revert to a carbonyl compound by expelling the best leaving group. The CCl $_{3}$ carbonium is resonance stabilised therefore it is better leaving group than OH. These initial products exchange a proton to give ion of carboxylic acid and trichloromethane ($CHCl_{3}$).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The increasing order of basicity of the following compounds is
questions.solution:
The basicity of amines depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation. Greater delocalization of the lone pair decreases its availability, thereby decreasing the basicity of the compound.

Let us analyze the delocalization of the nitrogen lone pair in each compound:

1. In compound I, the lone pair on the nitrogen atom is conjugated with the exocyclic double bond and the two double bonds of the ring. Delocalization of this lone pair generates a dipolar resonance structure where the five-membered ring becomes a cyclopentadienyl anion. Since the cyclopentadienyl anion has $6\pi$ electrons, it is aromatic and highly stable. This strong aromatic stabilization of the ground state makes the lone pair highly delocalized and least available for protonation. Thus, compound I is the least basic.

2. In compound III, the nitrogen lone pair is in conjugation with the exocyclic double bond, which is further conjugated with the double bond inside the ring. This extended conjugation delocalizes the lone pair over a larger system compared to a simple enamine, reducing its availability for protonation.

3. In compound II, the nitrogen lone pair is conjugated only with the exocyclic double bond. The double bond inside the ring is isolated and does not participate in conjugation with the nitrogen lone pair. Therefore, the lone pair is the least delocalized among the three compounds and is most available for protonation, making compound II the most basic.

Comparing the extent of delocalization: I (aromatic stabilization) > III (extended conjugation) > II (simple conjugation).

Since basicity is inversely proportional to the extent of delocalization of the lone pair, the increasing order of basicity is I < III < II.

Answer: I < III < II

questions.solution:
The correct order is $2    < 1 < 3 < 4$. Since, more the number of $2^{\circ}$ - NH- group, more is the basic nature.
(1) Thus, 3 and 4 are more basic than 1 and 2 .
(ii) Between 3 and 4,4 has one two - $\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group while 3 has only one $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group. $\therefore 4$ is more basic than 3
(iii) Between 1 and 2,1 has one $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group with no resonance, thus is more basic than 2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?
options: Strach is composed of repeating $\alpha$–D glucose units
Nylon-6 is an addition polymer whereas nylon–6,6 is a condensation polymer
Isoprene is the monomer unit of natural rubber
Bakelite is obtained from reaction between phenol and acetaldehyde
questions.solution:
Starch is a polysaccharide composed of repeating $\alpha$-D-glucose units, which form amylose and amylopectin. Thus, statement (A) is correct.

Nylon-6 is synthesized from caprolactam and Nylon-6,6 is synthesized from hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid. Both are polyamides and are classified as condensation polymers. Thus, statement (B) is incorrect.

Natural rubber is a linear polymer of isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene) and is also called cis-1,4-polyisoprene. Thus, statement (C) is correct.

Bakelite is a condensation polymer obtained from the reaction between phenol and formaldehyde, not acetaldehyde. Thus, statement (D) is incorrect.

Answer: Strach is composed of repeating $\alpha$–D glucose units; Isoprene is the monomer unit of natural rubber

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct about the given compound?
questions.solution:
The given compound is D-glucose, which is an aldohexose.

(A) D-glucose exhibits ring-chain tautomerism as it exists in an equilibrium between its open-chain aldehyde form and its cyclic hemiacetal forms ($\alpha$ and $\beta$ pyranose forms). Thus, this statement is correct.

(B) D-glucose reacts with three equivalents of phenylhydrazine to form glucosazone (an osazone). Thus, this statement is correct.

(C) The open-chain form of D-glucose has $4$ chiral centers. The total number of stereoisomers for this constitution is $2^4 = 16$, not $8$. Thus, this statement is incorrect.

(D) Being an aldose, it is a reducing sugar. The aldehyde group can be oxidized by Tollen's reagent, giving a positive test (silver mirror). Thus, this statement is correct.

Answer: It exhibits ring-chain tautomerism; It forms osazone with phenylthydrazine; It responds to Tollen's reagent

questions.q: The compound, which evolves carbon dioxide on treatment with aqueous solution of sodium bicarbonate at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C},$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}O-\mathrm{Cl}$ hydrolyses to form $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
even at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C},$ which subsequently reacts with $NaHCO_{3}$ (sodium bicarbonate) present in the same medium to form carbon dioxide $\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right)$.
The reaction involved is given below. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl}+\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
Thus, the option (b) is correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The ease of hydrolysis in the compounds $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl}(\mathrm{I}), \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{COCH}_{3}(\mathrm{II})$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}(\mathrm{III})$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}$ (IV) is of
the order
questions.solution:
The ease of hydrolysis of carbonyl compounds depend on the group attached to the carbonyl carbon. Among the given options, chlorine $(-$ Cl) group is clectron withdrawing group which makes the carbonyl group electrophilic. Hence, hydrolysis can occur most easily. The order for electron withdrawing tendency among given options is as follows:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
-\mathrm{Cl}>-\mathrm{OCOCH}_{3}>-\mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}>-\mathrm{NH}_{2} \\\end{array}$$
So, the correct order for the ease of hydrolysis in the given compounds is as follows:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOCOCH}_{3}$
$>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}>\mathrm{CH}_{3}{\mathrm{IV}} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Peroxide ion is
questions.solution:
The peroxide ion is $\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$.

Total number of electrons in $\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$ is $8 + 8 + 2 = 18$ electrons.

According to Molecular Orbital Theory, the electronic configuration of $\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$ is:

$\sigma_{1s}^{2} \sigma_{1s}^{*2} \sigma_{2s}^{2} \sigma_{2s}^{*2} \sigma_{2p_z}^{2} \pi_{2p_x}^{2} = \pi_{2p_y}^{2} \pi_{2p_x}^{*2} = \pi_{2p_y}^{*2}$

Since all the electrons are paired in the molecular orbitals, the peroxide ion has no unpaired electrons.

Therefore, the peroxide ion is diamagnetic.

Answer: Diamagnetic

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In which of the following species, $sp^3d^2$ hybridisation is not associated?
questions.solution:
The hybridization of a central atom is found using the steric number formula $H = \dfrac{1}{2}(V + M - C + A)$, where $V$ is the number of valence electrons, $M$ is the number of monovalent surrounding atoms, $C$ is the cationic charge, and $A$ is the anionic charge.

For $XeF_6$, $V = 8$ and $M = 6$. $H = \dfrac{1}{2}(8 + 6) = 7$, which corresponds to $sp^3d^3$ hybridization.

For $BrF_6^+$, $V = 7$, $M = 6$, and $C = 1$. $H = \dfrac{1}{2}(7 + 6 - 1) = 6$, which corresponds to $sp^3d^2$ hybridization.

For $IF_5$, $V = 7$ and $M = 5$. $H = \dfrac{1}{2}(7 + 5) = 6$, which corresponds to $sp^3d^2$ hybridization.

For $XeF_4$, $V = 8$ and $M = 4$. $H = \dfrac{1}{2}(8 + 4) = 6$, which corresponds to $sp^3d^2$ hybridization.

Thus, $XeF_6$ is not associated with $sp^3d^2$ hybridization.

Answer: $XeF_6$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among $N_2O, ClF_2^-, SO_2$ and $I_3^+$, the species having the linear structures are
questions.solution:
For $N_2O$, the central nitrogen atom is $sp$ hybridized and has no lone pairs. The structure is $N \equiv N^+ - O^- \leftrightarrow N^- = N^+ = O$, which gives a linear geometry.

For $ClF_2^-$, the central chlorine atom has $7$ valence electrons plus $1$ from the negative charge, giving $8$ electrons. It forms $2$ sigma bonds with fluorine atoms and has $3$ lone pairs. The steric number is $5$ ($sp^3d$ hybridization). The three lone pairs occupy the equatorial positions to minimize repulsion, resulting in a linear molecular geometry.

For $SO_2$, the central sulfur atom has $6$ valence electrons. It forms $2$ sigma bonds and has $1$ lone pair. The steric number is $3$ ($sp^2$ hybridization), resulting in a bent or V-shaped geometry.

For $I_3^+$, the central iodine atom has $7$ valence electrons minus $1$ from the positive charge, giving $6$ electrons. It forms $2$ sigma bonds with the other iodine atoms and has $2$ lone pairs. The steric number is $4$ ($sp^3$ hybridization), resulting in a bent geometry.

Therefore, only $N_2O$ and $ClF_2^-$ have linear structures.

Answer: $N_2O, ClF_2^-$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The H $-N-H$ angle in ammonia is $107.6^{\circ}$ while the $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{P} -\mathrm{H}$ angle in phosphine is $93.5^{\circ} .$ Relative to phosphine, the $p$ -character of the lone-pair on ammonia is expected to be
questions.solution:
Key point As the percentage of s character in a bond increases, the bond angle also increases. Therefore.
as the bond angle increases, the percentage of $p$ -character decreases.
In the given options, Ammonia ( $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ ) has bond angle $=107.6^{\circ}$ and Phosphine $\left(\mathrm{PH}_{3}\right)$ has bond angle $=93.5^{\circ}$
$\because$ Bond angle $\propto \frac{1}{p-\text { character }}$ and $\propto s$ -character
Bond angle of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}$ due to lone-pair lone-pair repulsion. Thus, the lone pair on $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ has less $p$ -character. Hence, option (a) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has the strongest H-bond?
questions.solution:
The strongest H-bond is $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{F}$ because in this case, a hydrogen is bonded to a most. electronegative atom i.e. fluorine. In all other options, hydrogen is bonded to oxygen and sulphur that are less electronegative atom than fluorine.
The correct electronegativity order of elements are as follows: $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{O}>\mathrm{S}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which statements are correct for the
peroxide ion?
options: It has five completely filled anti-bonding molecular orbitals
questions.solution:
The correct statements for peroxide ion $\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}\right)$ are that it is diamagnetic and it has bond
order one. Electronic configuration of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}$ (peroxide ion) is
$\sigma \mathrm{l} s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2},\left[\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}\right\}$
$$
\left[\pi^{\prime} 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{2}\right], \sigma^{*} 2 p_{t}^{0}
$$
$\because$ Bond order $=$ No. of electrons present in bonding  No. of electrons present in non - bonding
$\therefore \quad B O=\frac{10-8}{2}=\frac{2}{2}=1$
Number of unpaired electrons $=0$ 
$\mathrm{So}, \mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}$ is diamagnetic in nature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The boiling points of $\mathrm{HF}, \mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HBr}$ and $\mathrm{HI}$ follow the order
questions.solution:
Due to hydrogen bonding, HF shows highest boiling point.
Thereafter van der Waals' force decide the boiling point.
Larger the size or molecules mass, greater is the van der Waals' forces. Hence, higher is the boiling point.
$\because$ van der Waals' forces is more for HI than HBr, which is in turn is more than HCl.
Hence, correct order is $\mathrm{HF}>\mathrm{Hl}>\mathrm{HBr}>\mathrm{HCl}$

$$
\text { B.O }=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2}=\frac{10-4}{2}=3
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$, variation of concentration is plotted against time as shown below.

Which of the following statements is true?
options: Both the Regions (2) and (3) indicate equilibrium
questions.solution:
In the given graph, the concentration of reactant A decreases and the concentration of product B increases with time.

At equilibrium, the rates of the forward and backward reactions become equal, and the concentrations of both reactants and products become constant. They no longer change with time.

From the graph, it is evident that in region (1) and region (2), the concentrations of A and B are still changing. However, in region (3), the curves for both A and B become parallel to the time axis, indicating that their concentrations have become constant.

Therefore, region (3) indicates the state of equilibrium.

Answer: Region (3) indicates equilibrium

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Consider the following gas phase dissociation, $\mathrm{PCl}_5(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_3(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ with equilibrium constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ at a particular temperature and at pressure $P$. The degree of dissociation $(\alpha)$ for $\mathrm{PCl}_5(\mathrm{~g})$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{lccc} & \mathrm{PCl}_5(\mathrm{g}) &\rightleftharpoons & \mathrm{PCl}_3(\mathrm{g}) & + & \mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{g}) \\
\text{initially} & 1 & & 0 & & 0 \\
\text{moles taken} & & & \\
\begin{array}{l} \text{moles of equilibrium} \end{array} & 1-\alpha & & \alpha & & \alpha \\
& & &\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{PC}_3} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{Cl}_2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{PCl}_5}} & \because \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{gas}} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}} \\
\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot \mathrm{P}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot \mathrm{P}\right)} & \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{PC}_3}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{Cl}_2}=\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha} \\
\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\alpha^2 \mathrm{P}}{1-\alpha^2} & \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{PC}_5}=\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \\
\alpha=\left(\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}+\mathrm{P}}\right)^{1 / 2} & \end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The equilibrium constant for the reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$ is $4 \times 10^{-4}$ at $2000 \mathrm{~K}$. In presence of a catalyst the equilibrium is attained 10 times faster. Therefore, the equilibrium constant, in presence of the catalyst at $2000 \mathrm{~K}$ is
questions.solution:
As per Van't Hoff Equation
$\frac{\mathrm{d}\left(\ln \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}\right)}{\mathrm{dT}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{T}}}{\mathrm{RT}^{2}}$
As catalyst don't affect enthalpy of reaction $\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{r}}\right)$ and Temperature is constant. Therefore, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$ (equilibrium constant) will remain constant i.e. $4 \times 10^{-4}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The equilibrium constant for the following reactions are given at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\begin{array}{l}
2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{1}=1.0 \\
2 \mathrm{~B} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{K}_{2}=16 \\
2 \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{K}_{3}=25
\end{array}$
The equilibrium constant for the reaction $\mathrm{P} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~B}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
Hint:
We can manipulate the given equations as follows
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~B}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}, \quad \mathrm{K}_{1}^{\prime}=1$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{D} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}, \quad \mathrm{K}_{2}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{16}}$
$\mathrm{P} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{D}, \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{3}}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{25}}$
$\mathrm{P} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~B}, \quad \mathrm{~K}_{\text {final }}=1 \times \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{5}=\frac{1}{20}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the equilibrium, $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI},$ if at a given temperature the concentrations of the reactants are increased, the value of the cquilibrium constant, $K_{C}$, will
questions.solution:
In the equilibrium $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}$, if at a
given temperature, the concentrations of the reactants are increased, the value of the equilibrium constant $K_{C}$ will remain the same because equilibrium constant does not depend on the molar concentration of reactants.
The option (c) is correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The following equilibrium constants are given
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} ; K_{1} \\
\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO} ; K_{2}
\end{array}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; K_{3}$
The equilibrium constant for the oxidation of 2 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ to give NO is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\text {Given, } \\
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} ; K_{1} &=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}} \\
\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons & 2 \mathrm{NO} ; K_{2}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]} \\
\mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; K_{3}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}
\end{aligned}
\end{array}$
The chemical equation for the oxidation of 2 mol of
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ to give NO is
$2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(ii) and To get the equation (iv) from equation (i),
(iii) following steps are followed:
Reversing equation (i), we get
$2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \text { so } K^{\prime}=\frac{1}{K_{1}}$
Multiplying cquation (iii) by 3, we get
$3 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \mathrm{so} \mathrm{K}^{\prime}=K_{3}^{3}$
Adding equation (ii) and (vi)
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
so, $K^{\prime}=K_{2} \cdot K_{3}^{3}$
On combining (v) and (vii), we get the required equation having equilibrium constant $K^{\prime}=K_{2} \cdot \frac{K_{3}^{3}}{K_{1}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Equilibrium constants for the following reactions at $1200 \mathrm{K}$ are given
$2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)$
$K_{1}=6.4 \times 10^{-8}$
$2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)$
$K_{2}=1.6 \times 10^{-6}$
The equilibrium constant for the reaction? $\mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(g)$
at $1200 \mathrm{K}$ will be
questions.solution:
For the given reactions, $2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}, K_{1}=6.4 \times 10^{-8}$
or, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}, K_{1}^{\prime}=\sqrt{K_{1}}$
or $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}, \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, K_{1}^{\prime \prime \prime}=1 / \sqrt{K_{1}} \ldots$
$2 \mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_{2}, K_{2}=1.6 \times 10^{-6}$
or $\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}, K_{2}^{\prime}=\sqrt{K_{2}}$
From (i) and (ii) [on adding] $K=\frac{\sqrt{K_{2}}}{\sqrt{K_{1}}}=\frac{\sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-6}}}{\sqrt{6.4 \times 10^{-8}}}$
$K=\sqrt{\frac{10^{2}}{4}}=\sqrt{25}=5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following plots represent an exothermic reaction?
questions.solution:
For exothermic reaction.
$\because$
$K_{p} \propto \frac{1}{T}$
Thus, as $\frac{1}{T}$ increases (i.e. $T$ decreases), $K_{p}$ increases.
$\because$ $\Delta$ H for exothermic reactions is negative.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction, $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{g}) \mathrm{at} 300 \mathrm{K},$ the
value of $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is $-690.9 R$. The equilibrium constant value for the reaction at that temperature is $(R$ is gas constant $)$
questions.solution:
For the reaction,
$2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(g)$
Given that, $\Delta G^{\circ}=-690.9 R, T=300 \mathrm{K}$
From the formula, $\Delta G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K$
$-690.9 R=-R \times 300 \times \ln K$
or $\ln k=\frac{690.9}{300}=2.303$
of $=2.303 \log K=2.303$
of $K=10^{1}=10$
Unit of $K=(a \operatorname{tm}) \Delta^{n}=(a t m)^{2-3}=(a t m)^{-1}$
$\therefore \quad K=10$ atm $^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
If the equilibrium constants of the following equilibria
\(\mathrm{SO}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_3 \text { and } 2 \mathrm{SO}_3 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2\)
are given by \(\mathrm{K}_1\) and \(\mathrm{K}_2\) respectively, which of the following relations is correct
questions.solution:
Hints: \(\mathrm{K}_1=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]^{1 / 2}}\)
\(\mathrm{K}_2=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_2\right]^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]^2}\)
Thus \(\mathrm{K}_2=\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_1}\right)^2\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Calculate $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ for the reversible process given below if $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=167$ and $\mathrm{T}=800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\mathrm{CaCO}_3(\mathrm{~s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
questions.solution:
Hints: $K_p=K_C(R T)^{\Delta n}$
for $\mathrm{eq}^{\mathrm{n}} \quad \mathrm{CaCO}_3(\mathrm{~s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}), \Delta \mathrm{n}=1$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}}}=\frac{167}{(0.0821 \times 1073)^1}=1.89$

questions.q: For the reaction $S O_2+\frac{1}{2} O_2=S O_3$, if we write $K_p=K_c(R T)^x$, then $\mathrm{x}$ becomes

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a first order reaction the concentration of reactant decreases from $400$ moles lit$^{-1}$ to $50$ moles lit$^{-1}$ in $7.5 \times 10^3$ s. The rate constant of the reaction is (approximately)
questions.solution:
For a first order reaction, the concentration after $n$ half-lives is given by:

$[A]_t = \dfrac{[A]_0}{2^n}$

Substituting the given values:

$50 = \dfrac{400}{2^n}$

$2^n = 8 \Rightarrow n = 3$

The total time $t$ is related to the half-life $t_{1/2}$ by:

$t = n \times t_{1/2}$

$7.5 \times 10^3 = 3 \times t_{1/2}$

$t_{1/2} = 2.5 \times 10^3$ s

The rate constant $k$ for a first order reaction is:

$k = \dfrac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}$

$k = \dfrac{0.693}{2.5 \times 10^3} = 2.77 \times 10^{-4}$ s$^{-1}$

Answer: $2.77 \times 10^{-4}$ s$^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An organic compound undergoes first order decomposition. The time taken for its decomposition to $\dfrac{1}{8}$ th and $\dfrac{1}{10}$ th of its initial concentration are $t_{1/8}$ and $t_{1/10}$ respectively. The value of $\left[\dfrac{t_{1/8}}{t_{1/10}}\right]$ is [Given $\log_{10} 2 = 0.3$]
questions.solution:
For a first order reaction, the time $t$ required for the concentration to reach $[A]$ from an initial concentration $[A]_0$ is given by:

$t = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{[A]_0}{[A]} \right)$

For decomposition to $\dfrac{1}{8}$th of its initial concentration, $[A] = \dfrac{[A]_0}{8}$:

$t_{1/8} = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \log_{10} 8 = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \times 3 \log_{10} 2 = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \times 3 \times 0.3 = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \times 0.9$

For decomposition to $\dfrac{1}{10}$th of its initial concentration, $[A] = \dfrac{[A]_0}{10}$:

$t_{1/10} = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \log_{10} 10 = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \times 1$

Taking the ratio of the two times:

$\dfrac{t_{1/8}}{t_{1/10}} = \dfrac{0.9}{1} = 0.9$

Answer: $0.9$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a chemical reaction, half-life period $\left(\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}\right)$ is 10 minutes. How much reactant will be left after 20 minutes if one starts with 100 moles of reactant and the order of the reaction be (i) zero, (ii) one and (iii) two?
questions.solution:
For zero order, $k=\frac{x}{t}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{~K}} \\
& 10=\frac{100}{2 \times \mathrm{K}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~K}=5 \\
& 5=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{20} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=100 \therefore(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})=0
\end{aligned}$

For 1st order, No. of half lives $=\frac{20}{10}=2$
$\therefore(a-x)=\frac{100}{2^2}=25$

For 2nd order, $\mathrm{k}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{at}(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{ak}} \\
& 10=\frac{1}{100 \times \mathrm{k}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=10^{-3} \\
& \therefore 10^{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{100 \times 20 \times(100-\mathrm{x})} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=66.66 \\
& \therefore(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})=33.33
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An egg takes 4.0 minutes to boil at sea level where the boiling point of water is $T_1 K$, where as it takes 8.0 minutes to boil on a mountain top where the boiling point of water is $\mathrm{T}_2 \mathrm{~K}$. The activation energy for the reaction that takes place during the boiling of egg is
questions.solution:
As per Arrhenius Equation
$\ln \left(\frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{\mathrm{~K}_1}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{~T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2}\right]$

According to question
$\begin{aligned}
& \ln \left(\frac{1 / 8}{1 / 4}\right)=\frac{E_a}{R}\left[\frac{T_1-T_2}{T_1 T_2}\right] \\
& \ln 2=\frac{E_a}{R}\left[\frac{T_1-T_2}{T_1 T_2}\right] \\
& 0.693=\frac{E_a}{R}\left[\frac{T_1-T_2}{T_1 T_2}\right] \\
& \therefore E_a=0.693 R \frac{T_1 T_2}{T_1-T_2}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements is true about equilibrium constant and rate constant of a single step chemical reaction?
options: Equilibrium constant may increase or decrease but rate constant always increases with temperature
Both equilibrium constant and rate constant increase with temperature
Rate constant may increase or decrease but equilibrium constant always increases with temperature
Both equilibrium constant and rate constant decrease with temperature
questions.solution:
Hint : According to Van't Hoff equation
$\log \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)=\frac{\Delta H}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right]$
Where $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$ are Equilibrium Constants at $\mathrm{T}_1$ and $\mathrm{T}_2$ temperature respectively. When $\Delta \mathrm{H}\gt0$ (Endothermic process) then Equilibrium Constant increases with increase in temperature When $\Delta \mathrm{H} \lt 0$ (Exothermic process) then Equilibrium Constant decreases with increases in temperature. According to Arrhenius equation
$\log \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)=\frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right]$
where $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$ are Rate Constants at $\mathrm{T}_1$ and $\mathrm{T}_2$ temperature respectively. $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ (Activation energy) $\gt0$
When temperature increases rate constant will increase

questions.q:
Which of the following would give a linear plot?
(\(k\) is the rate constant of an elementary reaction and \(\mathrm{T}\) is temp. in absolute scale)
questions.solution: Hint : Arrhenius equation gives us \(\begin{aligned} & k=A e^{-E_a / R T} \\ & \ln k=\ln A-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) \\ & y=c-m x \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
options: Not possible to tell with the given data
First order with respect to both \(A\) and \(B\)
First order with respect to \(A\) and second order with respect to \(B\)
Second order with respect to \(A\) and first order with respect to \(B\).
questions.solution:
Hint : Let us assume \(R=K[A]^x[B]^y\)
Where \(\mathrm{x}\) and \(\mathrm{y}\) are orders wrt A and B respectively
\(\therefore\) We can write from given data
\(\begin{aligned}
& 100=\mathrm{k}(1)^{\mathrm{x}}(10)^{\mathrm{y}} \quad ---- (1)\\
& 1=\mathrm{k}(1)^{\mathrm{x}}(1)^{\mathrm{y}} \quad ---- (2)\\
& 10=\mathrm{k}(10)^{\mathrm{x}}(1)^{\mathrm{y}} \quad ---- (3)\\
\end{aligned}\)
\(2 \div 1\) gives
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{100}=\frac{\mathrm{k}(1)^{\mathrm{x}}(1)^y}{\mathrm{k}(1)^{\mathrm{x}}(10)^y}, \quad \frac{1}{100}=\left(\frac{1}{10}\right) \mathrm{y} \\
& \mathrm{y}=2
\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& 3 \div 1 \text { gives } \frac{10}{1}=\frac{K(10)^x(1)^y}{k(1)^x(1)^y} \\
& 10=(10)^x \quad x=1
\end{aligned}\)
So reaction is 2 nd order w.r.t \(B\) but 1 st order w.r.t \(A\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If in case of a radio isotope the value of half-life \(\left(T_{1/2}\right)\) and decay constant \((\lambda)\) are identical in magnitude, then their value should be
questions.solution:
Hint: For a radio decay \(T_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{\lambda}\)
If \(T_{1 / 2}=\lambda=x\) then \(\mathrm{x}=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{x}}\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^2=0.693, \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}=\lambda=(0.693)^{1 / 2}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Under the same reaction conditions, initial concentration of $1.386 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$ of a substance becomes half in $40 \mathrm{~s}$ and 20 s through first-order and zero-order kinetics respectively. Ratio $\left(\frac{k_{1}}{k_{0}}\right)$ of the rate constants for first-order $\left(k_{1}\right)$ and zero-order $\left(\mathrm{k}_{0}\right)$ of the reactions is
questions.solution:
For First order reaction
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}_{1}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_{1}=\frac{0.693}{40} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\quad_{-} \text {(i) }$
For Zero order reaction
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{2 \mathrm{k}_{0}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_{0}=\frac{1.386}{2 \times 20} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{dm}^{3} / \mathrm{s} \quad_{-} \text {(ii) }$
$\underline{\text { Using (i) and (ii) }}$
$\frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{\mathrm{k}_{0}}=\frac{0.693 / 40}{1.386 / 2 \times 20}=\frac{0.693}{1.386}=0.5 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}-\mathrm{dm}^{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a reaction $2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{P}$, when concentration of $\mathrm{B}$ alone is doubled, rate does not change and when concentrations of both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is doubled, rate increases by a factor of 4 . The unit of rate constant is,
questions.solution:
Hint :
Rate law: $\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\alpha}[\mathrm{B}]^{\beta} \quad \mathrm{B}$ follows first order kinetics. $\therefore \beta=1$
$\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}=\frac{[2 A]^{\alpha}[2 B]^{p}}{[A]^{\alpha}[B]^{\beta}}$
or, $4=2^{a+\beta}$
or, $\alpha+\beta=2$
or, $\alpha=1$
$\therefore$ overall order of reaction $=2$
Hence unit of rate constant $=\mathrm{L} \cdot \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{S}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Consider the following two first order reactions occurring at 298 K with same initial concentration of $A$ :(1) $A \rightarrow B ;$ rate constant, $k=0.693 \mathrm{min}^{-1}$ (2) $A \rightarrow C$; half-life, $l_{1 / 2}=0.693$ min Choose the correct option.
options: Both reactions proceed at the same rate.
Since two different products are formed, rates cannot be compared.
questions.solution:
Given, for two first order reactions.
$$
A \rightarrow B k=0.693 \mathrm{min}^{-1}...(1)
$$
and
$$
A \rightarrow C, t_{1 / 2}=0.693 \mathrm{min}...(2)
$$
For first order reaction
$$
t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{k}
$$
Thus, for the reaction $A \rightarrow B$
$$
t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{k}=\frac{0.693}{0.693}=1.0 \mathrm{min}
$$
Also, for first order reaction, lower the $t_{1 / 2}$ value, faster is the reaction.
$$
t_{1 / 2} \propto \frac{1}{k}
$$
and $k$ for $A \rightarrow C$ is $\frac{0.693}{0.693}=1 \mathrm{min}^{-1}$
Thus, reaction (2) is faster than reaction
(1) or reaction (1) is slower than reaction (2) Hence, option (b) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The half-life period of ${ }_{53} I^{125}$ is 60 days. The radioactivity after 180 days will be
questions.solution:
Let, initial concentration $(a)=100$
Given, half-life $\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)=60$ days 
To find, radioactivity,
i.e. $(a-x)$ after time $T$ ( 180 days)
$$
\because \quad T=n \times t_{1 / 2}
$$
where, $n=$ no. of half-lives
$$
\begin{aligned}
180 &=n \times 60 \\
n &=\frac{180}{60}=3
\end{aligned}
$$
and $(a-x)=\frac{a}{2^{n}}=\frac{100}{2^{3}}=\frac{100}{8}$
$$
(a-x)=12.5 \%
$$
Hence, (b) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Consider, the radioactive disintegration ${ }_{82} A^{210} \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow{ }_{82} D^{206}$, The sequence of emission can be
questions.solution:
Given, $82^{A^{210}} \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow{ }_{82} D^{206}$
i.e. difference in mass no. between $A$ and $D$ is of 4 units i.e., mass no. is decreased by 4 units while atomic no. remains the same, i.e. 82 for $A$ and $D$.
Also,
(i) On emission of each $\beta$ -particle, mass no. remains the same but atomic no. is increased by one unit.
(ii) On emission of each $\alpha$ -particle, mass no. is decreased by 4 units, while atomic no. is decreased by 2 units.
Thus, on emission of $2-\beta$ and $1-\alpha$ particle, we get $_{82} D^{206}$
i.e. ${ }_{82} A^{210} \stackrel{-\beta}{\longrightarrow}{ }_{83} A^{210} \stackrel{-\beta}{\longrightarrow}{ }_{84} A^{210} \stackrel{-a}{\longrightarrow}{ }_{82} A^{206}$
Thus, option (d) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The kinetic study of a reaction like $v A \rightarrow P$ at $300 \mathrm{K}$ provides the following curve, where concentration is taken in mol $\mathrm{dm}^{-3}$ and time in min. Identify the correct order $(n)$ and rate constant $(k)$
questions.solution:
From the given graph Slope $=\frac{(\text { rate })^{1 / 2}}{[A]}=4$
$\because$ rate $(r)=k[A]^{n}$
Where, $k=$ rate constant $[A]=$ concentration at time $t$ (in min).
$n=$ order.
According to the graph $(n=2)$ $\therefore \quad k=\frac{\text { rate }}{[A]^{n}}=[4]^{2}=16$
Hence, $n=2$ and
$$
k=16.0 \mathrm{dm}^{3} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \min^{-1}
$$
and option (d) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The half-life of $C^{14}$ is 5760 years. For a $200 \mathrm{mg}$ sample of $\mathrm{C}^{14}$, the time taken to change to $25 \mathrm{mg}$ is
questions.solution: Given. Half-life of $\mathrm{C}^{4}, t_{1 / 2}=5760$ years Initial concentration of sample of $\mathrm{C}^{4}, \mathrm{N}_{0}=200 \mathrm{mg}$ Final concentration of sample of $\mathrm{C}^{4}, \mathrm{N}_{1}=2 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{mg}$ The given decay is radioactive and all radiouctive decay follows first order kinetics. $$ t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{\lambda} $$ $\therefore$ $$ \lambda=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{5760} \mathrm{yr}^{-1} $$ We know that, for first order reaction $\begin{aligned} t &=\frac{2.303}{\lambda} \log \frac{N_{0}}{N_{1}}=\frac{2303}{0.693} \log \frac{[200]}{125} \\ &=\frac{2303 \times 5760}{0.693} \log 8 \\ &=17,28678 \mathrm{yrs} \approx 17.280 \mathrm{yrs} \end{aligned}$ Therefore, the time taken to change 200 me to $25 \mathrm{mg}$ is $17,286.78 \mathrm{yr}$, which is very close to option (d)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The nucleus $^{64}_{29}Cu$ accepts an orbital clectron to yield.
questions.solution:
The nucleus $^{64}_{29}Cu$ accepts an orbital electron to yield $^{64}_{28}Ni$. The atomic number of $\mathrm{Cu}$ is 29 , which is equal to the number of electrons and also equal to the number of protons. When $^{64}_{29}Cu$ accepts an orbital electron then electrons subtract from the atomic number, i.e.
$29-1=28$
$$
^{64}_{29}Cu+{ }_{-1} e^{0} \longrightarrow{ }_{28}^{64} \mathrm{Ni}
$$

questions.solution: When a positron is emitted, a proton is converted into neutron and neutrino. Thus, the number of protons and hence the atomic number decreases by one unit.

questions.solution: During the $\beta$ -emission, a neutron gets converted into a proton along with the formation of antineutrino and electron.

questions.solution: Emission of a positron results in the conversion of a proton into neutron, along with the release of neutrino. Thus, the number of protons decreases and of neutron increases, i.e. $n / p$ ratio increases and the nuclei becomes stable.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \({ }_{98} \mathrm{Cf}^{246}\) was formed along with a neutron when an unknown radioactive substance was bombarded with \({ }_{6} \mathrm{C}^{12}\). The unknown substance was :
questions.solution:
Let, the unknown radioactive substance be ${ }_{x} A^{y}$.
$x A^{y}+{ }_{6} C^{12} \longrightarrow{ }_{98} C f^{246}+{ }_{0} n^{1}$
On comparing atomic numbers, we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
x+6=98+0 \\
x=98-6=92
\end{array}
$$
On comparing mass numbers, we get
$$
y+12=246+1
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
y &=247-12 \\
&=235
\end{aligned}
$$
Since, uranium has the atomic number 92 , thus, the given unknown substance was ${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{235}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate of a certain reaction is given by, rate $=k\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{n}$. The rate increases 100 times when the pH changes from 3 to 1 . The order $(n)$ of the reaction is
questions.solution:
Given, rate $=k\left[\mathrm{H}^{*}\right]^{n}$ 
Initial $\mathrm{pH}=3 \mathrm{so}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=1 \times 10^{-3}$, initial rate $=\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{1}}$
Final $\mathrm{pH}=1$ so $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=1 \times 10^{-1}$
Final rate $=r_{2}=100 r_{1}$
On substituting values, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
r_{1}=k\left[1 \times 10^{-3}\right]^{n} \\
r_{2}=100 r_{1}=k\left[10^{-1}\right]^{n}
\end{array}
$$
Dividing eqs (i) by (ii).
$$
\frac{r_{1}}{100 r_{1}}=\left[\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-1}}\right]^{n}
$$
$\therefore$
$$
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{1}{100}=\left[\frac{10^{-2}}{1}\right]^{n} \Rightarrow\left[\frac{1}{100}\right]^{1}=\left[\frac{1}{100}\right]^{n} \\
n=1
\end{array}
$$
Thus, the reaction is of first order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A piece of wood from an archaeological sample has 5.0 counts min $^{-1}$ per gram of $\mathrm{C} - 14,$ while a fresh sample of wood has a count of $15.0 \mathrm{min}^{-1} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. If half-life of $\mathrm{C}-14$ is $5770 \mathrm{yr}$ the age of the archaeological sample is
questions.solution:
Age of archaeological sample.
$$
t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \left(\frac{N_{0}}{N}\right)
$$
(Here, $N_{0}=$ initial activity. $\mathrm{N}=$ remaining activity)
$$
\begin{aligned}
k &=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}} \\
\quad \text { (where } t_{1 / 2} &=\text { half-life) } \\
&=\frac{0.693}{5770} \mathrm{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$
$$
t=\frac{2.303 \times 5770}{0.693} \log \left(\frac{15}{5}\right)
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=19175.0 \log 3 \mathrm{yr} \\
=9148 \mathrm{yr} \approx 9200 \mathrm{yr}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For a chemical reaction at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C},$ the activation energy is $600 \mathrm{R}$. The ratio of the rate constants at $327^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to that of at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
will be
questions.solution:
From Arrhenius equation
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\operatorname{In} \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{R}\left[\frac{1}{T_{1}}-\frac{1}{T_{2}}\right] \\
\operatorname{In} \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{600 \mathrm{R}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{600}\right)
\end{array}
$$
or $\ln \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=600\left(\frac{2-1}{600}\right)$
of $\ln \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=1$ or $\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=e$

questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{F}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \mathrm{F}(g)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Rate of reaction } &=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{d p\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)}{d t}\right] \\
&=-\left[\frac{d p\left(F_{2}\right)}{d t}\right] \\
&=+\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{d p\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2} F\right)}{d t}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.q: Radioactivity of a sample ( $\mathrm{z}=22$ ) decreases $90 \%$ after 10 years. What will be the half life of the sample?
questions.solution:
Hints: $t=10$ yrs $\quad t_{\frac{1}{2}}=?$
$$
\lambda=\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{\mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{t}}}
$$
Since radioactivity decreases $90 \%$ in 10 yrs. $\Rightarrow N_0=100 \& N_t=10$
Thus $\lambda=\frac{2.303}{10} \log \frac{100}{10} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{2.303}{10}$
since $\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{0.693}{\lambda}=\frac{2.303 \times \log 2}{\lambda} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{2.303 \times \log 2}{2.303 / 10}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=(\log 2) \times 10 \simeq 3$ years

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The first electron affinity of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{O}$ will be of the order
questions.solution:
Key Point
(i) Half-filled/fully-filled configuration have (-) ve or low electron affinity i.e., (+) ve or high electron gain enthalpy.
(ii) Smaller the size, more is value of electron affinity, i.e. more $(-)$ ve will be the electron gain enthalpy.
On moving across a period, the size of atoms decreases due to increase in nuclear charge.
(i) Electron configuration of the elements are shown below. 
$C(Z=6)=1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{2}$
$\mathrm{N}(Z=7)=1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{3}$
$\mathrm{O}(Z=8)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 p^{4}$
(ii) Due to half-filled electronic configuration of nitrogen (i.e. 3 electrons in $3 p$ orbitals), it is highly stable and has $(+)$ ve value of electron gain enthalpy (i.e. about $30.9 \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ).
(iii) As size of ' $\mathrm{O}^{\prime},$ is smaller than that of ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime},{ }^{\prime} \mathrm{O}^{\prime}$
atom has higher $(-)$ ve electron gain enthalpy (about $-141.1 \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) than that of carbon (C-atom) [about $-122.3 \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol}]$.
Hence, correct order is $\mathrm{O}>\mathrm{C}>\mathrm{N}$ and $(\mathrm{b})$ is the correct option.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.q:
Among $\mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{N}$ and MeCN
(Me $=$ methyl group) the electronegativity of $\mathrm{N}$ is in the order
questions.solution: $\because$ In Me $_{3} \mathrm{N} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}$ is $s p^{3}$ -hybrid and in $\mathrm{MeCN} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}$ is $s p$ -hybrid Also electronegativity of any element $\alpha \%$ $s$ -character in hybrid orbitals. Thus, order of electronegativity for nitrogen is $$ \mathrm{MeCN}>\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{N} < \mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{N} $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: An element $X$ belongs to fourth period and fifteenth group of the periodic table. Which of the following statements is true?
options: It has completely filled s-orbital and a partialy filled $d$ -orbital
it has completely filled s-and $p$ -orbitals and a partially filled $d$ -orbital
It has completely filled $s$ - and $p$ -orbitals and a hat filled $d$ -orbital
It has a half filled $p$ -orbital and completely fled s-and $d$ -orbitals
questions.solution:
The element belonging to 4 th period and 15 th group is arsenic (As). The outer electronic configuration of 15 th group element is $n s^{2} n \rho^{3}$. Since arsenic belongs to 4 th period, therefore it has filled 'd' orbital too. Thus, the electronic configuration of As is $[A r] 4 s^{2} 3 d^{10} 4 \rho^{3} .$
So, it has filled's' and'd orbital and half-fliled porbital.

questions.q: Among Be, B, Mg and Al, the second ionisation potential is maximum for
questions.solution:
Group II A Group III A
$\quad \quad \quad \mathrm{Be} \quad  \quad \quad \mathrm{B}$
$\quad \quad \quad \mathrm{Mg} \quad  \quad \quad \mathrm{Al}$
The electronic configuration of boron is $2 s^{2}2p$. And electronic configuration of $\mathrm{B}^{+}$ is $2 \mathrm{s}^{2}$. Hence it is difficult to remove second electron ty $2 s^{2}$ shell because half-filled and fully orbitals are more stable than others.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.q: $\left({ }_{32} \mathrm{Ge}^{76},{ }_{34} \mathrm{Se}^{76}\right) \text { and } \left.{(}_{14} \mathrm{Si}^{30},{ }_{16} \mathrm{S}^{32}\right) \text { are examples of }$
questions.solution:
${ }_{32} \mathrm{Ge}^{76}$ and $\mathrm{sq} \mathrm{Se}^{76}$ have the same mass number but different atomic number, so they are isobars. Number of neutrons,
in  $_{14} e^{\mathrm{Si}^{30}}=30-14=16$
in $_{16} e^{\mathrm{Si}^{32}}=32-16=16$
Because of the presence of same number of neutrons, $_{14} \mathrm{Si}^{30}$ and ${ }_{16} \mathrm{S}^{32}$ are called isotones.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The bond angle in $\mathrm{NF}_{3}\left(102.3^{\circ}\right)$ is smaller than $\mathrm{NH}_{3}\left(107.2^{\circ}\right) .$ This is because of
options: large size of F compared to H
large size of N compared to F
opposite polarity of $\mathrm{N}$ in the two molecules
questions.solution: In $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$, $\mathrm{N}$ is less electronegative as compared to F but in $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, it is more electronegative than $\mathrm{H}$. And in case of same central atom. as the electronegativity of other atoms increases, bond angle decreases, Thus, bond angle in $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$ is smaller than that in $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ because of the difference in the polarity of $\mathrm{N}$ in these molecules.

options: due to relativistic contraction of the $6 s$ orbitals of $\mathrm{Pb}$ and $\mathrm{Bi}$, leading to inert pair effect
due to attainment of noble liquid configuration
questions.solution:
$_{82} \mathrm{Pb}=6 \mathrm{s}^{2} 6 p^{2}$
$_{83}{\mathrm{Bi}}=6 s^{2} 6 p^{3}$
Due to inertness of $6 s^{2}$ electrons in $\mathrm{Pb}$ and $\mathrm{Bi}$, they show bivalency and trivalency respectively instead of tetra and pentavalency.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The calculated magnetic moment for low spin $[Ru(EDTA)]^-$ is
questions.solution:
Let the oxidation state of Ru in the complex $[\text{Ru}(\text{EDTA})]^-$ be $x$.

The ligand $\text{EDTA}^{4-}$ has a charge of $-4$.

Therefore, $x + (-4) = -1 \Rightarrow x = +3$.

Ruthenium (Ru) is a $4d$-series element in Group 8. The electronic configuration of neutral Ru is $[\text{Kr}] 4d^7 5s^1$.

For the $\text{Ru}^{3+}$ ion, three electrons are removed, giving the configuration $[\text{Kr}] 4d^5$.

The complex is given as low spin. In an octahedral crystal field, the five $d$-electrons will pair up in the lower energy $t_{2g}$ orbitals.

The electronic arrangement will be $t_{2g}^5 e_g^0$.

This configuration has $1$ unpaired electron ($n = 1$).

The spin-only magnetic moment $\mu$ is calculated using the formula:

$\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)} \text{ BM}$

$\mu = \sqrt{1(1+2)} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73 \text{ BM}$

Answer: $1.73$ BM

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of conductivity of $0.001$ (M) separate aqueous solutions of $[Pt(NH_3)_6]Cl_4$ (i); $[Cr(NH_3)_6]Cl_3$ (ii); $[Co(NH_3)_4Cl_2]Cl$ (iii) and $K_2PtCl_6$ (iv) each containing octahedral complex species is
questions.solution:
The conductivity of an aqueous solution of a coordination compound depends on the number of ions it produces upon dissociation in water. Greater the number of ions, higher is the conductivity.

The given complexes dissociate in water as follows:

(i) $[Pt(NH_3)_6]Cl_4 \rightarrow [Pt(NH_3)_6]^{4+} + 4Cl^{-}$ (Total $5$ ions)

(ii) $[Cr(NH_3)_6]Cl_3 \rightarrow [Cr(NH_3)_6]^{3+} + 3Cl^{-}$ (Total $4$ ions)

(iii) $[Co(NH_3)_4Cl_2]Cl \rightarrow [Co(NH_3)_4Cl_2]^{+} + Cl^{-}$ (Total $2$ ions)

(iv) $K_2[PtCl_6] \rightarrow 2K^{+} + [PtCl_6]^{2-}$ (Total $3$ ions)

The number of ions produced follows the order: (iii) < (iv) < (ii) < (i).

Therefore, the correct order of conductivity is (iii) < (iv) < (ii) < (i).

Answer: (iii) < (iv) < (ii) < (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the metal complex $[Co(NH_3)_5SO_4]Br$, coordination number, oxidation number, number of d-electrons and number of unpaired d-electrons are respectively
questions.solution:
The given complex is $[Co(NH_3)_5SO_4]Br$.

The coordination sphere contains five $NH_3$ molecules and one $SO_4^{2-}$ ion. Since both $NH_3$ and $SO_4^{2-}$ act as monodentate ligands in this complex, the coordination number is $5 + 1 = 6$.

Let the oxidation state of Co be $x$. The complex dissociates into $[Co(NH_3)_5SO_4]^+$ and $Br^-$. The sum of oxidation states of all species in the complex ion equals its net charge:
$x + 5(0) + (-2) = +1$
$x = +3$
Thus, the oxidation number of Co is $3$.

The atomic number of Co is $27$, and its ground state electronic configuration is $[Ar] 3d^7 4s^2$. In the $+3$ oxidation state, it loses three electrons (two from $4s$ and one from $3d$), resulting in the configuration $[Ar] 3d^6$. Therefore, the number of d-electrons is $6$.

For $Co^{3+}$, $NH_3$ acts as a strong field ligand. This leads to the pairing of electrons in the $t_{2g}$ orbitals, forming a low-spin octahedral complex with the configuration $t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$. Since all electrons are paired, the number of unpaired d-electrons is $0$.

The values are $6, 3, 6, 0$ respectively.

Answer: $6, 3, 6, 0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following have tetrahedral structure?
questions.solution:
In $[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$, the oxidation state of $Ni$ is $+2$ ($3d^8$ configuration). Since $CN^-$ is a strong field ligand, it causes pairing of electrons in the $3d$ orbitals. This leaves one $3d$ orbital empty, resulting in $dsp^2$ hybridization and a square planar geometry.

In $[Ni(CO)_4]$, the oxidation state of $Ni$ is $0$ ($3d^8 4s^2$ configuration). $CO$ is a strong field ligand and forces the $4s$ electrons to pair up in the $3d$ orbitals, giving a $3d^{10}$ configuration. The empty $4s$ and $4p$ orbitals undergo $sp^3$ hybridization, resulting in a tetrahedral geometry.

In $[NiCl_4]^{2-}$, the oxidation state of $Ni$ is $+2$ ($3d^8$ configuration). Since $Cl^-$ is a weak field ligand, no pairing of electrons occurs. The complex undergoes $sp^3$ hybridization, resulting in a tetrahedral geometry.

In $CrO_4^{2-}$, the oxidation state of $Cr$ is $+6$ ($3d^0$ configuration). It forms four bonds with oxygen atoms, utilizing $d^3s$ hybridization, which gives it a tetrahedral geometry.

Thus, $[Ni(CO)_4]$, $[NiCl_4]^{2-}$, and $CrO_4^{2-}$ have tetrahedral structures.

Answer: $[Ni(CO)_4]$; $[NiCl_4]^{2-}$; $CrO_4^{2-}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The conductivity measurement of a coordination compound of cobalt (III) shows that it dissociates into 3 ions in solution. The compound is
questions.solution:
Co-ordinate compound of Co (III) dissociates into 3 ions in the solution means it has two ionisable-ions out side the coordination sphere with oxidation state of cobalt $(\mathrm{Co})=+3$
In option Name : Hexaammine cobalt (III) chloride Formula : $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$
$\because$ Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Co}=(+) 3$
In option (b) Name : Pentaammine sulphatocobalt (III) chloride Formula : $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)\right] \mathrm{Cl}$
$\because$ Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Co}=+3$
In option (c) Name : Pentaamminechloridocobalt
(III) sulphate Formula : $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
$\because$ Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Co}=+3$
In option (d) Name : Pentaamminechloridocobalt
(III) chloride Formula: $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
$\because$ Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Co}=+3$
Dissociation of this compound is shown below:
$\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO} \underbrace{\left.\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right]^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}}_{3 \text { ions }}$
This compound can give total 3 -ions, as follows. one $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right]^{2+}$ and two $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ions.
Hence, option (d) is the correct answer.

questions.q: A given amount of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ is oxidized by $\mathrm{x}$ mol of $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}$ in acidic medium. The number of moles of $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}{ }^{2-}$ required to oxidize the same amount of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ in acidic medium is
questions.solution:
As per Law of Equivalence
no. of eq. of $\mathrm{Fe}^{+2}=$ no. of eq. of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}=$ no. of eq. of $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{-2}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{O} \cdot \text { Agent })+5 \mathrm{e}^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{+2}+4\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
(\mathrm{n} . \mathrm{f}=5) \\
\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{-2}(\mathrm{O} \cdot \mathrm{A})+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{+3}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
(\mathrm{n} . \mathrm{f}=6)
\end{array}$
no. of eq. of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}=$ no. of moles $\times \mathrm{n}$. factor $=\mathrm{x} \times 5$
no. of eq. of $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{-2}=$ no. of moles $\times n . \mathrm{f}=\mathrm{y} \times 6$
From Law of equivalence
$\begin{array}{l}
6 y=5 x \\
\text { or } y=5 / 6 x=0.83 x
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The second ionisation energy of the following elements follows the order
questions.solution:
Key Point For similar electronic configuration of outer most shell, size will decide the value of ionisation energy. More the size, lesser is the value of ionisation energy.
Electronic configuration - IInd I.E
$\mathrm{Zn}^{+}$ ion $(Z=30)=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{d}^{10} 4 \mathrm{s}^{1}, - \quad(1734 \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol})$
$\mathrm{Cd}^{+}$ ion $(Z=48)=[\mathrm{Kr}] 4 d^{10} 5 \mathrm{s}^{1} - (1631 \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol})$
$\mathrm{Hg}^{+}$ ion $(Z=80)=[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 f^{14} \cdot 5 d^{10} 6 \mathrm{s}^{1} - (1809 \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol})$
$\because$ Size of $\mathrm{Cd}^{+}>\mathrm{Zn}^{+}$, thus it has lower IInd ionisation energy while due to lanthanoid effect (i.e., poor screening by $4 f$ and $5 d$ electrons, $\mathrm{Hg}^{+}$ has higher IInd ionisation-energy. Hence, correct order is,
$\mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cd} < \mathrm{Hg}$ and option (a) is the correct answer.

questions.solution:
Key Point A compound having more ionic character, has more melting point.
Be and Ca belong to the same group and ionic character increases down the group.
Thus, $\mathrm{BeCl}_{2}$ is less ionic than $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$. This means that melting point of $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$ is higher than that of $\mathrm{BeCl}_{2}$
Also, $\mathrm{Hg}$ is a transition metal, its compound is less ionic than $\mathrm{BeCl}_{2}$. Therefore, order of melting point will be
or
$$
\begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}>\mathrm{BeCl}_{2}>\mathrm{HgCl}_{2} \\
(\mathrm{ii})>(\mathrm{i})>(\mathrm{iii})
\end{array}
$$Hence, option (b) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which atomic species cannot be used as a nuclear fuel?
questions.solution: Among the given options, ${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238}$ is an isotope of uranium but cannot be used as a nuclear-fuel. U- 238 is non-fissile, i.e., it does not undergo fission by thermal neutrons. The energy released when U-238 absorbs a neutron is insufficient to carry out nuclear fission. Hence, (d) is the correct option.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A copper coin was electroplated with $\mathrm{Zn}$ and then heated at high temperature until there is a change in colour. What will be the resulting colour?
questions.solution:
A copper coin was electroplated with zinc
(Zn) and then heated at high temperature until there is a change in colour. The resulting colour will be golden. The golden colour is due to the zinc migrating through the copper to form the alpha-form of brass alloy (percentage of $\mathrm{Cu}>65 \%$ and that of $\mathrm{Zn}    < $ $35 \%)$
$$
\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{Cu} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \text { Brass }
$$
Thus, the option (d) is correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement(s) is (are) correct when a mixture of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ is gently warmed with conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} ?$
options: A deep red vapour is evolved
The vapour when passed through NaOH solution, gives a yellow solution
questions.solution:
(A) When $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ warmed with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (i.e. in acidic medium), they produce deep red vapours of chromyl chloride $\left(\mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)$
(B) When NaOH is passod, the product $CrO_{3}$, formed In step (a) will react with NaOH and gives yellow colour solution
(C) Chlorine gas is not evolved, thus false
(D) In the given reaction, chromyl chloride $\left(\mathrm{CrO}_2\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)$ is formed. All reactions are as follows
(i) $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KHSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{CrO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(ii) $\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}+\mathrm{HCl}$
(iii) $\mathrm{CrO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
(iv) $\mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{HCl}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Cu}$ is
questions.solution:
Atomic number of Cu is 29 .
Thus, its electronic configuration can be written as
$$
\mathrm{Cu}_{29}=1s^{2}, 2 s^{2}, 2 p^{6}, 3 s^{2}, 3 p^{6}, 3 d^{10}, 4 s^{1}
$$
(Since, fully filled orbitals are more stable, so one electron from 4 s orbital transfers into $3 d$ orbital.
The electronic configuration of neon is
$$
\mathrm{Ne}_{10}=1 \mathrm{s}^{2}, 2 s^{2}, 2 p^{6}
$$
Thus, using inert gas, the configuration of Cu can also be represented as
$$
\mathrm{Cu}_{29}=[\mathrm{Ne}] 3 \mathrm{s}^{2}, 3 p^{6}, 3 d^{10}, 4 \mathrm{s}^{1}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a conductance experiment, aqueous $AgNO_3$ solution is added to aqueous $KCl$ solution gradually and simultaneously the molar conductivity $(\lambda_m)$ is measured. The correct plot of $\lambda_m$ versus volume of $AgNO_3$ solution is
questions.solution:
During the conductometric titration of aqueous $KCl$ with $AgNO_3$, the following reaction takes place:

$AgNO_3(aq) + KCl(aq) \rightarrow AgCl(s) \downarrow + KNO_3(aq)$

Initially, the conductance of the solution is due to $K^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions. As $AgNO_3$ is added, the $Cl^-$ ions are precipitated as $AgCl$, and they are replaced in the solution by $NO_3^-$ ions.

Since the ionic mobilities (and hence ionic conductances) of $Cl^-$ and $NO_3^-$ are nearly the same, the overall conductance of the solution remains almost constant before the equivalence point.

After the equivalence point, all the $Cl^-$ ions have been precipitated. Further addition of $AgNO_3$ introduces excess $Ag^+$ and $NO_3^-$ ions into the solution. This increases the total number of ions, leading to a sharp increase in the conductance.

Therefore, the plot of conductance versus the volume of $AgNO_3$ added will be a nearly horizontal line followed by a steeply rising line. This corresponds to the graph shown in option (D).

Answer:

questions.q: The molar conductances of $\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2, \mathrm{BaCl}_2$ and $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ at infinite dilution are $523.28,280.0$ and $129.8 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The molar conductance of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ at infinite dilution will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} 2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left[\mathrm{NH} \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{OH}\right]}^{\infty} & =2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}[\mathrm{NH} 4 \mathrm{Cl}]}^{\infty}-\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left[\mathrm{BaCl}_2\right]}^{\infty}+\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left[\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2\right]}^{\infty} \\ \left.2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NH} 4} \mathrm{OH}\right) & =2 \times(129.8)-280+523.28 \\ \left.2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NH} 4}^{\infty} \mathrm{OH}\right) & =502.88 \\ \lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NH} 4 \mathrm{OH})}^{\infty} & =251.44 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The specific conductance \((\mathrm{k})\) of \(0.02(\mathrm{M})\) aqueous acetic acid solution at 298 K is \(1.65 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\). The degree of dissociation of acetic acid is \(\left[\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}=349.1 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right.\) and \(\left.\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}}=40.9 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]\)
questions.solution:
Hint: Firstly we find $\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\left(\mathrm{aq} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}\right)$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{K} \times 1000}{\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{M})}=\frac{1.65 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000}{0.02}=8.25 \mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}\right.$ at infinite dilution $)=\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)+\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right)$
Now degree of dissociation
$\begin{aligned} & \alpha=\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH} \text { at } 0.02(\mathrm{M})\right]}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH} \text { at infinite dilution }\right]} \\ & \alpha=\frac{8.25}{349.1+40.9}=0.021\end{aligned}$

questions.solution:
Reaction $2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}$ $\therefore \mathrm{n}=2, \quad \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}$ As $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}}^{0} \frac{-0.059}{\mathrm{n}} \log \mathrm{Q}=-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \mathrm{Q}$ $\therefore$ if $\mathrm{Q}>1 \quad \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}} < 0$ (A) $Q=\frac{1}{1}=1 \quad X$ (B) $Q=\frac{1}{2^{2}} < 1 \quad X$ (C) $Q=\frac{2}{12}>1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The charge carried by 1 millimole of $M^{n+}$ ions is 193 coulombs. The value of $n$ is
questions.solution:
One millimole $=0.001 \mathrm{mol}$
i.e. $1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{mol}$
$\because 96500 \mathrm{C}$ is required for $1 \mathrm{mol}$ of electrons.
$\therefore 193 \mathrm{C}$ give $\frac{193 \times 1}{96500}=0.002 \mathrm{mol},$ i.e. $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{mol}$ of
electrons.
Thus, value of $n$ in $M^{n+}$ is $=\frac{0.002}{0.001}=2$
Hence, option (b) is the correct answer.

Electrolysis of copper sulphate solution using Cu-electrodes, 
Ions present : $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}, \mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
At cathode $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(s)$
At anode $\mathrm{Cu}(s)-2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)$
So, the option (d) is correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What amount of clectricity can deposit
I mole of Al metal at cathode when passed through molten $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3} ?$
questions.solution:
The number of electrons involved in the reaction are three as shown below
$$
\mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}
$$
It means the conversion of every aluminium ion to aluminium atom requires three electrons. Therefore, the amount of electricity required for one mole of $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ ions $=3 \mathrm{F}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution: Given, $\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} ; E^{\circ}=+0.76 \mathrm{V}$ $$ \mathrm{Fe} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 e^{-} ; E^{\circ}=+0.41 \mathrm{V} $$ On reversing the above equation (i) and (ii), we get $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn} ; E^{\circ}=-0.76 \mathrm{V}$ $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe} ; E^{\circ}=-0.41 \mathrm{V}$ [where, $E^{\circ}=$ standard reduction potential] To find, the standard emf of the cell with the reaction. So, $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}$ $=-0.41 \mathrm{V}+0.76 \mathrm{V}=+0.35 \mathrm{V}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At temperature of $298 \mathrm{K}$, the emf of the following electrochemical cell. $\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Ag}^{+*}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Zn}^{2 +}(0.1 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})$ will be (Given, $E^{\prime \prime}$ call $=-1.562 \mathrm{V}$ )
questions.solution:
From the given cell, the cell reaction is
$2 \mathrm{Ag}(s)+Z \mathrm{n}^{2+}(01 \mathrm{M}) \longrightarrow$ $2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(0.1 \mathrm{M})+\mathrm{Zn}(s)$
The Nemst equation is,
$E_{\text {cel }}=E_{\text {cel }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{\left.\mid \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}$
or, $E_{\text {cal }}=(-1.562)-\frac{(0.0591)}{2} \log \frac{(0.1)^{2}}{(0.1)}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Or, } E_{\text {cel }} &=(-1.562)-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log 10^{-1} \\ &=-1.562+\frac{0.0591}{2} \\ &=-1.562+0.02955 \\ &=-1.532 \mathrm{V} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The quantity of electricity needed to separately electrolyze 1 M solution of $\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}$, $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ completely is in the ratio of
questions.solution:
From the Faradiay's IInd law, we know that
$$
W \propto \text { Zit, } \quad W \propto \frac{E}{96500} i t
$$
or $\quad$ it $\propto \frac{1}{E}$ or electricity $\propto \frac{1}{E}$ 
Thus, the amount of electricity required for $\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}, \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is in the ratio of
2: 3: 1 . (Here, 2,3 and 1 are the valencies of metal atom in the given compounds). 
Alternatively 
In $Z n S O_{4}$,
$Z n^{2+}+2 e^{-}$ $\rightarrow Z \mathrm{n}$
$1mol$  $\text{2 mol or 2 Faraday}$
In $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$,
$\mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}$
$1mol$  $\text{3F}$
In $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$,
$\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}$
$1mol$  $\text{1F}$
Thus, the ratio of amounts of electricity required is 2: 3: 1

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\begin{array}{lllll}\text {The two } & \text { half-cell } & \text { reactions } & \text { of } & \text { an }\end{array}$ electrochemical cell is given as $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag} ; \quad E_{\mathrm{Ax}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=-0.3995 \mathrm{V}$ $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3 *}+e^{-} ; E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} \mathrm{Fv}^{2+}}=-0.7120 \mathrm{V}$. The value of cell EMF will be
questions.solution:
Species with more negative $E^{\circ}$ (standard reduction potential) generally acts as reducing agent while with less negative $E^{\circ}$ acts as oxidising agent. Thus, the overall reaction is
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{Ag} \\ \Delta E^{\circ} &=E_{\mathrm{OA}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{RA}}^{\circ} \\ &=-0.3995-(-0.7120) \mathrm{V} \\ &=-0.3995+0.7120 \mathrm{V} \\ &=+0.3125 \mathrm{V} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the molar conductance of $0.007 \mathrm{M}$ hydrofluoric acid is 150 mho $\mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ and its $\Lambda_{m}^{\circ}=500$mho$cm^2$$mol^{-1}$ The value of the dissociation constant of the acid at the given concentration  at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution: Degree of dissociation, $\alpha=\frac{\lambda^{\circ} c}{\lambda^{\circ}m}=\frac{150}{500}=0.3$ Given, $C=0.007 \mathrm{M}$ Hydrofluoric acid dissociates in the following manner Dissociation constant, $$ K_{a}=\frac{\left[H^{+}\right]\left[F^{-}\right]}{[H F]}=\frac{C \alpha \cdot C \alpha}{C(1-\alpha)}=\frac{C \alpha^{2}}{(1-\alpha)} $$ On substituting values, we get $$ \begin{aligned} K_{a}=\frac{0.007 \times(0.3)^{2}}{(1-0.3)} &=\frac{63 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2}}{0.7} \\ &=9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The formal potential of $Fe^{3+}/Fe^{2+}$ in a sulphuric acid and phosphoric acid mixture $\left(E^{\circ}=+0.61 \mathrm{V}\right)$ is much lower than the standard potential $\left(E^{\circ}=+0.77 \mathrm{V}\right)$. This is due to
questions.solution: Since, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$, both are strong acids so their presence makes the medium more acidic. The $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions form complex, IFeHPO $\left._{4}\right]^{+}$ with these acid and hence, the concentration of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ion reduces which results in lowering in the formal reduction potential.

questions.q: The correct order of equivalent conductances at infinite dilution in water at room temperature for $\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{HO}^{-}$ ions is
questions.solution: Equivalent conductance $\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{eq}}\right)$ is defined as the conducting power of all the ions produced by one gram equivalent of an electrolyte in a given solution.in case of weak electrolytes (as $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}, \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{AgCl}$ etc. $),$ ionisation is very small compared to strong electrolytes (as KCl, NaOH etc.), hence $\Lambda_{\text {oq }}$ of weak electrolyte is low. Moreover, ionisation of $\mathrm{H}^{*}$ is maximum among given ions. Thus, correct of order of $\Lambda_{\mathrm{eq}}$ is $\mathrm{H}^{+}>\mathrm{OH}^{-}>\mathrm{K}^{+}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is present in maximum amount in 'acid rain'?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (sulphuric acid) is present in maximu $\mathrm{h}_{4}$ amount in 'acid rain'. Oxides of nitrogen and sulphur, released into the atmosphere from industries and automobiles thermal power plants, are the miain sources of acid rain. These oxides on oxidation followed by hydrolysis give sulphuric acid and nitric acid that alongwith $\mathrm{HCl}$ are responsible for the acidity of rain. The oxidation reaction is catalysed by particulate matter present in the polluted atmosphere. $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(a q)$
$4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\left(h \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(a q)\right.$
