questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many isomers can a compound with molecular formula $C_3H_5Br$ have?
questions.solution:
The degree of unsaturation for the molecular formula $C_3H_5Br$ is calculated as $C + 1 - \dfrac{H + X - N}{2} = 3 + 1 - \dfrac{5 + 1 - 0}{2} = 4 - 3 = 1$.

This indicates that the isomers can either be acyclic with one double bond or cyclic with one ring.

For acyclic isomers, the parent carbon skeleton is propene, $CH_2=CH-CH_3$. Substituting one hydrogen atom with a bromine atom gives the following possible structures:

Bromine at C-1 gives $CH(Br)=CH-CH_3$ (1-bromoprop-1-ene). This compound can exist as two geometrical isomers: cis-1-bromoprop-1-ene and trans-1-bromoprop-1-ene.

Bromine at C-2 gives $CH_2=C(Br)-CH_3$ (2-bromoprop-1-ene), which is one isomer.

Bromine at C-3 gives $CH_2=CH-CH_2Br$ (3-bromoprop-1-ene), which is one isomer.

Total acyclic isomers = $2 + 1 + 1 = 4$.

For cyclic isomers, the parent ring is cyclopropane. Substituting one hydrogen atom with a bromine atom gives bromocyclopropane, which is one isomer.

Total number of isomers = $4 + 1 = 5$.

Answer: 5

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Rank the following anions in order of decreasing nucleophilicity in a polar protic solvent (most $\rightarrow$ least nucleophilic)
questions.solution:
Nucleophilicity in a polar protic solvent depends on the size (polarizability) of the nucleophilic atom and the availability of the lone pair (charge density).

First, compare the nucleophilic atoms: sulfur in anion 2 and oxygen in anions 1 and 3. In polar protic solvents, larger atoms are less heavily solvated by hydrogen bonding and are more polarizable. Thus, the thiolate ion (2) is a much better nucleophile than the oxygen-based anions. This gives $2 > 1$ and $2 > 3$.

Next, compare the oxygen-based anions 1 and 3. In anion 1 (propoxide), the negative charge is localized on a single oxygen atom, making it highly reactive. In anion 3 (propanoate), the negative charge is delocalized over two oxygen atoms via resonance. This resonance stabilization decreases the electron density on any single oxygen atom, making it a weaker nucleophile than the alkoxide. Thus, $1 > 3$.

Combining these observations, the order of decreasing nucleophilicity is $2 > 1 > 3$.

Answer: $2 > 1 > 3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The IUPAC name of  is
questions.solution:
The given compound is a substituted benzene ring with three substituents: a chloro group ($-Cl$), a methyl group ($-CH_3$), and a nitro group ($-NO_2$).

According to IUPAC nomenclature rules, the carbon atoms of the benzene ring are numbered to give the lowest possible locant set to the substituents.

Evaluating the possible numbering schemes:
Starting from the chloro group and numbering towards the nitro group gives locants $1, 2, 4$.
Starting from the nitro group and numbering towards the chloro group gives locants $1, 2, 5$.
Starting from the methyl group and numbering towards the nitro group gives locants $1, 3, 4$.

The lowest locant set is $1, 2, 4$. Thus, the chloro group is at position $1$, the nitro group is at position $2$, and the methyl group is at position $4$.

The substituents are cited in alphabetical order: Chloro, Methyl, Nitro.

Combining these with the parent name benzene, the IUPAC name is $1$-Chloro-$4$-methyl-$2$-nitrobenzene.

Answer: 1-Chloro-4-methyl-2-nitrobenzene

questions.q: The order of decreasing ease of abstraction of hydrogen atoms in the following molecule
questions.solution:
Abstraction of $\left(\mathrm{H}_{a}\right)$ hydrogen atom results in the formation of $3^{\circ}$ allylic carbocation wheres abstraction of $\mathrm{H}_{c}$ gives $2^{\circ}$ allylic carbocation (which is less stable as compared to former one). However, abstraction of $\mathrm{H}_{b}$ gives only a $2^{\circ}$ carbocation which is less stable as compared to former two. More the stability of generated carbocation, higher is the tendency of abstraction of hydrogen. Thus, the order of decreasing ease of abstraction of hydrogen atoms from the given molecule is
$$
\mathrm{H}_{a}>\mathrm{H}_{c}>\mathrm{H}_{b}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Amongst the following, the one which can exist in free state as a stable compound is
questions.solution:
Molecules having whole number for degree of unsaturation can exist in free state as stable compounds. 
Degree of unsaturation $=\frac{\Sigma n(v-2)}{2}+1$
where, $n=$ number of atoms of a particular type $v=$ valency of the atom.
(a) For $\mathrm{C}_{7} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{O}$,
$D U=\frac{7(4-2)+9(1-2)+1(2-2)}{2}+1=3 \cdot 5$
(b) For $\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}$
$$
\mathrm{DU}=\frac{8(4-2)+9(1-2)+1(2-2)}{2}+1=3 \cdot 0
$$
(c) For $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{O}$
$$
\mathrm{DU}=\frac{6(4-2)+11(1-2)+1(2-2)}{2}+1=1 \cdot 5
$$
(d) For $\mathrm{C}_{10} \mathrm{H}_{17} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
$D U=\frac{10(4-2)+17(1-2)+2(2-2)}{2}+1=2 \cdot 5$
$\therefore \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}$ will exist in free state or a stable compound.

questions.solution:
In the Bayer's process, the leaching of alumina is done by using $\mathrm{NaOH}$.
$\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(s)+2 \mathrm{NaOH}(a q) \stackrel{150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 35 \mathrm{atm}}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{Na}\left[\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

$\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s) \underset{\longrightarrow}{\text{1470K}} \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(s)+x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(g)$

questions.solution:
Lintz and Donawitz (L.D.) process is an important process for manufacturing of steel. In India, Rourkela steel plant is based upon this process.
This process is carried out in a converter similar to bessemer converter having lining (usually refractory made of $\mathrm{Mg} \mathrm{O}$ and $(\mathrm{CaO})$.
In this method scrap iron can be used along with flux in converter : On passing oxygen gas at $4-12$ atm pressure into converter, temperature of $2000-2500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ range produced which separates C. Si, Mn etc., impurities as slag from
iron quickly remaining pure iron is mixed with spiegeleisen (alloy Fe, $\mathrm{Mn}$, C) to produce better quality steel.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The major product in the following reaction is
questions.solution:
The reaction proceeds via nucleophilic aromatic substitution.

In the reactant 2,6-dichloro-1,4-dinitrobenzene, the $NO_2$ group at C1 (top) is flanked by two bulky ortho $Cl$ groups. This steric crowding forces the top $NO_2$ group out of the plane of the benzene ring, resulting in steric inhibition of resonance.

Because it is non-planar, this $NO_2$ group can only exert a $-I$ effect and cannot effectively stabilize the intermediate via its $-R$ effect if a nucleophilic attack were to occur at the ortho $Cl$ positions.

Conversely, the $NO_2$ group at C4 (bottom) is unhindered, coplanar with the ring, and provides strong resonance stabilization ($-R$ effect) for a negative charge developing at its para position (C1).

When the nucleophile $MeO^-$ attacks C1, the intermediate Meisenheimer complex is highly stabilized by the coplanar para $NO_2$ group. Attack at C2 to displace $Cl$ yields an intermediate that lacks this strong resonance stabilization.

Furthermore, the sterically hindered $NO_2$ group at C1 acts as an excellent leaving group, and its departure relieves significant steric strain.

Therefore, $MeO^-$ selectively displaces the top $NO_2$ group, yielding the product shown in option (4).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Indicate the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction of a secondary alkyl halide with a strong base ($\text{CH}_3\text{ONa}$) proceeds via an E2 elimination mechanism.

E2 elimination in a cyclohexane ring requires an anti-periplanar (trans-diaxial) arrangement of the leaving group and the $\beta$-atom being abstracted.

In the reactant, the -Br group is on a wedge (up). For anti-periplanar elimination, the $\beta$-atom must be on a dash (down).

At the upper $\beta$-carbon, the -$\text{CH}_3$ group is on a dash, but it cannot be eliminated. The -H atom is on a wedge, so it is syn-periplanar and cannot be eliminated.

At the lower $\beta$-carbon, the -D atom is on a dash, making it anti-periplanar to the -Br group. The -H atom is on a wedge.

Therefore, the base abstracts the anti-periplanar -D atom, leading to the elimination of DBr and the formation of a double bond at the lower position.

The resulting product retains the -H atom on the newly formed vinylic carbon and the original stereochemistry at the upper allylic carbon, which corresponds to option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In the following reaction sequence, the product $Y$ is
$Br(CH_2)_{12} - C \equiv CH \xrightarrow{\text{NaNH}_2} X \xrightarrow[\text{catalyst}]{\text{H}_2,\text{Lindler}} Y$
questions.solution:
The given reactant is $Br(CH_2)_{12} - C \equiv CH$.

When treated with $NaNH_2$ (a strong base), the acidic terminal alkyne proton is abstracted to form an acetylide ion:
$Br(CH_2)_{12} - C \equiv CH \xrightarrow{NaNH_2} Br(CH_2)_{12} - C \equiv C^- Na^+$

This acetylide ion undergoes an intramolecular $S_N2$ reaction. The nucleophilic carbon attacks the carbon bearing the bromine atom, displacing the bromide ion. This results in the formation of a 14-membered cyclic alkyne, cyclotetradecyne (Product X).

Next, cyclotetradecyne is treated with $H_2$ in the presence of Lindlar's catalyst. Lindlar's catalyst ($Pd/CaCO_3$ poisoned with lead acetate or quinoline) is used for the partial reduction of alkynes to alkenes. It specifically undergoes syn-addition of hydrogen, converting the alkyne into a cis-alkene.

Therefore, the triple bond in cyclotetradecyne is reduced to a cis double bond, yielding cis-cyclotetradecene as Product Y.

Option (B) correctly represents the structure of cis-cyclotetradecene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The products $X$ and $Y$ in the following reaction sequence are
questions.solution:
The first step is the Birch reduction of anisole. The electron-donating methoxy ($-OCH_3$) group directs the reduction such that the substituent remains on one of the double bonds of the resulting unconjugated diene. Thus, the reduction yields 1-methoxy-1,4-cyclohexadiene as product X.

In the second step, the reaction of $CHBr_3$ with the strong base EtONa generates dibromocarbene ($:CBr_2$). This carbene is an electrophilic species that undergoes a [2+1] cycloaddition with alkenes.

Product X contains two double bonds: an enol ether double bond and an isolated double bond. The enol ether double bond is significantly more electron-rich and nucleophilic due to the +M (resonance) effect of the $-OCH_3$ group. Therefore, the electrophilic dibromocarbene preferentially attacks the enol ether double bond.

This regioselective addition forms the dibromocyclopropane ring at the substituted position, yielding product Y as shown in Option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the following sequence of reactions, what is the end product '$Z$'?
questions.solution:
The reaction of cyclohexyl methyl ketone with $\text{PCl}_5$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$ converts the carbonyl group into a geminal dichloride. The product X is 1-cyclohexyl-1,1-dichloroethane.

Treatment of the gem-dichloride X with the strong base $\text{NaNH}_2$ causes double dehydrohalogenation, forming a terminal alkyne. The subsequent acidic workup with $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ protonates the intermediate sodium acetylide to give cyclohexylacetylene as product Y.

Hydroboration-oxidation of the terminal alkyne Y using $\text{B}_2\text{H}_6$ followed by $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2/\text{OH}^-$ results in the anti-Markovnikov addition of water across the triple bond. This initially forms an enol, which rapidly tautomerizes to the corresponding aldehyde. The final product Z is cyclohexylacetaldehyde, which consists of a cyclohexyl ring attached to a $-\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}$ group.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$RO-CH_2-C \equiv CH \xrightarrow{X} \xrightarrow{Y} RO-CH_2-C \equiv C-CH_2CH_2-Br$
To carry out the above conversion $X$ and $Y$ are respectively
questions.solution:
The conversion involves the alkylation of a terminal alkyne.

The terminal alkyne $RO-CH_2-C \equiv CH$ is first deprotonated by a strong base, $NaNH_2$ (reagent $X$), to form the corresponding acetylide ion $RO-CH_2-C \equiv C^- Na^+$.

The acetylide ion acts as a nucleophile and undergoes an $S_N2$ reaction with a dihalide (reagent $Y$). Since the final product contains a $-CH_2CH_2-Br$ group, the nucleophile must attack a carbon attached to a leaving group that is better than bromide.

The leaving group ability of halide ions follows the order $I^- > Br^- > Cl^- > F^-$.

Therefore, 1-bromo-2-iodoethane ($I-CH_2-CH_2-Br$) is the appropriate reagent $Y$. The acetylide ion selectively displaces the better leaving group (iodide ion), leaving the bromine atom intact in the product:

$RO-CH_2-C \equiv C^- + I-CH_2-CH_2-Br \rightarrow RO-CH_2-C \equiv C-CH_2-CH_2-Br + I^-$

Answer: $NaNH_2, I-CH_2-CH_2-Br$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A $5.0$ cm$^3$ solution of $H_2O_2$ liberates $1.27$ g of iodine from an acidified $KI$ solution. The percentage strength of $H_2O_2$ is close to
questions.solution:
The chemical reaction is given by:

$H_2O_2 + 2KI + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow I_2 + K_2SO_4 + 2H_2O$

Moles of $I_2$ liberated = $\dfrac{1.27}{254} = 0.005$ mol

From the stoichiometry of the reaction, $1$ mole of $H_2O_2$ produces $1$ mole of $I_2$.

Moles of $H_2O_2$ reacted = $0.005$ mol

Mass of $H_2O_2$ = $0.005 \times 34 = 0.17$ g

This mass of $H_2O_2$ is present in $5.0$ cm$^3$ of the solution.

Percentage strength (w/v) is the mass of solute in $100$ mL of solution.

Percentage strength = $\dfrac{0.17}{5.0} \times 100 = 3.4\%$

Answer: $3.4$

options: Ortho-form is more stable than para-form
They differ in their nuclear spin
The ratio of ortho to para hydrogen changes with change in temperature
questions.solution: (i) Ortho and para hydrogens have difterent nuclear spin. In $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ molecule, two protons in two H atoms with pare spin are called ortho-hydrogen (ii) Ortho-hydrogen is more stable than para form at and above room temperature only, therefore para form always tends to revert in ortho-form. Thus, not correct. (iii) The per cent composition of ortho and para changes with the lemperature. Thus, their ratio also change. (iv) They have slightly different boling point. Hence, (b) is the answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Commercial sample of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ is labeled as $10 \mathrm{V}$. Its % strength is nearly
questions.solution:
10 V $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$  means $1 \mathrm{L}$ of this solution will produce $10 \mathrm{LO}_{2}$ at STP.
$$
\begin{array}{c}
2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\quad \mathrm{O}_{2} \\
68 \mathrm{g} \quad \quad \quad \quad 22.4 \text{L at STP}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore 22.4 \mathrm{L}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is obtained from $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}=68 \mathrm{g}$
10 L of $O_2$ will be obtained from
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}=\frac{68}{22.4} \times 10=30.36 \mathrm{g}
$$
$1000 \mathrm{mL}$ of the given solution contains $30.36 \mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and $100 \mathrm{mL}$ of the given solution contains
$$
\frac{30.36 \times 100}{1000}=3.03 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}
$$
Thus, $\%$ strength of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ is $3.03(\approx 3)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A buffer solution contains $100$ ml of $0.01$ (M) $CH_3COOH$ and $200$ ml of $0.02$ (M) $CH_3COONa$. $700$ ml of water is added subsequently to the buffer solution. The pH before and after dilution are [given, $pK_a = 4.74$; $\log 2 = 0.301$]
questions.solution:
Initial millimoles of $CH_3COOH = 100 \times 0.01 = 1$ mmol

Initial millimoles of $CH_3COONa = 200 \times 0.02 = 4$ mmol

Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for the buffer solution:

$pH = pK_a + \log \left( \dfrac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} \right)$

$pH = 4.74 + \log \left( \dfrac{4}{1} \right)$

$pH = 4.74 + 2 \log 2 = 4.74 + 2(0.301) = 4.74 + 0.602 = 5.342 \approx 5.34$

When $700$ ml of water is added, the volume of the solution increases, but the ratio of the number of moles of salt to acid remains unchanged. Therefore, the pH of the buffer solution remains constant upon dilution.

pH after dilution $= 5.34$

Answer: $5.34, 5.34$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Equal volume of two solutions A and B of a strong acid having $\mathrm{pH}=6.0$ and $\mathrm{pH}=4.0$ respectively are mixed together to form a new solution. The pH of the new solution will be in the range
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{pH}=6 \quad \therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-6} \mathrm{M}$

Let volume is 1 L
$1 \mathrm{~L} 10^{-6} \mathrm{M}$ SA contains $10^{-6}$ moles $\mathrm{H}^{+}$
$\mathrm{pH}=4 \quad \therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$
$1 \mathrm{~L} 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$ SA contains $10^{-4}$ moles $\mathrm{H}^{+}$
$\therefore$ Total $\mathrm{H}^{+}=10^{-6}+10^{-4}=1.01 \times 10^{-4}$ moles
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{1.01 \times 10^{-4}}{2}(\mathrm{M})=5.05 \times 10^{-5} \\
& \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left(5.05 \times 10^{-5}\right)=4.29
\end{aligned}$

$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\ell) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \quad \mathrm{K}=10^{-14}$
$\begin{aligned} \Delta G^{\circ} & =-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \text { logK } \\ & =-2.303 \times 1.987 \times 298 \log 10^{-14} \\ & =2.303 \times 1.987 \times 298 \times 14 \\ & =19091 \mathrm{Cal} \\ & \approx 19.1 \mathrm{KCal}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $2.5 \mathrm{ml}$ of $0.4(\mathrm{M})$ weak monoacidic base $\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{b}}=1 \times 10^{-12}\right.$ at $\left.25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ is titrated with $\frac{2}{15}(\mathrm{M}) \mathrm{HCl}$ in water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$at equivalence point is $\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{w}}=1 \times 10^{-14}\right.$, at $\left.25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$,
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}=\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}$ (At equivalence point)
$0.4 \times 2.5=\frac{2}{15} \times \mathrm{V}_{2}$ 
$\mathrm{BOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{B} \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(I)$
$\therefore \mathrm{V}_{2}=\frac{0.4 \times 2.5}{\frac{2}{15}}=\frac{0.4 \times 2.5}{2} \times 15=7.5 \mathrm{ml}$
$\therefore$ Concentration of salt $=\frac{0.4 \times 2.5}{2.5+7.5}$
or, Salt of weak base and strong acid = $0.1$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{Ch}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}} \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14} \times 0.1}{10^{-12}}}=\sqrt{10^{-3}}=3.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$
as $C h^{2}=\frac{K_{w}}{K_{b}} \quad \therefore h=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w}}{K_{b} C}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Solubility products $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}\right)$ of the salts of types $\mathrm{MX}, \mathrm{MX}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{M}_{3} \mathrm{X}$ at temperature $\mathrm{T}$ are $4.0 \times 10^{-8}, 3.2 \times 10^{-14}$ and $2.7 \times$ $10^{-15}$ respectively. Solubilities (in $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$ ) of the salts at temperature $\mathrm{T}$ are in the order
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{MX} & \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=4 \times 10^{-8}=\mathrm{S}^{2} \therefore \mathrm{S}=2 \times 10^{-4} \\
\mathrm{MX}_{2} & \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=3.2 \times 10^{-14}=4 \mathrm{~S}^{3}
\end{array}$
$\therefore \mathrm{S}^{3}=\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-14}}{4}$
$\mathrm{~S}^{3}=8 \times 10^{-15} \quad \mathrm{~S}=2 \times 10^{-5}$
$\mathrm{M}_{3} \mathrm{X} \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=2.7 \times 10^{-15}=27 \mathrm{~S}^{4} \quad \therefore \mathrm{S}=10^{-4}$
$\therefore \mathrm{MX}>\mathrm{M}_{3} \mathrm{X}>\mathrm{MX}_{2}$

questions.q: A saturated solution of $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}$. The solubility of $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$ in $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ at this temperature will be

$\begin{array}{r}
\mathrm{BaSO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-} \\
\mathrm{s} \quad \mathrm{s}+0.1 \\
\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-} \\
0.2 \quad 0.1
\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{+2}\right]\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{-2}\right] \\
\therefore 4 \times 10^{-5}=\mathrm{s} \times(\mathrm{s}+0.1)
\end{array}$
or $4 \times 10^{-5} \approx \mathrm{s} \times 0.1 \quad \therefore \mathrm{s}=4 \times 10^{-4}$ as $\mathrm{s}<< < 0.1 \quad \therefore \mathrm{s}+0.1 \approx 0.1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}, \mathrm{KOH}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}$ of identical concentration are provided. The pair (s) of solutions which form a buffer upon mixing is (are)
questions.solution:
A Weak acid and its salt with a strong base forms a buffer.$\therefore$ (D)

$\mathrm{HNO}_{3}+\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{COO}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$
If $\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]>\left[\mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right]$, then solution contains weak acid $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)$ and its conjugate base $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right) . \quad \therefore(\mathrm{C})$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solution is saturated with $\mathrm{SrCO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Sr} \mathrm{F}_{2}$. The $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]$ is found to be $1.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$. The concentration of $\mathrm{F}$ - in the solution would be
questions.solution:
Hint:
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Sr} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \stackrel{\mathrm{S}}{\rightleftarrows} \mathrm{Sr}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\mathrm{S}_{1}+\mathrm{S}_{2} \quad \mathrm{~S}_{1} \\
\mathrm{SrF}_{2}(\mathrm{~s}) \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Sr}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{~F}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\mathrm{S}_{1}+\mathrm{S}_{2} \quad 2 \mathrm{~S}_{2}
\end{aligned}$
Where $\mathrm{S}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ are solubilities of $\mathrm{SrCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$ and $\mathrm{Sr} \mathrm{F}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})$ respectively.
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{S}_{1}=1.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}(\text { Given }) \\
\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}\left(\mathrm{SrCO}_{3}\right)=\left[\mathrm{Sr}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{-2}\right] \\
7 \times 10^{-10}=\left(\mathrm{S}_{1}+\mathrm{S}_{2}\right) \mathrm{S}_{1}-\ldots-\text { (i) } \\
\mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}\left(\mathrm{SrF}_{2}\right)=\left[\mathrm{Sr}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{F}^{-}\right]^{2} \\
7.9 \times 10^{-10}=\left(\mathrm{S}_{1}+\mathrm{S}_{2}\right)\left(2 \mathrm{~S}_{2}\right)^{2}
\end{array}$
Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i)
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{7.9 \times 10^{-10}}{7 \times 10^{-10}}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~S}_{2}^{2}}{\mathrm{~S}_{1}} \\
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \mathrm{S}_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{7.9}{7} \times \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4}} \\
\quad=1.84 \times 10^{-2} \\
\therefore\left[\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{]}=2 \mathrm{~S}_{2}=2 \times 1.84 \times 10^{-2}=3.68 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}\right.
\end{array}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following mixtures will have the lowest pH at 298 K?
questions.solution:
Key Point For buffer solutions,
$$
\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{a}+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }] /[\text { Base }]}
$$
Moles of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}=5 \mathrm{mL} \times 0.01 \mathrm{m} \mathrm{mol}=0.5 \mathrm{m} \mathrm{mol}$
Moles of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}=10 \mathrm{mL} \times 0.05 \mathrm{m} \mathrm{mol}$
$$
=0.5 \mathrm{m} \mathrm{mol}
$$
Total volume of solution $=5+10=15 \mathrm{mL}$
$$
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{a}+\log \frac{0.5 / 15}{0.5 / 15} \Rightarrow \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{a}
$$
The value of $\mathrm{p} K_{a}$ is the lowest for the mixture of $\begin{array}{llll}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} & \text { and } \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa} & \text { (acidic buffer). }\end{array}$
Therefore, it has lowest pH. Hence, pH value for option (c) is the lowest.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Dissolving NaCN in de-ionised water will result in a solution having
questions.solution:
$\because$ On dissolving NaCN in de-ionised water, following reaction takes place.
$\mathrm{CN}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HCN}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
CN- ions come from the salt of strong base and weak acid.
$\therefore$ The solution becomes basic and $\mathrm{pH}>7$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Of the following compounds, which one is the strongest Bronsted acid in a aqueous solution?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Higher be the oxidation number of central atom in oxo-acids, more strongly it behave as Bronsted-acid. Oxidation number for $\mathrm{Cl}$ in $\mathrm{HClO}_{3} \rightarrow+5$ 
Oxidation number for $\mathrm{Cl}$ in $\mathrm{HClO}_{2} \rightarrow+3$ 
Oxidation number for $\mathrm{Cl}$ in $\mathrm{HClO} \rightarrow+1$ 
Oxidation number for $\mathrm{Cl}$ in $\mathrm{HBrO} \rightarrow+1$ 
Hence, $\mathrm{HClO}_{3}$, is the strongest Bronsted acid in aqueous solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The molar solubility (in mol $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$ ) of a sparingly soluble salt $M X_{4}$ is ${ }^{\prime} S$. The corresponding solubility product is $K_{\mathrm{sp}} . S$ in terms of $'K_{\text {ip }}$' s given by the relation
questions.solution:
$\because$ For a compound $A_{x}B_{y}$
$$
K_{s p}=x^{x} \cdot y^{y} \cdot S^{x+y} . \text { (where, } S=\text { solubility })
$$
For $M x_{4}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
K_{\mathrm{sp}}=1 \times(4)^{4} \cdot \mathrm{S}^{1+4} \\
K_{\mathrm{sp}}=256 \times \mathrm{S}^{5}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore S=\left(\frac{K_{s}}{256}\right)^{1 / 5}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In which of the following mixed aqueous solutions, $\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{a}$ at equilibrium?
(1) $100 \mathrm{mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+100 \mathrm{mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{MCH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}$
(2) $100 \mathrm{mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{MCH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+50 \mathrm{mL}$ of 0.1 $\mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$
(3) $100 \mathrm{mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+100 \mathrm{mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$
(4) $100 \mathrm{mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+100 \mathrm{mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
questions.solution: pH of buffer solutions is given by $$ \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{a}+\mathrm{log} \frac{[\mathrm{salt}]}{[\mathrm{acid}]} $$ (1) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}$ $\quad \quad$ $100 \times 0.1$ $\quad \quad$$100 \times 0.1$ No. of milimoles $=10 \quad=10$ $\because[$ salt $]=\mid a c i d]=10, p H=p K_{a}+\log \frac{10}{10}$ $\therefore \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK_{a}}$ $\because \quad$ (salt) $=[\mathrm{acid}]=5, \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{5}{5}$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ $[$ salt $]=10$ and $[a c i d]=0$ $p H=p K_{a}+\log \frac{10}{0}$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH} \neq \mathrm{pK}_{a}$ (4) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONH}_{4}$ initial $10 \quad \quad \quad \quad 10 \rightarrow$ No buffer action Final Due to weak $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONH}_{4}$ salt $\therefore \quad p H \neq p K_{a}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The ratio of volumes of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH } \text{ 0.1}$ (N) to CH COONa 0.1 (N) required to prepare a buffer solution of $\mathrm{pH} 5.74$ is  (Given, $p K_{a}$ of $C H_{3} C O O H$ is $\left.4.74\right)$
questions.solution:
Given, that $\mathrm{pH}=5.74, \mathrm{pK}_{a}=4.74$
Suppose that volume of acid solution $=x \mathrm{L}$ Volume of salt solution $=y \mathrm{L}$
From Henderson equation,
$$
\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{[\mathrm{Salt}]}{[\mathrm{Acid}]}
$$
or, $\mathrm{pH}-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}=\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}$
or, $5.74-4.74=1=\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}$
or $\frac{\left.\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}=10$
or $\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]}=\frac{1}{10}=\frac{\frac{x+y}{0.1 \mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}$
Thus, $\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{10}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Within the list shown below, the correct pair of structures of alanine in $\mathrm{pH}$ ranges $2-4$ and $9-11$ is
I. $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{N}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{H}$
II. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$
III. $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{N}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$
$\mathrm{IV} . \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{H}$
questions.solution:
Isoelectric point for neutral amino acids ranges from $\mathrm{pH} 5.5$ to 6.3 . Acidic amino acids have isoelectric point around 3 while basic amino acids have ranges from pH 7.6 to 10.8 . At isoelectric point, amino acids exist as Zwitter ions. At low pH, it exists as cation and at high $\mathrm{pH}$, it exists as anion. Hence, alanine (pH range $2-4$ ) will exist as cation while alanine (pH range $9-11$ ) will exist as anion.
Acidic medium:
$$
\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{N}^{+} -\mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{H}
$$
Basic medium:
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The pH of $10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$ KOH solution will be
questions.solution:
$10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KOH}=10^{-4} \mathrm{M}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
We know that
$$
\left.\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \mathrm{IOH}^{-1}\right]=1 \times 10^{-14}
$$
$$
\begin{array}{c}
{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \times 10^{-4}=1 \times 10^{-14}} \\
{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{1 \times 10^{-4}}=1 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{M}} \\
\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left(1 \times 10^{-10}\right) \\
=10
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{pH}$ of a $10^{-8} \mathrm{M}$ aqueous $\mathrm{KOH}$
solution will be
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-} \quad\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-8} \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-7} \mathrm{M}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]_{\text {total }} &=\left(10^{-8}+10^{-7}\right) \mathrm{M} \\
&=10^{-7}(1.1) \\
&=1.1 \times 10^{-7} \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pOH} &=\log 1.1 \times 10^{-7}=6.98 \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH} &=14-6.98 \\
&=7.02
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.q: $1 \times 10^{-3}$ mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$ is added to a buffer solution made up of $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ acetic acid and 0.01 M sodium acetate. The final pH of the buffer will be (given, $\mathrm{p} K_{a}$ of acetic acid is 4.75 at $\left.25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{pH}=& \mathrm{pK}_{a}+\log \frac{(\text { salt })}{(\text { acid })}=4.75+\log \frac{0.009}{0.011} \\
&=4.66
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A $100$ mL, $0.1=\mathrm{M}$ solution of ammonium acetate is diluted by adding $100 \mathrm{mL}$ of water. The pH of the resulting solution will be $\left(p K_{a}\right.$ of acetic acid is nearly equal to $\mathrm{pK}_{b}$ of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ )
questions.solution:
Ammonium acetate is a salt of weak acid and weak base. When solutions of such salts diluted resulting pH of solution is calculated from following relationship
$$
\mathrm{pH}=7+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{p} \mathrm{K}_{a}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{b}}
$$
$\because p K_{a}=p K_{b}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}$ of diluted solution $=7$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A weak acid of dissociation constant $10^{-5}$ is being titrated with aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution. The $\mathrm{pH}$ at the point of one-third neutralisation of the acid will be
questions.solution:
Hints: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=10^{-5} \Rightarrow \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=-\log 10^{-5}=5$
$$
\begin{array}{llcc} 
& \mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaA}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
\text { Initial } & 1 \mathrm{~mole} 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text { Final } & (1-1 / 3) \mathrm{mole} & 1 / 3 \mathrm{~mole} & 1 / 3 \mathrm{~mole} \\
& =2 / 3 \mathrm{~mole} & 1 / 3 \mathrm{~mole}
\end{array}
$$
(Assumed weak acid to be monoprotic, since only one dissociation constant value is provided)
Final solution acts as an acidic buffer.
$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]} \Rightarrow \mathrm{pH}=5+\log \frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{2}{3}}=5+\log \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{pH}=5-\log 2
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Borazole is prepared by heating the product isolated by reacting
questions.solution:
Diborane reacts with ammonia to form an addition product, diammoniate of diborane.

$3B_2H_6 + 6NH_3 \rightarrow 3[BH_2(NH_3)_2]^+[BH_4]^-$

Upon heating this product, borazole (borazine) is formed.

$3[BH_2(NH_3)_2]^+[BH_4]^- \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2B_3N_3H_6 + 12H_2$

Thus, borazole is prepared by heating the product isolated by reacting diborane with ammonia.

Answer: diborane with ammonia

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The main reason that $\mathrm{SiCl}_{4}$ is easily hydrolysed as compared to $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$ is that
options: Si can extend its coordination number beyond four
questions.solution: It is less reactive than other oxides of chlorine and does not react with $P, S,$ coal or paper at room temperature. The main reason that $\mathrm{SiCl}_{4}$ is easily hydrolysed as compared to $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$, is that Si can extend its coordination number beyond four because it has vacant $d$ -orbital. On the other hand, carbon has no $d$ -orbital thus, it cannot extend its coordination number beyond four, so its halides are not attacked (hydrolysed) by water. The vacant $d$ -orbitals of Si can coordinate with water molecules and hence their halides are hydrolysed by water. The hydrolysis of $\mathrm{SiCl}_{4}$ occurs due to coordination of $-\mathrm{OH}$ with empty $3 d$ -orbitals of Si-atom of $\mathrm{SiCl}_{4}$ molecule.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the set of oxides are arranged in the order of proper basic, amphoteric, acidic?
questions.solution:
The correct set of oxides arranged in the proper order of basic, amphoteric, acidic are $\mathrm{BaO}, \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{SO}_{2}$
Oxides of non-metallic elements are acidic such $\mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{NO}_{2}, \mathrm{SO}_{2}$ etc. Oxides of less electropositive elements (such as BeO, $A l_{2} O_{3}, B i_{2} O_{3}$, ZnO etc.) are amphoteric i.e. these behaves as acids toward strong bases and as bases towards strong acids. Oxides of elcctropositive elements $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{CaO}, \mathrm{T}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \quad \mathrm{Ba} 0\right.$
etc.) are basic and contain discrete $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the structure of pyrosulphuric acid?
questions.solution:
The chemical formula of pyrosulphuric acid (also known as oleum) is $H_2S_2O_7$.

In its structure, two sulphur atoms are linked together through an oxygen atom, forming a $S-O-S$ bridge. Each sulphur atom is further bonded to two oxygen atoms via double bonds and one hydroxyl ($-OH$) group via a single bond.

Let us analyze the given structures:
Option (A) represents peroxymonosulphuric acid ($H_2SO_5$), also known as Caro's acid, which contains a peroxy ($-O-O-$) linkage.
Option (B) represents peroxydisulphuric acid ($H_2S_2O_8$), also known as Marshall's acid, which contains a peroxy ($-O-O-$) linkage between two sulphur atoms.
Option (C) represents the hydrogen sulphate ion ($HSO_4^-$).
Option (D) represents pyrosulphuric acid ($H_2S_2O_7$), showing the correct $S-O-S$ linkage.

Answer:

questions.solution:

options: hydrogen chloride $>$ hydrogen bromide > hydrogen iodide
hydrogen iodide $>$ hydrogen bromide > hydrogen chloride
hydrogen bromide $>$ hydrogen chloride > hydrogen iodide
hydrogen iodide $>$ hydrogen chloride > hydrogen bromide
questions.solution:
In halogen acids, as the size of halogen atom increases, the bond between halogen and hydrogen atom weakens. Therefore, the case with which the bond can be broken increases.
The correct order of the addition reaction rates of halogen acids with ethylene is hydrogen iodide (HI) $>$ Hydrogen bromide (HBr)
Hydrogen chloride (HCl) So, the option (b) is correct answer.

questions.solution:
Only KNO $_{3}$ on heating do not give $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$, but all other give
(a) $2 \mathrm{KNO}_{3} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{KNO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(b) $2 \mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \stackrel{\mathrm{A}}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{P} \mathrm{bO}+4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(c) $2 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \stackrel{\mathrm{A}}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{CuO}+4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(d) $2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \frac{\Delta}{\text { Aed het }} 2 \mathrm{Ag}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$
Heavy metal nitrates liberate nitrogen dioxide on heating.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ bond length in $\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ is
questions.solution:
Correct order of $O-O$ bond length is
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}>\mathrm{O}_{3}>\mathrm{O}_{2}
$$
(i) Between $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$
$\because$ Bond order of $\mathrm{O}_{2}>\mathrm{O}_{3}$
Thus, bond length of $\mathrm{O}_{3}>\mathrm{O}_{2}$
$$
\left ( \because \text { Bond order } \propto \frac{1}{\text { Bond length }}\right)
$$
(ii) Between $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
$\because \mathrm{O}_{3}$ has $\pi$ -bonds in resonance so, has shorter bond length than $O-O$ in $H_{2} O_{2}$
$\therefore$ Hence, (b) is the answer.

It happens because the noble gases have higher ionisation energies
It happens because the compounds are formed with electronegative ligands
Octet of electrons provide the stable arrangements
questions.solution:
Ionisation potential values of noble gases decrease down the group with increase in atomic size. Xenon forms binary fluorides by the direct reaction of elements.
The reason is that only the heavier noble gases form such compounds. Octet of electrons provide the stable arrangements but this statement is not very much valid in cases of higher element which can be ionised easily due to large distance between nucleus and valence electrons.

Ozone is used as germicide and disinfectant for the purification of air.
questions.solution: Hints : Due to resonance the bond order in ozone is 1.5 , hence $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ bond length in $\mathrm{O}_3>\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ bond length in $\mathrm{O}_2$

questions.q: Which one of the following cations gives a chocolate brown precipitate upon addition of aqueous solution of $K_4[Fe(CN)_6]$?
questions.solution:
Reaction of $Cu^{2+}$ with potassium ferrocyanide, $K_4[Fe(CN)_6]$, yields a chocolate brown precipitate of cupric ferrocyanide.

$2Cu^{2+} + K_4[Fe(CN)_6] \rightarrow Cu_2[Fe(CN)_6] \downarrow + 4K^+$

The other ions give different coloured precipitates with $K_4[Fe(CN)_6]$:
$Fe^{3+}$ gives a Prussian blue precipitate of $Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3$.
$Zn^{2+}$ gives a white precipitate of $Zn_2[Fe(CN)_6]$.
$Ca^{2+}$ gives a white precipitate of $CaK_2[Fe(CN)_6]$.

Answer: $Cu^{2+}$

questions.q: Which of the following is least thermally stable?
questions.solution:
BeCO, is least thermally stable. The thermal stability of carbonates increases down the group
i.e from Be to Ba. $\mathrm{BeCO}_{3}    < \mathrm{MgCO}_{3} < \mathrm{CaCO}_{3} < \mathrm{SrCO}_{3} < \mathrm{BaCO}_{3}$
$(523 \mathrm{K}) \quad(813 \mathrm{K})$
$(1173 \mathrm{K}) \quad(1562 \mathrm{K}) \quad(1633 \mathrm{K})$
BeCO, is unstable to the extent that it is stable only in atmosphere of $\mathrm{CO}_{y}$. These carbonates show reversible decomposition in closed container.
$M \mathrm{CO}, \quad \rightarrow \mathrm{MO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
More stable the oxide is formed, lesser will be stability of carbonates.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following arrangements is correct in respect of solubility in water?
questions.solution:
The correct order of solubility of sulphates in water is
$\mathrm{BeSO}_{4}>\mathrm{MgSO}_{4}>\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}>\mathrm{SrSO}_{4}>\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$
Solublity of 2nd group sulphates decreases as we down the group due to less release of hydration energy. $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}    < \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} < \mathrm{Ca}^{2+} < \mathrm{Sr}^{2+} < \mathrm{Ba}^{24}$ (lonic Size)
As hydration energy decreases more rapidly than latice energy, the solubility decreases down the group. Hydration Energy $=\frac{\text { Charge }}{\text { Size }}$
While lathice energy almost remains constant. Hence, solubility decreases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A compound (X) when treated with $CuSO_4$ solution yields a brown precipitate. On adding hypo solution the precipitate turns white. The compound (X) is
questions.solution:
When potassium iodide ($KI$) is treated with copper sulphate ($CuSO_4$) solution, it forms unstable cupric iodide ($CuI_2$), which immediately decomposes to give a white precipitate of cuprous iodide ($Cu_2I_2$) and liberates iodine ($I_2$).

$2CuSO_4 + 4KI \rightarrow Cu_2I_2 + I_2 + 2K_2SO_4$

The liberated iodine imparts a brown colour to the mixture, making the precipitate appear brown.

When hypo solution ($Na_2S_2O_3$) is added to this mixture, it reacts with the liberated iodine to form soluble sodium tetrathionate ($Na_2S_4O_6$) and sodium iodide ($NaI$).

$I_2 + 2Na_2S_2O_3 \rightarrow Na_2S_4O_6 + 2NaI$

As the brown iodine is consumed in the reaction, the white precipitate of cuprous iodide ($Cu_2I_2$) becomes visible. Therefore, the compound (X) is $KI$.

Answer: $KI$

As per the following equation, 0.217 g of HgO (molecular mass $=217 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) reacts with excess iodide. On titration of the resulting solution, how many mL of 0.01 M HCl is required to reach the equivalence point?
$\mathrm{HgO}+4 \mathrm{I}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HgI}_4^{2-}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{HgO}+4 \mathrm{I}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{HgI}_4^{-2}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
(limiting (excess
reagent) reagent)
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{HC} \ell)+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\ell) \\
& \therefore \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{HgO}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{HC} \ell} \\
& \frac{0.217}{217}=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{M} \times \frac{\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{~mL})}{1000}=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.01 \times \frac{\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{~mL})}{1000} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{~V}(\mathrm{~mL})=200
\end{aligned}$

Hence equivalent weight of $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{1}=\mathrm{M}$

questions.solution:

For Neutralisation Number of equivalent of base (NaOH) Number of equivalent of acid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: You are supplied with $500 \mathrm{mL}$ each of $2 \mathrm{N} \mathrm{HCl}$ and $5 \mathrm{N} \mathrm{HCl}$. What is the maximum volume of 3M HCl that you can prepare using only these two solutions?
questions.solution:
As per given relation,
$\begin{array}{l}
N_{1} V_{1}+N_{2} V_{2}=N\left(V_{1}+V_{2}\right) \\
N_{1}=2 N_{2}=5 \\
V_{1}=500 V_{2}=\operatorname{say}-x
\end{array}$
Thus, $2 \times 500+5 x=3(x+500)$ or $2 x=500^{\circ}$
ie. $x=250$
Hence, maximum volume of
$3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}=500+250=750 \mathrm{mL}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ is passed through hot aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$, the products formed have Cl in different oxidation states. These are indicated as
questions.solution:
If $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ is passed through hot aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$, it results in the formation of sodium chloride and sodium chlorate as
$$
6 \mathrm{NaOH}+3 \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow 5 \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{NaClO}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
In $\mathrm{NaCl}$, let oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cl}$ be $\mathrm{x}$
$$
+1+x=0 \text { or } \quad x=-1
$$
In $\operatorname{NaClO}_{3}$, let oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cl}$ be $y$
$+1+y+(-2) 3=0$ 
$y-5=0$
$\therefore y=+5$
Thus, the obtained compounds have Cl in -1 and +5 oxidation states.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A compound contains two types of atoms A and B. Its crystal structure is a cubic lattice with 'A' atoms at the corner of the unit cells and 'B' atoms at the body centres. The simplest formula of the compound will be
questions.solution:
Number of atoms of A per unit cell = $8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = 1$

Number of atoms of B per unit cell = $1 \times 1 = 1$

The ratio of A to B is $1:1$.

The simplest formula of the compound is $AB$.

Answer: $AB$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If three elements $A, B, C$ crystalise in a cubic solid lattice with $B$ atoms at the cubic centres, $C$ atoms at the centre of edges and $A$ atoms at the corners, then formula of the compound is
questions.solution:
Number of A atoms $=\frac{1}{8} \times 8=1$
Number of $C$ atoms $=\frac{1}{4} \times 12=3$
Number of B atom = 1
Hence $\mathrm{ABC}_3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Metallic conductors and semiconductors are heated separately. What are the changes with respect to conductivity?
questions.solution:
Hint : For Metallic Conductors, $R \propto T$ ( $R$ is the resistance)
Hence conductivity decreases with increase of Temperature
For semi-conductors, conductivity increases with rise of temperature due increase in number of electron-hole pairs.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the edge length of the unit cell of a body centred cubic crystal of an element whose atomic radius is \(75 \mathrm{pm}\) ?
questions.solution:
Hint : In BCC, \(4 r=\sqrt{3} a\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{a}=\frac{4 \mathrm{r}}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{4 \times 75}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{300}{\sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{3} \times 100 \mathrm{pm}=173.2 \mathrm{pm}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of atoms in body centred and face centred cubic unit cell respectively are
questions.solution: $Z_{\mathrm{BCC}}=\frac{1}{8} \times 8+1=2 \quad Z_{\mathrm{FCC}}=\frac{1}{8} \times 8+\frac{1}{2} \times 6=1+3=4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An element crystallizes in a body centred cubic lattice. The edge length of the unit cell is $200 \mathrm{pm}$ and the density of the element is $5.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. Calculate the number of atoms in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of this element.
questions.solution:
$\rho_{\text {unit cell }}(\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cc})=\frac{\mathrm{Z} \times \mathrm{M} \cdot \mathrm{Mass}(\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol})}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}\left(\mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right) \times(\mathrm{a} \mathrm{pm})^{3} \times 10^{-30}} \quad \mathrm{Z}_{\text {B.c.c }}=2$
or $5=\frac{2 \times \text { M.Mass }}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times(200)^{3} \times 10^{-30}}$
or M.Mass $=\frac{5 \times 8 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 10^{-24}}{2}=2 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 10^{-23}$
Now, no. of moles in $100 \mathrm{~g}=\frac{100}{2 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 10^{-23}}$
$\therefore$ no. of atoms $=\frac{100}{2 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 10^{-23}} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=50 \times 10^{23}=5.0 \times 10^{24}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the face-centred cubic lattice structure of gold the closest distance between gold atoms is ('a' being the edge length of the cubic unit cell)
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Closest distance between two gold atoms in fcc lattice of gold is $\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} .$ This is the distance between the corner atom and the closest face centre atom.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A compound formed by elements $X$ and $Y$ crystallises in the cubic structure, where $X$ atoms are at the corners of a cube and $Y$ atoms are at the centre of the body. The formula of the compounds is
questions.solution: Number of $X$ atoms at the corners $=8$ Number of $X$ atoms per unit cell $=8 \times \frac{1}{8}=1$ atom Number of $Y$ atoms at the centre of the body $=1$ atom Hence, the formula of the compound is $X Y$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The ground state magnetic property of $\mathrm{B}_{2}$ and
$\mathrm{C}_{2}$ molecules will be
questions.solution:
According to molecular orbital theory, outer configuration for
$$
\left[\mathrm{C}_{2}\right]=\mathrm{C}_{12} \rightarrow \pi p_{x}^{2}=\pi p_{y}^{2}
$$
Thus, is diamagnetic in nature.
$$
\left[\mathrm{B}_{2}\right]=\mathrm{B}_{10}->\pi p_{x}^{1}=\pi p_{y}^{1}
$$
Thus, is paramagnetic in nature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a close-packed body-centred cubic lattice of potassium, the correct relation between the atomic radius $(r)$ of potassium and the edge-length  of the cube is
questions.solution:
$\because$ For be packing, $\sqrt{3} a=4 r \therefore r=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot a$
where $a=$ edge length $r=$ radius of lattice sphere

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ionic solids with Schottky defect may contain in their structure
options: equal number of cation and anion vacancies
questions.solution: In Schotthy defect, equal number of cations and anions are missing creating equal number of cation and anion vacancies.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Suppose the mass of a single Ag-atom is $m$. Ag metal crystallises in fcc lattice with unit cell of length $a$. The density of Ag metal in terms of $a$ and $m$ is
questions.solution:
Given that,
Mass of single Ag-atom $=m$
Unit cell length $=a$
Total atoms present per unit cell of fcc lattice $=4$ 
Therefore, mass of unit cell becomes $4 \mathrm{m}$. 
Volume of unit cell $=a^{3}$
Density $(\rho)=\frac{\text { Mass of unit cell }}{\text { Volume of unit cell }}=\frac{4 \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{a}^{3}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Glucose is added to $1$ litre of water to such an extent that $\Delta T_f/K_f$ equals to $\dfrac{1}{1000}$. The weight of glucose added is
questions.solution:
Using the formula for depression in freezing point:

$\Delta T_f = K_f \times m$

where $m$ is the molality of the solution.

Given $\dfrac{\Delta T_f}{K_f} = \dfrac{1}{1000}$

Therefore, $m = \dfrac{1}{1000}$ mol/kg.

Mass of $1$ litre of water is $1$ kg.

Molality $m = \dfrac{\text{Moles of solute}}{\text{Mass of solvent in kg}}$

Moles of glucose $= \dfrac{1}{1000} \times 1 = 10^{-3}$ mol.

Molar mass of glucose $(C_6H_{12}O_6)$ is $180$ g/mol.

Weight of glucose added $= 10^{-3} \times 180 = 0.18$ gm.

Answer: $0.18$ gm

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The van't Hoff Factor (i) for a dilute aqueous solution of $Na_2SO_4$ is
questions.solution:
The dissociation of $Na_2SO_4$ in an aqueous solution is given by:

$Na_2SO_4 \rightleftharpoons 2Na^+ + SO_4^{2-}$

Let the initial number of moles of $Na_2SO_4$ be $1$ and the degree of dissociation be $\alpha$.

Moles at equilibrium:
$Na_2SO_4 = 1 - \alpha$
$Na^+ = 2\alpha$
$SO_4^{2-} = \alpha$

Total number of moles at equilibrium $= 1 - \alpha + 2\alpha + \alpha = 1 + 2\alpha$

The van't Hoff factor $i$ is the ratio of the total number of moles of particles at equilibrium to the initial number of moles.

$i = \dfrac{1 + 2\alpha}{1} = 1 + 2\alpha$

Answer: $1 + 2\alpha$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: P and Q combines to form two compounds $\mathrm{PQ}_2$ and $\mathrm{PQ}_3$. If $1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{P} Q_2$ is dissolved in 51 g benzene the depression of freezing point becomes $0.8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. On the other hand if $1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{PQ}_3$ is dissolved in 51 g of benzene, the depression of freezing point becomes $0.625^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The atomic mass of $P$ and $Q$ are $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}\right.$ of benzene $\left.=5.1 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& 0.8=\frac{5.1 \times 1000 \times 1}{51 \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{PQ}_2}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{PQ}_2}=125 \\
& 0.625=\frac{5.1 \times 1000 \times 1}{51 \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{PQ}_3}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{PQ}_3}=160
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ At wt of $P=55$ and $Q=35$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the incorrect statement among the following:
options: Viscosity of liquid always decreases with increase in temperature
Surface tension of liquid always decreases with increase in temperature
Viscosity of liquid always increases in presence of impurity
Surface tension of liquid always increases in presence of impurity
questions.solution: Hint : Both options C and D are incorrect, as Viscosity and Surface Tension may increase or decrease upon addition of impurities.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Equal volumes of aqueous solution of $0.1(\mathrm{M}) \mathrm{HCl}$ and $0.2(\mathrm{M}) \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ are mixed. The concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions in the resulting solution is
questions.solution:
Hint : Concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$in molarity $=\frac{\text { Total no. of moleof } \mathrm{H}^{+}}{\text {Total volume of solution }(\mathrm{in} \mathrm{L})}=\frac{\sum \mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{inL}) \times \text { Basicity }}{\sum \mathrm{V}}$
$=\frac{V \times 0.1 \times 1+V \times 0.2 \times 2}{V+V}=\frac{0.5}{2}=0.25 \mathrm{M}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of boiling point of the given aqueous solution is
questions.solution:
Hint : Elevation in boiling point $\propto$ No. of solute particles
$\mathrm{KNO}_3$ (strong electrolyte); $\mathrm{KNO}_{3(\text { (aq) }} \rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{NO}_{3 \text { (aq) }}^{-}$
NaCl (Strong electrolyte); $\mathrm{NaCl}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{\text {(aq) }}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}_{\text {(aq) }}$
$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}$ (Weak electrolyte); $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}_{(\text {aq) }}+\mathrm{H}_{\text {(aq) }}^{+}$
Sucrose (Non-electrolyte)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solution containing \(4 \mathrm{~g}\) of polymer in 4.0 litre solution at \(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) shows an osmotic pressure of \(3.0 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~atm}\). The molar mass of the polymer in \(\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}\) is
questions.solution:
Hint : \(\pi=i C(M) R T\)
\(3.0 \times 10^{-4}=1 \times C(M) \times 0.0821 \times 300\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{M})=1.22 \times 10^{-5} \text {, Molarity }=\frac{\text { no. of moles }}{\text { vol. of solution }(\mathrm{L})}\)
\(1.22 \times 10^{-5}=\frac{4 / \mathrm{M}}{4}\). Hence \(M=81967 \approx 82000 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Pick the correct statement.
options: Relative lowering of vapour pressure is independent of $\mathrm{T}$.
Osmotic pressure always depends on the nature of solute.
Elevation of boiling point is independent of nature of the solvent.
Lowering of freezing point is proportional to the molar concentration of solute.
questions.solution: Relative lowering of vapour pressure is $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}^{\circ}}$, which is equal to mole fraction of solute which is independent of temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $20 \mathrm{~g}$ of naphthoic acid $\left(\mathrm{C}_{11} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right)$ is dissolved in $50 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene, a freezing point depression of $2 \mathrm{~K}$ is observed. The vant Hoff factor (i) is $\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.72^{\circ} \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right]$
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m} \\
2=\mathrm{i} \times 1.72 \times \frac{20 \times 1000}{172 \times 50} \\
\therefore \mathrm{i}=\frac{2}{0.01 \times 2 \times 200}=\frac{1}{2}=0.5
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{K}_{f}$ (water) $=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The temperature at which ice begins to separate from a mixture of 10 mass $\%$ ethylene glycol is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{m}$
$=1.86\left(\frac{10}{62} \times \frac{1000}{90}\right)$
$=3.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore$ Temparature at which ice begins to separate $=3.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Equal weights of ethane and hydrogen are mixed in an empty container at 25 C. The fraction of total pressure exerted by hydrogen is
questions.solution:
Given, equal weights of ethane and hydrogen are mixed in an empty container at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Initial gram weight $=\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} & \mathrm{H}_{2} \\ \mathrm{Wg} & \mathrm{Wg}\end{array}$
Number of moles $=\frac{w}{30} \quad \frac{w}{2}$ According to Henry's law,
$$
\begin{array}{l}\frac{p_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{p_{\text {total }}}=\chi_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{n_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}+n_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}}=\frac{\frac{w}{2}}{\frac{w}{2}+\frac{w}{30}} \\ \frac{p_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{p_{\text {total }}}=\frac{\frac{w}{2}}{\frac{15 w+w}{30}}=\frac{w}{2} \times \frac{30}{16 w}=\frac{15}{16}\end{array}
$$
So, the fraction of total pressure exerted by
hydrogen is 15: 16

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assuming the compounds to be completely dissociated in aqueous solution, identify the pair of the solutions that can be expected to be isotonic at the same temperature.
questions.solution:
$\because$ For Isotonic solution
$$
\pi_{1}=\pi_{2} \text { i.e. } i_{1} C_{1} R T=i f_{2} R T
$$
For option $(c)$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
i_{1}=2, i_{2}=3 \\
C_{1}=0.03 \\
C_{2}=0.02
\end{array}
$$
Thus, $\quad i_{1} \times C_{1}=i_{2} \times C_{2}$
Hence, are isotonic, i.e.
$$
0.06=0.06
$$
In all other options, $i_{1} \times C_{1} \neq i_{2} \times C_{2}$ Thus, are not isotonic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $p^{\circ}$ and $p$ are the vapour pressure of the pure solvent and solution and $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are the moles of solute and solvent respectively in the solution then the correct relation between $p$ and $p^{\circ}$ is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At a certain temperature, the value of the slope of the plot of osmotic pressure $(\pi)$ against concentration $\left(C\right.$ in $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}$ ) of a certain polymer solution is $291 \mathrm{R.}$ The temperature at which osmotic pressure is measured, is $(R$ is gas constant)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: To observe an elevation of boiling point of $0.05^{\circ} \mathrm{C},$ the amount of a solute (mol. wt. $=100$ ) to be added to $100 \mathrm{g}$ of water $\left(K_{b}=0.5\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Elevation of boiling point,
$$
\Delta T_{b}=\frac{w \times K_{b} \times 1000}{M \times W}
$$
(Here, w and $W=$ weights of solute and solvent respectively.
$M=$ molecular weight of solute and $K_{b}=$ constant
On substituting values, we get
of
$$
\begin{aligned}
0.05 &=\frac{W \times 0.5 \times 1000}{100 \times 100} \\
w &=\frac{0.05 \times 100 \times 100}{0.5 \times 1000}=1 \mathrm{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The measured freezing point depression for a $0.1 \mathrm{m}$ aqueous $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ solution is $0.19^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The acid dissociation constant $K_{a}$ at this concentration will be (Given, $K_{f}$ the molal cryoscopic constant $=1.86 \mathrm{K}$ $\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ )

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $58.4 \mathrm{g}$ of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $180 \mathrm{g}$ of glucose were separately dissolved in $1000 \mathrm{mL}$ of water. Identify the correct statement regarding the elevation of boiling point (b.p) of the resulting solutions.
options: NaCl solution will show higher elevation of boiling point
Glucose solution will show higher elevation of boiling point
Both the solutions will show equal elevation of boiling point
The boiling point elevation will be shown by neither of the solutions
questions.solution:
Elevation in boiling point, $\Delta T_{b}=i \times K_{b} \times m$ 
Molality of NaCl solution $=\frac{n}{W} \times 1000$
$=\frac{58.5}{W_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}} \times 1000=\frac{1000}{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{M}_{2}} \mathrm{O}}$
Molality of $f \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{1} \not \mathrm{O}_{6}$ solution = $\frac{\frac{180}{180} \times 1000}{w_{\mathrm{H}_{2}} \mathrm{O}}$
$\frac{1000}{W_{M_{2}} O}$. Both the solutions have same molarity but the values for $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and glucose are ${2}$ and 1respectively. $\left.\therefore \quad \Delta T_{b(\mathrm{NaG})}=2 \times \Delta T_{b C C_{6} H_{1} 2} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following will show a negative deviation from Raoult's law?
questions.solution: Acetone and chloroform will show a negative deviation due to their association after mixing.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many oxygen atoms are present in 0.36 g of a drop of water at STP?
questions.solution:
0.36 g water drop has $\frac{0.36}{18}$ moles
$\frac{0.36}{18}$ moles of water has $\frac{0.36}{18} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of water 
 1 molecule of water has 1 oxygen atom.
$\therefore$ Number of O atoms $=\frac{0.36}{18} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}=1.205 \times 10^{22}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following in order of increasing mass
I. 1 mole of \(\mathrm{N}_2\)
II. \(0.5 \mathrm{~mole}^2\) of \(\mathrm{O}_3\)
III. \(3.011 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{molecules}\) of \(\mathrm{O}_2\)
IV. 0.5 gram atom of \(\mathrm{O}_2\)
questions.solution:
Hint : 1 mole \(\mathrm{N}_2=28 \mathrm{~g}\)
\(0.5 \mathrm{~mole} \mathrm{O}_3=24 \mathrm{~g}\)
\(3.011 \times 10^{23}\) molecules of \(\mathrm{O}_2=1 / 2\) mole \(\mathrm{O}_2=1\) mole \(\mathrm{O}=16 \mathrm{~g}\)
\(0.5 \mathrm{~g}\) atom \(\mathrm{O}_2=1 / 2\) mole of atoms of \(\mathrm{O}=8 \mathrm{~g}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \(63 \mathrm{~g}\) of a compound (Mol. Wt. \(=126)\) was dissolved in \(500 \mathrm{~g}\) distilled water. The density of the resultant solution as \(1.126 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{ml}\). The molarity of the solution is
questions.solution:
Hint : Mass of compound (solute) \(=63 \mathrm{~g}\)
Mole of compound \(=\frac{63}{126}=\frac{1}{2}\) mole
\(\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of solution}& =\text { Mass of solute }+ \text { Mass of solvent } \\
& =63+500 \\
& =563 \mathrm{~g}
\end{aligned}\)
Volume of solution \(=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Density }}=\frac{563}{1.126} \mathrm{ml}\)
Molarity \(=\frac{\text { mole of compound }}{\text { volume of solution }(\text { inL })}\)
\(\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1 / 2 \times 1000}{563 / 1.126} \\ & =\frac{1.126 \times 1000}{2 \times 563}=1\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Avogadro's law is valid for
questions.solution: Fact

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A metal (M) forms two oxides. The ratio $\mathrm{M}: \mathrm{O}$ (by weight) in the two oxides are $25: 4$ and $25: 6$. The minimum value of atomic mass of $M$ is
questions.solution:
Let two oxide be
$\mathrm{M}_2 \mathrm{O}_x$ and $\mathrm{M}_2 \mathrm{Oy}$
$\underline{\text{As per question}}$
$\frac{2 a}{16 x}=\frac{25}{4}$ and $\frac{2 a}{16 y}=\frac{25}{6}$
$x=\frac{a}{50}, y=\frac{3 a}{100}$ where $a=$ atomic mass of Metal
As $x$ and $y$ to be an integer,
If we take $a=50$, then $x=1, y=1.5$ (not possible)
If we take $a=100$ then $x=2, y=3$ (possible)
$\therefore$ Minimum Atomic Mass $=100 \mathrm{u}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $1 \mathrm{~mL}$ of water has 25 drops. Let $\mathrm{N}_0$ be the Avogadro number. What is the number of molecules present in 1 drop of water ? (Density of water $=1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$ )
questions.solution:
Volume of one drop $=\left(\frac{1}{25}\right) \mathrm{mL}$
$\therefore$ Mass of $1 \mathrm{drop}=\mathrm{V} \times \mathrm{d}$
$=\left(\frac{1}{25} \mathrm{~mL}\right)(1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL})$
$=\frac{1}{25} \mathrm{~g}$
Number of moles of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=\frac{\text { Massof water inonedrop }}{\text { Molarmassof water }}=\frac{\frac{1}{25}}{18}=\frac{1}{25 \times 18}$
$\therefore$ Number of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ Molecule $=\frac{1}{25 \times 18} \mathrm{~N}_0=\frac{1}{50 \times 9} \mathrm{~N}_0=\frac{0.02}{9} \mathrm{~N}_0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6(\mathrm{liq})+\frac{15}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{liq})$
Benzene burns in oxygen according to the above equation. What is the volume of oxygen (at STP) needed for complete combustion of 39 gram of liquid benzene?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6}=\frac{39}{78}=\frac{1}{2}$ mole
From Balanced equation,
$\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{G}}}=\frac{2}{15} \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \\
&\frac{1}{2}=\frac{2}{15} \times \frac{\mathrm{xL}}{22.4 \mathrm{~L}} \\
&\therefore \mathrm{x}=84 \mathrm{~L}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The mole fraction of a solute in a binary solution is $0.1 .$ At $298 \mathrm{~K}$, molarity of this solution is same as as its molality. Density of this solution at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is $2.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. The ratio of molecular weights of the solute and the solvent $\left(\mathrm{M}_{\text {solute }} / \mathrm{M}_{\text {solvent }}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{X}_{2}=0.1 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{2}+\mathrm{n}_{1}}=0.1$ $\ldots(1)$
$\mathrm{M}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{2} \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \times \mathrm{W}} \times \mathrm{d}$ $\ldots(2)$
$\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{2} \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \times \mathrm{W}_{1}(\mathrm{~g})}$ $\ldots(3)$
$\mathrm{d}=2 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$ $\ldots(4)$
Given M = m (numerically)
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{W}_{2} \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \times\left(\mathrm{W}_{2}+\mathrm{W}_{1}\right)} \times \mathrm{d}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{2} \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \times \mathrm{W}_{1}(\mathrm{~g})} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{W}_{2}+\mathrm{W}_{1}=\mathrm{W}_{1} \times \mathrm{d}=2 \mathrm{~W}_{1} \\ 
\Rightarrow \mathrm{W}_{2}=\mathrm{W}_{1} 
\end{array}$ 
Now, $\frac{n_{2}}{n_{2}+n_{1}}=0.1 \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{w_{2}}{M_{2}}}{\frac{W_{2}}{M_{2}}+\frac{W_{1}}{M_{1}}}=0.1 \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{W_{2}}{M_{2}}}{W_{2}\left(\frac{1}{M_{2}}+\frac{1}{M_{1}}\right)}=0.1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{1}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}}{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{1}+\mathrm{M}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{M}_{2}}}=0.1 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{M}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}+\mathrm{M}_{2}}=\frac{1}{10} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{M}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}+\mathrm{M}_{2}-\mathrm{M}_{1}}=\frac{1}{10-1} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{M}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}=\frac{1}{9} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{M}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}=9$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The mole fraction of ethanol in water is $0.08$. Its molality is
questions.solution:
Hint:
$\begin{aligned} \text { Molality } &=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{EtOH}}}{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{MW}_{\text {solvent }}} \\ &=\frac{0.08}{0.92} \times \frac{1000}{18} \\ &=4.83 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $5 \mathrm{~m}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} ~ \mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$ is mixed with $10 \mathrm{ml}$ of $0.02 \mathrm{M} ~ \mathrm{KI}$. The amount of $\mathrm{Pbl}_{2}$ precipitated will be about
questions.solution:
Hint:
mole of $\mathrm{Pbl}_{2} \quad \mathrm{PPt}=\frac{0.1}{1000}=1 \times 10^{-4}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has the largest number of atoms?
questions.solution:
Hint:
Number of atoms of $\mathrm{Ag}=\frac{1}{108} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=0.009 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
Number of atoms of $\mathrm{Fe}=\frac{1}{56} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=0.0178 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What will be the mass of one atom of ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ ?

questions.q: At S.T.P. the volume of $7.5 \mathrm{g}$ of a gas is $5.6 \mathrm{L}$. The gas is
questions.solution:
Given, mass of gas $(W)=7.5 \mathrm{g}$
Volume of gas at $\operatorname{STP}(V)=56 \mathrm{L}$
$\because$ Moles of gas at $\mathrm{STP}=\frac{V(\mathrm{L})}{22.4(\mathrm{L})}=\frac{W}{M}$
Where $M=$ molar mass of gas.
$\therefore \quad M=\frac{W \times 22.4}{V}$
$$
=\frac{7.5 \times 22.4}{5.6}=30.00 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}
$$
Among the given options Molar mass $(M)$ of
$\mathrm{NO}=14+16=30.00 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
(b) $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}=28+16=44.00 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
(c) $\mathrm{CO}=12+16=28.00 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
(d) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}=12+32=44.00 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
$\because$ Molar mass of $\mathrm{NO}=30 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Thus, the given gas is NO. Hence, is the correct option.

questions.q: A metal $M$ (specific heat 0.16 ) forms a metal chloride with $65 \%$ chlorine present in it. The formula of the metal chloride will be
questions.solution:
Given, specific heat $=0.16$ 
Let metal chloride be $M \mathrm{Cl}_{x}$ then. $\frac{6.4}{\text { specific heat }}=$ Atomic weight of metal $\frac{6.4}{0.16}=$ Atomic weight of metal
Atomic weight $=40$ 
40 is the atomic weight of calcium. According to question, metal chloride $\left(M C l_{x}\right)$ have $\approx 65 \%$ chlorine present in it. $\frac{x \times \text { Atomic weight of chlorine }}{40+x \times \text { Atomic weight of chlorine }} \times 100=65$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x \times 355}{40+x \times 355} \times 100=65 \\
x=209 \approx 2(a p p r o x .)
\end{array}
$$
So, the formula of metal chloride will be $M \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$

$\therefore \quad \frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\frac{W_{1}}{M_{1}} \times \frac{M_{2}}{W_{2}}\left\{\begin{array}{l}n_{1}=n_{\mathrm{so}_{1}} \\ n_{2}=n_{\mathrm{Oi}_{2}}\end{array}\right.$
$=\frac{2}{64} \times \frac{16}{1}$
$\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\frac{1}{2},$ i.e. 1: 2
Also $n \propto N$
$\therefore$ Ratio of number of molecules is 1: 2.

questions.q: The system that contains the maximum number of atoms is
questions.solution:
Number of atoms $=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { molar mass }} \times N_{A}$ x Number of atoms in 1 mole
$\therefore$ Number of atoms in $4.25 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{4.25}{17} \times N_{A} \times 4 \\
=N_{A}
\end{array}
$$
Number of atoms in $8 \mathrm{g}$
$$
\mathrm{O}_{2}=\frac{8}{32} \times \mathrm{N}_{A} \times 2=\frac{N_{\mathrm{A}}}{2}
$$
Number of atoms in $2 \mathrm{g}$
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}=\frac{2}{2} \times \mathrm{N}_{A} \times 2=2 \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}
$$
Number of atoms in $4 \mathrm{g}$
$$
\mathrm{He}=\frac{4}{4} \times N_{A} \times 1=N_{A}
$$
Thus, $2 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ contains the maximum number of atoms among the given.

questions.q: Number of hydrogen ions present in 10 milionth part of $1.33 \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ of pure water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
10 million $=10^{-7}$ 
$\therefore$ 10 $million^{th}$ part of $1.33 \mathrm{cm}^{3}=1.33 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ $=1.33 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{mL}$
For pure water,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] &=10^{-7} \mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\Rightarrow 1$ L water contains $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-7} \mathrm{mol}$
or $1 \mathrm{mL}$ water contains $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{10^{-7}}{1000}=10^{-10} \mathrm{mol}$
or 10 million $^{\text {th }}$ part of $1.33 \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ water contains $\begin{aligned}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] &=1.33 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{mol} \\ &=1.33 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Number of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ions $=1.33 \times 10^{-17} \times N_{A}$
$=1.33 \times 10^{-17} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$
$=8.009 \times 10^{6}=8.01$ million

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The van der Waal's equation: $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{4V^2}\right)\left(V - \dfrac{b}{2}\right) = \dfrac{RT}{2}$ is valid for
questions.solution:
The standard van der Waals equation for $n$ moles of a real gas is given by:
$\left(P + \dfrac{an^2}{V^2}\right) (V - nb) = nRT$

The given equation is:
$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{4V^2}\right) \left(V - \dfrac{b}{2}\right) = \dfrac{RT}{2}$

Comparing the right-hand side of the given equation with the standard equation:
$\dfrac{RT}{2} = nRT$
Thus, $n = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

Let us verify this by comparing the other terms.
In the volume term: $nb = \dfrac{1}{2}b = \dfrac{b}{2}$, which matches the given equation.
In the pressure term: $\dfrac{an^2}{V^2} = \dfrac{a \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2}{V^2} = \dfrac{a}{4V^2}$, which also matches the given equation.

The van der Waals equation describes the behavior of real gases by accounting for intermolecular forces and the finite size of gas molecules through the constants $a$ and $b$ respectively. Therefore, the given equation is valid for $\dfrac{1}{2}$ mole of a real gas.

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{2}$ mole of a real gas

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following plot(s) is/are correct representation(s) of Boyle's Law?
questions.solution:
According to Boyle's law, for a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature, the pressure is inversely proportional to its volume.

$P \propto \dfrac{1}{V}$

$P = \dfrac{k}{V}$

$PV = k$

Analyzing the given plots:

For plot (A): The equation $P = k \left(\dfrac{1}{V}\right)$ is of the form $y = mx$. Thus, the plot of $P$ versus $\dfrac{1}{V}$ is a straight line passing through the origin. This is correctly represented.

For plot (B): Taking the logarithm on both sides of $PV = k$, we get:
$\log P + \log V = \log k$
$\log P = -\log V + \log k$
This equation is of the form $y = mx + c$ with a slope $m = -1$ and an intercept $c = \log k$. Thus, the plot of $\log P$ versus $\log V$ is a straight line with a negative slope. This is correctly represented.

For plot (C): The equation $PV = k$ represents a rectangular hyperbola. Thus, the plot of $P$ versus $V$ is a curve (rectangular hyperbola). This is correctly represented.

For plot (D): The plot of $P$ versus $V$ is shown as a straight line with a negative slope, which contradicts Boyle's law. This is incorrect.

Therefore, plots (A), (B), and (C) are correct representations of Boyle's law.

Answer: ; ;

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An LPG (Liquified Petroleum Gas) cylinder weighs 15.0 kg when empty. When full, it weighs 30.0 kg and shows a pressure of 3.0 atm . In the course of usage at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the mass of the full cylinder is reduced to 24.2 kg . The volume of the used gas in cubic metre at the normal usage condition ( 1 atm and $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) is (assume LPG to be normal butane and it behaves ideally)
questions.solution:
Weight of gas $=15 \mathrm{~kg}$
Weight of gas in the cylinder after use $=24.2-15.0=9.2$
$\therefore$ Gas used $=15-9.2=5.8 \mathrm{~kg}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore V & =\frac{W}{M} \times \frac{R T}{P}=\frac{5.8 \times 10^3 \times 0.0821 \times 300}{58 \times 1} L \\
& =\frac{100 \times .0821 \times 300}{10^3} \mathrm{~m}^3 \\
& =2.463 \mathrm{~m}^3
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $360 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of a hydrocabon diffuses in 30 minutes, while under the same conditions $360 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ gas diffuses in one hour. The molecular formula of the hydrocabon is
questions.solution:
According to Graham's Law: (MW = Molecular Weight)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{d}}(\text { hydrocarbon })}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{d}}\left(\mathrm{SO}_2\right)}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{MW}_{\mathrm{SO}_2}}{\mathrm{MW}_{\text {hydrocarbon }}}} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{360 / 30}{360 / 60}=\sqrt{\frac{64}{\mathrm{MW}_{\text {hydrocarbon }}}} \\
& \Rightarrow 4=64 / \mathrm{MW}_{\text {hydrocabon }} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{MW}_{\text {hydrocarbon }}=\frac{64}{4}=16
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{CH}_4$ is the hydrocarbon with possible molecular weight 16.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The compressibility factor for a van der Waal gas at high pressure is
questions.solution:
For 1 mol van der Waal's gas
$\left(P+\frac{a}{V^2}\right)(v-b)=R T$
At high pressure $\mathrm{P}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}^2} \approx \mathrm{P}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore P(V-b)=R T \\ & \therefore P V-P b=R T \\ & P V=R T+P b \\ & z=\frac{P V}{R T}=\frac{R T}{R T}+\frac{P b}{R T} \\ & \therefore z=1+\frac{P b}{R T}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The root mean square (rms) speed of \(X_2\) gas is \(\mathrm{x} \mathrm{m/s}\) at a given temperature. When the temperature is doubled, the \(\mathrm{X}_2\) molecules dissociated completely into atoms. The root mean square speed of the sample of gas then becomes (in \(\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}\) )
questions.solution:
Hint : \(C_{m m s}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}\)
\(\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{T}_1=\mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T}_2=2 \mathrm{~T} \\
\mathrm{M}_1=\mathrm{M} & \mathrm{M}_2=\mathrm{M} / 2 \\
\mathrm{C}_1=\mathrm{X} & \mathrm{C}_2=?
\end{array}\)
\(\frac{C_1}{C_2}=\sqrt{\frac{T_1}{M_1} \times \frac{\mathrm{M}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{M}} \times \frac{\mathrm{M} / 2}{2 T}}=\frac{1}{2}\)
\(\therefore \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{1}{2}\), Hence \(\mathrm{C}_2=2 \mathrm{x~} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Case \(-1:\) An ideal gas of molecular weight \(M\) at temperature \(T\).
Case \(-2:\) Another ideal gas of molecular weight \(2 \mathrm{M}\) at temperature \(\mathrm{T} / 2\)
Identify the correct statement in context of above two cases.
options: Average kinetic energy and avereage speed will be the same in the two cases
Only average speed is halved in the second case
questions.solution:
Hint : As temperature is halved, average \(K E\) is halved.
Average speed \((C) \propto \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}}\)
In case \(-I,(C) \propto \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}}\)
\(\text { In case }-II ~(C) \propto \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \times 2 M}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}}\)
So average speed is also halved.
