questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The units of surface tension and viscosity of liquids respectively are
questions.solution:
From the formula, Surface tension, $(\gamma)$
$$
=\frac{\Delta W}{\Delta A}=\frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{m}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}}{\mathrm{m}^{2}}=\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{s}^{-2}
$$
Form the formula, $F=\eta \cdot A \cdot \frac{d V}{d x}$ or, $\eta$ (coefficient of viscosity)
$$
=\frac{F}{A \cdot \frac{d V}{d x}}=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{m}^{2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{\mathrm{m}}}=\mathrm{Nm}^{-2} \cdot \mathrm{s}
$$
or $\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{ms}^{-2} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{-2} \cdot \mathrm{s}=\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{-1} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The compressibility factor $(Z)$ of one mole of a van der Waals' gas of negligible ' $a$ ' value is
questions.solution:
van der Waals' equation is $\left(p+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right)(V-b)=R T$
(for 1 mole)
If $a$ is negligible, $p+\frac{a}{V^{2}} \approx p$
$$
p(V-b)=R T \Rightarrow p V-p b=R T
$$
or $\frac{p V}{R T}-\frac{p b}{R T}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{p V}{R T}=1+\frac{p b}{R T}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Two gases $X$ (molecular weight $M_{X}$ ) and Y (molecular weight $M_{Y} ; M_{Y}>M_{X}$ ) are at the same temperature $T$ in two different containers. Their root mean square velocities are $C_{X}$ and $C_{Y}$ respectively. If the average kinetic energies per molecule of two gases $X$ and $Y$ are $E_{x}$ and $E_{Y}$ respectively then which of the following relation(s) is(are) true?
questions.solution:
Given, molecular weight of $X=M_{X}$ 
Mol. wt. of $Y=M_{Y}$ and $M_{Y}>M_{x}$
Root mean square velocitios $=C_{X}$ and $C_{Y}$ respectively 
Average $\mathrm{KE}$/molecule $=E_{X}$ and $E_{Y}$ respectively We know that. Root mean square velocity.
$$
C=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}} \text { or } C \propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{M}}
$$
$\therefore \quad \frac{C_{X}}{C_{Y}}=\frac{M_{Y}}{M_{X}}$
since $\quad M_{Y}>M_{X}$
$\therefore$
$$
C_{X}>C_{Y}
$$
KE/molecules $=\frac{3}{2} n R T$
$\Rightarrow \quad E_{X} \not \subset E_{Y} \not = \frac{3}{2} R T$
Further. $\quad E_{X}=E_{Y}=\frac{3}{2} \boldsymbol{k}_{\boldsymbol{B}} \boldsymbol{T}$

questions.q:
$$
\text { At identical temperature and pressure, the rate of diffusion of hydrogen gas is } 3 \sqrt{3} \text { times that }
$$
of a hydrocarbon having molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}-2}$. What is the value of ' $n$ '?
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Hints : } \frac{r_{H_2}}{r_{C_n H_{2 n-2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}-2}}}{\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{H}_2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}-2}}}{2}} \\
& \because \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}-2}}}{2}}=3 \sqrt{3}=\sqrt{27} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}-2}}=27 \times 2=54
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, $12 n+(2 n-2) \times 1=54 \Rightarrow 14 n=56 \Rightarrow n=4$ Thus Hydrocarbon is $\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_6$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The plot of radial probability density $(4\pi r^2 R^2)$ against $r$ for an electron in $np$ orbital of a many electron atom is given below:

The value of $n$ is
questions.solution:
From the given radial probability density plot, the number of radial nodes (where the curve touches the horizontal axis between peaks) is $1$.

The number of radial nodes is given by the formula $n - l - 1$.

For an $np$ orbital, the azimuthal quantum number $l = 1$.

Therefore, $n - 1 - 1 = 1$
$\Rightarrow n - 2 = 1$
$\Rightarrow n = 3$

Alternatively, the number of peaks in the curve is $n - l$. Since there are $2$ peaks and $l = 1$, we get $n - 1 = 2 \Rightarrow n = 3$.

Answer: $3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The mass of an electron is $9.1 \times 10^{-31}$ kg. If its K.E. is $3.0 \times 10^{-25}$ J, its wavelength is (approximately)
questions.solution:
Given:
Mass of electron, $m = 9.1 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
Kinetic energy, $K = 3.0 \times 10^{-25}$ J
Planck's constant, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J s

The de Broglie wavelength $\lambda$ is given by the formula:
$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$

Substituting the given values:
$\lambda = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.0 \times 10^{-25}}}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{54.6 \times 10^{-56}}}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{7.389 \times 10^{-28}}$

$\lambda \approx 0.8967 \times 10^{-6}$ m

$\lambda = 896.7 \times 10^{-9}$ m $\approx 900$ nm

Answer: $900$ nm

${ }_5 \mathrm{~B}^{10}+{ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4 \rightarrow \mathrm{X}+{ }_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{n}^1$

& \therefore \mathrm{~b}=7

Balancing mass numbers; we have
$\begin{aligned}

& \therefore a=13
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The calculated spin-only magnetic moment values in \(\mathrm{BM}\) for \(\left[\mathrm{FeCl}_4\right]^{-}\)and \(\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}\) are
questions.solution:
Hint : \(\mathrm{Fe}^{+3}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 4 \mathrm{~s}^0\) \(\left[\mathrm{FeCl}_4\right]^{-}\)
No pairing as \(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\)is weak field ligand, hence have five unpaired electron \((n=5)\). \(\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mu=\sqrt{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+2)} \text { B.M }=\sqrt{5(5+2)} \text { B.M }=5.9 \text { B.M } \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{-3}}\end{aligned}\)
Pairing takes place as \(\mathrm{CN}^{-}\)is strong field ligand but has one unpaired electron \((n=1)\) \(\therefore \mu=\sqrt{n(n+2)} \text { B.M }=\sqrt{1(1+2)}=1.732 \text { B.M }\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength $(\lambda)$ for electron (e), proton $(p)$ and $\mathrm{He}^{2+}$ ion $(\alpha)$ are in the following order. (Speed of e, p and $\alpha$ are the same)
questions.solution:
$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}$
$\because$ speed are same
$\begin{aligned}
&\therefore \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}} \\
&\therefore \lambda_e>\lambda_p>\lambda_\alpha \text { as } \quad \mathrm{m}_\alpha>\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}>\mathrm{m}_e
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In Bohr model of atom, radius of hydrogen atom in ground state is $\mathrm{r}_1$ and radius of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in ground state is $\mathrm{r}_2$. Which of the following is correct?
questions.solution:
$r_n=a_0 \times \frac{n^2}{z}$
$\underline{\text {For H-atom}}$
$r_1=a_0$
$\underline{\text {Also, For}}$ $\underline {\mathrm{He}^{+}}$ - $\underline{\text { ion }}$
$r_2=a_0 \times \frac{1^2}{2}=\frac{r_1}{2}$
$\therefore \frac{r_2}{r_1}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction \(14 N+\alpha \longrightarrow{}^{17} O+p\), $1.16 \mathrm{MeV}($ Mass equivalent $=0.00124 \mathrm{amu})$ of energy is absorbed. Mass on the reactant side is $18.00567$ amu and proton mass $=1.00782 \mathrm{amu}$. The atomic mass of ${ }^{17} \mathrm{O}$ will be
questions.solution:
${ }^{14} \mathrm{~N}_{7}+{ }_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{17} \mathrm{O}_{8}+{ }_{1}^{1} \mathrm{H}$ mass defect $=0.00124 \mathrm{amu}$ $\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{o}^{17}}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{H}^{1}}\right)-\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{N}^{44}}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{He}^{4}}\right)=0.00124$ $\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{o}^{17}}+1.00782-[18.00567]=0.00124$ amu $\quad$ amu
solving: $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{o}^{17}}=16.99909 \mathrm{amu}$ $\therefore 16.9991$ (approx)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The atomic masses of helium and neon are $4.0$ and $20.0$ amu respectively. The value of the de Broglie wavelength of helium gas at $-73^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{M}$ times the de Broglie wavelength of neon at $727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The value of $\mathrm{M}$ is
questions.solution:
$\lambda_{\mathrm{He}}(200 \mathrm{~K})=\mathrm{M} \times \lambda_{\mathrm{Ne}}(1000 \mathrm{~K})$ $\left(\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{v}}\right)$
$\frac{\mathrm{h}}{4 \mathrm{amu} \times \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{mp}}(200)}=\mathrm{M} \times \frac{\mathrm{h}}{20 \mathrm{amu} \times \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{mp}}(1000)} \quad\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{mp}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{KT}}{\mathrm{m}}}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~K} \times 200}{4}}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{20 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~K} \times 1000}{20}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \times \sqrt{50}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{20 \times \sqrt{50}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{M}=5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The difference between orbital angular momentum of an electron in a 4 f orbital and another electron in a 4 s orbital is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Orbital angular momentum $=\sqrt{\ell(\ell+1)} \frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}$
For 4 f orbital; $\ell=3 \therefore$ Orbital angular momentum $=\sqrt{3(3+1)} \frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}=2 \sqrt{3} \frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}$
For 4 s orbital; $\ell=0 \therefore$ Orbital angular momentum $=0$
$\therefore$ Difference $=(2 \sqrt{3}) \frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following electronic arrangements is absurd?
questions.solution: The three quantum numbers $n, l$ and $m$ that describe an orbital has integer values of 0,1,2,3 and so on. The electronic arrangement of option (c) is absurd because $$ \begin{array}{l} n=2 \\ l=0 \\ m=0 \end{array} $$ To sum up, where $n=2, l=0, m=0$ Hence, for $l=0,$ value of $m$ should be zero.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of these species will have non-zero magnetic moment?
questions.solution:
Key point The species which has one or more unpaired electrons have non-zero magnetic moment.
Electronic configuration of
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{Na}^{+}(Z=11)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 p^{6} \\
\mathrm{Mg}(Z=12)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 p^{6} 3 \mathrm{s}^{2} \\
\mathrm{F}^{-}(Z=9)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{6} \\
\quad \mathrm{Ar}^{+}(Z=18)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 p^{6} 3 \mathrm{s}^{2} 3 p^{5}
\end{array}
$$
As, Ar $^{+}$ has an unpaired electron, it has non-zero magnetic moment. Hence, (d) is the correct option.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
If the given four electronic configurations.
(i) $n=4, l=1$
(ii) $n=4, l=0$
(iii) $n=3, l=2$
(iv) $n=3, l=1$
are arranged in order of increasing energy, then the order will be
questions.solution:
According to $(n+l)$ rule;
(i) More be the sum of $(n+l)$ value, more be the energy.
(ii) For same value of sum, more be the value of $n$, more be the energy.
For
(i) $n+l \rightarrow 4+1=5$
(ii) $n+l \rightarrow 4+0=4$
(iii) $n+l \rightarrow 3+2=5$
(iv) $n+l \rightarrow 3+1=4$
Hence, order of energy will be
(iv) $    < $ (ii) $ < $ (iii) $ < $ (i)

questions.solution:

$\therefore 19^{\text {b }}$ electron enters in $4 s$ -orbital and its set of quantum numbers is $n=4, l=0, m=0, m_{x}=+\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following corresponds to a photon of highest energy?
questions.solution:
$\because E=h v=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=h c \cdot \bar{v} \quad\left(\because \frac{1}{\lambda}=\bar{v}\right)$
where $E=$ energy of photon 
$c=$ velocity of photon (-light) 
$\lambda=$ wavelength of photon 
$h=$ plank's constant.
$\therefore$ For $(\mathrm{a})$
$E=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{4} / 300 \times 10^{-9}$
$E=1.98 \times 10^{-25} \cdot \mathrm{J} / 300 \times 10^{-9}$
$\therefore \quad E=\frac{1.98 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{J}}{300 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}$
$(a) \rightarrow E=6.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}$
For (b) $E=h v=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{4}$
$(b) \rightarrow E=1.98 \times 10^{-35} \mathrm{J}$
For (c) $E=h c \times v$
$\because \frac{1}{3}$
$$
E=6.63 \times 10^{-4} \times 3 \times 10^{*} \times 30 \times 10^{2}
$$
(c) $\Rightarrow E=5.96 \times 10^{\circ}$
For $(d) d \rightarrow E=6.62 \times 10^{-27} J$
Hence, highest energy for photon is in (a).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The energy required to break one mole of hydrogen-hydrogen bonds in $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is $436 \mathrm{kJ}$. What is the longest wavelength of light required to break a single hydrogenhydrogen bond?
questions.solution:
Given, energy of one mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ bonds $=436 \mathrm{k} \mathrm{J}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\because E=\frac{n \cdot h c}{\lambda}\\
\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{n.h c}{E} \\
\lambda=\frac{6.634 \times 10^{-} 34 \mathrm{s} \times 3 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{ms}^{-1} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{436 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{J}} \mathrm{m} \\
=0.2736 \times 10^{-34+8+23-3} \\
=0.2736 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m} \\
=273.6 \mathrm{nm}=274 \mathrm{nm}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The time taken for an electron to complete one revolution in Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom is
questions.solution:
Let the distance travelled in $T$ time $=2 \pi r$ (Circumference of orbit) 
$\therefore \quad$ Velocity $(v)=\frac{2 \pi r}{T}$
Also, $v=\frac{n \cdot h}{2 \pi m r}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{T \text { (Time period) }}=$ frequency $v=\text{v} \times \frac{1}{2 \pi r}$
From Eq.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{v}=\frac{1}{T}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi \times 2 \pi m r} \\
v=\frac{n h}{4 \pi^{2} r^{2} m}
\end{array}
$$
$T$ (time taken in one revolution)
$$
=\frac{4 \pi^{2} r^{2} m}{n h}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The emission spectrum of hydrogen discovered first and the region of the electromagnetic spectrum in which it belongs, respectively are
questions.solution:
In 1885 , Balmer for the first time showed that the wave numbers of spectral lines present in the visible region in hydrogen spectrum are given by
$$
\bar{v}\left(\mathrm{cm}^{-1}\right)=109677\left(\frac{1}{(2)^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right)
$$
Here, $n=3,4,5, \ldots$
Thus, Balmer spectrum of hydrogen was discovered first and it lies in the visible region.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct statement.
options: Quantum numbers $(n, l, m, s)$ are arbitrarily
All the quantum numbers $(n, 1, m, s)$ for any pair of electron in an atom can be identical under special circumstance
All the quantum numbers $(n, 1, m, s)$ may not be required to describe an electron of an atom completely
All the quantum numbers $(n, 1, m, s)$ are required to describe an electron of an atom completely
questions.solution: All the quantum numbers $(n, l, m, s)$ are required to describe an electron of an atom completely. e.g., $n$ describes the position and energy of the electron in an orbit or shell. l is used to describe subshell and the shape of the orbital occupied by the electron. $m$ describes the preferred orientation of orbitals in space. s describes the spining of an electron on its axis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following does not represent the mathematical expression for the Heisenberg uncertainty principle?
questions.solution:
From Heisenberg uncertainty principle, $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}$
or $\quad \Delta x \cdot m \Delta v \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}$
or $\quad \Delta x \cdot \Delta v \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi m}$
This principle is also applicable for pairs like energy-time $(\Delta E \cdot \Delta t) \quad$ and $\quad$ angular $\begin{array}{llll}\text { moment-angle } & (\Delta w \cdot \Delta \theta) & \text { along } & \text { with }\end{array}$
position-moment $(\Delta x \cdot \Delta p)$
Thus,
$$
\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The electronic transitions from \(\mathrm{n}=2\) to \(\mathrm{n}=1\) will produce shortest wavelength in (where \(\mathrm{n}=\) principal quantum state)
questions.solution:
Hints: \(\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{z}^2 \cdot \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}\left[1 / \mathrm{n}_1^2-1 / \mathrm{n}_2^2\right]\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda}=(\mathrm{z})^2 \cdot \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}\left\{\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{4}\right\}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{z}^2 \\
& \therefore \lambda \propto 1 / \mathrm{z}^2
\end{aligned}\)
Hence, for shortest \(\lambda, \mathrm{z}\) must be maximum, which is for \(\mathrm{Li}^{+2}\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The amount of electrolytes required to coagulate a given amount of Agl colloidal solution (-ve charge) will be in the order
questions.solution:
Since, AgI is a negatively charged sol, so according to Hardy Schulze law, higher the positive charge on the metal atoms, more is the tendency of metal atom to cause coagulation of negatively charged colloid. The order of positive charge is
$$
\mathrm{Na}^{+}    < \mathrm{Ba}^{2+} < \mathrm{AI}^{3 +}
$$
Thus, the order of amount of electrolytes required is in the order
$$
\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}>\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}>\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{3}
$$
Note In paper, the formula of aluminium nitrait is incorrect. Correct formula of aluminium nitrea is $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Three engines A, B and C take steam at $130°$C and reject it at $20°$C, $40°$C and $50°$C respectively. The most efficient engine will be
options: All the three engines will be equally efficient
questions.solution:
The efficiency of a heat engine is given by $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$, where $T_1$ is the temperature of the source and $T_2$ is the temperature of the sink.

For all three engines, the source temperature $T_1 = 130^\circ\text{C} = 403\text{ K}$.

The sink temperatures for engines A, B, and C are $T_{2A} = 20^\circ\text{C} = 293\text{ K}$, $T_{2B} = 40^\circ\text{C} = 313\text{ K}$, and $T_{2C} = 50^\circ\text{C} = 323\text{ K}$ respectively.

Since $\eta$ is inversely related to $T_2$ for a given $T_1$, the efficiency will be maximum when the sink temperature $T_2$ is minimum.

Engine A has the lowest sink temperature ($20^\circ\text{C}$), so it will be the most efficient.

Answer: A

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$1$ mole of an ideal gas undergoes the following processes:
Process A $\rightarrow$ Isothermal expansion at $400$K from volume $V_1$ to volume $V_2$, such that $V_2 = 4V_1$
Process B $\rightarrow$ Adiabatic expansion from volume $V_1$ to volume $V_2$, such that $V_2 = 4V_1$
options: Work done by gas in Process A is greater than in Process B
Final temperature in Process B is less than $400$K
Change in internal energy is $0$ in Process A but non-zero in Process B
Heat absorbed by the gas is positive in Process A but zero in Process B
questions.solution:
In Process A (isothermal expansion), the temperature remains constant at $400K$. Since the internal energy of an ideal gas depends only on temperature, the change in internal energy $\Delta U_A = 0$. Using the first law of thermodynamics, $Q_A = \Delta U_A + W_A = W_A$. Since the gas expands ($V_2 > V_1$), $W_A > 0$, so $Q_A > 0$. Thus, heat is absorbed by the gas.

In Process B (adiabatic expansion), no heat is exchanged with the surroundings, so $Q_B = 0$. Using the first law, $Q_B = \Delta U_B + W_B \Rightarrow \Delta U_B = -W_B$. Since the gas expands, $W_B > 0$, which means $\Delta U_B < 0$. Therefore, the internal energy decreases, and the final temperature must be less than the initial temperature of $400K$.

Comparing the work done, on a P-V diagram starting from the same initial state $(P_1, V_1)$, the adiabatic curve ($P \propto V^{-\gamma}$) is steeper than the isothermal curve ($P \propto V^{-1}$) because $\gamma > 1$. For a given final volume $V_2 = 4V_1$, the area under the isothermal curve is greater than the area under the adiabatic curve. Thus, the work done in Process A is greater than the work done in Process B ($W_A > W_B$).

Evaluating the given options, all statements are correct descriptions of the processes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Adiabatic free expansion of ideal gas must be
questions.solution:
According to first law of thermodynamics
$\begin{aligned}
& Q=d U-W \\
& \Rightarrow Q=d U-\left[-P_{\text {external }}\left(V_f-V_i\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$
[In Adiabatic Process $Q=0$, In Free expansion $P_{\text {extermal }}=0$ ]
$\therefore$ Putting the values, we get $\Rightarrow \mathrm{dU}=0 \quad \therefore \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{constant}$
As internal energy remains unchanged; temperature must be constant.
$\therefore$ Isothermal Process

questions.q: For the equilibrium, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{v}),$ which of the following is correct?
questions.solution:
For the given relation $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(t) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\eta)$
(i) At equilibrium $\Delta G=0$
(ii) Entropy change, i.e. $\Delta S$ is the measurement of randomness. It increases in vapour-state,
i.e. $\quad \Delta S=(+)$ ve or $\Delta S>0$
When liquid $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ changes into vapour $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, it requires heat energy. Thus, $\Delta H>0$ Hence, correct set is
$\Delta G=0, \Delta H>0$ and $\Delta S>0$
So, option (d) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At constant pressure, the heat of formation of a compound is not dependent on temperature, when
questions.solution:
Key Point According to Kirchhoff sequation
$$
\Delta H_{(f)}=\Delta H_{(i)}+C_{P}(\Delta T)
$$
At constant pressure
$$
\Delta C_{p}=O
$$
Therefore, heat of formation $\left(\Delta H_{f}\right)$ does not depend on temperature and value of $\Delta C_{p}$ i.e. when $\Delta C_{p}=0$
Hence, option (a) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct statement(s):
options: The oxidation number of $\mathrm{Cr}$ in $\mathrm{CrO}_{5}$ is +6
$\Delta H>\Delta U$ for the reaction $N_{2} O_{4}(g) \rightarrow 2 N O_{2}(g)$ provided both gases behave ideally.
$\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{N} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is less than that of $0.1 \mathrm{N} \mathrm{HC}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
questions.solution: In $\mathrm{CrO}_{5}$ oxidation no. of $\mathrm{Cr}$ is +6. (b) For the reaction (in ideal case) $$ \begin{array}{r} \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g) \\ \Delta H=\Delta U+p \cdot \Delta V \end{array} $$ $\because \quad \Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n_{g} R T$ $\left(\because p \Delta V=\Delta n_{g} R T\right)$ Where, $\Delta n_{g}=$ (gaseous moles of product)-(gaseous moles of reactant) $$ \Delta n_{g}=2-1=1 $$ Thus, $\Delta H>\Delta U$ (c) ${p H}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{N} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is less than of $0.1 \mathrm{HCl}$ at $25^{\circ}$ is a wrong statement. $\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ $$ \because \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left[\mathrm{l} 0^{-2}\right] $$ $\because \quad(N=C \times Z)$ and $\left(Z=2\right.$ for $\left.\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)$ $\mathrm{pH}=2$ and $\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ $$ \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{l} 0^{-1}\right]=1 $$ Thus, $\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}>\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$. (d) $\frac{R T}{F}=0.0591$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is a wrong statement. Where, $R=$ Gas constant $=8.314$ $T=$ Temperature $=273+25=298 \mathrm{K}$ $F=$ Charge over one mole of electrons $=96500 \mathrm{C}$ Thus, $\frac{R T}{F}=\frac{8.314 \times 298}{96500}=0.02567$ The correct value is $\frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.0591$ Hence, only option and (b) are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: During a reversible adiabatic process, the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature. The ratio $\frac{C_{p}}{C_{V}}$ for the gas is
questions.solution:
For a reversible adiabatic process.
$$
p T^{\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}}=\text { constant }
$$
According to question, during a reversible adiabatic process the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature.
$$
\begin{array}{c}
p \propto T^{3} \\
p T^{-3}=\text {constant }
\end{array}
$$
Equating equation (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}=-3
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\gamma=-3(1-\gamma) \\
\gamma=-3+3 \gamma \\
-2 \gamma=-3 \\
\gamma=\frac{3}{2}
\end{array}
$$
As we know, the ratio of molar heat capacities at constant pressure and constant volume is represented by $y$. So, the ratio of $\frac{C}{C_{V}}$ for the gas is $\frac{3}{2}$

questions.q: The condition for a reaction to occur spontaneously is
questions.solution:
For a spontaneous reaction, $\Delta G$ = negative and $\Delta S=$ positive
$$
\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S=-v e
$$
Hence, $\Delta H-T \Delta S=-$ ve only when $\Delta S=+$ ve and
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction. $X_{2} Y_{4}(I) \rightarrow 2 X Y_{2}(g)$ at $300 \mathrm{K}$, the values of $\Delta U$ and $\Delta$ S are $2 \mathrm{kcal}$ and 20 cal $\mathrm{K}^{-1}$ respectively. The value of $\Delta G$ for the reaction is
questions.solution:
For the reaction $X_{2} Y_{4}(I) \longrightarrow 2 X Y_{2}(g)$
$\Delta n_{g}=$ number of gaseous products - number of gaseous reactants
$$
\Delta n_{g}=2-0=2
$$
Given that, $\Delta U=2$ kcal
$$
\Delta S=20 \text { cal } K^{-1}
$$
From the formula,
$$
\Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n_{g} R T
$$
Putting the values, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &=2+\left(\frac{2 \times 2 \times 300}{1000}\right) \\
&=3.2 \mathrm{kcal}=3.2 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{cal} \\
\text { Also, } \Delta G &=\Delta H-T \Delta S \\
=& 3.2 \times 10^{3}-300 \times 20 \\
=& 3.2 \times 10^{3}-6 \times 10^{3} \\
=&-2800 \mathrm{cal}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For one mole of an ideal gas, the slope of $V$ vs. $T$ curve at constant pressure of 2 atm is $X$ L $\mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1} .$ The value of the ideal universal gas constant 'R' in terms of $X$ is
questions.solution:
Ideal gas equation is
$$
p V=n R T \quad \text { or } \quad V=\frac{n R T}{p}+C
$$
or $\quad V=\frac{R T}{p}+0 \quad$ (for $1 \mathrm{mol}$ )
When $V$ vs $T$ is plotted, a straight line is obtained, slope of which is given by $R / p$. Thus.
$$
\text { Slope }=\frac{R}{p} \Rightarrow X=\frac{R}{2}
$$
or
$$
R=2 X \mathrm{L} \text { atm } \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The values of $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ of a certain reaction are $-400 \mathrm{k} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ and $-20 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1}$ respectively. The temperature below which the reaction is spontaneous, is
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad \Delta H=-400 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
$$
\Delta S=-20 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1}
$$
Gibbs Helmholtz equation is
$$
\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S
$$
For a reaction to be spontaneous, $\Delta G$ must be $\therefore \quad 0 \geq \Delta H-T \Delta S$
$$
\begin{array}{r}
T \Delta S \leq \Delta H \\
T \leq \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} \leq \frac{-400}{-20} \leq 20 \mathrm{K}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Given that $\begin{array}{c} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2} ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-x \mathrm{k} \mathrm{J} \\ 2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2} ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-y \mathrm{k} \mathrm{J}\end{array}$. The heat of formation of carbon monoxide will be
questions.solution:
Given.
$$
\begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}: \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{x} \mathrm{kJ} ...(i) \\
2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}: \Delta H^{\circ}=-\mathrm{y} \mathrm{kJ} ...(ii)
\end{array}
$$
Required equation is
$$
\mathrm{C}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}, \mathrm{AH}_{1}^{\circ}=?
$$
On reversing eq. (ii), we get $2 \mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}^{\circ}=+\mathrm{y} \mathrm{kJ}$ ...(iii)
Dividing eq. (iii) by 2 gives $\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} ; \Delta H_{3}^{0}=+\frac{y}{2} \mathrm{kJ}$ ...(iv)
On adding eq. (i) and (iv), we get (required equation)
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{C}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO} ; \Delta H_{1}^{\circ} &=\Delta H^{\circ}+\Delta H_{3}^{\circ} \\ &=\left(-x+\frac{y}{2}\right) \mathrm{k} \mathrm{J} \\ &=\frac{y-2 x}{2} \mathrm{kJ} \end{aligned}$

questions.solution:
For isothermal expansion of an ideal gas
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Delta U=0 \text { and, from } \\
\Delta H=n C_{\rho} \Delta t=0
\end{array}

From first law of thermodynamics,

\Delta U=Q+W
$$
as
$$
\Delta U=0 \Rightarrow Q \neq 0

$$

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Given $P(x) = x^4 + ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$ such that $x = 0$ is the only real root of $P'(x) = 0$. If $P(-1) < P(1)$, then in the interval $[-1, 1]$.
options: $P(-1)$ is the minimum but $P(1)$ is not the maximum of $P$
$P(-1)$ is not minimum but $P(1)$ is the maximum of $P$
neither $P(1)$ is the minimum nor $P(1)$ is the maximum of $P$
$P(-1)$ is the minimum and $P(1)$ is the maximum of $P$.
questions.solution:
Given $P(x) = x^4 + ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$

$P'(x) = 4x^3 + 3ax^2 + 2bx + c$

Since $x = 0$ is the only real root of $P'(x) = 0$, we have $P'(0) = 0 \Rightarrow c = 0$.

$P'(x) = x(4x^2 + 3ax + 2b)$

For $x = 0$ to be the only real root, the quadratic equation $4x^2 + 3ax + 2b = 0$ must have no real roots (or $x=0$ is a repeated root, which implies $a=0, b=0$). In either case, $4x^2 + 3ax + 2b > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ because the leading coefficient is positive.

Thus, $P'(x) < 0$ for $x < 0$ and $P'(x) > 0$ for $x > 0$.

This means $P(x)$ is strictly decreasing on $[-1, 0]$ and strictly increasing on $[0, 1]$.

The minimum value of $P(x)$ in $[-1, 1]$ occurs at $x = 0$. Therefore, $P(-1)$ is not the minimum.

The maximum value of $P(x)$ in $[-1, 1]$ must occur at one of the endpoints, $x = -1$ or $x = 1$.

Since it is given that $P(-1) < P(1)$, the maximum value is $P(1)$.

Hence, $P(-1)$ is not the minimum but $P(1)$ is the maximum of $P$.

Answer: $P(-1)$ is not minimum but $P(1)$ is the maximum of $P$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which of the following statements is always true?
options: If $f(x)$ is decreasing, then $\dfrac{1}{f(x)}$ is increasing
If $f(x)$ is decreasing, then $\dfrac{1}{f(x)}$ is also decreasing
If both $f$ and $g$ are positive functions such that $f$ is decreasing and $g$ is increasing, then $\dfrac{f}{g}$ is a decreasing function
If both $f$ and $g$ are positive functions such that $f$ is increasing and $g$ is decreasing then $\dfrac{f}{g}$ is a decreasing function
questions.solution:
Let $x_1 < x_2$.

Since $f(x)$ is a decreasing positive function, $0 < f(x_2) \le f(x_1)$.

Since $g(x)$ is an increasing positive function, $0 < g(x_1) \le g(x_2)$.

Taking the reciprocal of the second inequality, we get $\dfrac{1}{g(x_1)} \ge \dfrac{1}{g(x_2)} > 0$.

Multiplying the two inequalities, we obtain $\dfrac{f(x_2)}{g(x_2)} \le \dfrac{f(x_1)}{g(x_1)}$.

This implies that $\dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}$ is a decreasing function.

Alternatively, assuming differentiability, let $h(x) = \dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}$.

$h'(x) = \dfrac{f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)}{(g(x))^2}$

Since $f(x)$ is decreasing and positive, $f'(x) \le 0$ and $f(x) > 0$.

Since $g(x)$ is increasing and positive, $g'(x) \ge 0$ and $g(x) > 0$.

Therefore, $f'(x)g(x) \le 0$ and $f(x)g'(x) \ge 0$, which gives $f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x) \le 0$.

Thus, $h'(x) \le 0$, meaning $h(x)$ is a decreasing function.

Answer: If both $f$ and $g$ are positive functions such that $f$ is decreasing and $g$ is increasing, then $\dfrac{f}{g}$ is a decreasing function

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $0 < \alpha < \beta < \gamma < \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, then the equation $\dfrac{1}{x - \sin\alpha} + \dfrac{1}{x - \sin\beta} + \dfrac{1}{x - \sin\gamma} = 0$ has
questions.solution:
Let $a = \sin\alpha$, $b = \sin\beta$, and $c = \sin\gamma$.

Since $0 < \alpha < \beta < \gamma < \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, the sine function is strictly increasing in this interval, giving $0 < a < b < c < 1$.

The given equation is:

$\dfrac{1}{x - a} + \dfrac{1}{x - b} + \dfrac{1}{x - c} = 0$

Multiplying by $(x-a)(x-b)(x-c)$, we obtain a quadratic equation:

$f(x) = (x-b)(x-c) + (x-a)(x-c) + (x-a)(x-b) = 0$

Evaluating $f(x)$ at $x = a, b, c$:

$f(a) = (a-b)(a-c) > 0$ (since $a < b$ and $a < c$)

$f(b) = (b-a)(b-c) < 0$ (since $b > a$ and $b < c$)

$f(c) = (c-a)(c-b) > 0$ (since $c > a$ and $c > b$)

Since $f(a) > 0$ and $f(b) < 0$, by the Intermediate Value Theorem, there is one real root in the interval $(a, b)$.

Similarly, since $f(b) < 0$ and $f(c) > 0$, there is another real root in the interval $(b, c)$.

Alternatively, expanding $f(x)$ gives $3x^2 - 2(a+b+c)x + (ab+bc+ca) = 0$.

The discriminant is $D = 4(a+b+c)^2 - 12(ab+bc+ca) = 2((a-b)^2 + (b-c)^2 + (c-a)^2)$.

Since $a, b, c$ are distinct, $D > 0$.

Thus, the quadratic equation has two real and unequal roots.

Answer: real and unequal roots

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A figure is bounded by the curves $y = x^2 + 1, y = 0, x = 0$ and $x = 1$. The point at which a tangent should be drawn to the curve $y = x^2 + 1$ for it to cut off trapezium of the greatest area from the figure is
questions.solution:
Let the point on the curve $y = x^2 + 1$ be $(t, t^2 + 1)$.

The derivative of the curve is $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 2x$.

The slope of the tangent at $x = t$ is $2t$.

The equation of the tangent at this point is:
$y - (t^2 + 1) = 2t(x - t)$
$\Rightarrow y = 2tx - t^2 + 1$

This tangent line intersects the vertical lines $x = 0$ and $x = 1$ at $y_1$ and $y_2$ respectively:
At $x = 0$, $y_1 = 1 - t^2$
At $x = 1$, $y_2 = 2t - t^2 + 1$

The area $A$ of the trapezium formed by the tangent, $x = 0$, $x = 1$, and $y = 0$ is given by:
$A = \dfrac{1}{2}(y_1 + y_2) \times (1 - 0)$
$A = \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - t^2 + 2t - t^2 + 1)$
$A = 1 + t - t^2$

To maximize the area, we differentiate $A$ with respect to $t$ and set it to zero:
$\dfrac{dA}{dt} = 1 - 2t = 0$
$\Rightarrow t = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Since $\dfrac{d^2A}{dt^2} = -2 < 0$, the area is maximized at $t = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

The corresponding $y$-coordinate on the curve is:
$y = \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 + 1 = \dfrac{1}{4} + 1 = \dfrac{5}{4}$

Therefore, the required point is $\left(\dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{5}{4}\right)$.

Answer: $\left(\dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{5}{4}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)$ be a twice differentiable function in $[1, 3]$ and $f(1) = f(3)$. Further if $|f''(x)| \leq 2$, then for all $x$ in $[1, 3]$
questions.solution:
Since $f(1) = f(3)$, by Rolle's Theorem, there exists $c \in (1, 3)$ such that $f'(c) = 0$.

By Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem for $f'(x)$ on the interval between $x$ and $c$, there exists $d$ such that:

$f''(d) = \dfrac{f'(x) - f'(c)}{x - c}$

$\Rightarrow f'(x) = f''(d)(x - c)$

Taking absolute values on both sides:

$|f'(x)| = |f''(d)| |x - c|$

We are given that $|f''(x)| \leq 2$ for all $x \in [1, 3]$. Thus:

$|f'(x)| \leq 2 |x - c|$

Since $x \in [1, 3]$ and $c \in (1, 3)$, the maximum possible distance between $x$ and $c$ is strictly less than $3 - 1 = 2$.

$\Rightarrow |x - c| < 2$

Therefore, $|f'(x)| < 2 \times 2 = 4$.

Hence, $|f'(x)| < 4$ for all $x \in [1, 3]$.

Answer: $|f'(x)| < 4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Tangent at a point $P_1$ (other than $(0, 0)$) on the curve $y = x^3$ meets the curve again at $P_2$. The tangent at $P_2$ meets the curve at $P_3$ and so on. Then the abscissae of $P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n$ form
questions.solution:
The equation of the curve is $y = x^3$.

Let the coordinates of $P_1$ be $(x_1, x_1^3)$.

Differentiating the curve with respect to $x$, we get $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 3x^2$.

The slope of the tangent at $P_1$ is $3x_1^2$.

The equation of the tangent at $P_1$ is $y - x_1^3 = 3x_1^2(x - x_1)$.

To find the point of intersection $P_2$ of this tangent with the curve, we substitute $y = x^3$ into the tangent equation:

$x^3 - x_1^3 = 3x_1^2(x - x_1)$

$x^3 - 3x_1^2 x + 2x_1^3 = 0$

Since the line is tangent to the curve at $P_1$, $x = x_1$ is a repeated root of this cubic equation. Let the third root be $x_2$, which is the abscissa of $P_2$.

Using the sum of the roots for the cubic equation:

$x_1 + x_1 + x_2 = 0$

$x_2 = -2x_1$

Similarly, the tangent at $P_2$ meets the curve at $P_3$, so the abscissa of $P_3$ will be $x_3 = -2x_2$.

In general, $x_n = -2x_{n-1}$.

Therefore, the abscissae $x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n$ form a geometric progression (G.P.) with a common ratio of $-2$.

Answer: a G.P. with common ratio $(-2)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the curve $x = 1 - 3t^2, y = t - 3t^3$. The tangent to the curve at the point $t$ is inclined at an angle $\phi$ to OX and the tangent at $P(-2, 2)$ meets the curve again at $Q$. Then
options: the curve is symmetrical about x-axis
the curve is symmetrical about y-axis
tangents at P and Q are at right angle
questions.solution:
First, let's find the Cartesian equation of the curve to check for symmetry.
Given parametric equations:
$x = 1 - 3t^2 \Rightarrow 3t^2 = 1 - x$
$y = t - 3t^3 = t(1 - 3t^2) = tx$

Squaring the second equation:
$y^2 = t^2 x^2$
Substituting $t^2 = \dfrac{1 - x}{3}$:
$y^2 = \left(\dfrac{1 - x}{3}\right)x^2$
$3y^2 = x^2(1 - x)$

Replacing $y$ with $-y$ yields $3(-y)^2 = x^2(1 - x) \Rightarrow 3y^2 = x^2(1 - x)$, which is the same as the original equation. Thus, the curve is symmetrical about the x-axis. Option (1) is correct.

Replacing $x$ with $-x$ changes the equation to $3y^2 = x^2(1 + x)$, so it is not symmetrical about the y-axis. Option (2) is incorrect.

The slope of the tangent at any parameter $t$ is $\tan \phi$:
$\tan \phi = \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{\dfrac{dy}{dt}}{\dfrac{dx}{dt}} = \dfrac{1 - 9t^2}{-6t} = \dfrac{9t^2 - 1}{6t}$

Rearranging this gives a quadratic in $3t$:
$6t \tan \phi = 9t^2 - 1$
$(3t)^2 - 2(3t)\tan \phi - 1 = 0$
Solving for $3t$ using the quadratic formula:
$3t = \dfrac{2\tan \phi \pm \sqrt{4\tan^2 \phi - 4(1)(-1)}}{2}$
$3t = \tan \phi \pm \sqrt{\tan^2 \phi + 1}$
$3t = \tan \phi \pm \sec \phi$
This shows $3t = \tan \phi + \sec \phi$ is a valid relation. Option (3) is correct.

For the point $P(-2, 2)$:
$x = 1 - 3t^2 = -2 \Rightarrow 3t^2 = 3 \Rightarrow t = \pm 1$
$y = t - 3t^3 = 2$
If $t = -1$, $y = -1 - 3(-1) = 2$. Thus, point $P$ corresponds to $t = -1$.

Slope of the tangent at $P$ ($t = -1$):
$m_1 = \dfrac{9(-1)^2 - 1}{6(-1)} = -\dfrac{4}{3}$

Equation of the tangent at $P(-2, 2)$:
$y - 2 = -\dfrac{4}{3}(x + 2)$
$4x + 3y + 2 = 0$

This tangent meets the curve again at point $Q$. Let $Q$ correspond to the parameter $t$. Substituting $x = 1 - 3t^2$ and $y = t - 3t^3$:
$4(1 - 3t^2) + 3(t - 3t^3) + 2 = 0$
$9t^3 + 12t^2 - 3t - 6 = 0$
Dividing by 3:
$3t^3 + 4t^2 - t - 2 = 0$

Since the tangent touches the curve at $P$ (where $t = -1$), $(t + 1)^2$ must be a factor:
$(t + 1)^2 (3t - 2) = 0$
For point $Q$, $3t - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow t = \dfrac{2}{3}$.

Slope of the tangent at $Q$ ($t = \dfrac{2}{3}$):
$m_2 = \dfrac{9\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 - 1}{6\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)} = \dfrac{3}{4}$

Product of the slopes:
$m_1 \times m_2 = \left(-\dfrac{4}{3}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) = -1$
Since the product is $-1$, the tangents at $P$ and $Q$ are at right angles. Option (4) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)$ be continuous on $[0,5]$ and differentiable in $(0,5)$. If $f(0)=0$ and $\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right| \leq \frac{1}{5}$ for all $x$ in $(0,5)$, then $\forall x$ in $[0,5]$.
questions.solution:
$\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right| \leq \frac{1}{5}, \Rightarrow\left|\frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}\right| \leq \frac{1}{5}$
$\Rightarrow\left|\frac{f(x)}{x}\right| \leq \frac{1}{5}, \quad|f(x)| \leq \frac{|x|}{5} \Rightarrow|f(x)| \leq 1 \quad\left(0 \leq x \leq 5 \Rightarrow 0 \leq \frac{x}{5} \leq 1\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x=-1$ and $x=2$ are extreme points of $f(x)=\alpha \log |x|+\beta x^2+x,(x \neq 0)$, then
questions.solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\alpha}{|x|} \cdot \frac{|x|}{x}+2 \beta x+1, f(-1)=f(2)=0$
So, $-\alpha-2 \beta+1=0 
 \Rightarrow \alpha+2 \beta-1=0$ ...(i)
$\& \frac{\alpha}{2}+4 \beta+1=0 \Rightarrow \alpha+8 \beta+2=0$....(ii)
(ii) - (i) $\Rightarrow 6 \beta+3=0 \Rightarrow \beta=-1 / 2$
(i) $\Rightarrow \alpha-1-1=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $p(\mathrm{x})$ be a real polynomial of least degree which has a local maximum at $\mathrm{x}=1$ and a local minimum at $\mathrm{x}=3$. If $\mathrm{p}(1)$ $=6$ and $p(3)=2$, then $p^{\prime}(0)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Degree 3
$\begin{aligned}
& P^{\prime}(x)=a(x-1)(x-3) \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{x}^2-4 \mathrm{x}+3\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{a}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^3}{3}-\frac{4 \mathrm{x}^2}{2}+3 \mathrm{x}\right)+\mathrm{b} \\
& P(1)=6 \Rightarrow a\left(\frac{1}{3}-2+3\right)+b=6 \Rightarrow \frac{4 a}{3}+b=6 \\
& \Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{a}+3 \mathrm{~b}=18 &...(i) \\
& \Rightarrow P(3)=2 \Rightarrow a(9-18+9)+b=2 &...(ii) \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=2
\end{aligned}$

Put in (i) $\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{a}+6=18 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=3$
So, $p^{\prime}(x)=3(x-1)(x-3) \Rightarrow P^{\prime}(0)=9$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\phi(x)=f(x)+f(2 a-x), x \in[0,2 a]$ and $f^{\prime \prime}(x)\gt0$ for all $x \in[0, a]$. Then $\phi(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$\phi^{\prime}(x)=f^{\prime}(x)-f^{\prime}(2 a-x) \quad \because f^{\prime \prime}(x)\gt0$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$ is increasing
So, $\phi(x)$ is decreasing in $(0, a)$ as $f^{\prime}(x) \lt f^{\prime}(2 a-x)$ in $(0, a)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A function $f$ is defined by $f(x)=2+(x-1)^{2 / 3}$ on $[0,2]$. Which of the following statements is incorrect?
questions.solution:
$f(x)=2+(x-1)^{2 / 3}$
$f(x)$ is not differentiable at $x=1$
Hence Rolle's theorem is not applicable on [0, 2]

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=2 x^3-3 x^2-12 x+4, x \in \mathbb{R}$ has
options: one local maximum and one local minimum
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=0 \quad \Rightarrow x=-1,2 \\ & x=-1 \text { is local maxima } \\ & x=2 \text { is local minima }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f$ be a function which is differentiable for all real $x$. If $f(2)=-4$ and $f^{\prime}(x) \geq 6$ for all $x \in[2,4]$
questions.solution:
By LMVT
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{f(4)-f(2)}{4-2}$
as $f^{\prime}(x) \geq 6 \Rightarrow f(4) \geq 8$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)$ be a second degree polynomial. If $f(1)=f(-1)$ and $p, q, r$ are in A.P., then $f^{\prime}(p), f^{\prime}(q), f^{\prime}(r)$ are
options: neither in A.P. or G.P. or H.P.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & f(-1)=f(1) \Rightarrow b=0 \\ & f(x)=a x^2+c \quad \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=2 a x\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)=x^3, x \in[-1,1]$. Then which of the following are correct?

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $y=f(x)$ be any curve on the $X-Y$ plane \& $P$ be a point on the curve. Let $C$ be a fixed point not on the curve. The length $P C$ is either a maximum or a minimum, then
options: PC is perpendicular to the tangent at P
PC is parallel to the tangent at $P$
PC meets the tangent at an angle of $45^{\circ}$
PC meets the tangent at an angle of $60^{\circ}$
questions.solution:
- Let \(P(x, y)\) be a point on the curve, and let \(C(a, b)\) be a fixed point not on the curve.
- The distance \(d\) from \(C\) to \(P\) is given by \(d=\sqrt{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}\).
- To find the extremum, we would set up an optimization problem for \(d\) with respect to the curve's parameters.
- A necessary condition for \(d\) to be a maximum or minimum is that the gradient of \(d\) with respect to \(P\) must be perpendicular to the curve at \(P\).
Thus, \(P C\) must be perpendicular to the tangent at \(P\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be given by $f(x)=\left|x^2-1\right|$, then
options: $f$ has a local minima at $x= \pm 1$ but no local maxima
$f$ has a local maxima at $x=0$, but no local minima
f has a local minima at $x= \pm 1$ and a local maxima at $x=0$
f has neither any local maxima nor any local minima
questions.solution: By figure, $f$ has a local minima at $x= \pm 1$ and a local maxima at $x=0$

options: Local minima do not exist for this function
questions.solution:
Hint : f(x) = x (x- 1) (x - 2) (x - 3) .... (x â€“ 100)
