questions.q: The number of irrational terms in the binomial expansion of $\left(3^{1 / 5}+7^{1 / 3}\right)^{100}$ is
questions.solution:
General term of $\left(3^{1 / 5}+7^{1 / 3}\right)^{100}$ is given by $T_{\gamma+1}={ }^{100} C_{r}\left(3^{1 / 5}\right)^{100-r}\left(7^{1 / 3}\right)^{r}$
$={ }^{100} C_{1} \cdot 3^{\frac{100-r}{5}} \cdot 7^{\frac{r}{3}}$
For a rational term. $\frac{100-r}{5}$ and $\frac{r}{3}$ must be integer.
Hence, $r=0,15,30,45,60,75,90$
$\therefore$ There are seven rational terms. Hence, number of irrational terms
$$
=101-7=94
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the coefficient of $x^{8}$ in $\left(a x^{2}+\frac{1}{b x}\right)^{13}$ is equal to the coefficient of $x^{-8}$ in $\left(a x-\frac{1}{b x^{2}}\right)^{13},$ then $a$ and $b$ will satisfy the relation
questions.solution:
The general term in $\left(a x^{2}+\frac{1}{b x}\right)^{13}$ is
$T_{r+1}={ }^{13} C_{r}\left(a x^{2}\right)^{13-r}\left(\frac{1}{b x}\right)^{r}$
$$
={ }^{13} C_{r} a^{13-r} \times b^{-r}(x)^{26-3 r}
$$
For coefficient of $x^{8}$. put $26-3 r=8$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 r=18 \Rightarrow r=6$
$\therefore$
$T_{7}={ }^{13} C_{6} a^{13-6} b^{-6} x^{8}$
$={ }^{13} C_{6} a^{7} b^{-6} x^{8}$
Now, the general term in $\left(a x-\frac{1}{b x^{2}}\right)^{13}$ is
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{r+1}^{'} &={ }^{13} C_{r}(a x)^{13-r}\left(-\frac{1}{b x^{2}}\right)^{r} \\
&={ }^{13} C_{r}^{*} a^{13-r} \times b^{-r} \times(x)^{13-3 r}(-1)^{\prime}
\end{aligned}
$$
For coefficient of $x^{-8}$, put $13-3 r=-8$
According to the given condition, Coefficient of $x^{8}$ in $\left(a x^{2}+\frac{1}{b x}\right)^{13}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\text { Coefficient of } x^{-8} \text {in }\left(a x-\frac{1}{b x^{2}}\right)^{13} \\
\therefore{ }^{13} C_{6} a^{7} b^{-6}=-{ }^{13} C_{7} a^{5} b^{-7} \\
\Rightarrow{ }^{13} C_{7} \frac{a^{7}}{b^{6}}=-{ }^{13} C_{7} \frac{a^{6}}{b^{7}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a^{7}}{a^{6}}=-\frac{b^{6}}{b^{7}} \Rightarrow \quad a=-\frac{1}{b} \Rightarrow a b+1=0
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the sum $\left({ }^{n} C_{1}\right)^{2}+\left({ }^{n} C_{2}\right)^{2}+\left({ }^{n} C_{3}\right)^{2}+\ldots+\left({ }^{n} C_{n}\right)^{2}$ is
questions.solution:
We know that
$$
\begin{array}{r}
(1+x)^{n}={ }^{n} C_{0}+{ }^{n} C_{1} x+{ }^{n} C_{2} x^{2} \\
\quad+\ldots+{ }^{n} C_{n} x^{n} \\
\text { and }(x+1)^{n}={ }^{n} C_{0} x^{n}+{ }^{n} C_{1} x^{n-1} \\
\quad+{ }^{n} C_{2} x^{n-2}+\ldots+{ }^{n} C_{n}
\end{array}
$$
On multiplying Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
(1+x)^{2 n}=\left({ }^{n} C_{0}+{ }^{n} C_{1} x+{ }^{n} C_{2} x^{2}+\ldots+{ }^{n} C_{n} x^{n}\right) \\
\quad \times\left({ }^{n} C_{0} x^{n}+{ }^{n} C_{1} x^{n-1}+{ }^{n} C_{2} x^{n-2}+\ldots+{ }^{n} C_{n}\right)
\end{array}
$$
Coefficient of $x^{n}$ in RHS
$$
=\left({ }^{n} C_{0}\right)^{2}+\left({ }^{n} C_{1}\right)^{2}+\ldots+\left({ }^{n} C_{n}\right)^{2}
$$
and coefficient of $x^{n}$ in $\mathrm{LHS}={ }^{2 n} \mathrm{C}_{n}$
$$
\begin{array}{c}
\therefore\left({ }^{n} C_{0}\right)^{2}+\left({ }^{n} C_{1}\right)^{2}+\ldots+\left({ }^{n} C_{n}\right)^{2}=\frac{2 n !}{n ! n !} \\
\Rightarrow\left({ }^{n} C_{1}\right)^{2}+\ldots+\left({ }^{n} C_{n}\right)^{2}=\frac{(2 n) !}{n ! n !}-1 \\
\quad={ }^{2 n} C_{n}-1
\end{array}
$$

questions.solution:
Let the number of terms, $n=2 \mathrm{m}$ 
Now, by condition
$\frac{\text { Largest coefficient in }(1+x)^{\circ}}{\text { Second largest coefficient in }(1+x)^{n}}=\frac{11}{10}$
(given)
$\Rightarrow$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2 m}{2 m} C_{m} &=\frac{11}{10} \\
\frac{(m-1) !(m+1) !}{m ! m !} &=\frac{11}{10}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{m} \frac{m(m-1) !(m+1) m !}{m ! m !}=\frac{11}{10}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m+1}{m} \frac{m+m !}{m ! m !}=\frac{11}{10}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 10 m+10=11 m \Rightarrow m=10$
$\therefore \quad n=20$
Hence, total number of term
$$
=n+1=20+1=21
$$

& { }^9 \mathrm{C}_2 3^7 a^2={ }^9 \mathrm{C}_3 3{ }^6 \mathrm{a}^3 \\
& \frac{9}{7}=a
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let all the points on the curve $x^2 + y^2 - 10x = 0$ are reflected about the line $y = x + 3$. If the locus of the reflected points is in the form $x^2 + y^2 + gx + fy + c = 0$, then the value of $(g + f + c)$ is
questions.solution:
The given curve is $x^2 + y^2 - 10x = 0$.

Rewriting it by completing the square, we get:
$(x - 5)^2 + y^2 = 25$

This represents a circle with center $C(5, 0)$ and radius $r = 5$.

When a circle is reflected about a line, the reflected curve is also a circle with the same radius, and its center is the reflection of the original center about the given line.

Let the center of the reflected circle be $C'(h, k)$. The line of reflection is $x - y + 3 = 0$.

Using the formula for the reflection of a point $(x_1, y_1)$ about the line $ax + by + c = 0$:
$\dfrac{h - x_1}{a} = \dfrac{k - y_1}{b} = -2\dfrac{ax_1 + by_1 + c}{a^2 + b^2}$

Substituting the values $(x_1, y_1) = (5, 0)$ and the line $x - y + 3 = 0$:
$\dfrac{h - 5}{1} = \dfrac{k - 0}{-1} = -2\dfrac{1(5) - 1(0) + 3}{1^2 + (-1)^2}$

$\dfrac{h - 5}{1} = \dfrac{k}{-1} = -2\left(\dfrac{8}{2}\right) = -8$

From this, we get:
$h - 5 = -8 \Rightarrow h = -3$
$k = 8$

The center of the reflected circle is $C'(-3, 8)$ and its radius is $r = 5$.

The equation of the reflected circle is:
$(x + 3)^2 + (y - 8)^2 = 25$

Expanding the equation:
$x^2 + 6x + 9 + y^2 - 16y + 64 = 25$
$x^2 + y^2 + 6x - 16y + 48 = 0$

Comparing this with the given form $x^2 + y^2 + gx + fy + c = 0$, we find:
$g = 6$
$f = -16$
$c = 48$

Therefore, the value of $(g + f + c)$ is:
$g + f + c = 6 - 16 + 48 = 38$

Answer: $38$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If two circles which pass through the points (0, a) and (0, - a) and touch the line y = mx + c, cut orthogonally then
questions.solution:
Let the equation of the circles be
\(x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+d=0 \quad \ldots(1)\)
Since, these circles pass through \((0, a)\) and \((0,-a)\) then
\(a^2+2 f a+d=0 \quad \ldots(2)\)and \(a^2-2 f a+d=0 \quad \ldots(3)\)
Solving (2) and (3), we get
\(\mathrm{f}=0 \text { and } \mathrm{d}=-\mathrm{a}^2 \text {. }\)
Substituting these values of \(f\) and \(d\) in (1), we obtain
\(x^2+y^2+2 g x-a^2=0 \quad \ldots(4)\)
Now, \(\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}\) touch this circle.
Therefore, perpendicular distance from the centre = radius
\(\Rightarrow \frac{|-m g-0+c|}{\sqrt{1+m^2}}=\sqrt{\left(g^2+a^2\right)}\)
Squaring both the sides
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow(c-m g)^2=\left(1+m^2\right)\left(g^2+a^2\right) \\
& \Rightarrow g^2+2 m c g+a^2\left(1+m^2\right)-c^2=0
\end{aligned}\)
Let \(\mathrm{g}_1, \mathrm{~g}_2\) are the roots of this equation
\(g_1 g_2=a^2\left(1+m^2\right)-c^2 \quad \ldots(5)\)
Now, the equation of the two circles represented by (4) are
\(x^2+y^2+2 g_1 x-a^2=0 \text { and } x^2+y^2+2 g_2 x-a^2=0\)
These two circles will cut orthogonal, if
\(\begin{aligned}
& 2 g_1 g_2+0=-a^2-a^2 \\
& \Rightarrow g_1 g_2=-a^2 \quad \ldots(6)
\end{aligned}\)
From (5) and (6),
\(\begin{aligned}
& -a^2=a^2\left(1+m^2\right)-c^2 \\
& \Rightarrow c^2=a^2\left(2+m^2\right)
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Two circle $S_1=p x^2+p y^2+2 g^{\prime} x+2 f^{\prime} y+d=0$ and $S_2=x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+d^{\prime}=0$ have a common chord $P Q$. The equation of $\mathrm{PQ}$ is
questions.solution: $\frac{\mathrm{S}_1}{\mathrm{p}}-\mathrm{S}_2=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{S}_1-\mathrm{pS}_2=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the equation of one tangent to the circle with centre at $(2,-1)$ from the origin is $3 x+y=0$, then the equation of the other tangent through the origin is
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\because \mathrm{CA}=\mathrm{CB} \\ &\Rightarrow \frac{5}{\sqrt{10}}=\left|\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}+1}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{m}^2}}\right| \end{aligned} $$ squaring $$ \frac{25}{10}=\frac{4 m^2+4 m+1}{1+m^2} $$ $$ \Rightarrow 5+5 m^2=8 m^2+8 m+2 \Rightarrow 3 m^2+8 m-3=0 \Rightarrow m=\frac{1}{3},-3 $$ $\therefore$ Equation of tangent $\mathrm{OB}$ is $y=\frac{x}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A straight line meets the co-ordinate axes at $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$. A circle is circumscribed about the triangle $\mathrm{OAB}, \mathrm{O}$ being the origin. If $\mathrm{m}$ and $\mathrm{n}$ are the distances of the tangent to the circle at the origin from the points $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ respectively, the diameter of the circle is
questions.solution: Clearly, AB is one of diameter $\because \mathrm{AM}$ and $\mathrm{BN}$ are parallel and $\angle \mathrm{BNO}=\angle \mathrm{AMO}=\pi / 2$ $\therefore$ Points $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{M}$ are collinear. $\therefore \triangle \mathrm{BNMA}$ is a rectangle $\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{MN}=\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $P$ be a variable point on a circle $C$ and $Q$ be a fixed point outside $C$. If $R$ is the midpoint of the line segment $P Q$, then locus of $R$ is
options: a circle and a pair of straight lines
questions.solution: Let circle be $\left(x-x_{1}\right)=r \cos \theta$ $y-y_{1}=r \sin \theta$ $h=\frac{a+x_{1}+r \cos \theta}{2}$ $k=\frac{b+y_{1}+r \sin \theta}{2}$ $\Rightarrow\left(h-\frac{\left(a+x_{1}\right)}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(k-\frac{\left(b+y_{1}\right)}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{r^{2}}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of circle of radius $\sqrt{17}$ unit, with centre on the positive side of $x$ -axis and through the point $(0,1)$ is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Let centre be $\equiv(a, 0)(a>0)$
So, circle : $(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}=17 ;$ as it passes through $(0,1)$, so $a^{2}+1=17 \quad \Rightarrow a=4(a \neq-4, a>0)$
$\therefore$ Equation is : $x^{2}+y^{2}-8 x-1=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A variable circle passes through the fixed point $A(p, q)$ and touches $X$ -axis. The locus of the other end of the diameter through $A$ is
questions.solution:
In a circle, $A B$ is a diameter where the coordinate of $A$ is $(p, q)$ and let the coordinate of $B\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$
Equation of circle in diameter form is
$$
(x-p)\left(x-x_{1}\right)+(y-q)\left(y-y_{1}\right)=0
$$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}-\left(p+x_{1}\right) x+p x_{1}+y^{2}-\left(y_{1}+q\right) y+q y_{1}=0$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}-\left(p+x_{1}\right) x+y^{2}-\left(y_{1}+q\right) y+p x_{1}+q y_{1}=0$
Since, this circle touches $X$ -axis
$\therefore$
$$
y=0
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}-\left(p+x_{1}\right) x+p x_{1}+q y_{1}=0$
Also, the discriminant of above equation will be equal to zero because circle touches $X$ -axis.
$\therefore \quad\left(p+x_{1}\right)^{2}=4\left(p x_{1}+q y_{1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow p^{2}+x_{1}^{2}+2 p x_{1}=4 p x_{1}+4 q y_{1}$
$\Rightarrow x_{1}^{2}-2 p x_{1}+p^{2}=4 q y_{1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(x_{1}-p\right)^{2}=4 q y_{1}$
Therefore, the locus of point $B$ is
$$
(x-p)^{2}=4 q y
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The angle between a pair of tangents drawn from a point $P$ to the circle
$x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x-6 y+9 \sin ^{2} \alpha+$
$13 \cos ^{2} \alpha=0$ is $2 \alpha .$ The equation of the locus of the point $P$ is
questions.solution: We have equation of circle $x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x-6 y+9 \sin ^{2} \alpha+13 \cos ^{2} \alpha=0$ Here, $\begin{aligned} C &=(-2,3) \\ \text { Radius } &=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(3)^{2}-\left(9 \sin ^{2} \alpha+13 \cos ^{2} \alpha\right)} \\ &=\sqrt{4+9-9 \sin ^{2} \alpha-13 \cos ^{2} \alpha} \\ &=\sqrt{13-13\left(1-\sin ^{2} \alpha\right)-9 \sin ^{2} \alpha} \\=& \sqrt{13 \sin ^{2} \alpha-9 \sin ^{2} \alpha} \\=& \sqrt{4 \sin ^{2} \alpha}=2 \sin \alpha \\ \text { Here, } & \sin \alpha=\frac{A C}{P C} \end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow \quad P C \sin \alpha=A C$ $\Rightarrow \quad P C^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha=A C^{2}=(2 \sin \alpha)^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad\left[\left(h+2)^{2}+(k-3)^{2}\right] \sin ^{2} \alpha=4 \sin ^{2} \alpha\right.$ $\Rightarrow \quad(h+2)^{2}+(k-3)^{2}=4$ $\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}+4+4 h+k^{2}+9-6 k=4$ $\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}+k^{2}+4 h-6 k+9=0$ Hence, locus of a point is $x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x-6 y+9=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If one of the diameter of the circle, given by the equation $x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x+6 y-12=0,$ is a chord of a circle $S,$ whose centre is $(2,-3$ ), the radius of $S$ is
questions.solution: Given, equation of circle is $x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x+6 y-12=0$ Centre $C(-2,-3)$ and radius $=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-3)^{2}+12}=5$ $$ x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x+6 y-12=0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{1} C_{2} &=\sqrt{\left(2+{2})^{2}+(-3+3)^{2}\right.} \\ &=\sqrt{(4)^{2}+(0)^{2}}=4 \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ Radius of circle, $S$ $$ =\sqrt{(4)^{2}+({5})^{2}}=\sqrt{16+25}=\sqrt{41} \text { unit } $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
A chord $A B$ is drawn from the point $A(0,3)$ on
the circle $x^{2}+4 x+(y-3)^{2}=0,$ and is extended to $M$ such that $A M=2 A B$. The locus
of $M$ is
questions.solution: Given, $A M=2 A B$ $\Rightarrow B$ is mid-point of $A M$ $\therefore$ Coordinate of $B$ is $\left(\frac{0+x_{1}}{2}, \frac{3+y_{1}}{2}\right)$ $$ =\left(\frac{x_{1}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+3}{2}\right) $$ since, $B$ lies on the circle $x^{2}+4 x+(y-3)^{2}=0$ $\therefore\left(\frac{x_{1}}{2}\right)^{2}+4\left(\frac{x_{1}}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{y_{1}+3}{2}-3\right)^{2}=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x_{1}^{2}}{4}+2 x_{1}+\left(\frac{y_{1}-3}{2}\right)^{2}=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x_{1}^{2}}{4}+2 x_{1}+\frac{y_{1}^{2}+9-6 y_{1}}{4}=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+8 x_{1}-6 y_{1}+9=0$ Hence, locus of a point is $x^{2}+y^{2}+8 x-6 y+9=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $A$ be the centre of the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-4 y-20=0 .$ Let $B(1,7)$ and
$D(4,-2)$ be two points on the circle such that tangents at $B$ and $D$ meet at $C$. The area of the quadrilateral $A B C D$ is
questions.solution: Given, equation of circle is $$ x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-4 y-20=0 $$ Center $\left(1,2\right).$ and radius $=\sqrt{(1)^{2}+\left(2)^{2}+20\right.}=5$ Coordinate of intersecting point of tangents at $B$ and $D$ is $C(06,7)$ Area of quadrilateral $A B C D$ $$ =2 \times \operatorname{ar}(\Delta A B C) $$ $=2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 15 \times 5=75 \mathrm{sq}$ units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The common chord of the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-4 y=0 \quad$ and $\quad 2 x^{2}+2 y^{2}=32$ subtends at the origin an angle equal to
questions.solution:
Given, equation of circles are $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-4 y=0$ and $2 x^{2}+2 y^{2}=32$
or $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-4 y=0$
and $x^{2}+y^{2}=16$
$\therefore$ Equation of common chord is
$\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-4 y\right)-\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-16\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-4 x-4 y+16=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x+y=4$
This common chord passes through (2,2)
i.e. centre of first circle. Also, (0,0) is at the circumference of the first circle.
$\therefore$ Common chord will subtent $\frac{\pi}{2}$ angle at (0,0)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of the mid-points of the chords of the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}+2 x-2 y-2=0,$ which make an angle of $90^{\circ}$ at the centre is
questions.solution: Given, equation of circle is $x^{2}+y^{2}+2 x-2 y-2=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad(x+1)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=4$ $\therefore$ Centre (-1,1) and radius $=2$ Let $(h, k)$ be the mid-point of chord. From figure, $$ O P=\sqrt{(h+1)^{2}+(k-1)^{2}} $$ $\ln \Delta O A P$ $$ \begin{aligned} \sin 45^{\circ}=& \frac{O P}{O A} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{(h+1)^{2}+(k-1)^{2}}}{2} \end{aligned} $$ On squaring both sides, we get $$ (h+1)^{2}+(k-1)^{2}=2 $$ $\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}+k^{2}+2 h-2 k=0$ $\therefore$ Locus of $P$ will be $$ x^{2}+y^{2}+2 x-2 y=0 $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If one of the diameters of the curve $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-6 y+9=0$ is a chord of a circle with centre $(1,1),$ the radius of this circle is
questions.solution: Given circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-6 y+9=0$ $\therefore$ Centre $=\left(-\frac{1}{2} \times(-4),-\frac{1}{2} \times(-6)\right)$ $=(2.3)$ $\begin{aligned} \text { Radius } &=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-3)^{2}-9} \\ &=\sqrt{4+9-9} \\ &=2 \end{aligned}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad A B &=\sqrt{(2-1)^{2}+(3-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{1+4}=\sqrt{5} \\ \therefore \quad A C &=\sqrt{A B^{2}+B C^{2}}=\sqrt{(\sqrt{5})^{2}+(2)^{2}} \\ &=\sqrt{5+4}=\sqrt{9}=3 \end{aligned} $$ Radius of required circle $=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of a line parallel to the line $3 x+4 y=0$ and touching the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=9$ in the first quadrant, is
questions.solution: Equation of circle is $x^{2}+y^{2}=9$ ...(i) And equation of line is $3 x+4 y=0$ ....(ii) Equation of line parallel to the line (ii) . $=$ $$ \begin{aligned} 3 x+4 y &=k \\ y &=\frac{-3 x}{4}+\frac{k}{4} ...(iii) \end{aligned} $$ $\because$ Circle touches the line (iii), so by the condition of tangency $$ c^{2}=a^{2}\left(1+m^{2}\right) $$ $\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{k}{4}\right)^{2}=9\left[1+\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^{2}\right]$ $\left[\because c=\frac{k}{4}, a=3, m=\frac{-3}{4}\right]$ $\Rightarrow$ $$ k=\pm 15 $$ Hence, $3 x+4 y=15$ touches the circle in the first quadrant.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of the mid-points of chords of the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=1$, which subtends a right angle at the origin, is
questions.solution:
Let $(h, k)$ be the coordinates of the mid-point of a chord which subtends a right angle at the origin. Then, equation of the chord is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\quad h x+h y-1=h^{2}+k^{2}-1 \quad[u \operatorname{sing} T=S] \\
\Rightarrow \quad h x+k y=h^{2}+k^{2}
\end{array}
$$
The combined equation of the pair of lines joining the origin to the points of intersection of $x^{2}+y^{2}=1$ and $h x+k y=h^{2}+k^{2}$ is
$$
x^{2}+y^{2}-1\left(\frac{h x+h y}{h^{2}+k^{2}}\right)^{2}=0
$$
Lines given by the above equation are at right angle. Therefore, coefficient of $x^{2}+$ coefficient of $y^{2}=0$ 
ie. $\quad h^{2}+k^{2}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore x^{2}+y^{2}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angle of intersection between the curves $y=[|\sin x|+|\cos x|]$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}=10,$ where $[x]$ denotes the greatest integer $\leq x,$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $y=\left[|\sin x|+|\cos x|\right.$ land $x^{2}+y^{2}=10$
We know that $(|\sin x|+|\cos x|) \in[1, \sqrt{2}]$
$\therefore$
$$
y=1
$$
The point of intersection of given curve is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& & x^{2}+1^{2} &=10 \\
\Rightarrow & & x^{2} &=9 \\
\Rightarrow & & x &=\pm 3
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Point of intersection is (±3,1) $\begin{array}{lr}\text { Now, } & x^{2}+y^{2}=10 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x} & =0\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x}{y}$
At point (-3,1) $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3}{1}=3 \Rightarrow m_{1}=3$
Slope of line $y=1$ is $m_{2}=0$
$\therefore$ Angle between two curves is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \theta &=\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{1+m_{1} m_{2}}=3 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \theta &=\tan ^{-1}(3)
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$ cuts the three circles $x^{2}+y^{2}-5=0$ $x^{2}+y^{2}-8 x-6 y+10=0$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x+2 y-2=0$ at the extremities of their diameters, then
questions.solution:
Centres and constant terms in the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}-5=0, x^{2}+y^{2}-8 x-6 y+10=0$ and
$x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x+2 y-2=0$ are $C_{1}^{\prime}(0,0), c_{1}=-5$
$C_{2}^{\prime}(4,3), c_{2}=10$ and $C_{3}^{\prime}(2,-1), c_{3}=-2$
Also, centre and constant of circle $x^{2}+y^{2}+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$
is $\quad C_{4}^{\prime}(-g,-f)$ and $c_{4}=c$
since, the first circle intersect all the three at the
extremities of diameter, therefore they are orthogonal to each other.
$\therefore \quad 2\left(g_{1} g_{2}+f_{1} f_{2}\right)=c_{1}+c$
$\therefore \quad 2[-g(0)+(-f)(0)]=c-5$
$\Rightarrow \quad c=5$
$$
2[-g(4)+(-f)(3)]=c+10
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad-2(4 g+3 f)=5+10$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 g+3 f=-\frac{15}{2}$
and $2[-g(2)+(-f)(-1)]=c-2$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2[-2 g+f]=5-2$
$\Rightarrow \quad-2 g+f=\frac{3}{2}$
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $f=-\frac{9}{10}$ and $g=-\frac{12}{10}$
$\therefore \quad f g=\frac{-9}{10} \times-\frac{12}{10}=\frac{27}{25}$
and $\quad 4 f=4 \times \frac{-9}{10}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 f=\frac{-36}{10}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 f=3 g$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A point $P$ lies on the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=169$. If $Q=(5,12)$ and $R=(-12,5),$ then the $\angle Q P R$ is
questions.solution: Given equation of circle is $$ x^{2}+y^{2}=169 $$ Its centre $=(0,0)$ and radius $=13$ Now, slope of $O R=\frac{-5}{12}=m_{1}\left(\because\right.$ slope $\left.=\frac{y_{2}-y}{x_{2}-x}\right)$ and slope of $O Q=\frac{12}{5}=m_{2}$ $\because m_{1} \cdot m_{2}=-1 \Rightarrow \angle R O Q=\frac{\pi}{2}$ We know that, angle made by the chord of circle at circumference is equal to the half of the angle made by the same chord at the centre of circle. $\therefore \quad \angle Q P R=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \angle R O Q=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A point moves, so that the sum of squares of its distance from the points (1,2) and (-2,1) is always $6 .$ Then, its locus is
options: a circle with centre $\left(-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right)$ and radius $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
a parabola with focus (1,2) and directrix passing through (-2,1)
questions.solution:
Let $P$ be any point, whose coordinate is $(h, k)$
Given.

P moves, so that the sum of squares of its distances from the points $A(1,2)$ and $B(-2,1)$ is $6 .$
1.e., $(P A)^{2}+(P B)^{2}=6$
$\Rightarrow \quad(h-1)^{2}+(k-2)^{2}+(h+2)^{2}+(k-1)^{2}=6$
$\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}+1-2 h+k^{2}+4-4 k+h^{2}+4+4 h$
$$
+k^{2}+1-2 k=6
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 h^{2}+2 k^{2}+2 h-6 k+4=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}+k^{2}+h-3 k+2=0$
$\therefore$ Required locus is
$$
x^{2}+y^{2}+x-3 y+2=0
$$
Which represent a circle. Whose centre is $\left(\frac{-1}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right)$ and radius $=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}+\frac{9}{4}-2}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{2}-2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A circle passing through (0,0),(2,6) (6,2) cut the $x$ -axis at the point $P \neq(0,0)$ Then, the lenght of $O P,$ where $O$ is the origin, is
questions.solution:
Let the equation of circle is
$$
x^{2}+y^{2}+2 g x+2 f y+c=0
$$
When, circle (i) passes through the onigin
Then, $\quad c=0$
When, circle (i) passes through the point (2,6). 
Then $4+36+4 g+12 f+0=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 g+12 f+40=0$
$\Rightarrow g+3 f=-10$
When, circle (1) passes through the point (6,2) Then, $36+4+12 g+4 t+0=0$ [from Eq. $\Rightarrow \quad 12 g+4 f+40=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 g+t+10=0$
On solving Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get $g=\frac{-5}{2}$ and $f=\frac{-5}{2}$
$\therefore$ Equation of circle becomes
$$
x^{2}+y^{2}-5 x-5 y=0
$$
Circle cut the $x$ -axis.
So, put $y=0$ in Eq. $(v)$, we get
$$
x^{2}-5 x=0 \Rightarrow x(x-5)=0
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=5$
So, the circle cut the $x$ -axis at point $P(5,0)$
$\therefore$ The length of $O P=5$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If one end of a diameter of the circle $3 x^{2}+3 y^{2}-9 x+6 y+5=0$ is $(1,2),$ then the other end is
questions.solution:
Given equation of circle is
$$
\begin{array}{r}
3 x^{2}+3 y^{2}-9 x+6 y+5=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+y^{2}-3 x+2 y+\frac{5}{3}=0
\end{array}
Centre
=\left(\frac{3}{2},-1\right)
$$
and radius $=\sqrt{\frac{9}{4}+1-\frac{5}{3}}$
$$
=\sqrt{\frac{19}{12}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{19}{3}}
$$
We know that, centre of the circle is the mid-point of the diameter.
Lives one and of point of diameter in (1,2) Let the other end point of diameter is $(h, k)$
Then, $\quad\left(\frac{3}{2},-1\right)=\left(\frac{1+h}{2}, \frac{2+k}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1+h}{2}=\frac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 1+h=3$
$\Rightarrow h=2$
and $\frac{2+k}{2}=-1$
$\Rightarrow 2+k=-2$
$\Rightarrow k=-4$
So, the other end point is (2,-4)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the circle passing through the point (1,1) and the points of intersection of $x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x-8=0$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}-6=0$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\quad S_{1}=x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x-8=0$
and $\quad S_{2}=x^{2}+y^{2}-6=0$
Now, the equation of the circle passing through the point (1,1) and the point of intersection of $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ is
$$
S_{1}+\lambda S_{2}=0
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x-8\right)+\lambda\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-6\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(1+\lambda) x^{2}+(1+\lambda) y^{2}-6 x$
$$
+(-8-6 \lambda)=0 \ldots 0
$$
Since, Eq. (i) passes through the point (1,1)
$\because$ Put $x=1, y=1$ in Eq. (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
(1+\lambda)+(1+\lambda)-6+(-8-6 \lambda)=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad-4 \lambda-12=0
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-3$
On putting the value of ' $\lambda$ ' in Eq. ( 0 , we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
-2 x^{2}-2 y^{2}-6 x+10=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+y^{2}+3 x-5=0
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equations of the circles, which touch both the axes and the line $4 x+3 y=12$ and have centres in the first quadrant, are
questions.solution: Radius $(r)=$ perpendicular distance on line $4 x+3 y=12$ from centre $\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{|4 r+3 r-12|}{\sqrt{16+9}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad|7 r-12|=5 r$ $\Rightarrow \quad 7 r-12=\pm 5 r$ $\therefore \quad 2 r=12 \Rightarrow r=6$ and $12 r=12 \Rightarrow r=1$ (1) When centre is (1,1) and radius is $1,$ then equation of circle is $$ (x-1)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=1 $$ $\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-2 y+1=0$ (ii) When centre is (6,6) and radius is 2 , then equation of circle is $(x-6)^{2}+(y-6)^{2}=36$ $\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+y^{2}-12 x-12 y+36=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}+2 x+2 k y+6=0$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}+2 k y+k=0$ intersect orthogonally. then $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Two circles are orthogonally if and only if $\quad 2\left(g_{1} g_{2}+f_{1} f_{2}\right)=c_{1}+c_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2[(1 \times 0+(k) k]=6+k$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 k^{2}=6+k$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 k^{2}-k-6=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 k^{2}-4 k+3 k-6=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \quad 2 k(k-2)+3(k-2)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(k-2)(2 k+3)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad k=2-\frac{3}{2}$

questions.solution: $x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ $$ \begin{array}{l} (x-2)^{2}+y^{2}=1 \\ C_{1}=(0,0), C_{2}=(2,0) \end{array} $$ On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $$ \begin{aligned} x=\frac{7}{4}, y=\pm \frac{\sqrt{15}}{4} \\ C_{1} N=\frac{7}{4}, P Q=\frac{2 \sqrt{15}}{4} &=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{2} \\ \frac{\text { Area }\left(\Delta C_{1} P Q\right)}{\text { Area }\left(\Delta C_{2} P Q\right)}=& \frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot P Q \cdot C_{1} N}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot P Q \cdot C_{2} N} \\ &=\frac{C_{1} N}{C_{2} N}=\frac{\frac{7}{4}}{2-\frac{7}{4}}=\frac{\frac{7}{4}}{\frac{1}{4}}=7: 1 \end{aligned} $$ Hence, the required option is (c).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The expression $\sum_{k=1}^{32}(3K + 2)\left\{\sum_{r=1}^{10}\left(\sin\dfrac{2r\pi}{11} - i\cos\dfrac{2r\pi}{11}\right)\right\}^{k}$ represents
questions.solution:
Let $S = \sum_{r=1}^{10}\left(\sin\dfrac{2r\pi}{11} - i\cos\dfrac{2r\pi}{11}\right)$

$S = -i\sum_{r=1}^{10}\left(\cos\dfrac{2r\pi}{11} + i\sin\dfrac{2r\pi}{11}\right) = -i\sum_{r=1}^{10}e^{i\dfrac{2r\pi}{11}}$

Since the sum of all 11th roots of unity is zero, $1 + \sum_{r=1}^{10}e^{i\dfrac{2r\pi}{11}} = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{r=1}^{10}e^{i\dfrac{2r\pi}{11}} = -1$

$S = -i(-1) = i$

The given expression becomes $P = \sum_{k=1}^{32}(3k + 2)i^{k}$

We can group the terms into 8 blocks of 4 terms each. For any integer $m \ge 0$, the sum of 4 consecutive terms starting from $k = 4m+1$ is:

$(3(4m+1)+2)i^{4m+1} + (3(4m+2)+2)i^{4m+2} + (3(4m+3)+2)i^{4m+3} + (3(4m+4)+2)i^{4m+4}$

$= (12m+5)i + (12m+8)i^2 + (12m+11)i^3 + (12m+14)i^4$

$= (12m+5)i - (12m+8) - (12m+11)i + (12m+14)$

$= 6 - 6i$

Since there are $32$ terms, there are exactly $8$ such blocks.

$P = 8(6 - 6i) = 48(1 - i)$

Answer: $48(1 - i)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The total number of polynomials of the form $x^3 + ax^2 + bx + c$ which is divisible by $x^2 + 1$, where $a, b, c \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10\}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $P(x) = x^3 + ax^2 + bx + c$.

Since $P(x)$ is divisible by $x^2 + 1$, the roots of $x^2 + 1 = 0$, which are $x = i$ and $x = -i$, must satisfy $P(x) = 0$.

Substituting $x = i$ in $P(x)$, we get:

$P(i) = i^3 + a(i)^2 + b(i) + c = 0$

$-i - a + bi + c = 0$

$(c - a) + (b - 1)i = 0$

Equating the real and imaginary parts to zero, we obtain:

$c - a = 0 \Rightarrow a = c$

$b - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow b = 1$

We are given that $a, b, c \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10\}$.

Since $b = 1$, there is only $1$ choice for $b$.

Since $a = c$, $a$ can take any integer value from $1$ to $10$, giving $10$ choices. The value of $c$ is uniquely determined by $a$.

Therefore, the total number of such polynomials is $10 \times 1 = 10$.

Answer: $10$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $Z_1, Z_2$ be the roots of the equation $Z^2 + pZ + q = 0$, where the coefficients $p$ and $q$ may be complex numbers and also let $A, B$ represent $Z_1, Z_2$ respectively in the complex plane. If $\angle AOB = \alpha \neq 0$ and $OA = OB$, where $O$ is the origin, then the value of $\dfrac{p^2}{q}\sec^2\dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ will be
questions.solution:
Let the roots of the equation be $Z_1$ and $Z_2$.

Since $OA = OB$, we have $|Z_1| = |Z_2| = r$.

Given $\angle AOB = \alpha$, the roots can be related as $Z_2 = Z_1 e^{i\alpha}$.

From the quadratic equation $Z^2 + pZ + q = 0$, the sum and product of the roots are:

$Z_1 + Z_2 = -p$

$Z_1 Z_2 = q$

Evaluating the expression $\dfrac{p^2}{q}$:

$\dfrac{p^2}{q} = \dfrac{(-(Z_1 + Z_2))^2}{Z_1 Z_2} = \dfrac{Z_1^2 + Z_2^2 + 2Z_1 Z_2}{Z_1 Z_2} = \dfrac{Z_1}{Z_2} + \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1} + 2$

Substituting $Z_2 = Z_1 e^{i\alpha}$:

$\dfrac{p^2}{q} = e^{-i\alpha} + e^{i\alpha} + 2$

Using Euler's formula $e^{i\alpha} + e^{-i\alpha} = 2\cos\alpha$:

$\dfrac{p^2}{q} = 2\cos\alpha + 2 = 2(1 + \cos\alpha)$

Using the half-angle identity $1 + \cos\alpha = 2\cos^2\dfrac{\alpha}{2}$:

$\dfrac{p^2}{q} = 4\cos^2\dfrac{\alpha}{2}$

Therefore:

$\dfrac{p^2}{q}\sec^2\dfrac{\alpha}{2} = 4\cos^2\dfrac{\alpha}{2} \sec^2\dfrac{\alpha}{2} = 4$

Answer: $4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z_1, z_2$ are complex numbers such that $\frac{2 z_1}{3 z_2}$ is a purely imaginary number, then the value of $\left|\frac{z_1-z_2}{z_1+z_2}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Given:
\(\frac{2 z_1}{3 z_2}\) is purely imaginary \(\Rightarrow \operatorname{Re}\left(\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right)=0 \Rightarrow \frac{z_1}{z_2}\) is purely imaginary
Let \(\frac{z_1}{z_2}=i k\) for some real \(k\)
Then \(z_1=i k z_2\)
Now compute:
\(\left|\frac{z_1-z_2}{z_1+z_2}\right|=\left|\frac{i k z_2-z_2}{i k z_2+z_2}\right|=\left|\frac{z_2(i k-1)}{z_2(i k+1)}\right|=\left|\frac{i k-1}{i k+1}\right|\)
Let \(z=\frac{i k-1}{i k+1}\)
Now,
\(|z|=\left|\frac{i k-1}{i k+1}\right|=\sqrt{\frac{\left(k^2+1\right)}{\left(k^2+1\right)}}=1\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\omega(\neq 1)$ be a cubic root of unity. Then the minimum value of the set $\left\{\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^2\right|^2 ; a, b, c\right.$ are distinct non-zero integers} equals
questions.solution:
$\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^2\right|^2=a^2+b^2+c^2-a b-b c-c a$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[(a-b)^2+(b-c)^2+(c-a)^2\right] \\
& \therefore \operatorname{Min}=\frac{1+1+4}{2}=3 \quad(a, b, c \in\{1,2,3\})
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\left|Z_1\right|=\left|Z_2\right|=\left|Z_3\right|=1$ and $Z_1+Z_2+Z_3=0$, then the area of the triangle whose vertices are $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3$ is
questions.solution:
Given:
- \(\left|Z_1\right|=\left|Z_2\right|=\left|Z_3\right|=1\)
- \(Z_1+Z_2+Z_3=0\)
Concept:
If three complex numbers lie on the unit circle and their sum is zero, they form an equilateral triangle on the Argand plane.
For equilateral triangle with side \(a=1\), the area is:
\(\text {Area }=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^2=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(1)^2=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\)
But since all sides are of length 1 on the unit circle, and the centroid (origin) divides the medians in 2:1, the full triangle formed by 3 such points has area:
\(\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z_1$ and $z_2$ be two roots of the equation $z^2+a z+b=0, a^2 \lt 4 b$, then the origin, $z_1$ and $z_2$ form an equilateral triangle if
questions.solution: Hint: Here, $z_1+z_2=-a$ and $z_1 z_2=b$ $z_2=z_1 e^{i \pi / 3}=z_1\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right)=z_1\left(\frac{1}{2}+i \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2 z_2-z_1=\sqrt{3} \cdot i z_1 \Rightarrow\left(2 z_2-z_1\right)^2=-3 z_1^2 \Rightarrow 4 z_2^2+4 z_1^2=4 z_1 z_2 \Rightarrow z_1^2+z_2^2=z_1 z_2 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(z_1+z_2\right)^2-2 z_1 z_2=z_1 z_2 \Rightarrow a^2-2 b=b \Rightarrow a^2=3 b\end{aligned}$

questions.q: If the vertices of a square are \(z_1, z_2, z_3\) and \(z_4\) taken in the anti-clockwise order, then \(z_3=\)
questions.solution: Hint : In \(\triangle A B C \frac{z_1-z_2}{z_3-z_2}=\left|\frac{z_1-z_2}{z_3-z_2}\right|\mathrm{i}^{\pi / 2}=\left|\frac{z_1-z_2}{z_3-z_2}\right| \cdot i=\frac{A B}{B C} \cdot i=i\) \(\Rightarrow \mathrm{z}_1-\mathrm{z}_2=\mathrm{i}\left(\mathrm{z}_3-\mathrm{z}_2\right) \Rightarrow-i \mathrm{z}_1+\mathrm{i} \mathrm{z}_2=\mathrm{z}_3-\mathrm{z}_2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{z}_3=-\mathrm{i} \mathrm{z}_1+(\mathrm{i}+1) \mathrm{z}_2=-\mathrm{i} \mathrm{z}_1+(1+\mathrm{i}) \mathrm{z}_2\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Reflection of the line \(\bar{a} z+a \bar{z}=0\) in the real axis is given by
questions.solution:
Hint:
Let, \(a=\alpha+i \beta\) and \(z=x+i y\) \(\begin{aligned} & \therefore \bar{a} z+a \bar{z}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\alpha-i \beta)(x+i y)+(\alpha+i \beta)(x-i y)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(\alpha x+\beta y)=0 \Rightarrow \alpha x+\beta y=0 \text { which passes through origin } \therefore \text { slope }=-\frac{\alpha}{\beta} \end{aligned}\) \(\therefore\) Reflected line's slope \(=\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\) \(\therefore\) Reflected line's equation : \(\alpha x -\beta y=0\) \(\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{a+\bar{a}}{2}\right) x-\left(\frac{a-\bar{a}}{2 i}\right) y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{a+\bar{a}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{z+\bar{z}}{2}\right)-\left(\frac{a-\bar{a}}{2 i}\right)\left(\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a z+\bar{a} \bar{z}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a z+\bar{a} z=0 \end{aligned}\)

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $|z-25 i| \leq 15$, then Maximum $\arg (z)-$ Minimum $\arg (z)$ is equal to
questions.solution: $$ \because \cos \theta=\frac{15}{25}=\frac{3}{5} $$ $\therefore \operatorname{Min} \arg (z)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)$ $\operatorname{Max} \arg (z)=\pi-\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)$ $\therefore$ difference $=\frac{\pi}{2}+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)-\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)=2 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)=2 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)$

questions.q: If $z=x$ - iy and $z^{1 / 3}=p+$ iq $(x, y, p, q \in \mathbb{R})$, then $\frac{\left(\frac{x}{p}+\frac{y}{q}\right)}{\left(p^2+q^2\right)}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$z=p^3-3 p q^2+i\left(3 p^2 q-q^3\right)=x-i y$
$\begin{aligned}
&x=p\left(p^2-3 q^2\right) \\
&y=-q\left(3 p^2-q^2\right) \\
&\therefore \frac{x}{p}+\frac{y}{q}=p^2-3 q^2-3 p^2+q^2=-2\left(p^2+q^2\right)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The side $A B$ of $\triangle A B C$ is fixed and is of length $2 a$ unit. The vertex moves in the plane such that the vertical angle is always constant and is $\alpha$. Let $x$-axis be along $A B$ and the origin be at $A$. Then the locus of the vertex is
questions.solution: Let, $c=z=x+i y$ $\begin{aligned} &\arg \left(\frac{2 \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{z}}{0-\mathrm{z}}\right)=\alpha \\ &\Rightarrow \arg \left(\frac{(\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{a})+\mathrm{iy}}{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{iy}}\right)=\alpha \\ &\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2-2 \mathrm{ax}-2 \mathrm{ay} \cot \alpha=0 \end{aligned}$

Principal value of $\arg \left(z_1 / z_2\right)=$ Principal value of $\arg z_1-$ Principal value of $\operatorname{argz} z_2$
Principal value of $\arg \left(z_1 / z_2\right)$ may not be $\arg z_1-\arg z_2$
questions.solution:
$\arg \left(z_1 z_2\right)=\operatorname{argz_{1}}+\operatorname{argz_{2}}+2 k \pi$
$\arg \left(z_1 / z_2\right)=\operatorname{argz_{1}}-\operatorname{argz_{2}}+2 \mathrm{k} \pi$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general value of the real angle $\theta$, which satisfies the equation. $(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta)(\cos 2 \theta+i \sin 2 \theta) \ldots$$(\cos n \theta+t \sin n \theta)=1$ is given by, (assuming $k$ is an integer)
questions.solution:
We have,
$(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)(cos2\theta+isin2\theta)...(cosn\theta+isinn\theta) = 1$
$\Rightarrow e^{i\theta}.e^{i(2\theta)}.e^{i(3\theta)}. ...e^{i(n\theta)} = 1$
$\Rightarrow e^{i\theta(1+2+3+...+n}=1$
$\Rightarrow e^{\frac{in(n+1)\theta}{2}}=1$
$\Rightarrow cos(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\theta)+isin(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\theta = 1 + 0i$
$\Rightarrow cos(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\theta)=1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\theta = 2k\pi$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{4k}{n(n+1)}\pi$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For any non-zero complex number $z$, the minimum value of $|z|+|z-1|$ is
questions.solution:
$|z|+|z-1| a$ is complex number
$$
\begin{array}{c}
|z|+|-(1-z)| \quad|a|=|-a| \\
|z|+|1-z| \quad\{\because|a|=|-a|\} \\
|z|+|1-z| \geq|z+1-z| \\
|z|+|1-z| \geq|1| \\
|z|+|1-z| \geq 1 \\
|z|+|-(z-1)| \geq 1 \\
|z|+|z-1| \geq 1
\end{array}
$$
Minimum value of $|z|+|z-1|=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\theta \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\frac{1-i \cos \theta}{1+2 i \cos \theta}$ is real number, then $\theta$ will be (when $I:$ Set of integers)
questions.solution:
Let $Z=\frac{1-i \cos \theta}{1+2 i \cos \theta}$
$\therefore \quad \bar{z}=\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta}$
Since, $z$ is a real number, then $z-\bar{z}=0$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1-i \cos \theta}{1+2 i \cos \theta}=\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta}$
$\Rightarrow(1-i \cos \theta)(1-2 i \cos \theta)=(1+i \cos \theta)(1+2 i \cos \theta)$
$\Rightarrow 1-2 i \cos \theta-i \cos \theta-2 \cos ^{2} \theta$ $=1+2 i \cos \theta+i \cos \theta-2 \cos ^{2} \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad 6 i \cos \theta=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \theta=(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{2}, n \in I$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $Z_{r}=\sin \frac{2 \pi}{11}-i \cos \frac{2 \pi}{11},$ then $\sum_{r=0}^{10} Z_{r}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $Z,=\sin \frac{2 \pi r}{11}-i \cos \frac{2 \pi}{11}$
$$
=-i\left(\cos \frac{2 \pi r}{11}+i \sin \frac{2 \pi r}{11}\right)
$$
$\therefore$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=-i e^{\frac{i 2 \pi}{11}} \\
\sum_{r=0}^{10} Z_{r} &=-i \sum_{r=0}^{10} e^{\frac{i 2 \pi}{11}}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
=-i \times 0=0
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$ be two non-zero complex numbers such that $\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}+\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}=1,$ then the origin and the points represented by $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$
questions.solution:
We know that, if $z_{1}, z_{2}$ and $z_{3}$ are the vertices of an equilateral triangle. Then,
$$
z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}+z_{3}^{2}-z_{1} z_{2}-z_{2} z_{3}-z_{3} z_{1}=0
$$
Now, but we have
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}+\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}} &=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2} &=z_{1} z_{2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}-z_{1} z_{2} &=0 \\
z_{3} &=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, given points form an equilateral triangle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$ be complex numbers such that $z_{1} \neq z_{2}$ and $\left|z_{1}\right|=\left|z_{2}\right| .$ If $\operatorname{Re}\left(z_{1}\right)>0$ and
$\operatorname{Im}\left(z_{2}\right)    < 0,$ then $\frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $z_{1}=x_{1}+i y_{1}$ and $z_{2}=x_{2}+i y_{2}$
$\operatorname{Re}\left(z_{1}\right)>0 \Rightarrow x_{1}>0$
$\begin{array}{lr}\text { and } & \operatorname{Im}\left(z_{2}\right)    < 0 \\ \Rightarrow & y_{2} < 0 \\ \text { Given, } & \left|z_{1}\right|=\left|z_{2}\right|\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left|z_{1}\right|^{2}=\left|z_{2}^{2}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad z_{1} \bar{z}_{1}=z_{2} \bar{z}_{2}$
Now, $\left(\frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}\right)+\left(\frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}\right)$
$=\left(\frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}\right)+\left(\frac{\bar{z}_{1}+\bar{z}_{2}}{\bar{z}_{1}-\bar{z}_{2}}\right)$
$=\frac{z_{1} \bar{z}_{1}+z_{2} \bar{z}_{1}-z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}-z_{2} \bar{z}_{2}+z_{1} \bar{z}_{1}+z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}-z_{2} \bar{z}_{1}+z_{2} \bar{z}_{2}}{\left(z_{1}-z_{2}\right)\left(\bar{z}_{1}-\bar{z}_{2}\right)}$
$=\frac{2\left(\left|z_{1}\right|^{2}-\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}\right)}{\left(z_{1}-z_{2}\right)\left(\bar{z}_{1}-\bar{z}_{2}\right)}=0 \quad\left(\because\left|z_{1}\right|^{2}=\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}\right)$
$=\frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}$ is purely imaginary.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The expression $\frac{(1+i)^{n}}{(1-i)^{n-2}}$ equals

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $z=x+i y,$ where $x$ and $y$ are real. The points $(x, y)$ in the $X-Y$ plane for which $\frac{z+i}{z-i}$
is purely imaginary, lie on
questions.solution:
$\frac{z+i}{z-i}=\frac{(x+j)+i}{(x+i y)-i}$
$=\frac{x+(1+y) i}{x+(y-1) i}$
$=\frac{x+(y+1) i}{x+(y-1) i} \times \frac{x-(y-1) i}{x-(y-1) i}$
$=\frac{x^{2}-x(y-1) i+x(y+1) i-(y+1)(y-1) i^{2}}{x^{2}-(y-1)^{2} i^{2}}$
$=\frac{x^{2}+i[-x y+x+x y+x]+\left(y^{2}-1\right)}{x^{2}+(y-1)^{2}}$
$=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-1}{x^{2}+(y-1)^{2}}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+(y-1)^{2}} i$
Now, $\frac{z+i}{z-i}$ is purely imaginary.
$\therefore$
$\operatorname{Re}\left(\frac{z+i}{z-i}\right)=0$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow & \frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-1}{x^{2}+(y-1)^{2}}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x^{2}+y^{2}=1\end{array}$
$\therefore(x, y)$ lies on a circle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of $x^{2}+x+1=0 .$ If $n$ be a positive integer, then $\alpha^{n}+\beta^{n}$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $x^{2}+x+1=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{3} i}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\frac{-1+\sqrt{3} i}{2}$ and $\beta=\frac{-1-\sqrt{3} i}{2}$
or $\quad \alpha=e^{\frac{i2\pi}{3}}$ and $\beta=e^{\frac{-2\pi i}{3}}$
$\therefore$
$\alpha^{n}+\beta^{n}=e^{\frac{2 n i t}{3}}+e^{\frac{-2\pi i}{3}}$
$=2\left(\frac{e^{\frac{2n\pi i}{3}}+e^{\frac{-2n\pi i}{3}}}{2}\right)$
$=2 \cos \left(\frac{2 n \pi}{3}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The complex number $z$ satisfying the equation $|z-i|=|z+1|=1$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $|z-i|=|z+1|=1$
Let, $\quad z=x+iy$
$\therefore \quad|z-i|=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad|x+iy-i \mid=1$
$\Rightarrow|x+(y-1) i|=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=1$
Also, $\quad|z+1|=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad|x+ iy+1 \mid=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad|(x+1)+i y|=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}=1$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $x=y=0$ and $x=-1, y=1$
$\therefore \quad z=0,-1+i$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\sum_{n=1}^{13}\left(i^{n}+i^{n+1}\right), i=\sqrt{-1}$ is
questions.solution:
We have. 
$\begin{aligned} & \sum_{n=1}^{13}\left(i^{n}+i^{n+1}\right) \\=& \sum_{n=1}^{13} i^{n}+\sum_{n=1}^{13} i^{n+1} \\=& i \left(\frac{1-i^{13}}{1-i}\right)+i^{2}\left(\frac{1-i^{13}}{1-i}\right) \\=& i \left(\frac{1-i}{1-i}\right)-\left(\frac{1-i}{1-i}\right) \\=& i-1 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $z_{1}, z_{2}, z_{3}$ are imaginary numbers such that $\left|z_{1}\right|=\left|z_{2}\right|=\left|z_{3}\right|=\left|\frac{1}{z_{1}}+\frac{1}{z_{2}}+\frac{1}{z_{3}}\right|=1,$ then
$\left|z_{1}+z_{2}+z_{3}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $\left|z_{1}\right|=\left|z_{2}\right|=\left|z_{3}\right|=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left|z_{1}\right|^{2}=\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}=\left|z_{3}\right|^{2}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \quad z_{1} \bar{z}_{1}=z_{2} \bar{z}_{2}=z_{3} \bar{z}_{3}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{z_{1}}=\bar{z}_{1} \frac{1}{z_{2}}=\bar{z}_{2} \frac{1}{z_{3}}=\bar{z}_{3}$
Now. $\quad\left|\frac{1}{z_{1}}+\frac{1}{z_{2}}+\frac{1}{z_{3}}\right|=1$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \left|\bar{z}_{1}+\bar{z}_{2}+\bar{z}_{3}\right|=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \left|\overline{z_{1}+z_{2}+z_{3}}\right|=1 \\ \therefore & \left|z_{1}+z_{2}+z_{j}\right|=1\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $1+{ }^{n} C_{1} \cos \theta+{ }^{n} C_{2} \cos 2 \theta+\ldots+{ }^{n} C_{n} \cos n \theta$ equals
questions.solution:
Given, $1+{ }^{n} C_{1} \cos \theta+{ }^{n} C_{2} \cos 2 \theta+\ldots+{ }^{n} C_{n} \cos n \theta$
Which is real part of complex number. $\left({ }^{n} C_{0}+{ }^{n} C_{1} e^{i\theta}+\ldots\right)$
ie. $\operatorname{Re}\left({ }^{n} \mathrm{C}_{0}+{ }^{n} \mathrm{C}_{1} e^{i\theta}+\ldots\right)$
$=\operatorname{Re}\left(1+e^{\theta i}\right)^{n}=\operatorname{Ro}(1+\cos \theta+i \sin \theta)^{n}$
$=$ Re $\left(2 \cos ^{2} n \frac{\theta}{2}+i 2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
$\left[\begin{array}{c}\because 1+\cos \theta=2 \cos ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2} \text { and } \\ \sin \theta=2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\end{array}\right]$
$=\left(2 \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)^{n} \operatorname{Re}\left(\cos \frac{\theta}{2}+i \sin \frac{\theta}{2}\right)^{n}$
$=\left(2 \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)^{n} \operatorname{Re}\left(\cos \frac{n \theta}{2}+i \sin \frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
[by De moivre's theorem] 
$=\left(2 \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)^{n} \cdot \cos \frac{n \theta}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z=\sin \theta-i \cos \theta,$ then for any integer $n$
questions.solution:
Given, $z=\sin \theta-i \cos \theta$
$=\cos \left(\theta-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)+i \sin \left(\theta-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=e^{i(\theta-\frac{\pi}{2}).}$
Now. $z^{n}=e^{i(n\theta-\frac{n\pi}{2}).}=\cos \left(n \theta-\frac{n \pi}{2}\right)+i \sin \left(n \theta-\frac{n \pi}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{z^{n}}=e^{i\left(\frac{n \pi}{2}-n\theta\right)}=\cos \left(n \theta-\frac{n \pi}{2}\right)-i \sin \left(n \theta-\frac{n \pi}{2}\right)$
$\therefore \quad z^{n}+\frac{1}{z^{n}}=2 \cos \left(n \theta-\frac{n \pi}{2}\right)=2 \cos \left(\frac{n \pi}{2}-n \theta\right)$
and $z^{n}-\frac{1}{z^{n}}=2 i \sin \left(n \theta-\frac{n \pi}{2}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{3} i}{1-\sqrt{3 i}}\right)^{64}+\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{3} i}{1+\sqrt{3} i}\right)^{64}$ is
questions.solution:
We know that, $\omega=\frac{-1+\sqrt{3} i}{2} 1-\sqrt{3} i=-2 \omega$
and $\omega^{2}=\frac{-1-\sqrt{3} i}{2} 1+\sqrt{3} i=-2 \omega^{2}$
Now, $\begin{aligned}\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{3} i}{1-\sqrt{3 i}}\right)^{64}+\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{3} i}{1+\sqrt{3} i}\right)^{64} \\ &=\left(\frac{-2 \omega^{2}}{-2 \omega}\right)^{64}+\left(\frac{-2 \omega}{-2 \omega^{2}}\right)^{64} \end{aligned}$
$=\omega^{64}+\frac{1}{\omega^{64}}=\omega+\omega^{2}$
$$
\left[\because \cdot \omega^{3}=1\right]
$$
$$
\left.=-1 \quad \mid \because 1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0\right]
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Find the maximum value of $|z|$ when $\left|z-\frac{3}{z}\right|=2,$ where $z$ being a complex number.
questions.solution:
We have, $|z|=\left|z-\frac{3}{z}+\frac{3}{z}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad|z| \leq\left|z-\frac{3}{z}\right|+\left|\frac{3}{z}\right|$
[using triangle inequality] $\Rightarrow \quad|z| \leq 2+\left|\frac{3}{z}\right|$
$\left[\because\left|z-\frac{3}{z}\right|=2\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad|z| \leq 2+\frac{3}{|z|}$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow & |z|^{2}-2|z|-3 \leq 0 \\ \Rightarrow & (|z|-3)(|z|+1) \leq 0\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \quad-1 \leq|z| \leq 3$
Thus, the maximum value of $|z|$ is 3 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $|z|^{2}+|z-3|^{2}+|z-i|^{2}$ is minimum when $z$ equals
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$
$\therefore|z|^{2}+|z-3|^{2}+|z-i|^{2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
=|x+i y|^{2}+\mid &\mid x-3)+\left.i y\right|^{2} \\
&+|x+i(y-1)|^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
$=x^{2}+y^{2}+(x-3)^{2}+y^{2}+x^{2}+(y-1)^{2}$
$=x^{2}+y^{2}+x^{2}-6 x+9+y^{2}+x^{2}+y^{2}$
$=3 x^{2}+3 y^{2}-6 x-2 y+10$
$=3\left(x^{2}-2 x+1\right)+3\left(y^{2}-\frac{2}{3} y+\frac{1}{9}\right)$
$\quad=3(x-1)^{2}+3\left(y-\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2}+\frac{20}{3}$
It is minimum, when $x-1=0$ and $y-\frac{1}{3}=0$
$\therefore \quad x=1 \quad$ and $\quad y=\frac{1}{3}$
$\therefore \quad z=1+\frac{1}{3} i$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $z_{1}, z_{2}$ be two fixed complex numbers in the argand plane and $z$ be an arbitrary point satisfying $\left|z-z_{1}\right|+\left|z-z_{2}\right|=2\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right|$. Then, the locus of $z$ will be
options: a bisector of the line segment joining $z_1$ and $z_2$
questions.solution:
We know that $\left|z-z_{1}\right|+\left|z-z_{2}\right|=k$ will represent an ellipse, if $\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right|    < k$
Hence. the equation $\left|z-z_{1}\right|+\left|z-z_{2}\right|$ $=2\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right|$ represent an ellipse.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $z_{1}$ be a fixed point on the circle of radius 1 centred at the origin in the argand plane and $z_{1} \neq \pm 1 .$ Consider an equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle with $z_{1}, z_{2}, z_{3}$ as the
direction. Then, $z_{1} z_{2} z_{3}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $z_{1} \neq\pm 1$ 
Since, $z_{2}$ and $z_{3}$ can be obtained by rotating
vector representing through $\frac{2 \pi}{3}$ and $\frac{4 \pi}{3}$
respectively
$\therefore \quad z_{2}=z_{1} \omega$
and
$$
z_{3}=z_{1} \omega^{2}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad z, z_{2} z_{3} &=z_{1} \times z_{1} \omega \times z_{1} \omega^{2} \\
&=z_{1}^{3} \omega^{3} \\
&=z_{1}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Suppose that $z_{1}, z_{2}, z_{3}$ are three vertices of an equilateral triangle in the angand plane. Let $\alpha=\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{3}+i)$ and $\beta$ be a non-zero complex number. The points $a z_{1}+\beta, \alpha z_{2}+\beta, \alpha z_{3}+\beta$ will be
options: the vertices of an equilateral triangle
the vertices of an isosceles triangle
the vertices of a scalene triangle
questions.solution:
Since, $z_{1}, z_{2}$ and $z_{3}$ are the vertices of an
equilateral triangle, therefore
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right| &=\left|z_{2}-z_{3}\right| \\
&=\left|z_{3}-z_{1}\right|=k \\
\alpha &=\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{3}+i)
\end{aligned}
$$
Also,
$\Rightarrow \quad|\alpha|=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{3+1}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2=1$
Let $A=\alpha z_{1}+\beta, B=\alpha z_{2}+\beta$
and
$$
C=\alpha z_{3}+\beta
$$
Now. $|A B|=\left|\alpha z_{2}+\beta-\left(\alpha z_{1}+\beta\right)\right|$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\left|\alpha\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right)\right| \\
&=|\alpha|\left|z_{2}-z_{1}\right| \\
&=|1|\left|z_{2}-z_{1}\right| \\
&=1\left|z_{2}-z_{1}\right| \\
&=\left|z_{2}-z_{1}\right|=k \\
\text { Similarly, } & B C=C A=k
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, the points $\alpha z_{1}+\beta, \alpha z_{2}+\beta$ and $\alpha z_{3}+\beta$
are the vertices of an equilateral triangle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In the argand plane, the distinct roots of $1+z+z^{3}+z^{4}=0(z$ is a complex number) represent vertices of
questions.solution:
Given equation is
$1+z+z^{3}+z^{4}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(1+z)+z^{3}(1+z)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(1+z)\left(1+z^{3}\right)=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad z=-1, z^{3}=-1$
$\Rightarrow \quad z=-1, z=-1,-\omega-\omega^{2}$
Hence, these roots are the vertices of an equilateral triangle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\alpha, \beta$ denote the cube roots of unity other than 1 and $\alpha \neq \beta .$ Let $S=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{n}\left(\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)^{n} .$ Then the value of $S$ is
questions.solution:
Case I Let $\alpha=\omega$ and $\beta=\omega^{2}$
$S=\sum_{n=0}^{302}(-1)^{n}\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega^{2}}\right)^{n}$
$=\sum_{n=0}^{302}(-1)^{n}\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{n}$
$=1-\omega^{2}+\omega^{4}-\omega^{6}+\omega^{8}-\omega^{10}+\omega^{12}$
$\quad+\ldots+\omega^{600}-\omega^{602}+\omega^{604}$
$=1-\omega^{2}+\omega-1+\omega^{2}-\omega+1+\ldots$
$=0+\ldots+1-\omega^{2}+\omega$
$=-\omega^{2}-\omega^{2}=-2 \omega^{2} \quad\left[\because 1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0\right]$
Case II Let $\alpha=\omega^{2}$ and $\beta=\omega$
$\therefore S=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty_{2}}(-1)^{n}\left(\frac{\omega^{2}}{\omega}\right)^{n}$
$=\sum_{n=0}^{302}(-1)^{n}\left(\frac{\omega^{4}}{\omega^{3}}\right)^{n}$
$=\sum_{n=0}^{302}(-1)^{n}(\omega)$
$=1-\omega+\omega^{2}-\omega^{3}+\omega^{4}-\omega^{5}+\omega^{6}-\ldots$
$+\omega^{300}-\omega^{301}+\omega^{302}$
$=1-\omega+\omega^{2}-1+\omega-\omega^{2}+1$
$=0+\ldots+1+\omega^{2}-\omega$
$=-\omega-\omega=-2 \omega$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $z_{1}=2+3 i$ and $z_{2}=3+4 i$ be two points on the complex plane. Then, the set of complex number $z$ satisfying $\left|z-z_{1}\right|^{2}+\left|z-z_{2}\right|^{2}$
$=\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right|^{2}$ represents
questions.solution:
Given, $z_{1}=2+3 i$ and $z_{2}=3+4 i$
Now, we have $\left|z-z_{1}\right|^{2}+\left|z-z_{2}\right|^{2}=\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right|^{2}$
(let $z=x+i y)$
$\Rightarrow|(x+i y)-(2+3 i)|^{2}+|(x+i y)-(3+4 i)|^{2}$
$\quad=|(2+3 i)-(3+4 i)|^{2}$
$\Rightarrow|(x-2)+i(y-3)|^{2}+|(x-3)+i(y-4)|^{2}$
$\quad=|-1-i|^{2}$
$\Rightarrow(x-2)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}+(x-3)^{2}+(y-4)^{2}=1+1$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+4-4 x+y^{2}+9-6 y$
$\quad+x^{2}+9-6 x+y^{2}+16-8 y=2$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x^{2}+2 y^{2}-10 x-14 y+36=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+y^{2}-5 x-7 y+18=0$
which represent a circle with centre $\left(\frac{5}{2}, \frac{7}{2}\right)$ and radius $\sqrt{\frac{25}{4}+\frac{49}{4}-18}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are roots of $x^{2}-x+1=0,$ then the value of $\alpha^{2013}+\beta^{2013}$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation is $x^{2}-x+1=0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1-4}}{2} \quad\left(\because x=\frac{b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}\right) \\
&=\frac{1 \pm i \sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{2} \\
\Rightarrow-x &=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{-1-i \sqrt{3}}{2} \\
\Rightarrow+x &=\omega,-\omega^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
Since, $(\alpha, \beta)$ are the roots of given equation. Then, $\alpha=-\omega$ and $\beta=-\omega^{2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \alpha^{2013}+\beta^{2013} &=-(\omega)^{2013}+\left(-\omega^{2}\right)^{2013} \\
&=-\omega^{2013}-\omega^{4026} \\
&=-\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{671}-\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{1342} \\
&=-(1)^{671}-(1)^{1342} \\
&=-1-1=-2
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z=x+i y,$ where $x$ and $y$ are real numbers and $i=\sqrt{-1}$, then the points $(x, y)$ for which $\frac{z-1}{z-i}$ is real, lie on
questions.solution:
Given, $z=x+i y$
Now, $\frac{z-1}{z-i}=\frac{(x+y)-1}{(x+i y)-i}$
$=\frac{(x-1)+i y}{x+i(y-1)} \times \frac{x-i(y-1)}{x-i(y-1)}$
$=\frac{x(x-1)+i x y-i(x-1)(y-1)+y(y-1)}{x^{2}+(y-1)^{2}}$
$=\frac{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-x-y\right)+i(x y-x y+y+x-1)}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}+1^{-2 y}\right)}$
$=\left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-x-y}{x^{2}+y^{2}-2 y+1}\right)+i\left(\frac{x+y-1}{x^{2}+y^{2}-2 y+1}\right) \ldots(0)$
Given, $\frac{z-1}{z-i}$ is real.
So, its imaginary part should be zero. i.e.. $\quad \frac{x+y-1}{x^{2}+y^{2}-2 y+1}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x+y=1$
$\left(\because x^{2}+y^{2}-2 y+1 \neq 0\right)$
Which represent a straight line.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $P, Q$ and $R$ are angles of an isosceles triangle and $\angle P=\frac{\pi}{2},$ then the value of $\left(\cos \frac{P}{3}-i \sin \frac{P}{3}\right)^{3}+(\cos Q+i \sin Q) \\ (\cos R-i \sin R)+(\cos P-i \sin P) \\ (\cos Q-i \sin Q)(\cos R-i \sin R) \text { is }$
questions.solution:
Given that, $P, Q$ and $R$ are angles of an isosceles triangle and $\angle P=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\therefore \quad Q=R=\frac{\pi}{4}\left(\because P+Q+R=180^{\circ}\right)$
Now, $\left(\cos \frac{P}{3}-i \sin \frac{P}{3}\right)^{3}+(\cos Q+i \sin Q)$
$(\cos R-i \sin R)$
$+(\cos P-i \sin P)(\cos Q-i \sin Q)$
$\quad(\cos R-i \sin R)$
$=\left(e^{-i \frac{P}{3}}\right)^{3}+e^{i Q} \cdot e^{-i R}+e^{-i \rho} e^{-i Q} \cdot e^{-i R}$
$\left(\because \cos \theta+i \sin \theta=e^{i \theta}\right)$
$=e^{-i P}+e^{\gamma Q-R)}+e^{-i(P+Q+R)}$
$=e^{-i \pi / 2}+e^{(0)}+e^{-i \pi} \quad\left(Q=R=\frac{\pi}{2} P=\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$=-i+1+(-1)=-i$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The maximum value of $|x|$, when the complex number $z$ satisfies the condition $\left|z+\frac{2}{z}\right|=2$ is
questions.solution:
We have the identity,
$$
\begin{aligned}|z|=\left|z+\frac{2}{z}-\frac{2}{z}\right| \leq\left|z+\frac{2}{z}\right|+\left|\frac{2}{z}\right| \\ \Rightarrow \quad|z| \leq 2+\frac{2}{|z|} \end{aligned}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad|z|^{2} \leq 2|z|+2$
$\Rightarrow \quad|z|^{2}-2|z|+1 \leq 3$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & (|z|-1)^{2} \leq 3 \\
\Rightarrow & -\sqrt{3} \leq|z|-1 \leq \sqrt{3} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$\Rightarrow \quad-\sqrt{3}+1 \leq|z| \leq \sqrt{3}+1$
Hence, the maximum value $=\sqrt{3}+1$

questions.q:
If $\left(\frac{3}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{50}=3^{25}(x+i y)$ where $x$ and $y$
are real, then the ordered pair $(x, y)$ is
questions.solution:
Let $z=\frac{3}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\therefore$
$r=\sqrt{\frac{9}{4}+\frac{3}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{12}{4}}=\sqrt{3}$
$\left.\begin{array}{rl}\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right. \\ \frac{3}{2}\end{array}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6}$
$\frac{3}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}=\sqrt{3} e^{\frac{i \pi}{6}}$
$\therefore$
$\left(\frac{3}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{50}=\left(\sqrt{3} e^{\frac{i \pi}{6}}\right)^{50}$
$=(\sqrt{3})^{50}\left(e^{\frac{i \pi}{6}}\right)^{50}=3^{25} e^{\frac{50 \pi}{6}}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{3}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{50} &=3^{25} e^{\frac{i 25 \pi}{3}} \\ &=3^{25}\left(\cos \frac{25 \pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{25 \pi}{3}\right) \\ &=3^{25}(\cos 1500+i \sin 1500) \end{aligned}$
$=3^{25}[\cos (360 \times 4+60)+i \sin (360$
$\times 4+60)]$
$=3^{25}(\cos 60+i \sin 60)$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{3}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{50}=3^{25}\left(\frac{1}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$
According to question, $\left(\frac{3}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{50}=3^{25}(x+i y)$
$3^{25}\left(\frac{1}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=3^{25}(x+6 y)$
which is true only when $x=\frac{1}{2}, y=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\frac{z-1}{z+1}$ is purely imaginary, then
questions.solution:
Let $\omega=\frac{z-1}{z+1}$
Then, using componendo and dividendo, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\quad z=\frac{1+\omega}{1-\omega} \\
\Rightarrow \quad|z|=\left|\frac{\omega+1}{\omega-1}\right| \\
\Rightarrow \quad|z|=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad|\omega+1|=|\omega-1|
\end{array}
$$
Let $6=u+i v$
Then, $|\omega+1|=|u+i v+1|$
$$
=|(u+1)+i v|=\sqrt{(u+1)^{2}+v^{2}}
$$
and $|\omega-1|=\sqrt{(t u-1)^{2}+v^{2}}$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (i)
$$
\begin{array}{rlr} 
& \sqrt{(u+1)^{2}+v^{2}}=\sqrt{(u-1)^{2}+v^{2}} \\
\Rightarrow & (u+1)^{2}+v^{2} & =(v-1)^{2}+v^{2} \\
\Rightarrow & u=0
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore \omega=\frac{z-1}{z+1}$ is a pure imaginary number.

questions.q: The points representing the complex number z for which arg $\left(\frac{z-2}{z+2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}$ lie on
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$ $\therefore \quad \frac{z-2}{z+2}=\frac{x+iy-2}{x+i y+2}$
$=\frac{(x-2)+i y}{(x+2)+6 y} \times \frac{(x+2)-i y}{(x+2)-i y}$
$=\frac{(x-2)(x+2)+i y(x+2)-i y(x-2)-i^{2} y^{2}}{(x+2)^{2}-(i y)^{2}}$
$=\frac{x^{2}-4+i x y+2 i y-i x y+2 i y+y^{2}}{(x+2)^{2}+y^{2}}$
$x^{2}+y^{2}-4-\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} y=0,$ which represents a
circle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For a real number $y$, consider $[y]$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $y$. If $f(x) = \dfrac{\tan(\pi[x - \pi])}{1 + [x]^2}$, then
questions.solution:
Given the function $f(x) = \dfrac{\tan(\pi[x - \pi])}{1 + [x]^2}$.

We know that for any real number $x$, the value of $[x - \pi]$ is always an integer. Let $[x - \pi] = k$, where $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

The numerator of the function becomes $\tan(k\pi)$. Since the tangent of any integer multiple of $\pi$ is exactly zero, we have $\tan(k\pi) = 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

For the denominator, since $[x]$ is an integer, $[x]^2 \ge 0$, which implies $1 + [x]^2 \ge 1$. Thus, the denominator is never zero and is well-defined for all real $x$.

Therefore, the function simplifies to:

$f(x) = \dfrac{0}{1 + [x]^2} = 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

Since $f(x)$ is a constant function, its derivative is zero everywhere:

$f'(x) = 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

Hence, $f'(x)$ exists for all $x$.

Answer: $f'(x)$ exists for all $x$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)=|1-2 x|$, then
options: $f(x)$ is continuous but not differentiable at $x=\frac{1}{2}$
$f(x)$ is differentiable but not continuous at $x=\frac{1}{2}$
$f(x)$ is both continuous and differentiable at $x=\frac{1}{2}$
$f(x)$ is neither differentiable nor continuous at $x=\frac{1}{2}$
questions.solution:
The absolute value function can be rewritten as a piecewise function:
\(f(x)= \begin{cases}1-2 x, & \text { if } x \leq \frac{1}{2} \\ -(1-2 x), & \text { if } x>\frac{1}{2}\end{cases}\)
2. Check for continuity at \(x=\frac{1}{2}\) :
Left-hand limit: \(\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}}(1-2 x)=1-2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=0\)
Right-hand limit: \(\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}}-(1-2 x)=-\left(1-2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)=0\)
Function value: \(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\left|1-2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right|=|1-1|=0\)
Since the left-hand limit, right-hand limit, and function value are all equal, the function is continuous at \(x=\frac{1}{2}\).
3. Check for differentiability at \(x=\frac{1}{2}\) :
Left-hand derivative: \(f^{\prime}(x)=-2\) for \(x<\frac{1}{2}\), so \(\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}} f^{\prime}(x)=-2\).
Right-hand derivative: \(f^{\prime}(x)=2\) for \(x>\frac{1}{2}\), so \(\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}^{+}} f^{\prime}(x)=2\).
Since the left-hand derivative and right-hand derivative are not equal, the function is not differentiable at \(x=\frac{1}{2}\).

\end{aligned}\)
