questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f:[1,3] \rightarrow R$ be a continuous function that is differentiable in (1,3) an $f^{\prime}(x)=|f(x)|^{2}+4$ for all $x \in(1,3).$ Then,
questions.solution:
Given that $f:[1,3] \rightarrow R$ be a continuous and differentiable in (1,3) 
and $f^{\prime}(x)=|f(x)|^{2}+4$
By applying $L M V T,$ there exist at least one point $c \in(1,3)$ such that
$$
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{f(3)-f(1)}{3-1}=f^{\prime}(c)\left[\because f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}\right] \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{f(3)-f(1)}{2}=f^{\prime}(c) \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{f(3)-f(1)}{2}=\left[f|c|^{2}+4\right] \\
\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(c)=|f(c)|^{2}+4 \\
\Rightarrow \quad f(3)-f(1)=2 \cdot|f(c)|^{2}+8
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad f(3)-f(1) \geq 8$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f$ and $g$ be differentiable on the interval $I$ and let $a, b \in I, a    < b$. Then,
options: If $f(a)=0=f(b)$, the equation $f^{\prime}(x)+f(x) g^{\prime}(x)=0$ is solvable in $(a, b)$
If $f(a)=0=f(b)$, the equation $f^{\prime}(x)+f(x) g^{\prime}(x)=0$ may not be solvable in $(a, b)$
If $g(a)=0=g(b)$, the equation $g^{\prime}(x)+k g(x)=0$ is solvable in $(a, b), k \in \mathbb{R}$
If $g(a)=0=g(b)$, the equation $g^{\prime}(x)+k g(x)=0$ may not be solvable in $(a, b), k \in \mathbb{R}$.
questions.solution:
From option (a)
We have,
$f(a)=f(b)=0$
$\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(a) \cdot f^{\prime}(b)    < 0$
Again, let $h(x)=f^{\prime}(x)+f(x) g^{\prime}(x)$
$\Rightarrow h(a)=f^{\prime}(a)+fg^{\prime}(a)=f^{\prime}(a)$
and $h(b)=f^{\prime}(b)+f(b) g^{\prime}(b)=f^{\prime}(b)$
$\therefore \quad h(a) \cdot h(b)=f^{\prime}(a) \cdot f^{\prime}(b) < 0$
$\therefore h(x)=0$ has root between $(a, b)$
From option (c) 
We have, $g(a)=g(b)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad g^{\prime}(a)\cdot g^{\prime}(b) < 0$
Again, let $m(x)=g^{\prime}(x)+k g(x)$
$\Rightarrow m(a)=g^{\prime}(a)+k g(a)=g^{\prime}(a)$
$\Rightarrow \quad m(b)=g^{\prime}(b)+k g(b)=g^{\prime}(b)$
$\therefore \quad m(a) \cdot m(b)=g^{\prime}\cdot g^{\prime}(b) < 0$
$\therefore \quad m(x)=0$ has root between $(a, b)$ So, option (a) and (c) are correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f:[a, b] \rightarrow R$ be such that $f$ is differentiable in $(a, b)$ is continuous at $x=a$ and $x=b$ and moreover $f(a)=0=f(b)$. Then
options: there exists at least one point $c$ in $(a,b)$ such that $f^{\prime}=f$
$f^{\prime}(x)=f(x)$ does not hold at any point in $(a, b)$
at every point of $(a, b) f^{\prime}(x)>f(x)$
at every point of $(a, b), f^{\prime}(x)    < f(x)$
questions.solution:
Let $g(x)=e^{-x} f(x)$
such that $g(a)=0, g(b)=0$
and $g(x)$ is continuous and differentiable.
Then, for atleast one value of $c \in(a, b)$ such that $g(c)=0$
Now, $g(x)=e^{-x} f^{\prime}(x)+\left(-e^{-x}\right) f(x)$
$\Rightarrow \quad g(c)=e^{-c} f^{\prime}(c)+\left(-e^{-\ell}\right) f(c)=0$
$\Rightarrow e^{-c} f^{\prime}(c)=e^{-c} f(c) \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(c)=f(c)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}-2 \sin x, & \text { if } x \leq-\frac{\pi}{2} \\ A \sin x+B, & \text { if }-\frac{\pi}{2}    < x < \frac{\pi}{2} \text { . Then, } \\ \cos x, & \text { if } x \geq \frac{\pi}{2}\end{array}\right.$
options: $f$ is continuous for all real values of $A B$
questions.solution:
At $\quad x=-\frac{\pi}{2}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { LHL }=-2 \\
\text { RHL }=-A+B \\
\text { For continuity, } \mathrm{LHL}=\mathrm{RHL}=f(-\pi / 2)
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad-A+B=2$
At $x=\pi / 2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{LHL} &=A+B \\
\mathrm{RHL} &=0 \\
\text { For continuity, } \mathrm{LHL} &=\mathrm{RHL}=f(\pi / 2)
\end{aligned}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad A+B=0$
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
A=-1 \text { and } B=1
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $[x]$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $x$. Then, the value of $\alpha$ for which the function $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c}\frac{\sin \left[-x^{2}\right]}{\left[-x^{2}\right]}, x \neq 0 \\ \alpha, \quad x=0\end{array}\right.$ is continuous at $x=0,$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\frac{\sin \left(-x^{2}\right)}{[-x^{2}]} & , x \neq 0 \\ \alpha, & x=0\end{array}\right.$
Now, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left(-x^{2}\right)}{\left.\mid-x^{2}\right]}=\frac{\sin (-1)}{(-1)}=\sin (1)$
Since, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=0$.
$\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=f(0) \Rightarrow \sin (1)=\alpha$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f:R\rightarrow R$ be defined as
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lr}0, & x \text { is irrational} \\ \sin |x|, & x \text { is rational }\end{array}\right.$ Then, which of the following is true?
options: f is discontinuous for all x
f is continuous for all x
f is discontinuous at $x=k\pi$ where k is an integer
f is continuous at $x=k\pi$ where k is an integer
questions.solution:
We have, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}0, & x \text { is irrational } \\ \sin |x|, x \text { is rational }\end{array}\right.$
If $f(x)$ is continuous, then $\sin |x|=0$ $\Rightarrow x=k \pi,$ where $k$ is an integer.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be such that $f(2 x-1)=f(x)$ for all $x \in R .$ If $f$ is continuous at $x=1$ an $f(1)=1,$ then
questions.solution:
Given, $f: R \rightarrow R$
and $\quad f(2 x-1)=f(x), x \in R$
Hence, $f$ is continuous at $x=1$ and $f(1)=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=\frac{\tan \left\{\pi\left[x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right\}}{2+[x]^{2}},$ where $[x]$ denotes the greatest integer $\leq x$, is
options: not differentiable for some values of $x$
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=\frac{\tan \left\{\pi\left[x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right\}}{2+[x]^{2}}$
Since. $\left[x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ is an integer for all $x$, therefore $\pi\left[x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ is an integral multiple of $\pi$ for all $x$. Hence, $\tan \left\{\pi\left[x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right\}=0$ for all $x$
Also, $2+[x]^{2} \neq 0$ for all $x$
Hence, $f(x)=0$ for all $x$ Hence, $f(x)$ is continuous and derivable for all $x$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=a \sin |x|+b e^{| x \mid} \quad$ is differentiable at $x=0$ when
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=a \sin |x|+b e^{|x|}$
We know that $\sin |x|$ and $e^{|x|}$ is not
differentiable at $x=0$.
Therefore, for $f(x)$ to differentiable at $x=0$, we
must have $a=b=0$.
$\therefore$
$$
a+b=0
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Suppose that $f(x)$ is a differentiable function such that $f^{\prime}(x)$ is continuous, $f^{\prime}(0)=1$ and $f^{\prime \prime}(0)$ does not exist. Let $g(x)=x f^{\prime}(x) .$ Then,
questions.solution:
Given, $f^{\prime}(0)=1$ and $f^{\prime \prime}$ " (0) does not exist.
Also, given $g(x)=x f^{\prime}(x)$
$\therefore \quad g^{\prime}(x)=x f^{\prime \prime}(x)+f^{\prime}(x)$
Put $x=0,$ we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
g^{\prime}(0) &=0 f^{\prime \prime}(0)+f^{\prime}(0) \\
&=0+1=1
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\int_{0}^{x}|1-t| d t, & x>0 \\ x-\frac{1}{2}, & x \leq 1\end{array} .\right.$ Then
questions.solution:
Given. $f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \int_{0}^{x}|1-t| d t, & x>1 \\ x-\frac{1}{2}, & x \leq 1 \end{aligned}\right.$
Now, for $x>1$, $\int_{0}^{x}|1-t| d t$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\int_{0}^{1}(1-t) d t+\int_{1}^{x}(t-1) d t \\
&=\left[t-\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{1}+\left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}-t\right]_{1}^{x} \\
&=\left[1-\frac{1}{2}-0\right]+\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}-x-\frac{1}{2}+1\right] \\
&=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{2}-x-\frac{1}{2}+1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{2}-x+1
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Eq. (i) becomes, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\frac{x^{2}}{2}-x+1, & x>1 \\ x-\frac{1}{2}, & x \leq 1\end{array}\right.$
Continuity at $x=1$
and $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{LHL} &=\lim (f(x)\\ &=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1}\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right)=1-\frac{1}{2} \\ \mathrm{RHL} &=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x) \\ &=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}-x+1\right) \\ &=\frac{1}{2}-1+1=\frac{1}{2} \\ f(1) &=1-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad f(1)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)$
Hence, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=1$ 
Differentiability at $x=1$
$\begin{aligned} L H D &=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h} \\ &=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1-h)-\frac{1}{2}-\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)}{-h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-h}{-h}=1 \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{RHD} &=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h} \\ &=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{(1+h)^{2}}{2}-(1+h)+1-\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)}{h} \\ &=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{1+h^{2}+2 h}{2}-1-h+1-\frac{1}{2}}{h} \\ &=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{h^{2}}{2}+h-h-\frac{1}{2}}{h} \\ &=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h^{2}}{2 h}=0 \end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{LHD} \neq \mathrm{RHD}$
Hence, $f(x)$ is not differentiable at $x=1$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}x^{3}-3 x+2, & x     < 2 \\ x^{3}-6 x^{2}+9 x+2, & x \geq 2\end{array}\right.$ then
options: $f$ is continuous but not differentiable at $x=2$
questions.solution:
Given. $f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} x^{3}-3 x+2, & x     < 2 \\ x^{3}-6 x^{2}+9 x+2, & x \geq 2 \end{aligned}\right.$
LHL
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=f(2-0)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(2-h)^{3}-3(2-h)+2 \\
=(2)^{3}-6+2=8-6+2=4 \\
\begin{aligned}
LH L=f(2+0) &=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(2+h)^{3}-6(2+h)^{2} \\
&=(2)^{3}-6(2)^{2}+9(2)+2 \\
&=8-24+18+2=4
\end{aligned}
\end{array}
$$
$\because \mathrm{LHL}=\mathrm{RHL}$
$\therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow 2} f(x)$ exist
and $\begin{aligned} f(2)=(2)^{3} &-6(2)^{2}+9(2)+2 \\ &=8-24+18+2=4 \end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{LHL}=\mathrm{RHL}=f(2)$
So, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=2$
Now, $\quad f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}3 x^{2}-3, & x < 2 \\ 3 x^{2}-12 x+9, & x \geq 2\end{array}\right.$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore \quad & L f^{\prime}(2)=3(2)^{2}-3=12-3=9 \\ \text { and } \quad & R f^{\prime}(2)=3(2)^{2}-12(2)+9 \\ & =12-24+9=-3\end{array}$
$\because \quad L f^{\prime}(2) \neq R f^{\prime}(2)$
$\therefore f(x)$ is not differentiable at $x=2$
Hence. $f$ is continuous but not differentiable at $x=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the function
\(f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{x^2-(A+2) x+A}{x-2} & \text { for } x \neq 2 \\ 2 & \text { for } x=2\end{cases}\)
is continuous at \(x=2\), then
questions.solution:
Hints: \(\frac{4-(A+2) 2+A}{0}=\frac{-A}{0}\)
Put \(\mathrm{A}=0\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
\(f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}{[x]+[-x],} & \text { when } x \neq 2 \\ \lambda & \text { when } x=2\end{array}\right.\)
If \(f(x)\) is continuous at \(x=2\), the value of \(\lambda\) will be
questions.solution:
Hints: \(\mathrm{LHL}=\operatorname{Lt}_{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0}[2-\mathrm{h}]+[-(2-\mathrm{h})]\)
\(=\operatorname{Lt}_{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} 1+(-2+\mathrm{h})=1-2=-1\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{RML}=\underset{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0}{\operatorname{Lt}}[2+\mathrm{h}]+(-(2+\mathrm{h})) \\
& =2+(-2-\mathrm{h})=2-3=-1 \\
& \lambda=-1
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $f(x)=x+|x|$ is continuous for
questions.solution: $$ \text { Hints: } f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lc} 2 x ; & x \geq 0 \\ 0 ; & x < 0 \end{array}\right. $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{r=1}^{2013}\dfrac{x}{x^2 + r^2}\right)\left(\prod_{r=1}^{2013}(x^2 + r^2)\right)dx = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\prod_{r=1}^{2013}(1 + r^2) - K^2\right]$, then $K$ is
questions.solution:
Let $f(x) = \prod_{r=1}^{2013}(x^2 + r^2)$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$\ln f(x) = \sum_{r=1}^{2013} \ln(x^2 + r^2)$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$\dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)} = \sum_{r=1}^{2013} \dfrac{2x}{x^2 + r^2}$

$\dfrac{1}{2} f'(x) = f(x) \sum_{r=1}^{2013} \dfrac{x}{x^2 + r^2}$

Substituting this into the given integral:

$\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1} \dfrac{1}{2} f'(x) dx = \dfrac{1}{2} [f(x)]_{0}^{1} = \dfrac{1}{2} [f(1) - f(0)]$

Evaluating $f(1)$ and $f(0)$:

$f(1) = \prod_{r=1}^{2013}(1 + r^2)$

$f(0) = \prod_{r=1}^{2013}(0 + r^2) = \prod_{r=1}^{2013} r^2 = (2013!)^2$

The integral evaluates to:

$\dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \prod_{r=1}^{2013}(1 + r^2) - (2013!)^2 \right]$

Comparing this with the given expression $\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\prod_{r=1}^{2013}(1 + r^2) - K^2\right]$:

$K^2 = (2013!)^2 \Rightarrow K = 2013!$

Answer: $(2013)!$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The least positive value of 'a' for which the equation $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{x}(t^2 - 8t + 13)\,dt = x\sin\dfrac{a}{x}$ has a solution is
questions.solution:
Evaluating the integral on the left side, we get

$\displaystyle\int_{0}^{x}(t^2 - 8t + 13)\,dt = \left[ \dfrac{t^3}{3} - 4t^2 + 13t \right]_{0}^{x} = \dfrac{x^3}{3} - 4x^2 + 13x$

The given equation becomes

$\dfrac{x^3}{3} - 4x^2 + 13x = x\sin\dfrac{a}{x}$

Since $x$ is in the denominator of the argument of sine, $x \neq 0$. Dividing both sides by $x$, we obtain

$\dfrac{x^2}{3} - 4x + 13 = \sin\dfrac{a}{x}$

Completing the square for the expression on the left side yields

$\dfrac{1}{3}(x^2 - 12x + 39) = \dfrac{1}{3}((x - 6)^2 + 3) = \dfrac{1}{3}(x - 6)^2 + 1$

Thus, the equation can be written as

$\dfrac{1}{3}(x - 6)^2 + 1 = \sin\dfrac{a}{x}$

Since $(x - 6)^2 \geq 0$, the minimum value of the left side is $1$, which occurs at $x = 6$. On the right side, the maximum value of the sine function is $1$. For the equation to have a solution, both sides must be exactly equal to $1$.

This requires $x = 6$ and $\sin\dfrac{a}{6} = 1$.

$\sin\dfrac{a}{6} = 1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{a}{6} = 2n\pi + \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ for some integer $n$.

$a = 12n\pi + 3\pi$

For the least positive value of $a$, we substitute $n = 0$, which gives

$a = 3\pi$

Answer: $3\pi$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)$ be a real valued function which is monotonic and differentiable. Then for any reals $a$ and $b$, $\displaystyle\int_{f(a)}^{f(b)} 2x\{b - f^{-1}(x)\}\,dx =$
questions.solution:
Let $I = \displaystyle\int_{f(a)}^{f(b)} 2x\{b - f^{-1}(x)\}\,dx$

Substitute $x = f(t)$, which gives $dx = f'(t)\,dt$.

When $x = f(a)$, $t = a$.

When $x = f(b)$, $t = b$.

The integral becomes:

$I = \displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} 2f(t)\{b - t\}f'(t)\,dt$

Using integration by parts, taking $(b - t)$ as the first function and $2f(t)f'(t)$ as the second function:

$I = \left[ (b - t)f^2(t) \right]_{a}^{b} - \displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} (-1)f^2(t)\,dt$

$I = (0 - (b - a)f^2(a)) + \displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} f^2(t)\,dt$

$I = \displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} f^2(t)\,dt - \displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} f^2(a)\,dt$

$I = \displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} (f^2(x) - f^2(a))\,dx$

Answer: $\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b}(f^2(x) - f^2(a))\,dx$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $a, b, c$ be non-zero real numbers, such that $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}(1 + \cos^8 x)(ax^2 + bx + c)\,dx = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{2}(1 + \cos^8 x)(ax^2 + bx + c)\,dx$, then $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$ has
questions.solution:
Given $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}(1 + \cos^8 x)(ax^2 + bx + c)\,dx = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{2}(1 + \cos^8 x)(ax^2 + bx + c)\,dx$

$\Rightarrow \displaystyle\int_{1}^{2}(1 + \cos^8 x)(ax^2 + bx + c)\,dx = 0$

Let $f(x) = 1 + \cos^8 x$ and $g(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$.

We know that $f(x) \ge 1 > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

If $g(x)$ does not change sign in the interval $(1, 2)$, then the product $f(x)g(x)$ will also not change sign in $(1, 2)$. Since $g(x)$ is a non-zero polynomial, the definite integral of $f(x)g(x)$ over $(1, 2)$ would be strictly positive or strictly negative, which contradicts the fact that the integral is zero.

Therefore, $g(x)$ must change sign in the interval $(1, 2)$.

Since $g(x)$ is a continuous function, by the Intermediate Value Theorem, $g(x) = 0$ must have at least one root in $(1, 2)$.

Answer: at least one root in $(1, 2)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x) > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and $f(x)$ is bounded. If $\lim_{n \to \infty}\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n} a^{r-1}\displaystyle\int_{(r-1)a}^{ra}\dfrac{f(x)\,dx}{f(x) + f(2ra - a - x)} = \dfrac{3}{5}$ where $0 < a < 1$, then the value(s) of $a$ is/are
questions.solution:
Let $I_r = \displaystyle\int_{(r-1)a}^{ra} \dfrac{f(x)}{f(x) + f(2ra - a - x)} \,dx$

Using the definite integral property $\displaystyle\int_{A}^{B} g(x) \,dx = \displaystyle\int_{A}^{B} g(A+B-x) \,dx$, with $A = (r-1)a$ and $B = ra$, we have $A+B = 2ra - a$.

$I_r = \displaystyle\int_{(r-1)a}^{ra} \dfrac{f(2ra - a - x)}{f(2ra - a - x) + f(x)} \,dx$

Adding the two expressions for $I_r$, we get:

$2I_r = \displaystyle\int_{(r-1)a}^{ra} \dfrac{f(x) + f(2ra - a - x)}{f(x) + f(2ra - a - x)} \,dx = \displaystyle\int_{(r-1)a}^{ra} 1 \,dx = ra - (r-1)a = a$

$I_r = \dfrac{a}{2}$

Substituting $I_r$ into the given limit:

$\lim_{n \to \infty} \displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n} a^{r-1} \left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right) = \dfrac{a}{2} \lim_{n \to \infty} (1 + a + a^2 + \dots + a^{n-1})$

Since $0 < a < 1$, the sum of the infinite geometric progression is $\dfrac{1}{1-a}$.

$\dfrac{a}{2(1-a)} = \dfrac{3}{5}$

$5a = 6(1-a)$

$5a = 6 - 6a$

$11a = 6$

$a = \dfrac{6}{11}$

Answer: $\dfrac{6}{11}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_0^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{4+3 \sin x}{4+3 \cos x}\right) d x$ is
questions.solution:
$I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{4+3 \sin x}{4+3 \cos x}\right) d x$

Applying King's property
$I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{4+3 \cos x}{4+3 \sin x}\right) d x$
$\begin{aligned} & 2 I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\left(\frac{4+3 \sin x}{4+3 \cos x}\right)\left(\frac{4+3 \cos x}{4+3 \sin x}\right)\right) d x=0 \\ & I=0\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{-1}^1 \frac{x^3+|x|+1}{x^2+2|x|+1} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$I=\int_{-1}^1 \frac{x^3+|x|+1}{x^2+2|x|+1} d x$

Applying King's property
$\mathrm{I}=\int_{-1}^1 \frac{-\mathrm{x}^3+|\mathrm{x}|+1}{\mathrm{x}^2+2|\mathrm{x}|+1} \mathrm{dx}$

Applying $I=\int_{-1}^1 \frac{|x|+1}{x^2+2|x|+1} d x$ being even function
$I=2 \int_0^1 \frac{x+1}{(x+1)^2} d x=2[\ln (x+1)]_0^1=2 \ln 2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^{1.5}\left[x^2\right] d x$ is equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \int_0^{1.5}\left[x^2\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^1\left[x^2\right] d x+\int_1^{\sqrt{2}}\left[x^2\right] d x+\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{3 / 2}\left[x^2\right] d x \\ & =0+1(\sqrt{2}-1)+2(1.5-\sqrt{2}) \\ & =2-\sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_3^6 \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{9-x}+\sqrt{x}} d x$ is
questions.solution:
$I=\int_3^6 \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{9-x}+\sqrt{x}} d x$

Applying King's property
$I=\int_3^6 \frac{\sqrt{9-x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{9-x}} d x$

Adding $2 \mathrm{I}=\int_3^6 \mathrm{dx}=3 \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{3}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)=\max \{x+|x|, x-[x]\}$, where $[x]$ stands for the greatest integer not greater than $x$. Then $\int_{-3}^3 f(x) d x$ has the value
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\max \cdot\{x+|x|, x-[x]\}=\max \{x+|x|,\{x\}\} \\ & \int_{-3}^3 f(x) d x \\ & =\int_{-3}^0\{x\} d x+\int_0^3 2 x d x \\ & =\frac{3}{2}+9=\frac{21}{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_{-100}^{100} \frac{\left(x+x^3+x^5\right)}{\left(1+x^2+x^4+x^6\right)} d x$ is
questions.solution: $\int_{-100}^{100} \frac{\text { odd function }}{\text { Even function }}=\int_{-100}^{100}$ odd function $=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\int_0^{\sin ^2 x} \sin ^{-1} \sqrt{t}$ $dt$ and $g(x)=\int_0^{\cos ^2 x} \cos ^{-1} \sqrt{t}$ $dt$, then the value of $f(x)+g(x)$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \text f^{\prime}(x)+g^{\prime}(x)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow f(x)+g(x)=\text { constant }(\text { say } c) \\ & \text { putting } x=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ \therefore & c=\int_0^{1 / 2}\left(\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{t}+\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{t}\right) d t=\frac{\pi}{4}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\frac{e^x}{1+e^x}, l_1=\int_{f(-a)}^{f(a)} x g(x(1-x)) d x$ and $l_2=\int_{f(-a)}^{f(a)} g(x(1-x)) d x$, then the value of $\frac{l_2}{l_1}$ is
questions.solution:
Hint: $f(a)+f(-a)=\frac{e^a}{1+e^a}+\frac{e^{-a}}{1+e^{-a}}=1$
Let $f(-a)=t \therefore f(a)=1-t$
Now, $\mathrm{l}_1=\int_{\mathrm{t}}^{1-\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{xg}\left(\mathrm{x}(1-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}=\int_{\mathrm{t}}^{1-\mathrm{t}}(1-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{g}((1-\mathrm{x})(1-(1-\mathrm{x})) \mathrm{dx}\right.$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_t^{1-t}(1-x) g(x(1-x)) d x \\ & \therefore 2 l_1=\int_t^{1-t} g(x(1-x)) d x=I_2 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{I}_2}{I_1}=2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: All values of a for which the inequality $\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_1^a\left(\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x}+1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right) \mathrm{dx} \lt 4$ is satisfied, lie in the interval
questions.solution:
Hint : $\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_1^a\left(\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x}+1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right) \mathrm{dx} \lt 4$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \cdot\left[\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{x^{3 / 2}}{\frac{3}{2}}+x-\frac{x^{1 / 2}}{1 / 2}\right]_1^a \lt 4 & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\left(a^{3 / 2}+a-2 a^{1 / 2}-1-1+2\right)\gt4 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{a^{1 / 2}} \cdot\left(a^{3 / 2}+a-2 a^{1 / 2}\right) \lt 4 & \Rightarrow a+a^{1 / 2}-2 \lt 4 \\
\Rightarrow \quad\left(a^{1 / 2}\right)^2+a^{1 / 2}-6 \lt 0 & \Rightarrow\left(a^{1 / 2}+3\right)\left(a^{1 / 2}-2\right) \lt 0 \\
\text { but, } a^{1 / 2}+3 \nless 0 \quad \therefore a^{1 / 2}-2 \lt 0 \quad \Rightarrow a \lt 4 \text { and } a\gt0 \quad \therefore a \in(0,4)
\end{array}$

questions.solution:

to find extremum points we solve $f^{\prime}(x)=0$
so differentiating equation (i) using Newton Leibnitz Rule,
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\left(x^2-1\right)\left(x^2-4\right)}{2+e^{x^2}} \cdot(2 x) \quad \text { so, } f^{\prime}(x)=0 \text { at } x=0, \pm 1, \pm 2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The expression \(\frac{\int_0^n[x] d x}{\int_0^n\{x\} d x}\), where \([x]\) and \(\{x\}\) are respectively integral and fractional part of \(x\) and \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), is equal to
questions.solution:
Hint : \(\frac{\int_0^n[x] d x}{\int_0^n\{x\} d x}=\frac{I_1}{I_2}\)
\(\begin{aligned} & I_1=\int_0^1[x] d x+\int_1^2[x] d x+\ldots+\int_{n-1}^n[x] d x \\ & =0+1+\ldots .+(n-1)=\frac{n(n-1)}{2} \\ & I_2=\int_0^n\{x\} d x=n \int_0^1\{x\} d x=n\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^1=\frac{n}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{n(n-1)}{n}=n-1\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: \(\int_0^{2 \pi} \theta \sin ^6 \theta \cos \theta d \theta\) is equal to
questions.solution:
Hint : Let \(I=\int_0^{2 \pi} \theta \sin ^6 \theta \cos \theta d \theta \ldots \ldots .(I)\)
applying King's Property,
\(\int_0^{2 \pi}(2 \pi-\theta) \sin ^6 \theta \cdot \cos \theta d \theta \ldots \ldots .(II)\)
adding (I) and (II), \(2 \mathrm{I}=2 \pi \int_0^{2 \pi} \sin ^6 \theta \cos \theta d \theta\)
\(\Rightarrow I=\pi \int_0^{2 \pi} \sin ^6 \theta \cos \theta \ldots \ldots .(III)\)
Using Queen's Property, \(\int_0^{2 a} f(x) d x=2 \int_0^a f(x) d x\), when \(f(2 a-x)=f(x)\)
applying Queen's Property on (III), I \(=2 \pi \int_0^\pi \sin ^6 \theta \cos \theta d \theta\)
again, using Queen's Property, \(\int_0^{2 a} f(x) d x=0\), when, \(f(2 a-x)=-f(x)\)
\(I=0\)

questions.solution:

Average ordinate will be \(=\frac{1}{\pi-0} \int_0^\pi \sin x d x=\frac{[-\cos x]_0^\pi}{\pi}=\frac{2}{\pi}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(f\) be a non-negative function defined on \(\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\). If \(\int_0^x\left(f^{\prime}(t)-\sin 2 t\right) d t=\int_x^0 f(t) \tan t d t, f(0)=1\), then \(\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} f(x) d x\) is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
\text {Hint } & f^{\prime}(x)-\sin 2 x=-f(x) \tan x \\
& \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)+\tan x f(x)=\sin 2 x \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d f(x)}{d x}+\tan x . f(x)=\sin 2 x
\end{aligned}\)
\(\text { If }=e^{f \tan x d x}=e^{\ln |\sec x|}=|\sec x|=\sec x \quad \text { As, } x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\)
Solution is,
\(\begin{aligned}
& f(x) \times \sec x=\int \sin 2 x \sec x d x=\int 2 \sin x \cos x \sec x d x=2 \int \sin x d x \\
& f(x) \sec x=-2 \cos x+C \\
& \text { Put } x=0 ; f(0)=1 \\
& f(0) \sec 0=-2 \cos 0+c \\
& \Rightarrow 1=-2+C \quad \therefore C=3
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore\) Required solution is;
\(\begin{aligned}
& f(x) \sec x=-2 \cos x+3 \\
& f(x)=-2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x
\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& =-2\left(\frac{1+\cos 2 x}{2}\right)+3 \cos x=-1-\cos 2 x+3 \cos x \\
& f(x)=-1-\cos 2 x+3 \cos x
\end{aligned}\)
Now \(\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} f(x) d x\)
\(\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(-1-\cos 2 x+3 \cos x) d x=\left[-x-\frac{\sin 2 x}{2}+3 \sin x\right]_0 ^{-\frac{\pi}{2}}=\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}+3\right)-0=3-\frac{\pi}{2}\)
\(\left[-x-\frac{\sin 2 x}{2}+\left.3 \sin x\right|_0 ^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\right.\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which of the following statements are true?
options: If \(f(x)\) be continuous and periodic with periodicity \(T\), then \(I=\int_a^{a+T} f(x) d x\) depend on ' \(a\) '.
If \(f(x)\) be continuous and periodic with periodicity \(T\), then \(I=\int_a^{a+T} f(x) d x\) does not depend on ' \(a\) '.

Let \(f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}1 \text {, if } x \text { is rational } \\ 0, \text { if } x \text { is irrational }\end{array}\right.\), then \(f\) is periodic of the periodicity \(T\) only if \(T\) is rational
\(f\) defined in (C) is periodic for all \(T\)
questions.solution:
Hint: (A) \(I=\int_a^{a+T} f(x) d x=\int_0^T f(x) d x\) Independent of a
(B) \(I=\int_a^{a+T} f(x) d x=\int_0^T f(x) d x\) does not depend on ' \(a\) '
(C) \(f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}1, x \in Q \\ 0, x \notin Q\end{array}\right.\) is periodic for all \(T\) with undefined fundamental period.
(D) \(f\) defined on \((C)\) is periodic for all \(T\) with undefined fundamental period.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{f}$ be derivable in $[0,1]$, then
options: there exists $c \in(0,1)$ such that $\int_0^c f(x) d x=(1-c) f(c)$
there does not exist any point $d \in(0,1)$ for which $\int_0^d f(x) d x=(1-d) f(d)$
$\int_0^c f(x) d x$ does not exist, for any $c \in(0,1)$
$\int_0^c f(x) d x$ is independent of $c, c \in(0,1)$
questions.solution:
Let $g(x)=x \int_0^x f(t) d t-\int_0^x f(t) d t$
Now, $g(0)=0$
$g(1)=0$
By Rolle's Theorem
$g^{\prime}(x)=0$
for some $x \in(0,1)$
$g^{\prime}(x)=x f(x)+\int_0^x f(t) d t-f(x)=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f$ be a non-negative function defined in $[0, \pi / 2], f^{\prime}$ exists and be continuous for all $x$ and $\int_0^x \sqrt{1-\left(f^{\prime}(t)\right)^2} d t=\int_0^x f(t) d t$ and $f(0)=0$. Then
questions.solution:
$\int_0^x \sqrt{1-\left(f^{\prime}(t)\right)^2} d t=\int_0^x f(t) d t$
Using Leibnitz Rule,
$\begin{aligned}
&\sqrt{1-\left(f^{\prime}(x)\right)^2}=f(x) \\
&\therefore f^{\prime}(x)=\pm \sqrt{1-f^2(x)} \Rightarrow \int \frac{d f(x)}{\sqrt{1-f^2(x)}}=\pm \int d x \\
&\sin ^{-1}(f(x))=\pm x+c \\
&\therefore f(x)=\sin (\pm x+C) \\
&\because f(0)=0 \Rightarrow C=0 \\
&\therefore f(x)=\sin x \text { or }-\sin x
\end{aligned}$
But $f$ is non-negative on $[0, \pi / 2]$
$\begin{aligned}
&\therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\operatorname{Sin} \mathrm{x} \\
&\because \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})  < \mathrm{x} \text { for all } \mathrm{x} > 0 \\
&\therefore \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) < \frac{4}{3} \text { and } \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) < \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}$

$\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \cdot \cos ^2 \mathrm{x}  < \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}^2} \cos ^2 \mathrm{x} < \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}^2} < \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{2}}$ for $0 < \mathrm{x} < 1$
$\therefore$ By domination Law, $\mathrm{I}_4$ is maximum
$\therefore \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_4$

questions.solution:
$\int_{a-1}^{a} \frac{e^{-t}}{t-a-1} d t$
$=\int_{\mathrm{a}-1}^{\mathrm{a}} \frac{\mathrm{e}^{-(2 \mathrm{a}-1-\mathrm{t})}}{2 \mathrm{a}-1-\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{a}-1} \mathrm{dt}$
$=\int_{\mathrm{a}-1}^{\mathrm{a}} \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}-2 \mathrm{a}+1}}{\mathrm{a}-2-\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}$
$t-(a-1)=x \quad \Rightarrow d t=d x$
$=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{e^{x} \cdot e^{-a}}{-x-1} d x$
$=-e^{-a} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{e^{x}}{x+1} d x=-b e^{-a}$

To evaluate $I_{2}=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x$, it is possible to put $x=\sec t$

By
$\mathrm{x}=1 / \mathrm{t}, \quad \mathrm{I}_{1}=\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2} \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{4 \mathrm{t}^{2}+1}     < 0$
So not possible

Since $x \in(0,1)$ and $\operatorname{cosec} \theta \in(-\infty,-1] \cup[1, \infty)$
So not possible

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integration $\int_{-\pi / 4}^{\pi / 4}\left(\lambda|\sin x|+\frac{\mu \sin x}{1+\cos x}+\gamma\right) d x$
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\pi / 4}\left(\lambda|\sin x|+\frac{\mu \sin x}{1+\cos x}+\gamma\right)$
$\begin{aligned} &=\int_{-\pi / 4}^{\pi / 4}(\lambda|\sin x|+\gamma) d x+\mu \int_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x} d x \\ \text { Let } f(x)=\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x} & \\ \Rightarrow f(-x)=\frac{\sin (-x)}{1+\cos (-x)} &=\frac{-\sin x}{1+\cos x}=-f(x) \end{aligned}$
$f(x)$ is an odd function.
$\int_{-\pi}^{\pi / 4}\left(\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right) d x=0$
$\left.I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\pi / 4} \lambda|\sin x|+\gamma\right) d x$
$\therefore I$ is independent of $\mu$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{-1}^{1}\left\{\frac{x^{2015}}{e^{\mid x \mid}\left(x^{2}+\cos x\right)}+\frac{1}{e^{\mid{x} \mid}}\right\} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{-1}^{1}\left\{\frac{x^{2015}}{\left(e^{\mid x\mid} \left(x^{2}+\cos x\right)\right.}+\frac{1}{e^{|x|}}\right\} d x$
$\begin{aligned}=\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{x^{2015}}{e^{|x|}\left(x^{2}+\cos x\right)} & d x+\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{1}{e^{|x|}} d x \\ \text { Let } f(x)=\frac{x^{2015}}{e^{|x|}\left(x^{2}+\cos x\right)} \text { and } g(x)=\frac{1}{e^{|x|}} \\ \text { Again, } f(-x)=\frac{(-x)^{2015}}{e^{|-x|}\left((-x)^{2}+\cos (-x)\right)} \\ &=\frac{-x^{2015}}{e^{|x|}\left(x^{2}+\cos x\right)}=-f(x) \\ \text { and } \quad g(-x)=\frac{1}{e^{|-x|}}=\frac{1}{e^{|x|}}=g(x) \end{aligned}$
$\because f(x)$ is odd function and $g(x)$ is even function $\int_{-1}^{1} f(x) d x=0$ and $\int_{-1}^{1} g(x) d x=2 \int_{0}^{1} g(x) d x$
$\begin{aligned} I &=2 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{e^{\mid  x \mid}} d x \\ &=2 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{e^{x}} d x=2 \int_{0}^{1} e^{-x} d x \\ &=-2\left[e^{-x}\right]_{0}^{1} \\ &=-2\left[e^{-1}-e^{0}\right] \\ &=-2\left(e^{-1}-1\right) \\ &=2\left(1-e^{-1}\right) \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{3}{n}\left\{1+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3}}+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+6}}+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+9}}\right.$$+\ldots+\sqrt{\frac{1}{n+3}(n-1)}$
questions.solution:
$\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{3}{n}\left[1+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3}}+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+6}}+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+9}}+\ldots\right.+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3(n-1)}}$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{3}{n}\left[\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3 \times 0}}+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3 \times 1}}+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3 \times 2}}\right.$
$+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3 \times 3}}+\ldots+\sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3(n-1)}}$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{3}{n}\left[\sum_{r=0}^{n-1} \sqrt{\frac{n}{n+3 r}}\right]$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{3}{n}\left[\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}\left[\frac{1}{1+3\left(\frac{r}{n}\right)}\right]\right.$
$=3 \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{r=0}^{n-1} \left(\sqrt{1+3\left(\frac{r}{n}\right)}\right)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$
$=3 \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{1}{1+3 x}} d x$
$=3 \int_{0}^{1}(1+3 x)^{-1 / 2} d x$
$=3\left[\frac{2 \sqrt{1+3 x}}{3}\right]_{0}^{1}$
$=[4-2]$
$=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $I_{n}=\int_{0}^{1} x^{n} \tan ^{-1} x d x .$ If $a_{n} I_{n+2}+b_{n} I_{n}=c_{n}$ for all $n \geq 1,$ then
questions.solution:
We have,
$I_{n}=\int_{0}^{1} x^{n} \tan ^{-1} x d x$
$=\left[\tan ^{-1} x \cdot \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}\right]_{0}^{1}-\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} \cdot \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x$
$=\frac{\pi}{4(n+1)}-\frac{1}{n+1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{2} \cdot x^{n-1}}{1+x^{2}} d x$
$=\frac{\pi}{4(n+1)}-\frac{1}{n+1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(1+x^{2}\right) x^{n-1}-x^{n-1}}{1+x^{2}} d x$
$=\frac{\pi}{4(n+1)}-\frac{1}{n+1} \int_{0}^{1} x^{n-1} d x+\frac{1}{n+1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{n-1}}{1+x^{2}} d x$
$=\frac{\pi}{4(n+1)}-\frac{1}{n+1}\left[\frac{x^{n}}{n}\right]_{0}^{1}+\frac{1}{n+1}$
$\left[x^{n-1} \cdot \tan ^{-1} x\right]_{0}^{1}-\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x \cdot(n-1) x^{n-2} d x$
$\begin{aligned}=\frac{\pi}{4(n+1)}-\frac{1}{n(n+1)}+\frac{\pi}{4(n+1)}-\frac{n-1}{n+1} & \int_{0}^{1} x^{n-2} \tan ^{-1} x d x \end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad I_{n}=\frac{\pi}{2(n+1)}-\frac{1}{n(n+1)}-\frac{n-1}{n+2} I_{n-2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad I_{n}+\frac{n-1}{n+2} I_{n-2}=\frac{\pi}{2(n+1)}-\frac{1}{n(n+1)}$
Put $n=n+2,$ we get $I_{n+2}+\frac{n+1}{n+3} I_{n}=\frac{\pi}{2(n+3)}-\frac{1}{(n+2)(n+3)}$
$\Rightarrow(n+3) I_{n+2}+(n+1) I_{n}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{1}{n+2}$
$\therefore a_{n}=n+3, b_{n}=n+1, c_{n}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{1}{n+2}$
$\therefore a_{n}$ and $b_{n}$ are in $\mathrm{AP}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $M=\int_{0}^{n / 2} \frac{\cos x}{x+2} d x, N=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin x \cos x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x,$ then
the value of $M-N$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $M=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos x}{(x+2)} d x$
$\begin{aligned} \text { and } \quad N &=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}  \frac{\sin x \cos x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x \\ &=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sin 2 x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x \end{aligned}$
Put $2 x=t \Rightarrow d x=\frac{a}{2}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad N &=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}  \frac{\sin t}{4(t / 2+1)^{2}} d t \\ &=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin t}{(t+2)^{2}} d t \end{aligned}$
$\therefore M-N=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\cos x) \cdot \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x$
$-\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin x}{(x+2)^{2}} d x$
$=\left(\frac{\sin x}{x+2}\right)_{0}^{\pi 2}-\int_{0}^{\pi 2}-\frac{\sin x}{(x+2)^{2}} d x$
$-\frac{\sin \pi / 2}{\pi / 2+2}=\frac{1}{\frac{\pi+4}{2}}=\frac{2}{\pi+4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $I=\int_{1 / 2014}^{2014} \frac{\tan ^{-1} x}{x} d x$ is
questions.solution:
We have,
$$
I=\int_{1 / 2014}^{2014} \frac{\tan ^{-1} x}{x} d x
$$
Let
$$
x=\frac{1}{t}
$$
$\Rightarrow$
$$
d x=\frac{-1}{t^{2}} d t
$$
Now,
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &=\int_{2014}^{1 / 2014} \frac{\tan ^{-1}(1 / 7)}{1 / t}\left(\frac{-1}{t^{2}} d t\right) \\
&=\int_{1 / 2014}^{2014} \frac{\cot ^{-1} t}{t} d t \\
&=\int_{1 / 2014}^{2014} \frac{\cot ^{-1} x}{x} d x
\end{aligned}
$$
On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 I &=\int_{1 / 2014}^{2014} \frac{\pi / 2}{x} d x=\frac{\pi}{2}(\log x)_{1 / 2014}^{2014} \\
&=\frac{\pi}{2}(\log 2014-\log 1 / 2014)
\end{aligned}
$$
$\Rightarrow$
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &=\frac{\pi}{4}\left(\log 2014-\log \frac{1}{2014}\right) \\
&=\frac{\pi}{4}(\log 2014+\log 2014) \\
&=\frac{\pi}{4}(2 \log 2014)=\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2014
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $I=\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 3} \frac{\sin x}{x} d x$. Then
questions.solution:
We have,
$$
I=\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 3} \frac{\sin x}{x} d x
$$
since, $\frac{\sin x}{x}$ is a decreasing function.
$$
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{\pi}{12} \times \frac{\sin \pi / 3}{\pi / 3} \leq 1 \leq \frac{\pi}{12} \times \frac{\sin \pi / 4}{\pi / 4} \\
\frac{\sqrt{3}}{8} \leq I \leq \frac{\sqrt{2}}{6}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of
$I=\int_{\pi / 2}^{5 \pi / 2} \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1}}(\sin x)}{e^{\tan ^{-1}(\sin x)}+e^{\tan ^{-1}(\cos x)}} d x,$ is
questions.solution:
Let
$I=\int_{\pi / 2}^{5 \pi / 2} \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1}}(\sin x)}{e^{\tan ^{-1}(\sin x)}+e^{\tan ^{-1}(\cos x)}} d x$
$=\int_{0}^{5 \pi / 2} \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1}(\sin x)}}{e^{\tan ^{-1}(\sin x)}+e^{\tan ^{-1}(\cos x)}} d x$
$-\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1}}(\sin x)}{e^{\tan ^{-1}(\sin x)}+e^{\tan ^{-1}(\cos x)}} d x \ldots(\mathrm{i}$
$I=\int_{0}^{5 \pi / 2} \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1}(\cos x)}}{e^{\tan ^{-1}(\cos x)}+e^{\tan ^{-1}(\sin x)}} d x$
$\quad-\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1}}(\cos x)}{e^{\tan ^{-1}(\cos x)}+e^{\tan ^{-1}(\sin x)}} d x \ldots$ (ii)
$\quad\left[\right.$ using, $\left.\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x\right]$
On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
2 I=\int_{0}^{5 \pi / 2} d x-\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} d x \\
\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=(x)_{0}^{5 \pi / 2}-(x)_{0}^{\mathrm{v} / 2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\frac{5 \pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{2}=2 \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad I=\pi
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of
$\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n}\left\{\sec ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4 n}+\sec ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{4 n}+\ldots+\sec ^{2} \frac{n \pi}{4 n}\right] is$
questions.solution:
We have, $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n}\left\{\sec ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4 n}+\sec ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{4 n}+\ldots+\sec ^{2} \frac{n \pi}{4 n}\right\}$
$\quad=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{r=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} \sec ^{2}\left(\frac{r \pi}{4 n}\right)=\int_{0}^{1} \sec ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi x}{4}\right)$
$=\frac{4}{\pi}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi x}{4}\right)\right]_{0}^{1}$
$=\frac{4}{\pi} \times 1=\frac{4}{\pi}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $I=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{3} \cos 3 x}{2+x^{2}} d x$. Then
questions.solution:
$I=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{3} \cos 3 x}{2+x^{2}} d x$
Here, $\quad-1    < \cos 3 x < 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad-x^{3} < x^{3} \cos 3 x < x^{3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{-x^{3}}{x^{2}} < \frac{-x^{3}}{x} < \frac{-x^{3}}{2+x^{2}} < \frac{x^{3} \cos 3 x}{2+x^{2}}$
$ < \frac{x^{3}}{2+x^{2}} < \frac{x^{3}}{x} < \frac{x^{3}}{x^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \int_{0}^{1}-x^{2} d x < I < \int_{0}^{1} x^{2} d x$
$\Rightarrow$
$\left(\frac{-x^{3}}{3}\right)_{0}^{1} < I < \left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)_{0}^{1}$
$\frac{-1}{3} < I < \frac{1}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $I=\int_{10}^{19} \frac{\sin x}{1+x^{6}} d x$. Then,
questions.solution:
For $x>10,$ we have
$|\sin x|    < 1$ and $1+x^{8}>10^{8}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{1+x^{8}} \leq 10^{-8}$
$\therefore \quad\left|\int_{10}^{19} \frac{\sin x}{1+x^{8}} d x\right| \leq \int_{10}^{19} \frac{|\sin x|}{1+x^{8}} d x$
$\quad \leq \int_{10}^{19} 10^{-8}=9 \times 10^{-8} < 10^{-7}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\lim _{n \rightarrow\infty}\left[\frac{n}{n^{2}+1^{2}}+\frac{n}{n^{2}+2^{2}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{2 n}\right]$ is
questions.solution:
$\lim _{n \rightarrow-}\left[\frac{n}{n^{2}+1^{2}}+\frac{n}{n^{2}+2^{2}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{2 n}\right]$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow-}\left[\frac{n}{n^{2}+1^{2}}+\frac{n}{n^{2}+2^{2}}+\ldots+\frac{n}{n^{2}+n^{2}}\right]$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow-} \sum_{r=1}^{-\infty} \frac{n}{n^{2}+r^{2}}$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow-\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{r=1}^{-\infty} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{r}{n}\right)^{2}}$
$\quad=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x$
$\quad=\left[\tan ^{-1} x\right]_{0}^{1}$
$\quad=\tan ^{-1} 1-\tan ^{-1} 0$
$\quad=\frac{\pi}{4}-0=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{0}^{1} e^{x^{2}} d x$
options: is less than or equal to 1
lies in the closed interval $[1, e]$
questions.solution:
We know that, $0 \leq x \leq 1$ $\Rightarrow \quad x^{2} \leq 1$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & e^{x^{2}} \leq e \\ \Rightarrow & \int_{0}^{1} e^{x^{2}} d x \leq \int_{0}^{1} e d x \\ \Rightarrow & \int_{0}^{1} e^{x^{2}} d x \leq e \\ \therefore \quad \int_{0}^{-} e^{x^{2}} \in[1, e]\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{0}^{100} e^{x-[x]} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{0}^{100} e^{x-[x]} d x$
$=100 \int_{0}^{1} e^{x-[x]} d x$
$[\because x-[x]$ is a periodic function of period 1 and $\int_{0}^{mT} f(x) d x=m \int_{0}^{T} f(x) d x,$ where $T$ is period of $f(x)]$ $=100 \int_{0}^{1} e^{x} d x[\because x-[x]=x$ for $0    < x < 1]$
$=100\left[e^{x}\right]_{0}^{1}$
$=100\left[e^{1}-e^{0}\right]$
$=100(e-1)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\int_{-1}^{x}|t|$ dt, then for any $x \geq 0, f(x)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, 
$\begin{aligned} f(x) &=\int_{-1}^{x} t\left|d t=\int_{-1}^{0}\right| t\left|d t+\int_{0}^{x}\right| t \mid d t \\ &=-\int_{-1}^{0} t d t+\int_{0}^{x} t d t \\ &=\left[-\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{-1}^{0}+\left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{x} \\ &=-\frac{1}{2}\left(0^{2}-(-1)^{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(x^{2}-0^{2}\right) \\ &=-\frac{1}{2}(-1)+\frac{1}{2}\left(x^{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \end{aligned}$

questions.q: Let $I=\int_{0}^{{100 \pi }} \sqrt{(1-\cos 2 x)} d x$, then
questions.solution:
$I=\int_{0}^{1004} \sqrt{1-\cos 2 x} d x$
$=\int_{0}^{100 \pi} \sqrt{2 \sin ^{2} x} d x$
$=\sqrt{2} \int_{0}^{100 \pi} \sin x \mid d x$
$=\sqrt{2} \times 100 \int_{0}^{n} \sin x|d x|$
$[\sin x \mid$ has period of $\pi]$
$=100 \sqrt{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} sin x d x$
$=100 \sqrt{2}[-\cos x]_{0}^{\pi}$
$=100 \sqrt{2}\{(-\cos \pi)-(-\cos 0)]$
$=100 \sqrt{2}[-(-1)-(-1)]$
$=100 \sqrt{2} \times 2$
$=200 \sqrt{2}$

questions.q: Let $f$ be a non-constant continuous function for all $x \geq 0$. Let $f$ satisfy the relation $f(x) f(a-x)=1$ for some $a \in R^{+}$. Then, $I=\int_{0}^{a} \frac{d x}{1+f(x)}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$I=\int_{0}^{a} \frac{d x}{1+f(x)}$
$=\int_{0}^{a} \frac{1}{1+f(a-x)} d x$
$\quad\left[\because \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x\right]$
$=\int_{0}^{a} \frac{d x}{1+\frac{1}{f(x)}} \quad[\because f(x) f(a-x)=1]$
$I=\int_{0}^{a} \frac{f(x)}{f(x)+1} d x$
On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $2 I=\int_{0}^{a} \frac{f(x)+1}{f(x)+1} d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{a} 1 d x$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & 2 t=[x]_{0}^{a} \\ \Rightarrow & 2 I=a \\ \Rightarrow & I=\frac{a}{2}\end{array}$
72. $(b,$ d) We have, $x=1-2 x$
$\Rightarrow y=\frac{1-2 x}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-2 x-(1-2 x) \cdot 1}{x^{2}}$
$\quad=\frac{-2 x-1+2 x}{x^{2}}=\frac{-1}{x^{2}}    < 0$
since, $a x+b y+c=0$ is tangent to the curve $x=1-2 x$
$\therefore \quad \frac{-a}{b} < 0 \Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}>0$
$\Rightarrow$ Either $a>a b>0$ or $a < 0, b < 0$

questions.q: $\int_{0}^{1} \log \left(\frac{1}{x}-1\right) d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $\quad I=\int_{0}^{1} \log \left(\frac{1}{x}-1\right) d x$
$\therefore$
$=\int_{0}^{1} \log \left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right) d x$
$I=\int_{0}^{1} \log \left(\frac{x}{1-x}\right) d x=-1$
$\quad\left[\because \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x\right]$
$2 l=0 \Rightarrow I=0$

questions.q: The value of $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{n+1}+\sqrt{n+2}+\ldots+\sqrt{2 n-1}}{n^{3 / 2}}\right\}$ is
questions.solution:
$\because \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty \left[\frac{\sqrt{n+1}+\sqrt{n+2}+\ldots+\sqrt{2 n-1}}{n^{\frac{3}{2}}}\right]}$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty 1}\left[\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{n}}+\sqrt{1+\frac{2}{n}}+\ldots+\sqrt{1+\frac{n-1}{n}}\right] \frac{1}{n}$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty 1} \sum_{r=1}^{n-1} \frac{1}{n} \sqrt{1+\frac{r}{n}}$
$=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{1+x} d x=\left[\frac{2}{3}(1+x)^{3 / 2}\right]_{0}^{1}=\frac{2}{3}(2 \sqrt{2}-1)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be a continuous function which satisfies $f(x)=\int_{0}^{x} f(t) d t .$ Then. the value of $f\left(\log _{e} 5\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad f(x)=\int_{0}^{x} f(t) d t$
Using Leibnitz theorem, we get
$$
f^{\prime}(x)=f(x) \Rightarrow f(x)=k e^{x}
$$
On putting $x=0$, we get $f(0)=\int_{0}^{0} f(t) d t$
$\Rightarrow \quad k e^{0}=0$
$\left.\because \int_{a}^{a} f(x) d x=0\right]$
$\left.\Rightarrow \quad k=0 \quad \mid \because e^{0}=1\right]$
$\therefore \quad f(x)=0 \Rightarrow f\left(\log _{e} 5\right)=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\ldots+\sqrt{n-1}}{n \sqrt{n}}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\ldots+\sqrt{n-1}}{n \sqrt{n}}$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\ldots+\sqrt{n-1}+\sqrt{n}}{n \sqrt{n}}-\frac{n}{n \times n}\right)$
$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{r=1}^{n}\left(\frac{r}{n}\right)^{1 / 2}-\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} \times \frac{n}{n}=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{x} d x+0=\frac{2}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)$ denotes the fractional part of a real number $x$. Then, the value of $\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}} f\left(x^{2}\right) d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}} f\left(x^{2}\right) d x=\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}\left\{x^{2}\right\} d x$
$\left.=\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}\left(x^{2}-\mid x^{2}\right)\right) d x$
$=\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}} x^{2} d x-\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}\left|x^{2}\right| d x$
$=\left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}-1 \int_{0}^{1}\left|x^{2}\right| d x+\int_{1}^{\sqrt{2}}\left|x^{2}\right| d x+\int_{\sqrt{2}}\left|x^{2}\right| d x \mid$
$=\sqrt{3}-\left[\int_{0}^{1} 0 d x+\int_{1}^{\sqrt{2}} 1 d x+\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{3}} 2 d x\right]$
$=\sqrt{3}-\left\{0+|x|_{i}^{\sqrt{2}}+|2 x| \sqrt{2}\right\}$
$=\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2}+1-2 \sqrt{3}+2 \sqrt{2}$
$=\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{3}+1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $I=\int_{0}^{2} e^{x^{4}}(x-a) d x=0$, then $\alpha$ lies in the interval
questions.solution:
Given, $I=\int_{0}^{2} e^{x^{4}}(x-\alpha) d x=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \int_{0}^{2} e^{x^{4}} x d x=\int_{0}^{2} e^{x^{4}} \alpha d x$
Here, we see that $\int_{0}^{2} e^{x^{4}} x d x$ gives us an area between two curves $e^{x^{4}}$ and $x$ from $x=0$ to $x=2$
Similarly. $\int_{0}^{2} e^{x^{4}} \alpha d x$ gives us a same area, so $a$ should lies between 0 to $2, i . e ., \alpha \in(0,2)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Suppose $M=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x}{x+2} d x$ $N=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x \cos x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x$ Then, the value of $(M-N)$ equals
questions.solution:
Given, $M=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x}{(x+2)} d x$
and $\quad N=\int_{0}^{\sqrt{4}} \frac{\sin x \cos x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad N=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{1}{2} \frac{\sin 2 x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x$
Put $2 x=t \Rightarrow d x=\frac{d t}{2}$
$\begin{aligned} N &=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin t}{4\left(\frac{t}{2}+1\right)^{2}} d t \\ &=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin t}{(t+2)^{2}} d t \\ &=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin x}{(x+2)^{2}} d x \end{aligned}$
$M-N=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \cos x \times \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x$
$=\left[\frac{\sin x}{x+2}\right]_{0}^{\pi / 2}-\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}-\frac{\sin x}{(x+2)^{2}} d x$
$-\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin x}{(x+2)^{2}} d x$
$=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{2}}{\frac{\pi}{2}+2}=\frac{1}{\frac{\pi+4}{2}}=\frac{2}{\pi+4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{-1}^{1}\left\{\frac{x^{2013}}{e^{|x|}\left(x^{2}+\cos x\right)}+\frac{1}{e^{|x|}}\right\} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $\quad I=\int_{-1}^{1}\left\{\frac{x^{2013}}{e^{\mid x\left|(x^{2}+\cos x\right)}}+\frac{1}{e^{|x|}}\right\} d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{x^{2013}}{e^{|x|+\left(x^{2}+\cos x\right)}} d x+\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{1}{e^{|x|}} d x$
Here, $\frac{x^{2013}}{e^{|x|}\left(x^{2}+\cos x\right)}$ is an odd function
and $\frac{1}{e^{\mid x|}}$ is an even function. $\left\{\because \int_{-a}^{a} f(x) d x=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}2 \int_{0}^{9}(x) d x ; & f(x) \text { is even } \\ 0, & f(x) \text { is odd }\end{array}\right\}\right.$
$\left.\therefore \quad 1=0+2 \int_{0}^{1} e^{-x} d x=-2 (e^{-x})\right\}_{0}^{1}=-2\left(e^{-1}\right)$
$=2\left(1-e^{-1}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of $I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left(\tan ^{n+1} x\right) d x$
$+\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \tan ^{n+1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) d x$ is
questions.solution:
.Given. $I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\tan ^{n+1} x\right) d x+\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \tan ^{n-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) d x$
In second integral, putt $=\frac{x}{2} \Rightarrow d x=2 d t$
$\Rightarrow \quad$ Also, when $x=0$ then $t=0$
When $x=\pi / 2,$ then $t=\pi / 4$
Then, $\begin{aligned} I &=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\tan ^{n+1} x\right) d x+\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{n-1} t d t \\ & I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{n+1} x \cdot d x+\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{n-1} x d x \\ & \quad\left\{\because \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(y) d y\right\} \end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\tan ^{n+1} x+\tan ^{n-1} x\right) d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{n-1} x \cdot\left(\tan ^{2} x+1\right) d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{n-1} x\left(\sec ^{2} x\right) d x$
Put $\quad t=\tan x$
$\Rightarrow \quad d t=\sec ^{2} x d x$
Also, when $x=0,$ then $t=0$
when $x=\pi / 4,$ then $t=1$ $I=\int_{0}^{1} t^{n-1} d t=\left[\frac{t^{n}}{n}\right]_{0}^{1}=\frac{1}{n}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The sum of the series
$\frac{1}{1 \times 2}^{25} C_{0}+\frac{1}{2 \times 3}{ }^{25} C_{1}+\frac{1}{3 \times 4}{ }^{25} C_{2}+\ldots \\
+\frac{1}{26 \times 27}{ }^{25} C_{25} \text { is }$
questions.solution:
Given series is, $\frac{1}{1 \times 2} \stackrel{25}{C_{0}}+\frac{1}{2 \times 3}{ }^{25} C_{1}+\frac{1}{3 \times 4}{ }^{25} C_{2}$
$+\ldots+\frac{1}{26 \times 27}^{25} \mathrm{C}_{25}$
$\begin{aligned} \int_{0}^{x}(1+x)^{25} d x &=\int_{0}^{x}\left[1^{25} C_{0}+{ }^{25} C_{1} x\right.\\ &\left.+{ }^{25} C_{2} x^{2}+\ldots+{ }^{25} C_{25} x^{25}\right] d x \end{aligned}$
On integrating w. I.t. $x$, taking limits 0 to $x$, we get $\left[\frac{(1+x)^{26}}{26}\right]_{0}^{x}$
$=\left[2^{2} \mathrm{C}_{0} x+{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{1} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2}+{ }^{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \frac{x^{3}}{3}+\ldots+{ }^{2} \mathrm{C}_{25} \cdot \frac{x^{26}}{26}\right]_{0}^{x}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad &\left\{\frac{1}{26}(1+x)^{26}-\frac{1}{26}\right\} \\ &={ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{0} x+{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{1} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2}+\ldots+{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{25} \cdot \frac{x^{26}}{26} \end{aligned}$
Again, integrating w.r.t. $x$, taking limits 0 to $1,$ we get
$\left.\frac{1}{26} \int_{0}^{1}[1+x)^{26}-1\right] d x$
$\begin{aligned} &\left.\frac{1}{26} \int_{0}^{1}[1+x)^{26}-1\right] d x \\=& \int_{0}^{1}\left[2^{25} C_{0} x+{ }^{25} C_{1} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2}+\ldots+{ }^{25} C_{25} \frac{x^{26}}{26}\right] d x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{1}{26}\left[\frac{(1+x)^{27}}{27}-x\right]_{0}^{1} \end{aligned}$
$=\left[{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{0} \frac{x^{2}}{2}+{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{1} \cdot \frac{x^{3}}{2 \times 3}+\ldots+{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{25} \frac{x^{27}}{26 \times 27}\right]_{0}^{1}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{26}\left\{\frac{2^{27}}{27}-1-\frac{1}{27}\right\}=\frac{1}{2} & C_{0}+\frac{1}{2 \times 3} \cdot{ }^{25} C_{1} \\ &+\ldots+\frac{1}{26 \times 27} \cdot{ }^{25} C_{25} \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{1}{1 \times 2} \cdot^{25} \mathrm{C}_{0} &+\frac{1}{2 \times 3}{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{1}+\frac{1}{3 \times 4}{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{2} \\ \quad &+\ldots+\frac{1}{26 \times 27} \cdot{ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_{25}=\frac{2^{27}-28}{26 \times 27} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{1}^{2} e^{x}\left(\log _{e} x+\frac{x+1}{x}\right) d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{1}^{2} e^{x}\left(\log _{e} x+\frac{x+1}{x}\right) d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{1}^{2}\left(e^{x} \cdot \log _{e} x+e^{x}+\frac{e^{x}}{x}\right) d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{1}^{2} e^{x} \log _{e} x d x+\int_{1}^{2} e^{x} d x+\int_{1}^{2} \frac{e^{x}}{x} d x$
$-\int_{1}^{2} e^{x} \log _{e} x d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\left(e^{2}-e^{1}\right)+\left(e^{2} \log _{e} 2-0\right)$
$=e^{2}\left(1+\log _{e} 2\right)-e$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If [a] denote the greatest integer which is less than or equal to a. Then, the value of the integral $\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}[\sin x \cos x] d x$ is
questions.solution: Let $/=\int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2}[\sin x \cdot \cos x] d x=\int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2}\left[\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x\right] d x$ Put $\theta=2 x \Rightarrow d \theta=2 d x$ Also, when $\quad x=-\pi / 2,$ then $\theta=-\pi$ when $\quad x=\frac{\pi}{2},$ then $\theta=\pi$ $\begin{aligned} \text { Then, } & I=\frac{1}{2} \int_{\pi}^{\pi}\left[\frac{1}{2} \sin \theta\right] d \theta \\ &=\frac{1}{2}\left[\int_{\pi}^{0}(-1) d x+\int_{0}^{\pi}(0) d x\right] \\ &=\frac{1}{2}[-x]_{\pi}^{2}+0=-\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of integral $\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}}\frac{(sinx-xcosx)}{x(x+sinx)}dx$ is
questions.solution:
Let $l=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{(\sin x-x \cos x)}{x(x+\sin x)} d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{(x+\sin x)-x(1+\cos x)}{x(x+\sin x)} d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1+\cos x}{x+\sin x}\right) d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1=[\log \times]_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}-\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{1+\cos x}{x+\sin x} \cdot d x$
$\left\{\begin{array}{l}\text { put } t=x+\sin x \\ \text { ot }=(1+\cos x) d x\end{array}\right.$ in lind term
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\left(\log \frac{\pi}{3}-\log \frac{\pi}{6}\right)-\int\left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \frac{d t}{t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\log 2-\left[\log t \mid\left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right.$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1=\log 2-\left[\log \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)-\log \left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{1}{2}\right)\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\log 2-\log \left(\frac{2 \pi+3 \sqrt{3}}{\pi+3}\right)$
$\left(\because \log m-\log n=\log \frac{m}{n}\right)$
$I=\log \left(\frac{2(\pi+3)}{2 \pi+3 \sqrt{3}}\right)$
$=\log \left(\frac{2 \pi+6}{2 \pi+3 \sqrt{3}}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{1+\sin 2 x+\cos 2 x}{\sin x+\cos x}\right) d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{1+\sin 2 x+\cos 2 x}{\sin x+\cos x}\right) d x$
$=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{1+2 \sin x \cos x+2 \cos ^{2} x-1}{(\sin x+\cos x)}\right) d x$
$=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 2} \frac{2 \cos x(\sin x+\cos x)}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x$
$=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 2} 2 \cos x d x=2[\sin x]_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 2}$
$=2\left(\sin \frac{\pi}{2}-\sin \frac{\pi}{6}\right)=2\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)=2 \times \frac{1}{2}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{1+(\tan x)^{-101}} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{1+(\tan x)^{-101}} d x$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{1+\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)\right\}^{101}}$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{1+(\cot x)^{101}}$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\tan x^{10 t}}{\tan x^{101}+1} d x$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1+\tan x^{101}-1}{1+\tan x^{101}}=[x]_{0}^{\pi / 2}-I$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1=\frac{\pi}{2}-I \Rightarrow 2 I=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of the integral $\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+\sin 2 x} d x$
is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+\sin 2 x} d x$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+2 \sin x \cos x} d x$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}-\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{(\sin x-\cos x)^{2}-4} d x$
Put $\sin x-\cos x=t$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\cos x+\sin x) d x=d t$
when $x=0 \Rightarrow t=-1$
and $x=\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow t=0$
$\therefore$ Eq. (i) becomes, $I=-\int_{-1}^{0} \frac{d t}{t^{2}-4}=-\left.\frac{1}{4}\left[\log \mid \frac{t-2}{t+2}\right]\right|_{-1} ^{0}$
$=-\frac{1}{4}(\log 1-\log 3)$
$=-\frac{1}{4}(0-\log 3)=\frac{1}{4} \log 3$

equal to
questions.solution:

$=\int_{-2}^{2} e^{|x|} d x+2 \int_{-2}^{2} \sin x e^{|x|} d x$
(Since, first function is even and second function is odd). $=2 \int_{0}^{2} e^{|x|} d x+2 \text{ instead of ,} 0=2\left[e^{|x|}\right]_{0}^{2}=2\left(e^{2}-1\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\int_{-1}^4 f(x) d x=4$ and $\int_2^4\{3-f(x)\} d x=7$ then the value of $\int_{-1}^2 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}$
questions.solution:
Hints : $\int_{-1}^4 f(x) d x=4$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 3(4-2)-\int_2^4 f(x) d x=7 \\
& \int_2^4 f(x) d x=-1 \\
& \int_{-1}^2 f(x) d x=\int_{-1}^4 f(x) d x+\int_4^2 f(x) d x=4-\int_2^4 f(x) d x=4-(-1)=5
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\det A = \begin{vmatrix} l & m & n \\ p & q & r \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$. If $(l - m)^2 + (p - q)^2 = 9, (m - n)^2 + (q - r)^2 = 16, (n - l)^2 + (r - p)^2 = 25$, then the value of $(\det A)^2$ is
questions.solution:
Let the points in the Cartesian plane be $P_1(l, p)$, $P_2(m, q)$, and $P_3(n, r)$.

The given equations represent the squares of the distances between these points:

$P_1 P_2^2 = (l - m)^2 + (p - q)^2 = 9 \Rightarrow P_1 P_2 = 3$

$P_2 P_3^2 = (m - n)^2 + (q - r)^2 = 16 \Rightarrow P_2 P_3 = 4$

$P_3 P_1^2 = (n - l)^2 + (r - p)^2 = 25 \Rightarrow P_3 P_1 = 5$

Since $P_1 P_2^2 + P_2 P_3^2 = 9 + 16 = 25 = P_3 P_1^2$, the triangle formed by these three points is a right-angled triangle.

The area of $\Delta P_1 P_2 P_3$ is given by:

Area $= \dfrac{1}{2} \times P_1 P_2 \times P_2 P_3 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 4 = 6$

The area of a triangle with vertices $(l, p)$, $(m, q)$, and $(n, r)$ can also be expressed using a determinant:

Area $= \dfrac{1}{2} \left| \begin{vmatrix} l & m & n \\ p & q & r \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} \right| = \dfrac{1}{2} |\det A|$

Equating the two expressions for the area:

$\dfrac{1}{2} |\det A| = 6 \Rightarrow |\det A| = 12$

Squaring both sides, we obtain:

$(\det A)^2 = 144$

Answer: $144$

questions.q: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}5 & 5 \alpha & \alpha \\ 0 & \alpha & 5 \alpha \\ 0 & 0 & 5\end{array}\right]$. If $|\mathrm{A}|^2=25$, then $|\alpha|$ equals to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
5 & 5 \alpha & \alpha \\
0 & \alpha & 5 \alpha \\
0 & 0 & 5
\end{array}\right] \\
& |A|=5(5 \alpha)-5 \alpha(0)+\alpha(0)
\end{aligned}$
$|A|=25 \alpha \quad \Rightarrow|A|^2=625 \alpha^2=25 \quad \Rightarrow \alpha^2=\frac{1}{25} \quad \Rightarrow|\alpha|=\frac{1}{5}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(A\) and \(B\) are orthogonal matrices and \(\operatorname{det} A+\operatorname{det} B=0\). Then
questions.solution:
Hint: \(A A^{\top}=I\) and \(B B^{\top}=I\)
\(\Rightarrow \operatorname{det}\left(A A^{\top}\right)=1\) and \(\operatorname{det}\left(B B^{\top}\right)=1\)
\(\operatorname{det}(A)=-\operatorname{det}(B)\) (Given) \( \Rightarrow(\operatorname{det}(A))^2=1 \Rightarrow(\operatorname{det}(B))^2=1\)
\(\therefore \operatorname{det}(A+B)=\operatorname{det}\left(A\left(B^{\top}+A^{\top}\right) B\right)\)
\(=-\operatorname{det}\left(B^{\top}+A^{\top}\right) \quad[\because(\operatorname{det}(A))(\operatorname{det}(B))=-1]\) as \(\operatorname{det}(A)=-\operatorname{det}(B)\)
\(=-\operatorname{det}(B+A)^{\top}\)
\(=-\operatorname{det}(A+B)^{\top}\)
\(=-\operatorname{det}(A+B)\)
\(\Rightarrow 2 \operatorname{det}(A+B)=0\)
\(\Rightarrow \operatorname{det}(A+B)=0\)
\(\therefore(A+B)\) is singular

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(\alpha, \beta\) be the roots of the equation \(a x^2+b x+c=0, a, b, c\) real and \(s_n=\alpha^n+\beta^n\) and \(\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3 & 1+s_1 & 1+s_2 \\ 1+s_1 & 1+s_2 & 1+s_3 \\ 1+s_2 & 1+s_3 & 1+s_4\end{array}\right|=k \frac{(a+b+c)^2}{a^4}\) then \(k=\)
questions.solution:
Hint: \(\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3 & 1+s_1 & 1+s_2 \\ 1+s_1 & 1+s_2 & 1+s_3 \\ 1+s_2 & 1+s_3 & 1+s_4\end{array}\right|\)
\(=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1+1+1 & 1+\alpha+\beta & 1+\alpha^2+\beta^2 \\
1+\alpha+\beta & 1+\alpha^2+\beta^2 & 1+\alpha^3+\beta^3 \\
1+\alpha^2+\beta^2 & 1+\alpha^3+\beta^3 & 1+\alpha^4+\beta^4
\end{array}\right|\)
\(\begin{aligned} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & \alpha & \beta \\ 1 & \alpha^2 & \beta^2\end{array}\right| \times\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & \alpha & \alpha^2 \\ 1 & \beta & \beta^2\end{array}\right| \\ & =\{(1-\alpha)(\alpha-\beta)(1-\beta)\}^2 \\ & =(1-(\alpha+\beta)+\alpha \beta)^2(\alpha-\beta)^2 \\ & =\left(1+\frac{b}{a}+\frac{c}{a}\right)^2\left(\frac{b^2}{a^2}-\frac{4 c}{a}\right) \\ & =\frac{(a+b+c)^2}{a^2} \times \frac{b^2-4 a c}{a^2}=\left(b^2-4 a c\right) \frac{(a+b+c)^2}{a^4} \\ & \therefore k=b^2-4 a c\end{aligned}\)
