questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\sin \theta \cos \phi & \sin \theta \sin \phi & \cos \theta \\ \cos \theta \cos \phi & \cos \theta \sin \phi & -\sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta \sin \phi & \sin \theta \cos \phi & 0\end{array}\right|$. Then
questions.solution:
$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\sin \theta \cos \phi & \sin \theta \sin \phi & \cos \theta \\ \cos \theta \cos \phi & \cos \theta \sin \phi & -\sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta \sin \phi & \sin \theta \cos \phi & 0\end{array}\right|$
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{C}_1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_1-\left(\frac{\cos \phi}{\sin \phi}\right) \mathrm{C}_2 \Rightarrow \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & \sin \theta \sin \phi & \cos \theta \\
0 & \cos \theta \sin \phi & -\sin \theta \\
-\frac{\sin \theta}{\sin \phi} & \sin \theta \cos \phi & \theta
\end{array}\right| \\
&=\frac{\sin \theta}{\sin \phi} \cdot \sin \phi\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right)= \sin \theta \rightarrow \text { Independent of } \phi \\
\frac{\mathrm{d} \Delta}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\left.\cos \theta \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \Delta}{\mathrm{d} \theta}\right|_{\theta-\frac{\pi}{2}}=0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \text { cost } & \text { sint } \\ 0 & -\sin t & \cos t\end{array}\right)$
Let $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \lambda_{3}$ be the roots of $\operatorname{det}\left(A-\lambda I_{3}\right)=0$, where $I_{3}$ denotes the identity matrix. If $\lambda_{1}+\lambda_{2}+\lambda_{3}=\sqrt{2}+1$, then the set of possible values of $t,-\pi \leq t     < \pi$ is
questions.solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1-\lambda & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos t-\lambda & \sin t \\ 0 & -\sin t & \cos t-\lambda\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow(1-\lambda)(\cos t-\lambda)^{2}+\sin ^{2} t=0$
$\Rightarrow(1-\lambda)\left(\lambda^{2}-2 \lambda \cos t+\cos ^{2} t\right)+\sin ^{2} t=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda^{2}-2 x \cos t+\cos ^{2} t-\lambda^{3}+2 \lambda^{2} \cos t+\lambda \cos ^{2} t+\sin ^{2} t=0$
$\Rightarrow-\lambda^{3}+\lambda^{2}(1+2 \cos t)+\lambda\left(\cos ^{2} t-2 \cos t\right)+1=0$
$\lambda_{1}+\lambda_{2}+\lambda_{3}=1+2 \operatorname{cost}=1+\sqrt{2}$
$\therefore$ cost $=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi}{4},-\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a_{n}(>0)$ be the $n^{\text {th }}$ term of a G.P. then $\left|\begin{array}{lll}\log a_{n} & \log a_{n+1} & \log a_{n+2} \\ \log a_{n+3} & \log a_{n+4} & \log a_{n+5} \\ \log a_{n+6} & \log a_{n+7} & \log a_{n+8}\end{array}\right|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\log a_{n}=\log \left(a r^{n-1}\right)=\log a+(n-1) \log r$
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\log \mathrm{a}+(\mathrm{n}-1) \log \mathrm{r} & \log \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{nlogr} & \log \mathrm{a}+(\mathrm{n}+1) \log \mathrm{r} \\ 3 \log \mathrm{r} & 3 \log \mathrm{r} & 3 \log \mathrm{r} \\ 6 \log r & 6 \log r & 6 \log r\end{array}\right|$ = 0

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The determinant $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^{2}+10 & a b & a c \\ a b & b^{2}+10 & b c \\ a c & b c & c^{2}+10\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
$=\frac{1}{a b c}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a\left(a^{2}+10\right) & a^{2} b & a^{2} c \\ a b^{2} & b\left(b^{2}+10\right) & b^{2} c \\ a c^{2} & b c^{2} & c\left(c^{2}+10\right)\end{array}\right|=\frac{a b c}{a b c}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^{2}+10 & a^{2} & a^{2} \\ b^{2} & b^{2}+10 & b^{2} \\ c^{2} & c^{2} & c^{2}+10\end{array}\right|$
$\mathrm{R}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}+\mathrm{R}_{3}$
$=\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\mathrm{c}^{2}+10\right)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ \mathrm{~b}^{2} & \mathrm{~b}^{2}+10 & \mathrm{~b}^{2} \\ \mathrm{c}^{2} & \mathrm{c}^{2} & \mathrm{c}^{2}+10\end{array}\right|$
$=\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\mathrm{c}^{2}+10\right)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & 0 \\ \mathrm{~b}^{2} & 10 & 0 \\ \mathrm{c}^{2} & 0 & 10\end{array}\right|\left[\begin{array}{c}\mathrm{C}_{2}-\mathrm{C}_{1} \\ \mathrm{C}_{3}-\mathrm{C} 1\end{array}\right]$
$=\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\mathrm{c}^{2}+10\right) 100$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & 3 x+2 & 2 x-1 \\ 2 x-1 & 4 x & 3 x+1 \\ 7 x-2 & 17 x+6 & 12 x-1\end{array}\right|=0$ is true for
questions.solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & 3 x+2 & 2 x-1 \\ 2 x-1 & 4 x & 3 x+1 \\ 7 x-2 & 17 x+6 & 12 x-1\end{array}\right|=0$
$\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-3 \mathrm{R}_{1}-2 \mathrm{R}_{2}$
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & 3 x+2 & 2 x-1 \\ 2 x-1 & 4 x & 3 x+1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0\end{array}\right|=0$
Infinite values of $x$ is possible.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{A}=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}3-\mathrm{t} & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 3-\mathrm{t} & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & 0\end{array}\right)$ and $\operatorname{det} \mathrm{A}=5$, then
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3-\mathrm{t} & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 3-\mathrm{t} & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & 0\end{array}\right|=3-\mathrm{t}=5, \mathrm{t}=-2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^{2} & b c & c^{2}+a c \\ a^{2}+a b & b^{2} & c a \\ a b & b^{2}+b c & c^{2}\end{array}\right|=k a^{2} b^{2} c^{2}$, then $k=$
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^{2} & b c & c^{2}+a c \\ a^{2}+a b & b^{2} & c a \\ a b & b^{2}+b c & c^{2}\end{array}\right|=(a b c)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & c & a+c \\ a+b & b & a \\ b & b+c & c\end{array}\right|$
opening through $\mathrm{R}-1=4 \mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{~b}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The system of equations $$ \begin{array}{l} \lambda x+y+3 z=0 \\ 2 x+\mu y-z=0 \\ 5 x+7 y+z=0 \end{array} $$ has infinitely many solutions in $R$. Then,
questions.solution:
Given, system of equations
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\lambda x+y+3 z=0 \\
2 x+\mu y-z=0 \\
5 x+7 y+z=0
\end{array}
$$
System has infinitely many solutions in $R$, if
$$
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\lambda & 1 & 3 \\
2 & \mu & -1 \\
5 & 7 & 1
\end{array}\right|=0
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda(\mu+7)-1(2+5)+3(14-5 \mu)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda(\mu+7)-7+3(14-5 \mu)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \mu+7 \lambda-7+42-15 \mu=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \mu+7 \lambda-15 \mu+35=0$
By checking the options, we get option (c) $(\lambda=1, \mu=3)$ satisfies the given equation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & 7 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 3 & 4 & 1\end{array}\right|=A$. Then $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}13 & -11 & 5 \\ -7 & -1 & 25 \\ -21 & -3 & -15\end{array}\right|$ is \begin{array}{l} \hline \end{array} $\left(I_{3}\right.$ denotes the det of the identity matrix of order 3 )
questions.solution:
We have.
$$
\begin{aligned}
A=&\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
-1 & 7 & 0 \\
2 & 1 & -3 \\
3 & 4 & 1
\end{array}\right| \\
&=-1(1+12)-7(2+9) \\
&=-13-77=-90
\end{aligned}
$$
And let $\quad B=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}13 & -11 & 5 \\ -7 & -1 & 25 \\ -21 & -3 & -15\end{array}\right|$
$$
=3 \times 5\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
13 & -11 & 1 \\
-7 & -1 & 5 \\
-7 & -1 & -1
\end{array}\right|
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=15\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
13 & -11 & 1 \\
-7 & -1 & 5 \\
0 & 0 & -6
\end{array}\right|\left(R_{3} \rightarrow R_{3}-R_{2}\right) \\
=15\{0-0-6(-13-77)\} \\
=15\{(-6)(-90)\}=90 \times 90
\end{array}
$$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii),
$$
B=A^{2}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $a_{r}=(\cos 2 r \pi+i \sin 2 r \pi)^{1 / 9}$, then the value of
$\left|\begin{array}{lll}a_{1} & a_{2} & a_{3} \\ a_{4} & a_{5} & a_{6} \\ a_{7} & a_{8} & a_{9}\end{array}\right|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have,
$a_{r}=(\cos 2 r \pi+i \sin 2 r \pi)^{1 / 9}=e^{\frac{2 r \pi i}{9}}$
Now, $\left|\begin{array}{lll}a_{1} & a_{2} & a_{3} \\ a_{4} & a_{5} & a_{6} \\ a_{7} & a_{8} & a_{9}\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\ e^{\frac{2 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{4 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{6 \pi i}{9}} \\ e^{\frac{8 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{10 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{12 \pi i}{9}} \\ e^{\frac{14 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{16 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{18 \pi i}{9}}\end{array}\right|$
$=e^{\frac{2 \pi i}{9}} \times e^{\frac{8 \pi i}{9}}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & e^{\frac{2 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{4 \pi i}{9}} \\ 1 & e^{\frac{2 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{4 \pi i}{9}} \\ e^{\frac{14 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{16 \pi i}{9}} & e^{\frac{18 \pi i}{9}}\end{array}\right|$
$=e^{\frac{2 \pi i}{9}} \times e^{\frac{8 \pi}{9}} \times 0 \quad\left(\because R_{1}\right.$ and $R_{2}$ are identical $)$
$=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $S_{r}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 r & x & n(n+1) \\ 6 r^{2}-1 & y & n^{2}(2 n+3) \\ 4 r^{3}-2 n r & z & n^{3}(n+1)\end{array}\right|$, then the
value of $\sum_{r=1}^{n} S_{r}$ is independent of
questions.solution:
We have, $S_{r}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 r & x & n(n+1) \\ 6 r^{2}-1 & y & n^{2}(2 n+3) \\ 4 r^{3}-2 n r & z & n^{3}(n+1)\end{array}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{r=1}^{n} S_{r}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 \sum_{r=1}^{n} r & x & n(n+1) \\ \sum_{r=1}^{n}\left(6 r^{2}-1\right) & y & n^{2}(2 n+3) \\ \sum_{r=1}^{n}\left(4 r^{3}-2 n r\right) & z & n^{3}(n+1)\end{array}\right|$
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}n(n+1) & x & n(n+1) \\ n^{2}(2 n+3) & y & n^{2}(2 n+3) \\ n^{3}(n+1) & z & n^{3}(n+1)\end{array}\right|$
$=0 \quad\left(\because C_{1}\right.$ and $C_{3}$ are identical $)$
Hence, $\sum_{r=1}^{n} S_{r}$ is independent of $x, y, z$ and $n$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the following three linear equations have a non-trivial solution, then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
x+4 a y+a z=0 \\
x+3 b y+b z=0 \\
x+2 c y+c z=0
\end{array}
$$
questions.solution:
For non-trivial solution, We have, $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 4 a & a \\ 1 & 3 b & b \\ 1 & 2 c & c\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1(3 b c-2 b c)-(4 a c-2 a c)+1(4 a b-3 a b)=0$
$\Rightarrow b c-2 a c+a b=0$
$\Rightarrow b c+a b=2 a c$
$\Rightarrow b(a+c)=2 a c$
$\Rightarrow b=\frac{2 a c}{a+c}$
$\Rightarrow a, b, c$ are in $\mathrm{HP}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the polynomial $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}(1+x)^{a} & (2+x)^{b} & 1 \\ 1 & (1+x)^{a} & (2+x)^{b} \\ (2+x)^{b} & 1 & (1+x)^{a}\end{array}\right|,$ then the
constant term of $f(x)$ is $[a$ and $b$ are positive integers $]$
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}(1+x)^{a} & (2+x)^{b} & 1 \\ 1 & (1+x)^{4} & (2+x)^{b} \\ (2+x)^{b} & 1 & (1+x)^{a}\end{array}\right|$
For constant term put $x=0$ $f(0)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2^{b} & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2^{b} & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=1\left(1-2^{b}\right)-2^{b}\left(1-2^{2 b}\right)+1\left(1-2^{b}\right)$
$=1-2^{b}-2^{b}+2^{3 b}+1-2^{b}$
$=2-3 \cdot 2^{b}+2^{3 b}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The linear system of equations
$\left.\begin{array}{l}8 x-3 y-5 z=0 \\ 5 x-8 y+3 z=0 \\ 3 x+5 y-8 z=0\end{array}\right\}$ has
options: only finite number of non-zero solutions
questions.solution:
We have, 
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 8x-3 y-5 z=0 \\
& 5 x-8 y+3 z=0 \\
& 3 x+5 y-8 z=0 \\
&D=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
8 & -3 & -5 \\
5 & -8 & 3 \\
3 & 5 & -8
\end{array}\right| \\
&=8(64-15)+3(-40-9)-5(25+24) \\
=& 8 \times 49+3 \times(-49)-5 \times 49 \\
=& 0
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ The system has infinitely many non-zero solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $A=\left(\begin{array}{cc}x+2 & 3 x \\ 3 & x+2\end{array}\right), B=\left(\begin{array}{cc}x & 0 \\ 5 & x+2\end{array}\right)$. Then
all solutions of the equation det $(A B)=0$ is
questions.solution:
We know that, $\operatorname{det}(A B)=$ det $(A)$ det $(B)$
$\therefore$ $\operatorname{det}(A B)=0$
$\Rightarrow$ det$(A) \cdot$ det $(B)=0$ $\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{cc}x+2 & 3 x \\ 3 & x+2\end{array} \| \begin{array}{cc}x & 0 \\ 5 & x+2\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow\left\{(x+2)^{2}-9 x\right\}\{x(x+2)-0\}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(x^{2}+4 x+4-9 x\right) \times(x+2)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x(x+2)\left(x^{2}-5 x+4\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x(x+2)(x-1)(x-4)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=0,-2,1,4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of det $A$, where $A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos \theta & 0 \\ -\cos \theta & 1 & \cos \theta \\ -1 & -\cos \theta & 1\end{array}\right)$, lies
questions.solution:
We have, $|A|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos \theta & 0 \\ -\cos \theta & 1 & \cos \theta \\ -1 & -\cos \theta & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=1[1-(-\cos \theta)(\cos \theta)]$
$-\cos \theta[-\cos \theta+\cos \theta]+0\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+1\right)$
$=1+\cos ^{2} \theta$
Now, we know that $-1 \leq \cos \theta \leq 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad 0 \leq \cos ^{2} \theta \leq 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1 \leq 1+\cos ^{2} \theta \leq 2$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1 \leq|A| \leq 2$
$\therefore \quad|A| \in[1,2]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $a, b, c$ be such that $b(a+c) \times 0$
If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & a+1 & a-1 \\ -b & b+1 & b-1 \\ c & c-1 & c+1\end{array}\right|$
$+\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a+1 & b+1 & c-1 \\ a-1 & b-1 & c+1 \\ (-1)^{n+2} a & (-1)^{n+1} b & (-1)^{n} c\end{array}\right|=0$
then the value of $n$ is
questions.solution:
We have,
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & a+1 & a-1 \\ -b & b+1 & b-1 \\ c & c-1 & c+1\end{array}\right|$ + $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a+1 & b+1 & c-1 \\ a-1 & b-1 & c+1 \\ (-1)^{n+2} a & (-1)^{n+1} b & (-1)^{n} c\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & a+1 & a-1 \\ -b & b+1 & b-1 \\ c & c-1 & c+1\end{array}\right|$ $+\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a+1 & a-1 & (-1)^{n+2} a \\ b+1 & b-1 & (-1)^{n+1} b \\ c-1 & c+1 & (-1)^{n} c\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & a+1 & a-1 \\ -b & b+1 & b-1 \\ c & c-1 & c+1\end{array}\right|$$+\left|\begin{array}{ccc}(-1)^{n+2} a & a+1 & a-1 \\ (-1)^{n+1} b & b+1 & b-1 \\ (-1)^{n} c & c-1 & c+1\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a\left(1+(-1)^{n+2}\right) & a+1 & a-1 \\ b\left(-1+(-1)^{n+1}\right) & b+1 & b-1 \\ c\left(1+(-1)^{n}\right) & c-1 & c+1\end{array}\right|=0$
$\therefore n$ is any odd integer.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x, y$ and $z$ are greater than 1 , then the value of $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \log _{x} y & \log _{x} z \\ \log _{y} x & 1 & \log _{y} z \\ \log _{z} x & \log _{z} y & 1\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \log _{x}y & \log _{x} z \\ \log _{y} x & 1 & \log _{y} z \\ \log _{z} x & \log _{z} y & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=\left|\begin{array}{lll}\frac{\log x}{\log x} & \frac{\log y}{\log x} & \frac{\log z}{\log x} \\ \frac{\log x}{\log y} & \frac{\log y}{\log y} & \frac{\log z}{\log y} \\ \frac{\log x}{\log z} & \frac{\log y}{\log z} & \frac{\log 2}{\log z}\end{array}\right|$
On taking $\frac{1}{\log x} \frac{1}{\log y} \frac{1}{\log z}$ common from $R_{1}, R_{2}$ and $R_{3}$
we got
$$
=\frac{1}{\log x \cdot \log y \cdot \log z}\left|\begin{array}{lll}
\log x & \log y & \log z \\
\log x & \log y & \log z \\
\log x & \log y & \log z
\end{array}\right|
$$
$\therefore \Delta=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\lambda$ such that the system of equations $2x-y-2 y=2 ; x-2 y+z=-4$,$x+y+\lambda z=4,$ has no solution, is
questions.solution:
If the given system of equations has no solution. then $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & -1 & -2 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & \lambda\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow 2(-2 \lambda-1)+1(\lambda-1)-2(1+2)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-4 \lambda-2+\lambda-1-6=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-3 \lambda-9=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$$
\text { If } f(x)=\begin{array}{|ccc|}
1 & x & x+1\\
2 x & x(x-1) & (x+1)x\\
3 x(x-1) & x(x-1)(x-2) & (x+1)x(x-1)
\end{array}
$$ Then, $f(100)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $f(x)= \begin{array}{|ccc|}1 & x &x+1 \\ 2 x & x(x-1) & (x+1)x\\ 3 x(x-1) & x(x-1)(x-2) &(x+1)x(x-1)\end{array}$ 
Taking common $x,(x-1)$ from $R_{2}$ and $R_{3}$ respectively, we ge $f(x)=x(x-1)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & x & x+1 \\ 2 & x-1 & x+1 \\ 3 x & x(x-2) & (x+1) x\end{array}\right|$
Taking commen $(x+1)$ from $C_{2}$, we get
$f(x)=x(x-1)(x+1)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & x & 1 \\ 2 & x-1 & 1 \\ 3 x & x(x-2) & x\end{array}\right|$
Taking common $x$ from $R_{3}$, we get $f(x)=x^{2}(x-1)(x+1)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & x & 1 \\ 2 & x-1 & 1 \\ 3 & x-2 & 1\end{array}\right|$
Applying $R_{1} \rightarrow R_{1}-R_{2}, R_{2}$ we get
$f(x)=x^2(x-1)(x+1)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ 3 & (x-2) & 1\end{array}\right|$
$\Rightarrow f(100)=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The least positive value of $t$, so that the lines $x=t+\alpha, y+16=0 \quad$ and $\quad y=\alpha x$ are concurrent, is
questions.solution:
Consider the given equation of lines,
$$
\begin{array}{r}
x-(t+\alpha)=0 \\
y+16=0
\end{array}
$$
and
$$
-\alpha x+y=0
$$
Since, these lines are concurrent, therefore the system of equations is consistent.
Now,
$$
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 0 & -(t+\alpha) \\
0 & 1 & 16 \\
-\alpha & 1 & 0
\end{array}\right|=0
$$
$$
\begin{array}{lr}
\Rightarrow & 1(0-16)-(t+\alpha)(0+\alpha)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & -16-\alpha(t+\alpha)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \alpha(t+\alpha)+16=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \alpha^{2}+t \alpha+16=0
\end{array}
$$
Clearly, $\alpha$ should be real. $\therefore t^{2}-4 \times 16 \geq 0 \Rightarrow t^{2}-64 \geq 0$
Hence, least positive value of $t$ is 8.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of distinct real roots of $\left|\begin{array}{lll}\sin x & \cos x & \cos x \\ \cos x & \sin x & \cos x \\ \cos x & \cos x & \sin x\end{array}\right|=0$ in the interval $-\frac{\pi}{4} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{4}$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\sin x & \cos x & \cos x \\ \cos x & \sin x & \cos x \\ \cos x & \cos x & \sin x\end{array}\right|=0$
$\sin x\left(\sin ^{2} x-\cos ^{2} x\right)-\cos x\left(\sin x \cos x-\cos ^{2} x \right ) + \cos x \left(\cos ^{2} x-\sin x \cos x\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \sin x(\sin x-\cos x)(\sin x+\cos x)$ $-\cos ^{2} x(\sin x-\cos x)-\cos ^{2} x$ $(\sin x-\cos x)=0$
$\Rightarrow(\sin x-\cos x)\left(\sin ^{2} x+\sin x \cos x-2 \cos ^{2} x\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow(\sin x-\cos x)\left(\sin ^{2} x+2 \sin x\right.\left.\cos x-\sin x \cos x-2 \cos ^{2} x\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow(\sin x-\cos x)^{2}(\sin x+2 \cos x)=0$
$\Rightarrow \sin x-\cos x=0$ or $\sin x+2 \cos x=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \tan x=1$ or $\tan x=-2$
But $\quad x \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right]$
$\Rightarrow$ Only one solution i.e. $x=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $f:[0, \pi / 2) \rightarrow R$ is defined as
$f(\theta)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \tan \theta & 1 \\ -\tan \theta & 1 & \tan \theta \\ -1 & -\tan \theta & 1\end{array}\right|$ Then, the range of $f$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $f(\theta)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \tan \theta & 1 \\ -\tan \theta & 1 & \tan \theta \\ -1 & -\tan \theta & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=1\left(1+\tan ^{2} \theta\right)-\tan \theta$
$(-\tan \theta+\tan \theta)+1\left(\tan ^{2} \theta+1\right)$
$=2\left(1+\tan ^{2} \theta\right)-0=2 \sec ^{2} \theta$
$\therefore$ Range of $f=(2, \infty)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\omega$ is an imaginary cube root of unity, then the value of the determinant $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+\omega & 0 & -\omega \\ 1+\omega^{2} & \omega & -\omega^{2} \\ \omega+\omega^{2} & \omega & -\omega^{2}\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+\omega & \omega^{2} & -\omega \\ 1+\omega^{2} & \omega & -\omega^{2} \\ \omega+\omega^{2} & \omega & -\omega^{2}\end{array}\right|$
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}0 & \omega^{2} & -\omega \\ 0 & \omega & -\omega^{2} \\ -1+\omega & \omega & -\omega^{2}\end{array}\right|$
$=(-1+\omega)\left(-\omega^{4}+\omega^{2}\right)=(\omega-1)\left(\omega^{2}-c\right.$
$=\omega^{3}-\omega^{2}-\omega^{2}+\omega=-3 \omega^{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $I$ denote the $3 \times 3$ identity matrix and $P$ be a matrix obtained by rearranging the columns of
$I$. Then,
options: there are six distinct choices for $P$ and $\operatorname{det}(P)=1$
there are six distinct choices for $P$ and $\operatorname{det}(P)=\pm 1$
there are more than one choices for $P$ and some of them are not invertible
there are more than one choices for $P$ and $P^{-1}=1$ in each choice
questions.solution:
Given, $I=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$
Then, $\quad \operatorname{det}(I)=1$
If we take $I$ as
$$
A_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right]
$$
Then, $\quad \operatorname{det}\left(I_{1}\right)=-1$
Similarly, there are four other possibilities,
$$
\left[\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}\right] \cdot\left[\begin{array}{lll}
0 & 0 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}\right]
$$
$$
\left[\begin{array}{lll}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right] \cdot\left[\begin{array}{lll}
0 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right]
$$
who will give a determinant either -1 or 1 . Hence, there are six distinct choices for $P$ and
$\operatorname{det}(P)=\pm 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of determinant $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+a^{2}-b^{2} & 2 a b & -2 b \\ 2 a b & 1-a^{2}+b^{2} & 2 a \\ 2 b & -2 a & 1-a^{2}-b^{2}\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Let
$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+a^{2}-b^{2} & 2 a b & -2 b \\ 2 a b & 1-a^{2}+b^{2} & 2 a \\ 2 b & -2 a & 1-a^{2}-b^{2}\end{array}\right|$
Apply $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{1}} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{1}-\mathrm{bC}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{aC}_{3}+\mathrm{C}_{2}$
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+a^{2}-b^{2}+2 b^{2} & 2 a b-2 a b & -2 b \\ 2 a b-2 a b & 1-a^{2}+b^{2}+2 a^{2} & 2 a \\ 2 b-b+a^{2} b+b^{3} & -2 a+a-a^{3}-a b^{2} & 1-a^{2}-b^{2}\end{array}\right|$
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right) & 0 & -2 b \\ 0 & \left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right) & 2 a \\ b\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right) & -a\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right) & \left(1-a^{2}-b^{2}\right)\end{array}\right|$
$=\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & -2 b \\ 0 & 1 & 2 a \\ b & -a & \left(1-a^{2}-b^{2}\right)\end{array}\right|$
$=\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}\left\{\left(1-a^{2}-b^{2}+2 a^{2}\right)+2 b^{2}\right\}$
$=\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right)=\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $P, Q$ and $R$ are angles of $\Delta P Q R$, then the value of $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & \cos R & \cos Q \\ \cos R & -1 & \cos P \\ \cos Q & \cos P & -1\end{array}\right|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Multiplying } C_{1} \text { by } p \text { and then doing }\\
&\begin{aligned}
C_{1} & \rightarrow C_{1}+q C_{2}+r C_{3} \text { , we get } \\
\Delta &=\frac{1}{p}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
-p & \cos R & \cos Q \\
p \cos R & -1 & \cos P \\
p \cos Q & \cos P & -1
\end{array} \mid\right.\\
&=\frac{1}{p}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
-p+q \cos R+r \cos Q & \cos R & \cos Q \\
p \cos R-q+r \cos P & -1 & \cos P \\
p \cos Q+q \cos P-r & \cos P & -1
\end{array}\right| \\
=\frac{1}{p}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & \cos R & \cos Q \\
0 & -1 & \cos P \\
0 & \cos P & -1
\end{array}\right|=0
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}
$$
Using the prejection formula $p=q \cos R+r \cos Q$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The number of real values of $\alpha$ for which the system of equations
$
\begin{array}{l}
x+3 y+5 z=\alpha x \\
5 x+y+3 z=\alpha y
\end{array}
$
$3 x+5 y+z=\alpha z$
has infinite number of solutions is
questions.solution:
The system of equations are
$$
\begin{array}{l}
x+3 y+5 z=\alpha x \\
5 x+y+3 z=\alpha y \\
3 x+5 y+z=\alpha z \\
(1-\alpha) x+3 y+5 z=0 \\
5 x+(1-\alpha) y+3 z=0 \\
3 x+5 y+(1-\alpha) z=0
\end{array}
$$
For infinite number of solutions, we must have $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1-\alpha & 3 & 5 \\ 5 & 1-\alpha & 3 \\ 3 & 5 & 1-\alpha\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc}9-\alpha & 3 & 5 \\ 9-\alpha & 1-\alpha & 3 \\ 9-\alpha & 5 & 1-\alpha\end{array}\right|=0,$
$C_{1} \rightarrow C_{2}+C_{2}+C_{3}$
Taking $(9-\alpha)$ common from the first column, we get $\begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow \quad(9-\alpha)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 1-\alpha & 3 \\ 1 & 5 & 1-\alpha\end{array}\right|=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (9-\alpha)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & 5 \\ 0 & -\alpha-2 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -\alpha-4\end{array} \mid=0\right. \\ \Rightarrow & (9-\alpha)\left|\begin{array}{cc}\alpha+2 & 2 \\ -2 & \alpha+4\end{array}\right|=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (9-\alpha)\left(\alpha^{2}+6 \alpha+8+4\right)=0 \\ (\alpha-9)\left(\alpha^{2}+6 \alpha+12\right)=0\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=9$ is the only real value of $\alpha$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The system of linear equations $\lambda x+y+z=3$
$x-y-2 z=6$
$-x+y+z=\mu$ has
options: infinite number of solutions for $\lambda \neq-1$ and all $\mu$
infinite number of solutions for $\lambda=-1$ and $\mu=3$
questions.solution:
Augmented matrix [A:B] = $\left[\begin{array}{rrrr}1 & -1 & -2 & 6 \\ -1 & 1 & 1 & \mu \\ \lambda & 1 & 1 & 3\end{array}\right]$
$\sim\left[\begin{array}{cccc}1 & -1 & -2 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & \mu+6 \\ \lambda+1 & 0 & -1 & 9\end{array}\right]$
Applying
$$
-\left[\begin{array}{cccc}1 & -1 & -2 & R_{2}+R_{1}, R_{3} \rightarrow R_{3}+R_{1} \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & \mu+6 \\ \lambda+1 & 0 & 0 & 3-\mu\end{array}\right]
$$
Infinite number of solutions for $\lambda=-1$ and $\mu=3$

questions.q: $\omega$ is an imaginary cube root of unity and $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x+\omega^2 & \omega & 1 \\ \omega & \omega^2 & 1+x \\ 1 & x+\omega & \omega^2\end{array}\right|=0$ then one of the values of $x$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Hints: } & \stackrel{C_1^{\prime} \rightarrow C_1+C_2+C_3}{\longrightarrow}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
x & \omega & 1 \\
x & \omega^2 & 1+x \\
x & x+\omega & \omega^2

\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\displaystyle\int \dfrac{\operatorname{cosec}^2 x - 2010}{\cos^{2010} x}\,dx = -\dfrac{f(x)}{(g(x))^{2010}} + c$, where $f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = 1$; then the number of solutions of the equation $\dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \{x\}$ in $[0, 2\pi]$ is/are (where $\{\cdot\}$ represents fractional part function)
questions.solution:
Let $I = \int \dfrac{\operatorname{cosec}^2 x - 2010}{\cos^{2010} x}\,dx$

We can rewrite the integral as:

$I = \int (\operatorname{cosec}^2 x - 2010) \cos^{-2010} x \,dx$

Consider the derivative of $\cot x \cos^{-2010} x$:

$\dfrac{d}{dx} \left( \cot x \cos^{-2010} x \right) = -\operatorname{cosec}^2 x \cos^{-2010} x + \cot x (-2010) \cos^{-2011} x (-\sin x)$

$= -\operatorname{cosec}^2 x \cos^{-2010} x + 2010 \left( \dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x} \right) \cos^{-2011} x \sin x$

$= -\operatorname{cosec}^2 x \cos^{-2010} x + 2010 \cos^{-2010} x$

$= -(\operatorname{cosec}^2 x - 2010) \cos^{-2010} x$

Therefore, the integral is:

$I = -\cot x \cos^{-2010} x + c = -\dfrac{\cot x}{\cos^{2010} x} + c$

Comparing this with the given expression $-\dfrac{f(x)}{(g(x))^{2010}} + c$, we get:

$f(x) = \cot x$ and $g(x) = \cos x$

We are given $f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = \cot\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = 1$, which satisfies the condition.

Now, we need to find the number of solutions for the equation $\dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \{x\}$ in $[0, 2\pi]$.

$\dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \dfrac{\cot x}{\cos x} = \dfrac{1}{\sin x} = \operatorname{cosec} x$

The equation becomes $\operatorname{cosec} x = \{x\}$.

We know that the range of the fractional part function is $0 \le \{x\} < 1$.

However, the range of $\operatorname{cosec} x$ is $(-\infty, -1] \cup [1, \infty)$.

Since the ranges of the two functions do not intersect, there are no solutions to the equation $\operatorname{cosec} x = \{x\}$ in $[0, 2\pi]$.

Thus, the number of solutions is $0$.

Answer: $0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $2x^2y\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \tan(x^2y^2) - 2xy^2$, given $y(1) = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}$ is
questions.solution:
Given differential equation is $2x^2y\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \tan(x^2y^2) - 2xy^2$

Rearranging the terms, we get:

$2x^2y\dfrac{dy}{dx} + 2xy^2 = \tan(x^2y^2)$

Let $v = x^2y^2$. Differentiating with respect to $x$, we get:

$\dfrac{dv}{dx} = 2xy^2 + 2x^2y\dfrac{dy}{dx}$

Substituting this in the differential equation:

$\dfrac{dv}{dx} = \tan(v)$

Separating the variables:

$\cot(v) dv = dx$

Integrating both sides:

$\int \cot(v) dv = \int dx$

$\ln|\sin(v)| = x + C$

Substituting $v = x^2y^2$:

$\ln|\sin(x^2y^2)| = x + C$

Given $y(1) = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}$, we substitute $x = 1$ and $y = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}$:

$\ln\left|\sin\left(1^2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\right| = 1 + C$

$\ln(1) = 1 + C \Rightarrow C = -1$

Therefore, the solution is:

$\ln|\sin(x^2y^2)| = x - 1$

$\sin(x^2y^2) = e^{x-1}$

Answer: $\sin(x^2y^2) = e^{x-1}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f: (0, 1) \to (0, 1)$ be a differentiable function such that $f'(x) \neq 0 \, \forall x \in (0, 1)$ and $f\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Suppose for all $x$, $\lim_{t \to x}\dfrac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t}\sqrt{1 - (f(s))^2}\,ds - \displaystyle\int_{0}^{x}\sqrt{1 - (f(s))^2}\,ds}{f(t) - f(x)} = f(x)$. Then the value of $f\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)$ belongs to
questions.solution:
The given limit is of the form $\dfrac{0}{0}$ as $t \rightarrow x$.

Applying L'Hopital's rule by differentiating the numerator and the denominator with respect to $t$:

$\lim_{t \rightarrow x} \dfrac{\dfrac{d}{dt} \left( \int_{0}^{t} \sqrt{1 - (f(s))^2} ds - \int_{0}^{x} \sqrt{1 - (f(s))^2} ds \right)}{\dfrac{d}{dt}(f(t) - f(x))} = f(x)$

Using Leibniz's rule for differentiation under the integral sign:

$\lim_{t \rightarrow x} \dfrac{\sqrt{1 - (f(t))^2} - 0}{f'(t) - 0} = f(x)$

$\dfrac{\sqrt{1 - (f(x))^2}}{f'(x)} = f(x)$

$\sqrt{1 - (f(x))^2} = f(x) f'(x)$

Integrating both sides with respect to $x$:

$\int \dfrac{f(x) f'(x)}{\sqrt{1 - (f(x))^2}} dx = \int 1 dx$

Let $1 - (f(x))^2 = u$. Then $-2f(x)f'(x) dx = du$, which means $f(x)f'(x) dx = -\dfrac{du}{2}$.

$\int \dfrac{-1}{2\sqrt{u}} du = x + C$

$-\sqrt{u} = x + C$

$-\sqrt{1 - (f(x))^2} = x + C$

Given $f\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Substituting $x = \dfrac{1}{2}$:

$-\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = \dfrac{1}{2} + C$

$-\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{3}{4}} = \dfrac{1}{2} + C$

$-\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}} = \dfrac{1}{2} + C$

$-\dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} + C$

$C = -1$

Thus, $-\sqrt{1 - (f(x))^2} = x - 1$

Squaring both sides:

$1 - (f(x))^2 = (x - 1)^2$

$(f(x))^2 = 1 - (x - 1)^2$

To find $f\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)$, substitute $x = \dfrac{1}{4}$:

$\left(f\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)\right)^2 = 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{4} - 1\right)^2$

$\left(f\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)\right)^2 = 1 - \left(-\dfrac{3}{4}\right)^2$

$\left(f\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)\right)^2 = 1 - \dfrac{9}{16}$

$\left(f\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)\right)^2 = \dfrac{7}{16}$

$f\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right) = \pm \dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}$

Since the codomain of $f$ is $(0, 1)$, $f(x) > 0$ for all $x \in (0, 1)$.

Therefore, $f\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}$.

This value belongs to the set $\left\{ \dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}, \dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{4} \right\}$.

Answer: $\left\{\dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}, \dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\right\}$

questions.q: The population $p(t)$ at time $t$ of a certain mouse species follows the differential equation $\frac{d p(t)}{d t}=0.5 p(t)-450$. If $p(0)=850$, then the time at which the population becomes zero is
questions.solution:
$\frac{d p}{d t}=0.5 P-450$
or, $\int_{850}^0 \frac{d p}{P-900}=\int_0^{\mathrm{t}} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{2}$
or, $[\ln |\mathrm{P}-900|]_{850}^0=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{2}$
or, $\frac{t}{2}=\ln \left|\frac{900}{50}\right|=\ln |18|$
or, $t=2 \ln |18|$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $U_n(n=1,2)$ denote the $n^{\text {th }}$ derivative $(n=1,2)$ of $U(x)=\frac{L x+M}{x^2-2 B x+C}(L, M, B, C$ are constants $)$, then $P U_2+Q U_1$ $+R U=0$, holds for
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Hint: } U(x)=\frac{L x+m}{x^2-2 B x+c}, U(x)\left(x^2-2 B x+c\right)=L x+m \\ & U(x)(2 x-2 B)+\left(x^2-2 B x+c\right) U_1(x)=L \\ & U(x)2 \times 2(2 x-2 B) U_1(x)+(2 x-2 B) U_1(x)+\left(x^2-2 B x+c\right) U_2(x)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow U_2\left(x^2-2 Bx+c\right)+U_1(4 x-4 B)+2 U=0 \\ & \therefore P=x^2-2 B x+c, Q=4(x-B), R=2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x y^{\prime}+y-e^x=0, y(a)=b$, then $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1} y(x)$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {Hint : } \quad xy^{\prime}+y-e^x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=e^x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x d y+y d x=e^x d x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \int d(x y)=\int e^x d x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x y=e^x+c \\ & \quad y(a)=b \Rightarrow c=a b-e^a \\ & \therefore \quad y(x)=\frac{e^x+a b-e^a}{x} \\ & \therefore \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow 1} y(x)=e+a b-e^a\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f$ be a differential function with $\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x)=0$. If $y^{\prime}+y f^{\prime}(x)-f(x) f^{\prime}(x)=0, \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} y(x)=0$ then
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{d y}{d x}+y f^{\prime}(x)=f(x) f^{\prime}(x) \\
& \text {I.F }=e^{\int f^{\prime}(x) d x}=e^{f(x)} \\
& \text {y. } e^{f(x)}=\int e^{f(x)} f(x) f^{\prime}(x) d x
\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text {Let } f(x)=t \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) d x=d t \\
& \Rightarrow y e^{f(x)}=\int e^t t d t \\
& \Rightarrow y e^{f(x)}=e^t(t-1)+c \\
& \Rightarrow y e^{f(x)}=e^{f(x)}(f(x)-1)+c
\end{aligned}\)
Given, \(\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x)=0, \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} y(x)=0\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 0 . e^0=e^0(0-1)+c \Rightarrow c=1 \\
& \therefore y e^{f(x)}=e^{f(x)}(f(x)-1)+1 \\
& \Rightarrow y=f(x)-1+e^{-f(x)} \\
& \Rightarrow y+1=f(x)+e^{-f(x)}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(x=\sin \theta\) and \(y=\sin k \theta\), then \(\left(1-x^2\right) y_2-x y_1-\alpha y=0\), for \(\alpha=\)
questions.solution:
Hint : \(x=\sin \theta\)
\(y=\sin k \theta\)
\(\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y / d \theta}{d x / d \theta}=\frac{k \cos k \theta}{\cos \theta}=y_1 \ldots\ldots (I)\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& y_2=\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{d}{d \theta}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \times \frac{d \theta}{d x}=\left[\frac{-k^2 \sin (k \theta) \cdot \cos \theta+k \cos (k \theta) \sin \theta}{\cos ^2 \theta}\right] \times \frac{1}{\cos \theta} \\
& y_2=\frac{k \cos (k \theta) \sin \theta-k^2 \sin (k \theta) \cos \theta}{\cos ^3 \theta} \ldots \ldots \text { (II) }
\end{aligned}\)
also, \(1-x^2=1-\sin ^2 \theta=\cos ^2 \theta \ldots \ldots \text { (III) }\)
So, putting the values of \(1-x^2, y_1\) and \(y_2\) from (I), (II), (III) we get
\(\begin{aligned}
& \left(1-x^2\right) y_2-x y_1=\alpha y \\
& \Rightarrow\left(\cos ^2 \theta\right)\left[\frac{k \cos (k \theta) \sin \theta-k^2 \sin (k \theta) \cdot \cos \theta}{\cos ^3 \theta}\right]-\frac{k \sin \theta \cos (k \theta)}{\cos \theta}=\alpha(\sin k \theta) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{k \cos (k \theta) \sin \theta-k^2 \sin (k \theta) \cos \theta-k \sin \theta \cos (k \theta)}{\cos \theta}=\alpha \sin (k \theta) \\
& \Rightarrow-k^2 \sin (k \theta)=\alpha \sin (k \theta)
\end{aligned}\)
So, \(\alpha=-k^2\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The family of curves \(y=e^{a \sin x}\), where ' \(a\) ' is arbitrary constant, is represented by the differential equation
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \log y=a \sin x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\log y}{\sin x}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}(a) \Rightarrow \frac{\sin x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{y}-\log y \times \cos x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y \log y=\tan x \times \frac{d y}{d x}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \frac{f(x y)}{f^{\prime}(x y)}$, then $|f(x y)|$ is equal to
questions.solution: $x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \frac{f(x y)}{f^{\prime}(x y)} \Rightarrow \frac{d(x y)}{d x}=x \frac{f(x y)}{f^{\prime}(x y)} \Rightarrow \frac{f^{\prime}(x y)}{f(x y)} d(x y)=x d x \Rightarrow \ln |f(x y)|=\frac{x^2}{2}+k \Rightarrow f(x y)=C e^{\frac{x^2}{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $\cos y \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+\sin y}+x^2 e^{\sin y}$ is $f(x)+e^{-\sin y}=C$ (C is arbitrary real constant) where $f(x)$ is equal to
questions.solution: $-e^{-\sin y} \cos y \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left[e^x+x^2\right] \Rightarrow d\left(e^{-\sin y}\right)+\left(e^x+x^2\right) d x=0 \Rightarrow e^{-\sin y}+e^x+\frac{x^3}{3}=C$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{x f(x y)}{f^{\prime}(x y)}$, then $|f(x y)|$ is equal to
questions.solution: $x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{x f(x y)}{f^{\prime}(x y)} \Rightarrow \frac{x d y+y d x}{d x}=\frac{x f(x y)}{f^{\prime}(x y)} \Rightarrow \frac{f^{\prime}(x y) d(x y)}{f(x y)}=x d x \Rightarrow \ln f(x y)=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+C \Rightarrow|f(x y)|=k e^{x^{2} / 2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation of all the ellipses centred at the origin and have axes as the co-ordinate axes is
options:
$x^{2} y^{\prime}+x y^{\prime \prime}-3 y=0$
Where $y^{\prime} \equiv \frac{d y}{d x}, y^{\prime \prime} \equiv \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}$
questions.solution: equation of ellipse centred at origin and have axes as the coordinate axes is $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ $\Rightarrow \frac{x}{a^{2}}+\frac{y y^{\prime}}{b^{2}}=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{-b^{2}}{a^{2}}=\frac{y y^{\prime}}{x}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 0=\frac{x\left(y^{\prime \prime} y^{\prime}+y^{\prime 2}\right)-y y^{\prime}}{x^{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f$ be a differentiable function with $\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x)=0 .$ If $y^{\prime}+y f^{\prime}(x)-f(x) f^{\prime}(x)=0, \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} y(x)=0$, then $\left(\right.$ where $\left.y^{\prime}=\frac{d y}{d x}\right)$
questions.solution:
Hint:
$\frac{d y}{d x}+f^{\prime}(x) y=f^{\prime}(x) f(x)$
$\Rightarrow y \times e^{f(x)}=\int f^{\prime}(x) f(x) e^{f(x)} d x$
$\Rightarrow y \times e^{f(x)}=e^{f(x)}(f(x)-1)+c \quad[$ Putting $f(x)=0 ; y=0, c=1]$
$\Rightarrow y \times e^{f(x)}=e^{f(x)}(f(x)-1)+1$
$\Rightarrow y=f(x)-1+e^{-f(x)}$
$\Rightarrow y+1=e^{-f(x)}+f(x)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) d y=\left[y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x\right] d x, x>0$ and $y(1)=\frac{\pi}{2}$ then the value of $\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$\operatorname{sinv}\left(v+x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}\right)=v \sin v-1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \sin \mathrm{v} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-1$
$\Rightarrow \int \sin v \mathrm{~d} v=-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}$
$\Rightarrow-\cos v=-\log x+\mathrm{c}$
at $x=1 ; \mathrm{y}=\frac{\pi}{2} ; \mathrm{c}=0$
$\cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation of the family of curves $y=e^{x}(A \cos x+B \sin x)$ where $A, B$ are arbitrary constants is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$y=e^{x}(A \cos x+B \sin x)$
Differentiating w.r.t. x:-
$y^{\prime}=y+e^{x}(-A \sin x+B \cos x)$
Differentiating w.r.t. $x$ once again:-
$\begin{array}{c}
y^{\prime \prime}=y^{\prime}+\left(y^{\prime}-y\right)+e^{x}(-A \cos x-B \sin x) \\
=2 y^{\prime}-y-y \quad \Rightarrow y^{\prime \prime}-2 y^{\prime}+2 y=0
\end{array}$

questions.q: Let $\mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{1+\mathrm{x}+\ln \mathrm{x}}$, Then
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$x \frac{d y}{d x}=y(y \ln x-1)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of the differential equation $\left(1+e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right) d x+\left(1-\frac{x}{y}\right) e^{x / y} d y=0$ is$(C$ is an arbitrary constant $)$
questions.solution:
$\left(1+e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right) d x+\left(1-\frac{x}{y}\right) e^{x / y} d y=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(1+e^{x / y}\right) d x=-e^{x / y}\left(1-\frac{x}{y}\right) d y$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{-e^{x / y}(1-x / y)}{\left(1+e^{x / y}\right)}$ ...(i)
This is homogeneous differential equation.
So, put $\quad x=v y$
$\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y \frac{d v}{d y}$ ....(ii)
Therefore, $v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{-e^{v} (1-v)}{1+e^{v}}$
[from Eqs.(i) and (ii)]
$\Rightarrow \quad y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{-e^{v}+ve^{v}}{1+e^{v}}-v$
$=\frac{-e^{v}+ve^{v}-v\left(1+e^{v}\right)}{1+e^{v}}$
$=-e^{v}+v e^{v}-v-v e^{v}$
$\Rightarrow \quad y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{-\left(e^{v}+v\right)}{1+e^{v}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1+e^{v}}{v+e^{r}} d v=-\frac{d y}{y} \quad$ [variable separation $]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac{1+e^{v}}{v+e^{v}} d v=-\int \frac{d y}{y} \quad$ [by integration $] \ldots$ (iii)
Let $\left.v+e^{v}=t \Rightarrow (1+e^{v}\right) d v=d t$
$\int \frac{1+e^{v}}{v+e^{v}} d v=\int \frac{d t}{t}=\log t=\log \left(v+e^{v}\right)$ ...(iv)
$\Rightarrow \log \left(v+e^{v}\right)=-\log y+\log C$
[from Eqs. (iii) and (iv)] $\Rightarrow \log \left(v+e^{v}\right)+\log y=\log C$
$\Rightarrow \quad y\left(v+e^{v}\right)=C$
$y\left(\frac{x}{y}+e^{x / y}\right)=C$
$y\left(\frac{x+ye^{x / y}}{y}\right)=C$
$\Rightarrow \quad x+y e^{x / y}=C$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: General solution of $(x+y)^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=a^{2}, a \neq 0$ is $(C$ is an arbitrary constant)
questions.solution:
$(x+y)^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=a^{2}, a \neq 0$
Let $x+y=t$ 
$1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}-1$
Therefore, $t^{2}\left(\frac{d t}{d x}-1\right)=a^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad t^{2} \frac{d t}{d x}-t^{2}=a^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad t^{2} \frac{d t}{d x}=a^{2}+t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{t^{2}}{a^{2}+t^{2}} d t=d x$ [variable separation $]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac{t^{2}}{\left(t^{2}+a^{2}\right)} d t=\int d x$
[integration]
$\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac{t^{2}+a^{2}-a^{2}}{\left(t^{2}+a^{2}\right)} d t=x+C'$
[doing $a^{2}$ adding and subtracting] $\Rightarrow \quad \int\left(1-\frac{a^{2}}{t^{2}+a^{2}}\right) d t=x+C'$
$\Rightarrow \quad \int d t-\int \frac{a^{2}}{t^{2}+a^{2}} d t=x+C'$
$\Rightarrow \quad t-a^{2} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+a^{2}}=x+C^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow t-a^{2}\left[\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)\right]=x+C'$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x+y)-a \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+y}{a}\right)$
$=x+C'$ [on putting the value of t] $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{y-C^{\prime}}{a}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+y}{a}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x+y}{a}=\tan \left(\frac{y-C^{\prime}}{a}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{x+y}{a}=\tan \left(\frac{y+c}{a}\right)$
[where, $C=-C^{\prime}$ is an arbitrary constant $]$ $\Rightarrow \quad \tan \left(\frac{y+C}{a}\right)=\frac{x+y}{a}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation representing the family of curves $y^{2}=2 d(x+\sqrt{d})$ where $d$ is a parameter, is of
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad y^{2}=2 d(x+\sqrt{d})$
$\Rightarrow$
2y $y_{1}=2 d \Rightarrow d=y y_{1}$
From Eq. (i),
$$
y^{2}=2 y y_{1}\left(x+\sqrt{y y_{1}}\right)
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad y^{2}-2 y y_{1} x=\sqrt{y y_{1}} \cdot 2 y y_{1}$
$\Rightarrow\left(y^{2}-2 y y_{1} x\right)^{2}=4\left(y y_{1}\right)^{3}$
So, degree of above equation is 3.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $y(x)$ be a solution of $\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y-4 x^{2}=0$ and $y(0)=-1 .$ Then y(1) s equal to
questions.solution:
We have. $\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y-4 x^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right) y=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}$
Here. $\mathrm{IF}=e^{\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}}=e^{\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)}=1+x^{2}$
$\therefore \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int\left(1+x^{2}\right) \times \frac{4 x^{2}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)} d x+C$
$\Rightarrow y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int 4 x^{2} d x+C$
$\Rightarrow \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3}+C$
$\Rightarrow \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3}-1$
$$
[y(0)=-1]
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad y=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3\left(1+x^{2}\right)}-\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}$
$\therefore \quad y(1)=\frac{4}{6}-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{6}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Solution of $(x+y)^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=a^{2}$ ('a' being a constant) is
options: $x y=a \tan C x, C$ is an arbitrary constant
$x y=\tan (x+C), C$ is an arbitrary constant
questions.solution:
We have, $(x+y)^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=a^{2}$
Let $x+y=v$
$\therefore$
$1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}$
$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d x}-1$
$v^{2}\left(\frac{d v}{d x}-1\right)=a^{2}$
$\Rightarrow$
$v^{2} \frac{d v}{d x}=v^{2}+a^{2}$
$\quad \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}+a^{2}}{v^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{v^{2}}{v^{2}+d^{2}} d v=d x$
On integrating both sides, we get $\int \frac{v^{2}}{v^{2}+a^{2}} d v=\int d x$
$\int\left(1-\frac{a^{2}}{v^{2}+a^{2}}\right) \mathrm{d} v=x+C^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow \quad v-\frac{a^{2}}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{v}{a}=x+C^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x+y-a \tan ^{-1} \frac{x+y}{a}=x+C^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow \quad y=a \tan ^{-1} \frac{x+y}{a}+C^{\prime}$
$=\frac{y-C^{\prime}}{a}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{x+y}{a}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{x+y}{a}\right)=\tan \left(\frac{y+C}{a}\right)$
where $-C^{\prime}=C$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integrating factor of the first order differential equation $x^{2}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+x\left(x^{2}+1\right) y=x^{2}-1$ is
questions.solution:
We have,
$x^{2}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+x\left(x^{2}+1\right) y=x^{2}-1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)} y=\frac{1}{x^{2}}$
$\begin{aligned} I F=& e^{\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)} d x} \\ &=e^{\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}} d x \end{aligned}$
Let $\frac{x^{2}+1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x-1}+\frac{C}{x+1}$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}+1=A(x-1)(x+1)+B x(x+1)$ $\quad+\operatorname{Cx}(x-1)$
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { Put } x=0, & \\ \therefore \quad & 1=-A \\ \Rightarrow & A=-1 \\ \Rightarrow & 
\end{array}$
Put $x=1
\therefore \quad 2=2 B$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & B=1 \\ \Rightarrow & x=-1\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad 2=2 C$
$\Rightarrow C=1$
$\therefore \frac{x^{2}+1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{x-1}+\frac{1}{x+1}$
$\therefore \quad I F=e^{\int\left(\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{x-1}+\frac{1}{x+1}\right) d x}$
$=e^{[-\log x+\ln (x-1)+\log (x+1)]}$
$=e^{\ln \left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x}\right)}=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x}$
$=x-\frac{1}{x}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the solution of the differential equation $x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x e^{x}$ be $x y=e^{x} \phi(x)+C,$ then $\phi(x)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x e^{x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=e^{x}$
On comparing Eq. by $\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q$, we get
$\therefore$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
P=\frac{1}{x} \text { and } Q=e^{x} \\
IF=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}
\end{array}
$$
$$
=e^{log^{x}}=x
$$
Hence, solution of differential equation,
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\quad y \cdot x=\int x e^{x} d x+C \\
\Rightarrow \quad x y=x e^{x}-\int e^{x} d x+C \\
\Rightarrow \quad x y=x e^{x}-e^{x}+C
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad x y=e^{x}(x-1)+C$
$\because xy=e^{x} \phi(x)+c$ [given]
$$
\phi(x)=(x-1)
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The order of the differential equation of all parabolas whose axis of symmetry along $X$ -axis is
questions.solution:
The general equation of the parabola is $x=a y^{2}+b$.
where a and $b$ are arbitrary constants.
So, the differential equation is of order 2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: General solution of $y \frac{d y}{d x}+b y^{2}=a \cos x$ $0 \leq x    < 1$ is (Here, $c$ is an arbitrary constant)
options: $y^{2}=2 a(2 b \sin x+\cos x)+c e^{-2 b x}$
$\left(4 b^{2}+1\right) y^{2}=2 a(\sin x+2 b \cos x)+c e^{-2 b x}$
$\left(4 b^{2}+1\right) y^{2}=2 a(\sin x+2 b \cos x)+c e^{2 b x}$
$y^{2}=2 a(2 b \sin x+\cos x)+c e^{-2 b x}$
questions.solution:
Given. $y \frac{d y}{d x}+b y^{2}=a \cos x, 0    < x < 1$
$y^{2}=z$
$2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad y \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d z}{d x}$
$\frac{1}{2} \frac{d z}{d x}+b y^{2}=a \cos x$
$\frac{d z}{d x}+2 b y^{2}=2 a \cos x$
Now, $I F=e^{2 b \int d x}=e^{2 b x}$
$\therefore \quad z \cdot e^{2 b x}=\int 2 a \cos x \cdot e^{2 b x} d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad y^{2} e^{2 b x}=\frac{2 a}{4 b^{2}+1}(\sin x+2 b \cos x) e^{2 b x}+C$
$\Rightarrow\left(4 b^{2}+1\right) y^{2}=2 a(\sin x+2 b \cos x)+C e^{-2 b x}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integrating factor of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(3 x^{2} \tan ^{-1} y -x^{3}\right)\left(1+y^{2}\right)=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(3 x^{2} \tan ^{-1} y-x^{3}\right)\left(1+y^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{3}\left(1+y^{2}\right)-3 x^{2}\left(\tan ^{-1} y\right)\left(1+y^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\left(1+y^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{3}-3 x^{2} \tan ^{-1} y$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+3 x^{2} \tan ^{-1} y=x^{3}$
Put $\tan ^{-1} y=t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{d t}{d x}+3 t x^{2}=x^{3}$
which is linear differential equation in $t$.
Now.
$\mathrm{I} \quad \mathrm{F}=e^{\int 3 \mathrm{x}^{2} d x}=e^{x^{3}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y=e^{-x} \cos 2 x$, then which of the following differential equation is satisfied?
questions.solution:
Given, $y=e^{-x} \cos 2 x$
$\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{-x}(-\sin 2 x) 2+\cos 2 x \cdot e^{-x}(-1)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=-2 \sin 2 x \cdot e^{-x}-y$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+y=-2 \sin 2 x \cdot e^{-x}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-2\left[\sin 2 x \cdot e^{-x}(-1)\right. \left.e^{-x} 2 \cos 2 x\right]$
$=2 \sin 2 x \cdot e^{-x}-4 y \quad$ |from Eq. $\left.(0)\right]$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{d y}{d x}-y-4 y$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}+5 y=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\cos x$ and $\sin x$ are solutions of the differential equation $a_{0} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+a_{1} \frac{d y}{d x}+a_{2} y=0$ where $a_{0}, a_{1}$ and $a_{2}$ are real constants, then which of the following is/are always true?
options: $A \cos x+B \sin x$ is a solution, where $A$ and $B$ are feal constants
$A \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is a solution, where $A$ is a real constant
$A \cos x \sin x$ is a solution, where $A$ is a real constant
$A \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+B \sin \left(x -\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is a solution, where A and $B$ are real constants
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=\cos x$ and $g(x)=\sin x$ Consider the Wronskian of $f(x)$ and $g(x)$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
f(x) & g(x) \\
f^{\prime}(x) & g^{\prime}(x)
\end{array}\right| \\
&=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
\cos x & \sin x \\
-\sin x & \cos x
\end{array}\right| \\
&=\cos ^{2} x+\sin ^{2} x=1 \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$
Thus, the functions are linearly independent. So, the general solution of given differential equation is given by $y=A \cos x+B \sin x,$ where $A$ and $B$ are real constants.
[$\because$ if $y_{1}$ and $y_{2}$ are linearly independent solutions of the differential equation $a y^{\prime \prime}+b y^{\prime}+c=0,$ then the general solution is $y=c_{1} y_{1}+c_{2} y_{2},$ where $c_{1}$ and $c_{2}$ are constants] Hence, option (a) is true.
(b) Let $y=A \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=A\left(\cos x \cdot \cos \frac{\pi}{4}-\sin x \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\
=\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}(\cos x-\sin x) \\
=\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x+\left(-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \sin x
\end{array}
$$
which is in the form of general solution.
Hence, option (b) is true.
(c) Let $y=A \cos x \sin x$
which cannot be
expressed in the form of general solution.
(d) Let $y=A \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+B \sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=A\left(\cos x \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\sin x \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\
\quad+B\left(\sin x \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\cos x \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\
=\cos x\left(\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{B}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\sin x\left(\frac{B}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \text { which is }
\end{array}
$$
in the form of general solution. Hence, option (d) is true.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integrating factor of the differential equation $\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=e^{\tan ^{-1} x}$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation is
$$
\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=e^{\tan ^{-1} x}
$$
or it can be rewritten as
$$
\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)} y=\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{1+x^{2}}
$$
It is a linear differential equation of the form of
$$
\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q
$$
where, $P=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}$ and $Q=\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{1+x^{2}}$
$$
=e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x}=e^{\tan ^{-1} x}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\sqrt{y}=\cos ^{-1} x$, then it satisfies the differential equation $\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=c,$ where $c$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $\sqrt{y}=\cos ^{-1} x \Rightarrow y=\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)^{2}$
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get
$$
\frac{d y}{d x}=2\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}
$$
Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-2\left[\begin{array}{c}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \times \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \\ -\cos ^{-1} x \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{(-2 x)}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}} \\ \hline\left(\sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right)^{2}\end{array} \right ].$
$=-2\left[\frac{-1+\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}\right]$
$\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left[\frac{2-\frac{2 x \cos ^{-1} x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2+x \frac{d y}{d x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=2$
But, it is given
$\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=c$
$\therefore c=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $y \frac{d y}{d x}=x\left[\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{\phi\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}{\phi^{\prime}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}\right]$ is (where, $c$ is a constant)
questions.solution:
Given differential equation can be rewritten as
$$
\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{x \phi\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}{y \phi^{\prime}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}
$$
Put $y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}$
$\therefore$ Given equation becomes.
$$
v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x}{x}+\frac{x \phi\left(\frac{v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}{v x \phi^{\prime}\left(\frac{v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{\phi\left(v^{2}\right)}{v \phi^{\prime}\left(v^{2}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{v \psi^{\prime}\left(v^{2}\right)}{\phi\left(v^{2}\right)} d v=\frac{d x}{x}$
On integrating both sides, we get
$$
\frac{1}{2} \log \phi\left(v^{2}\right)=\log x+\log c_{1}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \log \phi\left(v^{2}\right)=2 \log x c_{1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \phi\left(v^{2}\right)=\left(x c_{1}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \phi\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)=x^{2} c_{1}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \phi\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)=x^{2} c \quad$ (put $\left.c_{1}^{2}=c\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The curve $y=(\cos x+y)^{1 / 2}$ satisfies the differential equation
options: $(2 y-1) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\cos x=0$
$\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\cos x=0$
$(2 y-1) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\cos x=0$
$(2 y-1) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\cos x=0$
questions.solution:
Given curve is
$$
y=(\cos x+y)^{1 / 2}
$$
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x,$ we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d y}{d x} &=\frac{1}{2(\cos x+y)^{1 / 2}} \times\left(-\sin x+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \\
&=\frac{\left(-\sin x+\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{2 y} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & 2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=-\sin x+\frac{d y}{d x} \\
\Rightarrow \quad(2 y-1) \frac{d y}{d x} &=-\sin x
\end{aligned}
$$
Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
(2 y-1) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}\left(2 \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=-\cos x \\
\Rightarrow \quad(2 y-1) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}\left(2 \frac{d y}{d x}\right)+\cos x=0
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x \log _{e} x}=\frac{1}{x}$ under the condition $y=1$ when $x=e$ is
questions.solution:
Given, differential equation is $\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x \log _{e} x}=\frac{1}{x}$
It is a linear equation of the form $\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q$
where $\quad P=\frac{1}{x \log _{e} x}$ and $Q=\frac{1}{x}$
$\therefore$ Integrating factor, $\mathrm{IF}=e$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=e^{\int \frac{1}{x \log _{e} x} d x} \\
=e^{\left.\log \log _{e} x\right)} \\
=\log _{e} x
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ Solution of differential equation is $y \times \log _{e} x=\int \frac{1}{x} \log _{e} x d x$
$\Rightarrow y \times \log _{e} x=\frac{\left(\log _{e} x\right)^{2}}{2}+C$
When $y=1$ and $x=e,$ then
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1 \times \log _{e} e=\frac{\left(\log _{e} e\right)^{2}}{2}+C \\
\Rightarrow \quad 1 &=\frac{1}{2}+C \\
\Rightarrow \quad C=\frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
On putting $C=\frac{1}{2}$ in Eq. (i), we get
$$
y \times \log _{e} x=\frac{\left(\log _{e} x\right)^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{2}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 y=\log _{e} x+\frac{1}{\log _{e} x}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $u(x)$ and $v(x)$ are two independent solutions of the differential equation $\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+b \frac{d y}{d x}+c y=0$ then additional solution(s) of the given differential equation is(are)
options: $y=c_{1}\{u(x)-v(x)\}+c_{2} v(x), c_{1}$ and $c_{2}$ are arbitrary constants
$y=c_{1}u(x) v(x)+c_{2} u(x) / v(x) . \quad c_{1}$ and $c_{2}$ are arbitrary constants
questions.solution:
We know that $u(x)$ and $v(x)$ are two independent solutions of the given differential equation, then their linear combination is also the solution of the given equation.
Here, we see that $y=5 u(x)+8 v(x)$ is a linear combination and $y=c_{1}\{u(x)-v(x)\}+c_{2} v(x)$ is also a linear combination of two independent solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $\left(y^{2}+2 x\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=y$ satisfies $x=1, y=1$. Then the solution is
questions.solution:
Given differential equation is 
$\left(y^{2}+2 x\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=y \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\left(\frac{y^{2}+2 x}{y}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d x}{d y}=y+\frac{2 x}{y}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} \cdot x=y$
$\mathrm{IF}=e^{\int \frac{2}{y} d y}=e^{-2 \mathrm{bg} \gamma}=\mathrm{y}^{-2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{y}^{2}}$
$\therefore$ Complete solution is $x \cdot \frac{1}{y^{2}}=\int y \cdot \frac{1}{y^{2}} d y+C$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{y^{2}}=\int \frac{d y}{y}+C=\log _{e} y+c$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=y^{2} \log _{e} y+C y^{2}$
$A t+x=1 y=1$ then from Eq $(i),$ we get
$$
1=0+C \Rightarrow C=1
$$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (i), we get
$$
x=y^{2}\left(\log _{e} y+1\right)
$$
Which is the required solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y+1}{2 x+2 y+1}$ is
questions.solution:
Given differential equation, $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y+1}{2 x+2 y+1}$
Put $x+y=v$ $\Rightarrow \quad 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}-1$
$\therefore$ Eq.
(i) becomes, $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{v+1}{2 v+1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d x}-1=\frac{v+1}{2 v+1} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v+1}{2 v+1}+1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v+1+2 v+1}{2 v+1} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{3 v+2}{2 v+1}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 v+1}{3 v+2} d v=d x \Rightarrow \frac{\left[\frac{2}{3}(3 v+2)-\frac{1}{3}\right]}{3 v+2} d v=d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left[\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{(3 v+2)}\right] d v=d x$
Integrating both sides, we get $\Rightarrow \quad \int\left(\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{(3 v+2)}\right) d v=\int d x+C^{\prime}$
Where $c$ is a constant of integration.
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{3} v-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\log (3 v+2)}{3}=x+C^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{3}(x+y)-\frac{1}{9} \log (3 x+3 y+2)=x+C$
$\Rightarrow \quad 6 x+6 y-\log (3 x+3 y+2)=9 x+C$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x-6 y+\log (3 x+3 y+2)=C$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integrating factor of the differential equation $3 x \log _{e} x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 \log _{e} x$ is given by
questions.solution:
Given, $3 x \log _{e} x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 \log _{e} x$
Dividing both sides by $3 x \log _{e} x,$ we get $\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{3 x \log _{e} x} y=\frac{2 \log _{e} x}{3 x \log _{e} x}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{3 x \log _{e} x} y=\frac{2}{3 x}$
which is linear form $\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q,$ where $P$ and $Q$ are function of $x$ and the integrating factor is given by the following formula $e^{\int P d x}$.
$\therefore$
$\mathrm{IF}=e^{\int \frac{1}{3 x \log _{t} x}} d x$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Put } \log _{e} x &=t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x} d x=d t \\ &=e^{1 / 3} \int \frac{d t}{t}=e^{1 / 3} \log t \\ &=e^{\log 1^{1 / 3}}=t^{1 / 3}=\left(\log _{e} x\right)^{-1 / 3} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $25 \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-10 \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0$, $y(0)=1, y(1)=2 e^{1 / 5}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $y=e^{\pi x}$ be the solution of given differential equation,
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=m e^{n x} \Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=m^{2} e^{m x}$
$\therefore \quad 25 \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-10 \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 25 \mathrm{m}^{2} e^{m x}-10 m e^{m x}+e^{m x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad e^{\operatorname{mux}}\left(25 m^{2}-10 m+1\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow$ Auxiliary equation
$\Rightarrow \quad(5 m-1)^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad m=\frac{1}{5}, \frac{1}{5}$
since, roots are real and euqal.
$\therefore$ General solution is $y=\left(c_{1}+c_{2} x\right) e^{x / 5} \ldots .$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
y(0)=1 \Rightarrow c_{1}=1 \\
y(1)=2 e^{1 / 5} \Rightarrow 2 c^{1 / 5}=\left(c_{1}+c_{2}\right) e^{1 / 5}
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad c_{1}+c_{2}=2 \Rightarrow c_{1}=1$
Putting the value of $c_{1}$ and $c_{2}$ in Eq. (i), we get particular solution
$$
y=(1+x) e^{x / 5}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $y$ be the solution of the differential equation
$x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}}{1-y \log x}$ satisfying $y(1)=1 .$ Then, $y$
satisfies
questions.solution:
Let $y=x^{y} \Rightarrow \log y=y \log x$
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get $\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=y \times \frac{1}{x}+\log x \frac{d y}{d x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}-\log x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{y}-\log x\right)=\frac{y}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{1-y \log x}{y}\right)=\frac{y}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}}{(1-y \log x)}$

questions.q: The equation of one of the curves whose slope at any point is equal to $\mathrm{y}+2 \mathrm{x}$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Hints: } \frac{d y}{d x}=y+2 x \quad \text { Put } y+2 x=z \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+z=\frac{d z}{d x} \\
& \frac{d z}{d x}-2=z, \quad \frac{d z}{d x}=z+2 \Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{z+2}=\int d x \\
& \log (z+2)=x+c, \log (y+2 x+2)=x+c \\
& y+2 x+2=x+c, y=2\left(e^x-x-1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If ' $f$ ' is the inverse function of ' $g$ ' and $g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^n}$, then the value of $f^{\prime}(x)$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & g^{-1}(x)=f(x) \\ & g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^n} \quad \Rightarrow g^{\prime}\left(g^{-1}(x)\right)=\frac{1}{1+\left(g^{-1}(x)\right)^n}=\frac{1}{1+(f(x))^n} \\ & g\left(g^{-1}(x)\right)=x \\ & g^{\prime}\left(g^{-1}(x) \cdot\left(g^{-1}(x)\right)^{\prime}=1\right. \\ & \left(g^{-1}(x)\right)^{\prime}=\frac{1}{g^{\prime}\left(g^{-1}(x)\right)}=1+(f(x))^n\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a particle moves in a straight line according to the law $x=a \sin (\sqrt{\lambda} t+b)$, then the particle will come to rest at two points whose distance is [symbols have their usual meaning]
questions.solution:
Hint : $\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{a} \cos (\sqrt{\lambda} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{b}) \cdot \sqrt{\lambda}=0\left[\begin{array}{l}\text { at rest, } \\ \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0\end{array}\right] \Rightarrow \cos (\sqrt{\lambda} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{b})=0$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow & \sqrt{\lambda} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{b}=\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{-\pi}{2} \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}_1 & =\mathrm{a} \sin \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \text { and } \mathrm{x}_2=\mathrm{a} \sin \left(\frac{-\pi}{2}\right) \\
& =\mathrm{a} \quad=-\mathrm{a}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad$ Distance between the points $=\left|\mathrm{x}_2-\mathrm{x}_1\right|=2 \mathrm{a}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The acceleration $f \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{sec}^2$ of a particle after a time $t$ sec starting from rest is given by $f=6-\sqrt{1.2 t}$. Then the maximum velocity v and time T at attend this velocity are
questions.solution:
Hint: Given $a^n c^n=f=\frac{d v}{d t}=6-\sqrt{1.2 t} \quad(t$ is in secs) 
at $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{v}=0$ and let the maximum velocity is ' v ' which is attained at ' $\mathrm{t}_0$ ' at maximum velocity, $\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_0=30 \mathrm{~s}$
so, $\int_0^v d v=\int_0^{30}(6-\sqrt{1.2 t}) d t \Rightarrow v=60 \mathrm{ft/s}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)=\log _e\left(\frac{x}{n}\right)^n\), then \(A y_2+B y_1+C y=0\) is possible for
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text { Hint: } \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-\frac{y^2}{b^2}}} \times \frac{1}{b} y_1=\left(\frac{n}{n \times \frac{x}{n}}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{-1 y_1}{\sqrt{b^2-y^2}}=\frac{n}{x} \\
& \Rightarrow y_1 x+n \sqrt{b^2-y^2}=0 \\
& \Rightarrow y_1+x y_2+\frac{n}{2 \sqrt{b^2-y^2}} \cdot(-2 y) y_1=0
\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow y_1+x y_2+\frac{n^2}{x} y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y_1 x+x^2 y_2+n^2 y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2 y_2+x y_1+n^2 y=0 \\ & A=x^2, B=x, C=n^2\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function \(y=e^{k x}\) satisfies \(\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)=y \frac{d y}{d x}\). It is valid for
questions.solution:
Hint : \(y=e^{k x}\)
\(\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=k e^{k x}=k y \Rightarrow \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=k^2 e^{k x}=k^2 y\)
Now LHS
\(=\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)=\left(k^2 y+k y\right)(k y-y)=k(k+1)(k-1) y^2=k\left(k^2-1\right) y^2\)
Now RHS \(y \frac{d y}{d x}=k y^2, A / q~ k\left(k^2-1\right) y^2=k y^2 \Rightarrow k\left[k^2-2\right]=0 \Rightarrow k=0, k= \pm \sqrt{2}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Given \(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\cot x \frac{d y}{d x}+4 y \operatorname{cosec}^2 x=0\). Changing the independent variable \(x\) to \(z\) by the substitution \(z=\log \tan \frac{x}{2}\), the equation is changed to
questions.solution:
Hint : \(\frac{d z}{d x}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \sec ^2(x / 2)}{\tan (x / 2)}=\frac{1}{\sin x}=\operatorname{cosec} x\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d z} \cdot \frac{d z}{d x}=\operatorname{cosec} x \frac{d y}{d z}, \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{d}{d z}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\left(\frac{d z}{d x}\right) \\
& =\frac{d}{d z}\left(\operatorname{cosec} \cdot \frac{d y}{d z}\right) \operatorname{cosec} x \\
& =\left(\operatorname{cosec} x \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-\operatorname{cosec} x \cdot \cot x \frac{d x}{d z} \frac{d y}{d z}\right) \operatorname{cosec} x=\operatorname{cosec}^2 x \frac{d^2 y}{d z^2}-\operatorname{cosec} x \cdot \cot x \frac{d y}{d z}
\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\cot x \frac{d y}{d x}+4 y \operatorname{cosec}{ }^2 x=0 \Rightarrow \operatorname{cosec}^2 x \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-\operatorname{cosec} x \cdot \cot x \frac{d y}{d z}+\cot x \cdot \operatorname{cosec} x \cdot \frac{d y}{d z}+4 \operatorname{cosec}^2 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d^2 y}{d z^2}+4 y=0\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A missile is fired from the ground level rises \(x\) meters vertically upwards in \(t \sec\), where \(x=100 t-\frac{25}{2} t^2\). The maximum height reached is
questions.solution:
Hint : \(x=100 t-\frac{25}{2} t^2\)
\(\frac{d x}{d t}=100-25 \mathrm{t}\) for maximum and minimum \(\frac{d x}{d t}=0 \Rightarrow t=4 \mathrm{sec}\)
\(\frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}=-25  < 0\)
\(\therefore\) at \(\mathrm{x}=4\) it will have maixmum height
\(x_{\max }=100 \times 4-\frac{25}{2} \times 16=400-200=200 \mathrm{~m}\)

questions.solution:
$\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \times \frac{1}{1+x^2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(1+x^2\right) y^{\prime}=y$
or $2 x y^{\prime}+\left(1+x^2\right) y^{\prime \prime}=y^{\prime}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the transformation $z=\log \tan \frac{x}{2}$ reduces the differential equation $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\cot x \frac{d y}{d x}+4 y \operatorname{cosec}{ }^2 x=0$ into the form $\frac{d^2 y}{d z^2}+k y=0$ then $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\frac{d^2 y}{d z^2}=\frac{d}{d z}\left(\frac{d y}{d z}\right)=\frac{d}{d z}\left(\frac{\frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{d z}{d x}}\right)$
$=\frac{d}{d z}\left(\frac{\frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{1}{\sin x}}\right) \quad\left(\because \frac{d z}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sin x}\right)$
$=\frac{d}{d z}\left(\sin x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{\frac{d}{d x}\left(\sin x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{\frac{d z}{d x}}$
$=\sin x\left[\cos x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\sin x \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right]$
$\because \frac{d^2 y}{d z^2}+k y=0$
$\therefore \sin x \cdot \cos x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\sin ^2 x \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+k y=0$
Dividing the equation by $\sin ^2 x$, we get $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\cot x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+k \cdot \operatorname{cosec}^2 x \cdot y=0$
Comparing with the equation
$\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\cot x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+4 \operatorname{cosec}^2 x \cdot y=0$
We get $k=4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For $y=\sin ^{-1}\left\{\frac{5 x+12 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{13}\right\} ;|x| \leq 1$, if $a\left(1-x^{2}\right) y_{2}+b x y_{1}=0$ then $(a, b)=$
questions.solution:
$y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{5 x+12 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{13}\right)$
Let $\sin \theta_{1}=\frac{5}{13}$ and $\cos \theta_{2}=x$
$\begin{array}{l}
y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \theta_{1} \cdot \cos \theta_{2}+\cos \theta_{1} \cdot \sin \theta_{2}\right. \\
y=\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}=\sin ^{-1} \frac{5}{13}+\cos ^{-1} x
\end{array}$
$y_{1}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$
$y_{2}=\frac{-x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}_{2}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)=\mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{y}_{1}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}_{2}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)-\mathrm{xy}_{1}=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~b}=-1$
$(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b})=(1,-1)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential of $f(x)=\log _{e}\left(1+e^{10 x}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(e^{5 x}\right)$ at $x=0$ and for $d x=0.2$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{f(x+d x)-f(x)}{d x}=f^{\prime}(x) \\
f(x+d x)-f(x)=f^{\prime}(x) d x
\end{array}$
Now,
$\begin{array}{l}
f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{10 e^{10 x}}{1+e^{10 x}}-\frac{5 \cdot e^{5 x}}{1+e^{10 x}}=\frac{10 e^{10 x}-5 e^{5 x}}{1+e^{10 x}} \\
\left(f^{\prime}(x)\right)_{x-0}=\frac{5}{2} \\
\Delta f(x)=0.2 \times \frac{5}{2}=0.5
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)>0$ for all $x$ and $f^{\prime}(x)$ exists for all $x$. If $f$ is the inverse function of $h$ and $h^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+\log x} \cdot$ Then, $f^{\prime}(x)$ will be
questions.solution:
Given,
$$
h\{f(x)\}=x
$$
differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
h^{\prime}\{f(x)\}, f^{\prime}(x)=1 \\
f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{h^{\prime}\{f(x)\}}
\end{array}
$$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{1+\log \{f(x)\}}} & \\ \Rightarrow \quad\left[\because h^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+\log x}\right] \\ \Rightarrow & f^{\prime}(x)=1+\log (f(x)) \end{aligned}$

\(f(-2)=1\) and \(f(2)=5\) and \(f\) is continuous also.
So intermediate value theorem, function \(f(x)\) takes all values between 1 to 5 .
\(\Rightarrow \quad 2 \frac{1}{3}\) and \(3 \frac{1}{4}\) lies in 1 to 5 so option \(B, C\) are correct

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f_{1}(x)=e^{x}, f_{2}(x)=e^{f_{1}(x)} \ldots \ldots$
$f_{n+1}(x)=c^{f n(x)}$ for all $n \geq 1 .$ Then for any fixed
$n \cdot \frac{d}{d x} f_{n}(x)$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $f_{1}(x)=e^{x}$
$$
\begin{array}{r}
f_{2}(x)=e^{f_{1}(x)} \\
\cdots \cdot \cdots \cdots \\
\cdots f_{n+1}(x)=e^{f_{n}(x)}
\end{array}
$$
$f_{n}(x)=e^{f_{n-1}(x)}$
On taking log both sides, we get
$\quad \log \left\{f_{n}(x)\right\}=f_{n-1}(x) \log e$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d}{d x} \log \left(f_{n}(x)\right)=\frac{d}{d x} f_{n-1}(x) \quad(\because \log e=1)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{f_{n}(x)} \frac{d}{d x} f_{n}(x)=f_{n-1}^{\prime}(x)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d}{d x} f_{n}(x)=f_{n}(x) f_{n-1}^{\prime}(x) \quad \ldots$ (i)
Now, $\quad f_{1}^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}$
$\begin{array}{l}\text { and } \quad f_{2}(x)=e^{f_{1}(x)} \\ \Rightarrow \log f_{2}(x)=f_{1}(x) \log e=f_{1}(x) \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{f_{2}(x)} \cdot f_{2}^{\prime}(x)=f_{1}^{\prime}(x) \\ \Rightarrow \quad \begin{aligned} f_{2}^{\prime}(x) &=f_{2}(x) \cdot f_{1}^{\prime}(x) \\=f_{2}(x) \cdot e^{x} &\left(\because f_{1}^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}\right) \end{aligned} \\ =f_{2}(x) \cdot f_{1}(x) & {\left[\because e^{x}=f_{1}(x)\right] \ldots(\mathrm{ii})}\end{array}$
From Eq. (i). $\frac{d}{d x} f_{n}(x)=f_{n}(x) \cdot f_{n-1}(x) \ldots f_{1}(x)[\mathrm{using} \mathrm{Eq}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The law of motion of a body moving along a straight line is $x=\frac{1}{2}$ vt. $x$ being its distance from a fixed point on the line at time $t$ and $v$ is its velocity there, Then
questions.solution:
We have, $\quad x=\frac{1}{2} v t$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d x}{d t} t$
$\left[\because v=\frac{d x}{d t}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 d t}{t}=\frac{d x}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \cdot \int \frac{d t}{t}=\int \frac{d x}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \log |t|+\log |c|=\log |x|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \log \left(t^{2} \cdot c\right)=\log x$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=t^{2} c$
$\Rightarrow$
$\frac{d x}{d t}=2 t c$
$\Rightarrow$
$\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=2$
$\Rightarrow$ acceleration $f$ is constant.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the non-constant differentiable function $f$ of one variable which obeys the relation $\frac{f(x)}{f(y)}=f(x-y) .$ If $f^{\prime}(0)=p$ and $f^{\prime}(5)=q,$ then $f^{\prime}(-5)$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $\frac{f(x)}{f(y)}=f(x-y)$
$\Rightarrow \quad f(x)=a^{k x}$
$\therefore \quad f^{\prime}(x)=k a^{b x} \log a$
$\begin{array}{lr}\text { Again, } & f^{\prime}(0)=P \\ \Rightarrow & k a^{\circ} \log a=P \\ \Rightarrow & k \log a=P\end{array}$
Also, $\quad f^{\prime}(5)=q$
$\Rightarrow \quad k a^{5 k} \log a=q$
$a^{5 k} p=q$
$a^{5 k}=\frac{q}{P}$
Now,  $\begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(-5) &=k a^{-4} \log a \\ &=\frac{k \log a}{a^{\Delta}} \end{aligned}$ $=\frac{P}{\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)}=\frac{p^{2}}{q}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\log _{5} \log _{3} x,$ then $f^{\prime}(e)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have,
$\therefore$
$\begin{aligned} f(x) &=\log _{5} \log _{3} x \\ &=\frac{\log \log _{3} x}{\log 5} \\ &=\frac{\log \left(\frac{\log x}{\log 3}\right)}{\log 5} \\ &=\frac{\log \log x-\log \log 3}{\log 5} \\ f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{\log 5} \cdot \frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{1}{x} \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(e) &=\frac{1}{e \log 5 \cdot \log e} \\ &=\frac{1}{e \log 5} \quad[\because \log e=1] \\ &=\frac{1}{e \log _{n} 5} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $F(x)=e^{x}, G(x)=e^{-x}$ and $H(x)=G(F(x)),$ where $x$ is a real variable. Then, $\frac{d H}{d x}$ at $x=0$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $F(x)=e^{x}, G(x)=e^{-x}$ $\therefore \quad
H(x)=G(F(x))$ $\begin{aligned} &=G\left(e^{x}\right) \\ &=e^{-e^{x}} \\ \frac{d H}{d x} &=e^{-e^{x}} \cdot\left(-e^{x}\right) \\ &=-e^{x} e^{-e^{x}} \end{aligned}$
$\left.\therefore \quad \frac{d H}{d x}\right|_{x=0}=-e^{0} \cdot e^{-e^{0}}$
$=-1 . e^{-1}$
$=-e^{-1}$
$=\frac{-1}{e}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $y=e^{m \sin ^{-1} x}$ then $\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-k y=0,$ where $k$ is
equal to
questions.solution:
$y=e^{msin^{-1}x}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{msin^{-1}x} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=m e^{msin^{-1}x}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=m y$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-2 x) \frac{d y}{d x}=m \frac{d y}{d x}$
$\Rightarrow\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=m \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}$
$\Rightarrow\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=m \cdot(m y)$
$\Rightarrow\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-m^{2} y=0$
$\therefore$
$k=m^{2}$
