questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $f(x)=x^{n}, n$ being a non-negative integer, then the values of $n$ for which
$f^{\prime}(\alpha+\beta)=f^{\prime}(\alpha)+f^{\prime}(\beta)$ for all $\alpha, \beta>0$ is
questions.solution:
We have,
$\begin{aligned} f(x) &=x^{n} \\ f^{\prime}(x) &=n x^{n-1} \end{aligned}$
Now, $f^{\prime}(\alpha+\beta)=f^{\prime}(\alpha)+f^{\prime}(\beta)$
$\Rightarrow \quad n(\alpha+\beta)^{n-1}=n \alpha^{n-1}+n \beta^{n-1}$
$\Rightarrow(a+\beta)^{n-1}=a^{n-1}+\beta^{n-1}$
From options we see that $n=2$, satisfy the above equation.
$\therefore$
$$
n=2
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y=(1+x)\left(1+x^{2}\right)\left(1+x^{4}\right) \ldots\left(1+x^{2 n}\right),$ then the value of $\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)$ at $x=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given,
$y=(1+x)\left(1+x^{2}\right)\left(1+x^{4}\right)+\ldots+\left(1+x^{2 n}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \log y=\log (1+x)+\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)$ $+\log \left(1+x^{4}\right)+\ldots+\log \left(1+x^{2n}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+x}+\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{3 x^{2}}{1+x^{3}}+\ldots+\frac{2 n x^{2 n-1}}{1+x^{2 n}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=y\left[\frac{1}{1+x}+\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{3 x^{2}}{1+x^{3}}+\ldots+\frac{2 n x^{2 n-1}}{1+x^{2 n}}\right]$
$\therefore \quad\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{x=0}=y\left(\frac{1}{1+0}\right)=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\log \left(\frac{e}{x^{2}}\right)}{\log \left(e x^{2}\right)}\right]+\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{3+2 \log x}{1-6 \log x}\right]$ then the value of $f^{\prime \prime}(x)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\log \left(\frac{e}{x^{2}}\right)}{\log \left(e x^{2}\right)}\right]+\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{3+2 \log x}{1-6 \log x}\right]$
$=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\log e-2 \log x}{\log e+2 \log x}\right]+\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{3+2 \log _{x} x}{1-6 \log x}\right]$
Let $2 \log x=\tan \theta,$ we get
$=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right]+\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{3+\tan \theta}{1-3 \tan \theta}\right]$
$\left.=\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right]+[\tan ^{-1} (\tan ((\tan ^{-1} 3)+\theta))\right]$
$=\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta+\tan ^{-1} 3+\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+\tan ^{-1} 3$
$\therefore \quad f(x)=$ constant
$\therefore f^{\prime}(x)=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The points of the ellipse $16 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=400$ at which the ordinate decreases at the same rate at which the abscissa increases is/are given by
questions.solution:
Given that, the ordinate decreases at the same rate at which the abscissa increases, therefore
$$
\frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{d x}{d t}
$$
Also, equation of ellipse
$$
16 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=400
$$
On differentiating wrt. $t$, we get 
$16 \times 2 x \frac{d x}{d t}+9 \times 2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 16 x \frac{d x}{d t}+9 y \frac{d y}{d t}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9 y \frac{d y}{d t}=-16 x \frac{d x}{d t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9 y \frac{d y}{d t}=-16 x\left(-\frac{d y}{d t}\right) \quad$ [using $\left.\mathrm{Eq.}(i)\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9 y=16 x \Rightarrow y=\frac{16}{9} x$
$16 x^{2}+9\left(\frac{16}{9} x\right)^{2}=400$
$\Rightarrow \quad 16 x^{2}+\frac{(16)^{2}}{9} x^{2}=400$
$\Rightarrow \quad 16 x\left[x+\frac{16}{9} x\right]=400$
$\Rightarrow \quad 16 x\left[\frac{25}{9} x\right]=400 \Rightarrow x^{2}=\frac{400 \times 9}{25 \times 16}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}=9 \Rightarrow x=\pm 3$
$\therefore$ Required points are $\left(3, \frac{16}{3}\right)$ and $\left(-3-\frac{16}{3}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A particle starts moving from rest from a fixed point in a fixed direction. The distance s from the fixed point at a timet is given by $s=t^{2}+a t-b+17,$ where $a$ and $b$ are real numbers. If the particle comes to rest after 5s at a distance of $s=25$ units from the fixed point, then values of $a$ and $b$ are. respectively
questions.solution:
Given, $s=t^{2}+a t-b+17$ 
After 5 s. $\left[\frac{d s}{d t}\right]_{t=5}=[2 t+a]_{t=5}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=-10$
At $t=5 \mathrm{s}, s=25$ units
$\therefore \quad 25=5^{2}+5 a-b+17$
$$
\mid \because s=25 \text { and } t=5 \mid
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad 25=25+5(-10)-b+17$
$\Rightarrow \quad 50-17=-b$
$\Rightarrow \quad b=-33$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For the curve $x^{2}+4 x y+8 y^{2}=64$ the tangents are parallel to the $x$ -axis only at the points
questions.solution:
Given curve is, $x^{2}+4 x y+8 y^{2}=64$...(i)
On differentiating w.r.t $x,$ we get $\quad 2 x+4\left(y+x \frac{d y}{d x}\right)+16 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x+4 y+(4 x+16 y) \frac{d y}{d x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{(x+2 y)}{2(x+4 y)}$
Since, tangent are parallel to $x$ -axis only.
$i, e$
$\frac{d y}{d x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-\frac{(x+2 y)}{2(x+4 y)}=0 \Rightarrow x+2 y=0$
$\ldots$
Now, on putting the valus of $x$ from Eqs. (i) in
(ii). we get
$$
\begin{array}{ll} 
& 4 y^{2}-8 y^{2}+8 y^{2}=64 \\
\Rightarrow & y^{2}=16 \\
\Rightarrow & y=\pm 4
\end{array}
$$
From Eq. (ii)
When $\quad y=4, x=-8$
and when $\quad y=-4, x=8$
Hence required points are (-8,4) and $(8-4)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $y=\left(\frac{3^{x}-1}{3^{x}+1}\right) \sin x+\log _{e}(1+x) \quad x>-1$ Then, at $x=0, \frac{d y}{d x}$ equals
questions.solution:
Given, $y=\left(\frac{3^{x}-1}{3^{x}+1}\right) \sin x+\log _{e}(1+x), x>-1$
Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(\frac{3^{x}-1}{3^{x}+1}\right) \sin x\right]+\frac{d}{d x} \log _{e}(1+x)$
$=\left(\frac{3^{x}-1}{3^{x}+1}\right) \frac{d}{d x} \sin x+\sin x \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{3^{x}-1}{3^{x}+1}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{1+x} \frac{d}{d x}(1+x)$
$=\left(\frac{3^{x}-1}{3^{x}+1}\right) \cos x+\sin x$
$\frac{\left(3^{x}+1\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(3^{x}-1\right)-\left(3^{x}-1\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(3^{x}+1\right)}{\left(3^{x}+1\right)^{2}}$
$+\frac{1}{1+x}$
$=\left(\frac{3^{x}-1}{3^{x}+1}\right) \cos x+\sin x\left(3^{x}+1\right)\left(3^{x} \log _{e} 3\right)$
$\quad \frac{-\left(3^{x}-1\right)\left(3^{x} \log _{t} 3\right)}{\left(3^{x}+1\right)^{2}}+\frac{1}{1+x}$
$\therefore \quad\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\text {at } x=0}=0+0+\frac{1}{1+0}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For $-\frac{\pi}{2}     < x < \frac{3 \pi}{2}, \quad$ the $\quad$ value $\quad$ of $\frac{d}{d x}\left\{\tan ^{-1} \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right\}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\frac{d}{d x}\left\{\tan ^{-1} \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right\}$
$=\frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right)^{2}} \frac{d}{d x} \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}$
$=\frac{(1+\sin x)^{2}}{1+\sin ^{2} x+2 \sin x+\cos ^{2} x}$
$=\frac{(1+\sin x)(-\sin x)-\cos (\cos x)}{(1+\sin x)^{2}}$
$=\frac{1}{2+2 \sin x} \times\left[-\left(\sin x+\sin ^{2} x+\cos ^{2} x\right)\right]$
$=\frac{1}{2(1+\sin x)} \times-(1+\sin x)=-\frac{1}{2}$

questions.q: Let \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\). Then \(\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})\) is \(=0\), when \(\mathrm{x}\) is equal to

questions.q: If \(y=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}-1}{x}\), then \(y^{\prime}(1)=\)

\(\begin{aligned} & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sec \theta-1}{\tan \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sin ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}\right)=\tan ^{-1} \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ & =\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \tan ^{-1} x, y^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+x^2\right)} \\ & y^{\prime}(1)=\frac{1}{2.2}=\frac{1}{4}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The minimum length of intercept on any tangent to the ellipse $\dfrac{x^2}{4} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$ cut by the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 25$ is
questions.solution:
The equation of any tangent to the ellipse $\dfrac{x^2}{4} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$ at the point $(2\cos\theta, 3\sin\theta)$ is given by:

$\dfrac{x\cos\theta}{2} + \dfrac{y\sin\theta}{3} = 1$

The perpendicular distance $p$ from the center of the circle $(0,0)$ to this tangent is:

$p = \dfrac{|-1|}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{4} + \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{9}}}$

Squaring both sides, we get:

$p^2 = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{4} + \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{9}}$

The length of the intercept $L$ made by the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 25$ (which has radius $R = 5$) on the tangent is given by:

$L = 2\sqrt{R^2 - p^2} = 2\sqrt{25 - p^2}$

To find the minimum length of the intercept, $L$ must be minimized, which means $p^2$ must be maximized. For $p^2$ to be maximum, the denominator $\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{4} + \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{9}$ must be minimum.

We know that the minimum value of $\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{4} + \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{9}$ occurs when $\cos\theta = 0$ and $\sin^2\theta = 1$, giving a minimum value of $\dfrac{1}{9}$.

Therefore, the maximum value of $p^2$ is $9$.

Substituting this back into the expression for $L$, we get the minimum length of the intercept:

$L_{\text{min}} = 2\sqrt{25 - 9} = 2\sqrt{16} = 2 \times 4 = 8$

Answer: $8$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the following ellipse: $\dfrac{x^2}{f(K^2 + 2K + 5)} + \dfrac{y^2}{f(K + 11)} = 1$, where $f(x)$ is a positive decreasing function. Then the value (values) of $K$ for which the major axis coincides with x-axis is
questions.solution:
The equation of the given ellipse is $\dfrac{x^2}{f(K^2 + 2K + 5)} + \dfrac{y^2}{f(K + 11)} = 1$.

For the major axis to lie along the x-axis, the denominator of $x^2$ must be strictly greater than the denominator of $y^2$.

$f(K^2 + 2K + 5) > f(K + 11)$

Since $f(x)$ is a decreasing function, the inequality reverses for its arguments:

$K^2 + 2K + 5 < K + 11$

$K^2 + K - 6 < 0$

$(K + 3)(K - 2) < 0$

$-3 < K < 2$

Thus, $K \in (-3, 2)$.

Answer: $K \in (-3, 2)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
A line of fixed length $a + b, a \neq b$ moves so that its ends are always on two fixed perpendicular straight lines. The
locus of a point which divides the line into two parts of legnth a and b is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2=(a+b)^2 \\ & A P=a ; P B=b \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{h^2}{a^2}+\frac{k^2}{b^2}=1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
With origin as a foucs and x = 4 as corresponding directrix, a family of ellipse are drawn. Then the locus of an end of
minor axis is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & h=-a e, k=b, \quad \frac{a}{e}-a e=4 \Rightarrow a\left(1-e^2\right)=4 e \Rightarrow \frac{b^2}{a}=4 e \\ & \Rightarrow b^2=4 a e \Rightarrow k^2=-4 h \Rightarrow y^2=-4 x \end{aligned}$ Which is a general conic and satisfying the condition of parabola.

A point $P$ with position vector $\vec{r}$ moves on that plane in such a way that $|\vec{r}-\vec{a}| \sim|\vec{r}-\vec{b}|=c$ (real constant). The locus of $P$ is a conic section whose eccentricity is
questions.solution: Hint : $e=\frac{|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|}{c}$

questions.q: The tangent at point \((a \cos \theta, b \sin \theta), 0  < \theta < \frac{\pi}{2}\), to the ellipse \(\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1\) meets the \(x\)-axis at \(T\) and \(y\)-axis at \(T_1\). Then the value of \(\min _{0 < \theta < \frac{\pi}{2}}(O T)\left(O T_1\right)\) is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the lines joining the focii of the ellipse \(\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1\) where \(a > b\), and an extremity of its minor axis is inclined at an angle \(60^{\circ}\), then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.solution:
Hint: \(\frac{b}{a e}=\tan 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=e \sqrt{3}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \because e^2=1-\frac{b^2}{a^2}=1-3 e^2 \\
& \Rightarrow e=+\frac{1}{2} \quad\left(e \neq-\frac{1}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Chords of an ellipse are drawn through the positive end of the minor axis. Their midpoint lies on
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{ll} \frac{x_1+0}{2}=h & \frac{y_1+b}{2}=k \\ \Rightarrow x_1=2 h & \Rightarrow y_1=2 k-b \end{array}$ $\frac{x_1^2}{a^2}+\frac{y_1^2}{b^2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{4 h^2}{a^2}+\frac{(2 k-b)^2}{b^2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{h^2}{a^2 / 4}+\frac{\left(k-\frac{b}{2}\right)^2}{b^2 / 4}=1$ $\therefore \frac{x^2}{a^2 / 4}+\frac{\left(y-\frac{b}{2}\right)^2}{b^2 / 4}=1 \rightarrow$ Locus of midpoint which is an ellipse

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The co-ordinate of a point on the auxiliary circle of the ellipse $x^{2}+2 y^{2}=4$ corresponding to the point on the ellipse whose eccentric angle is $60^{\circ}$ will be
questions.solution: $\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{2}=1$ Equation of auxiliary circle is $x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ $\therefore$ Point on the auxiliary circle is $\begin{array}{l} p\left(2 \cos 60^{\circ}, 2 \sin 60^{\circ}\right) \\ p(1, \sqrt{3}) \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The points of intersection of two ellipses $x^{2}+2 y^{2}-6 x-12 y+20=0$ and $2 x^{2}+y^{2}-10 x-6 y+15=0$ lie on a circle. The centre of the circle is
questions.solution:
$x^{2}+2 y^{2}-6 x-12 y+20+\lambda\left(2 x^{2}+y^{2}-10 x-6 y+15\right)=0$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow(1+2 \lambda) \mathrm{x}^{2}+(2+\lambda) \mathrm{y}^{2}-(10 \lambda+6) \mathrm{x}-(6 \lambda+12) \mathrm{y}+15 \lambda+20=0 \\
2+\lambda=1+2 \lambda \\
\Rightarrow \lambda=1
\end{array}$
$\therefore$ Centre $:\left(\frac{8}{3}, 3\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Area in the first quadrant between the ellipses $x^{2}+2 y^{2}=a^{2}$ and $2 x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}$ is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$A=\left(\frac{a^{2}}{6}+\int_{a / \sqrt{3}}^{a / \sqrt{2}} \sqrt{a^{2}-2 x^{2}} d x\right) \times 2=\frac{a^{2}}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ are the ends of minor axis and $\mathrm{S}$ and $\mathrm{S}^{\prime}$ are the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$, then the area of the rhombus
$\mathrm{SBS}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ will be
questions.solution: Hint: $\mathrm{B}=(0,3), \mathrm{B}^{\prime}=(0,-3)$ $\mathrm{S} \equiv(\mathrm{ae}, 0) \equiv\left(5 \times \sqrt{1-\frac{9}{25}}, 0\right) \equiv(4,0) \quad \therefore$ area $\mathrm{SBS}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}=2 \times$ area $\triangle \mathrm{BSB}^{\prime}=2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 6=24$ sq. unit

questions.q: Consider the curve $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$. The portion of the tangent at any point of the curve intercepted between the point of contact and the directrix subtends at the corresponding focus an angle of
questions.solution:

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider a tangent to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{y^{2}}{1}=1$ at any point. The locus of the midpoint of the portion intercepted between the axes is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Tangent at $\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$
$\frac{x x_{1}}{2}+\frac{y y_{1}}{1}=1$
Let mid point of intercept be $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$
$\mathrm{h}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}_{1}}, \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{y}_{1}}$ or $\mathrm{x}_{1}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~h}}, \mathrm{y}_{1}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{k}}$
locus is $\frac{1}{2 x^{2}}+\frac{1}{4 y^{2}}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Sand $T$ are the foci of an ellipse and $B$ is the end point of the minor axis. If $S T B$ is equilateral triangle, the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.solution: Equation of the ellipse is $$ \frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 $$ Foci are $S(a e, 0), T(-a e, 0)$ $B(0, b)$ is the end of the minor axis. STB is an equilateral triangle. $$ \begin{aligned} S B &=S T \\ S B^{2} &=S T^{2} \end{aligned} $$ $\Rightarrow \quad a^{2} e^{2}+b^{2}=4 a^{2} e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad b^{2}=3 a^{2} e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)=3 a^{2} e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 1-e^{2}=3 e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 4 e^{2}=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad e^{2}=\frac{1}{4}$ $\Rightarrow \quad e=\frac{1}{2}$ Eccentricity of the ellipse, $e=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $P$ is the extremity of the latusrectum of ellipse $3 x^{2}+4 y^{2}=48$ in the first quadrant. The eccentric angle of $P$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation of ellipse is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\quad 3 x^{2}+4 y^{2}=48 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 x^{2}}{48}+\frac{4 y^{2}}{48}=1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{12}=1 \\
\text { Here, } \quad a=4 \text { and } b=2 \sqrt{3}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$
$e=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}$
$=\sqrt{1-\frac{12}{16}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{16}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore$ Coordinates of $P$ are $=(2,3)$ $\left.(4 \cos \theta, 2 \sqrt{3} \sin \theta)=(2,3) \quad [\because p=\left(a e, \frac{b^{2}}{a}\right)\right]$
By comparing, we get
$$
4 \cos \theta=2 \text { and } 2 \sqrt{3} \sin \theta=3
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2}$ and $\sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}$ and $\sin \theta=\sin \frac{\pi}{3}$
$\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}$ and $\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}$
$\therefore$ eccentric angle of $P$ is $\frac{\pi}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $P$ be a point on the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$ and the line through $P$ parallel to the $Y$ -axis mects the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=9$ at $Q$ where $P, Q$ are on the same side of the $X$ -axis. If $R$ is a point on $P Q$ such that $\frac{P R}{R Q}=\frac{1}{2},$ then the locus of $R$ is
questions.solution: Since, point $P$ on the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$ $\therefore$ $P(3 \cos \theta, 2 \sin \theta)$ Now, equation of line parallel of $Y$ -axis is $$ x=3 \cos \theta $$ and above line meets circle at $Q$ $\therefore$ $Q(3 \cos \theta, 3 \sin \theta)$ Given, $\frac{P R}{R Q}=\frac{1}{2}$ $\therefore h=\frac{3 \cos \theta+6 \cos \theta}{3}, k=\frac{3 \sin \theta+4 \sin \theta}{3}$ $\Rightarrow \quad h=3 \cos \theta, \quad k=\frac{7}{3} \sin \theta$ $\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=h / 3, \sin \theta=\frac{3 k}{7}$ Now, $\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta=h^{2} / 9+\frac{9 k^{2}}{49}=1$ Hence, locus of a point is $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{9 y^{2}}{49}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $B$ is an extremity of the minor axis of an ellipse whose foci are $S$ and $S^{\prime} .$ If $\angle S B S^{\prime}$ is a right angle, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.solution: Slope of $S B, m_{1}=\frac{b-0}{0-a e}=-\frac{b}{a e}$ and slope of $S^{\prime} B, m_{2}=\frac{b-0}{0-(-a e)}=\frac{b}{a e}$ since, $\angle S B S^{\prime}$ is a right angle. $\therefore$ $$m_{1} m_{2}=-1$$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{-b}{a e} \times \frac{b}{a e}=-1$ $\Rightarrow \quad b^{2}=a^{2} e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 1-e^{2}=e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2 e^{2}=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad e^{2}=\frac{1}{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad e=\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad e=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ or $e=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Tangents are drawn to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1$ at the ends of both latusrectum. The area of the quadrilateral, so formed is
questions.solution: We have, $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad a=3$ and $b=\sqrt{5}$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad e &=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}} \\ &=\sqrt{1-\frac{5}{9}}=\frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Foci $=(\pm a e, 0)$ $=\left(\pm 3 \times \frac{2}{3}, 0\right)=(\pm 2,0)$ $\therefore$ Ends of latusrectum $=\left(\pm 2, \pm \frac{5}{3}\right)$ Equation of tangent at the end of latusrectum $P$ is $\frac{x \times 2}{9}+\frac{y \times 5 / 3}{5}=1$ $\frac{2}{9} x+\frac{1}{3} y=1$ $2 x+3 y=9$ $O A=\frac{9}{2}$ and $O B=3$ $\therefore$ Area of quadrilateral $A B C D$ $$ =4 \times \text { Area of } \Delta O A B $$ $=4 \times \frac{1}{2} \times O A \times O B$ $=4 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{9}{2} \times 3$ $=27 \mathrm{sq}$ units.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line $y=x+\lambda$ is tangent to the ellipse $2 x^{2}+3 y^{2}=1 .$ Then $, \lambda$ is
questions.solution:
The equation of the line is $y=x+\lambda$
On comparing it with $y=m x+c,$ we get
$$
m=1 \text { and } c=\lambda
$$
The equation of the ellipse is
$$
2 x^{2}+3 y^{2}=1
$$
or
$$
\frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2}}=1
$$
On comparing it with $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1,$ we get
$$
a^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } b^{2}=\frac{1}{3}
$$
If the line touches the ellipse, then
$$
c^{2}=a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2} \Rightarrow \lambda^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot 1+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{5}{6}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda^{2}=\frac{5}{6} \Rightarrow \lambda=\sqrt{\frac{5}{6}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of auxiliary circle of the ellipse $16 x^{2}+25 y^{2}+32 x-100 y=284$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation of ellipse is
$$
16 x^{2}+25 y^{2}+32 x-100 y=284
$$
Simplifying the given equation, we have ellipse as
$$
\frac{(x+1)^{2}}{25}+\frac{(y-2)^{2}}{16}=1
$$
So, the auxiliary equation of circle is
$$
\begin{array}{r}
(x+1)^{2}+(y-2)^{2}=25 \\
\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+2 x+1+y^{2}-4 y+4=25
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$
$x^{2}+y^{2}+2 x-4 y-20=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: On the ellipse $4 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=1$, the points at which the tangents are parallel to the line $8 x=9 y,$ are
questions.solution:
Given equation of ellipse is
$$
4 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=1
$$
On differentiating. we get $\Rightarrow \quad 8 x+18 yy'=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad y'=\frac{-8 x}{18 y}=m$
Also, $8 x=9 y \quad$ [equation of line] 
On differentiating. we got $\Rightarrow \quad 8=9 y^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow \quad y^{\prime}=\frac{8}{9}=m$
$[\because \text{tangents are parallel to this line}]$
So, by Eqs, we get $\frac{-8 x}{18 y}=\frac{8}{9}$
$\Rightarrow \quad-x=2 y$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=-2 y$
On substituting $x=-2 y$, we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
4(-2 y)^{2}+9 y^{2}=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad 16 y^{2}+9 y^{2}=1 \Rightarrow 25 y^{2}=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad y=\pm \frac{1}{5} \quad \therefore \quad x=\mp \frac{2}{5}
\end{array}
$$
So, required points are $\left(\frac{2}{5},-\frac{1}{5}\right)$ and $\left(-\frac{2}{5}, \frac{1}{5}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Number of intersecting points of the conics $4 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=1$ and $4 x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ is
questions.solution:
For curve I. 
$4 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2}}=1$
which is an equation of ellipee with $a=\frac{1}{2}$ and $b=\frac{1}{3}$
For curve $I I, 4 x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{1}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{(1)^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{(2)^{2}}=1$
which is also an equation of ellipse with $a=1$ and $b=2$
Hence, there is no intersecting point.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let the equation of an ellipse be $\frac{x^{2}}{144}+\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1$ Then, the radius of the circle with centre $(0, \sqrt{2})$ and passing through the foci of the ellipse is
questions.solution:
Given equation of ellipse is
Here,
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\frac{x^{2}}{144}+\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1 \\
a^{2}=144 \text { and } b^{2}=25
\end{array}
$$
Now.
$$
e=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{25}{144}}
$$
$$
=\sqrt{\frac{119}{144}}=\frac{\sqrt{119}}{12}
$$
Foci of an ellipse $=(\pm a e, 0)$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\left(\pm 12 \times \frac{\sqrt{119}}{12}, 0\right) \\
=(\pm \sqrt{119}, 0)
\end{array}
$$
Since, the circle with centre $(0, \sqrt{2})$ and passing through foci $(\pm \sqrt{119}, 0)$ of the ellipse.
$\therefore$ Radius of circle $=\sqrt{(\sqrt{119}-0)^{2}+(0-\sqrt{2})^{2}}$
$$
=\sqrt{119+2}=\sqrt{121}=11
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For the variable, the locus of the point of intersection of the lines $3 t x-2 y+6 t=0$ and $3 x+2 t y-6=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation of lines are
$$
3 t \times-2 y+6 t=0
$$
and $\quad 3 x+2 t y-6=0$
On multiplying Eq. (i) by $t$ and then adding in Eq. (ii), we get $\left(3 t^{2}+3\right) x+6 t^{2}-6=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{2\left(1-t^{2}\right)}{\left(1+t^{2}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x+x t^{2}=2-2 t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x+2) t^{2}=(2-x)$
$\Rightarrow \quad t^{2}=\frac{2-x}{2+x}$
On multiplying Eq. (ii) by $t$ and then subtract from Eq. (1). we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\left(-2-2 t^{2}\right) y+6 t+6 t=0 \\
12 t=2\left(1+t^{2}\right) y
\end{array}
$$
On squaring both sides, we get $144 t^{2}=4 y^{2}\left(1+t^{2}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 144\left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)=4 y^{2}\left(1+\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)^{2}$ [form Eq. (ii)]
$\Rightarrow \quad 36\left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)=y^{2}\left(\frac{4}{2+x}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 36 \frac{(2-x)}{(2+x)}=\frac{16 y^{2}}{(2+x)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 36\left(4-x^{2}\right)=16 y^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9\left(4-x^{2}\right)=4 y^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 36-9 x^{2}=4 y^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9 x^{2}+4 y^{2}=36$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$ which represents an ellipse

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of the mid-points of the chords of an ellipse $x^{2}+4 y^{2}=4$ that are drawn from the positive end of the minor axis, is
options: a circle with centre $\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0\right)$ and radius 1
a parabola with focus $\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0\right)$ and directrix $x=-1$
an ellipse with centre $\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\right),$ major axis 1 and minor axis $\frac{1}{2}$
a hyperbola with centre $\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\right),$ transverse axis 1 and conjugate axis $\frac{1}{2}$
questions.solution: Given equation of an ellipse is $$ x^{2}+4 y^{2}=4 $$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{1}=1$ $\therefore$ Coordinate of positive end of minor axis is $B(0,1)$ Let mid-point of the chord $B P$ is $M(h, k)$ Then, $\quad(h, k)=\left(\frac{0+x}{2}, \frac{1+y}{2}\right)$ $\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{x}{2} \quad \Rightarrow x=2 h$ and $\quad k=\frac{1+y}{2} \Rightarrow y=2 k-1$ $\therefore$ $P(x, y)=\{2 h,(2 k-1)\}$ Since, the point '$P$' lies ellipses so form Eq. (i). we get $(2 h)_{1}^{2}+4(2 k-1)^{2}=4$ $\Rightarrow \quad 4 h^{2}+4(2 k-1)^{2}=4$ $\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}+4 k^{2}+1-4 k=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}+4 k^{2}-4 k=0$ Thus, required locus is an ellipse whose equation is $x^{2}+4 y^{2}-4 y=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{(x-0)^{2}}{1}+\frac{\left(y-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)}=1$ whose centre $\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ and major and minor axis $\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line $y=x$ intersects the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{9}-\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1$ at the points $P$ and $Q .$ The eccentricity of ellipse with $P Q$ as major axis and minor axis of length $\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation of hyperbola and line are $\frac{x^{2}}{9}-\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1$ and $y=x$ respectively.
For intersection point of both curve put $y=x$, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x^{2}}{9}-\frac{x^{2}}{25}=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}=\frac{9 \times 25}{16}=\left(\frac{15}{4}\right)^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad x=\pm \frac{15}{4} \text { and } y=\pm \frac{15}{4}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ Intersetion points $P\left(\frac{15}{4}, \frac{15}{4}\right)$
and $\quad Q\left(\frac{-15}{4}, \frac{-15}{4}\right)$
Since, $P Q$ is major axis, then its length
$$
=2 \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{15}{4}=\frac{15}{\sqrt{2}}
$$
and length of minor axis is $\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}$ (given)
i.e., Major axis, $2 a=\frac{15}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow a=\frac{15}{2 \sqrt{2}}$
and minor axis, $2 b=\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow b=\frac{5}{2 \sqrt{2}}$
$\therefore$ Eccentricity of an ellipse
$$
=\sqrt{\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{2}}
$$
$=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{8}{9}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the distance between the foci of an ellipse is equal to the length of the latusrectum, then its eccentricity is
questions.solution:
Given,
In ellipse the distance between the foci
= Length of the latusrectum 
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow \quad & \text { 2ae }=\frac{2 b^{2}}{a} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \quad a^{2} e=b^{2} \Rightarrow e=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\end{array}$
$\because \quad e^{2}=1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad e^{2}=1-e \quad$ [from Eq.
$\Rightarrow e^{2}+e-1=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad e=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1+4}}{2}$
(by quadratic formula) $\Rightarrow \quad e=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow$
$$
e=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \quad(\because 1>e>0)
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line $x=2 y$ intersects the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{4}+y^{2}=1$ at the points $P$ and $Q .$ The equation of the circle with $P Q$ as diameter is
questions.solution: Solving $x=2 y$ and $\frac{x^{2}}{4}+y^{2}=1$ Put $x=2 y$ in Eq. (ii), we get $\frac{(2 y)^{2}}{4}+y^{2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{4 y^{2}}{4}+y^{2}=1$ $=$ $2 y^{2}=1 \Rightarrow y=\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\therefore$ From $\mathrm{E} q$ (i), $x=\pm \sqrt{2}$ $\therefore \quad P\left(\sqrt{2} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ and $Q\left(-\sqrt{2},-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ Equation of circle with $P Q$ as diameter is $$ \begin{array}{l} (x-\sqrt{2})(x+\sqrt{2})+\left(y-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(y+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad x^{2}-2+y^{2}-\frac{1}{2}=0 \Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}=\frac{5}{2} \end{array} $$

on $\frac{x^{2}}{10}+\frac{y^{2}}{8}=1$
Whose distance from centre (0,0) is 3 units. $\therefore 10 \cos ^{2} \theta+8 \sin ^{2} \theta=9=9\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^{2} \theta=1 \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}
$$

questions.q: The line $\mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{t}^2$ intersects the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$ in real points if
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Hints: } \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1 ; \mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{t}^2 \\
& \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{4 t^4}{4}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{9}+t^4=1 \Rightarrow x^2=9\left(1-t^4\right) \\
& x^2 \geq 0 \Rightarrow 9\left(1-t^4\right) \geq 0 \Rightarrow t^4-1 \leq 0 \\
& \Rightarrow\left(t^2-1\right)\left(t^2+1\right) \leq 0 \\
& \Rightarrow t^2-1 \leq 0 \quad\left(\because t^2+1>0\right) \\
& \Rightarrow|t| \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A mapping is selected at random from all mappings $f: A \to A$, where set $A = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n\}$. If the probability that the mapping is injective is $\dfrac{3}{32}$, then the value of $n$ is
questions.solution:
The total number of mappings from set $A$ to $A$, where $A$ has $n$ elements, is $n^n$.

The number of injective (one-one) mappings from $A$ to $A$ is $n!$.

The probability that a randomly selected mapping is injective is given by $\dfrac{n!}{n^n}$.

We are given that this probability is $\dfrac{3}{32}$.

Equating the two, we get $\dfrac{n!}{n^n} = \dfrac{3}{32}$.

Checking the given options, for $n = 4$, we have $\dfrac{4!}{4^4} = \dfrac{24}{256} = \dfrac{3}{32}$.

Thus, the value of $n$ is $4$.

Answer: $4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $A = [a, \infty)$ denotes the domain, then $f: [a, \infty) \to B$, which is defined by $f(x) = 2x^3 - 3x^2 + 6$ will have an inverse for the smallest real value of 'a' if
questions.solution:
Given $f(x) = 2x^3 - 3x^2 + 6$

Differentiating with respect to $x$, we get:

$f'(x) = 6x^2 - 6x = 6x(x - 1)$

For $f(x)$ to have an inverse, it must be bijective, which means it must be strictly monotonic (either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing) on its domain $[a, \infty)$.

The critical points are $x = 0$ and $x = 1$.

$f'(x) > 0$ for $x \in (-\infty, 0) \cup (1, \infty)$

$f'(x) < 0$ for $x \in (0, 1)$

For $f(x)$ to be strictly increasing on $[a, \infty)$, we must have $a \ge 1$. The smallest real value of $a$ for which $f(x)$ is one-one is $a = 1$.

For $f(x)$ to be onto, the codomain $B$ must be equal to the range of $f(x)$ on $[1, \infty)$.

Since $f(x)$ is strictly increasing on $[1, \infty)$, its minimum value occurs at $x = 1$:

$f(1) = 2(1)^3 - 3(1)^2 + 6 = 5$

As $x \rightarrow \infty$, $f(x) \rightarrow \infty$.

Thus, the range is $[5, \infty)$, which means $B = [5, \infty)$.

Answer: $a = 1, B = [5, \infty)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Number of elements in the range set of $f(x) = \left[\dfrac{x}{15}\right]\left[-\dfrac{15}{x}\right]$, for all $x \in (0, 90)$; (where $[.]$ denotes the greatest integer function) is
questions.solution:
Let $x = 15t$. Since $x \in (0, 90)$, we have $t \in (0, 6)$.

The given function can be rewritten as $f(x) = [t] \left[-\dfrac{1}{t}\right]$.

We can analyze the values of $f(x)$ by dividing the interval $t \in (0, 6)$ into two cases:

Case 1: $t \in (0, 1)$
Here, $[t] = 0$. Since $t > 0$, $-\dfrac{1}{t}$ is a finite negative number, making $\left[-\dfrac{1}{t}\right]$ a finite integer.
Thus, $f(x) = 0 \times \text{integer} = 0$.

Case 2: $t \in [1, 6)$
Here, $1 \le t < 6$, which implies $0 < \dfrac{1}{t} \le 1$.
Multiplying by $-1$, we get $-1 \le -\dfrac{1}{t} < 0$.
Therefore, the greatest integer $\left[-\dfrac{1}{t}\right] = -1$ for all $t \in [1, 6)$.

In this interval, the possible values for $[t]$ are $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.
Thus, $f(x) = [t] \times (-1) = -[t]$.
The possible values of $f(x)$ for $t \in [1, 6)$ are $-1, -2, -3, -4, -5$.

Combining both cases, the range set of $f(x)$ is $\{0, -1, -2, -3, -4, -5\}$.

The number of elements in the range set is $6$.

Answer: $6$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let domain and range of $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ is $[0, \infty)$. If $f(x)$ is an increasing function, $g(x)$ is a decreasing function, $h(x) = f\{g(x)\}, h(0) = 0$ and $p(x) = h(x^3 - 2x^2 + 2x) - h(4)$, then for all $x \in (0, 2)$
questions.solution:
Given $h(x) = f(g(x))$.

Since $f(x)$ is an increasing function and $g(x)$ is a decreasing function, their composition $h(x) = f(g(x))$ is a decreasing function.

Let $u(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 + 2x$.

Finding the derivative of $u(x)$:
$u'(x) = 3x^2 - 4x + 2$
The discriminant of this quadratic is $D = (-4)^2 - 4(3)(2) = 16 - 24 = -8 < 0$.
Since $u'(x) > 0$ for all $x$, $u(x)$ is an increasing function.

For the given interval $x \in (0, 2)$, the range of $u(x)$ is:
$u(0) = 0$
$u(2) = 2^3 - 2(2^2) + 2(2) = 8 - 8 + 4 = 4$
Thus, $0 < u(x) < 4$ for all $x \in (0, 2)$.

Given $p(x) = h(u(x)) - h(4)$.

Since $h(x)$ is a decreasing function and $0 < u(x) < 4$, we have:
$h(0) > h(u(x)) > h(4)$

It is given that $h(0) = 0$, so:
$0 > h(u(x)) > h(4)$

Subtracting $h(4)$ from all terms:
$0 - h(4) > h(u(x)) - h(4) > h(4) - h(4)$
$-h(4) > p(x) > 0$

Therefore, $0 < p(x) < -h(4)$ for all $x \in (0, 2)$.

Answer: $0 < p(x) < -h(4)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $|x + 1|^{\log_{(x+1)}(3 + 2x - x^2)} = (x - 3)|x|$ has
questions.solution:
For the given equation to be defined, the base and the argument of the logarithm must satisfy specific conditions.

Base of the logarithm: $x + 1 > 0$ and $x + 1 eq 1$
$\Rightarrow x > -1$ and $x eq 0$

Argument of the logarithm: $3 + 2x - x^2 > 0$
$\Rightarrow x^2 - 2x - 3 < 0$
$\Rightarrow (x - 3)(x + 1) < 0$

Since $x > -1$, we know $x + 1 > 0$. Therefore, we must have $x - 3 < 0 \Rightarrow x < 3$.
Thus, the domain of the equation is $x \in (-1, 0) \cup (0, 3)$.

In this domain, $x + 1 > 0$, so $|x + 1| = x + 1$. The equation simplifies to:
$(x + 1)^{\log_{(x+1)}(3 + 2x - x^2)} = (x - 3)|x|$

Using the logarithmic identity $a^{\log_a b} = b$, the left-hand side becomes $3 + 2x - x^2$.
$\Rightarrow 3 + 2x - x^2 = (x - 3)|x|$
$\Rightarrow -(x^2 - 2x - 3) = (x - 3)|x|$
$\Rightarrow -(x - 3)(x + 1) = (x - 3)|x|$

Since $x < 3$, we know $x - 3 eq 0$. Dividing both sides by $(x - 3)$, we get:
$-(x + 1) = |x|$

Now, we analyze this in the two intervals of our domain:

Case 1: $x \in (0, 3)$
Here, $|x| = x$. The equation becomes:
$-(x + 1) = x$
$\Rightarrow -x - 1 = x$
$\Rightarrow 2x = -1 \Rightarrow x = -\dfrac{1}{2}$
This value is not in the interval $(0, 3)$, so it is rejected.

Case 2: $x \in (-1, 0)$
Here, $|x| = -x$. The equation becomes:
$-(x + 1) = -x$
$\Rightarrow -x - 1 = -x$
$\Rightarrow -1 = 0$
This is a contradiction, yielding no solution.

Since both cases yield no valid values for $x$, the equation has no solution.

Answer: no solution

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the domain of $f(x)$ is $(0, 1)$, then the domain of $y = f(e^x) + f(\ln|x|)$ is
questions.solution:
The domain of $f(x)$ is given as $(0, 1)$.

For the function $f(e^x)$ to be defined, the argument $e^x$ must lie in the domain of $f(x)$.

$0 < e^x < 1$

Taking the natural logarithm, we get:

$x < 0$

For the function $f(\ln|x|)$ to be defined, the argument $\ln|x|$ must also lie in the domain of $f(x)$.

$0 < \ln|x| < 1$

This implies:

$1 < |x| < e$

Solving for $x$, we get:

$x \in (-e, -1) \cup (1, e)$

The domain of the function $y = f(e^x) + f(\ln|x|)$ is the intersection of the domains of $f(e^x)$ and $f(\ln|x|)$.

$x \in (-\infty, 0) \cap ((-e, -1) \cup (1, e))$

This gives:

$x \in (-e, -1)$

Answer: $(-e, -1)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f$ be a real valued function defined for all real numbers $x$ such that for some fixed $a > 0$, it satisfies $f(x + a) = \dfrac{1}{2} + \sqrt{f(x) - (f(x))^2} \, \forall x$, then $f(x)$ is periodic with period
questions.solution:
Given $f(x + a) = \dfrac{1}{2} + \sqrt{f(x) - (f(x))^2}$

Rearranging the terms, we get:

$f(x + a) - \dfrac{1}{2} = \sqrt{f(x) - (f(x))^2}$

Squaring both sides:

$\left(f(x + a) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = f(x) - (f(x))^2$

$\left(f(x + a) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} - \left(f(x) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2$

$\left(f(x + a) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(f(x) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{4}$

Replacing $x$ by $x + a$, we obtain:

$\left(f(x + 2a) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(f(x + a) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{4}$

Subtracting the first equation from the second equation:

$\left(f(x + 2a) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 - \left(f(x) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = 0$

$\left(f(x + 2a) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \left(f(x) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2$

From the given equation, $f(x + a) = \dfrac{1}{2} + \sqrt{f(x) - (f(x))^2} \ge \dfrac{1}{2}$ for all $x$. Thus, $f(x) \ge \dfrac{1}{2}$ for all $x$, which implies $f(x) - \dfrac{1}{2} \ge 0$.

Taking the positive square root on both sides:

$f(x + 2a) - \dfrac{1}{2} = f(x) - \dfrac{1}{2}$

$f(x + 2a) = f(x)$

Therefore, $f(x)$ is a periodic function with period $2a$.

Answer: $2a$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If a differentiable function satisfies
$(x - y)f(x + y) - (x + y)f(x - y) = 2(x^2y - y^3) \, \forall x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ and $f(1) = 2$, then
questions.solution:
Let $x + y = u$ and $x - y = v$.

Then $x = \dfrac{u+v}{2}$ and $y = \dfrac{u-v}{2}$.

The right hand side of the given equation can be simplified as:

$2(x^2y - y^3) = 2y(x^2 - y^2) = 2y(x-y)(x+y) = 2 \left( \dfrac{u-v}{2} \right) (v) (u) = uv(u-v)$

Substituting these into the given equation, we get:

$v f(u) - u f(v) = uv(u-v)$

Dividing the entire equation by $uv$ (for $u, v \neq 0$):

$\dfrac{f(u)}{u} - \dfrac{f(v)}{v} = u - v$

Rearranging the terms to separate the variables:

$\dfrac{f(u)}{u} - u = \dfrac{f(v)}{v} - v$

Since this holds for all $u, v \neq 0$, both sides must be equal to a constant $c$:

$\dfrac{f(u)}{u} - u = c \Rightarrow f(u) = u^2 + cu$

Since $f(x)$ is a differentiable function, it must be continuous everywhere, including at $x = 0$.

$f(0) = \lim_{u \to 0} (u^2 + cu) = 0$

Thus, $f(x) = x^2 + cx$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

We are given $f(1) = 2$. Substituting $x = 1$:

$1^2 + c(1) = 2 \Rightarrow c = 1$

Therefore, the function is $f(x) = x^2 + x$.

Checking the given options:

$\bullet$ $f(x) = x^2 + x$ is a polynomial function.

$\bullet$ $f(3) = 3^2 + 3 = 12$.

$\bullet$ $f(0) = 0^2 + 0 = 0$.

Answer: $f(x)$ must be a polynomial function; $f(3) = 12$; $f(0) = 0$

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For the mapping $f: \mathbb{R}-\{1\} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}-\{2\}$, given by $f(x)=\frac{2 x}{x-1}$, which of the following is correct ?
options: f is onto but not one-one
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{2 x}{x-1} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-2}{(x-1)^2}  < 0$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is one-one
Let, $\mathrm{y}_1 \in \mathbb{R}-\{2\}$ and $\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{x}_1\right)=\mathrm{y}_1$
Then, $y_1=\frac{2 x_1}{x_1-1} \Rightarrow x_1=\frac{y_1}{y_1-2} \in \mathbb{R}-\{1\}$
$\therefore f(x)$ is onto.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, X=\{x \mid 0  < x < 1\}$ is defined as $f(x)=\frac{2 x-1}{1-|2 x-1|}$. Then
options: f is neither injective nor surjective
questions.solution:
Put $2 \mathrm{x}-1=\mathrm{t}$, then the function becomes
$\begin{aligned}
&f(t)=\frac{t}{1-|t|},-1  < t < 1 \\
&\therefore f(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}
\frac{t}{1+t},-1 < t \leq 0 \\
\frac{t}{1-t}, 0 < t < 1
\end{array}\right.
\end{aligned}$
$\because$ It is continuous and $f\left(-1^{+}\right)=-\infty, f\left(1^{-}\right)=+\infty$
$\therefore$ Range $=(-\infty,-\infty)=\mathbb{R}$
It is differentiable also,
$f^{\prime}(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{(1+t)^2},-1 < t < 0 \\
\frac{1}{(1-t)^2}, 0 < t < 1
\end{array}\right.$
$\because f^{\prime}(t) > 0 \forall-1 < t < 1$
$\therefore$ f is injective

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=x^{2}-\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}$ for all $x \in R$. Then,
options: $f$ is one-one but not onto mapping
$f$ is onto but not one-one mapping
questions.solution:
We have,
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &=x^{2}-\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}} \\
f(-x) &=(-x)^{2}-\frac{(-x)^{2}}{1+(-x)^{2}} \\
&=x^{2}-\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}=f(x) \\
\therefore f(-x)=f(x) &
\end{aligned}
$$
So, $f(x)$ is many one Again, $f(x)=x^{2}-\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}$
$$
=\frac{x^{2}+x^{4}-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{x^{4}}{1+x^{2}}
$$
$\therefore$ Range of $f(x)$ will be $[0, \infty)$ But, co-domain of $f(x)=R$
$\therefore$ Range $\neq$ co-domain. So, $f(x)$ is onto.

questions.q: Let $S, T, U$ be three non-void sets and $f: S \rightarrow T, g: T \rightarrow U$ be so that $g o f: s \rightarrow U$ is surjective. Then,
options: $g$ is surjective, $f$ may not be so
$f$ is surjective, $g$ may not be so
$f$ and $g$ both may not be surjective
questions.solution:
We have,
gof : $S \rightarrow U$ is an onto function.
Let $Z$ be any arbitrary element such that $Z \in U$. Now, $g o f: S \rightarrow U$ is onto $\Rightarrow g_{0} f(x)=Z,$ for $x \in S$
$\Rightarrow g(f(x))=Z$
$\Rightarrow g(y)=Z,$ where $y=f(x) \in T$ for all $Z \in U$
$\therefore$ For all $Z \in U,$ there exists $y=f(x) \in T$ such that $g(y)=z$
$\therefore g: T \rightarrow U$ is an onto function.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Two particles $A$ and $B$ move from rest along a straight line with constant accelerations $f$ and $h,$ respectively. If $A$ takes $m$ seconds more than $B$ and describes $n$ units more than that of $B$ acquiring the same speed, then
questions.solution:
Let $B$ travels $x$ units, $v=u+a t$
According to problem, $h t=f(t+m)$ $\begin{aligned} h t=& f t+f m \\ h t-f t &=f m \\ t(h-f) &=f m \\ \frac{h-f}{f} &=\frac{m}{t} \end{aligned}$
$t=m\left(\frac{f}{h-f}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad t^{2}=m^{2}\left(\frac{f}{h-f}\right)^{2}$
Again, $n+x=\frac{1}{2} f(t+m)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad h+\frac{1}{2} h t^{2}=\frac{1}{2} f(t+m)^{2}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii) $\frac{m}{n}=\frac{2(h-f)}{m f h}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(h-f) n=\frac{m^{2} f h}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=e^{x}$ and $g: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $g(x)=x^{2}$. The mapping gof : $R \rightarrow R$ be defined by (gof) $(x)$ $=g f(x) \mid \forall x \in R .$ Then.
options: gof is bijective but $f$ is not injective
gof is injective and $g$ is injective
gof is injective but $g$ is not bijective
gof is surjective and $g$ is surjective
questions.solution:
We have, $f: R \rightarrow R$, defined by $f(x)=e^{x}$ and $g: R \rightarrow R$ defined by $g(x)=x^{2}$
Now, We have
$$
\begin{aligned}
(g v f)(x) &=g(f(x)) \\
&=g\left(e^{x}\right) \\
&=\left(e^{x}\right)^{2} \\
&=e^{2 x}, \forall x \in R
\end{aligned}
$$
$\Rightarrow$ gof is injective and $g$ is neither injective nor surjective. $\Rightarrow g o f$ is injective but $g(x)$ is not bijective.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the function $y=\log _{a}\left(x+\sqrt{\left.x^{2}+1\right)}\right.$ $a>0, a \neq 1 .$ The inverse of the function
questions.solution:
Given, $y=\log _{a}\left(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\right), a>0, a \neq 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{y}=\left(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \begin{aligned} a^{-y} &=\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}} \\ &=\sqrt{x^{2}+1}-x \end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{y}-a^{-y}=2 x \Rightarrow x=\frac{a^{y}-a^{-y}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad f^{-1}(y)=\frac{e^{y \log a}-e^{-y \log a}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow f^{-1}(y)=\sinh (y \log a)$$\quad\left(\because \frac{e^{x}-e^{-x}}{2}=\sinh (x)\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be such that $f$ is injective and $f(x) f(y)=f(x+y)$ for $\forall x, y \in R .$ If $f(x)$
$f(y), f(z)$ are in G.P., then $x, y, z$ are in
options: AP depending on the value of $x, y, z$
GP depending on the value of $x, y, z$
questions.solution:
Since, $f: R \rightarrow R$ is injective and $f(x) f(y)=f(x+y), \forall x, y \in R$
$\therefore \quad f(x)=a^{x}$
Again, $f(x), f(y), f(z)$ are in GP. $\Rightarrow \quad(f(y))^{2}=f(x) \cdot f(y)$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{2 y}=a^{x} \cdot a^{z}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{2 y}=a^{x+y}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 y=x+z$
$\therefore x, y, z$ are in $\mathrm{AP}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\log _{0.3}(x-1)    < \log _{0.09}(x-1),$ then $x$ lies in
the interval
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad \log _{0.3}(x-1)    < \log _{0.09}(x-1)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \log _{0.3}(x-1) < \log _{0.3^{2}}(x-1)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \log _{0.3}(x-1)^{2} < \log _{0.3}(x-1)$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x-1)^{2}>x-1 \quad[\because 0.3 < 1]$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}+1-2 x-x+1>0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}-3 x+2>0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x-1)(x-2)>0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x < 1, x>2 \Rightarrow x>2 \quad[\because x \neq 1]$
$\therefore \quad(2, \infty)$
Hence, x les in the interval $(2, \infty)$

$$

$$

questions.q: Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be defined as $f(x)=\frac{x^{2}-x+4}{x^{2}+x+4}$. Then, range of the function $f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
Let $y=\frac{x^{2}-x+4}{x^{2}+x+4}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2} y+x y+4 y=x^{2}-x+4$
$\Rightarrow(y-1) x^{2}+(y+1) x+4 y-4=0$
For $x$ to be real, discriminant of the above quadratic equation should be greater than equal to 0 
$\\ \Rightarrow \quad(y+1)^{2}-4(y-1)(4 y-4) \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(y+1)^{2}-16(y-1)^{2} \geq$
$\Rightarrow(y+1+4 y-4)(y+1-4 y+4) \geq$
$(5 y-3)(5-3 y) \geq 0$
$\therefore \quad y \in\left[\frac{3}{5}, \frac{5}{3}\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f: N \rightarrow R$ be such that $f(1)=1$ and $f(1)+2 f(2)+3 f(3)+\ldots+n f(n)=n(n+1) f(n),$ for all $n \in N, n \geq 2,$ where $N$ is the set of natural numbers and $R$ is the set of real numbers. Then, the value of $f(500)$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $f: N \rightarrow R$ such that $f(1)=1$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { and } f(1)+2 f(2)+3 f(3)+\ldots+n f(n) \\
\quad=n(n+1) f(n), \forall n \geq 2 \\
\text { Clearly, } f(1)+2 f(2)=2(2+1) f(2) \\
\Rightarrow \quad f(1)=6 f(2)-2 f(2) \\
\Rightarrow \quad f(1)=4 f(2) \\
\Rightarrow \quad f(2)=\frac{f(1)}{4}=\frac{1}{4}
\end{array}
$$
Similarly.
$$
f(1)+2 f(2)+3 f(3)=3(3+1) f(3)
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1+\frac{1}{2}+3 f(3)=12 f(3)$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9 f(3)=\frac{3}{2} \Rightarrow f(3)=\frac{1}{6}$
and $f(1)+2 f(2)+3 f(3)+4 f(4)=4(5) f(4)$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=16 f(4)$
$\Rightarrow \quad f(4)=\frac{2}{16}=\frac{1}{8}$
In general, $f(n)=\frac{1}{2 n}$
$\therefore \quad f(500)=\frac{1}{1000}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The range of the function $y=3 \sin \left(\sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{16}-x^{2}}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Given function is $y=3 \sin \left(\sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{16}-x^{2}}\right)$
Here, domain is $\left[0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right]$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\begin{aligned}
x=& 0 \\
y &=3 \sin \left(\sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{16}-0}\right) \\
&=3 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=3 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \\
\text { When } x=\frac{\pi}{4}, y &=3 \sin \left(\sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{16}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{16}}\right) \\
&=3 \sin (0)=0
\end{aligned}
\end{array}
$$
Hence, range of given function is $\left[0, \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of onto functions from the set $\{1,2, \ldots, 11\}$ to the set $\{1,2, \ldots, 10\}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\quad A=\{1,2, \ldots, 11\}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & n(A)=11 \text { and } B=\{1,2, \ldots, 10\} \\ \therefore & n(B)=10\end{array}$
$\therefore \text{Hence number of onto function}$ $=^{n(A)} C_{n(B)} \times n(B) ! \times n(B)$
$={ }^{11} C_{10} \times 10 ! \times 10$
$=(11 \times 10 !) \times 10=11 ! \times 10$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=2^{100} x+1, g(x)=3^{100} x+1$, then the set of real numbers $x$ such that $f[g(x)\}=x$ is
options: a finite set with more than one element
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad f(x)=2^{100} \cdot x+1$
$g(x)=3^{300} \cdot x+1$
Now, $f\{g(x)\}=x$
$\Rightarrow \quad f\left(3^{100}. x+1\right)=x$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2^{100}\left\{3^{100} \cdot x+1\right\}+1=x$
$\Rightarrow \quad 6^{100} \cdot x+2^{100}+1=x$
$\Rightarrow \quad x\left(1-6^{100}\right)=\left(1+2^{100}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{1+2^{100}}{1-6^{100}}$
Hence, fog $(x)=x$ represent a singleton set.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=x\left(\frac{1}{x-1}+\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x+1}\right), x>1$. Then
questions.solution:
Given function is
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &=x\left\{\frac{1}{x-1}+\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x+1}\right\}, x>1 \\
&=x\left\{\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}+\frac{1}{x}\right\}=\frac{2 x^{2}}{x^{2}-1}+1 \\
&=\left\{\frac{2}{\left(1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)}+1\right\}>3
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore \quad f(x)>3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which of the following real valued functions is/are not even functions?
options: $f(x)=x-[x]$, where $[x]$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $x$
questions.solution:
We know that, if $f(-x)=f(x)$, then function is even and $f(-x)=-f(x)$, then function is odd.
(a) $f(x)=x^{3} \sin x$
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(-x) &=(-x)^{3} \sin (-x) \\
&=-x^{3}(-\sin x) \\
&=x^{3} \sin x=f(x)
\end{aligned}
$$
So, $f(x)$ is even.
(b) $f(x)=x^{2} \cos x$
$$
f(-x)=(-x)^{2} \cos (-x)=x^{2} \cos x=f(x)
$$
So, $f(x)$ is even.
(c) $f(x)=e^{x} x^{3} \sin x$
$$
\begin{array}{r}
f(-x)=e^{-x}(-x)^{3} \sin (-x) \\
=e^{-x} x^{3} \sin x \neq f(x)
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore \quad f(x)$ is not even.
(d) $f(x)=x-[x]$ $f(-x)=(-x)-[-x] \neq f(x)$
$\therefore f(x)$ is not even.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $R$ be the set of real numbers and the functions $f: R \rightarrow R$ and $g: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=x^{2}+2 x-3$ and $g(x)=x+1 .$ Then, the value of $x$ for which $f(g(x))=g(f(x))$ is
questions.solution:
According to question,
$$
f(g(x))=g(f(x))
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad f(x+1)=g\left(x^{2}+2 x-3\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x+1)^{2}+2(x+1)-3=x^{2}+2 x-3+1$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+1+2 x+2 x+2-3=x^{2}+2 x-2$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+4 x=x^{2}+2 x-2$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+4 x-x^{2}-2 x+2=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x+2=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x=-2$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad x=-1
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A balloon starting from rest is ascending from ground with uniform acceleration of \(4 \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{sec}^2\). At the end of \(5 \mathrm{sec}\), a stone is dropped from it. If \(\mathrm{T}\) be the time to reach the stone to the ground and \(\mathrm{H}\) be the height of the balloon when the stone reaches the ground, then
questions.solution:
Hint :
Let after \(5 \mathrm{sec}\) ballon is at \(B\) Given: \(a=4 \mathrm{ft} / \sec ^2 u(\) Initial velocity \()=0\) \(\mathrm{t}=5 \mathrm{sec}\) Distance covered by ballon in \(5 \mathrm{sec}\); \(S=u t+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{at}^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 5^2=50 \mathrm{ft}\) Let Speed of balloon at \(B\) be \(\mathrm{V}\) \(V=u+\) at \(=0+4 \times 5=20 \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{sec}\) So, speed of ball when it's dropped at point \(B\) is \(v_1=20 \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{sec}\). \(S=v_1 t+\frac{1}{2} a_1 t^2\) \(\Rightarrow-50=20 t+\frac{1}{2}(-32) t^2 \Rightarrow t=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{sec}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(A(2 \sec \theta, 3 \tan \theta)\) and \(B(2 \sec \phi, 3 \tan \phi)\) where \(\theta+\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}\) be two points on the hyperbola \(\frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{9}=1\). If \((\alpha, \beta)\) is the point of intersection of normals to the hyperbola at \(A\) and \(B\), then \(\beta\) is equal to
questions.solution:
Hint : Equation of Normal at \(A\)
\(\frac{4 x}{2 \sec \theta}+\frac{9 y}{3 \tan \theta}=13\)
at \(B\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{4 x}{2 \operatorname{cosec} \theta}+\frac{9 y}{3 \cot \theta}=13 \\
& \Rightarrow(2 \cos \theta) x+(3 \cot \theta) y=13   \quad ----(1)\\
& \&~(2 \sin \theta) x+(3 \tan \theta) y=13 \quad ---(2)\\
& \Rightarrow y=-\frac{13}{3}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{P}(3 \sec \theta, 2 \tan \theta) \mathrm{Q}(3 \sec \phi, 2 \tan \phi)$ be two points on $\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{9}-\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{4}=1$ such that $\theta+\phi=\frac{\pi}{2} .0 < \theta, \phi < \frac{\pi}{2}$. Then the ordinate of the point of intersection of the normals at $P$ and $Q$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{a^2 x}{x_1}+\frac{b^2 y}{y_1}=a^2+b^2, P(3 \sec \theta, 2 \tan \theta) \\ \therefore & \frac{9 x}{3 \sec \theta}+\frac{4 y}{2 \tan \theta}=9+4=13 \\ \Rightarrow & 3 x \cos \theta+2 y \cot \theta=13 \end{aligned}$
Similarly, $3 x \cos \phi+2 y \cot \phi=13$
$3 x \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)+2 y \cot \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)=13$
$\Rightarrow 3 x \sin \theta+2 y \tan \theta=13 \ldots \ldots(2)$
$\therefore \frac{13-2 \mathrm{y} \cot \theta}{3 \cos \theta}=\frac{13-2 \mathrm{y} \tan \theta}{3 \sin \theta}$
$\Rightarrow 13 \sin \theta-2 y \cos \theta=13 \cos \theta-2 y \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow 13 \tan \theta-2 y=13-2 y \tan \theta$
$\Rightarrow 13(\tan \theta-1)=-2 y(\tan \theta-1)$
$\therefore \mathrm{y}=-\frac{13}{2}$
$\therefore$ Ordinate $=-\frac{13}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The normal to a curve at $P(x, y)$ meets the $x$-axis at $G$. If the distance of $G$ from the origin is twice the abscissa of $P$ then the curve is
questions.solution: equation of normal $Y-y=\left(\frac{\frac{-1}{d y}}{d x}\right) \cdot(X-x) \quad Y=0 \quad \therefore \quad y \frac{d y}{d x}+x=X \quad \therefore \quad a / c \text { to } Q \quad y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+x=2 x$ $\mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \int \mathrm{y} \mathrm{dy}=\int \mathrm{xdx} \quad \frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}+\mathrm{c} \quad \frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}=\mathrm{c}$ a hyperbola

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of the centre of the circles which touch both the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ax externally is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Let, centre $\equiv(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ and radius $=\mathrm{r}$ for the variable circle So, using $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}=\mathrm{r}_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2}$ for both cases we have:
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{h}^{2}+\mathrm{k}^{2}=(\mathrm{r}+\mathrm{a})^{2} \rightarrow(1) \text { and }(\mathrm{h}-2 \mathrm{a})^{2}+\mathrm{k}^{2}=(\mathrm{r}+2 \mathrm{a})^{2} \rightarrow(2) \\
\text { Eq. }(2)-\text { Eq. }(1), \text { gives }: \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{a}-4 \mathrm{~h}}{2} \rightarrow(3)
\end{array}$
Substitute $(3)$ in $(1)$ to get:
$12 h^{2}-4 k^{2}-24 a h+9 a^{2}=0$
$\therefore$ locus $: 12 x^{2}-4 y^{2}-24 a x+9 a^{2}=0$ i.e. a hyperbola

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A double ordinate $\mathrm{PQ}$ of the hyperbola $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=1$ is such that $\Delta \mathrm{OPQ}$ is equilateral, $\mathrm{O}$ being the centre of the hyperbola. Then the eccentricity e satisfies the relation
questions.solution: Hint: $P \equiv(a \sec \theta, b \tan \theta) \angle \mathrm{POM}=\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}} \sin \theta$ $\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{a}{b \sqrt{3}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}>\frac{1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow 1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}>\frac{4}{3} \quad \Rightarrow e>\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $P(4,3)$ be a point on the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=\mathrm{1}$. If the normal at $P$ intersects the $X$ -axis at (16,0) , then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is
questions.solution:
Given hyperbola equation
$H: \frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$
$P(4,3) $ lie on $H$
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{16}{a^{2}}-\frac{9}{b^{2}}=1$
Normal equation at $P(4,3)$ for $H$
$$
a^{2} y_{1}\left(x-x_{1}\right)+b^{2} x_{1}\left(y-y_{1}\right)=0
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 a^{2}(x-4)+4 b^{2}(y-3)=0$
Normal cuts the $X$ -axis at (16,0) $\Rightarrow \quad 3 a^{2}(16-4)+4 b^{2}(0-3)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 a^{2} 12+4 b^{2}(-3)=0$
$\Rightarrow$ $36 a^{2}-12 b^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow$ $3 a^{2}-b^{2}=0$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $3 a^{2}=b^{2}$
$\therefore$ Eccentricity of the hyperbola is $e=\frac{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}{a}$
$=\frac{\sqrt{a^{2}+3 a^{2}}}{a}=\frac{\sqrt{4 a^{2}}}{a}=\frac{2 a}{a}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{\cos ^{2} \alpha}-\frac{y^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \alpha}=1,$ which of the following remains fixed when $\alpha$ varies?
questions.solution:
Given, equation of hyperbola is
$$
\frac{x^{2}}{\cos ^{2} \alpha}-\frac{y^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \alpha}=1
$$
Here, $a^{2}=\cos ^{2} \alpha$ and $b^{2}=\sin ^{2} \alpha$
[i.e. comparing with standard
$$
\text { equation } \left.\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1\right]
$$
We know that, foci $=(\pm a e, 0)$ where, $a e=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}=\sqrt{\cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha}=1$
$\Rightarrow$ foci $(\pm 1,0),$ where vertices are $(\pm \cos \alpha, 0)$ Eccentricity, $a e=1$ or $e=\frac{1}{\cos \alpha}$
Hence, foci remains constant with change in $\alpha$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the directrices of the hyperbola $3 x^{2}-3 y^{2}-18 x+12 y+2=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation is
$$
3 x^{2}-3 y^{2}-18 x+12 y+2=0
$$
It can be written as
$\frac{(x-3)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{13}{3}}\right)^{2}}-\frac{(y-2)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{13}{3}}\right)^{2}}=1$
$\begin{aligned} \text { here, } a &=b=\sqrt{\frac{13}{3}} \\ \therefore \quad e &=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}} \\ &=\sqrt{1+1} \\ &=\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Equation of the directrix are
$$
x=3 \pm \sqrt{\frac{13}{6}}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The length of conjugate axis of a hyperbola is greater than the length of transverse axis. Then, the eccentricity e is
questions.solution:
Let the length of conjugate axis $=b$
and the length of transvers axis $=a$ Given that, $b>a$
Now,$b^{2}>a^{2}$
$\therefore$ Eccentricity $(e)=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}$
$e^{2}=\left(\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}\right)^{2}>2$
$e>\sqrt{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Equation of a tangent to the hyperbola $5 x^{2}-y^{2}=5$ and which passes through an external point (2,8) is
questions.solution:
Given equation of hyperbola can be write as
$\frac{x^{2}}{1}-\frac{y^{2}}{(\sqrt{5})^{2}}=1$
here, $a=1$ and $b=\sqrt{5}$
Since, $y=m x \pm \sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}-b^{2}}$
$y=m x \pm \sqrt{m^{2}-5}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(8-2 m)^{2}=\left(\sqrt{m^{2}-5}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(8-2 m)^{2}=m^{2}-5$
$\Rightarrow 64+4 m^{2}-32 m=m^{2}-5$
$\Rightarrow 3 m^{2}-32 m+69=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad m=\frac{22}{3}, 3 \quad$ [by solving equation $]$
When $m=\frac{23}{3},$ then equation of tangent
$$
(y-8)=\frac{23}{3}(x-2)
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 y-24=23 x-46$
$\Rightarrow \quad 23 x-3 y-46+24=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 23 x-3 y-22=0$
When $m=3$, then equation of tangent
$$
\begin{aligned}
& &(y-8) &=3(x-2) \\
\Rightarrow & & y-8 &=3 x-6
\end{aligned}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x-y-6+8=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x-y+2=0$
Hence, option (a) and (c) are correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let the eccentricity of the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ be reciprocal to that of the ellipse $x^{2}+9 y^{2}=9,$ then the ratio $a^{2}: b^{2}$ equals
questions.solution:
Given equation of ellipses is $x^{2}+9 y^{2}=9$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{1}=1$
Here, $a=3, b=1$
$c=\sqrt{(3)^{2}-(1)^{2}}=\sqrt{8}$
$\therefore$ Eccentricity of ellipse, $e=\frac{c}{a}$ $e=\frac{\sqrt{8}}{3}$
$\therefore$ Eccentricity of hyperbola $=\frac{3}{\sqrt{8}}$
$\Rightarrow$
$1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=\frac{9}{8}$
$\Rightarrow$
$\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=\frac{1}{8}$
$a^{2}: b^{2}=8: 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A hyperbola, having the transverse axis of length $2 \sin \theta$ is confocal with the ellipse $3 x^{2}+4 y^{2}=12$. It equation is
questions.solution:
Given. $2 a_{1}=2 \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad a_{1}=\sin \theta$
and $3 x^{2}+4 y^{2}=12$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1$
Here, $a^{2}=4$ and $b^{2}=3$
$\therefore$
$b^{2}=a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$
$3=4\left(1-e^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$
$e^{2}=1-\frac{3}{4}=\frac{1}{4}$
$e=\frac{1}{2}$
Focus, $F(a e, 0)=F\left(2 \times \frac{1}{2}, 0\right)$
$=F(1,0)$
For hyperbola foci are same
$a_{1} e_{1}=a e=1$
$\therefore$
$(\sin \theta) e_{1}=1$
$\Rightarrow$
$e_{1}=\operatorname{cosec} \theta$
and $b_{1}^{2}=a_{1}^{2}\left(e_{1}^{2}-1\right)=a_{1}^{2} e_{1}^{2}-a_{1}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad b_{1}^{2}=1-\sin ^{2} \theta=\cos ^{2} \theta$
$\frac{x^{2}}{a_{1}^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b_{1}^{2}}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \theta}-\frac{y^{2}}{\cos ^{2} \theta}=1$
$\Rightarrow x^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta-y^{2} \sec ^{2} \theta=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $P$ be the foot of the perpendicular from focus $S$ of hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ on the line $b x-a y=0$ and let $C$ be the centre of the hyperbola. Then, the area of the rectangle whose sides are equal to that of $S P$ and $C P$ is
questions.solution: Given, equation of hyperbola is $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ From figure, $\begin{aligned} S P &=\mid \frac{a b e}{\sqrt{b^{2}+a^{2}}} \\ &=\left|\frac{a b e}{a e}\right|=b \end{aligned}$ and $C S=a e$ Again, $\Delta S P C$ is right angled triangle at $P$. $$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad C P &=\sqrt{C S^{2}-S P^{2}} \\ &=\sqrt{a^{2} e^{2}-b^{2}} \\ &=\sqrt{a^{2}\left(1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)-b^{2}} \\ &=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}-b^{2}}=a \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ Area of rectangle $=C P \times S P$ $=a b$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line segment joining the foci of the hyperbola $x^{2}-y^{2}+1=0$ is one of the diameters of a circle. The equation of the circle is
questions.solution:
Given, equation of hyperbola is
$$
x^{2}-y^{2}+1=0
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad y^{2}-x^{2}=1$
On comparing it with $\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}-\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}=1,$ we get
$$
a=b=1
$$
Now, $e=\sqrt{1+\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}}=\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{1}}=\sqrt{2}$
$\therefore$ Foci $=(0, \pm b e)=(0, \pm \sqrt{2})$
since, line joining foci of hyperbola is diameter of circle.
$\therefore$ Centre of circle $=\left(\frac{0+0}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)=(0,0)$
$\begin{aligned} \text { and radius } &=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(0-0)^{2}+(\sqrt{2}-(-\sqrt{2}))^{2}} \\ &=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}}=\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Equation of circle will be
$$
(x-0)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}=(\sqrt{2})^{2}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+y^{2}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $A(-1,0)$ and $B(2,0)$ be two points. $A$ point $M$ moves in the plane in such a way that $\angle M B A=2 \angle M A B .$ Then, the point $M$ moves along
questions.solution: $$ \begin{array}{l} \text { Let } \angle M A B=\theta \text { , then } \angle M B A=2 \theta \\ \text { tan } \theta=\frac{k}{1+h} \text { and } \tan 2 \theta=\frac{k}{2-h} \\ \text { then } \tan 2 \theta=\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}=\frac{k}{2-h} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2(k / 1+h)}{1-(k / 1+h)^{2}}=\frac{k}{2-h} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{1+h} \times \frac{(1+h)^{2}}{(1+h)^{2}-k^{2}}=\frac{1}{2-h} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2(1+h)}{(1+h)^{2}-k^{2}}=\frac{1}{2-h} \\ \Rightarrow \quad 1+h^{2}+2 h-k^{2}=2(1+h)(2-h) \\ \Rightarrow \quad 1+h^{2}+2 h-k^{2}=2\left(2-h+2 h-h^{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad 1+h^{2}+2 h-k^{2}=2\left(2+h-h^{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad 1+h^{2}+2 h-k^{2}=4+2 h-2 h^{2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad 1+3 h^{2}-k^{2}=4 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 3 h^{2}-k^{2}=3 \end{array} $$ which represents hyperbola (d).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $P Q$ is a double ordinate of the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ such that $\Delta O P Q$ is equilateral. $O$ being the centre. Then, the eccentricity $e$ satisfies
questions.solution: Given equation of hyperbola is $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ and $\Delta O P Q$ is an equlateral triangle. $P Q$ is double ordinate of the hyperbola. Let the coordinates of $P$ and $Q$ be $(a \sec \theta, b$ tan $\theta)$ and $(a \sec \theta,-b$ tane $),$ respectively. In$\Delta O P Q$, $O P=P Q$ $\Rightarrow \quad a^{2} \sec ^{2} \theta+b^{2} \tan ^{2} \theta=(2 b \tan \theta)^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad a^{2} \sec ^{2} \theta=3 b^{2} \tan ^{2} \theta$ $\Rightarrow \quad \sin ^{2} \theta=\frac{a^{2}}{3 b^{2}}$ Now, $\sin ^{2} \theta < 1$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a^{2}}{3 b^{2}} < 1$ $\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}>\frac{1}{3}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}>\frac{4}{3}$ $\therefore$ $e^{2}>\frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow e>\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $16 x^{2}-3 y^{2}-32 x-12 y=44$ represents a hyperbola. Then,
options: length of the transverse axis is $2 \sqrt{3}$
questions.solution:
Given equation of hyperbola is $16 x^{2}-3 y^{2}-32 x-12 y=44$
$\Rightarrow 16 x^{2}+16-32 x-3 y^{2}-12-12 y$
$=44+4$
$\Rightarrow 16(x-1)^{2}-3(y+2)^{2}=48$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{(x-1)^{2}}{3}-\frac{(y+2)^{2}}{16}=1$
On comparing the equation with standard equation of hyperbola, we get $a=\sqrt{3}, b=4$
Now, length of transverse axis $=2 a=2 \sqrt{3}$
and length of latusrectum $=\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}=\frac{2 \times 16}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{32}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\therefore$ Eccentricity $(e)=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}$
$=\sqrt{1+\frac{16}{3}}=\sqrt{\frac{19}{3}}$
Equation of directrix is $x=\pm \frac{a}{e}$ $\Rightarrow x-1=\pm \frac{\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{19}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=1 \pm \frac{3}{\sqrt{19}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the straight line $(a-1) x-b y+4=0$ is normal to the hyperbola $x y=1$, then which of the following does not hold?
questions.solution:
Given equation of straight line is
$$
(a-1) x-b y+4=0
$$
Slope $=-\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } y}$ $=\frac{-(a-1)}{-b}=\frac{a-1}{b}$
Equation of hyperbola is $x y=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad y=\frac{1}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x^{2}}$
Slope of normal $=x^{2}>0$
$\therefore \quad \frac{a-1}{b}>0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a-1>0 \quad$ and $\quad b>0$
or $\quad a-1    < 0$ and $b < 0$ $\Rightarrow \quad a>1, b>0$ or $a < 1, b < 0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the common tangent with positive slope to the parabola $y^{2}=8 \sqrt{3} x$ and the hyperbola $4 x^{2}-y^{2}=4$ is
questions.solution:
Equation of tangent in slope form of parabola $y^{2}=8 \sqrt{3} x$ is
$$
y=m x+c
$$
where, $\quad c=\frac{a}{m}$
$\therefore$
$c=\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{m}$
Also, tangent to the hyperbola
$$
4 x^{2}-y^{2}=4
$$
or $\frac{x^{2}}{1}-\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$ is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
c^{2}=a^{2} m^{2}-b^{2} \\
c^{2}=1 m^{2}-4
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{m}\right)^{2}=m^{2}-4$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{12}{m^{2}}=m^{2}-4$
$\Rightarrow \quad m^{4}-4 m^{2}-12=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad m^{4}-6 m^{2}+2 m^{2}-12=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad m^{2}\left(m^{2}-6\right)+2\left(m^{2}-6\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(m^{2}+2\right)\left(m^{2}-6\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad m^{2}-6=0$ and $m^{2}+2 \neq 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad m^{2}=6$
$\Rightarrow \quad m=\pm \sqrt{6}$
i.e $m=\sqrt{6}$ as $m$ is positive slope.
$\therefore$ 
$y=\sqrt{6} x+\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{6}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad y=\sqrt{6} x+\sqrt{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of hyperbola whose coordinates of the foci are (±8,0) and the length of latusrectum is 24 units, is
questions.solution:
Let equation of hyperbola be
$$
\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1
$$
Given, foci, $(\pm 8,0)=(\pm a e, 0)$
$\Rightarrow \quad a e=8$
and length of latusrectum $=\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}$
$\therefore$
$24=\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}$
$\Rightarrow \quad b^{2}=12 a$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (ii).
$$
a^{2} e^{2}=64
$$
$\Rightarrow$
$a^{2}\left(\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)=64$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}+b^{2}=64$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}+12 a=64$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}+12 a-64=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}+16 a-4 a-64=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a(a+16)-4(a+16)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(a+16)(a-4)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=4$
[$\because$ a cannot be negative] On putting $a=4$ in Eq. (iii), we get
$$
b^{2}=12 \times 4 \Rightarrow b^{2}=48
$$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (i). $\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{48}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x^{2}-y^{2}=48$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For the variable $t,$ the locus of the points of intersection of lines $x-2 y=t$ and $x+2 y=\frac{1}{t}$ is
options: the circle with centre at the origin and radius 1
the ellipse with centre at the origin and one focus $\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}, 0\right)$
the hyperbola with centre at the origin and one focus $\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}, 0\right)$
questions.solution:
Given equation of lines are
$$
x-2 y=t
$$
and $\quad x+2 y=\frac{1}{t}$
On multiplying Eqs. (i) and (ii)  we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
(x-2 y)(x+2 y)=t \times \frac{1}{t} \\
\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}-4 y^{2}=1
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{(x-0)^{2}}{1}-\frac{(y-0)^{2}}{(1 / 4)}=1$
which represent a hyperbola. 
Here, $a^{2}=1$ and $b^{2}=\frac{1}{4}$
$\therefore$ Eccentricity $(e)=\sqrt{\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1+\frac{1}{4}}{1}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$
and focus $=(\pm a e, 0)=\left(\pm 1 \times \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}, 0\right)=\left(\pm \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}, 0\right)$
and centre $=(0,0)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\displaystyle\int \dfrac{\left(\sqrt[3]{x + \sqrt{2 - x^2}}\right)\left(\sqrt[6]{1 - x\sqrt{2 - x^2}}\right)}{\sqrt[3]{1 - x^2}}\,dx; \, (x \in (0, 1)) =$
questions.solution:
Let the given integral be $I = \int \dfrac{(x + \sqrt{2 - x^2})^{1/3} (1 - x\sqrt{2 - x^2})^{1/6}}{(1 - x^2)^{1/3}} \, dx$.

Consider the first term in the numerator. We can rewrite it by squaring the expression inside and changing the exponent:

$(x + \sqrt{2 - x^2})^{1/3} = \left((x + \sqrt{2 - x^2})^2\right)^{1/6}$

Expanding the square, we get:

$(x + \sqrt{2 - x^2})^2 = x^2 + (2 - x^2) + 2x\sqrt{2 - x^2} = 2 + 2x\sqrt{2 - x^2} = 2(1 + x\sqrt{2 - x^2})$

Substituting this back, the first term becomes:

$(x + \sqrt{2 - x^2})^{1/3} = 2^{1/6} (1 + x\sqrt{2 - x^2})^{1/6}$

Now, multiply this with the second term in the numerator:

Numerator $= 2^{1/6} (1 + x\sqrt{2 - x^2})^{1/6} (1 - x\sqrt{2 - x^2})^{1/6}$

Using the identity $(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2$, we get:

Numerator $= 2^{1/6} \left(1 - x^2(2 - x^2)\right)^{1/6}$

Numerator $= 2^{1/6} (1 - 2x^2 + x^4)^{1/6}$

Numerator $= 2^{1/6} \left((1 - x^2)^2\right)^{1/6}$

Since $x \in (0, 1)$, $1 - x^2 > 0$, so this simplifies to:

Numerator $= 2^{1/6} (1 - x^2)^{1/3}$

Substituting the simplified numerator back into the integral:

$I = \int \dfrac{2^{1/6} (1 - x^2)^{1/3}}{(1 - x^2)^{1/3}} \, dx$

$I = \int 2^{1/6} \, dx$

$I = 2^{1/6}x + c$

Answer: $2^{\dfrac{1}{6}}x + c$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\displaystyle\int \dfrac{(1 - x^2)}{\sqrt{x}\sqrt{(1 + x^2)^3}}\,dx = \alpha\dfrac{x^{\beta}}{(1 + x^2)^{\gamma}} + C; \, \alpha, \beta, \gamma \in \mathbb{R}$ and $C$ is constant of integration, then $\alpha : \beta : \gamma$ will be
questions.solution:
Let $I = \displaystyle\int \dfrac{1 - x^2}{\sqrt{x}(1 + x^2)^{3/2}} \,dx$

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by $x^2$, we get:

$I = \displaystyle\int \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{x^2} - 1}{\dfrac{\sqrt{x}(1 + x^2)^{3/2}}{x^2}} \,dx$

$I = \displaystyle\int \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{x^2} - 1}{\left(\dfrac{1 + x^2}{x}\right)^{3/2}} \,dx = \displaystyle\int \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{x^2} - 1}{\left(x + \dfrac{1}{x}\right)^{3/2}} \,dx$

Let $x + \dfrac{1}{x} = t$. Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$:

$\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{x^2}\right) \,dx = dt \Rightarrow \left(\dfrac{1}{x^2} - 1\right) \,dx = -dt$

Substituting this into the integral:

$I = \displaystyle\int \dfrac{-dt}{t^{3/2}} = -\displaystyle\int t^{-3/2} \,dt$

$I = -\left(\dfrac{t^{-1/2}}{-1/2}\right) + C = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{t}} + C$

Substituting back $t = \dfrac{x^2 + 1}{x}$:

$I = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{x^2 + 1}{x}}} + C = 2\dfrac{x^{1/2}}{(1 + x^2)^{1/2}} + C$

Comparing this with the given expression $\alpha\dfrac{x^{\beta}}{(1 + x^2)^{\gamma}} + C$, we get:

$\alpha = 2$, $\beta = \dfrac{1}{2}$, $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Therefore, the ratio is:

$\alpha : \beta : \gamma = 2 : \dfrac{1}{2} : \dfrac{1}{2} = 4 : 1 : 1$

Answer: $4 : 1 : 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\int \frac{\log _e\left(x+\sqrt{\left.1+x^2\right)}\right.}{\sqrt{1+x^2}} d x=f(g(x))+c$ then
questions.solution:
Hint $:$ Let $\log \left(x+\sqrt{1+x^2}\right)=t \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}+x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}} d x=d t}{x+\sqrt{1+x^2}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}}=\mathrm{dt} \quad \therefore \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}\right.}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}} \mathrm{dx}=\int \mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{dt} \\
& \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}+\mathrm{c}=\frac{\left[\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^2+1}\right]^2\right.}{2}+\mathrm{c} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{2}, \mathrm{~g}(\mathrm{x})=\log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}\right)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& I=\int_0^\pi e^{\cos ^2 x} \cos ^3(2 n+1) x d x, n \in Z \ldots(1) \\
& =\int_0^\pi e^{\cos ^2(\pi-x)} \cos ^3[(2 n+1)(\pi-x)] d x
\end{aligned}\)
Using \(\int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore I=\int_0^\pi e^{\cos ^2 x} \cos ^3[(2 n+1) \pi-(2 n+1) x] d x \\
& I=\int_0^\pi-e^{\cos ^2 x} \cos ^3(2 n+1) x d x \ldots \text { (2) }
\end{aligned}\)
Adding (1) and (2) we get
\(21=0 \Rightarrow 1=0\)

then,
options: I(R) and $\frac{\pi}{2 R}\left(1-e^{-R}\right)$ are not comparable

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(I=\int \frac{x^2 d x}{(x \sin x+\cos x)^2}=f(x)+\tan x+c\), then \(f(x)\) is
questions.solution:
Hint : \(I=\int \frac{x^2}{(x \sin x+\cos x)^2} d x=\int \frac{x}{(x \sin x+\cos x)^2} \times \frac{x}{\cos x} d x\)
\(I=-\frac{1}{(x \sin x+\cos x)} \cdot \frac{x}{\cos x}+\int \frac{1}{(x \sin x+\cos x)} \cdot \frac{(\cos x+x \sin x)}{\cos ^2 x} d x\)
\(\begin{aligned} & I=-\frac{1}{(x \sin x+\cos x)} \cdot \frac{x}{\cos x}+\int \sec ^2 x d x \\ & I=-\frac{x}{(x \sin x+\cos x) \cos x}+\tan x+C \\ & \therefore f(x)=\frac{-x}{\cos x(x \sin x+C)}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\int \frac{d x}{(x+1)(x-2)(x-3)}=\frac{1}{k} \log _e\left\{\frac{|x-3|^3|x+1|}{(x-2)^4}\right\}+c\), then the value of \(k\) is
questions.solution:
Hint: \(\int \frac{d x}{(x+1)(x-2)(x-3)}=\int\left(\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{(x-2)}+\frac{C}{x-3}\right) d x\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{(x+1)(x-2)(x-3)}=\frac{A(x-2)(x-3)+B(x+1)(x-3)+C(x+1)(x-2)}{(x+1)(x-2)(x-3)} \\
& \Rightarrow 1=A(x-2)(x-3)+B(x+1)(x-3)+C(x+1)(x-2) \\
& \text { for } x=-1 \\
& 1=A(-3)(-4) \Rightarrow A=\frac{1}{12} \\
& \text { for } x=2 \\
& 1=B(3)(-1) \Rightarrow B=-\frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}\)
\(\text { for } x=3\)
\(\begin{aligned} & 1=C(4)(1) \Rightarrow C=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \therefore I=\frac{1}{12} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{x-2} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{1}{x-3} d x \\ & =\frac{1}{12} \ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{3} \ln |x-2|+\frac{1}{4} \ln |x-3|+C \\ & =\frac{1}{12}\left[\ln |x+1|-\ln |x-2|^4+\ln |x-3|^3\right]+C \\ & =\frac{1}{12} \ln \frac{(|x+1|)(|x-3|)^3}{(x-2)^4}-C \\ & \therefore k=12\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of \(\int_0^{1 / 2} \frac{d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2 n}}}\) is \((n \in \mathbb{N})\)
questions.solution:
Hint : For 0 to \(\frac{1}{2}\)
\(\begin{aligned} & x^2 \geq x^{2 n} \\ & \Rightarrow-x^2 \leq-x^{2 n} \\ & \Rightarrow 1-x^2 \leq 1-x^{2 n} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2 n}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \int_0^{1 / 2} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x \geq \int_0^{1 / 2} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2 n}}} d x \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\sin ^{-1} x\right]_0^{1 / 2} \geq \mathrm{I} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{6} \geq I \\ & \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(I_n=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos ^n x \cos n x d x\), then \(I_1, I_2, I_3 \ldots\) are in
questions.solution:
Hint : \(I_n=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^n x \cdot \cos n x d x\)
\(I_1=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos x \cdot \cos x d x=\int_0^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{1+\cos 2 x}{2}\right) d x=\frac{\pi}{4}\)
\(I_2=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^2 x \cdot \cos 2 x d x=\int_0^{\pi / 2}-\sin ^2 x \cdot \cos 2 x d x\)
\(2 I_2=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^2 2 x=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{1+\cos 4 x}{2} d x=\frac{\pi}{4}\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{I}_2=\frac{\pi}{8}\)
\(I_3=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^3 x \cdot \cos 3 x d x=\int_0^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{3 \cos x+\cos 3 x}{4}\right) \cos 3 x d x\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{3}{4} \int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos x \cdot \cos 3 x d x+\frac{1}{4} \int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^2 3 x d x \\
& =\frac{3}{8} \int_0^{\pi / 2}(\cos 2 x+\cos 4 x) d x+\frac{1}{4} \int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{1+\cos 6 x}{2} d x \\
& =\frac{\pi}{16}
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{I}_1, \mathrm{I}_2, \mathrm{I}_3\) are in G.P.

questions.solution:
$\int \cos (\ln (x)) d x=x \cdot \cos (\ln x)-\int(-\sin (\ln x)) \times 1 / x(x) d x$
$=x \cos (\ln x)+\int \sin (\ln x) d x$
