questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: On $R$, a relation $\rho$ is defined by xpy if and only if $x-y$ is zero or irrational. Then.
options: $\rho$ is reflexive but neither symmetric nor transitive
$\rho$ is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
$\rho$ is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
questions.solution:
On the set $R$ $x p y \Rightarrow x-y$ is zero or irrational number.
Now, $x p x$
$\Rightarrow x-x=0$
$\Rightarrow \rho$ is reflexive.
If $x \rho y \Rightarrow x-y$ is zero or irrational.
$=-(y-x)$ is zero or irrational.
$\Rightarrow$ yox is zero or irrational.
$\Rightarrow \rho$ is symmetric.
And if $x \rho y \Rightarrow x-y$ is 0 or irrational.
$y \rho z \Rightarrow y-z$ is 0 or irrational.
Then, $(x-y)+(y-z)=x-z$ may be 0 or rational.
$\Rightarrow \rho$ is not transitive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: On the set $R$ of real numbers, the relation $\rho$ is defined by $x \rho y,(x, y) \in R$
options: If $|x-y|    < 2$, then $\rho$ is reflexive but neither symmetric nor transitive.
II $x-y    < 2$, then $\rho$ is reflexeve and symmetric bu not transitive.
$If(x \mid \geq y,$ then $\rho$ is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric.
If $x>|y|$, then $p$ is transitive but neither reflexve nor symmetric.
questions.solution:
On the set $R$ of real numbers
For reflexive,
$x \rho x \Rightarrow(x, x) \in R$
$\Rightarrow x > |x|$ which is not true. $\Rightarrow \rho$ is not reflexive.
For symmetric,
$(x, y) \in R \Rightarrow x > |y|$
and $(y, x) \in R \Rightarrow y > |x|$
So, $x > |y| \neq y > |x|$ $\Rightarrow \rho$ is not symmetric.
For transitive, $(x, y) \in R \Rightarrow x > |y|(y, z) \in R \Rightarrow y > |z|$
$\Rightarrow x > |z| \Rightarrow(x, z) \in R$
$\Rightarrow \rho$ is transitive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\rho$ be a relation defined on $N$, the set of natural numbers, as $\rho=\{(x, y) \in N \times N: 2 x+y=41\} .$ Then
questions.solution:
We have, $\rho=\{(x, y) \in N \times N: 2 x+y=41\}$
For reflexive,
$\begin{array}{l}
x p x \Rightarrow 2 x+x=41 \\
\Rightarrow 3 x=41
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow x=\frac{41}{3} \notin N$
So, $\rho$ is not reflexive.
For symmetric,
$x \rho y \Rightarrow 2 x+y=41$
and $y \rho x \Rightarrow 2 y+x=41$
$\Rightarrow x p y \neq y \rho x$
So, $\rho$ is not symmetric.
For transitive,
$x p y \Rightarrow 2 x+y=41$
and $y \rho z \Rightarrow 2 y+z=41$
$\Rightarrow x p z$
$\Rightarrow \rho$ is not transitive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: On the set $R$ of real numbers we define $x P y$ if and only if $x y \geq 0$. Then, the relation $P$ is
options: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
questions.solution:
For every real number $x, x^{2} \geq 0$
$\therefore$ $(x, x) \in P$
Hence, $P$ is reflexive.
Now let $(x,y) \in P \\
\Rightarrow xy \geq 0 \\
\Rightarrow yx \geq 0$
$(y, x) \in P$
Hence, $P$ is symmetric. 
Again (-1,0)$\in P$ and (0,2)$\in P$
But (-1,2)$\notin P$
$as(-1)(2)=-2    < 0$
Pis not transitive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: On $R$, the relation $\rho$ be defined by 'xpy holds if and only if $x-y$ is zero or irrational'. Then,
options: $\rho$ is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
$\rho$ is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
$\rho$ is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
questions.solution:
We have, $x p y \Rightarrow x-y$ is zero or irrational. Now, $x-x=0$
$\Rightarrow(x, x) \in \rho$
$\therefore \rho$ is reflexive. 
Again, if $x-y$ is either zero or irrational, then $y-x$ will also be either zero or inational.
$(x, y) \in \rho$
$\Leftrightarrow \quad(y, x) \in \rho$
$\therefore \rho$ is symmetric. 
Again, $(2, \sqrt{3}) \in \rho$ and $(\sqrt{3}, 4) \in \rho$
$But (2,4) \notin \rho$
$\therefore p$ is not transitive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
options: $R \cup S$ is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
$R \cup S$ is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
$R \cup S$ is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
questions.solution:
We have, $R=\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,1)\}$
$S=\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,3),(3,1)\}$
$\therefore R \cup S=\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,1),(1,3),(3,1)\}$
Since, $(2,1) \in R \cup S,(1,3) \in R \cup S$
but $(2,3) \in R \cup S$
$\therefore \mathrm{R} \cup \mathrm{S}$ is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: On $R$, the set of real numbers, a relation $\rho$ is defined as $a \rho b$ if and only if $1+a b>0$. Then,
options: $\rho$ is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
$\rho$ is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
questions.solution:
We know that, 
$1+a^{2}>0, a \in R$
$\Rightarrow \quad(a, a) \in \rho$
$\therefore \rho$ is reflexive
Again, $\quad$ let $(a, b) \in \rho$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1+a b>0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1+b a>0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(b, a) \in \rho$
$\therefore \rho$ is symmetric Now, $\quad(1,-0 \cdot 1) \in \rho$
and $(-0 \cdot 1,-9) \in \rho$
but $\quad(1,-9) \notin \rho$
$\therefore \rho$ is not transitive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $R$ be a relation defined on the set $Z$ of all integers and $x R y,$ when $x+2 y$ is divisible by 3 , then
questions.solution:
Reflexivity For reflexive $(x, x) \in R .$
$x+2 x=3 x$
which is divisible by 3.
$\Rightarrow$ xRx $\in R$ Hence, xRy is reflexive.
Symmetric Let $x+2 y=3 \lambda$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=3 \lambda-2 y$
$\begin{aligned}
\text {Now, } & \begin{aligned}
y+2 x &=y+2(3 \lambda-2 y) \\
&=y+6 \lambda-4 y \\
&=6 \lambda-3 y
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad y+2 x=3(2 \lambda-y)$
which is divisible by 3.
$\therefore$ $(x, y) \in R \Rightarrow(y, x) \in R$
Hence, xRy is symmetric.
$\begin{array}{ll}\text {Transifive } & x+2 y=3 \lambda \\ \text { and } & y+2 z=3 \mu\end{array}$
$x+3 y+2 z=3 x+3 \mu$
$\Rightarrow \quad x+2 z=3(\lambda+\mu-y)$
which is divisible by $3 .$
$\because (x, y) \in$ R and $(y, z) \in R$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x, z) \in R$
Hence, xRy is transitive
$\because$ R is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
$\therefore$ R is an equivalence relation.

If $A=\left\{5^{n}-4 n-1: n \in N\right\}$ and
$B=\{16(n-1): n \in N],$ then
questions.solution:
We have,
$\begin{aligned} A &=5^{n}-4 n-1=(1+4)^{n}-4 n-1 \\ &=\left({ }^{n} C_{0}+{ }^{n} C_{1} \times 4+{ }^{n} C_{2} \times 4^{2}+\ldots+{ }^{n} C_{n} \times 4^{n}\right) \\ &=4^{2}\left({ }^{n} C_{2}+{ }^{n} C_{3} \times 4+\ldots+{ }^{n} C_{n} \times 4^{n-2}\right) \end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ A contains some multiples of 16 
Clearly, $B$ contains all multiples of 16 including $0 .$
$A \subseteq B$

questions.solution:
We have, $a+b+c=21$ and $\frac{a+c}{2}=b$ $\Rightarrow \quad a+c+\frac{a+c}{2}=21$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & a+c=14 \Rightarrow \frac{a+c}{2}=7 \\ \Rightarrow & b=7\end{array}$
So, a can take values from 1 to 6 . C can have values from 8 to 13
or a=b=c=7
So, there are 7 such triplets.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A relation $\rho$ on the set of real number $R$ is defined as $\{x \rho y: x y>0\} .$ Then, which of the following is/are true?
questions.solution:
We have, $x p y: x y > 0$ (i) Reflexive Suppose $x \rho x \in R$ $\Rightarrow x^{2} > 0$ which is not true when $x=0$. Hence, relation is not reflexive. (ii) Symmetric $x p y \in R$ $\Rightarrow \quad x y>0$ $\Rightarrow \quad y x>0$ $\Rightarrow \quad y \rho x \in R$ If $(x, y) \in R,$ then, relation is symmetric. (iii) Transitive $\mathrm{xp} \mathrm{y} \in \mathrm{R}$ $x y>0$ and $\quad y \rho z \in R$ $y z>0$ $\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow x y^{2} z > 0 \\
\Rightarrow x z > 0 \\
\Rightarrow (x, z) \in R \\
\text {If }(x, y) \in R, \text { then }(y, z) \in R \\
\Rightarrow (x, z) \in R
\end{array}$ Hence, relation is transitive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: There is a group of 265 persons who like either singing or dancing or painting. In this group 200 like singing, 110 like dancing and 55 like painting. If 60 persons like both singing and dancing, 30 like both singing and painting and 10 hike all three activities, then the number of persons who like only dancing and painting is
questions.solution:
Let $D$ denotes dancing. $P$ denotes painting and $S$ denotes singing.
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \quad n(D \cup P \cup S)=265 \\
n(S)=200, n(D)=110, n(P)=55 \\
n(S \cap D)=60, n(S \cap P)=30 \\
\text { and } n(D \cap P \cap S)=10 \\
\because n(D \cup P \cup S)=n(D)+n(P)+n(S)-n(D \cap P) \\
-n(P \cap S)-n(S \cap D)+n(D \cap P \cap S) \\
\therefore \quad 265=110+55+200-n(D \cap P) \\
\Rightarrow \quad 265=285-n(D \cap P) \\
\Rightarrow \quad n(D \cap P)=20
\end{array}$
$\therefore$ Only person who like dancing and painting
$\begin{array}{l}
=n(D \cap P)-n(D \cap P \cap S) \\
=20-10=10
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $X_{n}=\left\{z=x+i y:|z|^{2} \leq \frac{1}{n}\right\}$ for all integers $n \geq 1 .$ Then, $\underset{n=1}{\cap} X_{n}$ is
options: a finite set with more than one element
questions.solution:
Given, $X_{n}=\left\{z=x+i y:|z|^{2} \leq \frac{1}{n}\right\}$
$=\left\{x^{2}+y^{2} \leq \frac{1}{n}\right\}$
$\therefore$
$X_{1}=\left\{x^{2}+y^{2} \leq 1\right\}$
$\begin{array}{c}
X_{2}=\left\{x^{2}+y^{2} \leq \frac{1}{2}\right\} \\
X_{3}=\left\{x^{2}+y^{2} \leq \frac{1}{3}\right\} \\
\ldots \\
\qquad \begin{aligned}
X_{\infty} &=\left\{x^{2}+y^2 \leq 0\right\} \\
\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} x_n &=X_{1} \cap X_{2} \cap X_{3} \cap \ldots \cap X_{\infty} \\
&=\left\{x^{2}+y^{2}=0\right\}
\end{aligned}
\end{array}$
Hence, $\underset{n=1}{\cap} X_{n}$ is a singleton set.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For any two real numbers $\theta$ and $\phi$ we define $\theta$. R $\phi$ if and only if $\sec ^{2} \theta-\tan ^{2} \phi=1$. The relation $R$ is
options: both reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
questions.solution:
Given relation is defined as $\theta R \phi$ such that $\sec ^{2} \theta-\tan ^{2} \phi=1$
For Reflexive
When $\theta R \theta$
$\sec ^{2} \theta-\tan ^{2} \theta=1$
$\Rightarrow 1=1,$ which is true.
Thus, it is reflexive.
For Symmetric When $\theta R \phi$
$\begin{array}{l}
\sec ^{2} \theta-\tan ^{2} \phi=1 \\
\Rightarrow\left(1+\tan ^{2} \theta\right)-\left(\sec ^{2} \phi-1\right)=1 \\
\Rightarrow 2+\tan ^{2} \theta-\sec ^{2} \phi=1 \\
\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} \phi-\tan ^{2} \theta=1
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \phi F \theta$
Thus, it is symmetric.
For Transitive
When $\theta R \phi$ and $\phi$ Fiy, then
$\sec ^{2} \theta-\tan ^{2} \phi=1$
and $\sec ^{2} \phi-\tan ^{2} \psi=1$
Now, $\theta$ R $\psi$
Then. $\sec ^{2} \theta-\tan ^{2} \psi=1$
$\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} \theta-\tan ^{2} \psi+1=1+1$
$\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} \theta-\tan ^{2} \psi+\sec ^{2} \phi-\tan ^{2} \phi=1+1$
$\Rightarrow \theta R \phi$ and $\phi R \psi$
Thus, it is transitive. Hence, it is an equivalence relation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: We define a binary relation - on the set of all $3 \times 3$ real matrices as $A \sim B,$ if and only if there exist invertible matrices $P$ and $Q$ such that $B=P A Q^{-1} .$ The binary relation $\sim$ is
options: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
questions.solution:
Let the relation defined as $R=\left\{(A, B): B=P A Q^{-1}\right\}$
For reflexive
$A=I A I^{-1}$
$\Rightarrow(A, A) \in R$
$\Rightarrow R$ is reflexive
For symmetric
$\operatorname{Let}(A, B) \in R$
$\therefore B=P A Q^{-1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad B Q=P A\left(Q^{-1} Q\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad B Q=P A$
$\Rightarrow \quad P^{-1}(B Q)=P^{-1} P(A)$
$\Rightarrow \quad P^{-1} B Q=A$
$\Rightarrow(B, A) \in R$
$\Rightarrow R$ is symmetric.
For transitive Let $(A, B) \in R,(B, C) \in R$
Then, $\quad A=P B Q^{-1}$ and $B=R C S^{-1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad A=P R C S^{-1} Q^{-1}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & A=(P R) C(Q S)^{-1} \\ \Rightarrow & (A, C) \in R\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow R$ is transitive. Hence, $R$ is an equivalence relation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In the set of all $3 \times 3$ real matrices a relation is defined as follows. A matrix $A$ is related to a matrix $B,$ if and only if there is a non-singular $3 \times 3$ matrix $P,$ such that $B=P^{-1} A P .$ This relation is
questions.solution:
Let the relation defined as $R=\left\{(A, B) \mid B=P^{-1} A P\right\}$
For reflexive, $A=I^{-1} A l$
$\Rightarrow (A, A) \in R$
$\Rightarrow R$ is reflexive
For symmetric $\operatorname{Let}(A B) \in R$
$\because$
$B=P^{-1} A P$
$\Rightarrow P B=A P \Rightarrow P B P^{-1}=A$
$\Rightarrow$
$A=\left(P^{-1}\right)^{-1} B\left(P^{-1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow (B, A) \in R \Rightarrow R$ is symmetric.
For transitive
Let $(A, B) \in R,(B, C) \in R$
$\because A=P^{-1} B P$ and $B=Q^{-} C Q$
$\Rightarrow A=P^{-} Q^{-1} C Q P=(Q P)^{-1} C(Q P)$
$\Rightarrow (A, C) \in R$
$\Rightarrow R$ is transitive. since, $A$ is reflexive, symmetric and transitive. So, $R$ is an equivalence relation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For any two real numbers $a$ and $b$, we define $a R b$ if and only if $\sin ^{2} a+\cos ^{2} b=1$. The relation $R$ is
questions.solution:
Let the given relation defined as
$R=\left\{(a, b) \mid \sin ^{2} a+\cos ^{2} b=1\right\}$
For reflexive, $\sin ^{2} a+\cos ^{2} a=1$
$\left(\because \sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta=1, \forall \theta \in R\right)$
$\Rightarrow { }_{a} R_{a} \Rightarrow(a, a) \in R$
$\Rightarrow R$ is reflexive.
For symmetric, $\sin ^{2} a+\cos ^{2} b=1$
$\Rightarrow 1-\cos ^{2} a+1-\sin ^{2} b=1$
$\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} b+\cos ^{2} a=1$
$\Rightarrow$ bRa
Hence, $R$ is symmetric.
For transitive
Let $a R b, b R c$ $\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} a+\cos ^{2} b=1$
and $\sin ^{2} b+\cos ^{2} c=1$
On adding Eqs. (0) and (ii), we get $\sin ^{2} a+\left(\sin ^{2} b+\cos ^{2} b\right)+\cos ^{2} c=2$
$\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} a+\cos ^{2} c+1=2$
$\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} a+\cos ^{2} c=1$
Hence, $A$ is transitive also. Therefore, relation $A$ is an equivalence relation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: There are 100 students in a class. In an examination, 50 of them failed in Mathematics, 45 failed in Physics, 40 failed in Biology and 32 failed in exactly two of the three subjects. Only one student passed in all the subjects. Then, the number of students failing in all the three subjects
options: cannot be determined from the given information
questions.solution:
According to question,
$\begin{array}{c}
a+b+c+d+e+f+g+h=100 \\
h=1 \\
a+b+d+e=50
\end{array}$

Venn diagram shows failed students $b+c+d+f=45$
$\begin{array}{l}
d+e+f+g=40 \\
b+e+f=32
\end{array}$
On solving, the above equations, we get $d=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For any two sets $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{A}-(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B})$ equals
questions.solution: Hints: $A-(A-B)=A-\left(A \cap B^c\right)=A \cap\left(A \cap B^c\right)^c=A \cap\left(A^c \cup B\right)=\left(A \cap A^c\right) \cup(A \cap B)=A \cap B$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $X$ is a random variable such that $\sigma(X)=2.6$ then $\sigma(1-4 x)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given that,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma(X) &=2.6=\sqrt{\operatorname{var}(x)} \\
\sigma(1-4 x) &=\sqrt{\operatorname{var}(1-4 x)} \\
&=\sqrt{16 \operatorname{var}(x)} \\
&=4 \times \sqrt{\operatorname{var}(x)}=4 \times 2.6=10.4
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Mean of $n$ observations $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}$ is $\bar{x}$. If an observation $x_{q}$, is replaced by $x_{q}^{\prime}$ then the new mean is
questions.solution:
We have,
$$
\bar{x}=\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+\ldots+x_{q}+\ldots+x_{2}}{n}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \Sigma x=n \bar{x}$
If $x_{q}$, is replaced by $x_{q}^{\prime}$, then new total will be $\Sigma x'=\Sigma x-x_{4}-x_{4}^{\prime}$
New mean will be
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\bar{x}^{\prime}=\frac{\Sigma x^{\prime}}{n} \\
\bar{x}^{\prime}=\frac{\sum x-x_{q}+x_{i}^{\prime}}{n} \\
\bar{x}^{\prime}=\frac{n \bar{x}-x_{q}+x_{i}^{\prime}}{n}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Standard deviation of $n$ observations $a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \ldots, a_{n}$ is ${\sigma} .$ Then, the standard deviation of the observations $\lambda a_{1}, \lambda a_{2}, \ldots, \lambda a_{n}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $x_{i}=a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \ldots, a_{n}$
and $y_{i}=\lambda a_{1}, \lambda a_{2}, \lambda a_{3}, \ldots, \lambda a_{n-}$
$$
y_{i}=\lambda x_{i}
$$
$\because$
$\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &=\frac{\Sigma y_{i}}{N} \\ &=\frac{\Sigma \lambda x_{i}}{n} \end{aligned}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\lambda \frac{\Sigma x_{i}}{n} \\
&=\lambda \bar{x} \\
\sigma_{y} &=\sqrt{\frac{\Sigma\left(y_{i}-\bar{y}\right)^{2}}{N}} \\
&=\sqrt{\frac{\Sigma(\lambda x_{i}-\lambda \bar{x})^{2}}{n}} \\
&=\sqrt{\frac{\lambda^{2} \Sigma\left(x_{i}-\bar{x}\right)^{2}}{n}} \\
&=\sqrt{\lambda^{2} \sigma_{x}^{2}}=|\lambda| \sigma_{x}=|\lambda| \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The variance of first 20 natural numbers is
questions.solution:
Variance of $n$ natural numbers $=\frac{n^{2}-1}{12}=\frac{(20)^{2}-1}{12} \quad$ $[\because n=20]$,
$=\frac{400-1}{12}=\frac{399}{12}=\frac{133}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The ends $A, B$ of a straight line segment of constant length $c$ slide upon the fixed rectangular axes OX, OY respectively. If the rectangle OAPB completed, then the locus of the foot of perpendicular drawn from $P$ to AB is
questions.solution:
Let the coordinates of $A$ be $(a, 0)$ and $B$ be $(0, b)$.

Since the length of $AB$ is $c$, $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.

The rectangle $OAPB$ is completed with $O$ as the origin, so the coordinates of $P$ are $(a, b)$.

The equation of the line $AB$ is $\dfrac{x}{a} + \dfrac{y}{b} = 1$, which simplifies to $bx + ay - ab = 0$.

Let $M(h, k)$ be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from $P(a, b)$ to the line $AB$.

The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $Ax + By + C = 0$ are given by:

$\dfrac{h - x_1}{A} = \dfrac{k - y_1}{B} = -\dfrac{Ax_1 + By_1 + C}{A^2 + B^2}$

Substituting the respective values:

$\dfrac{h - a}{b} = \dfrac{k - b}{a} = -\dfrac{b(a) + a(b) - ab}{b^2 + a^2}$

$\dfrac{h - a}{b} = \dfrac{k - b}{a} = -\dfrac{ab}{c^2}$

Solving for $h$:

$h = a - \dfrac{ab^2}{c^2} = \dfrac{a(c^2 - b^2)}{c^2} = \dfrac{a^3}{c^2} \Rightarrow a = (c^2 h)^{1/3}$

Solving for $k$:

$k = b - \dfrac{a^2 b}{c^2} = \dfrac{b(c^2 - a^2)}{c^2} = \dfrac{b^3}{c^2} \Rightarrow b = (c^2 k)^{1/3}$

Substituting $a$ and $b$ into the condition $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$:

$(c^2 h)^{2/3} + (c^2 k)^{2/3} = c^2$

$c^{4/3} h^{2/3} + c^{4/3} k^{2/3} = c^2$

$h^{2/3} + k^{2/3} = c^{2/3}$

Replacing $(h, k)$ with $(x, y)$, the locus is $x^{2/3} + y^{2/3} = c^{2/3}$.

Answer: $x^{2/3} + y^{2/3} = c^{2/3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line parallel to the $x$-axis passing through the intersection of the lines $a x+2 b y+3 b=0$ and $b x-2 a y-3 a=$ where $(a, b) \neq(0,0)$ is
options: above $x$-axis at a distance $\frac{3}{2}$ from it
above x -axis at a distance $\frac{2}{3}$ from it
below $x$-axis at a distance $\frac{3}{2}$ from it
below x -axis at a distance $\frac{2}{3}$ from it
questions.solution:
Let the line be $(a x+2 b y+3 b)+\lambda(b x-2 a y-3 a)=0$
$\because$ It is parallel to $x$-axis.
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} \lambda}{2 \mathrm{a} \lambda-2 \mathrm{~b}}=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~b}}$

So, equation is $a x+2 b y+3 b-\frac{a}{b}(b x-2 a y-3 a)=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow a x+2 b y+3 b-a x+\frac{2 a^2 y}{b}+\frac{3 a^2}{b}=0 \Rightarrow\left(2 b+\frac{2 a^2}{b}\right) y=-\left(\frac{3 a^2}{b}+3 b\right) \\
& \Rightarrow y=-\frac{3}{2}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider three points $P(\cos \alpha, \sin \beta), Q(\sin \alpha, \cos \beta), R(0,0)$, where $0 \lt \alpha, \beta \lt \frac{\pi}{4}$. Then
options: $P$ lies on the line segment RQ
Q lies on the line segment PR
$R$ lies on the line segment $P Q$
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text {Consider } \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\cos \alpha & \sin \beta & 1 \\
\sin \alpha & \cos \beta & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right| \\
& =\cos \alpha \cos \beta-\sin \alpha \sin \beta \\
& =\cos (\alpha+\beta) \neq 0 \text { as } 0 < \alpha+\beta < \pi / 2
\end{aligned}\)
Hence the given points are non-collinear.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In $\triangle A B C$, co-ordinates of $A$ are $(1,2)$ and the equation of the medians through $B$ and $C$ are $x+y=5$ and $x=4$ respectively. Then the midpoint of $B C$ is
questions.solution: Let $\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}$ be midpoint of $\mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{AC}$ and AB respectively. According to question equation of $B E: x+y=5$ Equation of $C F$ $x=4$ $\therefore$ Centroid $(4,1)$ Let point $D$ be $(\alpha, \beta)$ by section formula, $(\alpha, \beta)=\left(\frac{11}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If (1, 5) be the midpoint of the segment of a line between the line 5x â€“ y â€“ 4 = 0 and 3x + 4y â€“ 4 = 0, then the equation
of the line will be
questions.solution:
\(P_1 P_2\) be the intercept between the lines \(5 x-y-4=0\) and \(3 x+4 y-4=0\).
\(P_1 P_2\) makes an angle \(\theta\) with the positive \(x-a x i s\).
\(\left(\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{y}_1\right)=\mathrm{A}(1,5)\)
So, the equation of the line passing through \(\mathrm{A}(1,5)\) is
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{x-x_1}{\cos \theta}=\frac{y-y_1}{\sin \theta} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{\cos \theta}=\frac{y-5}{\sin \theta} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{y-5}{\mathrm{x}-1}=\tan \theta \\
& \text {Let } A P_1=A P_2=r
\end{aligned}\)
Then, the co-ordinates of \(P_1\) and \(P_2\) are given by
\(\Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{\cos \theta}=\frac{y-5}{\sin \theta}=r \text { and } \frac{x-1}{\cos \theta}=\frac{y-5}{\sin \theta}=-r\)
So, the co-ordinates of \(P_1\) and \(P_2\) are \(1+r \cos \theta, 5+r \sin \theta\) and
\(1-r \cos \theta, 5-r \sin \theta\) respectively.
Clearly, \(P_1\) and \(P_2\) lie on \(5 x-y-4=0\) and \(3 x+4 y-4=0\) respectively.
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore 5(1+r \cos \theta)-5-r \sin \theta-4=0 \text { and } \\
& 3(1-r \cos \theta)+4(5-r \sin \theta)-4=0 \\
& \Rightarrow r=\frac{4}{5 \cos \theta-\sin \theta} \text { and } r=\frac{19}{3 \cos \theta+4 \sin \theta} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{4}{5 \cos \theta-\sin \theta}=\frac{19}{3 \cos \theta+4 \sin \theta} \\
& \Rightarrow 95 \cos \theta-19 \sin \theta=12 \cos \theta+16 \sin \theta \\
& \Rightarrow 83 \cos \theta=35 \sin \theta \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}=\frac{83}{35}
\end{aligned}\)
Thus, the equation of the required line is
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{y-5}{x-1}=\tan \theta \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{y-5}{x-1}=\frac{83}{35} \\
& \Rightarrow 35 y-175=83-83 \\
& \Rightarrow 83 x-35 y+92=0
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore\) The equation of line is \(83 x-35 y+92=0\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\Gamma$ be the curve $y=b e^{-x / a} \& L$ be the straight line $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ where $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$
options: L touches the curve $\Gamma$ at the point where the curve crosses the axis of $y$.
L does not touch the curve at the point where the curve crosses the axis of y .
$\Gamma$ touches the axis of $x$ at the point.
questions.solution:
Hint : $\Gamma \equiv y=b e^{-x / a} \xrightarrow[\text { w.r.t } x]{\text { on differtiating }} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-b}{a} e^{-x / a}$ at $x=0, y=b, \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-b}{a}$
so, equation of tengent at $(0, b)$ is $y-b=-\frac{b}{a}(x-0) \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1}{L}$
so, $L$ touches the given curve at $(0, b)$
$\because \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}$ can be never be 0 , so, the given curve never touches the $\mathrm{x}-$ axis.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A square with each side equal to 'a' above the $x$-axis and has one vertex at the origin. One of the sides passing through the origin makes an angle $\alpha\left(0 \lt \alpha \lt \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ with the positive direction of the axis. Equation of the diagonals of the square.
questions.solution: The parametric coordinate of one vertex of square is \((0+a \cos a, 0+a \sin a)\) Inclination of diagonal not passing through origin is \(135^{\circ}+\mathrm{a}\) \(\therefore\) equation of the diagonal which is not passing through origin is \(\begin{aligned} & y-a \sin \alpha=\tan \left(135^{\circ}+\alpha\right)[x-a \cos \alpha] \\ & \Rightarrow y-a \sin a=-\tan \left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)[x-a \cos \alpha] \\ & \Rightarrow y-a \sin a=-\left(\frac{\cos a-\sin }{\sin \alpha+\cos \alpha}\right)[x-a \cos \alpha] \\ & \Rightarrow(y-a \sin \alpha)(\sin \alpha+\cos \alpha)+(\cos \alpha-\sin a)(x-a \cos \alpha)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y(\sin a+\cos \alpha)+x(\cos \alpha-\sin \alpha)=a \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(A\) be the point \((0,4)\) in the xy-plane and let \(B\) be the point \((2 t, 0)\). Let \(L\) be the midpoint of \(A B\) and let the perpendicular bisector of \(A B\) meet the \(y\)-axis \(M\). Let \(N\) be the midpoint of LM. Then locus of \(N\) is
questions.solution:
Hint :
Equation of LM \(\begin{aligned} & y-2=\frac{t}{2}(x-t) \\ & \Rightarrow M\left(0, \frac{4-t^2}{2}\right) \end{aligned}\) Midpoint of LM : N(h, k) \(\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad 2 h=t, 2 k=4-\frac{t^2}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2=2-y \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(4 a^2+9 b^2-c^2+12 a b=0\), then the family of straight lines \(a x+b y+c=0\) is concurrent at
questions.solution:
Hint : \(\because 2 a+3 b-c=0\) or \(2 a+3 b+c=0\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow c= \pm(2 a+3 b) \\
& \because \quad a x+b y+c=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad a x+b y \pm(2 a+3 b)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad a(x \pm 2)+b(y \pm 3)=0 \text { (family of lines) } \\
& \Rightarrow \quad(-2,-3) \quad \text { or }(2,3)
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of points \((x, y)\) in the plane satisfying \(\sin ^2 x+\sin ^2 y=1\) consists of
options: infinitely many circles that are all centered at the origin
questions.solution:
\(\begin{array}{r}\text {Hint: } 
\sin ^2 y=\cos ^2 x \\
\sin y= \pm \cos x
\end{array}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text { If } \sin y=\cos x=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) \\
& \Rightarrow y=n \pi+(-1)^n\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)
\end{aligned}\)
\(\text { If } \sin y=-\cos x=\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\mathrm{x}-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\)

questions.q: A rectangle \(A B C D\) has its side parallel to the line \(y=2 x\) and vertices \(A, B, D\) are on lines \(y=1, x=1\) and \(x=-1\) respectively. The coordinate of \(C\) can be
questions.solution:

line \(y=2 x\ldots(1)\) \(A D\) parallel to \(y=2 x\) \(\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{AD}}=2 ~\&~ \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{AB}}=-\frac{1}{2}\) for \(a=3\) \(A(3,1), B(1,2), C(-3,-6), D(-1,-7)\)
\(\begin{aligned} & \text { for } a=-3 \\ & C(3,3), A(-3,1), B(1,-1), D(-1,5)\end{aligned}\)
Case II AD perpendicular to line (1) \(\Rightarrow M_{A D}=\frac{1}{2}, M_{A B}=2 \Rightarrow b=3-2 a \Rightarrow d=\frac{a+1}{2}\)
for \(a=3, C(-3,-2)\)
for \(a=-3, C(3,7)\)
\({ }^*\) In both cases if abscissa of \(C: 3\) or -3 then co-ordinates of \(C\) can be \((3,3)\) or \((3,7)\) or \((-3,-6)\) or \((-3,-2)\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $A$ be the point $(0,4)$ and $B$ be a moving point on $x$-axis. Let $M$ be the midpoint of $A B$ and let the perpendicular bisector of $A B$ meets the $y$-axis at $R$. The locus of the midpoint $P$ of $M R$ is
questions.solution: Let B $=(2 \alpha, 0)$ $\therefore \mathrm{M}=(\alpha, 2)$ $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{4}{-2 \alpha}=-\frac{2}{\alpha}$ $\therefore \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{MR}}=\frac{\alpha}{2}, \therefore \mathrm{MR} \quad \mathrm{y}-2=\frac{\alpha}{2}(\mathrm{x}-\alpha)$ Put, $\mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=2-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{2}$ $\therefore \mathrm{R} \equiv\left(0,2-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{2}\right)$ $\therefore \mathrm{P} \equiv\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}, \frac{2-\alpha^{2} / 2+2}{2}\right) \equiv \left(\frac{\alpha}{2}, 2-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{4}\right)$ $\therefore \mathrm{y}=2-\mathrm{x}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x}^{2}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A moving line intersects the lines $x+y=0$ and $x-y=0$ at the points A, B respectively such that the area of the triangle with vertices $(0,0), A \& B$ has a constant area $C$. The locus of the mid-point $A B$ is given by the equation
questions.solution: Let mid point of $\mathrm{AB}=(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ Let, $A=(\alpha,-\alpha), B=(\beta, \beta)$ $\therefore \beta+\alpha=2 \mathrm{~h}, \quad \beta-\alpha=2 \mathrm{k}$ Area $\Delta \mathrm{AOB}=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OA} \times \mathrm{OB}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2 \alpha^{2}} \sqrt{2 \beta^{2}}=|\alpha \beta|=\mathrm{c}$ $\begin{array}{l} \therefore \alpha^{2} \beta^{2}=\mathrm{c}^{2} \Rightarrow 16 \alpha^{2} \beta^{2}=16 \mathrm{c}^{2} \Rightarrow\left\{(\beta+\alpha)^{2}-(\beta-\alpha)^{2}\right\}^{2}=16 \mathrm{c}^{2} \\ \Rightarrow\left(4 \mathrm{~h}^{2}-4 \mathrm{k}^{2}\right)^{2}=16 \mathrm{c}^{2} \rightarrow \text { locus }:\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{y}^{2}\right)^{2}=\mathrm{c}^{2} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a>0, b>0$ then the maximum area of the parallelogram whose three vertices are $O(0,0), A(a \cos \theta, b \sin \theta)$ and $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{a} \cos \theta,-\mathrm{b} \sin \theta)$ is
questions.solution: Area $\mathrm{OABC}=2 \times$ area $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$ $\begin{array}{l} =2 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OM} \times \mathrm{AB}\right) \\ =2 \times \frac{1}{2}|\mathrm{a} \cos \theta \times 2 \mathrm{~b} \sin \theta|=2 \times \mathrm{ab}|\sin \theta \cos \theta| \\ =\mathrm{ab}|\sin 2 \theta| \end{array}$ $\therefore$ Maximum is ab when $\theta=\pi / 4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A ray of light along $x+\sqrt{3}y=\sqrt{3}$ gets reflected upon reaching $x$-axis, the equation of the reflected ray is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{A} \equiv(\sqrt{3}, 0)$ Let, B $(0,1)$ $\therefore \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \equiv(0,-1)$ $\therefore$ Equation of $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime}: \frac{\mathrm{y}-0}{0+1}=\frac{\mathrm{x}-\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}-0} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}-\sqrt{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Two tangents to the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ at the points $A$ and $B$ meet at $M(-4,0)$. The area of the quadrilateral MAOB, where $O$ is the origin is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & O M=4, O A=2 \\ \therefore & M A=\sqrt{16-4}=\sqrt{12}=2 \sqrt{3} \\ \therefore & \text { Area }(M A O B) \\=& 2 \times \text { area } \Delta M A O \\=& 2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times M A \times O A \\=& 2 \sqrt{3} \times 2=4 \sqrt{3} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A plane meets the co-ordinate axes at the points $A, B, C$ respectively in such a way that the centroid of $\triangle A B C$ is $\left(1, r, r^{2}\right)$ for some real $r$. If the plane passes through the point $(5,5,-12)$ then $r=$
questions.solution: Centroid is $\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{a} / 3, \mathrm{~b} / 3, \mathrm{c} / 3) \equiv\left(1, \mathrm{r}, \mathrm{r}^{2}\right)$ $a / 3=1 \quad \Rightarrow a=3, b=3 r, c=3 r^{2}$ Now, $\begin{array}{l} \frac{5}{a}+\frac{5}{b}-\frac{12}{c}=1 \\ \Rightarrow \frac{5}{3}+\frac{5}{3 r}-\frac{12}{3 r^{2}}=1 \\ \Rightarrow \frac{5 r^{2}+5 r-12}{3 r^{2}}=1 \\ \Rightarrow 5 r^{2}+5 r-12=3 r^{2} \\ \Rightarrow 2 r^{2}+5 r-12=0 \\ \Rightarrow r=\frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{25+96}}{4} \\ \Rightarrow r=\frac{-5 \pm 11}{4}=-4,3 / 2 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $r \cos \left(\theta-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=2$ represents
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$r \cos \left(\theta-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=2 \quad \Rightarrow r \cos \theta \times \frac{1}{2}+r \sin \theta \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=2 \Rightarrow x+\sqrt{3} y=4(x=r \cos \theta, y=r \sin \theta)$
$\therefore$ a straight line

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let each of the equations $x^{2}+2 x y+a y^{2}=0 \& a x^{2}+2 x y+y^{2}=0$ represent two straight lines passing through the origin. If they have a common line, then the other two lines are given by
questions.solution:
Hint:
$\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}\right)^{2}+2\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}\right)+\mathrm{a}=0 \& \mathrm{a}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}\right)^{2}+2\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}\right)+1=0$ have exactly one root in common (taking $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}$ as a single
variable).
By, $\left(a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1}\right)\left(b_{1} c_{2}-b_{2} c_{1}\right)=\left(a_{1} c_{2}-a_{2} c_{1}\right)^{2}$
We get $: \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=1$ or $-3$
a cannot be 1
Taking $\mathrm{a}=-3$, roots of 1 st equation $: 1,-3$ and 2 nd equation : $1,-\frac{1}{3}$
So other lines: $\frac{x}{y}=-3$ and $\frac{x}{y}=-\frac{1}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A straight line through the origin $\mathrm{O}$ meets the parallel lines $4 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}=9$ and $2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+6=0$ at $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$ respectively. The point O divides the segment $\mathrm{PQ}$ in the ratio
questions.solution: Hint : $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{OP}}{\mathrm{OQ}}=\frac{\mathrm{OM}}{\mathrm{ON}}=\Rightarrow \frac{9 / 2}{12 / 2}=\frac{3}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Aline cuts the $x$ -axis at $A(7,0)$ and the $y$ -axis at $B(0,-5)$. A variable line $P Q$ is drawn perpendicular to $A B$ cutting the $x$ -axis at $P(a, 0)$ and the $y$ -axis at $Q(0, b)$. If $A Q$ and BP intersect at $R$, the locus of $R$ is
questions.solution: Hint: $\mathrm{P}$ is orthocentre of $\triangle \mathrm{ABQ}$ $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{BR}} \times \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{AR}}=-1$ $\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}+5}{\mathrm{~h}}\right) \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{h}-7}\right)=-1$ $\Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}-7 x+5 y=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the straight line passing through the point $(4,3)$ and making intercepts on the co-ordinate axes whose sum is $-1$ is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Let line be $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$, then $a+b=-1$
$\frac{4}{a}-\frac{3}{1+a}=1 \Rightarrow a=\pm 2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A variable line passes through a fixed point $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and meets the axes at $A$ and $B$. If the rectangle $O A P B$ be completed, the locus of $P$ is, $(O$ being the origin of the system of axes).
questions.solution: Let the equation of line be $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ since, the line passing through a fixed point $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$. $\therefore \quad \frac{x_{1}}{a}+\frac{y_{1}}{b}=1$ since, $O A P B$ is a rectangle, therefore the coordinate of $P$ will be $(a, b)$ Hence, locus of $P$ is $\frac{x_{1}}{x}+\frac{y_{1}}{y}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A straight line through the point (3,-2) is ndined at an angle $60^{\circ}$ to the line $\sqrt{3} x+y=1$. If it intersects the $X$ -axis, then its equation vill be
questions.solution:
Given line,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sqrt{3} x+y=1 \\
y=-\sqrt{3} x+c
\end{array}
$$
We know that, $m=-\sqrt{3}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { that, } m &=-\sqrt{3} \quad[\because y=m x+c] \\
\tan \theta &=\left|\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{1+m_{1} m_{2}}\right| \\
\tan 60^{\circ} &=\left|\frac{m_{1}-(-\sqrt{3})}{1+m_{1}(-\sqrt{3})}\right| \quad\left[\because \theta=60^{\circ}\right] \\
\sqrt{3}=&\left|\frac{m_{1}+\sqrt{3}}{1-\sqrt{3}} m_{1}\right| \\
\pm \sqrt{3}=& \frac{m_{1}+\sqrt{3}}{1-\sqrt{3} m_{1}}
\end{aligned}
$$
\begin{array}{l|l}
\hline taking + sign & taking - sign \\
\hline+\sqrt{3}=\frac{m_{1}+\sqrt{3}}{1-\sqrt{3} m_{1}} & -\sqrt{3}=\frac{m_{1}+\sqrt{3}}{1-\sqrt{3} m_{1}} \\
\sqrt{3}\left(1-\sqrt{3} m_{1}\right)=m_{1}+\sqrt{3} & \Rightarrow-\sqrt{3}\left(1-\sqrt{3} m_{1}\right) \\
& =m_{1}+\sqrt{3} \\
\Rightarrow \sqrt{3}-3 m_{1}=m_{1}+\sqrt{3} & \Rightarrow-\sqrt{3}-3 m_{1}=m_{1}+\sqrt{3} \\
\Rightarrow & \Rightarrow 3 m_{1}-m_{1}=2 \sqrt{3} \\
\Rightarrow 4m_{1}=0 & \Rightarrow \quad 2 m_{1}=2 \sqrt{3} \\
\Rightarrow m_{1}=0 & \Rightarrow \quad m_{1}=\sqrt{3}
\end{array}
given point (3,-2) 
$y=m x$ + c
$$
\begin{aligned}
-2 &=3 \sqrt{3}+c \\
c &=-2-3 \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, required equation
$$
\therefore \quad y=m x+c
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
y=\sqrt{3} x-2-3 \sqrt{3} \\
y-\sqrt{3} x+2+3 \sqrt{3}=0 \\
y-x \sqrt{3}+2+3 \sqrt{3}=0
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A variable line passes through the fixed point $(\alpha, \beta) .$ The locus of the foot of the perpendicular from the origin on the line is
questions.solution:
Let $(\alpha, \beta)$ be the given point, let $Q(x, y)$ be the foot of the perpendicular, and let $O$ be the origin. The line can have any direction
$$
\angle P Q O=90^{\circ}
$$
Point $Q$ lies on the circle having diameter $O P$.
The locus of point $Q$.
$$
(x-0)(x-\alpha)+(y-0)(y-\beta)=0
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}-x \alpha+y^{2}-y \beta=0$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+y^{2}-\alpha x-\beta y=0
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the point of intersection of the lines $2 a x+4 a y+c=0$ and $7 b x+3 b y-d=0$ lies in the 4 th quadrant and is equidistant from the two axes, where $a, b, c$ and $d$ are non-zero numbers, then $a d: b c$ equals to
questions.solution:
Let coordinate of the point be $(\alpha,-\alpha)$
since, $(\alpha,-\alpha)$ lie on $2 a x+4 a y+c=0$
and $\quad 7 b x+3 b y-d=0$
$\therefore \quad 2 a \alpha-4 a \alpha+c=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-2 a \alpha+c=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\frac{c}{2 a}$
Also, $7 b \alpha-3 b \alpha-d=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 b \alpha-d=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\frac{d}{4 b}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), 
$\begin{aligned} \frac{c}{2 a} &=\frac{d}{4 b} \\ 
\Rightarrow 2 a d &=4 b c \end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a d}{b c}=\frac{4}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a d}{b c}=\frac{2}{1}$
$a d: b c=2: 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $P(0,0), Q(1,0)$ and $R\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$ are three given points, then the centre of the circle for which the lines $P Q, Q R$ and $R P$ are the tangents is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} &\text { } I=\left(\frac{a x_{1}+b x_{2}+c x_{3}}{a+b+c}, \frac{a y_{1}+b y_{2}+c y_{3}}{a+b+c}\right)\\ &a=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}+\frac{3}{4}}=1, b=1, c=1\\ &\text { Centre of the circle. }\\ &=\left(\frac{1 \times 0+1 \times 1+1 \times \frac{1}{2}}{1+1+1}, \frac{1 \times 0+1 \times 0+1 \times \sqrt{3} / 2}{1+1+1}\right)\\ &=\left(\frac{0+1+\frac{1}{2}}{3} \frac{0+0+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{3}\right)\\ &=\left(\frac{3}{\frac{2}{3}}, \frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{3}\right) \end{aligned}$ $=\left(\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}\right)$ $=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Straight lines $x-y=7$ and $x+4 y=2$ intersect at $B$. Points $A$ and $C$ are so chosen on these two lines such that $A B=A C$. The equation of line $A C$ passing through (2,-7) is
questions.solution: Given equations of lines are $x-y=7$ ....(i) and $x+4y=2$ ...(ii) By solving Eqs.(i) and (ii), we get the point $B(6,-1)$ Let the slope of $A C$ be $m$ then, $A B=A C$ $\therefore \quad\left|\frac{m+\frac{1}{4}}{1-\frac{m}{4}}\right|=\left|\frac{-\frac{1}{4}-1}{1-\frac{1}{4}}\right|$ $\Rightarrow \quad m=\frac{-23}{7}, 1$ When $m=-\frac{23}{7},$ then equation of line $y+7=\frac{-23}{7}(x-2)$ $\Rightarrow \quad 7 y+49=-23 x+46$ $\Rightarrow \quad 23 x+7 y+3=0$ When $m=1$, then equation of line $y+7=(x-2)$ $\Rightarrow \quad x-y-9=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The point $Q$ is the image of the point $P(1,5)$ about the line $y=x$ and $R$ is the image of the point $Q$ about the line $y=-x .$ The circumcentre of the $\Delta P Q R$ is
questions.solution: Given, point P(1,5) image of the point $P(1,5)$ about the line $y=x$ is $Q(5,1)$ and image of the point $Q$ on line $y=-x$ is $R(-1,-5)$ $\therefore$ Required circumcentre $=$ Mid-point of $P$ and $R$ $=\left(\frac{1-1}{2}, \frac{5-5}{2}\right)=(0,0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angular points of a triangle are $A(-1,-7)$ $B(5,1)$ and $C(1,4)$. The equation of the bisector of the angle $\angle A B C$ is
questions.solution: Here, $A B=\sqrt{(5+1)^{2}+(1+7)^{2}}=\sqrt{36+64}=10$ $B C=\sqrt{\left(1-5)^{2}+(4-1)^{2}\right.}=\sqrt{16+9}=5$ By angle bisector theorem, $$ A P: C P=10: 5=2: 1 $$ $P\left(\frac{2 \times 1+1 \times(-1)}{2+1}, \frac{2 \times 4+1 \times(-7)}{2+1}\right)=P\left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right)$ Required equation of $B P$ is $y-1=\frac{\frac{1}{3}-1}{\frac{1}{3}-5}(x-5)$ $\Rightarrow \quad y-1=\frac{-2}{-14}(x-5)$ $\Rightarrow \quad 7 y-7=x-5$ $\Rightarrow \quad 7 y=x+2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A line cuts the $X$ -axis at $A(5,0)$ and the $Y$ -axis at $B(0,-3)$. A variable line $P Q$ is drawn perpendicular to $A B$ cutting the $X$ -axis at $P$ and the $Y$ -axis at $Q$. If $A Q$ and $B P$ meet at $R$, then the locus of $R$ is
questions.solution: Equation of line $A B$ is $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{x}{5}+\frac{y}{-3}=1 \\ 3 x-5 y=15 \end{array} $$ Perpendicular line to $A B$ is $$ 5 x+3 y=\lambda $$ Coordinate of $P$ is $\left(\frac{\lambda}{5}, 0\right)$ and coordinate of $Q$ is $(0, \lambda / 3)$ Now, equation of line $A Q$ is $x / 5+\frac{y}{\lambda / 3}=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{5}+\frac{3 y}{\lambda}=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 y}{\lambda}=1-\frac{x}{5}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{1}{3 y}\left(1-\frac{x}{5}\right)$ and equation of line $B P$ is $\frac{x}{\lambda / 5}+\frac{y}{-3}=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{5 x}{\lambda}-\frac{y}{3}=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{1}{5 x}\left(\frac{y}{3}+1\right)$ From Eqs. (i) and (ii), $\frac{1}{3 y}\left(1-\frac{x}{5}\right)=\frac{1}{5 x}\left(\frac{y}{3}+1\right)$ $\Rightarrow \quad 5 x\left(1-\frac{x}{5}\right)=3 y\left(\frac{y}{3}+1\right)$ $\Rightarrow \quad 5 x-x^{2}=y^{2}+3 y$ $\Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}-5 x+3 y=0$ which is a circle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the triangle formed by the intersection of a line parallel to $X$ -axis and passing through $P(h, k),$ with the lines $y=x$ and $x+y=2$ is $h^{2}$. The locus of the point $P$ is
questions.solution: Given, ar $(\Delta A B C)=h^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ k & k & 1 \\ 2-k & k & 1\end{array}\right|=\pm h^{2}$ $\Rightarrow 1(k-k)-1(k-2+k)+1\left(k^{2}-2 k+k^{2}\right)=\pm 2 h^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad-(2 k-2)+\left(2 k^{2}-2 k\right)=\pm 2 h^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2-2 k+2 k^{2}-2 k=\pm 2 h^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2 k^{2}-4 k+2=\pm 2 h^{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ $k^{2}-2 k+1=\pm h^{2}$ Hence, locus of a point is $\Rightarrow$ $(k-1)^{2}=h^{2}$ $y-1=\pm x$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=y-1$ or $x=-(y-1)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Transforming to parallel axes through a point $(p, q), \quad$ the $\quad$ equation $2 x^{2}+3 x y+4 y^{2}+x+18 y+25=0$ becomes
$2 x^{2}+3 x y+4 y^{2}=1 .$ Then
questions.solution:
Given equations are
$$
\begin{array}{r}
2 x^{2}+3 x y+4 y^{2}+x+18 y+25=0 ...(i)\\
2 x^{2}+3 x y+4 y^{2}+1=0 ....(ii)
\end{array}
$$
Let the origin be transferred to $(p, q)$ axes being parallel to the previous axes; then the equation
(i) becomes.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
2\left(x^{\prime}+p\right)^{2}+3\left(x^{\prime}+p\right)\left(y^{\prime}+q\right) \\
+4\left(y^{\prime}+q\right)^{2}+\left(x^{\prime}+p\right)+18\left(y^{\prime}+q\right)+25=0 \\
\Rightarrow 2 x^{2}+2 p^{2}+4 x^{\prime} p+3 x^{\prime} y^{\prime}+3 x^{\prime} q+3 p y^{\prime} \\
+3 p q+4 y^{2}+4 q^{2}+8 y^{\prime} q+x^{\prime}+p \\
+18 y^{\prime}+18 q+25=0 \\
\Rightarrow 2 x^{2}+4 y^{a}+3 x^{\prime} y^{\prime}+(4 p+3 q+1) x^{\prime} \\
1+(3 p+8 q+18) y^{\prime}+2 p^{2}+3 p q \\
+4 q^{2}+p+25=0
\end{array}
$$
From Eq. (ii) coefficient of $x^{\prime}$ and $y^{\prime}$ must be
zero.
$\therefore$
$$
4 p+3 q+1=0 ...(iii)
$$
$$
3 p+8 q+18=0 ...(iv)
$$
By solving Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get
$$
p=2, q=-3
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $A(2,-3)$ and $B(-2,1)$ be two angular points of $\Delta A B C$. If the centroid of the triangle moves on the line $2 x+3 y=1$, then the locus of the angular point $C$ is given by
questions.solution:
Let the coordinates of $C$ be $(\alpha, \beta)$.
$\therefore$ Coordinates of centroid
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\left(\frac{2-2+\alpha}{3}, \frac{-3+1+\beta}{3}\right) \\
=\left(\frac{\alpha}{3}, \frac{\beta-2}{3}\right)
\end{array}
$$
Since, centroid lie on $2 x+3 y=1$ 
$\therefore \quad \frac{2 \alpha}{3}+3\left(\frac{\beta-2}{3}\right)=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \alpha}{3}+\frac{3 \beta-6}{3}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \alpha+3 \beta-6=3 \Rightarrow 2 \alpha+3 \beta=9$
$\therefore$ Locus of point $C$ will be $2 x+3 y=9$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The point $P(3,6)$ is first reflected on the line $y=x$ and then the image point $Q$ is again reflected on the line $y=-x$ to get the image point $Q^{\prime} .$ Then, the circumcentre of the $\Delta P Q Q^{\prime}$ is
questions.solution: The coordinates of $Q$ and $Q^{\prime}$ will be (6,3) and $(-3,-6),$ respectively. Now, slope of $P Q=\frac{6-3}{3-6}=-1$ and Slope of $Q Q^{\prime}=\frac{-6-3}{-3-6}=1$ $\therefore$ Slope of $P Q \times$ Slope of $Q Q^{\prime}=-1 \times 1=-1$ $\therefore \Delta P Q Q^{\prime}$ is right angled triangle at $Q .$ $\therefore$ Circumcentre will be the mid-point of hypotenuse $P Q^{\prime}=\left(\frac{-3+3}{2}, \frac{-6+6}{2}\right)$ $$ =(0,0) $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $d_{1}$ and $d_{2}$ be the lengths of the perpendiculars drawn from any point of the line $7 x-9 y+10=0$ upon the lines $3 x+4 y=5$ and $12 x+5 y=7,$ respectively. Then,
questions.solution:
Let $(h, k)$ be any point on the line $7 x-9 y+10=0,$ then $7 h-9 k+10=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 7 h=9 k-10$
$\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{9 k-10}{7}$
Now, perpendicular distance from point
$(h, k)$ to the line $3 x+4 y=5$ is $d_{1}$ 
$d_{1}=\frac{3 h+4 k-5}{\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad d_{1}=\frac{3 h+4 k-5}{5}$
and perpendicular distance from $(h, k)$ to the line $12 x+5 y=7$ is $\bar{d}_{2}$
$\therefore \quad d_{2}=\frac{12 h+5 k-7}{\sqrt{12^{2}+5^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad d_{2}=\frac{12 h+5 k-7}{13}$
Now, $d_{1}-d_{2}=\frac{3 h+4 k-5}{5}-\frac{12 h+5 k-7}{13}$
$\Rightarrow d_{1}-d_{2}$
$=\frac{13(3 h+4 k-5)-5(12 h+5 k-7)}{65}$
$=\frac{39 h+52 k-65-60 h-25 k+35}{65}$
$=\frac{-21 h+27 k-30}{65}$
$=\frac{-21\left(\frac{9 k-10}{7}\right)+27 k-30}{65} \quad[$ from Eq.(i) $]$
$=\frac{-27 k+30+27 k-30}{65}=0$
$\Rightarrow d_{1}-d_{2}=0 \Rightarrow d_{1}=d_{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $S$ be the set of points, whose abscissae and ordinates are natural numbers. Let $P \in S$, such that the sum of the distance of $P$ from (8,0) and (0,12) is minimum among all elements in $S .$ Then, the number of such points $P$ in $S$ is
questions.solution:
Sum of distances of point $P$ from point (8,0) and (0,12) will be minimum, if points are collinear.
Equation of line at point (8,0) and (0,12)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{x}{8}+\frac{y}{12} &=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{y}{12} &=1-\frac{x}{8} \\
\Rightarrow \quad y &=12-\frac{x}{8} \times 12 \\
\Rightarrow \quad y &=12-\frac{3}{2} x
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Points $(x, y)=(2,9),(4,6)$ and (6,3) . Hence, the number of such points $P$ in S is 3.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The points $(-a,-b),(a, b),(0,0)$ and $\left(a^{2}, a b\right), a \neq 0, b \neq 0$ are always
questions.solution:
Let the four points be $A(-a,-b), B(a, b), C(0,0)$ and $D\left(a^{2}, a b\right)$
If $A,B$ and $C$ are collinear.
Then, $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-a & -b & 1 \\ a & b & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-a(b-0)+b(a-0)+1(0)=0$
$-a b+a b=0$
Hence, $A, B$ and $C$ ae collinear.
Again, if $B, C$ and $D$ are collinear.
Then, $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & b & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ a^{2} & a b & 1\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a(0-a b)-b\left(0-a^{2}\right)+1(0) ;=0$
$-a^{2} b+a^{2} b=0$
$\Rightarrow$
Hence, $B, C$ and $D$ are collinear. So, the points $A, B, C$ and $D$ are akways collinear.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line $A B$ cuts off equal intercepts $2 a$ from the axes. From any point $P$ on the line $A B$ perpendiculars $P R$ and $P S$ are drawn on the axes. Locus of mid-point of $R S$ is
questions.solution: $\Rightarrow$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{x}{2 a}+\frac{y}{2 a}=1 \\ x+y=2 a \end{array} $$ Let the coordinates of the mid-point of $\mathrm{RS}$ be $(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ So. $R$ and $S$ are $(2 h, 0)$ and $(0,2 k)$ Hence, the mid-point of $R S$ is $(h, k)$ P lies on line AB Then. from Eq we have $$ 2 h+2 k=2 a $$ $\Rightarrow \quad h+k=a$ So, the locus of $(h, k)$ is $x+y=a$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $x+8 y-22=0,5 x+2 y-34=0$, $2 x-3 y+13=0$ are the three sides of a triangle. The area of the triangle is
questions.solution:
Given three sides of a triangle are
$$
\begin{array}{r}
x+8 y-22=0 ...(i)\\
5 x+2 y-34=0 ...(ii)\\
2 x-3 y+13=0 ...(iii)
\end{array}
$$
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
x_{1}=6, y_{1}=2
$$
On solving Eqs. (il) and (iii), we get
$$
x_{2}=4, y_{2}=7
$$
On solving Eqs. (i) and (iii), we get
$$
x_{3}=-2, y_{2}=3
$$
If the points of the vertices of the triangle are
$\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$. then the area of triangle $=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}6 & 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 7 & 1 \\ -2 & 3 & 1\end{array}\right|$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{1}{2}[6(7-3)-2(4+2)+1(12+14)] \\
=\frac{1}{2}(24-12+26)=\frac{1}{2}(12+26) \\
=\frac{1}{2} \times 38
\end{array}
$$
$=19 \mathrm{sq}$ units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The line through the points $(a, b)$ and $(-a,-b)$
passes through the point
questions.solution:
Let the given points be $A(a, b)$ and $B(-a,-b)$. The equation of line passing through the points A and $B$ is
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
y-y_{1}=\frac{y_{2}-y_{1}}{x_{2}-x_{1}}\left(x-x_{1}\right) \\
\Rightarrow \quad y-b=\frac{-b-b}{-a-a}(x-a) \\
\Rightarrow \quad y-b=\frac{2 b}{2 a}(x-a)
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad y-b=\frac{b}{a}(x-a)$
$\Rightarrow \quad a y-a b=b x-a b$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad b x=a y ...(i)
$$
Since, from the given points $\left(a^{2}, a b\right)$ and $(a, b)$ are satisty the Eq. (i), but ( $a^{2}$, ab) is the required answer because $(a, b)$ is already given in question.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The locus of the point of intersection of the straight lines $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=K$ and $\frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=\frac{1}{K}$,
where $K$ is a non-zero real variable, is given by
questions.solution:
Given equations of straight lines
$$
\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=K
$$
and $\quad \frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=\frac{1}{K}$
Let the point of intersection be $(\alpha, \beta)$. 
So, from Eqs. we get
$$
\frac{\alpha}{a}+\frac{\beta}{b}=K
$$
and
$$
\frac{\alpha}{a}-\frac{\beta}{b}=\frac{1}{K}
$$
$\Rightarrow$
$\left(\frac{\alpha}{a}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{\beta}{b}\right)^{2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{\beta^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$
$\therefore$ Locus $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ which is the equation of a hyperbola.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the vertex of the conic $y^{2}-4 y=4 x-4 a$ always lies between the straight lines ${x}+y=3$ and $2 x+2 y-1=0,$ then
questions.solution: Equation of the vertex of conic $$ y^{2}-4 y=4 x-4 a $$ $\Rightarrow \quad y^{2}-4 y+4=4 x-4 a+4$ $\Rightarrow \quad(y-2)^{2}=4[x-(a-1)]$ $\therefore$ Vertex $=(a-12)$ From figure, clearly. $-\frac{3}{2} < a-1 < 1$ $\therefore$ $-\frac{1}{2} < a < 2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coordinates of a point on the line $x+y+1=0,$ which is at a distance $\frac{1}{5}$ unit from the line $3 x+4 y+2=0,$ are
questions.solution:
Let $(h, k)$ be the point on line $x+y+1=0$
So.
$$
h+k+1=0
$$
$$
h=-1-k
$$
Given, distance between $(h, k)$ and line $3 x+4 y+2=0$ is $\frac{1}{5}$
$\therefore$
$\frac{1}{5}=\left|\frac{(3 h)+(4 k)+2}{\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}}}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{5}=\left|\frac{3(-1-k)+4 k+2}{\sqrt{25}}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{5}=\pm\left(\frac{-3-3 k+4 k+2}{5}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$
$\frac{1}{5}=\pm\left(\frac{k-1}{5}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \pm(k-1)=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad k=0$ and 2
$\therefore \quad h=-1, -3$
Hence, the required points are (-1,0) and (-3,2)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Number of points having distance $\sqrt{5}$ fron the straight line $x-2 y+1=0$ and a distance $\sqrt{13}$ from the line $2 x+3 y-1=0,$ is
questions.solution:
Let the point be $(h, k)$
Then, $\left|\frac{h-2 k+1}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(-2)^{2}}}\right|=\sqrt{5}$
$h-2 k+1=\pm 5$
Also, $\left|\frac{2 h+3 k-1}{\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}}}\right|=$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 h+3 k-1=\pm 13$
On solving Eqs
(i) and (ii), we get four points So. there are much 4 points.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $2 x^{2}+5 x y-12 y^{2}=0$ represents a
options: pair of non-perpendicular intersecting straight lines
questions.solution:
Given equation is
$$
\begin{array}{r}
2 x^{2}+5 x y-12 y^{2}=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad 2 x^{2}+8 x y-3 x y-12 y^{2}=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad 2 x(x+4 y)-3 y(x+4 y)=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad(x+4 y)(2 x-3 y)=0 \\
\therefore \quad x+4 y=0 \text { and } 2 x-3 y=0
\end{array}
$$
which represent a pair of straight lines. 
Compair Eq. (i) with $a x^{2}+2 h x y+b y^{2}=0$
we get $a=2$ and $b=-12$
$\because a+b \neq 0$
So, lines are not perpendicular to each other. Hence, it is a pair of non-perpendicular intersecting straight lines.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of lines which pass through the point (2,-3) and are at a distance 8 from the point (-1,2) is
questions.solution:
The distance from the point (-1,2) of the line which passes through (2,-3)
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\sqrt{(2+1)^{2}+(-3-2)^{2}} \\
=\sqrt{9+25}=\sqrt{34}     < 8
\end{array}
$$
Given that, the distance between the point (-1,2) and the line is 8 But the maximum distance of the line passing through (2,-3) from (-1,2) is $\sqrt{34}$. So, there is no such line possible.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A line passing through the point of intersection of $x+y=4$ and $x-y=2$ makes an angle $\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)$ with the $x$ -axis. It intersects the parabola $y^{2}=4(x-3)$ at points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ respectively. Then, $\left|x_{1}-x_{2}\right|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given lines are
$$
x+y=4 \text { and } x-y=2
$$
On solving these lines, we get
$x=3$ and $y=1$ 
Now, the equation of line which passes through the intersection point (3, 1) having slope
$\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)$ is $(y-1)=\frac{3}{4}(x-3)$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 y-4=3 x-9$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x-4 y=5$
Now for the intersection point of the line (i) with parabola $y^{2}=4(x-3)$. Put $y=\left(\frac{3 x-5}{4}\right)$
then we get $\quad \frac{(3 x-5)^{2}}{16}=4(x-3)$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9 x^{2}+25-30 x=64 x-192$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9 x^{2}-94 x+217=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{94 \pm \sqrt{8836-7812}}{18}=\frac{94 \pm \sqrt{1024}}{18}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{94 \pm 32}{18}=\frac{126}{18}$ or $\frac{62}{18}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x_{1}=\frac{21}{3}=7$
and $\quad x_{2}=\frac{31}{9}$
$\therefore \quad\left|x_{1}-x_{2}\right|=\left|7-\frac{31}{9}\right|=\left|\frac{32}{9}\right|=\frac{32}{9}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The line joining $A(b \cos \alpha, b \sin \alpha)$
and
$B(a \cos \beta, a \sin \beta),$ where $a \neq b,$ is produced to the
point $M(x, y)$ so that $A M: M B=b: a$. Then, $x \cos \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}+y \sin \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Since, $A M: B M=b: a$ $\therefore M$ divides $A B$ extemally in the ratio $b: a$...(i)
$\therefore \quad x=\frac{b \cdot a \cos \beta-a b \cos \alpha}{b-a}$
$y=\frac{b a \sin \beta-a b \sin \alpha}{b-a}$
(ii)
Divide Eq. (i) by Eq, (ii), we get
$\frac{x}{y}=\frac{\cos \beta-\cos \alpha}{\sin \beta-\sin \alpha}=\frac{2 \sin \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2} \sin \frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}}{-2 \cos \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2} \sin \frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}}$
$\therefore \quad x \cos \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}+y \sin \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $P(2,-3), Q(-2,1)$ be the vertices of the $\Delta P Q R .$ If the centroid of $\Delta P Q R$ lies on the line $2 x+3 y=1,$ then the locus of $R$ is
questions.solution: Let the vertices of $R$ be $(h, k)$ $\therefore$ Centroid of triangle is given by $$ \left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}\right) $$ Le, $\left(\frac{2+h-2}{3}, \frac{-3+1+k}{3}\right)$ Le, $\left(\frac{h}{3}, \frac{-2+k}{3}\right)$ since, the point $\left(\frac{h}{3}, \frac{-2+k}{3}\right)$ lies on the line $2 x+3 y=1,$ therefore the point $\left(\frac{h}{3}, \frac{-2+k}{3}\right)$ must be satisfy the equation of the given line. Le., $2\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)+3\left(\frac{-2+k}{3}\right)=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 h-6+3 k}{3}=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2 h-6+3 k=3$ $\Rightarrow h+3 k=9$ Replace $h=x$ and $k=y,$ we get the required locus of $R$. ie, $\quad 2 x+3 y=9$

$$

$$
If $m=-1,$ then $x=-5$ and $m=-2,$ then $x=-1$ are the only 
possibilities.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a straight line passes through the point $(\alpha, \beta)$ and the portion of the line intercepted between the axes is divided equally at that point, then $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{\beta}$ is
questions.solution: Let the line be $A B=\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ $\ldots(0$ $\therefore$ The coordinates of $A$ and $B$ are respectively $(a, 0)$ and $(0, b)$ Since, $(\alpha, \beta)$ is the mid-point of $A B$ $\begin{array}{ll}\therefore \quad & \alpha=\frac{a}{2}, \beta=\frac{b}{2} \\ \therefore & a=2 x \text { and } b=2 \beta\end{array}$ Crom Eq. (i), $\frac{x}{2 x}+\frac{y}{2 \beta}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{\alpha}+\frac{y}{\beta}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A straight line through the point of intersection of the lines $x+2 y=4$ and $2 x+y=4$ meets the coordinate axes at $A$ and $B$. The locus of the mid-point of $A B$ is
questions.solution:
Given lines are,
$$
x+2 y=4
$$
and $\quad 2 x+y=4$
Solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
x=\frac{4}{3}, y=\frac{4}{3}
$$
Let $\quad A B: \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$
Meets $x$ -axis at $A$ and $y$ -axis at $B$. If the straight line (iii) passes through the point $\left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right),$ then $\frac{4}{3 a}+\frac{4}{3 b}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}=\frac{3}{4}$
Let the mid-point of $A B$ be $(h, k)$
$\therefore \quad h=\frac{a+0}{2}, k=\frac{0+b}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=2 h, b=2 k$
$\therefore$ From Eq. $(\mathrm{iv}), \frac{1}{2 h}+\frac{1}{2 k}=\frac{3}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 h+2 k=3 h k$
Replace $x, y$ from $h, k$ respectively, we get required locus
i.e. $2(x+y)=3 x y$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Intercepts of the plane $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{n} = d\,(\neq 0)$ on the coordinate axes respectively are
questions.solution:
The equation of the plane is given by $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{n} = d$.

For the intercept on the x-axis, the position vector of the point is $\vec{r} = x\hat{i}$.

Substituting this into the equation of the plane, we get:

$(x\hat{i}) \cdot \vec{n} = d$

$x(\hat{i} \cdot \vec{n}) = d$

$x = \dfrac{d}{\hat{i} \cdot \vec{n}}$

Similarly, for the intercept on the y-axis, the position vector is $\vec{r} = y\hat{j}$. Substituting this gives:

$(y\hat{j}) \cdot \vec{n} = d \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{d}{\hat{j} \cdot \vec{n}}$

For the intercept on the z-axis, the position vector is $\vec{r} = z\hat{k}$. Substituting this gives:

$(z\hat{k}) \cdot \vec{n} = d \Rightarrow z = \dfrac{d}{\hat{k} \cdot \vec{n}}$

Thus, the intercepts on the coordinate axes are $\dfrac{d}{\hat{i} \cdot \vec{n}}, \dfrac{d}{\hat{j} \cdot \vec{n}}, \dfrac{d}{\hat{k} \cdot \vec{n}}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{d}{\hat{i} \cdot \vec{n}}, \dfrac{d}{\hat{j} \cdot \vec{n}}, \dfrac{d}{\hat{k} \cdot \vec{n}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider a square ABCD of diagonal length $2a$. The square is folded along the diagonal AC so that the plane of $\triangle$ABC is perpendicular to the plane of $\triangle$ADC. In this case the shortest distance between AB and CD is
questions.solution:
Let the center of the square be the origin $O(0,0,0)$. The diagonal $AC$ lies along the x-axis. Since the diagonal length is $2a$, the coordinates of the vertices are $A(-a, 0, 0)$ and $C(a, 0, 0)$.

Before folding, the square lies in a single plane, and the other diagonal $BD$ is perpendicular to $AC$. After folding along $AC$, the plane of $\triangle ABC$ and the plane of $\triangle ADC$ are perpendicular. We can assume the plane of $\triangle ABC$ is the xy-plane and the plane of $\triangle ADC$ is the xz-plane.

The coordinates of the remaining vertices become $B(0, a, 0)$ and $D(0, 0, a)$.

The line $AB$ passes through $A(-a, 0, 0)$ with direction vector $\vec{v}_1 = \overrightarrow{AB} = (a, a, 0)$.

The line $CD$ passes through $C(a, 0, 0)$ with direction vector $\vec{v}_2 = \overrightarrow{CD} = (-a, 0, a)$.

The shortest distance $d$ between two skew lines passing through points with position vectors $\vec{a}_1$ and $\vec{a}_2$ and parallel to vectors $\vec{v}_1$ and $\vec{v}_2$ is given by:

$d = \dfrac{|(\vec{a}_2 - \vec{a}_1) \cdot (\vec{v}_1 \times \vec{v}_2)|}{|\vec{v}_1 \times \vec{v}_2|}$

Here, $\vec{a}_1 = (-a, 0, 0)$ and $\vec{a}_2 = (a, 0, 0)$, so $\vec{a}_2 - \vec{a}_1 = (2a, 0, 0)$.

Now, we find the cross product $\vec{v}_1 \times \vec{v}_2$:

$\vec{v}_1 \times \vec{v}_2 = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ a & a & 0 \\ -a & 0 & a \end{vmatrix} = a^2\hat{i} - a^2\hat{j} + a^2\hat{k} = a^2(1, -1, 1)$

The magnitude of the cross product is:

$|\vec{v}_1 \times \vec{v}_2| = a^2\sqrt{1^2 + (-1)^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{3}a^2$

The dot product is:

$(\vec{a}_2 - \vec{a}_1) \cdot (\vec{v}_1 \times \vec{v}_2) = (2a)(a^2) + (0)(-a^2) + (0)(a^2) = 2a^3$

Substituting these into the shortest distance formula, we get:

$d = \dfrac{2a^3}{\sqrt{3}a^2} = \dfrac{2a}{\sqrt{3}}$

Answer: $\dfrac{2a}{\sqrt{3}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The straight line $\frac{x-3}{3}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-1}{0}$ is
questions.solution:
The given equation of the straight line is:
\((x-3) / 3=(y-2) / 1=(z-1) / 0\)
The direction ratios of this line are 3,1, and 0. A line parallel to the \(z\)-axis has direction ratios 0,0, and 1.
If the dot product of the direction ratios of two lines is zero, then the lines are perpendicular.
Let's check the dot product of the direction ratios of the given line and a line parallel to the z-axis:
\(\left(3^* 0\right)+(1 * 0)+(0 * 1)=0\)
Since the dot product is zero, the given line is perpendicular to the z-axis.
Therefore, the correct answer is (4).

$\Rightarrow(2+\lambda) x-(1-\lambda) y+(3+\lambda) z+5-\lambda=0$ this will be perpendicular to the plane $2 x-y+3 z+5=0$
$\Rightarrow(2+\lambda) 2+(1-\lambda) \times 1+3(3+\lambda)=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-7}{2}$
$\therefore$ Equation of plane $3 x+9 y+z=17$

\(\therefore \frac{|k|}{\sqrt{6}}=\sqrt{6} \Rightarrow|k|=6\)

questions.solution:
Solving the \(3 \mathrm{eq}^n\) s will give as the required point of intersection
\(\begin{aligned} & x-2 y+4 z=-4 \\ & x+y+z=8 \\ & x-y+2 z=-1 \\ & D=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=1(3)+2(1)+4(-2)=-3 \\ & \begin{aligned} D_1=\left|\begin{array}{rrr} -4 & -2 & 4 \\ 8 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|= & -4(3)+2(17)+4(-7) \\ & =-6 \end{aligned} \\ & D_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -4 & 4 \\ 1 & 8 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=-15 \\ & D_3=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & -4 \\ 1 & 1 & 8 \\ 1 & -1 & -1 \end{array}\right|=-3 \\ & x=\frac{D_1}{D}=2, \quad y=\frac{D_2}{D}=5, \quad z=\frac{D_3}{D}=1 \\ & \end{aligned}\)
\((2, S, 1)\) is the required point.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the plane through the intersection of the planes $x+y+z=1$ and $2 x+3 y-z+4$ and parallel to the $x$-axis is
questions.solution:
$\quad(2 x+3 y-z+4)+\lambda(x + y+z-1)=0$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}=(2+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(3+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(-1+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}-3 z+6=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A line with positive direction cosines passes through the point $\mathrm{P}(2,-1,2)$ and makes equal angle with co-ordinate axes. The line meets the plane $2 x+y+z=9$ at point $Q$. The length of the line segment $P Q$ equals.
questions.solution:
Let d.c. of line is $(\mathrm{k}, \mathrm{k}, \mathrm{k})$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{k}^{2}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\Rightarrow \text { d.c. of line is }\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)
\end{array}$
Equation of a line passing through point $p$ is
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x-2}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{y+1}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{z-2}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=k \\
\Rightarrow x=k+2, y=k-1, z=k+2 \\
\therefore 2(k+2)+(k-1)+(k+2)=9 \\
4 k=4 \Rightarrow k=1
\end{array}$
$\therefore$ Point $\mathrm{Q}$ is $(3,0,3)$
$P Q=\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{3}$ units.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If from a point $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$, perpendiculars $\mathrm{PA}$ and $\mathrm{PB}$ are drawn to $\mathrm{YZ}$ and $\mathrm{ZX}$ planes respectively, then the equation of the plane $\mathrm{OAB}$ is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{o}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}) \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{o}, \mathrm{c}), \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{o}, \mathrm{o}, \mathrm{o})$ Perpendicular vector is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}$ $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & b & c \\ a & 0 & c\end{array}\right|=b c \hat{i}+a c \hat{j}-a b \hat{k}$ $\therefore$ Equation of plane is $b c x+a c y-a b z=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The plane $\ell \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{my}=0$ is rotated about its line of intersection with the plane $\mathrm{z}=0$ through an angle $\alpha$. The equation changes to
questions.solution:
$P_{1}: \ell x+m y=0, \quad P_{2}=z=0$
Plane through common line of $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ 
$\mathrm{P}_{3}: \ell \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{my}+\mathrm{nz}=0$
angle between $P_{1}$ and $P_{3}=\alpha$ 
$\therefore \cos \alpha=\hat{n}_{1}, \hat{n}_{2}$
$=\frac{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}}{\sqrt{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}} \sqrt{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}+\mathrm{n}^{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}}{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}+\mathrm{n}^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \cos ^{2} \alpha=\frac{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}}{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}+\mathrm{n}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^{2}=\left(\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}\right) \tan ^{2} \alpha$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\pm \sqrt{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}} \tan \alpha$
$\mathrm{P}_{3}: \ell \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{my} \pm \sqrt{\ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}}$ tan $\alpha=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the plane through the point $(2,-1,-3)$ and parallel to the lines $\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z}{-4}$ and $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-1}{-3}=\frac{z-2}{2}$ is
questions.solution:
The equation of plane passing through \((2,-1,-3)\) is \(a(x-2)+b(y+1)+c(z+3)=0\) Also, as the plane is parallel is the given two lines,
\(\therefore 3 a+2 b-4 c=0\) and \(2 a-3 b+2 c=0\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=-8, \mathrm{~b}=-14, \mathrm{c}=-13\)
\(\therefore\) The equation of the required plane is
\(-8(x-2)-14(y+1)-13(z+3)=0\)
\(\Rightarrow 8 x+14 y+13 z+37=0\)
So the answer is (a).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The sine of the angle between the straight line $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-4}{5}$ and the plane $2 x-2 y+z=5$ is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Let the angle be $\theta$. Then, $\cos \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{|3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}| \times|2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}|} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{3}{\sqrt{50} \sqrt{9}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{10}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The direction ratios of the normal to the plane passing through the points (1,2,-3) ,(-1,-2,1) and parallel to $\frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z}{4}$ is
questions.solution:
Direction ratios of line joining the points (1,2,-3) and (-1,-2,1) is $(-1-1,-2-2,1+3)$
i.e. (-2,-4,4)
Let the direction ratios of the normal to the plane is $(a, b, c),$ then
$$
\begin{array}{r}
2 a+3 b+4 c=0 ....(i)\\
-2 a-4 b+4 c=0 ....(ii)
\end{array}
$$
and
By Eqs.
(i) and (ii), we get
$\begin{aligned} \frac{a}{3(4)-(4)(-4)} &=\frac{b}{(-2)(4)-(2)(4)} \\ &=\frac{c}{(2)(-4)-(-2)(3)} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{a}{12+16} &=\frac{b}{-8-8}=\frac{c}{-8+6} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{a}{28} &=\frac{b}{-16}=\frac{c}{-2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{a}{14} &=\frac{b}{-8}=\frac{c}{-1} \end{aligned}$
Hence, direction ratios are (14,-8,-1)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the plane, which bisects the line joining the points (1,2,3) and (3,4,5) at right angles is
questions.solution:
The given points are $A(1,2,3)$ and $B(3,4,5)$ The direction ratios of line segment $A B$ is given by
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right),\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right),\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \\
(3-1),(4-2),(5-3)=(2,2,2)=(1,1,1)
\end{array}
$$
Since, the plane bisects $A B$ at right angles, $\mathbf{A B}$ is the normal to the plane. Which is n Therefore, $\mathbf{n}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$
Let $c$ be the mid-point of $A B$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2},\right.&\left.\frac{y_{1}+y_{2}}{2}, \frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{2}\right) \\
=& c\left(\frac{1+3}{2}, \frac{2+4}{2}, \frac{3+5}{2}\right) \\
=& c(2,3,4)
\end{aligned}
$$
Let this vector be $\mathbf{a}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$
Hence, the required equation of vector
$$
\{\mathbf{r}-(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \mathbf{k})\}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\mathbf{k})=0
$$
We know that, $\mathbf{r}=x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+z \hat{\mathbf{k}}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow\{(x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+z \hat{\mathbf{k}})-(2\hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}})\}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad(x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+z \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\
=(2\hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\
\Rightarrow x+y+z=2+3+4 \\
\Rightarrow x+y+z=9
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A point $P$ lies on a line through $Q(1,-2,3)$ and is parallel to the line $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z}{5},$ If $P$ lies on the plane $2 x+3 y-4 z+22=0,$ then segment PQ equals
questions.solution:
Equation of line through $Q(1,-2,3)$ and
parallel to the line $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z}{5}$ is $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+2}{4}=\frac{z-3}{5}=\lambda \quad$ (say)
since, point $P$ lies on above line.
$\therefore$
$$
P(\lambda+1,4 \lambda-2,5 \lambda+3)
$$
since, $P$ lies on the given plane. $\therefore \quad 2(\lambda+1)+3(4 \lambda-2)-4(5 \lambda+3)+22=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+2+12 \lambda-6-20 \lambda-12+22=0$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad-6 \lambda+6=0
$$
$\Rightarrow$
$\lambda=1$
$\therefore$
$P(2,2,8)$
$\therefore \quad P Q=\sqrt{(2-1)^{2}+(2+2)^{2}+(3-8)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad P Q=\sqrt{1+16+25}=\sqrt{42}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point (1,8,4) on the line joining the point (0,-11,4) and (2,-3,1) is
questions.solution: Equation of line joining points (0,-11,4) and (2,-3,1) is $$ \frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y+3}{8}=\frac{z-1}{-3}=\lambda $$ Let $P$ is any point of the above line then coordinate of $P$ is $(2 \lambda+2,8 \lambda-3,-3 \lambda+1)$ $\therefore \mathrm{DR}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}$ of $P Q$ is $(2 \lambda+1,8 \lambda-11,-3 \lambda-3$ Now, $(2 \lambda+1)(4+(8 \lambda-11)(8)+(-3 \lambda-3) (-3)=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad 4 \lambda+2+64 \lambda-88+9 \lambda+9=0$ $77 \lambda-77=0$ $\therefore$ Required foot of perpendicular. $$ P(4,5,-2) $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the plane through (1,2,-3) and (2,-2,1) and parallel to $X$ -axis is
questions.solution:
Equation of the plane will be $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x-1 & y-2 & z+3 \\ 2-1 & -2-2 & 1-(-3) \\ 1 & 0 & 0\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x-1 & y-2 & z+3 \\ 1 & -4 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 0\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1[4(y-2)+4(z+3)]=0$
[Expanding along $\left.R_{j}\right]$ 
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 y-8+4 z+12=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 y+4 z+4=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad y+z+1=0$

questions.q: Three lines are drawn from the origin $O$ with direction cosines proportional to (1,-1,1) (2,-3,0) and $(1,0,3) .$ The three lines are

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A straight line joining the points (1,1,1) and (0,0,0) intersects the plane $2 x+2 y+z=10$ at
questions.solution:
Equation of line joining the points (1,1,1) and (0,0,0) is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{x-0}{1-0} &=\frac{y-0}{1-0}=\frac{z-0}{1-0}=\lambda \\
\Rightarrow & x=y=z=\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$
So, the point is $(\lambda, \lambda, \lambda)$ The point intersects the plane $2 x+2 y+z=10$
$\therefore 2(\lambda)+2(\lambda)+\lambda=10$
$\Rightarrow 5 \lambda=10$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=2$
Hence, the point is (2,2,2) .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Angle between the planes $x+y+2 z=6$ and $2 x-y+z=9$ is
questions.solution:
Equations of planes are
$x+y+2 z-6=0$
and $\quad 2 x-y+z-9=0$
$\cos \theta=\frac{a_{1} a_{2}+b_{1}b_{2}+c
_{1}c_{2}}{\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+b_{1}^{2}+c_{1}^{2}} \sqrt{a_{2}^{2}+b_{2}^{2}+c_{2}^{2}}}$
$=\frac{1 \times 2+1 \times(-1)+2 \times 1}{\sqrt{1+1+4} \sqrt{4+1+1}}$
$=\frac{2-1+2}{\sqrt{6} \times \sqrt{6}}=\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$
$\therefore \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\lambda$ for which the straight line $\frac{x-\lambda}{3}=\frac{y-1}{2+\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{-1}$ may lie on the plane $x-2 y=0,$ is
questions.solution:
If a line $\frac{x-x_{1}}{a_{1}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{b_{1}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{c_{1}}$ lies
on a plane $a_{2} x+b_{2} y+c_{2} z=d,$ then
(i) $a_{1} a_{2}+b_{1} b_{2}+c_{1} c_{2}=0$
(ii) $a_{2} x_{1}+b_{2} y_{1}+c_{2} z_{1}=d$
According to the question. $3(1)+(2+\lambda)(-2)+(-1)(0)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3-4-2 \lambda=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-1 / 2$
Also, $\quad \lambda-2=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=2$
So. there is no such value of $\lambda$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of the equation $\sin^{100} x - \cos^{100} x = 1$ is
questions.solution:
The given equation is $\sin^{100} x - \cos^{100} x = 1$.

We know that for any real $x$, $\sin^{100} x \le 1$ and $\cos^{100} x \ge 0$.

Therefore, $\sin^{100} x - \cos^{100} x \le 1$.

The equality holds if and only if $\sin^{100} x = 1$ and $\cos^{100} x = 0$.

$\sin^{100} x = 1 \Rightarrow \sin^2 x = 1 \Rightarrow \cos^2 x = 0$.

This gives $\cos x = 0$.

The general solution for $\cos x = 0$ is $x = n\pi \pm \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, where $n \in I$.

Thus, the solution set is $\left\{n\pi \pm \dfrac{\pi}{2} : n \in I\right\}$.

Answer: $\left\{n\pi \pm \dfrac{\pi}{2} : n \in I\right\}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution set of the equation $\left(x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right) \tan (\pi \tan x)=\cot (\pi \cot x)$ is
questions.solution:
$\tan (\pi \tan x)=\cot (\pi \cot x)$
or, $\tan (\pi \tan x)=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\pi \cot x\right)$
or, $\quad \pi \tan x=\frac{\pi}{2}-\pi \cot x$
or, $\tan x=\frac{1}{2}-\cot x$
or, $\quad \tan x+\cot x=\frac{1}{2}$
or, $\quad \frac{\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x}{\sin x \cos x}=\frac{1}{2}$
or, $\quad \frac{1}{\sin 2 x}=\frac{1}{4}$
or, $\quad \sin 2 x=4 \quad x \in \phi$
