questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $0 \lt \theta \lt \frac{\pi}{2}$ and $\tan 3 \theta \neq 0$, then $\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta+\tan 3 \theta=0$ if $\tan \theta \cdot \tan 2 \theta=\mathrm{k}$ where $\mathrm{k}=$
questions.solution:
Hint: $\because 3 \theta=\theta+2 \theta$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \tan 3 \theta=\tan (\theta+2 \theta)=\frac{\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta}{1-\tan \theta \cdot \tan 2 \theta} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \tan 3 \theta=\frac{-\tan 3 \theta}{1-\mathrm{k}} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 1=\frac{-1}{1-\mathrm{k}} \quad[\because \tan 3 \theta \neq 0] \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 1-\mathrm{k}=-1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{k}=2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $A$ and $B$ are acute angles such that $\sin A=\sin ^2 B$ and $2 \cos ^2 A=3 \cos ^2 B$, then $(A, B)=$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Hint : } 2 \cos ^2 A=3\left(1-\sin ^2 B\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 2-2 \sin ^2 A=3-3 \sin A \quad\left(\because \sin ^2 B=\sin A\right)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sin A=1, \frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin B= \pm 1, \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \therefore(A, B) \equiv\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pm \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \text { or }\left(\frac{\pi}{6}, \pm \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\frac{1}{6} \sin \theta, \cos \theta, \tan \theta\) are in G.P., then the solution set of \(\theta\) is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text {Hint : } 6 \cos ^3 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta-1=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad(2 \cos \theta-1)\left(3 \cos ^2 \theta+2 \cos \theta+1\right)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2}\left(3 \cos ^2 \theta+2 \cos \theta+1 \neq 0\right)
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\sin 6 \theta+\sin 4 \theta+\sin 2 \theta=0$, then general
value of $\theta$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $\sin 6 \theta+\sin 4 \theta+\sin 2 \theta=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sin 6 \theta+\sin 2 \theta+\sin 4 \theta=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin 4 \theta \cdot \cos 2 \theta+\sin 4 \theta=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sin 4 \theta(2 \cos 2 \theta+1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sin 4 \theta=0$ or $2 \cos 2 \theta+1=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 \theta=n \pi$ or $\cos 2 \theta=\frac{-1}{2}=\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{n \pi}{4}$ or $2 \theta=2 n \pi \pm \frac{2 \pi}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{n \pi}{4}$ or $\theta=n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3},$ where $n \in z$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $\sin x(\sin x+\cos x)=k$ has real solutions, where k is a real number. Then,
questions.solution:
Let, $f(x)=\sin x(\sin x+\cos x)$
$=\sin ^{2} x+\sin x \cos x$
$=\frac{1-\cos 2 x}{2}+\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 x+\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x$
$=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}(\sin 2 x-\cos 2 x)$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Now, }-\sqrt{(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}} & \leq \sin 2 x-\cos 2 x \\ & \leq \sqrt{(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}} \end{aligned}$
$\left[\because \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}} \leq a \sin x+b \cos x \leq \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad-\sqrt{2} \leq \sin 2 x-\cos 2 x \leq \sqrt{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \leq \frac{\sin 2 x-\cos 2 x}{2} \leq \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \leq \frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sin 2 x-\cos 2 x}{2} \leq \frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1-\sqrt{2}}{2} \leq f(x) \leq \frac{1+\sqrt{2}}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1-\sqrt{2}}{2} \leq k \leq \frac{1+\sqrt{2}}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\cot \frac{2 x}{3}+\tan \frac{x}{3}=\operatorname{cosec} \frac{k x}{3},$ then the value of $k$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\cot \frac{2 x}{3}+\tan \frac{x}{3}=\operatorname{cosec} \frac{k x}{3}$
Let $\theta=\frac{x}{3},$ then
$\therefore \quad \cot 2 \theta+\tan \theta=\operatorname{cosec} k \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\cos 2 \theta}{\sin 2 \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}=\operatorname{cosec} k \theta$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}{2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}=\operatorname{cosec} k \theta$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1+2 \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}=\operatorname{cosec} k \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}=\operatorname{cosec} k \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad \operatorname{cosec} 2 \theta=\operatorname{cosec} k \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad k=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of real solutions of the equation $(\sin x-x)\left(\cos x-x^{2}\right)=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $(\sin x-x)\left(\cos x-x^{2}\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sin x=x$ or $\cos x=x^{2}$
Now, if $\sin x=x,$ then only one solution ie $x=0$ is possible. Also, if $\cos x=x^{2}$, then two solutions are possible. Hence, there are total 3 solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\{x \in R: |\cos x | \geq \sin x\} \cap\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]$ is equal to
questions.solution: Given, $\{x \in R:|\cos x| \geq \sin x\} \cap\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]$ If we draw the graphs of $|\cos x|$ and $\sin x,$ clearly $|\cos x| \geq \sin x$ when $x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right] \cup\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4} \cdot \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]$ $\therefore \quad x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right] \cup\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right] \cap\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]$ $\Rightarrow \quad x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right] \cup\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The trigonometric equation $\sin ^{-1} x=2 \sin ^{-1} 2 a$ has a real solution, if
questions.solution:
We know that, $-\frac{\pi}{2} \leq \sin ^{-1} x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-\pi}{2} \leq 2 \sin ^{-1} 2 a \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-\pi}{4} \leq \sin ^{-1} 2 a \leq \frac{\pi}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{-\pi}{4}\right) \leq 2 a \leq \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \leq 2 a \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \leq a \leq \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow|a| \leq \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be iwo distinct roots of $a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta=c,$ where $a, b, c$ are three
real constants and $0 \in|0,2 \pi|$. Then, $\alpha+\beta$ is also a root of the same equation, if
questions.solution:
Given equation is $a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta=c$
$\Rightarrow a\left(\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}\right)+\frac{2 b \tan \frac{\theta}{2}}{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}=c$
$\left[\because \cos \theta=\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}\right.$ and $\left.\sin \theta=\frac{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}}{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow a\left(1-\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right)+2 b \tan \frac{\theta}{2}=c\left(1+\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow a-\operatorname{atan}^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}+2 b \tan \frac{\theta}{2}-c-c \tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow(c+a) \tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}-2 b \tan \frac{\theta}{2}+(c-a)=0$
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of the equation $\therefore \alpha+\beta=\frac{2 b}{c+a}$ and $\alpha \beta=\frac{c-a}{c+a}$
Now, $\tan \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}=\frac{\frac{2 b}{c+a}}{1-\frac{c-a}{c+a}}$
$=\frac{\frac{c+a}{c+a-c+a}}{c+a}=\frac{b}{a}$
since, $\frac{b}{c}$ is a root of the equation. $\therefore \quad(c+a) \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}-2 b\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)+c-a=0$
$\Rightarrow b^{2} c+b^{2} a-2 b^{2} a+c a^{2}-a^{3}=0$
$\Rightarrow-b^{2} a+b^{2} c+c a^{2}-a^{3}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad b^{2} c-b^{2} a+c a^{2}-a^{3}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad b^{2}(c-a)+a^{2}(c-a)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(c-a)\left(b^{2}+a^{2}\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow c-a=0 \quad$ or $\quad b^{2}+a^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad c=a$ or $b^{2}+a^{2}=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which of the following statements is /are correct for $0    < \theta < \frac{\pi}{2} ?$
questions.solution:
$0    < 0 < \frac{\pi}{2}, cos \theta$ is monotonically decreasing function
Option
(a) When $\theta>\frac{\theta}{2},$ then $\cos \theta \leq \cos \frac{\theta}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \sqrt{\cos \theta} \leq \cos \frac{\theta}{2} \quad$ correct
(b) When $0>\frac{30}{4},$ then $\cos \theta \leq \cos \frac{30}{4}$
$\therefore \quad(\cos \theta)^{3 / 4} \leq \cos \frac{30}{4}$
incorrect|
(c) When $\theta>\frac{5 \theta}{6},$ then $\cos \theta \leq \cos \frac{50}{6}$
$\therefore \quad(\cos \theta)^{5 / 6} \leq \cos \frac{50}{6}$
[correct|
(d) When $\frac{7 \theta}{8} < \theta,$ then $\cos \theta \leq \cos \frac{7 \theta}{8}$
But $(\cos \theta)^{7 / 8} \leq \cos \frac{7 \theta}{8}$
|incorrect|

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\tan \frac{\pi}{5}+2 \tan \frac{2 \pi}{5}+4 \cot \frac{4 \pi}{5}$ is
questions.solution:
$\tan \frac{\pi}{5}+2 \tan \frac{2 \pi}{5}+4 \cot \frac{4 \pi}{5}$
$=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{5}}+2 \frac{\sin \frac{2 \pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{2 \pi}{5}}+4 \frac{\cos \frac{4 \pi}{5}}{\sin \frac{4 \pi}{5}}$
$=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{5}}+2 \frac{\sin \frac{2 \pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{2 \pi}{5}}+\frac{4\left(\cos ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{5}-\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{5}\right)}{2 \sin \frac{2 \pi}{5} \cos \frac{2 \pi}{5}}$
$=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{5}}+2 \frac{\sin \frac{2 \pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{2 \pi}{5}}+\frac{2\left(\cos ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{5}-\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{5}\right)}{\sin \frac{2 \pi}{5} \cos \frac{2 \pi}{5}}$
$=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{5}}+2\left[\frac{\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{5}+\cos ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{5}-\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{2 \pi}{5} \sin \frac{2 \pi}{5}}\right]$
$=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{5}}+\frac{2 \cos \frac{2 \pi}{5}}{\sin \frac{2 \pi}{5}}$
$=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{5}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{5}}+\frac{2\left(\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{5}-\sin ^{2} \frac{\pi}{5}\right)}{2 \sin \frac{\pi}{5} \cos \frac{\pi}{5}}$
$=\frac{\sin ^{2} \frac{\pi}{5}+\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{5}-\frac{\sin ^{2} \pi}{5}}{\sin \frac{\pi}{5} \cos \frac{\pi}{5}}$
$=\frac{\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{5}}{\sin \frac{\pi}{5} \cos \frac{\pi}{5}}$
$=\cot \frac{\pi}{5}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\cos \frac{2 \pi}{7}+\cos \frac{4 \pi}{7}+\cos \frac{6 \pi}{7}$
questions.solution:
Let $\frac{2 \pi r}{7}=\theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r=3 \theta+4 \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 \theta=2 \pi r-3 \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sin 4 \theta=\sin (2 \pi r-3 \theta)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sin 4 \theta=-\sin 3 \theta$
$\Rightarrow 2 \sin 2 \theta \cos 2 \theta=-\left[3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^{3} \theta\right.$
$\Rightarrow 2 \times 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta\left(2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1\right)$
$=-3 \sin \theta+4 \sin ^{3} \theta$
$\Rightarrow \sin \theta\left[8 \cos ^{3} \theta-4 \cos \theta+3-4\left(1-\cos ^{2} \theta\right)\right]=0$
$\Rightarrow 8 \cos ^{3} \theta+4 \cos ^{2} \theta-4 \cos \theta-1=0$ (i)
Thus, $\cos \frac{2 \pi}{7}, \cos \frac{4 \pi}{7}$ and $\cos \frac{6 \pi}{7}$ are the roots of
the above equation. $\therefore \quad \cos \frac{2 \pi}{7}+\cos \frac{4 \pi}{7}+\cos \frac{6 \pi}{7}=-\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Number of solutions of the equation $\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x, x \in[0, \pi]$ is
questions.solution:
$\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}+\frac{1}{\cos x}=2 \cos x$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sin x+1=2 \cos ^{2} x=2\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right)$
$=2-2 \sin ^{2} x$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin ^{2} x+\sin x-1=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin ^{2} x+2 \sin x-\sin x-1=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin x(\sin x+2)-1(\sin x+1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\sin x+2)(2 \sin x-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sin x=-2,$ which is not possible. or $\sin x=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}$
$x \in[0, \pi]$
$\therefore$ number of solution $=2$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\theta$ elimination from the equation $x^2 + y^2 = \dfrac{x\cos 3\theta + y\sin 3\theta}{\cos^3\theta} = \dfrac{y\cos 3\theta - x\sin 3\theta}{\sin^3\theta}$ will be
questions.solution:
Let $x^2 + y^2 = k$.

From the given condition, we can write the following two equations:
$x \cos 3\theta + y \sin 3\theta = k \cos^3 \theta$
$-x \sin 3\theta + y \cos 3\theta = k \sin^3 \theta$

Solving for $x$ and $y$ by multiplying the first equation by $\cos 3\theta$ and the second by $-\sin 3\theta$, then adding them:
$x(\cos^2 3\theta + \sin^2 3\theta) = k(\cos^3 \theta \cos 3\theta - \sin^3 \theta \sin 3\theta)$
$x = k(\cos^3 \theta \cos 3\theta - \sin^3 \theta \sin 3\theta)$

Similarly, multiplying the first equation by $\sin 3\theta$ and the second by $\cos 3\theta$, then adding them:
$y(\sin^2 3\theta + \cos^2 3\theta) = k(\cos^3 \theta \sin 3\theta + \sin^3 \theta \cos 3\theta)$
$y = k(\cos^3 \theta \sin 3\theta + \sin^3 \theta \cos 3\theta)$

Using the multiple angle formulas $\cos^3 \theta = \dfrac{\cos 3\theta + 3\cos \theta}{4}$ and $\sin^3 \theta = \dfrac{3\sin \theta - \sin 3\theta}{4}$, we substitute these into the expression for $x$:
$x = \dfrac{k}{4} [(\cos 3\theta + 3\cos \theta)\cos 3\theta - (3\sin \theta - \sin 3\theta)\sin 3\theta]$
$x = \dfrac{k}{4} [\cos^2 3\theta + 3\cos \theta \cos 3\theta - 3\sin \theta \sin 3\theta + \sin^2 3\theta]$
$x = \dfrac{k}{4} [1 + 3(\cos 3\theta \cos \theta - \sin 3\theta \sin \theta)]$
$x = \dfrac{k}{4} [1 + 3\cos 4\theta]$

Substituting into the expression for $y$:
$y = \dfrac{k}{4} [(\cos 3\theta + 3\cos \theta)\sin 3\theta + (3\sin \theta - \sin 3\theta)\cos 3\theta]$
$y = \dfrac{k}{4} [\cos 3\theta \sin 3\theta + 3\cos \theta \sin 3\theta + 3\sin \theta \cos 3\theta - \sin 3\theta \cos 3\theta]$
$y = \dfrac{k}{4} [3(\sin 3\theta \cos \theta + \cos 3\theta \sin \theta)]$
$y = \dfrac{k}{4} [3\sin 4\theta]$

From the simplified expressions for $x$ and $y$, we isolate the trigonometric terms containing $4\theta$:
$3\cos 4\theta = \dfrac{4x}{k} - 1 = \dfrac{4x - k}{k}$
$3\sin 4\theta = \dfrac{4y}{k}$

Squaring and adding these two equations to eliminate $\theta$:
$(3\cos 4\theta)^2 + (3\sin 4\theta)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4x - k}{k}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{4y}{k}\right)^2$
$9 = \dfrac{16x^2 - 8kx + k^2 + 16y^2}{k^2}$
$9k^2 = 16(x^2 + y^2) - 8kx + k^2$

Substitute back $x^2 + y^2 = k$:
$9k^2 = 16k - 8kx + k^2$
$8k^2 = 16k - 8kx$

Dividing by $8k$ (assuming $k \neq 0$):
$k = 2 - x$
$x^2 + y^2 + x - 2 = 0$

Evaluating option (2) which is $(x^2 + y^2 + 2x)(x^2 + y^2 - x) = 2y^2$:
Substituting $x^2 + y^2 = k$ gives $(k + 2x)(k - x) = 2y^2$
$k^2 + kx - 2x^2 = 2y^2$
$k^2 + kx = 2(x^2 + y^2) = 2k$
$k(k + x - 2) = 0$
Since $k \neq 0$, this simplifies exactly to $x^2 + y^2 + x - 2 = 0$.

Answer: $(x^2 + y^2 + 2x)(x^2 + y^2 - x) = 2y^2$

questions.q: If $\cos (\theta+\phi)=\frac{3}{5}$ and $\sin (\theta-\phi)=\frac{5}{13}, 0 \lt \theta, \phi \lt \frac{\pi}{4}$, then $\cot (2 \theta)$ has the value
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \tan 2 \theta=\tan ((\theta+\phi)+(\theta-\phi))\left[\begin{array}{l}\because \tan (\theta+\phi)=\frac{4}{3} \\ \tan (\theta-\phi)=\frac{5}{12}\end{array}\right] \\ & =\frac{\tan (\theta+\phi)+\tan (\theta-\phi)}{1-\tan (\theta+\phi) \tan (\theta-\phi)} \\ & =\frac{\frac{4}{3}+\frac{5}{12}}{1-\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{5}{12}}=\frac{16+5}{36-20}=\frac{21 \times 3}{16}=\frac{63}{16} \\ & \cot (2 \theta)=\frac{16}{63}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\theta \in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)$ then the value of $\sqrt{4 \cos ^{4} \theta+\sin ^{2} 2 \theta}+4 \cot \theta \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
$\sqrt{4 \cos ^{4} \theta+\sin ^{2} 2 \theta}+4 \cot \theta \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
$=\sqrt{4 \cos ^{4} \theta+(2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)^{2}} +2 \cot \theta\left[2 \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right]$
$=\sqrt{4 \cos ^{4} \theta+4 \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta} +2 \cot \theta\left[1+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right]$
$=\sqrt{4 \cos ^{2} \theta\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)}+2 \cot \theta(1+\sin \theta)$
$=|2 \cos \theta|+2 \cot \theta+2 \cos \theta$
$=-2 \cos \theta+2 \cot \theta+2 \cos \theta$
$\quad\left[\right.$ for $\left.\theta \in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right),|\cos \theta|=-\cos \theta\right]$
$=2 \cot \theta$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The sum of the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sin \left(\frac{n ! \pi}{720}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
We know that
$$
\begin{array}{c}
\sin n \pi=0, \forall n \in N \\
\therefore E=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sin \left(\frac{n ! \pi}{720}\right)=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{720}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{2 ! \pi}{720}\right) \\
+\sin \left(\frac{3 ! \pi}{720}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{4 ! \pi}{720}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{5 ! \pi}{720}\right) \\
+\sin \left(\frac{6 ! \pi}{720}\right)+\ldots+\sin \left(\frac{720 ! \pi}{720}\right) \\
+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{720}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{320}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{120}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30}\right) \\
\end{array}
$$
Here, we see that after five terms of the above series all angles are multiple of $\pi,$ i.e., $\sin n \pi$, so all further values are zero.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad E=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{720}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{320}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{120}\right) \\
&+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
For $0 \leq P, Q \leq \frac{\pi}{2},$ if $\sin P+\cos Q=2$, then the
value of $\tan \left(\frac{\vec{P}+Q}{2}\right)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $0 \leq P, Q \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$
and $\sin P+\cos Q=2$
This equation hold only when, $\quad P=\frac{\pi}{2}$
and $\quad Q=0$
$\mathrm{LHS}=\sin P+\cos Q=\sin \frac{\pi}{2}+\cos 0$
$$
=1+1=2=\mathrm{RHS}
$$
$\therefore \quad \tan \left(\frac{P+Q}{2}\right)=\tan \left(\frac{\frac{\pi}{2}+0}{2}\right)=\tan \frac{\pi}{4}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\cos ^{2} 75^{\circ}+\cos ^{2} 45^{\circ}+\cos ^{2} 15^{\circ}$ $-\cos ^{2} 30^{\circ}-\cos ^{2} 60^{\circ}$ is
questions.solution:
Given $\cos ^{2} 75^{\circ}+\cos ^{2} 45^{\circ}+\cos ^{2} 15^{\circ}-\cos ^{2} 30^{\circ}\cos^260^\circ$
$=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{2}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}$ $-\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}$
$=\left(\frac{3+1-2 \sqrt{3}}{8}\right)+\frac{1}{2}+\left(\frac{3+1+2 \sqrt{3}}{8}\right)-\frac{3}{4}-\frac{1}{4}$
$=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}-\frac{3}{4}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{2}-1=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The maximum and minimum values of $\cos ^{6} \theta+\sin ^{6} \theta$ are respectively
questions.solution:
Let $f(\theta)=\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad f(\theta)=\left(\sin ^{2} \theta\right)^{3}+\left(\cos ^{2} \theta\right)^{3}$
$=\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)$
$\left(\sin ^{4} \theta+\cos ^{4} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta-\cos ^{2} \theta\right)$
$\left[\because a^{3}+b^{3}=(a+b)\left(a^{2}+b^{2}-a b\right]\right.$
$=1 \cdot\left\{\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)^{2}-3 \sin ^{2} \theta \cdot \cos ^{2} \theta\right\}$
$=1 \cdot\left(1-\frac{3}{4} \cdot 4 \sin ^{2} \theta \cdot \cos ^{2} \theta\right)$
$=1-\frac{3}{4}(\sin 2 \theta)^{2}(\because \sin 2 A=2 \sin A \cos A)$
$=1-\frac{3}{8}(1-\cos 4 \theta)$
$=1-\frac{3}{8}+\frac{3}{8} \cos 4 \theta$
$f(\theta)=\frac{5}{8}+\frac{3}{8} \cdot \cos 4 \theta$
$-1 \leq \cos 4 \theta \leq 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{-3}{8} \leq \frac{3}{8} \cos 4 \theta \leq \frac{3}{8}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{5}{8}-\frac{3}{8} \leq \frac{5}{8}+\frac{3}{8} \cos 48 \leq \frac{5}{8}+\frac{3}{8}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{4} \leq f(\theta) \leq 1$
So, the maximum value is 1 and minimum value is $\frac{1}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f(\theta)=\left(1+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)\left(2-\sin ^{2} \theta\right) .$ Then, for all
values of $\theta$
questions.solution:
Given
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\theta)=\left(1+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)\left(2-\sin ^{2} \theta\right) \\
&=2+2 \sin ^{2} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta-\sin ^{4} \theta \\
&=-\sin ^{4} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta+2 \\
&=-\left(\sin ^{4} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta-2\right) \\
&=-\left\{\sin ^{4} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta+\frac{1}{4}-\frac{9}{4}\right\} \\
&=+\frac{9}{4}-\left(\sin ^{2} \theta-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \\
\because \quad-1 \leq \sin \theta \leq 1 \Rightarrow 0 \leq \sin ^{2} \theta \leq 1\\
\Rightarrow & 0 \geq-\left(\sin ^{2} \theta-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \geq-\frac{1}{4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \quad-\frac{1}{2} \leq \sin ^{2} \theta-\frac{1}{2} \leq \frac{1}{2}\\
\Rightarrow \quad 0 \leq\left(\sin ^{2} \theta-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \leq \frac{1}{4}
\Rightarrow & \frac{9}{4} \geq \frac{9}{4}-\left(\sin ^{2} \theta-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \geq \frac{9}{4}-\frac{1}{4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad 2 \leq f(\theta) \leq \frac{9}{4} & \quad[\text { from Eq. }(0)]
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\sin ^{2} \theta+3 \cos \theta=2,$ then $\cos ^{3} \theta+\sec ^{3} \theta$ is equal out to
questions.solution:
Given, $\sin ^{2} \theta+3 \cos \theta=2$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow & 1-\cos ^{2} \theta+3 \cos \theta=2 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos ^{2} \theta-3 \cos \theta+1=0\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{9-4}}{2}$ (by quadratic formula)
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$
Here, $\cos \theta=\frac{3-\sqrt{5}}{2}$
$\left(\because \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} > 1 \text{ and } -1 \leq \cos \theta \leq 1\right)$
and $\sec \theta=\frac{2}{3-\sqrt{5}} \times \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{3-\sqrt{5}}$
$=\frac{2(3+\sqrt{5})}{4}=\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$
Now, $\cos ^{3} \theta+\sec ^{3} \theta=(\cos \theta+\sec \theta)$
$\quad\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sec ^{2} \theta-\cos \theta \cdot \sec \theta\right)$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\left(\frac{3-\sqrt{5}+3+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right) \\
\quad \quad\left\{(\cos \theta+\sec \theta)^{2}-3 \cos \theta \cdot \sec \theta\right\} \\
=3 \cdot\left\{(3)^{2}-3\right\}=3(9-3)=3 \times 6=18
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A vector given by $\vec{P} = f(t)\hat{i} + g(t)\hat{j} + \hat{k}$ moves in such a way that it is always parallel to the vector $\vec{Q} = -f''(t)\hat{i} + f'(t)\hat{j} + \hat{k}$. The magnitude of $\vec{P}$ is
questions.solution:
Since $\vec{P}$ is parallel to $\vec{Q}$, their corresponding components must be proportional.

$\dfrac{f(t)}{-f''(t)} = \dfrac{g(t)}{f'(t)} = \dfrac{1}{1}$

From the above, we get $f(t) = -f''(t)$ and $g(t) = f'(t)$.

The equation $f''(t) + f(t) = 0$ is a linear second-order differential equation with the general solution:

$f(t) = A \sin t + B \cos t$

Using $g(t) = f'(t)$, we get:

$g(t) = A \cos t - B \sin t$

The magnitude of $\vec{P}$ is given by:

$|\vec{P}| = \sqrt{f(t)^2 + g(t)^2 + 1}$

Substituting the expressions for $f(t)$ and $g(t)$:

$f(t)^2 + g(t)^2 = (A \sin t + B \cos t)^2 + (A \cos t - B \sin t)^2$

$f(t)^2 + g(t)^2 = A^2 \sin^2 t + B^2 \cos^2 t + 2AB \sin t \cos t + A^2 \cos^2 t + B^2 \sin^2 t - 2AB \sin t \cos t$

$f(t)^2 + g(t)^2 = A^2(\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t) + B^2(\cos^2 t + \sin^2 t) = A^2 + B^2$

Therefore, $|\vec{P}| = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 1}$, which is independent of time $t$ and hence a constant.

Answer: constant

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The position vectors of two adjacent sides $\overrightarrow{OA}$ and $\overrightarrow{OB}$ of a rectangle OACB are $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ respectively, where O is the origin. If $16|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| = 3(|\vec{a}| + |\vec{b}|)^2$ and $\theta$ be the acute angle between the diagonals OC and AB, then the value of $\tan\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Let $|\vec{a}| = x$ and $|\vec{b}| = y$. Since OACB is a rectangle, the adjacent sides $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are perpendicular. Thus, $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$ and $|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| = xy$.

Given $16|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| = 3(|\vec{a}| + |\vec{b}|)^2$, we substitute the magnitudes:

$16xy = 3(x+y)^2$

$16xy = 3x^2 + 6xy + 3y^2$

$3x^2 - 10xy + 3y^2 = 0$

$(3x - y)(x - 3y) = 0 \Rightarrow y = 3x$ or $x = 3y$.

The diagonals of the rectangle are given by the vectors $\overrightarrow{OC} = \vec{a} + \vec{b}$ and $\overrightarrow{AB} = \vec{b} - \vec{a}$.

Let $\theta$ be the acute angle between the diagonals. The cosine of this angle is:

$\cos \theta = \left| \dfrac{\overrightarrow{OC} \cdot \overrightarrow{AB}}{|\overrightarrow{OC}| |\overrightarrow{AB}|} \right| = \left| \dfrac{(\vec{b} + \vec{a}) \cdot (\vec{b} - \vec{a})}{|\vec{b} + \vec{a}| |\vec{b} - \vec{a}|} \right|$

$\cos \theta = \left| \dfrac{|\vec{b}|^2 - |\vec{a}|^2}{\sqrt{|\vec{b}|^2 + |\vec{a}|^2} \sqrt{|\vec{b}|^2 + |\vec{a}|^2}} \right| = \left| \dfrac{y^2 - x^2}{y^2 + x^2} \right|$

Substituting $y = 3x$ (the result is the same for $x = 3y$):

$\cos \theta = \left| \dfrac{9x^2 - x^2}{9x^2 + x^2} \right| = \dfrac{8x^2}{10x^2} = \dfrac{4}{5}$

Using the half-angle formula for tangent:

$\tan\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 - \cos \theta}{1 + \cos \theta}}$

$\tan\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 - 4/5}{1 + 4/5}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1/5}{9/5}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{9}} = \dfrac{1}{3}$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The point of intersection of $\vec{r} \times \vec{a} = \vec{b} \times \vec{a}$ and $\vec{r} \times \vec{b} = \vec{a} \times \vec{b}$, where $\vec{a} = \hat{i} + \hat{j}$ and $\vec{b} = 2\hat{i} - \hat{k}$ is
questions.solution:
Given equations are $\vec{r} \times \vec{a} = \vec{b} \times \vec{a}$ and $\vec{r} \times \vec{b} = \vec{a} \times \vec{b}$.

From the first equation:
$(\vec{r} - \vec{b}) \times \vec{a} = \vec{0}$
$\Rightarrow \vec{r} - \vec{b} = \lambda \vec{a}$
$\Rightarrow \vec{r} = \vec{b} + \lambda \vec{a}$

From the second equation:
$(\vec{r} - \vec{a}) \times \vec{b} = \vec{0}$
$\Rightarrow \vec{r} - \vec{a} = \mu \vec{b}$
$\Rightarrow \vec{r} = \vec{a} + \mu \vec{b}$

Equating the two expressions for $\vec{r}$:
$\vec{b} + \lambda \vec{a} = \vec{a} + \mu \vec{b}$
$\Rightarrow (\lambda - 1)\vec{a} = (\mu - 1)\vec{b}$

Since $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are non-collinear vectors, the coefficients must be zero:
$\lambda - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$
$\mu - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \mu = 1$

Therefore, $\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \vec{b}$.

Substituting the given vectors $\vec{a} = \hat{i} + \hat{j}$ and $\vec{b} = 2\hat{i} - \hat{k}$:
$\vec{r} = (\hat{i} + \hat{j}) + (2\hat{i} - \hat{k}) = 3\hat{i} + \hat{j} - \hat{k}$

Answer: $3\hat{i} + \hat{j} - \hat{k}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{a} = \hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}, \vec{b} = \hat{i} - \hat{j} + \hat{k}, \vec{c} = \hat{i} + 2\hat{j} - \hat{k}$, then the value of $\begin{vmatrix}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a} & \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} & \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c} \\ \vec{b} \cdot \vec{a} & \vec{b} \cdot \vec{b} & \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c} \\ \vec{c} \cdot \vec{a} & \vec{c} \cdot \vec{b} & \vec{c} \cdot \vec{c}\end{vmatrix}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
The given determinant is the Gram determinant of vectors $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$, which is equal to the square of their scalar triple product, $[\vec{a} \ \vec{b} \ \vec{c}]^2$.

The scalar triple product is given by:

$[\vec{a} \ \vec{b} \ \vec{c}] = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \end{vmatrix}$

Expanding the determinant along the first row:

$= 1(1 - 2) - 1(-1 - 1) + 1(2 + 1)$

$= -1 + 2 + 3 = 4$

The value of the given determinant is $[\vec{a} \ \vec{b} \ \vec{c}]^2 = 4^2 = 16$.

Answer: $16$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\vec{a} = (x, y, z)$ be the vector with $|\vec{a}| = 2\sqrt{3}$, which makes equal angles with the vector $\vec{b} = (y, -2z, 3x)$ and $\vec{c} = (2z, 3x, -y)$ and is perpendicular to the vector $\vec{d} = (1, -1, 2)$. If the angle between $\vec{a}$ and the unit vector $\hat{j}$ is obtuse, then $\vec{a}$ is
questions.solution:
Given $\vec{a} = (x, y, z)$ and $|\vec{a}| = 2\sqrt{3}$, we have $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 12$.

Since $\vec{a}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{d} = (1, -1, 2)$, we have $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d} = 0 \Rightarrow x - y + 2z = 0 \Rightarrow x - y = -2z$.

The angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\hat{j}$ is obtuse, so $\vec{a} \cdot \hat{j} < 0 \Rightarrow y < 0$.

The vector $\vec{a}$ makes equal angles with $\vec{b} = (y, -2z, 3x)$ and $\vec{c} = (2z, 3x, -y)$.

Notice that $|\vec{b}|^2 = y^2 + 4z^2 + 9x^2$ and $|\vec{c}|^2 = 4z^2 + 9x^2 + y^2$, so $|\vec{b}| = |\vec{c}|$.

Thus, $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}$.

$x(y) + y(-2z) + z(3x) = x(2z) + y(3x) + z(-y)$

$xy - 2yz + 3zx = 2zx + 3xy - yz$

$zx - yz - 2xy = 0 \Rightarrow z(x - y) - 2xy = 0$

Substituting $x - y = -2z$ into the above equation:

$z(-2z) - 2xy = 0 \Rightarrow xy = -z^2$

Squaring $x - y = -2z$, we get:

$x^2 + y^2 - 2xy = 4z^2$

Substituting $xy = -z^2$ gives:

$x^2 + y^2 + 2z^2 = 4z^2 \Rightarrow x^2 + y^2 = 2z^2$

Using $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 12$, we get:

$2z^2 + z^2 = 12 \Rightarrow 3z^2 = 12 \Rightarrow z^2 = 4 \Rightarrow z = \pm 2$

For $z = -2$:

$xy = -4$ and $x - y = 4 \Rightarrow x = y + 4$

$(y + 4)y = -4 \Rightarrow y^2 + 4y + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow (y + 2)^2 = 0 \Rightarrow y = -2$

Since $y = -2 < 0$, this is valid. Then $x = 2$. The vector is $(2, -2, -2)$.

For $z = 2$:

$xy = -4$ and $x - y = -4 \Rightarrow x = y - 4$

$(y - 4)y = -4 \Rightarrow y^2 - 4y + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow (y - 2)^2 = 0 \Rightarrow y = 2$

Since $y = 2 > 0$, this is rejected.

Therefore, $\vec{a} = (2, -2, -2)$.

Answer: $(2, -2, -2)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\vec{r} = \sin x(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) + \cos y(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) + 2(\vec{c} \times \vec{a})$, where $\vec{a}, \vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ are three non-coplanar vectors. It is given that $\vec{r}$ is perpendicular to $(\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c})$. Then the possible value(s) of $(x^2 + y^2)$ is/are
questions.solution:
Given that $\vec{r}$ is perpendicular to $(\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c})$, we have:

$\vec{r} \cdot (\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c}) = 0$

Substituting the expression for $\vec{r}$:

$(\sin x(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) + \cos y(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) + 2(\vec{c} \times \vec{a})) \cdot (\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c}) = 0$

Using the property of the scalar triple product, $(\vec{u} \times \vec{v}) \cdot \vec{w} = [\vec{u} \vec{v} \vec{w}]$, and knowing that the scalar triple product is zero if any two vectors are identical, the expansion simplifies to:

$\sin x [\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}] + \cos y [\vec{b} \vec{c} \vec{a}] + 2 [\vec{c} \vec{a} \vec{b}] = 0$

Since $[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}] = [\vec{b} \vec{c} \vec{a}] = [\vec{c} \vec{a} \vec{b}]$ and the vectors $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ are non-coplanar (so $[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}] \neq 0$), we can divide by $[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}]$ to get:

$\sin x + \cos y + 2 = 0$

$\Rightarrow \sin x + \cos y = -2$

Since the minimum value of both $\sin x$ and $\cos y$ is $-1$, the only possible case is:

$\sin x = -1 \quad \text{and} \quad \cos y = -1$

The general solutions are:

$x = (4k - 1)\dfrac{\pi}{2} \quad \text{for any integer } k$

$y = (2m + 1)\pi \quad \text{for any integer } m$

Squaring both gives:

$x^2 = (4k - 1)^2 \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \in \left\{ \dfrac{\pi^2}{4}, \dfrac{9\pi^2}{4}, \dfrac{25\pi^2}{4}, \dots \right\}$

$y^2 = (2m + 1)^2 \pi^2 \in \{ \pi^2, 9\pi^2, 25\pi^2, \dots \}$

Now we check the possible values for $x^2 + y^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \left[ (4k - 1)^2 + 4(2m + 1)^2 \right]$:

For $k = 0, m = 0$:
$x^2 + y^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} + \pi^2 = \dfrac{5\pi^2}{4}$ (Matches Option A)

For $k = 0, m = 1$ or $m = -2$:
$x^2 + y^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} + 9\pi^2 = \dfrac{37\pi^2}{4}$ (Matches Option C)

Options B and D cannot be expressed in the form $\dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \left[ (4k - 1)^2 + 4(2m + 1)^2 \right]$ for any integers $k$ and $m$.

Answer: $\dfrac{5\pi^2}{4}$; $\dfrac{37\pi^2}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\vec{a}, \vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ be vectors of equal magnitude such that the angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ is $\alpha, \vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ is $\beta$ and $\vec{c}$ and $\vec{a}$ is $\gamma$. Then the minimum value of $\cos \alpha+\cos \beta+\cos \gamma$ is
questions.solution:
Let $|\mathrm{a}|=|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=\lambda$
We have, $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\lambda^2 \cos \alpha, \quad \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\lambda^2 \cos \beta, \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\lambda^2 \cos \gamma$
Now, $|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}| \geq 0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 3 \lambda^2+2 \lambda^2(\cos \alpha+\cos \beta+\cos \gamma) \geq 0 \\
& \Rightarrow \cos \alpha+\cos \beta+\cos \gamma \geq-3 / 2
\end{aligned}$

questions.q: Let $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ be unit vectors. Suppose $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=0$ and the angle between $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ is $\frac{\pi}{6}$. Then $\vec{a}$ is

$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \perp \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ & $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \perp \overline{\mathrm{c}}$

$\begin{aligned}

questions.q: If ' $\theta$ ' is the angle between two vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ such that $|\vec{a}|=7,|\vec{b}|=1$ and $|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|^2=k^2-(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^2$, then the values of $k$ and $\theta$ are

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A unit vector in $X Y$-plane making an angle $45^{\circ}$ with $\hat{i}+\hat{j}$ and an angle $60^{\circ}$ with $3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}$ is
questions.solution:
Hint: Let the vector be $x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}=\vec{r} \quad \vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$ and $\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \vec{r} \cdot \vec{a}=|\vec{r}||\vec{a}| \cos 45^{\circ}=1 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=1 \quad \Rightarrow x+y=1 \cdots-\cdots(1) \\
& \vec{r} \cdot \vec{b}=|\vec{r}||\vec{b}| \cos 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow 3 x-4 y=1 \cdot 5 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{2}{5}(3 x-4 y)=1 \Rightarrow 6 x-8 y=5 ...(2)
\end{aligned}$
Solving (1) and (2), $14 \mathrm{x}=13 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{13}{14} \quad \therefore \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{14}$
$\therefore \quad$ Unit vector $=\frac{13}{14} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{14} \hat{j}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of ' \(a\) ' for which the scalar triple product formed by the vectors \(\vec{a}=\hat{i}+a \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{\beta}=\hat{j}+a \hat{k}\) and \(\vec{\gamma}=a \hat{i}+\hat{k}\) is maximum, is
questions.solution:
Hint : \(\Delta=1+\mathrm{a}^3-\mathrm{a}\) For \(\max ; \frac{\mathrm{d} \Delta}{\mathrm{da}}=0\)
\(\frac{\mathrm{d} \Delta}{\mathrm{da}}=3 \mathrm{a}^2-1 \quad \mathrm{a}= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\)
\(\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \Delta}{\mathrm{da}^2}=6 \mathrm{a}  < 0\) for \(\mathrm{a}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the volume of the parallelopiped with \(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}, \vec{b} \times \vec{c}\) and \(\vec{c} \times \vec{a}\) as coterminous edges is 9 cu. units., then the volume of the parallelopiped with \((\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}),(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) \times(\vec{c} \times \vec{a})\) and \((\vec{c} \times \vec{a}) \times(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})\) as coterminous edges is
questions.solution:
Hint : Volume of Parallelopiped whose coterminous edges are \(\vec{a}, \vec{b}\) and \(\vec{c}=[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}]\)
So, Given that

\(\therefore[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}]=3
\quad \ldots \ldots (II)\)
Now volume of parallelopiped whose coterminous edges are
\(\begin{aligned} & (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}),(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) \times(\vec{c} \times \vec{a}),(\vec{c} \times \vec{a}) \times(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \\ & {[(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) \times(\vec{c} \times \vec{a})(\vec{c} \times \vec{a}) \times(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})]} \\ & {[\vec{a} \times \vec{b} \quad \vec{b} \times \vec{c} \quad \vec{c} \times \vec{a}]^2}\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& =(9)^2 \quad \text{(from (1))}\\
& =81
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\vec{c}$ is unit vector perpendicular to $\vec{a}$ and coplanar with $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, then unit vector $\vec{d}$ perpendicular to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{c}$ is
questions.solution:
$\because \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$ is normal to the plane of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$ or $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\pm 2(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \therefore$ unit vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{\alpha}$ is a unit vector, $\vec{\beta}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\gamma}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$, then the maximum value of $[\vec{\alpha} \vec{\beta} \vec{\gamma}]$ is
questions.solution:
$[\vec{\alpha} \vec{\beta} \vec{\gamma}]=\vec{\alpha} \cdot(\vec{\beta} \times \vec{\gamma})=\vec{\alpha} \cdot\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & k \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=\vec{\alpha} \cdot(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$ is maximum $\Rightarrow$ angle between $\vec{\alpha} \& \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ will be 0
$\therefore[\vec{\alpha} \vec{\beta} \vec{\gamma}]=|\vec{\alpha}||\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}|=\sqrt{6}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\vec{\alpha}, \vec{\beta}, \vec{\gamma}$ be three non-zero vectors which are pairwise non-collinear. If $\vec{\alpha}+3 \vec{\beta}$ is collinear with $\vec{\gamma}$ and $\vec{\beta}+2 \vec{\gamma}$ is collinear with $\vec{\alpha}$, then $\vec{\alpha}+3 \vec{\beta}+6 \vec{\gamma}$ is
questions.solution:
$\vec{\alpha}+3 \vec{\beta}=\mathrm{k}_{1} \vec{\gamma} \Rightarrow \vec{\beta}=\frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{3} \vec{\gamma}-\frac{\vec{\alpha}}{3}$
$\vec{\beta}+2 \vec{\gamma}=\mathrm{k}_{2} \vec{\alpha} \Rightarrow \vec{\beta}=\mathrm{k}_{2} \vec{\alpha}-2 \vec{\gamma}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{k}_{1} \vec{\gamma}}{3}-\frac{\vec{\alpha}}{3}=\mathrm{k}_{2} \vec{\alpha}-2 \vec{\gamma} \Rightarrow \vec{\alpha}\left(\mathrm{k}_{2}+\frac{1}{3}\right)=\vec{\gamma}\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{3}+2\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_{2}=-\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{3}=-2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_{1}=-6$
$\Rightarrow \vec{\alpha}+3 \vec{\beta}+6 \vec{\gamma}=\overrightarrow{0}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{a}(\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{\beta})+\mathrm{b}(\vec{\beta} \times \vec{\gamma})+\mathrm{c}(\vec{\gamma} \times \vec{\alpha})=\overrightarrow{0}$, where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are non-zero scalars, then the vectors $\vec{\alpha}, \vec{\beta}, \vec{\gamma}$ are
questions.solution: $\therefore \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{\beta}, \vec{\beta} \times \vec{\lambda}, \vec{\gamma} \times \vec{\alpha}$ are coplanar $\Rightarrow \vec{\alpha}, \vec{\beta}, \vec{\gamma}$ are coplanar

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The unit vector in ZOX plane, making angles $45^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively with $\vec{\alpha}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $\vec{\beta}=j-\hat{k}$ is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Let the vector be $\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+z \hat{k} \Rightarrow|r|=1$
$\vec{r} \cdot \vec{a}=|r| |\vec{\alpha}| \cos 45^{\circ}$
$\therefore 2 x-z=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\vec{r} \cdot \vec{\beta}=|\vec{r}| |\vec{\beta}| \cos 60^{\circ}$
$z=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{k}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the vectors $\vec{\alpha}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+a \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\mathbf{a}^{2} \hat{\mathbf{k}}, \vec{\beta}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+b \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\mathbf{b}^{2} \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, and $\vec{\gamma}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+c \hat{\mathbf{j}}+c^{2} \hat{k}$ are three non-coplanar vectors and $\left|\begin{array}{lll}\mathbf{a} & \mathbf{a}^{2} & 1+\mathrm{a}^{3} \\ \mathbf{b} & \mathbf{b}^{2} & 1+\mathrm{b}^{3} \\ \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}^{2} & 1+\mathrm{c}^{3}\end{array}\right|=0$,
then the value of abc is
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$\left|\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a}^{2} & 1 \\ \mathrm{~b} & \mathrm{~b}^{2} & 1 \\ \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}^{2} & 1\end{array}\right|(1+\mathrm{abc})=0$
$a b c=-1[\because \vec{\alpha}, \vec{\beta}, \vec{\gamma}$ are non-coplanar vector $]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\hat{\alpha}, \hat{\beta}, \hat{\gamma}$ be three unit vectors such that $\hat{\alpha} \times (\hat{\beta} \times \hat{\gamma})=\frac{1}{2}(\hat{\beta}+\hat{\gamma})$ where $\hat{\alpha} \times(\hat{\beta} \times \hat{\gamma})=(\hat{\alpha} \cdot \hat{\gamma}) \hat{\beta}-(\hat{\alpha} \cdot \hat{\beta}) \gamma \cdot$ If $\hat{\beta}$ is not parallel to $\hat{\gamma},$ then the angle between $\hat{\alpha}$ and $\hat{\beta}$ is
questions.solution:
Given,
$|\hat{\alpha}|=|\hat{\beta}|=|\hat{\gamma}|=1$
and $\quad \hat{\alpha} \times(\hat{\beta} \times \hat{\gamma})=\frac{1}{2}(\hat{\beta}+\hat{\gamma})$
$\left.\operatorname{Now}, \hat{\alpha} \times(\hat{\beta} \times \hat{\gamma})=\frac{1}{2} \hat{(\hat{\beta}}+\hat{\gamma}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\hat{\alpha} \cdot \hat{\gamma}) \hat{\beta}-(\hat{\alpha} \cdot \hat{\beta}) \hat{\gamma}=\frac{1}{2} \hat{\beta}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{\gamma}$
On comparing we get, $-(\hat{\alpha} \cdot \hat{\beta})=\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow|\hat{\alpha}||\hat{\beta}| \cos \theta=-\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=-\frac{1}{2} \quad[\because|\hat{\alpha}|=|\hat{\beta}|=1]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \theta=120^{\circ}$ or $\frac{2 \pi}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The position vectors of the points $A, B, C$ and $D$ are $3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}, 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \mathbf{k}, \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $4 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\lambda \hat{\mathbf{k}},$ respectively. If the points $A, B, C$ and $D$ lie on a plane, the value of $\lambda$ is
questions.solution:
The position vectors of the points $A, B, C$ and $D$ are $3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}, 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}, 5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and
$4 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\lambda \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ respectively
$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{B A} &=(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\mathbf{k})-(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \mathbf{k})=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \mathbf{k} \\
\mathbf{C A} &=(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})-(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \mathbf{k})=-2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\
\mathbf{D A} &=(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})-(4 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\lambda \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\
&=-\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}-(1+\lambda) \mathbf{k}
\end{aligned}$
These are coplanar, so
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & -3 \\ -2 & -1 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 & -(1+\lambda)\end{array}\right|=0$
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & -1 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1+\lambda\end{array}\right|=0$
$-1[1+\lambda-3]+1[2+2 \lambda-3]+3[2-1]=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-1[\lambda-2]+1[2 \lambda-1]+3=0$
$\Rightarrow -\lambda+2+2 \lambda-1+3=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda+4=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=-4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $\vec{\alpha}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{\beta}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and
$\vec{\gamma}=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ be three vectors. A vector $\vec{\delta},$ in the plane of $\vec{\alpha}$ and $\vec{\beta}$, whose projection on $\vec{\gamma}$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}},$ is given by
questions.solution:
We have,
$\overrightarrow{8}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{\beta}$
$=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})$
$\Rightarrow \quad \vec{\delta}=(1+\lambda) \hat{i}+(1-\lambda) \hat{j}+(1-\lambda) \hat{k}$
Given, $\quad \frac{\vec{\delta} \cdot \vec{\gamma}}{|\vec{\gamma}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(1+\lambda)(-1)+(1-\lambda)(1)+(1-\lambda)(-1)}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-2$
$\therefore$
$\vec{\delta}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $\vec{\alpha}, \vec{\beta}, \vec{\gamma}$ be the three unit vectors such that $\vec{\alpha} \cdot \vec{\beta}=\vec{\alpha} \cdot \vec{\gamma}=0$ and the angle between $\vec{\beta}$ and $\vec{\gamma}$
is $30^{\circ} .$ Then $\vec{\alpha}$ is
questions.solution:
$\vec{\alpha}=\lambda(\vec{\beta} \times \vec{\gamma})=\lambda\left(|\vec{\beta}||\vec{\gamma}|\left|\sin 30^{\circ}\right|\right.$
$\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{\alpha}|=|\lambda|\left(|\beta||\vec{\gamma}| \cdot \frac{1}{2}\right)$
$\begin{array}{lc}\Rightarrow & 1=|\lambda| \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & |\lambda|=2 \\ \rightarrow & \lambda-\pm 2\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad \vec{\alpha}=\pm 2(\vec{\beta} \times \vec{\gamma})$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For any vector $\mathbf{x},$ where $\hat{\mathbf{i}}, \hat{\mathbf{j}}, \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ have their usual meanings the value of $(\mathbf{x} \times \hat{\mathbf{i}})^{2}+(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{j})^{2}+(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{k})^{2}$ where $\mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j}, \mathbf{k}$ have their usual meanings, is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $\mathbf{x}=\alpha \mathbf{i}+\beta \mathbf{j}+\gamma \mathbf{k}$
Then, $\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{i}=-\beta \hat{\mathbf{k}}+\gamma \hat{\mathbf{j}}$
$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{x} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}} &=\hat{\mathbf{k}}-\gamma \hat{\mathbf{i}} \\ \mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{k} &=-\alpha \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\beta \hat{\mathbf{i}} \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \text {Now, }(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{i})^{2} &=(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{i}) \cdot(\mathbf{x} \times \hat{\mathbf{i}}) \\ &=(-\beta \hat{\mathbf{k}}+\gamma \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \cdot(-\beta \hat{\mathbf{k}}+\gamma \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \\ &=\beta^{2}+\gamma^{2} \end{aligned}$
Similarly, $(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{j})^{2}=\alpha^{2}+\gamma^{2}$
and $(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{k})^{2}=\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$
$\therefore(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{i})^{2}+(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{j})^{2}+(\mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{\mathbf { k }})^{2}$
$=\beta^{2}+\gamma^{2}+\alpha^{2}+\gamma^{2}+\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$
$=2\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+\gamma^{2}\right)=2|\mathbf{x}|^{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the sum of two unit vectors is a unit vector, then the magnitude of their difference is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let a and } \mathbf{b} \text { are two unit vectors. }\\ &\text { Then, } \quad|\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}|=1\\ &\Rightarrow \quad|\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}|^{2}=1 \end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(a+b) \cdot(a+b)=1$
$\Rightarrow|a|^{2}+|b|^{2}+2 a \cdot b=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1+1+2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=-1$
Again, $|\hat{\mathbf{a}}-\mathbf{b}|^{2}=(\mathbf{a}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}) \cdot(\mathbf{a}-\hat{\mathbf{b}})$
$=|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+|\mathbf{b}|^{2}-2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}$
$=1+1-(-1)$
$=1+1+1=3$
$|\hat{a}-\hat{b}|=\sqrt{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For non-zero verctors a and b, $\mid a+b \mid     < \mid a-b\mid$, then a and b are
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad|\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}|    < |\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}|$
Now, $|\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}|^{2} < |\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}|^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}) \cdot(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}) < (\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b})(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b})$
$\Rightarrow \quad|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+|\mathbf{b}|^{2}+2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} < |\mathbf{a}|^{2}+|\mathbf{b}|^{2}-2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 \mathrm{a} \cdot \mathrm{b} < 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} < 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}| \cos \theta < 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta < 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \theta \in$ obluse angle
$\therefore ~a$ and $b$ are inclined at an obtuse angle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which of the following is not always true?
options: $\mid a+b\mid^{2}=|a|^2+|b|^2. \text{if a and b are perpendicular to each other}$
$\mid a+\lambda b| \geq|a|$ for all $\lambda \in R$ if a and b are perpendicular to each other
$\mid a+\lambda b| \geq|a|$ for all $\lambda \in R$ if a is parallel to b
questions.solution:
(a) If a and b are perpendicular to each other, then
$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=0$
Now consider,
$\begin{aligned}
|\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}|^{2} &=(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}) \cdot(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}) \\
&=|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+|\mathbf{b}|^{2}
\end{aligned}$
So, option (a) is always true.
(b) If $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are perpendicular to each other, then
$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=0$
Now consider, $\begin{aligned}|\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b}|^{2} &=(\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b}) \cdot(\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b}) \\ &=|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+\lambda^{2}|\mathbf{b}|^{2} \\ \Rightarrow|\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b}| &=\sqrt{|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+\lambda^{2}|\mathbf{b}|^{2}} \end{aligned}$
$\geq|\mathbf{a}|$ for all $\lambda \in R$
So, option (b) is always true.
(c) Consider,
$\left|a+\left.b\right|^{2}+|a-b|^{2}=(a+b) \cdot(a+b) + (a-b) \cdot (a-b)\right.$
$=|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{a}+|\mathbf{b}|^{2}+|\mathbf{a}|^{2}-\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} -b \cdot a + |b|^2$
$=2\left(|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+|\mathbf{b}|^{2}\right)$
So, option (c) is always true.
(d) Consider.
$\mathbf{a}=-\mathbf{b} \text { and } \mathbf{b} \neq 0$
Then, $|\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b}| \geq|\mathbf{a}|$
$\Rightarrow|-\mathbf{b}+\lambda \mathbf{b}| \geq|-\mathbf{b}|$
$\Rightarrow \quad|\mathbf{b}||\lambda-1| \geq|\mathbf{b}| \Rightarrow|\lambda-1| \geq 1$
which is not true for all $\lambda,$ as we consider $\lambda=\frac{1}{2}$, then it is not true
Hence, option (d) is not always true.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the four points with position vectors $-2\hat i+\hat j + \hat k, \hat i+\hat j+\hat k,\hat j- \hat k$ and $\lambda \hat j + \hat k$ are coplanar, then $\lambda$ is equal to
questions.solution:
If four points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}, z_{1}\right),\left(x_{2}, y_{2}, z_{2}\right),\left(x_{3}, y_{3}, z_{3}\right)$ and
$\left(x_{4}, y_{4}, z_{4}\right)$ are coplanar, then $\left|\begin{array}{lll}x_{2}-x_{1} & y_{2}-y_{1} & z_{2}-z_{1} \\ x_{3}-x_{1} & y_{3}-y_{1} & z_{3}-z_{1} \\ x_{4}-x_{1} & y_{4}-y_{1} & z_{4}-z_{1}\end{array}\right|=0$
Now, $\quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 2 & \lambda-1 & 0\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3(0+2 \lambda-2)=0$
$\lambda=1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A resistor of resistance '$R$' draws power '$P$' when connected to an AC source. If an inductance is now placed in series with $R$, such that the impedance of the circuit becomes '$Z$', the power drawn will be
questions.solution:
Let the rms voltage of the AC source be $V$. The initial power drawn by the resistor $R$ is:

$P = \dfrac{V^2}{R} \Rightarrow V^2 = P R$

When an inductance is connected in series, the new impedance of the circuit becomes $Z$. The new rms current in the circuit is:

$I' = \dfrac{V}{Z}$

The new power drawn by the circuit is the power dissipated across the resistor (since an ideal inductor does not dissipate real power):

$P' = (I')^2 R = \left(\dfrac{V}{Z}\right)^2 R$

Substituting $V^2 = P R$ into the equation:

$P' = \dfrac{P R}{Z^2} R = P \left(\dfrac{R}{Z}\right)^2$

Answer: $P\left(\dfrac{R}{Z}\right)^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a closed circuit there is only a coil of inductance $L$ and resistance $100 \Omega$. The coil is situated in a uniform magnetic field. All on a sudden, the magnetic flux linked with the circuit changes by $5 \mathrm{Weber}$. What amount of charge will flow in the circuit as a result?
options: Value of $L$ is to be known to find the charge flown
questions.solution:
Emf $=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}($ Faraday's Law $)$
$\mathrm{i}_{\text {induced }}=\frac{\mathrm{Emf}}{\mathrm{R}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{dq}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{R}} \Rightarrow \int \mathrm{dq}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{R}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{q}_{\text {flown }}=\frac{\phi_{\mathrm{i}}-\phi_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{5 \text { weber }}{100}=0.05 \mathrm{C} .$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a $60 \mathrm{mH}$ inductor and a resistor are connected in series with an AC voltage source, the voltage leads the current by $60^{\circ}$. If the inductor is replaced by a $0.5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor, the voltage lags behind the current by $30^{\circ} .$ What is the frequency of the AC supply?
questions.solution:
Given, inductance of inductor, $L=60 \mathrm{mH}$
$$
=60 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}
$$
Phase difference between voltage and current in $L-R$ circuit, $\theta_{1}=60^{\circ}$
Capacitance of capacitor, $C=0.5 \mu \mathrm{F}=0.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{F}$ Phase difference between voltage and current in $\vec{R}-C$ circuit, $\theta_{2}=30^{\circ}$
For $L-R$ circuit.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\tan \theta_{1}=\frac{X_{L}}{R} \\
\tan \theta_{1}=\frac{\omega L}{R} \quad \ldots(\mathrm{i})\left[\because X_{L}=\omega L\right]
\end{array}
$$
Similarly, for $R$ - $C$ circuit,
$$
\tan \theta_{2}=\frac{X_{C}}{R}
$$
$$
\tan \theta_{2}=\frac{\frac{1}{{\omega C}}}{R} \quad \ldots(\mathrm{ii})\left[\because X_{C}=\frac{1}{\omega C}\right]
$$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\tan \theta_{1}}{\tan \theta_{2}}=\frac{\omega L}{R \times \frac{1}{\omega C R}}=\omega^{2} L C \\
\frac{\tan 60^{\circ}}{\tan 30^{\circ}}=\omega^{2} L C \\
\frac{\sqrt{3}}{1 / \sqrt{3}}=\omega^{2} L C \\
\omega^{2} L C=3 \\
\omega^{2}=\frac{3}{L C} \\
\omega^{2}=\frac{3}{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} \\
\omega^{2}=\frac{3}{30 \times 10^{-9}} \\
\omega=\sqrt{10^{8}} \\
\omega=10^{4}
\end{array}
$$
As we know that,
$$
\omega=2 \pi f
$$
$$
f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{10^{4}}{2 \pi} \mathrm{Hz}=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \times 10^{4} \mathrm{Hz}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor in series with a resistance of $100 \Omega$, an inductor of $20 \mathrm{mH}$ and an AC voltage source of variable frequency shows resonance at a frequency of $\frac{1250}{\pi}$ Hz If this capacitor is charged by a DC voltage source to a voltage 25 v, what amount of charge will be stored in cach plate of the capacitor?
questions.solution:
In given, Series $R-L-C$ circuit Resistance $R=100 \Omega$
Inductance of Inductor, $L=20 \mathrm{mH}=20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}$ 
Resonance frequency $f=\frac{1250}{\pi} \mathrm{Hz}$ 
Source voltage $V_{D C}=25 \mathrm{V}$ 
According to resonant frequency.
$$
\omega_{0}=2 \pi f_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}
$$
or $\left(2 \pi f_{0}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{L C}$
or $\quad 4 \pi^{2} \frac{1250 \times 1250}{\pi \times \pi}=\frac{1}{L C}$
Or
$$
\begin{aligned}
C=& \frac{1000}{1250 \times 1250 \times 4 \times 20} \\
&\left(\because \mathrm{By} \text { substituting } L=20 \times 10^{-3}\right) \\
C=& 8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{F}
\end{aligned}
$$
or
We know that, Charge $Q_{s}=C V$
$$
Q_{s}=8 \times 10^{-6} \times 25=0.2 \mathrm{mC}
$$
So, the amount of charge stored in each plate of capacitor is $0.2 \mathrm{mC}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An altermating current is flowing through a series $L-C - R$ circuit. It is found that the current reaches a value of $1 \mathrm{mA}$ at both $200 \mathrm{Hz}$ and $800 \mathrm{Hz}$ frequency. What is the resonance frequency of the circuit?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Resonance frequency, $f_{0}=\sqrt{f_{1} f_{2}}$
$$
=\sqrt{200 \times 800}=400 \mathrm{Hz}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the frequency of the $A C$ voltage applied to a series LCR circuit is gradually increased from a low value, the impedance of the circuit
questions.solution:
The impedance of LCR circuit $Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(\omega_{c}-\frac{1}{\omega_{c}}\right)^{2}}$
Where, $R=$ Resistance $L=$ Inductance
$C=$ Capacitance 
When frequency of AC voltage applied to a series LCR circuit, it will first increase and resonance state $\omega_{i}=\frac{1}{\omega_{c}},$ it will decrease. In that state $(Z=R),$ it will be minimum. So, it will first increases and then decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown below, the switch is kept in position $a$ for a long time and is then thrown to position $b .$ The amplitude of the resulting oscillating current is given by
questions.solution:
When switch is in position $a$, then capacitor will be charged, such that Charge on capacitor, $q=C E$ Energy stored in capacitor, $U=q^{2} / 2 C$ When switch is in position $b$, then circuit becomes an $L C$ oscillator. Let amplitude of current in $L C$ circuit be $I_{o}$. From conservation of energy, maximum electrical energy = maximum magnetic energy $\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} L I_{0}^{2}$
$[\because q=C E]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{C^{2} E^{2}}{C}=L I_{0}^{2}$
$\therefore \quad I_{0}=E \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An alternating current in a circuit is given by $I=20 \sin (100 \pi t+0.05 \pi)$ A. The r.m.s. value and the frequency of current respectively are
questions.solution:
Given, $I=20 \sin (100 \pi t+0.05 \pi)$
The root mean square value of alternating
$$
\text { current }=l_{\mathrm{ms}}=\frac{l_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{20}{\sqrt{2}}=10 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{A}
$$
Also, $\quad \omega=100 \pi$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi f=100 \pi$ or $f=50 \mathrm{Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An alternating current of rms value $10 \mathrm{~A}$ is passed through a $12 \Omega$ resistor. The maximum potential difference across the resistor is
questions.solution:
$I_{\mathrm{rms}}=10 \mathrm{~A}$
$\begin{aligned} & I_{r m s}=\frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow I_0=\sqrt{2} \times 10=10 \sqrt{2} \\ & \text { Max. P.D. }=\sqrt{2} \times 10 \times 12=120 \times 1.414=169.68 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron in 4th orbit is (where $r$ = radius of the 1st orbit)
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's model, the angular momentum of an electron in the $n$-th orbit is given by $mvr_n = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$.

The de-Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{mv}$.

Substituting $mv = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$ into the angular momentum equation, we get $\dfrac{h}{\lambda} r_n = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$, which simplifies to $2\pi r_n = n\lambda$.

The radius of the $n$-th orbit is related to the radius of the first orbit $r$ by $r_n = n^2 r$.

Substituting $r_n$ into the wavelength equation gives $2\pi (n^2 r) = n\lambda$, which simplifies to $\lambda = 2\pi n r$.

For the 4th orbit, $n = 4$.

Therefore, $\lambda = 2\pi (4) r = 8\pi r$.

Answer: $8\pi r$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron in Hydrogen atom jumps from the second Bohr orbit to the ground state and the difference between the energies of the two states is radiated in the form of a photon. This photon strikes a material. If the work function of the material is 4.2 eV , then the stopping potential is (Energy of electron in n -th orbit $=-\frac{13.6}{\mathrm{n}^2} \mathrm{eV}$ )
questions.solution:
Energy of photon emitted from hydrogen atom
$=-13.6\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{1^2}\right]=10.2 \mathrm{eV}$

Now using Einstein's photoelectric equation.
$\begin{aligned}
& 10.2 \mathrm{eV}=4.2 \mathrm{eV}+\mathrm{eV}_{\mathrm{s}} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{s}}=6 \text { Volt. }
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The minimum wavelength of Lyman series lines is $P$, then the maximum wavelength of these lines is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lambda_{\min }=\mathrm{P} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{\min }}=\mathrm{c}_1\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right) \\ & \lambda_{\min }=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{c}_1} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{\max }}=\mathrm{c}_1\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)=\mathrm{c}_1 \frac{3}{4} \\ & \lambda_{\max }=\frac{4 \mathrm{hc}}{3 \mathrm{c}_1}=\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{P}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the potential energy of a hydrogen atom in the first excited state is assumed to be zero, then the total energy of \(n\) \(=\infty\) state is,
questions.solution:
Hint : Potential energy in 1 st emited stage \(\left(\mathrm{U}_2\right)=-6.8 \mathrm{eV}\). When \(U_2\) is assumed to be zero then potential energy in \((n=\infty)\)
will be \(6.8 \mathrm{eV}\)
so, total energy for \((n=\infty)=6.8 \mathrm{eV}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Supose in a hypothetical world the angular momentum is quantized to be even integral multiples of $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}$. The largest possible wavelength emitted by hydrogen atoms in visible range in a world according to Bohr's model will be, (Consider hc $=1242 \mathrm{Mev}-\mathrm{fm}$ )
questions.solution: $\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{(2)^2}-\frac{1}{(4)^2}\right]$ $\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{3}{16}\right], \quad \lambda=\frac{912 \times 16}{3}=4864 \mathrm{~A}^0$ $\lambda \approx 487 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sample of hydrogen atom in its ground state is radiated with photons of $10.2 \mathrm{eV}$ energies. The radiation from the sample is absorbed by excited ionized $\mathrm{He}^{+}$. Then which of the following statement/s is / are true ?
options: In the $\mathrm{He}^{+}$emission spectra, there will be 6 lines
Smallest wavelength of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$spectrum is obtained when transition taken place from $n=4$ to $n=3$
questions.solution:
$\begin{gathered}
E=13.6 \times 2^2\left(\frac{1}{n^2}-\frac{1}{m^2}\right) \\
n=2 \text { to } m=4 \\
E=13.6 \times 2^2 \times\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=10.2 \mathrm{eV}
\end{gathered}$
So $\mathrm{He}^{+}$electron can move from $n=2$ to $n=4$
Number of spectral lines $=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=\frac{4}{2}(4-1)=6$
Smallest wavelength correspond to $n=4$ to $n=3$ transition

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{v}, \mathrm{E}$ be the radius of orbit, speed of electron and total energy of electron respectively in a $\mathrm{H}$-atom. Which of the following quantities according to Bohr theory, is proportional to the quantum number $\mathrm{n}$ ?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi} \quad \therefore \mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{m} 2 \pi}{\mathrm{h}} \cdot \mathrm{vr} \quad \therefore \mathrm{n} \propto \mathrm{vr}$

questions.q: A $12.5$ ev electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at ground state. The energy level upto which the hydrogen atoms would be excited is
questions.solution:
$12.5 < 12.75$

$\therefore$ 3rd state

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{R}$ is the Rydberg Constant in $\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$, then hydrogen atom does not emit any radiation of wave-length in the range of
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{f}^{2}}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{i}^{2}}}\right]$
For range of wavelengths:$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{i}}=1,2,3, \ldots . .$ for Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, $\ldots \ldots$
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{i}}+1$ and $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{f}}=\infty$ for upper and lower range
Thus, Lyman : $\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right.$ to $\left.\frac{4}{3 \mathrm{R}}\right]$, Balmer : $\left[\frac{4}{\mathrm{R}}\right.$ to $\left.\frac{36}{5 \mathrm{R}}\right]$, Paschen : $\left[\frac{9}{\mathrm{R}} \mathrm{to} \frac{144}{36 \mathrm{R}}\right]$, Bracket : $\left[\frac{16}{\mathrm{R}}\right.$ to $\left.\frac{400}{9 \mathrm{R}}\right]$, Pfund : $\left[\frac{25}{\mathrm{R}}\right.$ to $\left.\frac{900}{11 \mathrm{R}}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To which of the following the angular velocity of the electron in the $n$ -th Bohr orbit is proportional?
questions.solution:
According to the Bohr's atomic model.
$\text {Angular momentum, } L=m v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}....(i)$
since, angular velocity $\omega=\frac{v}{r}$
$\Rightarrow v=r \omega$
From Eq. (i), we get
$\begin{aligned}
m(r \omega) r &=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \\
m \omega r^{2} &=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \\
\omega &=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r^{2}}....(ii)
\end{aligned}$
since, the radius of the electron in $n^{t h}$ orbit of
Bohr's atomic model is given as, $r=\frac{n^{2} h^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{\pi m z e^{2}} \ldots$ (iii)
Squaring the Eq. (iii) and substituting its value in Eq. (ii), we get
$\omega=\frac{n h(\pi n z e)^{2}}{2 \pi m\left(n^{4} h^{4} \varepsilon_{0}^{2}\right)} \Rightarrow \omega \propto \frac{1}{n^{3}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: How the linear velocity $v$ of an electron in the Bohr orbit is related to its quantum number $m$
questions.solution:
$\because$ Linear velocity of an electron in a orbit of H like atom,
$v=218 \times 10^{6} \times \frac{Z}{n} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}=\frac{c_{0}}{137} \cdot \frac{Z}{n}$
where, $Z=$ number of protons in nucleus, $n=$ principal quantum number and $c_{o}=$ speed of light in free space, Thus, we have
$v \propto \frac{1}{n}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Let $v_{n}$ and $E_{n}$ be the respective speed and energy of an electron in the $n$ th orbit of radius $r_{n}$, in a hydrogen atom, as predicted by Bohr's model. Then.
options: plot of $\frac{E_{n} r_{n}}{E_{1} r_{1}}$ as a function of $n$ is a straight line of slope 0
plot of $\frac{r_{n} v_{n}}{r_{1} v_{1}}$ as a function of $n$ is a straight line of slope 1
plot of $\ln \left(\frac{r_{n}}{r_{1}}\right)$ as a function of $\ln (n)$ is a straight line of slope 2
plot of In $\left(\frac{r E_{1}}{E_{n} r_{1}}\right)$ as a function of $\ln (n)$ is a straight line of slope 4
questions.solution:
We know that, $v \propto \frac{1}{n}$ (speed)
$E_{n} \propto \frac{1}{n^{2}} \quad(\text { energy })$
$r_{n} \propto n^{2}$
$\therefore E_{n T_{n}} \propto n^{0}$
$\therefore E_{n} r_{n} \propto E_{1} r_{1}$
$\frac{E_{1} r_{1}}{E_{1} r_{1}}$
$r_{n} v_{s}=n^{2} \times \frac{1}{n} \propto n = constant$
$\therefore \frac{r_{n} v_{n}}{r_{1} v_{1}}=n$
$r_{\mathrm{e}} \sim n^{2}$
$\therefore \frac{r_{\mathrm{n}}}{r_{1}}=n^{2}$
$\log \left(\frac{r_{\mathrm{n}}}{r_{1}}\right)=2 \log n \quad(\because$ slope $=2)$
$\frac{r_{n}}{E_{n}}=n^{4}$
$\therefore \frac{r_{n}}{E_{n}} \times \frac{E_{1}}{r_{1}}=n^{4}$
$\log \left(\frac{r_{1} E_{1}}{E_{\alpha} r_{1}}\right)=4 \log (n)(\because$ slope $=4)$

questions.solution: The number of de-Broglie wavelength contained in the second Bohr orbit of Hydrogen atom is 2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The wavelength of second Balmer line in hydrogen &spectrum is 600 nm  The wavelength for its third line in Lyman series is
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]$
For Balmer series,
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore n_{1}=2, n_{2}=3,4,5 \ldots \\
\frac{1}{\lambda_{B}}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right]
\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{l}
=R\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}\right] \\
\frac{1}{\lambda_{B}}=R \cdot \frac{3}{16}
\end{array}$
Lyman series is obtained when $n_{1}=1, n_{2}=2,3,4, \ldots$ For third line in Lyman series,
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\lambda_{L}} &=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right]=R\left[1-\frac{1}{16}\right] \\
\frac{1}{\lambda_{L}} &=R \cdot \frac{15}{16} \\
\frac{1 / \lambda_{B}}{\frac{1}{\lambda_{L}}} &=\frac{R \cdot \frac{3}{16}}{R \cdot \frac{15}{16}} \\
\frac{\lambda_{L}}{\lambda_{B}} &=\frac{3}{15} \\
\lambda_{L} &=\frac{3}{15} \times \lambda_{\theta} \\
\lambda_{B} &=600 \mathrm{nm}=600 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m} \\
\lambda_{L} &=\frac{3}{15} \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \\
&=120 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=120 \mathrm{nm}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, none of the given options is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ionization energy of the hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV. The potential energy of the electron in $n=2$ state of hydrogen atom is
questions.solution:
Given, $E_{n}=13.6 \mathrm{eV}$
Energy of an electron in $n^{\text {th }}$ state
$E_{n}=\frac{-13.6 z^{2} \mathrm{eV}}{n^{2}}$
$\therefore$ Energy of an electron in $n=2$ state
$E_{2}=\frac{18-6 z^{2}}{(2)^{2}}=-34 e V$
So.
$\begin{aligned}
P E &=2 E_{n=2} \\
&=2 \times(-3.4) \\
&=-6.8 \mathrm{eV}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two elements $A$ and $B$ with atomic numbers $Z_{A}$ and $Z_{B}$ are used to produce characteristic X-rays with frequencies $v_{A}$ and $v_{B}$ respectively. If $Z_{A}: Z_{B}=1: 2,$ then $v_{A}: v_{B}$ will be
questions.solution:
Given $Z_{A}: Z_{B}=1: 2$ We know
$v \propto Z^{2}$
Hence,
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{v_{A}}{v_{B}}=\frac{(1)^{2}}{(2)^{2}} \\
v_{A}: v_{B}=1: 4
\end{array}$

$\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{4}{3 R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure shows two identical parallel plate capacitors \(A\) and \(B\) of capacitances \(C\) connected to a battery. The key \(\mathrm{K}\) is initially closed. The switch is now opened and the free spaces between the plates of the capacitors are filled with a dielectric constant 3. Then which of the following statement (s) is/are true?
options: Which the switch is closed, total energy stored in the two capacitors is \(\mathrm{CV}^2\)
When the switch is opened, no charge is stored in the capacitor B
When the switch is opened, energy stored in the capacitor \(\mathrm{B}\) is \(\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2\)
When the switch is opened, total energy stored in two capacitors is \(\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2\)
questions.solution:
Hint :
\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \mathrm{CV}^2 \\ & =\mathrm{CV}^2\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{KC}) \mathrm{V}^2=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2 \\ & \mathrm{U}_B=\frac{\mathrm{q}^2}{2 \mathrm{KC}}=\frac{[\mathrm{CV}]^2}{2 \mathrm{KC}}=\frac{\mathrm{CV}^2}{2 \mathrm{~K}}=\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{CV}^2\end{aligned}\) \(\underline{\text{Total energy when switch is open}}\) \(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KCV}^2+\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{CV}^2 \\ & =\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2+\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{CV}^2 \\ & =\frac{10}{6} \mathrm{CV}^2=\frac{5}{3} \mathrm{CV}^2 \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $400 \Omega$ resistor, a $250 \mathrm{mH}$ inductor and a $2.5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor are connected in series with an AC source of peak voltage $5 \mathrm{~V}$ and angular frequency $2 \mathrm{rad/sec}$. What is the peak value of the electrostatic energy of the capacitor?
questions.solution:
Hint: 
The angular frequency is $2 \mathrm{KHz}$. The unit given is incorrect Assuming it to be in radian / sec $X_{L}=2 \times 10^{3} \times 250 \times 10^{-3}=500 \Omega$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{3}}=200 \Omega$
$Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{c}\right)^{2}}=500 \Omega$
$\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)_{\text {peak }}=\mathrm{i}_{\text {peak }} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}\right)_{\text {peak }}}{\mathrm{Z}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{5}{500} \times 200=2 \mathrm{~V}$
$\left(\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times(2)^{2}=5 \mu \mathrm{J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Five identical capacitors, of capacitance $20 \mu \mathrm{F}$ each, are connected to a battery of $150 \mathrm{V}$, in a combination as shown in the diagram. What is the total amount of charge stored?
questions.solution: The given circuit shows a balanced Wheatstone bridge. Now, the circuit becomes In the branch $A B,$ both capacitor are arranged in the series combination. Hence, its cquivalent capacitance is given by $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{C_{A B}}=\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{20}=\frac{2}{20}=\frac{1}{10} \\ C_{A B}=10 \mu \mathrm{F} \end{array} $$ Similarly, in branch $\mathrm{CD}$, $$ C_{C D}=10 \mu \mathrm{F} $$ Now, $C_{A B}$ and $C_{C D}$ are connected in parallel combination. Hence, the equivalent capacitance of the circuit, $$ C_{\mathrm{eq}}=10+10=20 \mu \mathrm{F} $$ We know that, charge $Q=C_{\mathrm{eq}} V$ $$ Q=20 \times 10^{-6} \times 150 \mathrm{V} $$ Amount of charge stored $Q=3 \times 10^{-3} C$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An electric bulb, a capacitor, a battery and a switch are all in series in a circuit. How does the intensity of light vary when the switch is
turn on?
options: Gradually increases for sometime and then becomes steady
Sharply rises initially and then gradually decreases
Gradually increases for sometime and then gradually decreases
questions.solution: Initially, there will be no voltage drop across capacitor, so intensity of bulb will rise sharply and gradually voltage drop across capacitor will increase as a result voltage drop across bulb decreases, so intensity of bulb will decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The insulated plates of a charged parallel plate capacitor (with small separation between the plates) are approaching each other due to electrostatic attraction. Assuming no other force to be operative and no radiation taking place, which of the following graphs approximately shows the variation with time $(t)$ of the potential difference $(V)$ between the plates?
questions.solution: Force $=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} q$ $\therefore$ Relative acceleration of plates. $a_{\mathrm{rel}}=\frac{2 F}{M}$ Potential difference, $V=E d$ where, $d=d_{0}-\frac{1}{2} a_{\text {rel }} t^{2}$ (: plates are moving) $\therefore$ $V=E\left[d_{0}-\frac{1}{2} a_{\mathrm{rel}} t^{2}\right]$ This is a equation of a parabola with downward concavity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three capacitors of capacitance 1.0,2.0 and $5.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$ are connected in series to a $10 \mathrm{V}$ source. The potential difference across the $2.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor is
questions.solution: When the capacitors are connected in series, the resultant capacitance of combination $$ \frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} =\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{5}=\frac{17}{10} \\ C=\frac{10}{17} \mu \mathrm{F} \end{array} $$ The charge will be same on all the capacitors in series $$ Q=C V=\frac{10}{17} \times 10=\frac{100}{17} $$ The potential difference across $2.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor $$ V^{*}=\frac{Q}{C}=\frac{100 / 17}{2}=\frac{50}{17} \text { volt} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor is charged and then disconnected from the charging battery. If the plates are now moved farther apart by pulling at them by means of insulating handles, then
options: the energy stored in the capacitor decreases
the capacitance of the capacitor increases
the charge on the capacitor decreases
the voltage across the capacitor increases
questions.solution:
We know that
The parallel plate capacitor $C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} \cdot A}{d}$
When $d$ (distance between two parallel plate) increase, then $C$ will be decrease
$\because$
$Q=C V \quad$ where $Q=$ constant
$\therefore$ The voltage across the capacitor increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Half of the space between the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor is filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant $K$. The remaining half contains air as shown in the figure. The capacitor is now given a charge $Q$. Then
options: electric field in the dielectric-filled region is higher than that in the air-filled region
on the two halves of the bottom plate the charge densities are unequal
charge on the half of the top plate above the air-filled part is $\frac{Q}{K+1}$
capacitance of the capacitor shown above is $(1+K) \frac{C_{0}}{2},$ where $C_{0}$ is the capacitance of the same capacitor with the dielectric removed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four identical plates each of area $a$ are separated by a distance $d$. The connection is shown below. what is the capacitance between $P$ and $Q ?$
questions.solution: Suppose the pair of plates is connected to positive terminal of the battery and the pair of plates $Q$ is connected to the negative terminal of the battery. From figure, it is clear that we have two capacitors $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$. Positive plates of $C_{1}$ are connected to positive plate of $C_{2}$ and negative plate to negative. Therefore $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ are in parallel. So, $\quad C_{p}=C_{1}+C_{2}=2 C=\frac{2 \varepsilon_{0} a}{d}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the adjoining figure, the potential difference between $X$ and $Y$ is 60 V. The potential difference between the points $M$ and $N$ will be
questions.solution:
Capacitors in series
\(2 C_{1} \times V_{1}=C \times V_{2}\)
\(2 V_{1}=V_{2} \ldots\) (i)
and \(V_{1}+V_{2}+V_{1}=60 \ldots\) (ii)
Solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
\(V_{2}=30 \mathrm{~V}\)

\(\therefore\) Potential difference across \(\mathrm{R}_2, \mathrm{~V}=\mathrm{R}_2=\frac{\mathrm{ER}_2}{\mathrm{R}_2+\mathrm{r}}\)
\(\therefore\) Charge on the capacitor \(\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{CV}=\frac{\mathrm{CER}_2}{\mathrm{R}_2+\mathrm{r}}\)
