questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the charge on a capacitor is increased by 2 coulomb, the energy stored in it increases by $21 \%$. The original charge on the capacitor is
questions.solution:
$\frac{\frac{q_f^2}{2 C}-\frac{q_i^2}{2 C}}{\frac{q_i^2}{2 C}} \times 100=21$ and $q_f-q_i=2$
solving we get $q_i=20$ coulomb

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four capacitors of equal capacitance have an equivalent capacitance $\mathrm{C}_1$ when connected in series and an equivalent capacitance $\mathrm{C}_2$ when connected in parallel. The ratio $\frac{\mathrm{C}_1}{\mathrm{C}_2}$ is:
questions.solution: $C_1=\frac{C}{4}$ and $C_2=4 C \Rightarrow \frac{C_1}{C_2}=\frac{1}{16}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A ball falls from a height $h$ upon a fixed horizontal floor. The co-efficient of restitution for the collision between the ball and the floor is 'e'. The total distance covered by the ball before coming to rest is [neglect the air resistance]
questions.solution:
Dist $=\mathrm{h}+2 \mathrm{e}^2 \mathrm{~h}+2 \mathrm{e}^4 \mathrm{~h}+-----$
$d=\left[\frac{1+\mathrm{e}^2}{1-\mathrm{e}^2}\right] \mathrm{h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: The variation of density of a solid cylindrical rod of cross sectional area $\alpha$ and length $L$ is $\rho=\rho_0 \frac{x^2}{L^2}$, where x is the distance from one end of the rod. The position of its centre of mass from one end $(x=0)$ is
questions.solution: $\rho=\rho_0 \frac{x^2}{L^2}$, $\begin{aligned} & X=\frac{\int x d m}{\int d m} \quad d m=\alpha d x \rho=\frac{\alpha \rho_0 x^2}{L^2} d x \\ & =\frac{\rho_0 \int_0^L x^3 d x}{\frac{\rho_0}{L^2} \int_0^L x^2 d x}=\frac{\left[x^4 / 4\right]_0^L}{\left[x^3 / 3\right]_0^L}=3 / 4 L\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two spheres $\mathrm{S}_1$ and $\mathrm{S}_2$ of masses $\mathrm{m}_1$ and $\mathrm{m}_2$ respectively collide with each other. Initially $\mathrm{S}_1$ is at rest and $\mathrm{S}_2$ is movingwith velocity v along x -axis. After collision $\mathrm{S}_2$ has a velocity $\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}$ in a direction perpendicular to the original direction. The sphere $\mathrm{S}_1$ moves after collision
options: with a velocity in the direction $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)$ to the $x$-axis
with a velocity whose direction makes an angle $\theta$ with $x$-axis such that $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$ or $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)$
with a velocity of magnitude $\frac{m_1}{2 m_2} v \sqrt{5}$
questions.solution:
before collision
After collision
$\begin{aligned} & \rightarrow v_x=\frac{m_2 v}{m_1} \quad v_y=\frac{m_2 v}{2 m_1} \\ & v=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} v\left(\frac{m_2}{m_1}\right) \\ & \rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{v_y}{v_x}\left(\frac{1}{2} \text { or }-\frac{1}{2}\right) \quad \text { (As per choice of original direction) } \\ & \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \text { or } \tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A mouse of mass \(\mathrm{m}\) jumps on the outside edge of a rotating ceiling fan of moment of inertia I and radius \(\mathrm{R}\). The fractional loss of angular velocity of the fan as a result is
questions.solution:
Hint : \(I \omega_0=\left(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{mR}^2\right) \omega\)
\(\omega_0 \rightarrow\) Initial angular velocity
\(\omega=\frac{I \omega_0}{I+m R^2}\)
\(\omega \rightarrow\) Final angular velocity
So, \(\frac{\omega_0-\omega}{\omega_0}=I-\frac{I}{I+m R^2}=\frac{m R^2}{I+m R^2}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: There are \(n\) elastic balls placed on a smooth horizontal plane. The masses of the balls are \(m, \frac{m}{2}, \frac{m}{2^2}, \ldots . \frac{m}{2^{n-1}}\) respectively. If the first ball hits the second ball with velocity \(\mathrm{v}_0\), then the velocity of the \(\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}\) ball will be,
questions.solution:
Hint : 1st Collision
\(\mathrm{V}_1=\frac{2 \times \mathrm{m}}{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2}+\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{V}_0=\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{~V}_0\) 2nd Collision \(V_2=\frac{2 \times \frac{m}{2}}{\frac{m}{2}+\frac{m}{2^2}} \times V_0=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 V_0\) 3rd Collision \(\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^3 \mathrm{~V}_0\) \(\ldots(n-1)\) collision, \(\therefore V_{n-1}=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{n-1} V_0\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Centre of mass (C.M.) of three particles of masses $1 \mathrm{~kg}, 2 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ lies at the point $(1,2,3)$ and C.M. of another system of particles of $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ lies at the point $(-1,3,-2)$. Where should we put a particle of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ so that the C.M. of entire system lies at the C.M. of the first system ?
questions.solution:
$\left.\begin{array}{l}6 \mathrm{~kg}[1,2,3] \\ 5 \mathrm{~kg}[-1,3,-2] \\ 5 \mathrm{~kg}[\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{z}]\end{array}\right) \rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{x}}, \overline{\mathrm{y}}, \overline{\mathrm{z}})=(1,2,3)$
$\bar{x}=\frac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}$
$1=\frac{5(-1)+5 \times x}{10}$
$-5+5 x=10 \Rightarrow x=3$
$\bar{y}=2=\frac{5 \times 3+5 y}{10}, 15+5 y=20, y=1$
$\bar{z}=3=\frac{5(-2)+5 . z}{10},-10+5 z=30, z=8$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: An ideal gas of molar mass $\mathrm{M}$ is contained in a very tall vertical cylindrical column in the uniform gravitational field. Assuming the gas temperature to be $\mathrm{T}$, the height at which the centre of gravity of the gas is located is (R: universal gas constant)
questions.solution:
$\because$ field is uniform COM and COG coincides.
$$
\text { So } \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{cem}}=\frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{ydm}}{\int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{dm}}=\frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{y} \mathrm{Ady} \rho}{\int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{Ady} \rho}=\frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{y} \rho \mathrm{dy}}{\int_{0}^{\infty} \rho \mathrm{dy}}
$$
From Barometric formula :- $\rho=\rho_{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{Mgy} / \mathrm{RT}}$
So $y_{\text {cen }}=\frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} y e^{-M g y / R T}}{\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-M g y / R T}}=\frac{R T}{M g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A tennis ball hits the floor with a speed $v$ at an angle $\theta$ with the normal to the floor. If the collision is inelastic and the co-efficient of restitution is $\varepsilon$, what will be the angle of reflection?
questions.solution:
Hint:
$\tan \theta^{\prime}=\frac{\operatorname{usin} \theta}{\operatorname{eucos} \theta} \quad \tan \theta^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}} \tan \theta$ Since the floor is smooth. Hence tangential component of velocity remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two identical blocks of ice move in opposite directions with equal speed and collide with cach other. What will be the minimum speed required to make both the blocks melt completely, if the initial temperatures of the blocks were $-8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ each? (Specific heat of ice is $2100 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1}$ and latent heat of fusion of ice is $3.36 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1}$ )
questions.solution:
Maximum loss in $K . E=$ Total $K . E$ energy before collision of two ice blocks
$$
K . E_{\max } \text { loss }=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=m v^{2}
$$
This maximum loss in kinetic energy will be equal to loss in heat to melt two ice block.
$$
\Rightarrow \quad m v^{2}=(m s \Delta \theta+m L) 2
$$
According to question,
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &=336 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{kg}^{-1} \\
S=& 2100 \mathrm{J} \mathrm{kg}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \\
\Delta \theta=8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$
From Eq.
(i) $v=\sqrt{2(s-\Delta \theta+L)}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\sqrt{2\left(2100 \times 8+336 \times 10^{5}\right)} \\
v=840 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}
\end{array}
$$

questions.q: Two particles $A$ and $B$ (both initially at rest) start moving towards each other under a mutual force of attraction. At the instant, when the speed of $A$ is $v$ and the speed of $B$ is $2 v$, the speed of the centre of mass is
questions.solution:
In the absence of external force the speed
$$
u_{\mathrm{co}}=0
$$
$F_{ext}=0$ then
$$
v_{\mathrm{cm} .}=0 \text { always }
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A bullet of mass $4.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{kg},$ moving at a speed of $300 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$, gets stuck into a block with a mass 9 times that of the bullet. If the block is free to move without any kind of friction, the heat generated in the process will be
questions.solution:
Let mass of bullet = $m$
Mass of block $=\mathrm{M}$ 
Velocity of bullet $=v=300 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$
Velocity of combined system $M+m=V$
Here, fhom momentum conservation $\frac{M+m}{m} V=v$
$v=\frac{300 \times 42 \times 10^{-4}}{4.2 \times 10^{-4}+9\left(4.2 \times 10^{-4}\right)}$
$30 \mathrm{ms}$
Now, heat produced = Loss in kinetic energy of bullet
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{1}{2} m^{2}-\frac{1}{2}(M+m) V^{2} \\
=\frac{1}{2} \times 4.2 \times 10^{-4}(300)^{2}-\frac{1}{2}\left(4.2 \times 10^{-2}\right. \\
+9 \times 4.2 \times 1)(30)^{2} \\
=63 \times 270 \\
=1701 \mathrm{J} \\
=\frac{1701}{4.2} \mathrm{Cal}
\end{array}
$$
$=405 \mathrm{Cal}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q:
A small steel ball bounces on a steel plate held horizontally. On each bounce the speed of the ball arriving at the plate is reduced by a factor
e (coefficient of restitution) in the rebound, so that $V_{upward}=eV_{downward}$
If the ball is initially dropped from a height of $0.4 \mathrm{m}$ above the plate and if 10 seconds later the bouncing ceases, the value of $e$ is
questions.solution:
Given, steel ball bounces on a steel ple held horizontally, so $$
V_{\text {upward }}=e V_{\text {downward }}
$$
According to the relation,
$h_{n}=e^{2 n} h$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad t &=\sqrt{\frac{2 h}{g}}+2 \sqrt{\frac{2 h e^{2}}{g}}+2 \sqrt{\frac{2 h e^{4}}{g}}+\ldots \\ &=\sqrt{\frac{2 h}{g}}\left[1+2 e+2 e^{2}+\ldots\right] \\ &=\sqrt{\frac{2 h}{g}}\left[\frac{1+e}{1-e}\right] \\ \therefore \quad t &=10 \mathrm{sec} \\ \therefore 10 &=\sqrt{\frac{2(0 \cdot 4)}{10}}\left(\frac{1+e}{1-e}\right) \end{aligned}$
Solving above relation $\therefore e=\frac{25 \sqrt{2}-1}{25 \sqrt{2}+1}=\frac{17}{18}$

questions.solution: As large number of particles is situated at a distance $R$ from the ongin If particles are uniformly distubuted and make a circulat boundary atound the ongm, then centre of mass will be at the ongin While if the patticles are not uniformly distnbuted, then centre of mass will lie between particle and origin. This implies distance between centre of mass and ongin is always less than equal to $R$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A shell of mass $5 M$, acted upon by no external force and initially at rest, bursts into three fragments of masses $M, 2 M$ and $2 M$ respectively. The first two fragments move in opposite directions with velocities of magnitudes 2v and $v$ respectively. The third fragment will
options: move with a velocily $v$ in a direction perpendicular To the other two
move with a velocity $2 v$ in the direction of velocity of the first fragment
move with velocity $v$ in the direction of velocity of the second fragment
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of third fragment is $v'$. Then by the conservation of linear momentum
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 5(0)=M \times 2 \mathrm{v}-2 M \times \mathrm{v}+2 \mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{v}^{\prime} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \mathrm{v}^{\prime}=0
\end{aligned}
$$
i.e., the third fragment will be at rest.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.q:
A spherical ball $A$ of mass 4 kg, moving along a straight line strikes another spherical ball $B$ of mass $1 \mathrm{kg}$ at rest. After the collision, $A$ and $B$ move with velocities $y_{1} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ and $v_{2} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ respectively making angles of $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with
respect to the original direction of motion of $A .$ The ratio $\frac{y_{1}}{v_{2}}$ will be
questions.solution: Along $y$ -axis momentum remains zero. Here $$ \begin{aligned} 4 v_{1} \sin 30^{\circ} &=v_{2} \sin 60^{\circ} \\ \frac{y_{1}}{v_{2}} &=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two particles have masses $\mathrm{m} \& 4 \mathrm{~m}$ and their kinetic energies are in the ratio $2: 1$. What is the ratio of their linear momenta?
questions.solution: $\frac{K E_1}{K E_2}=\frac{\frac{p_1^2}{2 m}}{\frac{p_2^2}{2 \times 4 m}}=\frac{2}{1} \Rightarrow \frac{p_1}{p_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: If the kinetic energy of a body changes by $20 \%$ then its momentum would change by -
questions.solution:
$\frac{\frac{p_f^2}{2 m}-\frac{p_i^2}{2 m}}{\frac{p_i^2}{2 m}} \times 100=20$
$\Rightarrow \frac{p_f}{p_i}=\sqrt{1.2}=1.095 \Rightarrow \frac{p_f-p_i}{p_i}=0.095$
Therefore $\%$ increase $=9.5 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a fuse wire of length $l$ and radius $r$. The time of heating $(t)$ for passing the maximum current will depend on
questions.solution:
The heat produced by a current $I$ flowing through the fuse wire in time $t$ is given by Joule's law of heating:

$H = I^2 R t = I^2 \left(\rho \dfrac{l}{\pi r^2}\right) t$

where $\rho$ is the resistivity, $l$ is the length, and $r$ is the radius of the wire.

The heat required to raise the temperature of the fuse wire to its melting point is:

$Q = m s \Delta \theta = (\pi r^2 l d) s \Delta \theta$

where $m$ is the mass, $d$ is the density, $s$ is the specific heat, and $\Delta \theta$ is the required change in temperature.

Assuming negligible heat loss to the surroundings (adiabatic heating for a fast-blowing fuse), we equate the heat produced to the heat required:

$I^2 \left(\rho \dfrac{l}{\pi r^2}\right) t = \pi r^2 l d s \Delta \theta$

Solving for $t$, we get:

$t = \dfrac{\pi^2 r^4 d s \Delta \theta}{I^2 \rho}$

For a given current $I$, the time of heating $t$ is proportional to $r^4$ and is independent of the length $l$.

Therefore, $t \propto r^4 l^0$.

Answer: $t \propto r^4 l^0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer can be converted to a voltmeter of full-scale deflection $\mathrm{V}_{0}$ by connecting a series resistance $\mathrm{R}_{1}$ and can be converted to an ammeter of full-scale deflection $I_{0}$ by connecting a shunt resistance $\mathrm{R}_{2}$. What is the current flowing through the galvanometer at its full-scale deflection?
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$R_{1}=\frac{V_{0}}{I_{g}}-G_{\ldots} \ldots \ldots . .(1)$
$\mathrm{R}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{I}_{0}-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}} \ldots \ldots \ldots . .(2)$
Eliminating G we get
$I_{g}=\frac{V_{0}-I_{0} R_{2}}{R_{1}-R_{2}}$

questions.q: What is the current $I$ shown in the given circuit?
questions.solution: According to the question, Total resistance of the given circuit $$ R_{\mathrm{eq}}=2 R $$ $\therefore \quad I=\frac{V / 2 R}{8} \quad\left(\because I_{1}=V / 2 R\right)$ So, current $I$ in the circuit $I=\frac{V}{16 R} \quad\left(\because I=\frac{I_{1}}{8}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the value of $R$ in the balanced Nheatstone bridge, shown in the figure, is increased from $5 \Omega$ to $7 \Omega$, the value of shas to increased by $3 \Omega$ in order to maintain the palance. What is the initial values of $S$ ?
questions.solution:
According to the balanced condition of Wheatstone bridge.
In the first case,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S} \\
\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{5}{S}....(i)
\end{array}
$$
In the second case
$$
\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{7}{S+3}....(ii)
$$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\begin{aligned} \frac{5}{S} &=\frac{7}{S+3} \\ 5(S+3) &=7 S \\ 5 S+15 &=7 S \\ 2 S &=15 \\ S &=7.5 \Omega \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circult shown in the figure all the resistance are identical and each has the value $r \Omega$. The equivalent resistance of the combination between the points $A$ and $B$ will remain unchanged even when the followring pairs of points marked in the figure are connected through a resistance $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.solution: The circuit diagram is as shown below, According to question, when given pair of point in options are connected through a resistance $R$, then equivalent resistance between point $A$ and $B\left(R_{A}\right)$ remains unchanged. It is only possible when current. does not flows through resistance $R$ and circuit become Wheatstone bridge as shown in the figure below. At the balanced condition, $\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}$ The current flow in $C D$ branch will be zero. Now by checking each option, from option (a), circuit is, Now, $\because \frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S} \Rightarrow \frac{2 r}{4 r}=\frac{r}{2 r} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2}$ Hence, option is correct. From option (b), circuit is Now, $\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S} \Rightarrow \frac{3 r}{3 r} \neq \frac{r}{2 r}$ So, option (b) is also incorrect. From option (c) circuit is, Now, $\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S} \Rightarrow \frac{4 r}{2 r}=\frac{2 r}{r} \Rightarrow \frac{2}{1}=\frac{2}{1}$ So, option (c) is also correct. From option (d), circuit is Now, $\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S} \Rightarrow \frac{4 r}{2 r} \neq \frac{r}{2 r}$ So, option (d) is also incorrect. Hence, option (a) and (c) are correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four resistors, $100 \Omega, 200 \Omega, 300 \Omega$ and $400 \Omega$ are connected to form four sides of a square. The resistors can be connected in any order. What is the maximum possible equivalent resistance across the diagonal of the square?
questions.solution: For maximum equivalent resistance across the diagonal of the square, the given resistors connected as Resistance of $P Q R$ arm, $R_{1}=300+200=500 \Omega$ Resistance of $P S R$ arm, $R_{2}=400+100 \Omega=500 \Omega$ The equivalent resistance between $P$ and $R$. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{R_{e q}} &=\frac{1}{R_{1}}+\frac{1}{R_{2}} \\ &=\frac{1}{500}+\frac{1}{500}=\frac{1+1}{500} \\ \therefore \quad R_{e q} &=\frac{500}{2}=250 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What will be current through the $200 \Omega$ resistor in the given circuit, a long time after the switch $K$ is made on?
questions.solution: In steady state, arm having capacitors does not flow current. So, we can neglect them. $\therefore$ The given circuit reduces to $$ R_{\text {net }}=200+400=600 \Omega $$ $\therefore$ Current in circuit, $$ \begin{aligned} I=\frac{V}{R} &=\frac{6}{600}=0.01 \mathrm{A} \\ &=10 \mathrm{mA} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A non-zero current passes through the galvanometer $G$ shown in the circuit when the key $K$ is closed and its value does not change when the key is opened. Then, which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
options: The current through the galvanometer is $40 \mathrm{mA}$.
After the key is closed, the current through the $200 \Omega$ resistor is same as the current through the $300 \Omega$ resistor.
questions.solution: $\therefore I_{C}=\frac{10}{100+G}$ $300 I_{3}=\frac{10}{100+G} G$ $I_{3}=\frac{G}{30}\left[\frac{1}{100+G}\right]$ $\therefore \quad I_{3}+I_{G}=\frac{1}{(100+G)}\left[10+\frac{G}{30}\right]=\frac{(300+G)}{(100+G) \times 30}$ According to the problem, $10=\left(\frac{200}{3}+\frac{300 G}{300+G}\right)\left[\frac{(300+G)}{(100+6) 30}\right]$ $Y=R C$ $10=\frac{60000+1100 G}{3} \times \frac{1}{100+G} \times \frac{1}{30}$ $\Rightarrow 900(100+G)=60000+1100 G$ $\Rightarrow \quad 30000=200 G$ $\therefore$ $G=150 \Omega$ $I_{G}=\frac{10}{100+150}=\frac{10}{250}=40 \mathrm{mA}$ As $\quad \frac{200}{100}=\frac{300}{150}$ So, $\quad I_{200}=I_{300}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnets of two suspended coll galvanometers are of the same strength so that they produce identical uniform magnetic fields in the region of the coils. The coil of the first one is in the shape of a square of side a and that of the second one is circular of radius $\frac{a}{\sqrt{\pi}},$ When the same current is passed through the coils, the ratio of the torque experienced by the first coil to that experienced by the second one is
questions.solution:
In galvanometer $I \sim \theta=G \theta$ Where, $\quad G=\frac{k}{N A B}$
When coil is in square of shape,
$$
G=\frac{k}{N A B}=\frac{k}{N a^{2} B}
$$
When coil is in circular shape of radius $a / \sqrt{\pi}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
G &=\frac{k}{N A B}=\frac{k}{N \pi r^{2} \cdot B} \\
&=\frac{k}{N \pi\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{\pi}}\right)^{2} \cdot B}=\frac{k}{N a^{2} B}
\end{aligned}
$$
So, value of $G$ is same for both type of shape of coil. So, the torque $e=N B L A \sin \theta$ will be same in both the case
$$
e: e=1: 1
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Six wires, each of resistance $r$, are connected so as to form a tetrahedron. The equivalent resistance of the combination when current enters through one corner and leaves through some other corner is
questions.solution: Six wires each of resistance $r$ form a tetrahedron as shown in the following figure It is circuit of Wheatstone the equivalent resistance of upper circuit $\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{2 r}+\frac{1}{2 r}=\frac{2}{2 r}=\frac{1}{r}$ $$ R=r $$ It will in parallel with outer resistance $\frac{1}{R_{e q}}=\frac{1}{r}+\frac{1}{r}=\frac{2}{r}$ $R_{e q}=\frac{r}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the circuit shown in the figure. The value of the resistance $X$ for which the thermal power generated in it is independent of small variation of its resistance is
questions.solution: For above circuit $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{X}=\frac{R+X}{R X} \\ R^{\prime}=\frac{R X}{R+X} \end{array} $$ The current in the circuit $i=\frac{E}{R+R^{\prime}}=\frac{E}{\left(R+\frac{R X}{R+X}\right)}$ $V_{k=}=\frac{E \cdot \frac{R X}{R+X}}{\left(R+\frac{R X}{R+X}\right)}=\frac{E X}{R+2 X}$ Power dissipated in the circuit $\begin{aligned} P_{x} &=\frac{V_{B X}^{2}}{X}=\frac{E^{2} \cdot X^{2}}{X(R+2 X)^{2}} \\ &=\frac{E^{2} X}{(R+2 X)^{2}} \\ \frac{d P_{X}}{d X} &=E^{2} \frac{(R-2 X)}{(R+2 X)^{3}} \\ d P_{X} &=\frac{E^{2}(R-2 X)}{(R+2 X)^{3}} \cdot d X \end{aligned}$ $\left(d P_{x}\right)$ will be zero for all $(d X)$ if $X=\frac{R}{2} \quad\left[d P_{x}=\frac{E^{2}\left(R-2 \times \frac{R}{2}\right)}{\left(R+2 \times \frac{R}{2}\right)^{3}}\right]$ $$ =0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the circuit shown in the figure where all the resistances are of magnitude $1 \mathrm{k} \Omega$. If the current in the extreme right resistance $X$ is $1 \mathrm{mA},$ the potential difference between $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution: The potential between points $A$ and $B$ $V_{N}=34$ volt

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the circuit given here. The potential difference $V_{B C}$ between the points $B$ and $C$ is
questions.solution: From circuit the current, $$ I=\frac{E}{R} $$ The equivalent resistance of above circuit $$ \begin{array}{l} =3+2+1 \\ =6 \mathrm{k} \Omega \\ =6 \times 10^{3} \Omega \\ E=3 \text { volt } \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} I &=\frac{3}{6 \times 10^{3}} \\ &=0.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{A} \end{aligned} $$ Porential, $\begin{aligned} V_{A D} &=i R=0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3 \times 10^{3} \\ &=1.5 \mathrm{V} \end{aligned}$ Charge, $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{2} \\ C=\frac{2}{3} \\ Q=C \cdot V_{A D} \\ =\frac{2}{3} \times 1 \cdot 5 \\ =1 \mu \mathrm{C} \end{array} $$ Applying KVL (Kirchhoffs Voltage Law) from $B$ to $C$ $$ \begin{array}{c} v_{s}-0 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{3}+\frac{1}{2}=V_{c} \\ V_{n}-V_{c}=1-\frac{1}{2}=0.5 \mathrm{V} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two wires of same radius having lengths $l_{1}$
and $I_{2}$ and resistivities $\rho_{1}$ and $\rho_{2}$ are connected in series. The equivalent resistivity will be
questions.solution:
$R=\frac{\rho l}{A}$
where, $\rho=$ resistivity $l=$ length of the resistance wire $A=$ area of cross-section
When the wires are connected in senes, then
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &=R_{1}+R_{2} \\
R &=\frac{\rho_{1} l_{1}}{A_{1}}+\frac{\rho_{2}l_{2}}{A_{2}} \\
R &=\frac{\rho_{1} l_{1}}{A}+\frac{\rho_{2}l_{2}}{A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$
$\mathrm{\rho}_{\mathrm{eq}}=$ resistivity of combination
$$
\begin{array}{l}
I=I_{1}+I_{2} \\
A=A
\end{array}
$$
Thus, $\rho_{\mathrm{eq}} \frac{I_{1}+I_{2}}{A}=\frac{\rho _{1}l_{1}+\rho_{2} l_{2}}{A}$
$$
\rho_{e q}=\frac{\rho_{1}l_{1}+\rho_{2} l_{2}}{I_{1}+I_{2}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $1 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor $C$ is connected to a battery of $10 \mathrm{V}$ through a resistance 1 Mega Ohm. The voltage across $C$ after 1 s is approximately
questions.solution: The time constant of the circuit Here, $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &=C \cdot R \\ C &=1 \mu F=1 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} R=1 \mathrm{M\Omega}=1 \times 10^{6} \Omega \\ \tau=1 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{6}=1 \mathrm{s} \end{array} $$ We know that in one (1) time constant $63 \%$ charging is done. $\therefore \quad \frac{63}{100} \times q_{\max }$ $=\frac{63}{100} \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 10$ $=\frac{63}{100} \times 1 \mu \mathrm{F} \times 10=6.3 \mu \mathrm{C}$ $V=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{6.3 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}}{1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{F}}=6.3 \mathrm{V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two equal resistances, $400 \Omega$ each, are connected in series with a $8 \mathrm{V}$ battery. If the resistance of first one increases by $0.5 \%$, the change required in the resistance of the second one in order to keep the potential difference across it unaltered is to
questions.solution:
In series combination,
$$
R=R_{1}+R_{2}
$$
Same current will pass in $A_{1}$ and $R_{2}$. According to question, $R_{1}$ is increased by $0.5 \%$
$\therefore$ Increment in the resistance
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{0.5}{100} \times 400 \\
=2.0 \Omega
\end{array}
$$
So to keep the potential unchanged in second resistance the change required will be $2.0 ~ \Omega$ increment.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current $I=I_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$ is flowing in a circuit consisting of a parallel combination of resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$. The total charge over the entire pulse period is
questions.solution:
The current $1=1_{0} e^{-x}$ is of exponential nature.
$I=\frac{d Q}{d t}=I_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$
$d Q=I_{0} e^{-\lambda t} d t$
$\int_{0}^{Q} d Q=I_{0} \int_{t=0}^{t=-\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \cdot d t$
$Q=I_{0} \times \frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{I_{0}}{\lambda}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The effective resistance between $A$ and $B$ in the figure is $\frac{7}{12} \Omega$ if each side of the cube has $1 \Omega$ resistance. The effective resistance between the same two points, when the link $A B$ is removed, is
questions.solution: Let when link AB is removed, $x$ is an equivalent of remaining part of cube without link. AB and rest part will be in paralel, so according to question $$ \begin{array}{c} \frac{7}{12}=\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1}+R}=\frac{1(x)}{1+x}=\frac{x}{1+x} \\ \frac{7}{12}=\frac{x}{1+x} \\ 12 x=7+7 x \\ 12 x-7 x=7 \end{array} 5 x=7 $$ $$ x=\frac{7}{5} \Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current $I$ is in the circuit shown is
questions.solution: The circuit diagram can be redrawn as For the loop $A B C D A$, $$ +2-2 I^{\prime}+2-2 I=0 $$ For the loop $A B F E A$, $$ \begin{array}{r} 2-2 I+2-2\left(I-I^{\prime}\right)=0 \\ 4-2 I-2 I+2 I^{\prime}=0 \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 4-4 I+2 I^{\prime} &=0 \\ 2 &=2 I-I^{\prime} \end{aligned} $$ From Eqs (i) and (in), we get $$ 2=2 I-I^{\prime} \cdot 2=I+I^{\prime} $$ $$ 4=3 I, I=4 / 3=1.33 \mathrm{A} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $A$ metal wire of circular cross-section has a n'sistance $R_{1}$. The wire is now stretched without breaking, so that its length is doubled and the density is assumed to remain the same. If the resistance of the wire now beromes $R_{2},$ then $R_{2}: R_{1}$ is
questions.solution:
As we know that, $R_{1}=\rho \frac{1}{a}=\rho \frac{l^{2}}{V}$
where, $1=$ length of wire $a=$ area of cross-section of the wire and $V=$ volume of the wire $R_{1} \propto l^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\left(\frac{l_{1}}{I_{2}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow R_{2}: R_{1}=4: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two cells $A$ and $B$ of emf $2 \mathrm{V}$ and $1.5 \mathrm{V}$ respectively, are connected as shown in figure through an external resistance $10 \Omega$ The internal resistance of each cell is $5 \Omega$. The potential difference $E_{A}$ and $E_{B}$ across the terminals of the cells $A$ and $B$ respectively are
questions.solution: The figure can be redrawn as, The current through the circuit $$ \begin{aligned} i &=\frac{\text { net emf }}{\text { effective resistance }} \\ &=\frac{2-1.5}{5+5+10}=\frac{0.5}{20} \\ &=\frac{1}{40}=0.025 \mathrm{A} \end{aligned} $$ The terminal potential difference of the batteries and $$ \begin{aligned} V_{A} &=\varepsilon_{A}-i I_{A}=2-0.025 \times 5 \\ &=2-0.0125=1.875 \mathrm{V} \\ V_{B} &=\varepsilon_{B}+i I_{B} \end{aligned} $$ $$ =1.5+0.025 \times 5 $$ $$ =1,5+0.0125=1.625 \mathrm{V} $$

R=11990 \Omega

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four cells, each of emf $E$ and internal resistance $r,$ are connected in series across an external resistance $R .$ By mistake one of the cells is connected in reverse. Then the current in the external circuit is
questions.solution: Total emf of the cell $=3 E-E=2 E$ Total internal resistance $=4 r$ $\therefore$ Total resistance of the circuit $=4 r+R$ So, the current in the external circuit $\left(\because i=\frac{V}{B}\right)$ $\therefore$ $$ i=\frac{2 E}{4 r+R} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circuit consists of three batteries of emf $E_{1}=1 \mathrm{V}, E_{2}=2 \mathrm{V}$ and $E_{3}=3 \mathrm{V}$ and internal resistances $1 \Omega, 2 \Omega$ and $1 \Omega$ respectively which are connected in parallel as shown in the figure. The potential difference between points $P$ and $Q$ is
questions.solution: $1 \Omega .2 \Omega$ and $1 \Omega$ are in parallel So, the required internal resistance $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{r}=\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r-{2}}+\frac{1}{r-{3}} \\ \frac{1}{r}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{1} \\ \frac{1}{r}=\frac{2+1+2}{2} \Rightarrow r=\frac{2}{5} \Omega \end{array} $$ The potential difference between points $P$ and $Q$ $\begin{aligned} E_{\text {dif }} &=\frac{\frac{E_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}}{5_{2}}+\frac{E_{3}}{5_{3}}}{1 / r}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{2}{2}+\frac{3}{1} \\ &=\frac{2+2+6}{5 / 2} \\=& \frac{2}{5 / 2}=\frac{10 / 2}{5 / 2}=\frac{5}{5} \times 2=2 \mathrm{V} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the electrical circuit shown in the figure, the current through the $4 \Omega$ resistor is
questions.solution: The current through the various branches of the circuit will be shown as According to Kirchhoff's second law in closed circuit $B C D E B$ $$ \begin{array}{r} 2 I_{1}+4 l_{1}+2 l_{1}-8\left(1-l_{1}\right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 16 l_{1}-81=0 \end{array} $$ $\Rightarrow \quad l_{1}=\frac{8 l}{16} \Rightarrow l_{1}=\frac{1}{2} l$ In closed circuit ABEFA $$ -9+3 l+8\left(1-l_{1}\right)+2 l=0 $$ $\Rightarrow \quad 13l-8 I_{1}=9 \Rightarrow 13 /-8\left(\frac{1}{2} I\right)=9$ $\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{9}{9}=1 \mathrm{A}$ So current through $4 \Omega$ resistor $l_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times 1=0.5 \mathrm{A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cell of emf $E$ is connected to a resistance $R_{1}$ for time $t$ and the amount of heat generated in it is $H$. If the resistance $R_{1}$ is replaced by another resistance $R_{2}$ and is connected to the cell at the same time $t,$ the amount of heat generated in $R_{2}$ is $4 \mathrm{H}$. Then internal resistance of the cell is
questions.solution:
We have, $H=I^{2} R$ According to given condition, $I_{1}^{2} R_{1}=H$
and
$$
I_{2}^{2} R_{2}=4 H
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{E^{2}}{\left(R_{1}+r\right)^{2}} R_{1}=H \text { and } \frac{E^{2}}{\left(R_{2}+r\right)^{2}} R_{2}=4 H
$$
$\therefore \quad \frac{R_{2}}{\left(R_{2}+r\right)^{2}}=\frac{4 R_{1}}{\left(R_{1}+r\right)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{R_{2}}\left(R_{1}+r\right)=2 \sqrt{R_{1}}\left(R_{2}+r\right)$
On solving. $r=\frac{\sqrt{R_{1} R_{2}}\left[\sqrt{R_{1}}-2 \sqrt{R_{2}}\right]}{2 \sqrt{R_{1}}-\sqrt{R_{2}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One $\mathrm{Kg}$ of copper is drawn into a wire of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ diameter and a wire of $2 \mathrm{~mm}$ diameter. The resistance of the two wires will be in the ratio
questions.solution:
Mass $=\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_1^2 \ell_1\right) \sigma$ (Ist wire)
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Mass }=\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_1^2 \ell_2\right) \sigma(2 \text { nd wire) } \\ & \left(\pi \mathrm{r}_1^2 \ell_1\right) \sigma=\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_2^2 \ell_2\right) \sigma\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \left(\pi \mathrm{r}_1^2 \ell_1\right) \sigma=\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_2^2 \ell_2\right) \sigma \\ & \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^2\end{aligned}$
$\frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{\rho \frac{\ell_1}{A_1}}{\rho \frac{\ell_2}{A_2}}=\frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2} \times \frac{A_2}{A_1}=\frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2} \times\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^2$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^4 \\ & \Rightarrow 16: 1\end{aligned}$

$\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)=\frac{\ell_2}{\ell_1} \times \frac{A_1}{A_2}=3 \times\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2=3 \times 3 \Rightarrow R_2=45$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Radiation of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on a photocell. The fastest emitted electron has speed $v$. If the wavelength is changed to $\dfrac{3\lambda}{4}$, then the speed of the fastest emitted electron will be –
questions.solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi$

When the wavelength is changed to $\dfrac{3\lambda}{4}$, the new maximum kinetic energy is:

$\dfrac{1}{2}m(v')^2 = \dfrac{hc}{\dfrac{3\lambda}{4}} - \phi = \dfrac{4hc}{3\lambda} - \phi$

Substituting $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \phi$ into the second equation:

$\dfrac{1}{2}m(v')^2 = \dfrac{4}{3}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \phi\right) - \phi$

$\dfrac{1}{2}m(v')^2 = \dfrac{4}{3}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2\right) + \dfrac{1}{3}\phi$

Since the work function $\phi > 0$, we get:

$\dfrac{1}{2}m(v')^2 > \dfrac{4}{3}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2\right)$

$(v')^2 > \dfrac{4}{3}v^2$

$v' > v\sqrt{\dfrac{4}{3}}$

Answer: greater than $v\sqrt{\dfrac{4}{3}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength of a moving bus with speed is $v$ is $\lambda$. Some passengers left the bus at a stoppage. Now when the bus moves with twice of its initial speed. Its kinetic energy is found to be twice of its value. What is the de-Broglie wavelength of the bus now?
questions.solution:
Initially $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mE}}}$
Now acc. to question, $\mathrm{V}^{\prime}=2 \mathrm{~V}, \quad \mathrm{E}^{\prime}=2 \mathrm{E}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \cdot m^{\prime}(2 \mathrm{~V})^2=2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\mathrm{m}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2} \\
& \therefore \quad \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \frac{\mathrm{~m}}{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{E}}}=\lambda
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electric field component of an EM radiation varies with time as $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{a}\left(\cos \omega_{0} \mathrm{t}+\sin \omega \mathrm{t} \cos \omega_{0} \mathrm{t}\right)$, where ' $\mathrm{a}$ ' is a constant and $\omega=10^{15} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}, \omega_{0}=5 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$. This radiation falls on a metal whose stopping potential is $-2 \mathrm{ev}$. Then which of the following statement $(\mathrm{s})$ is/are true $?\left(\mathrm{~h}=6.62 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{S}\right)$
options: For light of frequency $\omega$, photoelectric effect is not possible
Stopping potential vs. frequency graph will be a straight line
The work function of the metal is $-2 \mathrm{ev}$
The maximum kinetic energy of the photo electrons is $1.95 \mathrm{ev}$.
questions.solution:
Stopping potential $=-2 \mathrm{~V} \Rightarrow \mathrm{KE}_{\max }=2 \mathrm{ev}$ (Option D incorrect) $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{a}\left(\cos \omega_{0} \mathrm{t}+\sin \omega \mathrm{t} \cos \omega_{0} \mathrm{t}\right)$ $=\mathrm{a} \cos \omega_{0} \mathrm{t}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}\left\{\sin \left(\omega+\omega_{0}\right) \mathrm{t}+\sin \left(\omega-\omega_{0}\right) \mathrm{t}\right\}$
Corresponding frequencies are $\frac{\omega_{0}}{\alpha \pi}, \frac{\omega+\omega_{0}}{\alpha \pi} \& \frac{\omega-\omega_{0}}{2 \pi}$
$\left(\frac{\omega+\omega_{0}}{2 \pi}\right)$ determines maximum KE \& Stopping potential
Now Energy corresponding to $\frac{\omega+\omega_{0}}{2 \pi}$
Corresponding energy $=\mathrm{hf}$
Stopping potential $=V_{0}=2 V=3.95 \mathrm{ev}$
Now $\mathrm{KE}_{\max }=\mathrm{ev}_{0}=\mathrm{hf}-\phi \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{ev}=3.95 \mathrm{ev}-\phi \Rightarrow \phi=1.95 \mathrm{ev}$
for $\mathrm{w}=10^{15} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$
frequency $=\frac{10^{15}}{2 \pi}=\mathrm{f}_{1}$
When frequency is $\mathrm{w}, \mathrm{f}_{1}=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{10^{15}}{2 \pi} \quad \mathrm{E}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{15}}{2 \pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{ev}=3.95 \mathrm{ev}$
Energy $=\mathrm{hf}_{1}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 10^{15}}{2 \pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{ev}=0.66 \mathrm{ev}     < \phi$ (work function)
Hence photoelectric effect not possible (option A correct)
Also ev $_{0}=h f-\phi$
(Straight line) (option B correct) $\quad \mathrm{KE}_{\max }=\mathrm{E}-\phi \Rightarrow \phi=\mathrm{E}-2 \mathrm{eV}=1.95 \mathrm{ev}$ (option $\mathrm{c}$ is incorrect)

options: Maximum kinetic energy if photo-electrons will decrease

and photo-electric current $\propto$ intensity
$\therefore$ Current will decrease

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A proton and an electron initially at rest are accelerated by the same potential difference. Assuming that a proton is 2000 times heavier than an electron, what will be the relation between the de Broglie wavelength of the proton $\left(\lambda_{p}\right)$ and that of electron $\left(\lambda_{c}\right) ?$
questions.solution:
As we know that de-Broglie wavelength is given as $\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}$
where, $h=$ Planck constant
$$
\begin{array}{l}
p=\text { momentum of particle } \\
v=\text { velocity of particle }
\end{array}
$$
and $m=$ mass of the particle.
Eq.(i) can be written as,
$$
\lambda=\frac{h}{2 m(\mathrm{KE})}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q v}} \quad[\because \mathrm{KE}=q v]
$$
where, $\mathrm{KE}=$ Kinetic energy of particle
Hence, $\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$
$$
\text { Now, } \frac{\lambda_{\text {proton }}}{\lambda_{\text {electron }}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{\text {electron }}}{m_{\text {proton }}}}
$$
Given, mass of proton, $m_{\text {proton }}=2000 \mathrm{m}_{\text {electron }}$
$\frac{\lambda_{\text {proton }}}{\lambda_{\text {electron }}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2000}}$
$\frac{\lambda_{\text {proton }}}{\lambda_{\text {electron }}}=\frac{1}{20 \sqrt{5}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{p}=\frac{\lambda_{e}}{20 \sqrt{5}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electrons are emitted with kinetic energy $T$ from a metal plate by an irradiation of light of intensity $J$ and frequency v. Then, which of the following will be true?
questions.solution: In photoelectric effect, if the incident light had a frequency less than a minimum frequency $v_{0}$, then no electrons are ejected regardless of the light's amplitude. This minimum frequency is also called the threshold frequency, and the value of $v_{0}$ depends on the metal. For frequency greater than $v_{0},$ electrons would be ejected from the metal. Furthermore, the kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is proportional to the light frequency The relationship between photoelectron kinetic energy $T$ and light frequency $v$ is shown in graph below Graph shows that kinetic energy $T$ is linearly increasing with light frequency (v). However, for a given photosensitive material and frequency of incident light, number of photoelectrons emitted per second is directly proportional to the intensity of incident light i.e., Number of electrons emitted $\propto J$. Also, photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron accelerated through a potential of $10000 \mathrm{V}$ from rest has a de-Broglie wave length $\lambda$. What should be the accelerating potential, so that the wavelength is doubled?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Kinetic energy of a electron due to accelerated by a potential $V, \mathrm{KE}=e V$
$$
\frac{1}{2} m_{r} v^{2}=c V
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1 \times p^{2}}{2 m_{e}}=e V \quad[\because p=m v]$
$\therefore$
$$
p=\sqrt{2 e V m_{e}}
$$
$\because$ de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having momentum $p$
$$
\lambda=\frac{h}{p}
$$
According to question, $\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 e V_{2} m_{c}}}{\sqrt{2 e V_{1} m_{e}}}=\sqrt{\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}}$
$\because$
$$
\lambda_{2}=2 \lambda_{1}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{2 \lambda_{1}}=\sqrt{\frac{V_{2}}{10000}}$
$\therefore \quad$ Potential $V_{2}=\frac{10^{4}}{4}=2500 \mathrm{V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron is $0.4 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}$ when its kinetic energy is $1.0 \mathrm{keV} .$ Its wavelength will be $1.0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}$
when its kinetic energy is
questions.solution:
De-Broglie wavelength, $\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}}$
$\lambda_{1}=$ Wave length of electron
$\begin{aligned} &=0.4 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m} \\ E_{1} &=1 \cdot \text { OkeV } \\ \lambda_{2} &=1 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m} \\ E_{2} &=? \\ \frac{0 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 10^{-10}} &=\sqrt{\frac{E_{2}}{1 \cdot 0 \mathrm{keV}}} \\ \frac{4}{10}=& \sqrt{\frac{E_{2}}{1}} \\ \frac{16}{100}=& \frac{E_{2}}{1} \\ E_{2}=& 0 \cdot 16 \mathrm{keV} \end{aligned}$

If the frequency of light is increased to $v_{2},$ the stopping potential changes to $V_{2}$. Therefore, the charge of an electron is given by

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential difference $V$ required for accelerating an electron to have the de-Broglie wavelength of $1 Å$ is
questions.solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron is given by.
$$
\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}
$$
where, $h=$ Planck's constant
$$
\begin{aligned}
m &=\text { mass of electron } \\
\mathrm{e} &=\text { charge on electron } \\
\mathrm{V} &=\text { potential difference } \\
\lambda &=1 \dot{\mathrm{A}}=10^{-10} \mathrm{m} \\
\lambda^{2} &=\frac{h^{2}}{2 \mathrm{meV}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 \mathrm{me} \lambda^{2}} \\
&=\frac{\left(6.6 \times 10^{-34}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times\left(1 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2}} \\
&=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{-20}} \\
&=\frac{6.6 \times 6.6 \times 10^{2}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 1.6}=\frac{4356 \times 10^{2}}{2912}=150 \mathrm{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work function of Cesium is $2.27 \mathrm{eV}$. The cut-off voltage which stops the emission of electrons from a cesium cathode irradiated with light of $600 \mathrm{nm}$ wavelength is
questions.solution:
Work function of cesium $=2.27 \mathrm{eV}$
The wavelength of incident light
$$
\lambda=600 \mathrm{nm}=600 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}
$$
The energy of incident photon $=h v$
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &=\frac{h c}{\lambda} \\
&=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} e \mathrm{V} \\
&=2.06 \mathrm{eV} \\
\frac{h c}{\lambda}    < \phi
\end{aligned}
$$
$2.06 \mathrm{eV} < 2.27 \mathrm{eV}$ 
So, no emission of electrons will take place. Hence, none of the given options is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance between a light source and photoelectric cell is d. If the distance is decreased to $\frac{d}{2},$ then
options: the emission of electron per second will be four times
maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons will be four times
the emission of electrons per second will be doubled
questions.solution:
The intensity of emitted electrons is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
I=\frac{1}{r^{2}} \\
I_{1}=\frac{1}{d^{2}} \\
I_{2}=\frac{1}{(d / 2)^{2}}=4 \cdot \frac{1}{d^{2}}
\end{array}
$$
and I $\propto$ Number of photons per second $=N$
$\therefore$
$$
N=\frac{1}{r^{2}}
$$
$\therefore$ Number of emithed electrons will become 4 times From Enstein's photo-electric equation
$$
K E_{max}=h v-\phi
$$
where, $\phi=$ work function of surtace
$\mathrm{v}=$ frequency of incident photon
Since, v remains unchanged, thus $KE_{max}$ as well as stopping potential remans unchanged
$\because \mathrm{KE}_{max}=\mathrm{eV}_{s}=\mathrm{v}$
where. $V_{s}$ = stopping potential

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work function of metals is in the range of $2 \mathrm{eV}$ to $5 \mathrm{eV}$. Find which of the following wavelength of light cannot be used for photoelectric effect? (Consider, Plank constant $=4 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{eVs},$ velocity of light $\left.=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
questions.solution:
Given that, $2 \mathrm{eV} \leq \phi \leq 5 \mathrm{eV}$
Wavelength corresponding to minimum and maximum values of work function are
$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\max } &=\frac{h C}{E}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-15} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2} \\
&=6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}=600 \mathrm{nm} \\
\text { and } \lambda_{\min } &=\frac{h C}{E}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-15} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{5} \\
&=2.4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}=240 \mathrm{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$
Clearly, wavelength range would be
$$
240 \leq \lambda \leq 600
$$
Thus, wavelength $650 \mathrm{nm}$ is not suitable for photoelectric effect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron is the same as that of a $50 \mathrm{keV}$ X-ray photon. The ratio of the energy of the photon to the kinetic energy of the electron is (the energy equivalent of electron mass is $0.5 \mathrm{MeV}$ )
questions.solution:
de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}$
The kinetic energy of the electron
$$
K_{\text {electron }}=\frac{1}{2 m} \cdot \frac{h^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}
$$
Where $h=$ Planck constant $\lambda=$ wavelength
The photon energy
$$
E_{\text {phocton }}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}
$$
From Eqs. (ii) and (i), we get
$\frac{E_{\text {photon }}}{K_{\text {electron }}}=\frac{h c / \lambda}{h^{2} / 2 m \cdot \lambda^{2}}=\frac{h c \cdot \lambda^{2} \times 2 m}{h^{2} \cdot \lambda}$
where $m=0.5 \mathrm{MeV}=5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{eV}$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{h}{\lambda c}=50 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{eV}=\frac{2 \mathrm{m} \lambda c}{h} \\=\frac{2 \mathrm{m}}{h / \lambda c} &=\frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{5}}{50 \times 10^{3}} \quad\left(\because \mathrm{m}=\frac{h}{c \lambda}\right) \\ &=20: 1 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the correct statement(s) about photoelectric effect.
options: There is no significant Ime delay between the absorption of a suitable tadiation and the emission of electrons
Einstein analysis gives a threshold frequency above which no electron can be emitted
The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is proportional to the frequency of incident radiation
The maximum kinetic energy of electrons does not depend on the intensity of radiation
questions.solution:
There is no significant time delay between the absorption of a suitable radiation and the emission of electrons and the maximum kinetic energy of electrons does not depend on the intensity of radiation.
So, (a) and (d) are correct options.

questions.q: The stopping potential for photoelectrons from a metal surface is $V_{1}$ when monochromatic light of frequency $v_1$ is incident on it. The stopping potential becomes $V_{2}$ when monochromatic light of another frequency is incident on the same metal surface. If $h$ be the Planck's constant and $e$ be the charge of an electron, then the frequency of light in the second case is
questions.solution:
Here, $h v_{1}=\phi_{0}=e V_{1}$ ...(i)
and $h v_{2}=\phi_{0}+e V_{2}$...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have
$\begin{aligned}
& h\left(v_{2}-v_{1}\right)=e\left(V_{2}-v_{1}\right) \\
\Rightarrow \quad & v_{2}=\frac{e}{h}\left(V_{2}-v_{1}\right)+v_{1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron moving with a velocity $c / 2$ ( $c=$ velocity of light in vacuum) is equal to the wavelength of a photon. The ratio of the kinetic energies of electron and photon is
questions.solution:
de-Broglie wavelength
$$
\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}
$$
Here, $\lambda_{e}=\frac{h}{m_{e} \frac{c}{2}}$ and $\lambda_{p}=\frac{h}{m_{p} c}$
Given, $\quad \lambda_{e}=\lambda_{B}$
So, $\quad \frac{h}{m_{e} \frac{c}{2}}=\frac{h}{m_{p} c}$
$\frac{m_{e}}{m_{p}}=2$
Ratio of KE
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{K_{e}}{K_{p}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} m_{e} v_{e}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} m_{p} v_{p}^{2}} \\
\frac{K_{e}}{K_{p}}=\frac{1}{2}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform but time varying magnetic field is present in a circular region of radius '$R$'. The magnetic field is perpendicular and into the plane of loop and the magnitude of field is increasing at constant rate $\alpha$. There is a straight conducting rod of length $2R$ placed as shown in figure. The magnitude of induced emf across the rod is.
questions.solution:
Let the center of the circular magnetic field region be the origin $(0,0)$. The straight conducting rod of length $2R$ is placed tangentially to the circle. By symmetry, it is located at $y = -R$ and extends from $x = -R$ to $x = R$.

To find the induced emf across the rod, we can construct a closed triangular loop connecting the center of the circle $O(0,0)$ to the ends of the rod, $A(-R, -R)$ and $B(R, -R)$.

The total induced emf in this closed loop is given by Faraday's law:
$\varepsilon_{\text{loop}} = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right|$

The induced electric field $\vec{E}$ forms concentric circles around the origin. Because the segments $OA$ and $OB$ are radial, the electric field is perpendicular to these paths. Thus, the emf induced along the radial segments is zero:
$\int_{O}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ and $\int_{B}^{O} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$

Therefore, the total emf of the loop is entirely due to the straight rod $AB$:
$\varepsilon_{\text{loop}} = \varepsilon_{\text{rod}}$

Now, we find the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the loop $OAB$. The magnetic field is only present inside the circular region. The loop intersects the circular region to form a sector.
The angle of this sector is determined by the coordinates of $A$ and $B$. The angle of $A$ from the negative y-axis is $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{R}{R}\right) = 45^{\circ}$, and similarly for $B$. The total angle subtended by the rod at the center is $45^{\circ} + 45^{\circ} = 90^{\circ}$.

The area of this $90^{\circ}$ sector is:
$\text{Area} = \dfrac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi R^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} \pi R^2$

The magnetic flux through the loop is:
$\Phi = B \times \text{Area} = B \left( \dfrac{1}{4} \pi R^2 \right)$

Given that the magnetic field is increasing at a constant rate $\alpha$ $\left( \dfrac{dB}{dt} = \alpha \right)$, the magnitude of the induced emf is:
$\varepsilon_{\text{rod}} = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{4} \pi R^2 \dfrac{dB}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{4} \pi R^2 \alpha$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{4}\pi R^2\alpha$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square of side $L$ lies in the $x - y$ plane, where the magnetic field is given by $\vec{B} = B_0\left(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}\right)$ where $B_0$ is constant. The magnetic flux passing through the square is
questions.solution:
The square lies in the $x - y$ plane, so its area vector is perpendicular to the $x - y$ plane, which is along the $z$-axis.

The area vector is given by $\vec{A} = L^2 \hat{k}$

The magnetic field is given by $\vec{B} = B_0(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 4\hat{k})$

The magnetic flux $\Phi$ passing through the square is the dot product of the magnetic field and the area vector:

$\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$

$\Phi = B_0(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}) \cdot (L^2 \hat{k})$

$\Phi = 4 B_0 L^2$

Answer: $4\,B_0 L^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An amount of charge Q passes through a coil of resistance R. If the current in the coil decreases to zero at a uniform rate during time \(T\), then the amount of heat generated in the coil will be,
questions.solution:
Hint :
Given, \(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_0 T=Q \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_0=\frac{2 \mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{T}}\) Equation of \(\mathrm{I}(\mathrm{t}) \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{I}_0}+\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}}=1\) \(\begin{aligned} & I=I_0\left(1-\frac{t}{T}\right)=\frac{2 Q}{T}\left(1-\frac{t}{T}\right) \\ & \text { Heat }=\int_0^T I^2 R d f \\ & =R \int_0^T \frac{4 Q^2}{T^2}\left(1-\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{4 Q^2 R}{T^2}\left[\int_0^T d t+\frac{1}{T^2} \int_0^T t^2 d t-\frac{2}{T} \int_0^T t d t\right]=\frac{4 Q^2 R}{T^2}\left[\not T+\frac{T}{3}-\not T\right]=\frac{4 Q^2 R}{3 T} \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small bar magnet of dipole moment $M$ is moving with speed $v$ along $x$ direction towards a small closed circular conducting loop of radius ' $a$ ' with its centre $O$ at $x=0$ (see figure). Assume $x>>a$ and the coil has a resistance $R$. Then which of the following statemenmt(s) is/are true?
options: Magnetic field at the centre $\mathrm{O}$ of the circular coil due to the bar magnet is $\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{x}^{3}}$
The magnetic moment $\mu$ due to induced current in the coil is proportional to $\mathrm{a}^{4}$
The heat produced is proportional to $\frac{1}{x^{6}}$
questions.solution: Magnetic field at an axial point is $2\left(\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\right) \frac{M}{x^{3}}$ $$ \phi=2\left(\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\right) \frac{M}{x^{3}} \cdot \pi a^{2} $$ $\mathrm{EMF}=\frac{-\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=-2\left(\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\right) \mathrm{M} \pi \mathrm{a}^{2}\left(\frac{-3}{\mathrm{x}^{4}}\right) \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=6\left(\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\right) \frac{\mathrm{M} \pi \mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{4}} \mathrm{~V} \quad \mathrm{EMF} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{4}}$ Current in coil $=\frac{E M F}{R} \quad i=6\left(\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\right) \frac{M \pi a^{2}}{x^{4}} \frac{V}{R}$ Magnetic moment $\mu=\mathrm{i} \pi \mathrm{a}^{2}, \mu \propto \mathrm{a}^{4}$ Rate at which heat is produced $=\mathrm{i}^{2} \mathrm{R} . \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{8}}$

questions.q: Consider a conducting wire of length $\mathrm{L}$ bent in the form of a circle of radius $\mathrm{R}$ and another conductor of length 'a' (a < < R) is bent in the form of a square. The two loops are then placed in same plane such that the square loop is exactly at the centre of the circular loop. What will be the mutual inductance between the two loops?
questions.solution:
Hint:
$2 \pi R=L_{\cdots} \ldots \ldots \ldots .(1)$ $4 s=a \ldots \ldots \ldots . .(2)$ $\frac{\mu_{0} I \times S^{2}}{2 R}=\phi, M=\frac{\mu_{0} S^{2}}{2 R}$ $=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2} \times \frac{a^{2} \times 2 \pi}{16 \times L}=\frac{\mu_{0} a^{2} \pi}{16 L}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ with the planc of the loop perpendicular to $\mathbf{B}$. Under which condition given an emf will be induced in the loop "If the loop is ....."
options: rotated about one of its diameters
questions.solution: According to the question. Given, A metallic loop is placed in uniform magnetic field B. Magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ and metallic loop are perpendicular to each other. Then, if metallic loop is moved along $\mathbf{B}$ or rotated about its own axis. the net flux associated with it remains constant. Thus, no emf will be induced in these cases. However, when the loop is squeezed to a smaller area or rotated about one of its diameters. Then its flux changes. Thus, emf is induced. So, option (b) and (d) both are correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a rectangular loop of a conducting wire is moving away with a constant velocity $v$ in a perpendiculat direction from a very long straight conductor carrying a steady current $l$. When the breadth of the rectangular loop is very small compared to its distance from the straight conductor, how does the emf. $E$ induced in the loop vary with time $t ?$
questions.solution: $\therefore$ Motional emf in the loop. $$ \begin{aligned} E &=E_{1}-E_{2}=vl l\left(B_{1}-B_{2}\right) \\ &=v\left(\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{I}{y}-\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(y+b)}\right] \\ &=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot I \cdot v l\left[\frac{y+b-y}{y(y+b)}\right]=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{v I l}{y(y+b)} b \end{aligned} $$ $\because \quad b< < y$ $\therefore \quad E=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{v l I}{y^{2}} b$ $\because v=$ constant $$ $$ Thus, $\quad E=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{v l l}{v^{2} t^{2}} b$ Hence, $\quad E \propto \frac{1}{t^{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coils of self-inductances $6 \mathrm{mH}$ and $8 \mathrm{mH}$ are connected in series and are adjusted for highest coefficient of coupling. Equivalent self-inductance $L$ for the assembly is approximately
questions.solution:
Given, $L_{1}=6 \mathrm{mH}=6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}$
$$
L_{2}=8 \mathrm{mH}=8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}
$$
If two coills $L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$ are connected in series, the equivalent self-inductance $L$ for assembly
$$
L=L_{1}+L_{2}+2 \sqrt{L_{1} L_{2}}
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=6 \times 10^{-3}+8 \times 10^{-3}+2 \sqrt{6 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}} \\
=14 \times 10^{-3}+2 \sqrt{48} \times 10^{-3} \\
=14 \times 10^{-3}+2 \times 7 \times 10^{-3} \\
=28 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}=28 \mathrm{mH}
\end{array} \quad(\because \sqrt{48}=6.92=7)
$$

A magnetic field $B=2 t+4 t^{2}$ (where, $t_{x}$ time) is applied perpendicular to the plany of a circular wire of radius $r$ and resistance
R. If all the units are in SI the electric charge that flows through the circular wire during $t=0 \mathrm{s}$ to $t=2 \mathrm{s}$ is

Given, $B=2 t+4 t^{2}$
at $\quad t=0, B_{1}=0$
and at $t=2, B_{2}=2 \times 2+4(2)^{2}$
$$
=4+16=20 \mathrm{Wb} / \mathrm{m}^{2}
$$
We have, $\Delta Q=\frac{\Delta \phi}{R}=\frac{\pi^{2}\left(B_{2}-B_{1}\right)}{R}$
$$
=\frac{\pi r^{2} \left[20-0\right]}{R}=\frac{20 \pi r^{2}}{R}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic flux through a loop of resistance $10 \Omega$ is given by $\phi=5 t^2-4 t+1$ Weber. How much current is induced in the loop after $0.2 \mathrm{sec}$ ?
questions.solution:
Hints : $\phi=5 t^2-4 t+1$
$$
\frac{d \phi}{d t}=10 t-4
$$
$$
\mathrm{I}=\frac{e}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{-d \phi / d t}{\mathrm{R}}=-\frac{10 t-4}{10}
$$
At $t=0.2 \mathrm{sec}$
$$
\mathrm{I}=\frac{-(10 \times 0.2-4)}{10}=-\frac{(2-4)}{10}=+\frac{2}{10}=+0.2 \mathrm{~A}=0.2 \mathrm{~A}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The displacement current flows through a capacitor when the voltage across its plates
questions.solution:
Displacement current is given by $I_d = \epsilon_0 \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$.

Since $\Phi_E = EA = \dfrac{V}{d}A$, we have $I_d = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A}{d} \dfrac{dV}{dt} = C \dfrac{dV}{dt}$.

For displacement current to flow, $I_d \neq 0$, which implies $\dfrac{dV}{dt} \neq 0$.

This means the voltage across the plates must be changing with time, i.e., it can be either increasing or decreasing with time.

Answer: is increasing with time; is decreasing with time

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Longitudinal waves cannot
questions.solution: Longitudinal waves cannot be polarized. The reason behind this is that the transverse waves can vibrate in all directions perpendicular to the direction of propagation. It is therefore feasible to continue the vibration to a single plane.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave in a medium is given by $\vec{E}(x, y, z, t)=E_0 \hat{n} e^{i k_0[(x+y+z)-c t]}$ where $c$ is the speed of light in free space. $\vec{E}$ field is polarized in the $x-z$ plane. The speed of wave is $v$ in the medium. Then
options: refractive index of the medium is $\sqrt{3}$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Hint : } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{E}_0 \hat{n} e^{i k_0(\hat{\mathrm{k}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{r}}-\mathrm{wt})} \\ & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow v=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{|\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}|}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { refractive index }=\sqrt{3} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \text { is in } z-x \text { plane and } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \hat{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a plane eletromagnetic wave, the electric field is given by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=90 \sin \left(0.5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{x}+1.5 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{k} . \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{m} .$ The corresponding magnetic field $\vec{B}$ will be
questions.solution:
$E=90 \sin \left(0.5 \times 10^{3} x+1.5 \times 10^{11} t\right) \hat{k} v / m$
Direction of propagation is $-\hat{i}$
$\& E_{0}=90$
$\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\mathrm{~B}_{0}}=\mathrm{C} \Rightarrow \mathrm{B}_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{90}{3 \times 10^{8}} \mathrm{~T}=3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$ is along $\hat{\mathrm{k}} \& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ should be along $-\hat{\mathrm{i}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ is should be along $\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ Hence equation of magnetic field is
$\mathrm{B}=3 \times 10^{-7} \sin \left(0.5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{x}+1.5 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{t}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A charged particle moves with constant velocity in a region where no effect of gravity is felt but an electrostatic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$
together with a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ may be present. Then which of the following cases are possible?
questions.solution:
If a charged particle is moving in a gravity-free space without changing its velocity, then three cases possible
(i) Particle can move with constant velocity in any direction, if \(\vec{E}=0, \vec{B}=0\).
(ii) If \(\vec{E}, \vec{B}\) are having values such that \(q E=q v B\) and hence both forces (Force due to magnetic field and force due to electric field) acts in such a way that they cancel each other, in this case also particle can move with uniform velocity.
(iii) If \(\vec{E}=0, \vec{B} \neq 0\) and \(\vec{B}\) is such that it acts in the direction of velocity then magnetic force would be zero and hence velocity can remain constant.

So, we can say that
$E_{y}>E_{X}>E_{v}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A simple pendulum of length $l$ has a bob of mass $m$, with a charge $q$. On it a vertical sheet of charge, with surface charge density '$\sigma$' passes through the point of suspension. At equilibrium, if the string makes an angle $\theta$ with the vertical, then
questions.solution:
The electric field due to an infinite vertical sheet of charge is $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}$.

The electric force acting on the charged bob is horizontal and is given by $F_e = qE = \dfrac{\sigma q}{2\varepsilon_0}$.

The gravitational force acting on the bob is vertical and is given by $W = mg$.

At equilibrium, the tension $T$ in the string balances both the electric and gravitational forces. Resolving the tension into horizontal and vertical components:

$T \sin\theta = \dfrac{\sigma q}{2\varepsilon_0}$

$T \cos\theta = mg$

Dividing the first equation by the second equation:

$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sigma q}{2\varepsilon_0 mg}$

Answer: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sigma q}{2\varepsilon_0 mg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two charges $+q$ and $-q$ are placed at points $A$ and $B$ respectively which are at a distance $2 L$ apart. $C$ is the mid point of $A$ and $B$. The workdone in moving a charge $+Q$ along the semicircle $\operatorname{CSD}\left(W_1\right)$ and along the line $\operatorname{CBD}\left(W_2\right)$ are
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } W_{C S D}=Q\left(V_{D A}-V_{C A}\right)=\frac{Q q}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 L}\left[\frac{1}{3}-1\right]=\frac{-2 Q q}{12 \pi \epsilon_0 L}=\frac{-Q q}{6 \pi \epsilon_0 L}=W_{C B D} \\ & W_{C B D}=W_{C S D} \text { path independent }\end{aligned}$

The maximum speed attained by the particle is
questions.solution:
$V_{\max }=\frac{1}{m} \int_0^{\frac{T}{2}} F d t,=\frac{1}{m} \int_0^{\frac{T}{2}} q E_0 \sin (\omega t) d t$
$=\frac{2 \mathrm{qE}_0}{\mathrm{~m} \omega}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

As a $\rightarrow 0$ and $q \rightarrow \infty$ while $q a^2=Q$ remains finite, the clectric field at a point $P$, at a distance $x(x \gg a)$ from $x=0$ is $E=\frac{\alpha Q}{4 \pi \in_0 x \beta} \hat{i}$. Then
questions.solution:
Hint : $E=\frac{2 k q a}{x^3}\left[\frac{1}{\left(1-\frac{a}{2 x}\right)^3}-\frac{1}{\left(1+\frac{a}{2 x}\right)^3}\right]$ $=\frac{2 \mathrm{kqa}}{\mathrm{x}^3}\left[1+\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{x}}-1+\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{x}}\right]=\frac{2 \mathrm{kqa}}{\mathrm{x}^3}\left[\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{x}}\right]=\frac{6 \mathrm{kqa}^2}{\mathrm{x}^4}=6 \frac{1}{4 \pi \in} \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{x}^4}$
$\alpha=6 \quad \alpha=\frac{3}{2} \beta$

questions.q: Consider two concentric conducting sphere of radii $\mathrm{R}$ and $2 \mathrm{R}$ respectively. The inner sphere is given a charge $+\mathrm{Q}$. The other sphere is grounded. The potential at $r=\frac{3 R}{2}$ is
questions.solution: $q \rightarrow$ Charge appearing on shell of radius $2 R$ after earthing Calculating $\mathrm{q} \quad \mathrm{V}_{\text {outer shell }}=\frac{\mathrm{KQ}}{2 \mathrm{R}}+\frac{\mathrm{Kq}}{2 \mathrm{R}}=0 \quad \mathrm{q}=-\mathrm{Q}$ Calculating potential at $r=\frac{3 R}{2}$ $$ V_{\text {inner shell }}+V_{\text {outer shell }}=\frac{K Q}{r}+\frac{K q}{2 R}=\frac{K Q}{\frac{3 R}{2}}+\frac{K(-Q)}{2 R}=\frac{K Q}{6 R}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{6 R} $$

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hemisphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is placed in a uniform electric field $\mathrm{E}$ so that its axis is parallel to the field. Which of the following statement(s) is / are true?
options: Flux through the curved surface of hemisphere is $\pi R^2 E$.
Flux through the circular surface of hemisphere is $\pi R^2 E$.
Work done in moving a point charge $q$ from $A$ to $B$ via the path $A C B$ depends upon $R$.
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\quad \text { as } \phi_{\text {in }}=0, \quad \phi_{\text {total }}=0 \\
&\phi_{\text {curved }}+\phi_{\text {flat }}=0 \\
&\phi_{\text {flat }}=-E \times \pi R^2 \\
&\therefore \phi_{\text {curved }}=E \times \pi R^2
\end{aligned}
$$
Also $\Delta \mathrm{V}=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$ for uniform eletrified.
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { as } \vec{E} \perp \Delta \vec{r} \\
&\Delta V=0 \\
&W=q \Delta V=0
\end{aligned}
$$
so work is independent of $R$ in moving charge $q$ from $A+B$

questions.q: A metal sphere of radius $R$ carrying charge $q$ is surrounded by a thick concentric metal shell of inner and outer radii a and $\mathrm{b}$ respectively. The net charge on the shell is zero. The potential at the centre of the sphere, when the outer surface of the shell is grounded will be
