questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A very long charged solid cylinder of radius 'a' contains a uniform charge density $\rho$. Dielectric constant of the material of the cylinder is $\mathrm{k}$. What will be the magnitude of electric field at a radial distance ' $x^{\prime}(x    < a)$ from the axis of the cylinder?
questions.solution: Hint: Using Gauss's Law $\mathrm{E}(2 \pi \times \ell)=\frac{\rho\left(\pi \mathrm{X}^{2} \ell\right)}{\mathrm{k} \varepsilon_{0}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{E}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{x}}{2 \mathrm{k} \varepsilon_{0}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pair of parallel metal plates are kept with a separation 'd'. One plate is at a potential $+\mathrm{V}$ and the other is at ground potential. A narrow beam of electrons enters the space between the plates with a velocity $\mathrm{v}_{0}$ and in a direction parallel to the plates. What will be the angle of the beam with the plates after it travels an axial distance L?
questions.solution:
Hint:
$t=\frac{L}{v_{0}}$ $v_{y}=\frac{e}{m} \cdot \frac{v}{d} \times \frac{L}{v_{0}}=\frac{e v L}{m d v_{0}}$ $\tan \theta=\frac{v_{y}}{v_{x}}=\frac{e v L}{m d v_{0} \cdot v_{0}}$ $\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{e v L}{m d v_{0}^{2}}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two pith balls, each carrying charge $+q$ are hung from a hook by two strings. It is found that when each charge is tripled, angle between the strings double. What was the initial angle between the strings?
questions.solution: Hint: $\mathrm{T} \sin \theta=\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{T} \cos \theta=\mathrm{mg}$ $\tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{Fe}}{\mathrm{mg}}=\frac{\mathrm{k} \cdot \mathrm{q}^{2}}{(2 \mathrm{~L} \sin \theta)^{2} \mathrm{mg}}$ In 2 nd case $\begin{array}{l} \tan 2 \theta=\frac{\mathrm{kq} \mathrm{q}^{2}}{(2 \mathrm{~L} \sin 2 \theta)^{2} \cdot \mathrm{mg}} \\ \frac{\tan 2 \theta}{\tan \theta}=\frac{9 \sin ^{2} \theta}{\sin ^{2} 2 \theta} \\ \frac{2}{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}=\frac{9}{4 \cos ^{2} \theta}=\frac{9}{4} \sec ^{2} \theta \\ \frac{2}{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}=\frac{9}{4}\left(1-\tan ^{2} \theta\right) \end{array}$ $\tan ^{2} \theta=x, \frac{2}{1-x}=\frac{9}{4}(1+x)$ $\Rightarrow 8=9-9 x^{2} \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \tan ^{2} \theta=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \theta=30^{\circ}$ Angle between the strings $=2 \theta=60^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Eleven equal point charges, all of them having a charge $+Q$, are placed at all the hour positions of a circular clock of radius $r$. except at the $10 \mathrm{h}$ position. What is the clectric field strength at the centre of the clock?
options: $\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}$ from the centre towards the mark 10
$\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}$ from the mark 10 towards the centre
$\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}$ from the centre towards the mark 6
questions.solution: The clock diagram is as shown below In the above diagram, charge $+Q$ is not placed at $10 \mathrm{h}$ position. So, net electric ficld strength at centre $C$. $$ \begin{array}{r} \mathrm{E}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{E}_{1}+\mathrm{E}_{2}+\mathrm{E}_{3}+\mathrm{E}_{4}+\mathrm{E}_{5}+\mathrm{E}_{6} \\ +\mathrm{E}_{7}+\mathrm{E}_{8}+\mathrm{E}_{9}+\mathrm{E}_{11}+\mathrm{E}_{12} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{E}_{1}+\mathrm{E}_{2}+\mathrm{E}_{3}+\mathrm{E}_{4} \\ +\mathrm{E}_{5}+\mathrm{E}_{6}+\left(-\mathrm{E}_{1}-\mathrm{E}_{2}-\mathrm{E}_{3}-\mathrm{E}_{5}-\mathrm{E}_{6}\right) \end{array} $$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{E}_{4}=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}},$ from centre towards the mark $10 .$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A negative charge is placed at the midpoint between two fixed equal positive charges, separated by a distance $2 d$. If the negative charge is given a small displacement $x(x< < a)$ perpendicular to the line joining the positive charges, how the force $(F)$ developed on it will approximately depend on $x ?$
questions.solution: According to the question, Force experienced by the charge $-q$ due to charge $Q$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \qquad F=-\frac{2 k Q q}{r^{2}} \cos \theta \ldots(\mathrm{i})\left[\text { where, } k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right] \\ \text { From diagram, } \cos \theta=\frac{x}{r} \end{array} $$ By substituting the value of $\cos \theta$ in Eq. (i) $$ F=-\frac{2 k Q q}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{x}{r} $$ or $$ F=-\frac{2 k Q x}{r^{3}} $$ or $$ F=-\frac{2 k Q x}{\left(x^{2}+d^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \quad\left[\begin{array}{l} \because r^{2}=x^{2}+d^{2} \\ r=\sqrt{x^{2}+d^{2}} \end{array}\right] $$ For, $x< < d,$ so $x^{2}$ can be neglected $$ F=-\frac{2 k Q x}{d^{3}} $$ So, the force developed by negative charge $(-q)$ due to the system of the charges as shown in the figure is, $$ F=\frac{-4 k Q q x}{d^{3}} $$ $\Rightarrow \quad F \propto x$ So, the force developed by negative charge is directly proportional to the distance $x$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A point charge $-q$ is carried from a point $A$ to another point $B$ on the axis of a charged ring of radius $r$ carrying a charge $+q$. If the point $A$ is at a distance $\frac{4}{3} r$ from the centre of the
ring and the point $B$ is $\frac{3}{4} r$ from the centre but
on the opposite side, what is the net work that need to be done for this?
questions.solution: A charged circular ring of radius $R$ is shown in figure. From the figure, $d=\sqrt{x^{2}+r^{2}}$ $\therefore$ Potential due to a uniform ring of positive charge $q$ at point $P$ $$ \begin{aligned} V &=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{d} \\ &=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{\sqrt{x^{2}+r^{2}}} \quad[\text { from Eq. } \end{aligned} $$ Now, $\therefore C B=\sqrt{O C^{2}+O B^{2}}=\sqrt{r^{2}+\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{2} r^{2}}=\frac{5}{4} r$ Similarly, $A C=\frac{5}{3} r$ $\therefore$ Work done in bringing a point charge $-q$ from $A$ to $B$, $\begin{aligned} W &=-q\left(V_{B}-V_{A}\right) \\ &=-q\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{C B}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{C A}\right] \\=& \frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot q^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\frac{5}{4} r}-\frac{1}{5}\right] \\=& \frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q^{2}}{5 r}[4-3]=-\frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q^{2}}{r} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a region in free space bounded by the surfaces of an imaginary cube having sides of length $a$ as shown in the figure. A charge $+Q$ is placed at the centre $O$ of the cube. $P$ is such a point out side the cube that the line $O P$ perpendicularly intersects the surface $A B C D$ at $R$ and also $O R=R P=a / 2$. A charge $+Q$ is placed at point $P$ also. What is the total electric flux through the five faces of the cube other than $A B C D ?$
questions.solution:
For surface $A B C D$ clectric flux is zero. Because at surface $A B C D$ net electric field is zero. Using Gauss's law,
$$
\oint \mathbf{E} \cdot d \mathbf{S}=\frac{Q_{\mathrm{in}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}
$$
Electric flux through the five faces of the cube,
$$
\phi_{E}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Four equal charges of value $+Q$ are placed at four vertices of a regular hexagon of side
W. By suitably choosing the vertices, what can the maximum possible magnitude of electric field at the centre of the hexagon?
questions.solution: (Regular hexagon) In $\triangle A O M, \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{A M}{A O}$ $A O=\frac{\frac{a}{2}}{1 / 2}=a$ For maximum electric field at centre $o$ charges should be placed at $F, A, B$ and $C .$ Electric field due to charges at $F$ and $C$ is equal and opposite at $0 .$ Net electric field at centre $o$ due to charges at $A$ and $B$ Angle between $E_{A}$ and $E_{B}$ is $60^{\circ} .$ $\therefore E_{\text {net }}$ at $O$ $E_{\text {net }}=\sqrt{E_{A}^{2}+E_{B}^{2}+2 E_{A} E_{B} \cos 60^{\circ}} \quad\left(\because E_{A}=E_{B}=E\right)$ $=\sqrt{E^{2}+E^{2}+2 E^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}} \quad\left(\because \cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2}\right)$ $=E \sqrt{3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{a^{2}} \sqrt{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light charged particle is revolving in a circle of radius $r$ in electrostatic attraction of a static heavy particle with opposite charge How does the magnetic field vary at the centre of the circle due to the moving charge depend on $r ?$
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{l} F_{\text {centripetal }}=m r \omega^{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \\ \therefore \quad \omega^{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{3}} \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m r^{3}}} \end{array}\\ &\omega \propto \frac{1}{r^{3 / 2}} \end{aligned} $$ Current produced due to moving $q_{1}$ $$ i=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi} q_{1} $$ Thus, magnetic field due to motion of $q_{1}$ in circular path at point $\underline{O}$ $B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2} \cdot \frac{i}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega q_{1}}{2 \pi r} \quad[$ using $\mathrm{Eq} \cdot(\mathrm{ii})]$ Hence, $B \propto \frac{\omega}{r}$ Magnetic field $B \propto \frac{1}{r^{3 / 2}}$ $[$ using Eq. (i) ]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two positive charges $Q$ and $4 Q$ are placed at points $A$ and $B$ respectively, where $B$ is at a distance $d$ units to the right of $A$. The total electric potential due to these charges is minimum at $P$ on the line through $A$ and $B$. What is (are) the distance (s) of $P$ from $A ?$
questions.solution: $\because V_{P}$ is minimum. $\therefore$ $E_{p}=0 \Rightarrow E_{A}=E_{B}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{4 Q}{(d-r)^{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{r}=\frac{2}{d-r} \Rightarrow d-r=2 r$ $\therefore$ Distance of $P$ from the $A r=\frac{d}{3}$ units to the right of $A .$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A positive charge $Q$ is situated at the centre of a cube. The electric flux through any face of the cube is (in SI units)
questions.solution: The positive charge $Q$ is situated at the centre of a cube According to Gauss theorem, the flux from any closed surface $$ \phi_{E}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ Cube has 6 faces, so electric flux from any face $\phi_{z}=\frac{Q}{6 \varepsilon_{0}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge of 0.8 coulomb is divided into two charges $Q_{1}$ and $Q_{2}$. These are kept at a separation of $30 \mathrm{cm}$. The force on $Q_{1}$ is maximum when
questions.solution: $$ \frac{Q_{1}}{30 c m} \stackrel{Q_{2}}{6} $$ Checking the given options one by one (i) $Q_{1}=Q_{2}=0 \cdot 4 \mathrm{C}$ The force on $Q_{1}$ due to $Q_{2}$ $$ \begin{aligned} F &=k_{p} \frac{Q_{1} \times Q_{2}}{30 \times 10^{-2}} \\ &=k \frac{Q_{1} Q_{2} \times 100}{30} \\ &=k \frac{0-4 \times 0.4 \times 100}{30} \\ &=\frac{k \times 0 \cdot 16 \times 100}{30} \end{aligned} $$ (ii) When $Q_{1}=0 \cdot 8 \mathrm{C} Q_{2}=0$ $$ F=\frac{k \times Q_{1} Q_{2}}{30 \times 10^{-2}}=0 $$ (iii) when $Q_{1}=0, Q_{2}=0-8 \mathrm{C}$ $$ F=0 $$ (iv) $Q_{1}=0 \cdot 2 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{Q}_{2}=0 \cdot 6 \mathrm{C}$ $$ \begin{aligned} F &=\frac{k \cdot 0 \cdot 2 \times 0 \cdot 6}{30 \times 10^{-2}} \\ &=\frac{k \times 0 \cdot 12 \times 10^{2}}{30} \end{aligned} $$ Thus we find that, in option(a), the force on $Q_{1}$ will be maximum. $\therefore$ correct answer is $Q_{1}=Q_{2}=0 \cdot 4 \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle with charge $Q$ coulomb, tied at the end of an inextensible string of length $R$ metre, revolves in a vertical plane. At the centre of the circular trajectory, there is a fixed charge of magnitude $Q$ coulomb. The mass of the moving charge $M$ is such that $M g=\frac{Q^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0} R^{2}}$. If at the highest position of the particle, the tension of the string just vanishes, the horizontal velocity at the lowest point has to be
questions.solution: According to the question, when tension in the string is zero. As $T=0$ $M g-\frac{K Q^{2}}{R^{2}}=\frac{m v^{2}}{R} \quad\left(k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right)$ $\frac{m v^{2}}{R}=$ centripetal force required to keep the body in a circular path $\Rightarrow \quad v=0$$\quad\left[\because M g=\frac{k Q^{2}}{R^{2}}\right]$ So the required work done to keep charge in vertical motion $\therefore \quad W_{g}=\Delta K E=$ change in $\mathrm{K.E.}$ $\Rightarrow \quad m g(2 R)=\frac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}$ \begin{array}{l} v_{0}^{2}=2 g \cdot 2 R=4 g R \\ v_{0}=\sqrt{4 g R}=2 \sqrt{g R} \end{array}

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle with charge $e$ and mass $m$, moving along the $X$ -axis with a uniform speed $u$, enters a region where a uniform electric field $E$ is acting along the $Y$ -axis. The particle starts to move in a parabola. Its focal length (neglecting any effect of gravity) is
questions.solution: From parabola $y=\frac{1}{2} \times\left[\frac{E e}{m}\right] \times \frac{x^{2}}{u^{2}}$ $=\frac{E \cdot e}{2 m u^{2}} \cdot X^{2}$ $\begin{array}{ll}\text { As } x=4 a y & \text { (for parabola) }\end{array}$ $\therefore \quad X^{2}=\frac{2 m u^{2}}{E \cdot e} \cdot y$ $a=\frac{2 m u^{2}}{4 E \cdot e}=\frac{m u^{2}}{2 E \cdot e}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow metal sphere of radius $R$ is charged with a charge $Q .$ The electric potential and intensity inside the sphere are respectively
questions.solution: Inside a hollow charged sphere, the electric field intensity is always zero. $\therefore$ $E=0$ but potential is constant. Potential on the surtace of the hollow sphere $$ V=\frac{K Q}{R}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R}=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R} $$ This is the potential $(\mathrm{V})$ inside the charged hollow meta sphere.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Angle between an equipotential surface and electric lines of force is
questions.solution: The electric lines of force are always perpendicular to the equpotential surtace $$ \theta=90^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The line $A A^{\prime}$ is on charged infinite $A$ conducting plane which is perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The plane has a surface density of charge $\sigma$ and $B$ is ball of mass $m$ with a like charge of magnitude $q . B$ is connected by string from a point on the line $A A^{\prime}$. The tangent of angle (0) formed between the line $A A^{\prime}$ and the string is
questions.solution: The diagram is as follows The electric field due to charged infinite conducting sheet is $E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}$ Now, force (electric force) on the charged ball is $F=q E=\frac{q \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}$ The resultant of electric force and $m g$ balance the tension produced in the string. So, tan $\theta=\frac{F_{e}}{m g}=\frac{q \sigma}{\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{m g}}=\frac{q \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0} m g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $q$ is placed at one corner of a cube. The electric flux through any of the three faces adjacent to the charge is zero. The flux through any one of the other three faces is
questions.solution: Consider the diagram In above the flux coverage of three face is shown in figure. Which is like a quadrant in any plane. So, flux will be $$ \phi=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{q}{6 \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{q}{24 \varepsilon_{0}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two charges $+q$ and $-q$ are placed at a distance $a$ in a uniform electric fleld. The dipole moment of the combination is $2 q a(\cos \theta i+\sin \theta)$. where, $\theta$ is the angle between the direction of the field and the line joining the two charges. Which of the following statement(s) is/ase correct?
options: The torque exerted by the field on the dipole vanishes
The net force on the dipole vanishes
The torque is independent of the choice of coordinates
The net force is independent of a
questions.solution: The situation can be diagrammatically as, The dipole moment, $$ \mathbf{P}=2 q a(\cos \theta \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\sin \theta \hat{\mathbf{j}}) $$ The net force on the dipole is always zero while net torque on the dipole is not zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An infinite sheet carrying a uniform surface charge density $\sigma$ lies on the $x y$ -plane. The work done to carry a charge $q$ from the point $\mathbf{A}=a(\mathbf{i}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$ to the point $\mathbf{B}=a(\mathbf{i}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+6 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$
(where $a$ is a constant with the dimension of length and $\varepsilon_{0}$ is the permittivity of free space) is
questions.solution:
The given
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &=a(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\
\mathbf{B} &=a(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+6 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\
\mathbf{A B} &=\mathbf{O B}-\mathbf{O A} \\
&=a(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+6 \hat{\mathbf{k}})-a(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\
\mathbf{A B} &=a(-4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})
\end{aligned}
$$
Work done $=q\left(\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\right) \hat{\mathbf{k}} \cdot \mathbf{A B} \quad$ (along to $Z$ -axisi
$$
\begin{array}{r}
=q\left(\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\right) \hat{\mathbf{k}} \cdot a(-4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})=\frac{3 q \sigma a}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \\
(\because \mathbf{i} \cdot \mathbf{i}=\mathbf{j} \cdot \mathbf{j}=\mathbf{i} \cdot \mathbf{k}=1 \text { and } \mathbf{i} \cdot \mathbf{j}=\mathbf{j} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}=\hat{\mathbf{k}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{i}}=0)
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider two concentric spherical metal shells of radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}\left(r_{2}>r_{1}\right)$. If the outer shell has a charge $q$ and the inner one is grounded, the charge on the inner shell is
questions.solution:
Let, the charge on the inner shell be $q^{\prime}$
The total charge will be zero
So, $\frac{k q^{\prime}}{r_{1}}+\frac{k q}{r_{2}}=0$
$\left(\because r_{2}>r_{1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad q^{\prime}=-\frac{K q / r_{2}}{K / r_{1}}$
$$
q^{\prime}=-\left(\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right) q
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in figure below, a charge $+2 \mathrm{C}$ is situated at the origin $O$ and another charge $+5 \mathrm{C}$ is on the $x$ -axis at the point $A .$ The later charge from the point $A$ is then brought to a point $B$ on the $y$ -axis. The work done is $\left(\right.$ given $\left.\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{F}\right)$
questions.solution:
Work done $=U_{f}-U_{i}$
$$
=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times 2 \times 5 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\right)=0
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sphere of radius $R$ has a volume density of charge $\rho=k r,$ where $r$ is the distance from the centre of the sphere and $k$ is constant. The magnitude of the electric field which exists at the surface of the sphere is given by $\left(\varepsilon_{0}=\right.$ permittivity of free space)
questions.solution:
By Gauss's theorem
\(\begin{aligned} \mathrm{E}\left(4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\right) &=\frac{\int \mathrm{p} \times 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{dr}}{\varepsilon_{0}} \\ &=\frac{\int \mathrm{kr} \times 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{dr}}{\varepsilon_{0}} \\ &=\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{Kr}^{2}}{4 \varepsilon_{0}} \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $+q$ is placed at the origin $O$ of $X-Y$ axes as shown in the figure. The work done in taking a charge $Q$ from $A$ to $B$ along the straight line $A B$ is
questions.solution: We know that and $\quad V=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}$ $\begin{aligned} \text { Hence, } \quad W &=q\left[\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} b}-\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}\right] \\ &=\frac{Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{a-b}{a b}\right] \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a region, the intensity of an electric field is given by $\mathbf{E}=2 \mathbf{i}+3 \mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}$ in $\mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The electric flux through a surface $\mathbf{S}=10 \mathbf{i} \mathrm{m}^{2}$ in the region is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two infinite parallel metal planes, contain electric charges with charge densities $+\sigma$ and $-\sigma$ respectively and they are separated by a small distance in air. If the permittivity of air is $\varepsilon_{0}$, then the magnitude of the field between the two planes with its direction will be
questions.solution:
$E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}$
$E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}$ towards negative charged plate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four charges equal to \(-Q\) are placed at the four corners of a square and a charge \(q\) is at its centre. If the system is in equilibrium, the value of \(q\) is
questions.solution:
Hints:
\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}^{\prime}=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~F}+\frac{\mathrm{F}}{2}=\mathrm{F}\left(\sqrt{2}+\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & \frac{\mathrm{q} Q}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{a}}{2}\right)^2}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}\left(\sqrt{2}+\frac{1}{2}\right), \mathrm{q}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{2}+1}{2}\right) \\ & q=\frac{Q}{4}(2 \sqrt{2}+1)\end{aligned}\)

questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.q: The current flowing through a wire depends on time as $I=3 t^2+2 t+5$. The charge flowing through the cross section of the wire in time from $t=0$ to $t=2 \mathrm{sec}$. is
questions.solution: $Q=\int_0^2\left(3 t^2+2 t+5\right) d t=22 C$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work done in carrying a charge $\mathrm{Q}$ once around a circle of radius $\mathrm{r}$ about a charge $\mathrm{q}$ at the centre is
questions.solution: Work done by conservative force in a round trip is zero

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a slide caliper, $(m+1)$ number of vernier divisions is equal to $m$ number of smallest main scale divisions. If $d$ unit is the magnitude of the smallest main scale division, then the magnitude of the vernicr constant is
questions.solution: $(m+1)$ vernier division $=m$ main scale division One division on vernier scale $=\left(\frac{m}{m+1}\right)$ division on main scale Vernier constant $=\left(1-\frac{m}{m+1}\right) d=\frac{d}{m+1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $x_1$ be the size of the magnified image and $x_2$ the size of the diminished image in Lens Displacement Method, then the size of the object is :
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A satellite of mass m rotates round the earth in a circular orbit of radius R. If the angular momentum of the satellite
is J, then its kinetic energy (K) and the total energy (E) of the satellite are
questions.solution:
where "I, is moment of inertia"
Hint: $K E=\frac{J^2}{2 I}=\frac{J^2}{2 m R^2}, \quad E=-K E$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An earth's satellite nejar the surface of the earth takes about 90 min per revolution. A satellite orbiting the moon also takes about 90 min per revolution. Then which of the following is true?
options: No conclusion can be made about the densities
questions.solution:
Hint :
\(m\left(E_g\right)=m \omega^2 R\) \(\begin{aligned} & m \cdot 4 \pi \frac{G \rho R}{3}=m \omega^2 R \\ & \Rightarrow \omega^2 \propto \rho \\ & \Rightarrow T \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho}} \end{aligned}\) If \(\mathrm{T}\) are equal, so will be \(\rho\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A modified gravitational potential is given by \(V=-\frac{G M}{r}+\frac{A}{r^2}\). If the constant \(A\) is expressed in terms of gravitational constant (G), mass ( \(M\) ) and velocity of light (c), then from dimensional analysis, \(A\) is,
questions.solution:
Hint : \(V=-\frac{G M}{r}+\frac{A}{r^2}\)
\([A]=\frac{[G M]}{[r]}\left[r^2\right]=[G M][r]\)
now, we know, \(\frac{G M}{r}\) gives dimension of \(c^2\)
\(\frac{[\mathrm{GM}]}{[\mathrm{r}]}=\left[\mathrm{c}^2\right] \Rightarrow[\mathrm{r}]=\frac{[\mathrm{GM}]}{\left[\mathrm{c}^2\right]}\)
\(\Rightarrow[\mathrm{A}]=\frac{[\mathrm{GM}][\mathrm{GM}]}{\left[\mathrm{c}^2\right]}\)
\([\mathrm{A}]=\frac{\mathrm{G}^2 \mathrm{M}^2}{\mathrm{c}^2}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $m$ is thrown vertically upward with speed $\sqrt{3} v_e$, where $v_e$ is the escape velocity of a body from earth surface. The final velocity of the body is
questions.solution: $-\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\sqrt{3} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)^2=0+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2, \quad \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assume that the earth moves around the sun in a circular orbit of radius $R$ and there exists a planet which also move around the sun in a circular orbit with an angular speed twice as large as that of the earth. The radius of the orbit of the planet is
questions.solution:
According to the Kepler's third law
$$
T^{2} \propto r^{3}
$$
where, $T=$ time period of revolution
$r=$ radius 
Now, $\left(\frac{T_{E}}{T_{P}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{r_{E}}{r_{P}}\right)^{3}=\frac{r_{E}}{r_{P}}=\left(\frac{T_{E}}{T_{P}}\right)^{2 / 3}$
$$
\frac{r_{E}}{r_{P}}=\left(\frac{2 \pi / \omega_{E}}{2 \pi / \omega_{P}}\right)^{2 / 3} \quad\left[\because T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}\right]
$$
$$
\frac{r_{E}}{r_{P}}=\left(\frac{\omega_{P}}{\omega_{E}}\right)^{2 / 3}
$$
According to the question, $\omega_{P}=2 \omega_{E}$ and $r_{E}=R$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{R}{r_{P}}=\left(\frac{2 \omega_{E}}{\omega_{E}}\right)^{2 / 3}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{R}{r_{P}}=(2)^{2 / 3} \\
r_{P}=\frac{R}{(2)^{2 / 3}}=R(2)^{-2 / 3}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of accelerations due to gravity $g_{1}:g_{2}$ on the surfaces of two planets is 5: 2 and the ratio of their respective average densities $\rho_{1}: \rho_{2}$ is 2: 1 . What is the ratio of respective escape velocities $v_{1}: v_{2}$ from the surface of the planets?
questions.solution:
Escape velocity from a planet. $v_{0}=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R^{2}}} R$
$$
=\sqrt{2 g R}
$$
According due to gravity $g=\frac{G M}{R^{2}}=\frac{G\frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} \cdot \rho}{R^{2}}=\frac{4}{3} G\pi R_{p}$
$\therefore \quad$ Radius $R=\frac{3 g}{4 \pi G \rho}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $v_{e}=\sqrt{2 g \cdot \frac{3 g}{4 \pi G \rho}}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2} \frac{g^{2}}{\pi G \rho}}$
Thus, $\quad v_{e} \propto \frac{g}{\sqrt{\rho}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{v_{n}}{v_{r_{2}}}=\frac{g_{1}}{\sqrt{\rho_{1}}} \times \frac{\sqrt{\rho_{2}}}{g_{2}}=\frac{5}{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
(given, $\frac{g_{1}}{g_{2}}=\frac{5}{2}$ and $\frac{Q}{\rho_{2}}=2: 1$ )
$=\frac{5}{2 \sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two particles of mass $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$; approach each other due to their mutual gravitational attraction only. Then,
options: accelerations of both the particles are equal
acceleration of the particle of mass $m_{1}$ is proportional to $\overline{m_{1}}$
acceleration of the particle of mass $m_{1}$ is proportional to $m_{2}$
acceleration of the particle of mass $m_{1}$ is inversely proportional to $m_{1}$
questions.solution:
The gravitational force acting between the two masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ is given by
$$
F_{O}=\frac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}
$$
Force on mass $m_{1}$.
$$
F_{1}=\frac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}=m_{1} a_{1}
$$
where, $a_{1}=$ acceleration
$$
\Rightarrow \quad a_{1}=\frac{G m_{2}}{I^{2}} \Rightarrow a_{1} \propto m_{2}
$$
and similarly, $a_{2} \propto m_{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A satellite has kinetic energy $K$, potential energy $V$ and total energy $E .$ Which of the following statements is true?
questions.solution:
As we know that for the earth and satellite system 
Kinetic energy, $\quad K=\frac{G M m}{2 a}$
Potential energy, $V=-\frac{G M m}{a}$
and total energy, $E=-\frac{G M m}{2 a}$
where, $a=$ radius of the orbit of the satellite and $m=$ mass of the satellite
On the basis of above three expressions for the energies, we have
$K=\frac{G M m}{2 a}=-\left(-\frac{G M m}{2}\right)=-\frac{V}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An artificial satellite moves in a circular orbit around the earth. Total energy of the satellite is given by $E$. The potential energy of the satellite is
questions.solution:
We know that
The potential energy of the satellite
$$
U=-\frac{G M_{e} m}{R_{0}}
$$
The kinetic energy of the satellite
$$
K=\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}
$$
The total energy
$$
\begin{aligned}
E=U+K &=-\frac{G M_{e} m}{R}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M_{e} m}{R_{0}} \\
E &=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M_{e} m}{R} \\
2 E &=-\frac{G M_{e} m}{R_{0}} \Rightarrow-2 E=U
\end{aligned}
$$
So. $\mathrm{PE}=-2(\mathrm{TE})$
$$
\mathrm{PE}=-2 E
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mass $M$ at rest is broken into two pieces having masses $m$ and $(M-m) .$ The two masses are then separated by a distance $r$ The gravitational force between them will be the maximum when the ratio of the masses $[m:(M-m)]$ of the two parts is
questions.solution:
The gravitation force between two masses,
$$
F=\frac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r}
$$
here, $\quad m_{1}=m$
and
$$
\begin{array}{l}
m_{2}=(M-m) \\
F=\frac{G m(M-m)}{r^{2}}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$
For maximum gravitational force, $\frac{d F}{d m}=0$
$\therefore$
$$
\frac{d}{d m}[m(M-m)]=0
$$
By solving, we get $m=\frac{M}{2}$
So, $\quad \frac{m}{(M-m)}=\frac{1}{1}$

questions.solution: Hints: \(\Delta \mathrm{U}=\frac{\mathrm{mgh}}{1+\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{R}}}=\frac{\mathrm{mgR}}{1+\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}}=\frac{\mathrm{mgR}}{2}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The change in the gravitational potential energy when a body of mass $m$ is raised to a height $n \mathrm{R}$ above the surface of the Earth is (here $R$ is the radius of the Earth)
questions.solution: Hints: $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\frac{m g h}{1+\frac{h}{\mathrm{R}}}=\frac{m g \times n \mathrm{R}}{1+\frac{n \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}}=\frac{n m g \mathrm{R}}{n+1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $2$ moles of an ideal gas with $\dfrac{C_p}{C_v} = \dfrac{5}{3}$ are mixed with $3$ moles of another ideal gas with $\dfrac{C_p}{C_v} = \dfrac{4}{3}$. The value of $\dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ for the mixture is
questions.solution:
For an ideal gas, the molar heat capacity at constant volume is given by $C_v = \dfrac{R}{\gamma - 1}$.

For the first gas ($n_1 = 2$ moles, $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{5}{3}$):
$C_{v1} = \dfrac{R}{\dfrac{5}{3} - 1} = \dfrac{3R}{2}$

For the second gas ($n_2 = 3$ moles, $\gamma_2 = \dfrac{4}{3}$):
$C_{v2} = \dfrac{R}{\dfrac{4}{3} - 1} = 3R$

The molar heat capacity at constant volume for the mixture is:
$C_{v_{mix}} = \dfrac{n_1 C_{v1} + n_2 C_{v2}}{n_1 + n_2}$

Substituting the values:
$C_{v_{mix}} = \dfrac{2 \left(\dfrac{3R}{2}\right) + 3(3R)}{2 + 3} = \dfrac{3R + 9R}{5} = \dfrac{12R}{5}$

The molar heat capacity at constant pressure for the mixture is:
$C_{p_{mix}} = C_{v_{mix}} + R = \dfrac{12R}{5} + R = \dfrac{17R}{5}$

The ratio of specific heats for the mixture is:
$\gamma_{mix} = \dfrac{C_{p_{mix}}}{C_{v_{mix}}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{17R}{5}}{\dfrac{12R}{5}} = \dfrac{17}{12}$

$\gamma_{mix} \approx 1.42$

Answer: $1.42$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Let $\overline{\mathrm{V}}, \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}, \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}$ denotes the mean speed, root mean square speed and most probable speed of the molecules each of mass $m$ in an ideal monoatomic gas at absolute temperature T Kelvin. Which statement(s) is/are correct?
options: No molecules can have speed greater than $\sqrt{2} V_{r m s}$
No molecules can have speed less than $V_p / \sqrt{2}$
Average kinetic energy of a molecule is $\frac{3}{4} m V_p^2$
questions.solution:
$V_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$
$V_{a v g}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{mp}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 R T}{\mathrm{M}}}, \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{p}} \lt \overline{\mathrm{V}} \lt \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}$
$\mathrm{K} . E=1 / 2 \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2=\frac{1}{2} m \frac{3 R T}{M}=\frac{3}{4} m V_p^2$

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If pressure of real gas $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ in a container is given by $\mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~V}-\mathrm{b}}-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{4 \mathrm{~b}^{2}}$, then the mass of the gas in the container is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~V}-\mathrm{b}}-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{4 \mathrm{~b}^{2}}$
$\left[\mathrm{P}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{4 \mathrm{~b}^{2}}\right]=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~V}-\mathrm{b}}$
$2\left[\mathrm{P}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{4 \mathrm{~b}^{2}}\right]\left[\mathrm{V}-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}\right]=\mathrm{RT}$
$\left[\mathrm{P}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{4 \mathrm{~b}^{2}}\right]\left[\mathrm{V}-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}\right]=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}=1 / 2 \mathrm{~mole}$
$\therefore$ Mass of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 32=16 \mathrm{gm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What will be the molar specific heat at constant volume of an ideal gas consisting of rigid diatomic molecules?
questions.solution:
For a gas at temperature $T$, the internal energy,
$$
U=\frac{f}{2} \mu R T
$$
where, $f=$ degree of freedom
$\Rightarrow$ Change in energy, $\Delta U=\frac{f}{2} \mu R \Delta T$
Also, as we know for any gas heat supplied at constant volume
$$
\left(\Delta Q_{V}\right)=\mu C_{V} \Delta T=\Delta U
$$
where, $C_{V}=$ molar specific heat at constant volume From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
C_{V}=\frac{1}{2} f R
$$
For diatomic gas, degree of freedom, $f=5$
$$
C_{V}=\frac{5}{2} R
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of a monoatomic ideal gas undergoes a quasistatic process, which is depicted by a straight line joining points $\left(V_{0}, T_{0}\right)$ and $\left(2 V_{0}, 3 T_{0}\right)$ in a ${V} \cdot T$ diagram. What is the value of the heat capacity of the gas at the point $\left(V_{0}, T_{0}\right) ?$
questions.solution:
Heat capacity of an ideal gas in a thermodynamic process,
$\because$ Ideal gas is monoatomic,
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{V} &=\frac{f R}{2}=\frac{3 R}{2} \\
C_{\text {process }} &=C_{V}+\frac{p}{n} \cdot \frac{d V}{d T} \\
&=\frac{3}{2} R+\frac{p}{n} \cdot \frac{V_{0}}{2 T} \\
\therefore C_{\text {process }}=\frac{3}{2} R+\frac{n R T}{n 2 T} &[\because p V=n R T] \\
=& \frac{3}{2} R+\frac{R}{2}=2 R
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Temperature of an ideal gas, initially at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is raised by $6^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The rms velocity of the gas molecules will
questions.solution:
Initial temperature of ideal gas,
$$
T_{1}=273+27=300 \mathrm{K}
$$
when temperature of gas is raised by $6^{\circ} \mathrm{C},$ the final temperature of gas
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{2} &=273+6+27 \\
&=306 \mathrm{K}
\end{aligned}
$$
Let initial velocities are $v_{r m_{1}},$ and $v_{r m_{2}}$
$v_{\mathrm{rms}} \propto \sqrt{T}$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{v_{\operatorname{mas}_{1}}}{v_{\operatorname{mas}_{2}}} &=\sqrt{\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}} \\ v_{\mathrm{rms}_{2}} &=\sqrt{\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} \times v_{\mathrm{ms}_{1}}} \\ &=\sqrt{\frac{306}{300}} \times v_{\mathrm{rms}} \\ &=1 \cdot 00 \times v_{\mathrm{rms}_{1}} \end{aligned}$
So, it will increase by $1 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the rms velocity of hydrogen gas at a certain temperature is $c,$ then the ras velocity of oxygen gas at the same temperature is
questions.solution:
$v_{\ln m}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$
$R=$ gas constant 
$T=$ absolute temperature 
$M=$ mass of gas
$v_{\text {rms }}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}}$
$v_{\text {rms }}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}}$
$\frac{v_{\mathrm{rms}} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2}}{v_{\mathrm{rma}} \cdot \mathrm{O}_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{O_{2}}}{M_{\mathrm{H}_{1}}}}$
Given $\quad v_{\operatorname{rms}} H_{2}=c$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{c}{v_{\operatorname{rms}} \mathrm{O}_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{0}}{M_{H_{1}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{32}{2}}=\sqrt{16}=4$
$\therefore \quad v_{rms}=\frac{C}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The r.m.s speed of the molecules of a gas at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $v .$ The temperature at which the r.m.s. speed will be $\sqrt{3} v$ is
questions.solution:
$v_{\text {tms }}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$
Given, $\quad T=100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=373 \mathrm{K}$
$\therefore \quad v=\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 373}{M}}$
For $\quad \sqrt{3} v=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\sqrt{3} v}{v}=\frac{\sqrt{3 R T / M}}{\sqrt{3 R \times 373 / M}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{3}=\sqrt{\frac{T}{373}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3=\frac{T}{373}$
$\Rightarrow \quad T=1119 \mathrm{K}$
or $\quad T=846^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 3 moles of mono-atomic gas $(\gamma=5 / 3)$ is mixed with 1 mole of a diatomic gas $(\gamma=7 / 3) .$ The value of $\gamma$ for the mixture will be
questions.solution:
The number of degrees of ireedom for the mixture is
$\therefore$$\begin{aligned} f_{\text {mix }} &=\frac{n_{1} f_{1}+n_{2} f_{2}}{n_{1}+n_{2}} \\ &=\frac{3 \times 3+1 \times 5}{4}=\frac{7}{2} \\ \gamma &=1+\frac{2}{f} \end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \gamma=1+\frac{4}{7}=\frac{11}{7}$

questions.q: The temperature of anideal gas is increased from $120 \mathrm{~K}$ to $480 \mathrm{~K}$. If at $120 \mathrm{~K}$, the root mean square speed of gas molecules is $v$, then at $480 \mathrm{~K}$ it will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three block of masses $m_1 = 2$ kg, $m_2 = 3$ kg and $m_3 = 5$ kg are placed on a horizontal frictionless surface and a force of $30$N pulls the system as shown below. The values of the tensions in the connecting strings are:
questions.solution:
Total mass of the system, $M = m_1 + m_2 + m_3 = 2 + 3 + 5 = 10 \text{ kg}$

Acceleration of the system, $a = \dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{30}{10} = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$

Let $T_1$ be the tension in the string connecting $m_1$ and $m_2$.
Equation of motion for $m_1$:
$T_1 = m_1 a = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ N}$

Let $T_2$ be the tension in the string connecting $m_2$ and $m_3$.
Equation of motion for the system of $m_1$ and $m_2$:
$T_2 = (m_1 + m_2) a = (2 + 3) \times 3 = 15 \text{ N}$

Therefore, the tensions in the connecting strings are $6 \text{ N}$ and $15 \text{ N}$.

Answer: $15$ N; $6$ N

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ is suspended from a point $O$ by a string of length $R$. It is given a velocity $u = 3\sqrt{gR}$ at the bottom. The difference in tension at point $B$ and at the point $C$ is
questions.solution:
Velocity at the lowest point A is $u = 3\sqrt{gR}$.

By conservation of mechanical energy, the velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ from the lowest point is given by:
$\dfrac{1}{2}mu^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgR(1 - \cos\theta)$
$v^2 = u^2 - 2gR(1 - \cos\theta)$

The tension $T$ in the string at this position is:
$T - mg\cos\theta = \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$
$T = \dfrac{m}{R}[u^2 - 2gR(1 - \cos\theta)] + mg\cos\theta$
$T = \dfrac{mu^2}{R} - 2mg + 3mg\cos\theta$

Substituting $u^2 = 9gR$:
$T = 9mg - 2mg + 3mg\cos\theta = 7mg + 3mg\cos\theta$

At point B, the string is horizontal, so $\theta = 90^\circ$:
$T_B = 7mg + 3mg\cos 90^\circ = 7mg$

At point C, the particle is at the highest point, so $\theta = 180^\circ$:
$T_C = 7mg + 3mg\cos 180^\circ = 7mg - 3mg = 4mg$

The difference in tension at point B and point C is:
$T_B - T_C = 7mg - 4mg = 3mg$

Answer: $3$ mg

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The minimum force required to start pushing a body up a rough (having co-efficient of friction $\mu$ ) inclined plane is $\vec{F}_1$ while the minimum force needed to prevent it from sliding is $\vec{F}_2$. If the inclined plane makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal such that $\tan \theta=2 \mu$, then the ratio $F_1 / F_2$ is
questions.solution: When body is being prevented from sliding down $\begin{aligned} & F_{\min }=F_2=m g \sin \theta-\mu m g \cos \theta \\ & F_2=m g \sin \theta-\mu m g \cos \theta\end{aligned}$ When body is pushed up friction acts downward $\mathrm{F}_1=\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta+\mu \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta$ $\left.\begin{array}{l}F_1-F_2=2 \mu \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta \\ F_1+F_2=2 m g \sin \theta\end{array}\right\} \quad \frac{F_1+F_2}{F_1-F_2}=\frac{\tan \theta}{\mu}=2: 1 \Rightarrow \frac{2 F_1}{2 F_2}=\frac{2+1}{2-1}$ or, $\frac{F_1}{F_2}=3: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=a \hat{i}+b \hat{\mathrm{j}}+c \hat{k}$ is acting on a body of mass $m$. The body was initially at rest at the origin. The co-ordinates of the body after time ' t ' will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \vec{F}=a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+c \hat{k} \\
& x=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a}{m}\right) t^2, y=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{b}{m}\right) t^2, z=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{c}{m}\right) t^2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a string, suspended from the ceilling is given a downward force $F_1$, its length becomes $L_1$, Its length is $L_2$, if the downward force is $\mathrm{F}_2$. What is its actual length?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} &\left(L_1-L\right)=\frac{F_1 L}{A y} ; L_2-L=\frac{F_2 L}{A y} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{L_1-L}{L_2-L}=\frac{F_1}{F_2} \Rightarrow F_2 L_1-F_2 L=F_1 L_2-F_1 L \Rightarrow L=\frac{F_2 L_1-F_1 L_2}{F_2-F_1} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A golf ball of mass $50 \mathrm{gm}$ placed on a tee, is struck by a golf-club. The speed of the golf ball as it leaves the tee is $100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, the time of contact on the ball is $0.02 \mathrm{~s}$. If the force decreases to zero linearly with time, then the force at the beginning of the contact is
questions.solution: Impulse $=$ Area under F-t graph $=$ change in linear momentum $$ =\frac{1}{2} \times F \times 0.02=\frac{50}{1000}(100-0) \Rightarrow F=500 \mathrm{~N} $$

questions.solution: $\mathrm{F}=6 \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{N}_{1}=5 \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{N}_{2}=3 \mathrm{a}$ $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{N}_{1}>\mathrm{N}_{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A simple pendulum, consisting of a small ball of mass $m$ attached to a massless string hanging vertically from the ceiling, is oscillating with an amplitude such that $T_{\max }=2 T_{\min }$ where $T_{\max }$ and $T_{\min }$ are the maximum and minimum tension in the string respectively. The value of maximum tension $\mathrm{T}_{\max }$ in the string is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{T}_{\min }=m g \cos \theta$ $\mathrm{T}_{\max }=\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\mathrm{mv}^{2}}{\mathrm{I}}=\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\mathrm{mv}^{2}}{\mathrm{I}}$ $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}=\mathrm{mgl}(1-\cos \theta)$ $\mathrm{T}_{\max }=2 \mathrm{~T}_{\min }$ $\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\mathrm{mv}^{2}}{\mathrm{I}}=2 \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta$ $\therefore \cos \theta=\frac{3}{4}$ $\mathrm{v}^{2}=2 \mathrm{gl}\left(1-\frac{3}{4}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{gl}}{2}$ $\mathrm{~T}_{\max }=\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{I}} \times \frac{\mathrm{g} \mathrm{l}}{2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{mg}}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass m rests on a horizontal table with a co-efficient of static friction $\mu .$ What minimum force must be applied on the block to drag it on the table?
questions.solution:
Hint:
$F \sin \theta+N=m g \quad N=m g-F \sin \theta$ $F \cos \theta=\mu(m g-F \sin \theta) \quad F=\frac{\mu m g}{\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta}$, for $F_{\min }, \frac{d}{d \theta}[\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta]=0$ $\therefore \tan \theta=\mu$ $\therefore F_{\min }=\frac{\mu \mathrm{mg}}{\sqrt{1+\mu^{2}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic block of mass $20 \mathrm{~kg}$ is dragged with a uniform velocity of $0.5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ on a horizontal table for $2.1 \mathrm{~s}$. The co-efficient of static friction between the block and the table is $0.10$. What will be the maximum possible rise in temperature of the metal block if the specific heat of the block is $0.1$ C.G.S unit ? Assume $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ uniform rise in temperature throughout the whole block. [lgnore absorption of heat by the table]
questions.solution:
Hint: 
friction $f=\mu \mathrm{mg}=0.1 \times 20 \times 10=20$
$\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{f}}=$ heat
$\Rightarrow(\mathrm{f.u}) \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{mc} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
$\Rightarrow 20 \times 0.5 \times 2.1=20 \times 0.1 \times 4.2 \times 10^{3} . \Delta \mathrm{T}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{T}=0.0025^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two weights of the mass $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}\left(>m_{1}\right)$ are joined by an inextensible string of negligible mass passing over a fixed frictionless pulley. The magnitude of the acceleration of the loads is
questions.solution: According to the question, we can draw the following diagram Here, $T$ is the tension in the string. In equilibrium condition. For mass $m_{1}, \quad T-m_{1} g=m_{1} a$....(i) For mass $m_{2}, \quad m_{2} g-T=m_{2} a$....(ii) After adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $$ \begin{aligned} m_{2} g-m_{1} g &=m_{1} a+m_{2} a \\ \left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right) g &=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) a \\ a &=\frac{\left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right)}{\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)} g \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $m_{2}$ is placed on a horizontal table and another block of mass $m_{1}$ is placed on top of it. An increasing horizontal force \(F=\alpha t\) is exerted on the upper block but the lower block never moves as a result. If the coefficient of friction between the blocks is $\mu_{i}$ and that between the lower block and the table is $\mu_{2}$, then what is the maximum possible value of $\mu_{1} / \mu_{2} ?$
questions.solution: According to the question. $\therefore \quad N_{2}=m_{2} g+N_{1}=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g$ : Lower block never moves. $\therefore \quad f_{r_{2}} \geq f_{n}$ $\quad \mu_{2} N_{2} \geq \mu_{1} N_{1} \Rightarrow \mu_{2}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g \geq \mu_{1} m_{1} g$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m_{1}+m_{2}}{m_{1}} \geq \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}} \leq 1+\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$ $\left.\therefore \quad \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}\right|_{\operatorname{man}}=1+\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The velocity $(v)$ of a particle (under a force $F$ ) depends on its distance $(x)$ from the origin (with $x>0) v \propto\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}$. Find how the magnitude
of the force $(F)$ on the particle depends on $x ?$
questions.solution:
According to the question.
$$
v \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}
$$
or $v=\frac{k}{\sqrt{x}}$
$$
\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t} \cdot \frac{K}{\sqrt{x}}=k \cdot \frac{-1}{2} \cdot x^{-\frac{1}{2}-1} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}
$$
$a=-\frac{k}{2} \cdot x^{-\frac{3}{2}} \cdot \frac{k}{\sqrt{x}}\left[\because \frac{d x}{d t}=v\right.$ and $v=\frac{k}{\sqrt{x}}$ from $\left.\mathrm{Eq}(i)\right]$
$$
=\frac{-k^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}}
$$
$=m \frac{k^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}}$
(magnitue
$F=\frac{k^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{m}{x^{2}}$
$\therefore$
$F x \frac{1}{x^{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Work done for a certain spring when it is streched to 1mm from mean position is 10J amount of work that must be done on the spring to stretch it further by $1 \mathrm{mm}$ is
questions.solution:
The work done in stretching the spring, $W=\frac{1}{2} K x^{2}$
$K=$ force constant of spring 
$x=$ extension in length of the spring
$$
\begin{array}{l}
W_{1}=\frac{1}{2} K x_{1}^{2}. \\
W_{2}=\frac{1}{2} K x_{2}^{2} \\
x_{1}=1 \\
x_{2}=1+1=2 \\
\frac{W_{1}}{W_{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} K(1)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} K(2)^{2}}=\frac{1}{4} \\
W_{2}=4 W_{1} \\
W_{1}=10 \mathrm{J} \\
W_{2}=40 \mathrm{J}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ More work required $=40 \mathrm{J}-10 \mathrm{J}=30 \mathrm{J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Block $B$ lying on a table weighs W. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the table is $\mu$. Assume that the cord between $B$ and the knot is horizontal. The maximum weight of the block $A$ for which the system will be stationary is
questions.solution: Let weight of $A$ is $W^{\prime}$. From the free body diagram. For equilibrium of the system, $$ \begin{aligned} T \cos \theta &=\mu N=\mu W \\ T \sin \theta &=W^{\prime} \end{aligned} $$ where $T=$ tension in the thread lying between knot and the support. On divindg, we get $\frac{T \sin \theta}{T \cos \theta}=\frac{W^{\prime}}{\mu W}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta=\frac{W^{\prime}}{\mu W}$ $\Rightarrow W^{\prime}=\mu W \tan \theta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A smooth massless string passes over a smooth fixed pulley. Two masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2},\left(m_{1}>m_{2}\right)$ are tied at the two ends of the string. The masses are allowed to move under gravity starting from rest. The total external force acting on the two masses is
questions.solution:
We know that Acceleration, $a_{\mathrm{CM}}=\left(\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right)^{2} \times g\left(\because m_{1}>m_{2}\right)$
So, resultant external force.
$$
\begin{aligned}
F &=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) a_{\mathrm{CM}} \\
&=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) \times\left(\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right)^{2} \times g \\
&=\frac{\left(m_{1}-m_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)} \times g
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.q: A block of mass $m(=0.1 \mathrm{kg})$ is hanging over a frictionless light fixed pulley by an inextensible string of negligible mass. The other end of the string is pulled by a constant force $F$ in the vertically downward direction. The linear momentum of the block increases by $2 \mathrm{kgms}^{-1}$ in $1 \mathrm{s}$ after the block starts from rest. Then, (given $g=10 \mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ )
options: The tension in the string is $F$
The tension in the string is $3 \mathrm{N}$
The work done by the tension on the block is $20 \mathrm{J}$ during this 1 s
The work done against the force of gravity is $10 \mathrm{J}$
questions.solution: The free body diagram $\Rightarrow \quad F=2+m g=3 N$ also, $a=\frac{\text { unbalanced force }}{\text { mass }}=\frac{2}{0.1}=20 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ $S=\frac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 1=10 \mathrm{m}$ Honce, work done by tension $$ =F \times 10=3 \times 10=30 \mathrm{J} $$ So, the work done against gravity $$ =m g \times S=1 \times 10=10 \mathrm{J} $$

questions.solution: We know that, $F=m a$ $$ a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{14}{7}=2 \mathrm{ms}^{-2} $$ Hence, from the figure $$ 14-N=4 a $$ $$ \begin{array}{r} 14-N=8 \\ N=6 \mathrm{N} \end{array} $$

questions.q: A boy of mass $40 \mathrm{~kg}$ is climbing a vertical pole at a constant speed. If the coefficient of friction between his palms and the pole is 0.8 and $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, the horizontal force that he is applying on the pole is

$$

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There is a ring of radius $r$ having linear charge density $\lambda$ and rotating with a uniform angular velocity $\omega$. The magnitude of the magnetic field produced by this ring at its own centre would be ($\mu_0$ = permeability of air)
questions.solution:
Total charge on the ring is $Q = \lambda (2\pi r)$.

Time period of rotation is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$.

Equivalent current due to the rotating charge is $I = \dfrac{Q}{T} = \dfrac{\lambda (2\pi r)}{2\pi / \omega} = \lambda \omega r$.

The magnetic field at the centre of a circular current-carrying loop is $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2r}$.

Substituting the value of $I$, we get $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 (\lambda \omega r)}{2r} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \lambda \omega}{2}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{\mu_0\lambda\omega}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil, carrying current, has radius $R$. The distance from the centre of the coil on the axis where the magnetic induction will be $\dfrac{1}{27}$ th of its value at the centre of the coil is
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is given by

$B_c = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$

The magnetic field at a distance $x$ on the axis of the coil is given by

$B_a = \dfrac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

Given that $B_a = \dfrac{1}{27} B_c$, we have

$\dfrac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1}{27} \left( \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R} \right)$

$\dfrac{R^2}{(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1}{27R}$

$\dfrac{R^3}{(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1}{27}$

Taking the cube root on both sides, we get

$\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{R^2 + x^2}} = \dfrac{1}{3}$

Squaring both sides, we get

$\dfrac{R^2}{R^2 + x^2} = \dfrac{1}{9}$

$9R^2 = R^2 + x^2$

$x^2 = 8R^2$

$x = 2\sqrt{2}R$

Answer: $2\sqrt{2}R$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic moment of an iron bar is $M$. It is now bent in such a way that it forms an arc section of a circle subtending an angle of $60°$ at the centre. The magnetic moment of the arc section is
questions.solution:
Let the pole strength of the iron bar be $m$ and its length be $L$.

The initial magnetic moment is given by $M = mL$.

When the bar is bent into an arc of a circle of radius $R$ subtending an angle $\theta = 60^{\circ} = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ radians at the centre, the length of the arc remains $L$.

Using the relation $L = R\theta$, we get:

$L = R \left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} \right) \Rightarrow R = \dfrac{3L}{\pi}$

The new magnetic length is the straight-line distance (chord length) between the two poles, which is given by:

$d = 2R \sin\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = 2R \sin(30^{\circ}) = 2R \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = R$

Substituting the value of $R$:

$d = \dfrac{3L}{\pi}$

The new magnetic moment $M'$ is:

$M' = md = m \left( \dfrac{3L}{\pi} \right) = \dfrac{3}{\pi} (mL) = \dfrac{3M}{\pi}$

Answer: $\dfrac{3M}{\pi}$

questions.solution:
Hint :
Velocity along z-direction will be const. \(\mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{s}}{V_z}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{sec}\) Path will be helical.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle of charge \(q\) and mass \(m\) is placed at a distance \(2 R\) from the centre of a vertical cylindrical region of radius \(R\) where magnetic field varies as \(\vec{B}=\left(4 t^2-2 t+6\right) \hat{k}\) where \(t\) is time. Then which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
options: Induced electric field lines form closed loops
Electric field varies linearly with \(r\) if \(r  < R\), where \(r\) is the radial distance from the centerline of the cylinder
The charged particle will move in clockwise direction when viewed from top
Acceleration of the charged particle is \(\frac{7 q}{2 m}\) when \(t=2 \mathrm{sec}\)
questions.solution:
Hint :
\(r < R\) \(E \times 2 \pi r=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(4 t^2-2 t+6\right) \times \pi r^2\) \(\begin{aligned} & E \times 2 \pi r=(8 t-2) \pi r^2 \\ & E=\frac{(8 t-2) r}{2} \\ & E=(4 t-1) r \end{aligned}\) \(E \propto r\) For \(r > R\) \(\begin{aligned} & E \times 2 \pi \cdot(2 R)=\frac{d}{d t}\left[4 t^2-2 t+6\right] \times \pi[R]^2 \\ & E .4 \pi R=[8 t-2] \pi R^2 \\ & E=\frac{[8 t-2] R}{4} \quad \text { at } t=2, E=\frac{14}{4} R=\frac{7 R}{2} \\ & \text { acceleration }=\frac{E q}{m}=\frac{7 R q}{2 m}=\frac{7 q R}{2 m} \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform magnetic field \(B\) exists in a region. An electron of charge \(q\) and mass \(m\) moving with velocity \(v\) enters the region in a direction perpendicular to the magnetic field. Considering Bohr angular momentum quantization, which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
options: The maximum velocity of the electron is \(\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{qB} \hbar}}{\mathrm{m}}\)
Transition frequency \(\omega\) between two successive levels is independent of \(n\)
questions.solution:
Hint: \(r=\frac{m v}{q B}\) \(m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\) \(m v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi r}\) \(r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi r q B}\) \(\begin{aligned} & r^2=\frac{n h}{2 \pi q B} \Rightarrow r=\sqrt{\frac{n h}{2 \pi q B}} \\ & r \propto \sqrt{n} \\ & v=\frac{q B r}{m}=\frac{q B}{m} \sqrt{\frac{n h}{2 \pi q B}} \\ & \underset{m i n}{v}=\sqrt{\frac{q^2 B^2}{m^2} \times \frac{n h}{2 \pi q B}}=\sqrt{\frac{n \mathrm{qBh}}{2 \pi m^2}}=\frac{1}{m} \sqrt{q B} \hbar\end{aligned}\) \(\begin{aligned} E & =\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} m\left[\frac{n q B h}{2 \pi m^2}\right]\end{aligned}\) \(E \propto n\)
\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_2-\mathrm{E}_1=\left(\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1\right) \frac{\mathrm{qBh}}{4 \pi \mathrm{m}} \\ & \mathrm{hf}=\left(\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1\right) \frac{\mathrm{qBh}}{4 \pi \mathrm{m}} \\ & \mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1=1 \text { for successive levels }\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two infinite line-charges parallel to each other are moving with a constant velocity $v$ in the same direction as shown in the figure. The separation between two line-charges is $d$. The magnetic attraction balances the electric repulsion when, [c = speed of light in free space]
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{q}_2 \mathrm{E}_1=\lambda_2 \ell \frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda_1}{\mathrm{~d}}$
$\mathrm{~F}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}_1 \mathrm{i}_2 \ell}{2 \pi \mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mu_0 \lambda_1 \mathrm{~V} \lambda_2 \mathrm{~V}}{2 \pi \mathrm{d}} \times \ell$
$\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \ell}{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mu_0 \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \mathrm{~V}^2 \ell}{2 \pi \mathrm{d}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \ell}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\mu_0 \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \mathrm{~V}^2 \ell}{2 \pi}$
$\mathrm{V}^2=\frac{1}{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}=\mathrm{c}^2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{c}$
