questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The poisson's ratio of a material is 0.5 . If a force is applied to a wire of this material, there is a decrease in the cross-sectional area by $4 \%$. The percentage increase in the length is :
questions.solution:
Poisson ratio $=0.5$
Therefore density is constant hence change in volume is zero we have $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{A} \times \ell=$ constant
$\log \mathrm{V}=\log \mathrm{A}+\log \ell$ or $\frac{d A}{A}+\frac{d \ell}{\ell}=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d \ell}{\ell}=-\frac{d A}{A}$
That is $4 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: From a tower of height $H$, a particle is thrown vertically upwards with a speed $u$. The time taken by the particle to hit the ground is $n$ times that taken by it to reach the highest point of its path. The relation between $H, u$ and $n$ is
questions.solution:
Time taken by the particle to reach the highest point is given by $v = u - gt$.

At the highest point, $v = 0$, so $t_1 = \dfrac{u}{g}$.

The time taken to hit the ground is $t_2 = n t_1 = \dfrac{nu}{g}$.

Using the equation of motion $S = ut - \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$ for the complete journey, taking upward direction as positive:

$-H = u t_2 - \dfrac{1}{2}g t_2^2$

Substituting $t_2 = \dfrac{nu}{g}$:

$-H = u \left( \dfrac{nu}{g} \right) - \dfrac{1}{2}g \left( \dfrac{nu}{g} \right)^2$

$-H = \dfrac{nu^2}{g} - \dfrac{n^2u^2}{2g}$

Multiplying the entire equation by $-2g$:

$2gH = n^2u^2 - 2nu^2$

$2gH = nu^2(n - 2)$

Answer: $2gH = nu^2(n - 2)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the velocity-time $(v - t)$ graph(s) can possibly represent one-dimensional motion of a particle?
questions.solution:
For a particle in one-dimensional motion, the velocity $v$ must be a single-valued function of time $t$. This means that at any given instant of time, the particle can have only one unique velocity.

In the graphs given in options (B) and (C), a vertical line drawn at certain values of $t$ intersects the curve at multiple points. This implies that the particle has multiple velocities at the same instant of time, which is physically impossible. Furthermore, time cannot flow backwards, which is depicted in parts of these graphs.

In the graphs given in options (A) and (D), for every value of time $t$, there is exactly one corresponding value of velocity $v$. Therefore, these graphs represent physically possible one-dimensional motions.

Answer: ;

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two particles are simultaneously projected in the horizontal direction from a point $P$ at a certain height. The initial velocities of the particles are oppositely directed to each other and have magnitude $v$ each. The separation between the particles at a time when their position vectors (drawn from the point $P$ ) are mutually perpendicular, is
questions.solution: According to the question, Representation of position vectors of two particles (drawn from the point $P$ In two dimension, the position vectors $\mathbf{r}_{1}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{2}$ represented as $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathbf{r}_{1}=v t \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2} \mathbf{j} ...(i) \\ \mathbf{r}_{2}=v t(-\hat{\mathbf{i}})-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}(\mathbf{j})...(ii) \end{array} $$ $\because$ We know that, when the two vectors are mutually perpendicular, i.e. $$ \theta=90^{\circ} $$ So, $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathbf{r}_{1} \cdot \mathbf{r}_{2}=r_{1} r_{2} \cos 90^{\circ} \\ \mathbf{r}_{1} \cdot \mathbf{r}_{2}=0 \end{array} $$ Substituting the values $\mathbf{r}_{1}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{2}$ in the above relation, we get $$ \left((v t) \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2} \mathbf{j}\right) \cdot\left(v t(-\hat{\mathbf{i}})-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}(\mathbf{j})\right)=0 $$ $-v^{2} t^{2}+\frac{1}{4} 4 g^{2} t^{4}=0($ where, $\hat{\mathbf{i}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{i}}=\hat{\mathbf{j}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{j}}=\mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{k}=1)$ $$ \begin{aligned} v^{2} t^{2} &=\frac{1}{4} g^{2} t^{4} \\ \Rightarrow \quad v^{2} &=\frac{1}{4} g^{2} t^{2} \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ Magnitude of velocity of the particles, $v=\frac{1}{2} g t$ We know that, separation distance between particles at a time $t$ $$ \begin{array}{c} \Delta x=2 v t \\ \Delta x=2 \times v \times \frac{2 v}{g} \Rightarrow \dot{\Delta} x=\frac{4 v^{2}}{g} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A train moves from rest with acceleration and in time $t_{1}$ covers a distance $x$. It then decelerates to rest at constant retardation $\beta$ for distance $y$ in time $t_{2}$. Then,
questions.solution: Shape of the graph $\mathrm{OP}$ shows the acceleration in train $\therefore \tan \theta=$ acceleration. $$ \boldsymbol{\alpha}=\frac{V_{0}}{t_{1}} $$ Shape of the graph pt shows the deceleration in train. $\therefore$ Similarly. $\quad \beta=\frac{v_{0}}{t_{2}}$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\boldsymbol{\beta}}{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}=\frac{\boldsymbol{v}_{0} / \boldsymbol{t}_{\boldsymbol{2}}}{\mathrm{v}_{0} / t_{1}}=\frac{t_{1}}{t_{2}}$ Displacement = area of graph obtained between v-t $$ \begin{array}{l} x=\frac{1}{2} t_{1}, v_{0} \\ y=\frac{1}{2} t_{2}, v_{0} \end{array} $$ $\therefore \frac{x}{y}=\frac{t_{1}}{t_{2}} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{y}=\frac{t_{1}}{t_{2}}=\frac{\beta}{a}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moves with constant acceleration along a straight line starting from rest. The percentage increase in its displacement during the 4th second compared to that in the 3rd second is
questions.solution:
We know that
$S=u+\frac{1}{2} a(2 n-1)$
$S_{3rd \text { sec}}=0+\frac{1}{2} a(2 \times 3-1)=\frac{5}{2} a$ (for $n=3$ s)
$S_{4th \text { sec}}=0+\frac{1}{2} a(2 \times 4-1)=\frac{7}{2} a \quad($ for $n=4 \mathrm{s})$
So, the percentage increase
$=\frac{S_{4 t h}-S_{3 \mathrm{rd}}}{S_{3 \mathrm{rd}}} \times 100$
$\begin{array}{l} =\frac{\frac{7}{2} a-\frac{5}{2} a}{\frac{5}{2} a} \times 100 \\ =\frac{\frac{2 a}{2}}{\frac{5}{2}} \times 100=2 \times 20=40 \% \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moves along $x$ -axis and its displacement at any time is given by $x(t)=2 t^{3}-3 t^{2}+4 t$ in SI units. The velocity of the particle when its acceleration is zero, is
questions.solution:
Given, $x(t)=2 t^{3}-3 t^{2}+4 t$
So, velocity, $v=\frac{d x}{d t}=\left(6 t^{2}-6 t+4\right)$
and acceleration $a=\frac{d v}{d t}=(12 t-6)$
when acceleration is zero, $i . e . .(12 t-6)=0$
or
$$
t=\frac{6}{12}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{s}
$$
$\therefore$ Velocity of the particle at zero acceleration is
$$
v=6\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}-6\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) 4=2.5 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is travelling along a straight line $O X$. The distance $x$ (in metre) of the particle from 0 at a time t is given by $x=37+27 t-t^{3}$, where t is time in seconds. The distance of the particle from $O$ when it comes to rest is
questions.solution:
Given, $x=37+27 t-t^{3}$
$$
v=\frac{d x}{d t}=27-3 t^{2}
$$
According to problem,
$$
v=0 \Rightarrow 27-3 t^{2}=0
$$
Here, $t=3 \mathrm{s}$
$$
x=37+27 \times 3-(3)^{2}=91 \mathrm{m}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bullet on penetrating $30 \mathrm{cm}$ into its target loses its velocity by $50 \%$. What additional distance will it penetrate into the target before it comes to rest?
questions.solution:
Given $S=30 \mathrm{cm}=30 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{m}$ and loss in
velocity is $50 \%$ Equation of motion
$$
v^{2}=u^{2}+2 a s
$$
Here, $\frac{u^{2}}{4}=u^{2}+2 a \times 30 \times 10^{-2}$
$$
0=\frac{u^{2}}{4}+3 a \times x
$$
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
x=10 \mathrm{cm}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The acceleration ' $a$ ' (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ ) of a body, starting from rest varies with time $t$ (in $s$ ) following the equation $a=3 t+4$ The velocity of the body at time $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
questions.solution:
$a=3 t+4$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d V}{d t}=3 t+4 \\ & \int_0^V d V=\int_0^t(3 t+4) d t \\ & V=\frac{3 t^2}{2}+4 t=\frac{12}{2}+8=14 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $m$ is thrown with velocity u from the origin of a co-ordinate axes at an angle $\theta$ with the horizon. The magnitude of the angular momentum of the particle about the origin at time $\mathrm{t}$ when it is at the maximum height of the trajectory is proportional to
questions.solution: $\vec{L}=m u \cos \theta h_{\max }=m u \cos \theta \frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g} \Rightarrow L=\frac{m u^3 \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta}{2 g} \quad L \propto u^3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is moving in $x-y$ plane according to $\vec{r}=b \cos \omega t \hat{i}+b \sin \omega t \hat{j}$. Where $\omega$ is constant. Which of the following statement(s) is / are true?
options: $\frac{E}{\omega}$ is a constant where $E$ is the total energy of the particle
The trajectory of the particle in $x-y$ plane is a circle
In $a_x-a_y$ plane, trajectory of the particle is an ellipse $\left(a_x, a_y\right.$ denotes the components of acceleration)
questions.solution:
$\vec{r}=b \cos \omega t i+b \sin \omega t j$
$\vec{v}=\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t}=-b \omega \sin \omega t+b \omega \cos \omega t j$
$(\vec{v})=\sqrt{b^2 \omega^2 \sin ^2 \omega t+b^2 \omega^2 \cos ^2 \omega t}$
$(\vec{v})=b \omega \rightarrow$ constant $\quad \therefore E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=$ constant
$E=\frac{1}{2} m\left[b^2 \omega^2\right] \quad \Rightarrow \frac{E}{\omega}=\frac{1}{2} m b^2 \cdot \omega=$ constant
$x=b \cos \omega t$
$y=b \sin \omega t$
$x^2+y^2=b^2 \rightarrow$ equation of circle
$\therefore \vec{a} \perp \vec{v} \quad$ So $\vec{a} \neq \omega^2 \vec{v}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A fighter plane, flying horizontally with a speed $360 \mathrm{kmph}$ at an altitude of $500 \mathrm{~m}$ drops a bomb for a target straight ahead of it on the ground. The bomb .should be dropped at what approximate distance ahead of the target ? Assume that acceleration due to gravity $(\mathrm{g})$ is $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$. Also neglect air drag
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$v=360 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}=100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} .$
$\mathrm{h}=500 \mathrm{~m}$
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{u} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~h}}{\mathrm{~g}}}=100 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 500}{10}}=1000 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile thrown with an initial velocity of $10 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ at an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. has a range of $5 \mathrm{m}$. Taking $g=10 \mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ and neglecting air resistance, what will he the estimated value of $\alpha$?
questions.solution:
Given, initial velocity of particle,
$$
u=10 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}
$$
Range, $R=5 \mathrm{m}$ Gravitational acceleration,
$$
g=10 \mathrm{ms}^{-2}
$$
As we known that, 
Range of projectile, $R=\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}$
or
$$
\begin{aligned}
5 &=\frac{(10)^{2} \sin 2 \alpha}{10} \\
\sin 2 \alpha &=\frac{5}{10} \\
\sin 2 \alpha &=\frac{1}{2} \\
\alpha &=15^{\circ} \\
\left(90^{\circ}-15^{\circ}\right.&\left.=75^{\circ}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cricket ball thrown across a field is at heights $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$ from the point of projection at times $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ respectively after the throw. The ball is caught by a fielder at the same height as that of projection. The time of flight of the ball in this journey is
questions.solution:
For vertically moment (for $\left.h_{1}\right)$
or
$\begin{array}{l}
h_{1}=u \sin \theta t_{1}-\frac{1}{2} g t_{1}^{2} \\
t_{1}=\frac{h_{1}+\frac{1}{2} g t_{1}^{2}}{u \sin \theta}
\end{array}$
$h_{2}=u \sin \theta t_{2}-\frac{1}{2} g t_{2}^{2}$
or $t_{2}=\frac{h_{2}+\frac{1}{2} g t_{2}^{2}}{u \sin \theta}$
Divide Eq. (i) by Eq. (ia)
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{t_{1}}{t_{2}}=\frac{h_{1}+\frac{1}{2} g t_{1}^{2} / u \sin \theta}{h_{2}+\frac{1}{2} g t_{2}^{2} / u \sin \theta} \\
\Rightarrow h_{1} t_{2}-h_{2} t_{1}=\frac{g}{2}\left(t_{1} t_{2}^{2}-t_{1}^{2} t_{2}\right)
\end{array}$
The time of flight of the ball $T=\frac{2 u \sin \theta}{g}=\frac{2}{g}(u \sin \theta) \quad$ 
$=\frac{2}{g}\left[\frac{h_{1}+\frac{1}{2} g t_{1}^{2}}{t_{1}}\right]=\frac{2}{t_{1}}\left[\frac{h_{1}}{g}+\frac{t_{1}^{2}}{2}\right]$
$=\frac{h_{1}}{t_{1}} \times \frac{2}{g}+t_{1}=\frac{h_{1}}{t_{1}} \times\left(\frac{t_{1} t_{2}^{2}-t_{1}^{2} t_{2}}{h_{1} t_{2}-h_{2} t_{1}}\right)+t_{1}$
$=\frac{h_{1} t_{1} t_{2}^{2}-h_{1} t_{1}^{2} t_{2}+h_{1} t_{1}^{2} t_{2}-h_{2} t_{1}^{3}}{t_{1}\left(h_{1} t_{2}-h_{2} t_{1}\right)}$
$=\left(\frac{h_{1} t_{2}^{2}-h_{2} t_{1}^{2}}{h_{1} t_{2}-h_{2} t_{1}}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A radioactive element ${}^{242}_{92}X$ emits two $\alpha$-particle, one electrons and two positrons. The transformed nucleus is represented by ${}^{234}_{P}Y$. The value of $P$ is
questions.solution:
The initial atomic number of the nucleus is $Z = 92$.

The change in atomic number due to the emissions is:

Two $\alpha$-particles (${}^{4}_{2}\text{He}$): $\Delta Z = -2 \times 2 = -4$

One electron (${}^{0}_{-1}\text{e}$): $\Delta Z = +1$

Two positrons (${}^{0}_{+1}\text{e}$): $\Delta Z = -2 \times 1 = -2$

The final atomic number $P$ is:

$P = 92 - 4 + 1 - 2 = 87$

Answer: $87$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A nucleus X emits a beta particle to produce a nucleus Y. If their atomic masses are $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{x}}$ and $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{y}}$ respectively, the maximum energy of the beta particle emitted is (where $m_{e}$ is the mass of an electron and $c$ is the velocity of light)
questions.solution:
Hint:
$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{zm}_{\mathrm{e}}$ $\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{y}}-(\mathrm{z}+1) \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{y}}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}$ $\mathrm{E}=\Delta \mathrm{mC}^{2}=\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{y}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right) \mathrm{C}^{2}=\left(\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{y}}\right) \mathrm{C}^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For nuclei with mass number close to 119 and 238 , the binding energies per nucleon are approximately $7.6 \mathrm{MeV}$ and $8.6 \mathrm{MeV}$ respectively. If a nucleus of mass number 238 breaks into two nuclei of nearly equal masses, what will be the approximate amount of energy released in the process of fission?
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$119 \rightarrow 7.6 \mathrm{MeV}$
$238 \rightarrow 8.6 \mathrm{MeV}$
$238 \rightarrow 119+119$
$E_{1}=238 \times 8.6$
$E_{t}=238 \times 7.6$
$\therefore E_{t}-E_{1}=-238 \mathrm{MeV}$
Anegative value indicates the fission cannot take place. The data given are incorrect. The BE per nucleon values are swapped in question, it should have been $7.6 \mathrm{MeV}$ for 238 and $8.6 \mathrm{MeV}$ for 119 . Thus $\Delta \mathrm{E}=+238 \mathrm{MeV}$. Thus 214 is closest and will be taken as right answer

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parent nucleus $X$ undergoes $\alpha$ -decay with a half-life of 75000 yrs. The daughter nucleus $Y$ undergoes $\beta$ -decay with a half-life of 9 months. In a particular sample, it is found that the rate of emission of $\beta$ -particles is nearly constant (over several months) at $10^{7} / \mathrm{h} .$ What will be the number of $\alpha$ -particles emitted in an hour?
questions.solution:
According to the question,
$X \stackrel{\text { a-particle }}{\longrightarrow} Y \longrightarrow Z(\text { say) }$
According to Rutherford Soddy law of radioactive decay, the rate of decay of radioactive along at any instant is proportional to the number of atoms present at that instant
$\frac{d N}{d t}=-\lambda N$
since, $\lambda=$ constant (decay constant) and $N=$ number of atoms
Rate of decay of $Y$ particle is given as,
$\lambda_{X} N_{X}-\lambda_{\gamma} N_{Y}=\frac{d N_{Y}}{d t}=0$
[ $\because$ Decay rate for $\beta$ -particle become constant after some time]
Given, rate of emission of $\beta$ -particle $=10^{7} \mathrm{h}$ $\lambda_{X} N_{X}=\lambda_{\gamma} N_{Y}=10^{7} / \mathrm{h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the half-life of a radioactive nucleus is 3 days, nearly what fraction of the initial number of nuclei will decay on the third day? (Given, $\sqrt[3]{0.25} \approx 0.63)$
questions.solution:
Given, $t_{1 / 2}=3$ days
Number of active nuclei remaining after time $t$,
$N=N_{0}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{t_{1 / 2}}}$
After $t=2$ days,
$N_{1}=N_{0}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2 / 3}=\frac{N_{0}}{2^{2 / 3}}=\frac{N_{0}}{4^{1 / 3}}=0.63 \mathrm{N}_{0}$
$\therefore \quad N_{1}=0.63 \mathrm{N}_{0}$
After $t=3$ days,
$N_{2}=\frac{N_{0}}{2}=0.5 \mathrm{N}_{0}$
Number of nuclei that will decay on the 3rd day. $N_{3}=N_{2}-N_{1}=0.63 \mathrm{N}_{0}-0.5 \mathrm{N}_{0}=013 \mathrm{N}_{0}$
In the term, fraction is 0.13.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Radon- 222 has a half-life of 3.8 days. If one starts with $0.064 \mathrm{kg}$ of radon-222, the quantity of radon- 222 left after 19 days will be
questions.solution:
Half life of radon $=3 \cdot 8$ days 
Total half-lives in 19 days
$\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{19}{3 \cdot 8} \\
=\frac{190}{38}
\end{array}$
$=5$ half-lives
Initially mass of radon $=0.064 \mathrm{kg}$
$0 \cdot 064 \frac{T_{1}}{T_{3}} 0 \cdot 032 \stackrel{T_{2}}{\longrightarrow}$
$0 \cdot 016 \stackrel{T_{4}}{\longrightarrow} 0 \cdot 004 \stackrel{T_{5}}{\longrightarrow} 0 \cdot 002$
So in five half-life periods, the Radon sample will reduce to $0.002 \mathrm{kg}$
$\text {Or } \frac{N}{N_{0}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5}$
$\Rightarrow \quad N=0 \cdot 064 \times \frac{1}{32}$
$=0 \cdot 002 \mathrm{kg}$

$\beta$ decay, which one of the following cannot occur?
options: Isobar of original nucleus is produced
Isotope of the original nucleus is produced
Nuclei with higher atomic number than that of the original nucleus is produced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The number of atoms of a radioactive substance of half-life $T$ is $N_{0}$ at $t=0 .$ The time necessary to decay from $N_{0} / 2$ atoms to $N_{0} / 10$ atoms will be
questions.solution:
We have, $\frac{N(t)}{N_{0}}=e^{-\lambda t}$ or $N(t)=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$
$\therefore$ For the given condition. $\frac{N_{0}}{2}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t_{1}}$ and $\frac{N_{0}}{10}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}=e^{-\lambda t_{1}}$ and $\frac{1}{10}=e^{-\lambda t_{2}}$
or $\quad e^{\lambda t_{1}}=2$ and $e^{\lambda t_{2}}=10$
Taking log on both sides, $\lambda t_{1}=\log 2 \Rightarrow t_{1}=\frac{\log 2}{\lambda}$
or $\quad t_{1}=\frac{\log 2 \times \mathrm{T}}{\log 2}$
and $\quad \lambda t_{2}=\log 10$
$\Rightarrow \quad t_{2}=\frac{\log 10}{\lambda}$
of $\quad t_{2}=\frac{\log 10 \times \mathrm{T}}{\log 2}$
$\therefore \quad\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)=T\left[\frac{\log 10}{\log 2}-1\right]=T\left[\frac{\log 10-\log 2}{\log 2}\right]$
$=T\left[\frac{\log 5}{\log 2}\right]$
$\Rightarrow\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)=T \left[\frac{\log 5}{\log 2}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An alpha particle $\left({ }^{4} \mathrm{He}\right)$ has a mass of 4.00300 amu. A proton has a mass of 1.00783 amu and a neutron has a mass of 1.00867 amu respectively. The binding energy of alpha estimated from these data is the closest to
questions.solution:
The mass defect, $\Delta m=2\left(m_{p}+m_{n}\right)-m_{\text {He }}$
$=2(1.00783+1.00867)-4.00300$
$=0.0300$ amu
So, the binding energy, $E=\Delta m c^{2}$
$=0.03 \times 931 \mathrm{MeV}=27.9 \mathrm{MeV}$

questions.q:
Two radionctive substances $A$ and $B$ have decay constants $5 \lambda$ and $\lambda$ respectively. At $t=0,$ they have the same number of nuclei. The ratio of number of nuclei of $A$ to that of $B$ will be $(1 / e)^{2}$
after a time interval of
questions.solution:
We know that
$N=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$
For $A$,
$N_{A}=N_{0} e^{-5 \lambda_{L}}$
For $B$,
$N_{B}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$
Given,
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{N_{A}}{N_{B}} &=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \\
\frac{N}{N_{B}} &=\frac{1}{e^{4 \lambda t}} \\
t &=\frac{1}{2 \lambda}
\end{aligned}$

questions.q: Half-life of a radioactive substance is 20 minute. The time between $20 \%$ and $80 \%$ decay will be :
questions.solution:
Hints : For $20 \%$ decay
$\frac{80 \mathrm{~N}_0}{100}=\mathrm{N}_0 e^{-\lambda t_1}$
For $80 \%$ decay
$\frac{20 N_0}{100}=N_0 e^{-\lambda t_2}$
On dividing
$\begin{aligned}
& 4=e^{\lambda\left(t_2-t\right)} \\
& 2 \ln 2=\frac{\ln 2}{t_{1 / 2}}\left(t_2-t_1\right) \\
& \Rightarrow t_2-t_1=2 \times 20=40 \mathrm{~min}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is subjected to two simple harmonic motions in the same direction having equal amplitudes and equal frequency. If the resultant amplitude is equal to the amplitude of the individual motion, the phase difference $(\delta)$ between the two motions is
questions.solution: $A_{\text {res }}^2=A_1^2+A_2^2+2 A_1 A_2 \cos \delta \quad A^2=A^2+A^2+2 A^2 \cos \delta, \cos \delta=-\frac{1}{2} \delta=120^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential energy of a particle of mass $0.02 \mathrm{~kg}$ moving along $x$-axis is given by $V=A x(x-4) J$ where $x$ is in metres and $\mathrm{A}$ is a constant. Which of the following is/are correct statement(s)?
options: The particle is acted upon by a constant force
The particle executes simple harmonic motion
The speed of the particle is maximum at $x=2 m$
The period of oscillation of the particle is $\frac{\pi}{5} \mathrm{sec}$
questions.solution:
$V=A x(x-4) J m=0.02=A x^{2}-4 A x$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{F}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-2 \mathrm{Ax}+4 \mathrm{~A} \text { (not constant) } \\
\mathrm{F}=-2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{x}-2)
\end{array}
$$
Hence position execute S.H.M mean position is when force $=0$ i.e. $x=2$ and speed is maximum at mean position
$$
\omega^{2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{~T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2 \mathrm{~A}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{100 \mathrm{~A}}}=\frac{\pi}{5} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{~A}}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In case of a simple harmonic motion, if the velocity is plotted along the $X$ -axis and the displacement (from the equilibrium position) is plotted along the $Y$ -axis, the resultant curve happens to be an ellipse with the ratio:
major axis (along X) $=20 \pi$ minor axis (along $Y)$
What is the frequency of the simple harmonic motion?
questions.solution:
In representation of simple harmonic motion in ellipse as velocity along $X$ -axis and displacement along $Y$ -axis. $\frac{\text { Major axis (along } X \text { -axis) }}{\text { Minor axis (along } X \text { -axis) }}=20 \pi$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\omega A}{A}=20 \pi \Rightarrow \omega=20 \pi$
The frequency of the simple harmonic motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The bob of a pendulum of mass $m$, suspended by an inextensible string of length $L$ as shown in the figure carries a small charge $a$. An infinite horizontal plane conductor with uniform surface charge density $\sigma$ is placed below it. What will be the time period of the pendulum for small amplitude oscillations?
questions.solution: $\therefore$ Apparent weight of the bob, $w^{\prime}=m g-q E$ $$ m y^{\prime}=m g-q E $$ $\therefore$ $g^{\prime}=g-\frac{q E}{m}$ Time period of a pendulum. $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g_{e f f}}}$ $=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g-\frac{q E}{m}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{\left(g-\frac{q \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0} m}\right)}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity of a particle executing a simple harmonic motion is $13 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$, when its distance from the equilibrium position $(Q)$ is $3 \mathrm{m}$ and its velocity is $12 \mathrm{ms}^{-1},$ when it is $5 \mathrm{m}$ away from $Q .$ The frequency of the simple harmonic motion is
questions.solution:
The speed of a particle executing simple
harmonic motion is $v=\omega \sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}$
where, $a=$ Amplitude $\omega=$ Angular frequency $x=$ Displacement
or
$$
v^{2}=\omega^{2}\left(a^{2}-x^{2}\right)
$$
According to the question,
Here
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{1}^{2} &=\omega^{2}\left(a^{2}-x_{1}^{2}\right) \\
v_{2}^{2} &=\omega^{2}\left(a^{2}-x_{2}^{2}\right) \\
v_{1}^{2}-v_{2}^{2} &=\omega^{2}\left(x_{2}^{2}-x_{1}^{2}\right) \\
\omega &=\sqrt{\frac{v_{1}^{2}-v_{2}^{2}}{x_{2}^{2}-x_{1}^{2}}} \\
v_{1}=& 13 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{2} &=12 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} \\
x_{1} &=3 \mathrm{m} \\
x_{2} &=5 \mathrm{m} \\
\omega &=\sqrt{\frac{(13)^{2}-(12)^{2}}{(5)^{2}-(3)^{2}}} \\
&=\sqrt{\frac{169-144}{25-9}}=\sqrt{\frac{25}{16}}=\frac{5}{4} \\
\omega &=2 \pi r \\
v &=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{5 / 4}{2 \pi} \\
v &=\frac{5}{8 \pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A unit negative charge with mass $M$ resides at the mid-point of the straight line of length $2 a$ adjoining two fixed charges of magnitude $+Q$ each. If it is given a very small displacement $x(x< < a)$ in a direction perpendicular to the straight line, it will
options: come back to its original position and stay there

execute oscillations with frequency
$$
\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} M a^{3}}}
$$

execute oscillations with frequency
$$
\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} M a^{2}}}
$$
questions.solution: From figure the net force $\begin{aligned} F_{\text {bet }} &=-F \cos \theta+(-F \cos \theta) \\ &=-2 F \cos \theta \\ &=-2 \times \frac{k Q \times 1}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)} \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}} \\ &=-\frac{2 k Q}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cdot x \\ F_{\text {net }} &=-\left(\frac{2 k Q}{a^{3}}\right) \cdot x \end{aligned}$ Frequency of oscillation $=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{2 k Q}{M a^{3}}}$ $=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot Q}{M a^{3}}}$ $=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{Q}{2 \pi e_{0} M a^{3}}}$ $\Rightarrow\left(^{*}\right.$ None of the option matches)

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a particle executing SHM oscillates with a frequency $v$, then the kinetic energy of the particle
options: changes periodically with a frequency of $v$
changes periodically with a frequency of $2 \mathrm{v}$
changes periodically with a frequency of $\mathrm{v} / 2$
questions.solution:
We know that
Kinetic energy $K=\frac{1}{2} m u^{2}$
where $u=\frac{d y}{d t}=\omega a \cos \omega t$
So. $K=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} a^{2} \cos ^{2} \omega t$
Hence, kinetic energy varies periodically with double the frequency of SHM. So, when a particle executing SHM oscillates with a frequency v, then the kinetic energy of particle changes periodically with a frequency of $2 v$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The displacement of a particle in a periodic motion is given by $y=4 \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \sin (1000 t)$. This displacement may be considered as the result of superposition of $n$ independent harmonic oscillations. Here $n$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $Y=4 \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \cdot \sin (1000 t)$
$=2 \times 2 \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \cdot \sin (1000 t)$
$\quad\left[\because 2 \cos ^{2} \frac{t}{2}=(1+\cos t)\right]$
$=2(1+\cos t) \sin (1000 t)$
$=2 \sin (1000 t)+2 \cos t \cdot \sin (1000 t)$
$=2 \sin (1000 t)+2 \sin (1000 t) \cdot \cos t$
$=2 \sin (1000 t)+\sin (1000 t+t)$
$$
\begin{aligned}[\because-2 \sin A \cdot \cos B=\sin (A+B)+\sin (A-B)] \\=2 \sin (1000 t)+\sin (1001 t)+\sin (999 t) \end{aligned}
$$
So, $n=3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small mass $m$, attached to one end of a spring with a negligible mass and an unstretched length $L$, executes virtual oscillation with angular frequency $\omega_{0}$. When the mass is rotated with an angular speed $\omega$ by holding the other end of the spring at a fixed point, the mass moves uniformly in a circular path in a horizontal plane. Then the increase in length of the spring during the rotation is
questions.solution: The given situation can be shown as From figure, $K\left(x \sin \theta=m \omega^{2}(L+x) \sin \theta\right.$ $\Rightarrow \quad K x=\operatorname{m\omega}^{2}(L+x)$...(i) Also, $\quad \sqrt{\frac{K}{m}}=\omega_{0}$ $\Rightarrow \quad K=m \omega_{0}^{2}$ Substituting the value in Eq. (i) $$ \begin{aligned} m \omega_{0}^{2} x &=m \omega^{2}(L+x) \\ \Rightarrow \quad x &=\frac{\omega^{2} L}{\omega_{0}^{2}-\omega^{2}} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two simple harmonic motions are given by $x_{1}=a \sin \omega t+a \cos \omega t$ and $x_{2}=a \sin \omega t+\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}} \cos \omega t$ The ratio of the amplitudes of first and second motion and the phase difference between them are respectively
questions.solution: The given situation can be shown as Fon second SHM Ratio of amplitude $=\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}$ and phase difference, $\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\pi}{12}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $M$ and charge $q$ is at rest at the mid point between two other fixed similar charges each of magnitude $Q$ placed a distance $2 d$ apart. The system is collinear as shown in the figure. The particle is now displaced by a small amount $x(x< < d)$ along the joining the two charges and is left to itself. It will now oscillate about the mean position with a time period $\left(\varepsilon_{0}=\right.$ permittivity of free space)
questions.solution:
Restoring force on displacement of $x$
$\begin{aligned} F &=K\left[\frac{q}{(d-x)^{2}}-\frac{Q q}{(d+x)^{2}}\right] \\ &=K Q q\left[\frac{1}{(d-x)^{2}}-\frac{1}{(d+x)^{2}}\right] \\ &=K Q q\left[\frac{4 d x}{\left(d^{2}-x^{2}\right)^{2}}\right] \\ &=K Q q\left[\frac{4 d x}{d^{4}}\right] \text { If }(d>>x) \\ F &=K Q q\left[\frac{4 x}{d^{3}}\right] \end{aligned}$
Acceleration, $a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{4 K Q q x}{M d^{3}}$
or $\omega^{2}=\frac{4 K Q q}{M d^{3}}$
$\therefore \quad T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{M d^{3}}{4 K O q}}$
$=2 \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{3} M d^{3} \varepsilon_{0}}{Q q}}$

questions.q: Two identical springs are connected to mass \(\mathrm{m}\) as shown \((\mathrm{k}=\) spring constant). If the period of the configuration in (a) is \(2 \mathrm{~S}\), the period of the configuration (b) is
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} & \text {Hints: } \frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}} \Rightarrow \frac{2}{\mathrm{~T}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{k}}{\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}}}=2 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}=1 \mathrm{~S}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass \(m\) is located in a one dimensional potential field where potential energy is given by : \(\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{A}(1-\cos \mathrm{px})\), where \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(\mathrm{p}\) are constants. The period of small oscillations of the particle is
questions.solution:
Hints: \(v_x=A(1-\cos p x)\)
\(F=-\frac{d u}{d x}=-A p \sin p x\)
For small (x)
\(\mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{AP}^2 \mathrm{x}\)
\(a=-\frac{A p^2}{m} x \quad a=-\omega^2 x\)
\(\omega=\sqrt{\frac{A P^2}{\mathrm{~m}}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{A p^2}}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is executing linear simple harmonic motion of amplitude A. At what displacement is the energy of the particle half potential and half kinetic?
questions.solution:
Hints : Total Energy $(\mathrm{E})=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 \mathrm{~A}^2$
P.E. $=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2$
As P.E. $=\frac{E}{2}$
Then, $\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 \mathrm{~A}^2 \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2$
$$
x^2=\frac{\mathrm{A}^2}{2} \Rightarrow x=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person has a minimum distance of distinct vision of 50 cm. The power of lens required to read a book at a distance of 25 cm is
questions.solution:
Given, the near point of the person is $v = -50$ cm.

The object distance is $u = -25$ cm.

Using the lens formula:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u}$

$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{-50} - \dfrac{1}{-25}$

$\dfrac{1}{f} = -\dfrac{1}{50} + \dfrac{1}{25} = \dfrac{1}{50}$ cm$^{-1}$

$f = 50$ cm $= 0.5$ m

The power of the lens is given by:

$P = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} = +2$ D

Answer: $2$ D

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A plano-convex lens fits exactly into a plano-concave lens. Their plane surfaces are parallel to each other. If lenses are made of different materials of refractive indices $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ and $R$ is the radius of curvature of the curved surface of the lenses, then the focal length of the combination is
questions.solution:
Let the focal length of the plano-convex lens be $f_1$ and its refractive index be $\mu_1$.

Applying the lens maker's formula for the plano-convex lens, assuming light is incident on the plane surface:
$\dfrac{1}{f_1} = (\mu_1 - 1) \left(\dfrac{1}{\infty} - \dfrac{1}{-R}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_1 - 1}{R}$

Let the focal length of the plano-concave lens be $f_2$ and its refractive index be $\mu_2$. Since the plano-convex lens fits exactly into it, the concave surface of the second lens has the same radius of curvature $R$ and matches the convex surface of the first lens.

Applying the lens maker's formula for the plano-concave lens:
$\dfrac{1}{f_2} = (\mu_2 - 1) \left(\dfrac{1}{-R} - \dfrac{1}{\infty}\right) = -\dfrac{(\mu_2 - 1)}{R}$

The equivalent focal length $F$ of the combination of thin lenses in contact is given by:
$\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{1}{f_1} + \dfrac{1}{f_2}$

Substituting the values of $\dfrac{1}{f_1}$ and $\dfrac{1}{f_2}$:
$\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{\mu_1 - 1}{R} - \dfrac{\mu_2 - 1}{R}$

$\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{\mu_1 - 1 - \mu_2 + 1}{R}$

$\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{\mu_1 - \mu_2}{R}$

$F = \dfrac{R}{\mu_1 - \mu_2}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{R}{(\mu_1 - \mu_2)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray of light travelling in air is incident on one face of a parallel glass slab of thickness $t$ a refractive index $\mu$ at an angle of incidence $i$. Total time spent by the ray inside the slab is
questions.solution:
By Snell's law, $\sin i = \mu \sin r$, which gives $\sin r = \dfrac{\sin i}{\mu}$.

The distance travelled by the ray inside the glass slab is $d = \dfrac{t}{\cos r}$.

The speed of light inside the glass slab is $v = \dfrac{c}{\mu}$.

The total time spent by the ray inside the slab is $T = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{t}{\cos r} \times \dfrac{\mu}{c} = \dfrac{\mu t}{c \cos r}$.

Using $\cos r = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 r} = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\sin^2 i}{\mu^2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\mu^2 - \sin^2 i}}{\mu}$.

Substituting $\cos r$ in the expression for $T$, we get $T = \dfrac{\mu t}{c \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{\mu^2 - \sin^2 i}}{\mu} \right)} = \dfrac{\mu^2 t}{c \sqrt{\mu^2 - \sin^2 i}}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{\mu^2 t}{c\sqrt{\mu^2 - \sin^2 i}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electromagnetic wave, whose wave normal makes an angle of $45°$ with the vertical, is travelling in air and strikes a horizontal liquid surface. While travelling through the liquid, it gets deviated by $15°$. If the speed of electromagnetic wave in air is $3 \times 10^8$ m/s, then the speed of electromagnetic wave in the liquid will be
questions.solution:
Angle of incidence, $i = 45^\circ$

Angle of deviation, $\delta = 15^\circ$

Since the wave travels from a rarer medium (air) to a denser medium (liquid), it bends towards the normal. The angle of refraction is given by:

$r = i - \delta = 45^\circ - 15^\circ = 30^\circ$

Using Snell's law:

$\dfrac{\sin i}{\sin r} = \dfrac{c}{v}$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{\sin 45^\circ}{\sin 30^\circ} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{v}$

$\dfrac{1/\sqrt{2}}{1/2} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{v}$

$\sqrt{2} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{v}$

$v = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2}} = 1.5 \times \sqrt{2} \times 10^8$

$v \approx 1.5 \times 1.414 \times 10^8 \approx 2.12 \times 10^8$ m/s

Answer: $2.1 \times 10^8$ m/s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Figure shows the graph of angle of deviation $\delta$ versus angle of incidence i for a light ray striking a prism. The prism angle is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & =i+e-A \\ & A=i+e-\delta \\ & =60+15-30 \\ & =75-30=45^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The convex lens $\left(L_1\right.$ and $\left.L_2\right)$ of equal focal length $f$ are placed at a distance $\frac{f}{2}$ apart. An object is placed at a distance $4 f$ in the left of $L_1$ as shown in figure. The final image is at
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \\ & \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-4 f}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{4 f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{4-1}{4 f} \Rightarrow v=\frac{4 f}{3} \\ & u_2=\frac{4 f}{3}-\frac{f}{2}=\frac{8 f-3 f}{6} \Rightarrow u_2=\frac{5 f}{6} \\ & \frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{u_2}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{6}{5 f}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v_2}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{6}{5 f} \\ & \frac{1}{v_2}=\frac{11}{5 f} \Rightarrow v_2=\frac{5 f}{11}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\hat{n}_1, \hat{n}_2$ and $\hat{t}$ represent, unit vectors along the incident ray, reflected ray and normal to the surface respectively, then
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Hint: } \hat{n}_1=-\cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{t}}+\sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{n}}_2=\cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{t}}+\sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{n}}_2-\hat{\mathrm{n}}_1=2 \cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{t}}=-2(\hat{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{t}}) \hat{\mathrm{t}} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{n}}_1 \cdot \hat{\mathrm{t}}=-\cos \theta \\ & \hat{n}_2=\hat{n}_1-2\left(\hat{n}_1 \cdot \hat{\mathrm{t}}\right) \hat{\mathrm{t}}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
When a convex lens is placed above an empty tank, the image of a mark at the bottom of the tank, which is 45 cm
from the lens is formed 36 cm above the lens. When a liquid is poured in the tank to a depth of 40 cm, the distance
of the image of the mark above the lens is 48 cm. The refractive index of the liquid is
questions.solution:
Initially without water, $\frac{1}{36}-\frac{1}{-45}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow f=20$ then, after pouring water upto 40 cm
apparent object distance $=\frac{40}{\mu}+5, \frac{1}{48}+\frac{1}{\frac{40}{\mu}+5}=\frac{1}{20} \Rightarrow \mu=1.366$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three identical convex lenses each of focal length \(f\) are placed in a straight line separated by a distance \(f\) from each other. An object is located at \(\mathrm{f} / 2\) in front of the leftmost lens. Then,
options: Final image will be at \(f / 2\) behind the rightmost lens and its magnification will be -1 .
Final image will be at \(f / 2\) behind the rightmost lens and its magnification will be +1 .
Final image will be at \(f\) behind the rightmost lens and its magnification will be -1 .
Final image will be at \(f\) behind the rightmost lens and its magnification will be +1 .
questions.solution:
Hint :
For first lens \(\begin{aligned} & u=-\frac{f}{2} \\ & \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{\frac{-f}{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{2}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{-1}{f} \\ & v=-f \\ & m_1=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-f}{\frac{-f}{2}}=2 \end{aligned}\) For second lens \(u=-(f+f)=-2 f\) \(\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-2 f}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{2 f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{2 f} \\ & v=2 f \\ & m_1=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{2 f}{-2 f}=-1 \end{aligned}\) For third lens \(\begin{aligned} & u=f \\ & f=f \\ & \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{2}{f} \Rightarrow v=\frac{f}{2} \end{aligned}\) \(m_3=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{\frac{f}{2}}{f}=\frac{1}{2}\) Total magnification \(=m_1 m_2 m_3=2 \times(-1) \times \frac{1}{2}=-1\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray of monochromatic light is incident on the plane surface of separation between two media \(X\) and \(Y\) with angle of incidence 'i' in medium \(X\) and angle of refraction ' \(r\) ' in medium \(Y\). The given graph shows the relation between sin \(i\) and \(\sin r\). If \(V_x\) and \(V_{\mathrm{Y}}\) are the velocities of the ray in media \(X\) and \(Y\) respectively, then which of the following is true?
options: Total internal reflection can happen when the light is incident in medium \(X\).
\(v_X=\sqrt{3} v_Y\), where \(v_X\) and \(v_Y\) are frequencies of the light in medium \(X\) and \(Y\) respectively.
questions.solution:
Hint :
\(\begin{aligned} & \mu_1 \sin i=\mu_2 \sin r \\ & \frac{C}{V_X} \sin i=\frac{C}{V_Y} \sin r \\ & \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{V_X}{V_Y}=\sqrt{3} \\ & V_Y=\frac{V_X}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & V_X=\sqrt{3} V_Y\end{aligned}\)
\(y=m x\) \(\sin r=\left(\tan 30^{\circ}\right) \sin \mathrm{i}\) \(\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{1}{\tan 30^{\circ}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{1}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The human eye has an approximate angular resolution of $\theta=5.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{rad}$ and typical photo printer prints a minimum of 300 dip (dots per inch, 1 inch $=2.54 \mathrm{~cm}$ ). At what minimal distance $d$ should a printed page be held so that one does not see the individual dots?
questions.solution: $\theta=\frac{x}{d}$ $5.8 \times 10^{-4}=\frac{2.54}{300} \times \frac{1}{d}$ $d=14.59 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The cross-section of a reflecting surface is represented by the equation $x^{2}+y^{2}=R^{2}$ as shown in the figure. A ray travelling in the positive $x$ direction is directed toward positive y direction after reflection from the surface at point $M$. The coordinate of the point $\mathrm{M}$ on the reflecting surface is
questions.solution: $x^{2}+y^{2}=R^{2}$ $2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0$ $\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x}{y}=\tan 45^{\circ}=1$ $\therefore x=-\frac{R}{\sqrt{2}}$ $y=+\frac{R}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical convex surface of power 5 dioptre separates object and image space of refractive indices $1.0$ and $\frac{4}{3}$ respectively. The radius of curvature of the surface is
questions.solution: $P=5 D$ $u=\infty$ then $\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{4 / 3}{f}=\frac{1-4 / 3}{R}$ $-\frac{4}{3 f}=-\frac{1}{3 R}$ $\frac{f}{4}=R \quad \quad f=4 R$ $\therefore \frac{100}{5}=4 R \Rightarrow R=\frac{25}{5}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object is placed $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ in front of a convex mirror of focal length $30 \mathrm{~cm}$. A plane mirror is now placed facing the object in between the object and the convex mirror such that it covers lower half of the convex mirror. What should be the distance of the plane mirror from the object so that there will be no parallax between the images formed by the two mirrors?
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{60}=\frac{1}{30}$
$\mathrm{v}=\frac{60}{3}=\pm 20 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore$ Distance between object and image $=60+20=80 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\therefore x=40 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin convex lens is placed just above an empty vessel of depth $80 \mathrm{~cm}$. The image of a coin kept at the bottom of the vessel is thus formed $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ above the lens. If now water is poured in the vessel up to a height of $64 \mathrm{~cm}$, what will be the approximate new position of the image. Assume that refractive index of water is $4 / 3$.
questions.solution: Hint: $\mathrm{u}=-80 \mathrm{~cm} \quad \mathrm{v}=+20 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{80}=\frac{5}{80}$ $f=16 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\mathrm{u}^{\prime}=16+\frac{64 \times 3}{4}=64 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\frac{1}{16}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{64} \quad \therefore \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{16}-\frac{1}{64}=\frac{3}{64}$ $v=21.33$ above the lens

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray of light is reflected by a plane mirror. $\hat{e}_{0}, \hat{e}$ and $\hat{n}$ be the unit vectors along the incident ray, reflected ray and the normal to the reflecting surface respectively. Which of the following gives an expression for ê?
questions.solution: $\text { (b) According to the question, }$ We know that incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in the same plane. and angle of incidence $=$ angle of reflection Therefore $\hat{\mathrm{n}}$ will be along the angle bisector of e and $-\hat{e}_{0}$ i.e. $\hat{\mathbf{n}}=\frac{\hat{e}+\left(-\hat{\mathbf{e}}_{0}\right)}{\left|\hat{e}-\hat{\mathbf{e}}_{0}\right|}$ [$\because$ Bisector will along a vector dividing in same ratio as the ratio of sides forming that angle ] But $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ is a unit vector where $\left|\mathbf{e}-\hat{\mathbf{e}}_{0}\right|=O C$ $\begin{aligned} &=20 P=2|\hat{e}| \\ \cos \theta &=2 \cos \theta \end{aligned}$ Substituting this value in Eq. (i), we get $\hat{\mathbf{n}}=\frac{\hat{\mathbf{e}}-\hat{\mathbf{e}}_{0}}{2 \cos \theta}$ $\begin{array}{l} \hat{e}=\hat{e}_{0}+(2 \cos \theta) \hat{n} \\ \left.\hat{e}=\hat{e}_{0}-2 \hat{n} \cdot \hat{e}_{0}\right) \hat{n}\left[\because \hat{n} \cdot \hat{e}_{0}=-\cos \theta\right] \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point object is placed on the axis of a thin convex lens of focal length $0.05 \mathrm{m}$ at a distance of $0.2 \mathrm{m}$ from the lens and its image is formed on the axis. If the object is now made to oscillate along the axis with a small amplitude of $A \mathrm{cm},$ then what is the amplitude of oscillation of the image? $\left[\right.$ you may assume, $\frac{1}{1+x} \approx 1-x,$ where $\left.x< < 1\right]$
questions.solution: According to the question, we can draw the following diagram Given, $u=-0.2 \mathrm{m}$ and $f=0.05 \mathrm{m}$ As we know that, $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \\ \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u} \\ \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{0.05}-\frac{1}{0.2} \\ \frac{1}{v}=\frac{100}{5}-\frac{10}{2} \\ \frac{1}{v}=20-5 \Rightarrow v=\frac{1}{15} \mathrm{m} \end{array} $$ Now, differentiating eq. (i), we get $$ \begin{array}{l} -\frac{d v}{v^{2}}=-\frac{d u}{u^{2}} \quad \therefore \quad d v=d u \frac{v^{2}}{u^{2}} \\ A_{\max }=A \times\left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{2} \times\left(\frac{1}{(-0.2)}\right)^{2} \\ A_{\max }=A \times \frac{1}{225} \times 25 \\ A_{\max }=\frac{A}{9} \end{array} $$ Here $A$ is in $\mathrm{cm} .$ Hence, $A_{\max }=\frac{A}{9} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A point source is placed at coordinates (0,1) in $x y$ -plane. A ray of light from the source is reflected on a plane mirror placed along the
$X$ -axis and perpendicular to the $x y$ -plane. The reflected ray passes through the point $(3,3) .$ What is the path length of the ray from (0,1) to (3,3)$?$
questions.solution: Ray diagram for the question. $I$ is image of source $S$ by the plane mirror placed perpendicularly along $X$ -axis. $S M=I M$ $\therefore P M+M S=P M+M I=P I$ $\therefore \quad P I=\sqrt{(3-0)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}}$ $=\sqrt{9+16}=\sqrt{25}=5$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical equiconvex lenses, each of focal length $f$ are placed side by side in contact with each other with a layer of water in between them as shown in the figure. If refractive index of the material of the lenses is greater than that of water, how the combined focal length $F$ is related to $f ?$
questions.solution: The given combination of lenses Here, $f_{1}=$ focal length of equiconvex lenses of glass. $f_{2}=$ focal length of lens formed by water (concave). The focal length of the combination. $$ \therefore \quad \frac{1}{F}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}+\frac{1}{f_{3}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}-\frac{1}{f_{2}}+\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\frac{2}{f_{1}}-\frac{1}{f_{2}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{F}=\frac{2 f_{2}-f_{1}}{f_{1} f_{2}} \Rightarrow F &=\frac{f_{1} f_{2}}{2 f_{2}-f_{1}} \\ F &=\frac{f_{1}}{2-\frac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}} \end{aligned} $$ $\left[\because f_{3}=f_{1}\right]$ Here, $\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\left(\mu_{g}-1\right) \frac{2}{R},$ for $L_{1}$ and $L_{3}$ and $\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\left(\mu_{w}-1\right)\left(-\frac{2}{R}\right),$ for $L_{2}$ $$ \text { Given, } \mu_{w} < \mu_{g} $$ Thus, $\frac{f_{1}}{f_{2}} < 1$ So. $$ F>\frac{f_{1}}{2} $$ From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $$ \frac{f_{1}}{2} < F < f_{1} \quad \text { or } \frac{f}{2} < F < f $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There is a small air bubble at the centre of a solid glass sphere of radius $r$ and refractive index $\mu .$ What will be the apparent distance of the bubble from the centre of the sphere, when viewed from outside?
questions.solution: ' As the object is at centre of the sphere. $\therefore$ All rays will fall normally on surface, hence they do not deflect. Thus, a virtual image is formed at the centre $O$. $\therefore$ Apparent depth $=$ Real depth

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray of light is incident on a right angled isosceles prism parallel to its base as shown in the figure. Refractive index of the material of the prism is $\sqrt{2}$. Then. which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
options: The refiection at $P$ is total internal.
The reflection at $Q$ is total internal.
The ray emerging at $R$ is parallel to the ray incident at $S .$
Total deviation of the ray is $150^{\circ}$
questions.solution: Critical angle $\theta_{C}=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=45^{\circ}$ Snell's law at points, $\left(\because n_{1} \sin \theta_{1}=n_{2} \sin \theta_{2}\right)$ $1 \sin 45^{\circ}=\sqrt{2} \sin \theta$ $\therefore \quad \theta=30^{\circ}$ At $P \because$ incidence angle at $P=75^{\circ}>\theta_{C}$ $\therefore$ TIR at point $P$ At $Q \because$ incidence angle at $Q=15^{\circ} < \theta_{C}$ $\therefore$ Partial reflection and refraction at $Q .$ As $S P \| Q R$, emergent ray at $R$ is parallel to incid, ray at $S$. $\therefore$ Net deviation $=180^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the diameter of the sun to the distance between the earth and the sun is approximately $0.009 .$ The approximate diameter of the image of the sun formed by a concave spherical mirror of radius of curvature $0.4 \mathrm{m}$ is
questions.solution: The angle subtended by sun on mirror ; $$ \begin{array}{l} Q=\frac{D}{d_{S E}}=\frac{d}{f} \\ d=\frac{D}{d_{s E}} \times f \end{array} $$ According to the question, $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{D}{d_{s k}} &=0 \cdot 009 \\ f &=\frac{r}{2}=\frac{0.4}{2}=0 \cdot 2 \mathrm{m} \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore \quad d=0 \cdot 009 \times 0 \cdot 2 \mathrm{m}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} =9 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m} \\ =18 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m} \\ =1.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{m} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point object is held above a thin equiconvex lens at its focus. The focal length is $0.1 \mathrm{m}$ and the lens rests on a horizontal thin plane mirror. The final image will be formed at
options: $0.1 \mathrm{m}$ above the center of the lens
$0.1 \mathrm{m}$ below the center of the lens
questions.solution: As the lens is placed on a horizontal thin plane mirror. So due to role of plane mirror the image will be formed at the same point $O$ above $0.1 \mathrm{m}$ distance above the center of the lens.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel beam of light is incident on a glass prism in the shape of a quarter cylinder of radius $R=0.05 \mathrm{m}$ and refractive index $n=1.5,$ placed on a horizontal table as shown in the figure. Beyond the cylinder, a patch of light is found whose the nearest distance $x$ from the cylinder is
questions.solution: Given, radius $R=0.05 \mathrm{m}$ Refractive index $n=1 \cdot 5$ The critical angle, $\sin c=\frac{1}{n}=\frac{1}{1 \cdot 5}$ $$ =\frac{10}{15}=\frac{2}{3} $$ From above figure, $\cos c=\frac{R}{R+X}$ $\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} c}=\frac{R}{R+X}$ $\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{R}{R+X}$ $\therefore$ $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\sqrt{5}}{3} &=\frac{R}{R+X} \\ \sqrt{5}(R+X) &=3 R \\ \sqrt{5} R+\sqrt{5} X &=3 R \\ X &=(3 \sqrt{5}-5) \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{m} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray of light strikes a glass plate at an angle of $60^{\circ}$. If the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other, the refractive index of glass is
questions.solution: Here, angle of incidence is $60^{\circ}$ $$ \mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r} $$ $\sin i=\mu \sin r$ here, $$ \begin{aligned} r &=90-i \\ \sin i &=\mu \sin \left(90^{\circ}-i\right)=\mu \cos i \\ \mu &=\frac{\sin i}{\cos i}=\tan i=\tan 60^{\circ} \\ \mu &=\sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light travels through a glass plate of thickness $t$ and having refractive index $\mu$. If $c$ be the velocity of light in vacuum, time taken by the light to travel through this thickness of glass is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object is located $4 \mathrm{m}$ from the first of two thin converging lenses of focal lengths $2 \mathrm{m}$ and $1 \mathrm{m},$ respectively. The lenses are separated by $3 \mathrm{m}$. The final image formed by the second lens is located from the source at a distance of
questions.solution:
From the lens formula (for first lens)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{f_{1}} &=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u_{1}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{(-4)} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4} &=\frac{1}{v_{1}}=\frac{3}{4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad v_{1} &=\frac{4}{3}, u_{2}=3-\frac{4}{3}=5 / 3
\end{aligned}
$$
This image will be treated as the source for second lens, then again from lens formula, we have
$\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}4$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{1}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}+5 / 3$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1-5 / 3=\frac{1}{v_{2}} \Rightarrow v_{2}=-3 / 2$
[By Eq. (i)]
This is the final image distance from 2 nd lens. So, the overall distance of image from the primary source(or object)
Let $d=4+3-1.5=5.5$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray of light is incident at an angle $i$ on a glass slab of refractive index $\mu$. The angla between reflected and refracted light is $90^{\circ}$ Then, the relationship between $i$ and $\mu$ is
questions.solution: As situation can be diagrammatically as below From law of reflection $$ i=\theta $$ Now, $\quad \theta+r+90^{\circ}=180^{\circ}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad i+r+90^{\circ} &=180^{\circ} \\ r &=90^{\circ}-i \end{aligned} $$ Also, from Snell's law $\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\mu \Rightarrow \frac{\sin i}{\sin \left(90^{\circ}-i\right)}=\frac{\sin i}{\cos i}=\mu$ $\Rightarrow \tan i=\mu$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When light is refracted from a surface, which of its following physical parameters does not change?
questions.solution: When light is refracted its frequency will remain unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A luminous object is separated from a screen by distance $d$. A convex lens is placed between the object and the screen such that it forms a distinct image on the screen. The maximum possible focal length of this convex lens is
questions.solution:
Focal length of a convex lens by displacement method
Focal length of the convex lens
$$
f=\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}}{4 a}
$$
where $a$ = distance between the image and object and $b=$ distance between two positions of lens
Fact The distance bemeen the ho points should be 4 times greater than the focal iength of the convex lens, ie, $d>4 f$.
So, the maximum possible focal length of this convex lens is $\frac{d}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The intermediate image formed by the objective of a compound microscope is
questions.solution: The intermediate image formed by the objective of a compound microscope is real, inverted and magnified.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A glass slab consists of thin uniform layers of progressively decreasing refractive indices RI (see figure) such that the RI of any layer is $\mu-m \Delta \mu$. Here, $\mu$ and $\Delta \mu$ denote the $\mathrm{RI}$ of 0 th layer and the difference in RI between any two consecutive layers, respectively. The integer $m=0,1,2,3, \ldots$ denotes the numbers of the successive layers. A ray of light from the oth layer enters the 1st layer at an angle of incidence of $30^{\circ}$. After undergoing the mth refraction, the ray emerges parallel to the interface. If $\mu=1.5$ and $\Delta \mu=0.015,$ the value of $m$ is
questions.solution:
By Snell's law. $\mu \sin i=$ constant
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu \sin i &=(\mu-m \Delta \mu) \sin r \\
\text { where } \mu=1.5, i &=30^{\circ}, r=90^{\circ}, \Delta \mu=0.015 \\
1.5 \sin 30^{\circ} &=(1.5-m \times 0.15) \sin 90^{\circ} \\
\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} &=(1.5-m \times 0.015) \times 1 \\
\frac{3}{4} &=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{15 m}{1000} \\
15 \mathrm{m} &=\left(\frac{3}{2}-\frac{3}{4}\right) \times 1000 \\
15 \mathrm{m} &=\frac{6-3}{4} \times 1000 \Rightarrow m=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1000}{15} \\
m &=\frac{3000}{60} \Rightarrow m=50
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.q: The least distance of vision of a long sighted person is $60 \mathrm{cm}$. By using a spectacle lens, this distance is reduced to $12 \mathrm{cm}$. The power of the lens is
questions.solution:
Here, $v=-60 \mathrm{cm}, u=-12 \mathrm{cm}$
$\therefore$ By using the relation, $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$
We have. $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{-60}-\frac{1}{-12}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{15} \mathrm{cm}$ or $\frac{100}{15} \mathrm{m}$
So, the power of lens, $P=\frac{100}{15}=+\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{D}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The glass prisms $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ are to be combined together to produce dispersion without deviation. The angles of the prisms $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ are selected as $4^{\circ}$ and $3^{\circ}$ respectively. If the refractive index of prism $P_{1}$ is $1.54,$ then that of $P_{2}$ will be
questions.solution:
Given for prism $\mathrm{P}_{1}$
$$
\mu=1.54,A=4^{\circ}
$$
For prism $\mathrm{P}_{2}$
$$
\mu^{\prime}=?, A^{\prime}=3^{\circ}
$$
For no deviation $=\delta +\delta ^{\prime}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\mu-1) A=\left(\mu^{\prime}-1\right) A^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(1.54-1) 4=\left(\mu^{\prime}-1\right) 3$
On solving we get $\mu^{\prime}=1.72$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object placed at a distance of $16 \mathrm{cm}$ from a $\begin{array}{llll}\text { convex lens } & \text { produces } & \text { an image } & \text { of }\end{array}$ magnification $m(m>1)$. If the object is moved towards the lens by $8 \mathrm{cm}$, then again an image of magnification $m$ is obtained. The numerical value of the focal length of the lens is
questions.solution:
Linear magnification, $m=\frac{f}{f+u}$
As given that magnification is same for both cases this is possible if the two different values of $u$ are of opposite sign.
$i, \theta,+\frac{f}{f-16}=\frac{-f}{f-8}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 16-f=f-8$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 f=24$
or $\quad f=12 \mathrm{cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A biconvex lens of focal length $f$ and radii of curvature of both the surfaces $R$ is made of a material of refractive index $n_{1}$. This lens is placed in a liquid of refractive index $n_{2}$. Now this lens will behave like
options: either as a convex or as a concave lens depending solely on $\mathrm{R}$
a convex lens depending on $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$
a concave lens depending on $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$
a convex lens of same focal length irrespective of $R, n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$
questions.solution: The lens will behave like a convex lens or a concave lens depending upon the value of $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$.

questions.solution:
Given $v=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}, d=10 \mathrm{m}$ and $\mu=1.5$
Here, $t=\frac{d}{c / \mu}$
$$
t=\frac{10 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{8}}
$$
$\left(\therefore \frac{c}{\mu}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.5}=2 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)$
$t=0.5 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{s}$

questions.solution: In water $\lambda=\frac{4200}{\frac{4}{3}}=3150 Å$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform rod AB is suspended from a point P, at a variable distance $x$, from A, as shown in figure. To make the rod horizontal, a mass '$m$' is suspended from its end A. Which set of variables will give a straight line when they are plotted?
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the uniform rod be $M$ and its length be $L$. The center of mass of the rod is at a distance $L/2$ from end A.

The rod is suspended at point P, which is at a distance $x$ from A. For the rod to remain horizontal in equilibrium, the net torque about the suspension point P must be zero.

The torque due to the suspended mass $m$ at A is $mgx$ in the anti-clockwise direction.

The torque due to the weight of the rod is $Mg\left(\dfrac{L}{2} - x\right)$ in the clockwise direction.

Equating the two torques for rotational equilibrium:

$mgx = Mg\left(\dfrac{L}{2} - x\right)$

$mx = M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right) - Mx$

Dividing the entire equation by $x$:

$m = \dfrac{ML}{2} \left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right) - M$

This equation is of the form $y = k z + c$, where $y = m$, $z = \dfrac{1}{x}$, $k = \dfrac{ML}{2}$, and $c = -M$.

Therefore, plotting $m$ against $\dfrac{1}{x}$ will give a straight line.

Answer: $m, \dfrac{1}{x}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The moment of inertia of a thin disc about axes $a, b, c, d$ are $I_1, I_2, I_3$ and $I_4$ respectively, as shown in figure. If the moment of inertia about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the disc is $I$ then,
questions.solution:
From the given figure, the axes $c$ and $d$ are mutually perpendicular. Similarly, the axes $a$ and $b$ are mutually perpendicular.

According to the perpendicular axis theorem for a planar body, the moment of inertia about an axis perpendicular to the plane is equal to the sum of the moments of inertia about any two mutually perpendicular axes in its plane intersecting at the same point.

Applying this theorem to the pairs of perpendicular axes:
$I = I_c + I_d \Rightarrow I = I_3 + I_4$
$I = I_a + I_b \Rightarrow I = I_1 + I_2$

For a uniform thin disc, the moment of inertia about any diameter is the same due to symmetry. Let this moment of inertia be $I_d$.
Thus, $I_1 = I_2 = I_3 = I_4 = I_d$.

Since $I = I_1 + I_2 = 2I_d$, we can also write:
$I = I_1 + I_3$ (since $I_1 + I_3 = I_d + I_d = 2I_d = I$).

Answer: $I = I_1 + I_2$; $I = I_3 + I_4$; $I = I_1 + I_3$

The angular velocity of the rod is 5 rad/s clockwise.
The angular velocity of the rod is 5 rad/s anticlockwise.
questions.solution: Get $\theta$ $$ 3 \cos \theta=4 \sin \theta $$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \omega=\frac{3 \sin \theta+4 \sin \theta}{\ell} \\ & (\mathrm{KE})_{\text {rotation }}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{12} \mathrm{ml}^2 \omega^2=\frac{25}{6} \end{aligned} $$

questions.q: A uniform thin rod of length $L$, mass $m$ is lying on a smooth horizontal table. A horizontal impulse $P$ is suddenly applied perpendicular to the rod at one end. The total energy of the rod after the impulse is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{P}=(\mathrm{mv}-0)$ angular impulse $\mathrm{J}=\mathrm{P} \times \frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}=\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{f}}-0$ $\mathrm{LE}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{\mathrm{PL}}{2}$ $=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{r}}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}+\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{f}}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{I}}$ $=\frac{(\mathrm{PL} / 2)^{2}}{2 \times \frac{\mathrm{mL}^{2}}{12}}$ $=\frac{\mathrm{P}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}+\frac{\mathrm{P}^{2} \mathrm{~L}^{2}}{4 \times \frac{\mathrm{mL}^{2}}{6}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}+\frac{3 \mathrm{P}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{P}^{2}}{\mathrm{~m}}$

If the temperature of the system is increased by $\Delta \mathrm{T}$, angular velocity becomes $\frac{\omega}{2}$. If coefficient of linear expansion of the rod is $\alpha(\alpha< < 1)$, then $\Delta \mathrm{T}$ will be
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{I} \omega=\mathrm{I}_{1} \frac{\omega}{2}$
$\quad \mathrm{I}_{1}=2 \mathrm{I}$
$\quad \frac{\mathrm{ml}_{1}^{2}}{3}=2 \frac{\mathrm{ml}_{0}^{2}}{3}$
$\left\{\mathrm{I}_{0}^{2}(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T})\right\}^{2}=2 \mathrm{I}_{0}^{2}, \therefore 1+2 \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}=2 \therefore \Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{1}{2 \alpha}$

At what distance from the steel wire the load must be applied? [Young's modulus of steel is $2 \times 10^{12}$ dynes $/ \mathrm{cm}^{2}$ and that of brass is $1 \times 10^{12}$ dynes $\left./ \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right]$
questions.solution:
Hint:
At equilibrium, Taking torque about point $\mathrm{O}$ $\begin{aligned} T_{1} x=T_{2}(15-x) & \Rightarrow Y_{1} x=Y_{2}(15-x) \\ \left[Y=\frac{T}{A} \times \frac{L}{\ell} ; T \propto Y\right] & 1 \times 10^{12} x=2 \times 10^{12}(15-x) ; 3 x=15, \quad \text { So, } x=5 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$

Hint: $v=\sqrt{2 g h}$ $=\sqrt{2 g \mid \sin \theta}$
$\omega_{1}=\frac{v}{l}=\sqrt{\frac{2 g \sin \theta}{1}}$ $m g h=\frac{1}{2} l \omega^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{mg} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{l}^{\prime} \sin \theta}{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{ml}^{2}}{3} \times \omega_{2}^{2}$ $\omega_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{~g} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{l}}}=\omega_{1} \Rightarrow \frac{2}{\mathrm{l}}=\frac{3}{\mathrm{l}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}^{\prime}=\frac{3}{2}I$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid spherical ball and a hollow spherical ball of two different materials of densities $\rho_{1}$ and $\rho_{2}$ respectively have same outer radii and same mass. What will be the ratio, the moment of inertia (about an axis passing through the centre) of the hollow sphere to that of the solid sphere?
questions.solution: $\therefore \quad M_{1}=\rho_{1} \frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}$ and $M_{2}=\rho_{2} \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right)$ $\therefore \quad \rho_{1} \frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=\rho_{2} \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right)$ $\Rightarrow \quad \rho_{1} R^{3}=\rho_{2}\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right)$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{2}}=1-\frac{r^{3}}{R^{3}}$ $\therefore \frac{r}{R}=\left(1-\frac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}$ Moment of inertia, $\begin{aligned} \frac{I_{M}}{I_{S}} &=\frac{\frac{2}{5} M_{2}\left[\frac{R^{5}-r^{3}}{R^{3}-r^{3}}\right]}{\frac{2}{5} M_{1} R^{2}} \\ &=\frac{\rho_{2} \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right)\left[\frac{R^{5}-r^{5}}{\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right)}\right]}{\rho_{1} \frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} R^{2}} \\ &=\frac{\rho_{2}}{\rho_{1}}\left[\frac{R^{5}-r^{5}}{R^{5}}\right]=\frac{\rho_{2}}{\rho_{1}}\left[1-\left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{3}\right] \end{aligned}$ $\therefore \quad \frac{I_{H}}{I_{s}}=\frac{\rho_{2}}{\rho_{1}}\left[1-\left(1-\frac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{2}}\right)^{\frac{5}{3}}\right]$

questions.solution: Let, the final velocity be V So, angular momentum will remain conserved along point of contact By conservation of angular momentum Angular momentum will remain conserved along point of contact $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\omega} &=\text { constant } \\ m v_{0} r &=m v r+\frac{2}{5} m r^{2} \times \omega \quad\left(\because \omega=\frac{v}{r}\right) \\ m v_{0} r &=m v r+\frac{2}{5} m r^{2}\left(\frac{v}{r}\right) \\ v_{0} &=v+\frac{2}{5} v \\ v_{0} &=\frac{7}{5} v \Rightarrow v=\frac{5}{7} v_{0} \end{aligned} $$

questions.solution: Loss in potential energy = gain in kinetic energy $\Rightarrow \quad m g \frac{L}{2} \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ So $\omega \propto \sqrt{\sin \theta}$ and $\quad v \propto \sqrt{\sin \theta} \quad\left(\because K=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H} \omega^{2}\right)$ The speed of end $B$ is proportional to $\sqrt{\sin \theta}$ and $$ U=m g h=m g \frac{L}{2}(1-\sin \theta) \quad(\because \tau=l \alpha) $$ $\Rightarrow \quad m g \frac{L}{2} \cos \theta=\frac{m I^{2}}{3} \times \boldsymbol{\alpha}$ $$ \alpha \propto \cos \theta $$ The angular acceleration is proportional to cos $\theta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The I - V characteristics graph shown below is exhibited by
questions.solution:
The given graph is plotted with Voltage ($V$) on the vertical axis and Current ($I$) on the horizontal axis.

The characteristic curve lies in the second quadrant, where $V > 0$ and $I < 0$. In this region, the electrical power $P = VI$ is negative, which indicates that the device is supplying electrical energy to the circuit rather than consuming it.

Let us evaluate the given options:
1. LED: Operates in forward bias, so its characteristics lie in the first quadrant ($V > 0, I > 0$).
2. Zener diode: Operates in the reverse breakdown region, so its characteristics lie in the third quadrant ($V < 0, I < 0$).
3. Photodiode: Operates in reverse bias, so its characteristics also lie in the third quadrant ($V < 0, I < 0$).
4. Solar cell: Generates an electromotive force (emf) when illuminated and supplies current to an external load without any external bias. Its $V-I$ characteristics lie in the second quadrant (or $I-V$ characteristics in the fourth quadrant), with intercepts corresponding to the open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) and short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$).

Therefore, the given graph exhibits the characteristics of a solar cell.

Answer: Solar cell

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The inputs to a digital circuit are as shown below. The output $Y$ is
questions.solution:
The inputs $A$ and $B$ are connected to a NAND gate, producing the output $\overline{A \cdot B}$.

The input $C$ is connected to a NOT gate, producing the output $\bar{C}$.

These two signals are fed into an OR gate to produce the final output $Y$:

$Y = \overline{A \cdot B} + \bar{C}$

Using De Morgan's theorem, $\overline{A \cdot B} = \bar{A} + \bar{B}$.

Substituting this into the expression for $Y$ gives:

$Y = \bar{A} + \bar{B} + \bar{C}$

Answer: $\bar{A} + \bar{B} + \bar{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The circuit has two oppositely connected ideal diodes in parallel as shown in the figure. What is the current flowing in the circuit?
questions.solution:
From the given circuit diagram, the positive terminal of the $12\text{ V}$ battery is connected to the top wire and the negative terminal to the bottom wire.

Diode $D_1$ has its cathode (n-side) connected to the higher potential and anode (p-side) to the lower potential. Therefore, $D_1$ is reverse-biased and acts as an open circuit, meaning no current flows through the $3\Omega$ resistor branch.

Diode $D_2$ has its anode connected to the higher potential and cathode to the lower potential. Therefore, $D_2$ is forward-biased. Since it is an ideal diode, it acts as a short circuit (zero resistance).

The effective circuit consists of the $12\text{ V}$ battery, the $4\Omega$ resistor, and the $2\Omega$ resistor in series.

The equivalent resistance of the circuit is:
$R_{\text{eq}} = 4\Omega + 2\Omega = 6\Omega$

The current flowing in the circuit is:
$I = \dfrac{V}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{12}{6} = 2.00\text{ A}$

Answer: $2.00$A

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For Boolean variables $A$ and $B$, $A \oplus B = A\bar{B} + \bar{A}B$. Then, which of the following statements is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Given $A \oplus B = A\bar{B} + \bar{A}B$

Evaluating the first expression:

$1 \oplus A = 1 \cdot \bar{A} + \bar{1} \cdot A = \bar{A} + 0 = \bar{A}$

Evaluating the second expression:

$A \oplus A = A \cdot \bar{A} + \bar{A} \cdot A = 0 + 0 = 0$

Evaluating the third expression:

$0 \oplus A = 0 \cdot \bar{A} + \bar{0} \cdot A = 0 + 1 \cdot A = A$

Evaluating the fourth expression:

$A \oplus \bar{A} = A \cdot \overline{\bar{A}} + \bar{A} \cdot \bar{A} = A \cdot A + \bar{A} = A + \bar{A} = 1$

The first, second, and fourth expressions are correct.

Answer: $1 \oplus A = \bar{A}$; $A \oplus A = 0$; $A \oplus \bar{A} = 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A Zener diode having break-down voltage $5.6 \mathrm{V}$ is connected in reverse bias with a battery of emf $10 \mathrm{V}$ and a resistance of $100 \Omega$ in series. The current flowing through the Zener diode is
questions.solution: Breakdown voltage of Zener diode $=5.6 \mathrm{V}$ Voltage of source $=10 \mathrm{V}$ Potential difference $\Delta V=(10-5.6) \mathrm{V}=4.4 \mathrm{V}$ Resistance of circuit $=100 \Omega$ The current passing through the Zener diode $$ \begin{aligned} l &=\frac{\Delta V}{R} \\ &=\frac{4.4}{100} \\ &=4.4 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{A} \\ &=44 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{A} \\ &=44 \mathrm{mA} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In case of a bipolar transistor $\beta=45$. The potential drop across the collector resistance $\begin{array}{llllll}\text { of } 1 \mathrm{k} \Omega & \text { is } 5 \mathrm{V} . & \text { The base } & \text { current } & \text { is }\end{array}$ approximately
questions.solution:
Current gain $\beta=45$ 
We know that, $\quad \beta=\frac{\Delta I_{c}}{\Delta I_{b}}$
$\therefore$
$$
\frac{\Delta I_{c}}{\Delta I_{b}}=45
$$
Potential drop across $1 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ or $1 \times 10^{3} \Omega$
Collector resistor is $5 \mathrm{V}$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &=I_{C} R \\
I_{C} &=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{5}{1 \times 10^{3}} \\
&=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{A}
\end{aligned}
$$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii).
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta I_{B} &-\frac{\Delta I_{C}}{45} \\
&=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{45}=\frac{1}{9} \times 10^{-3} \\
&=0.111 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{A} \\
&=111 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{A}=111 \mu \mathrm{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A NOR gate and a NAND gate are connected as shown in the figure. Two different sets of inputs are given to this setup. In the first case, the inputs to the gates are $A=0, B=0$, $C=0 .$ In the second case, the inputs are $A=1, B=0, C=1 .$ The output $D$ in the first case and second case respectively are
questions.solution:
In first case $A=0, B=0$
$\therefore$ Output of NOR gate, $Y=\overline{A+B}=1$ 
This output is the input for NAND gate, $i . e ., Y=1$
and $C=0$
$\therefore \quad D=\overline{Y \cdot C}=1$
In second case $A=1, B=0$
$\therefore$ Output of NOR gate, $Y=\overline{A+B}=0$ 
This output is the input for NAND gate $i . e . . Y=0$
and $C=1$
$\therefore \quad D=\overline{Y \cdot C}=1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical metal bars are heated in two different temperature and allowed to cool in the same surroundings. Which one of the following figures correctly shows their cooling curves?
questions.solution:
According to Newton's law of cooling, the rate of cooling of a body is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the body and its surroundings.

The temperature $T$ of the body at any time $t$ is given by the equation:
$T = T_0 + (T_i - T_0)e^{-kt}$
where $T_0$ is the temperature of the surroundings, $T_i$ is the initial temperature of the body, and $k$ is a positive constant that depends on the nature and area of the surface.

Since the two metal bars are identical, the cooling constant $k$ is the same for both.

Let the initial temperatures of the two bars be $T_{i1}$ and $T_{i2}$. The temperature difference between them at any time $t$ is:
$\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = (T_{i1} - T_{i2})e^{-kt}$

Since $T_{i1} \neq T_{i2}$, the temperature difference $\Delta T$ will never be zero for any finite time $t$. This means the cooling curves will never intersect.

Furthermore, as $t \to \infty$, both $e^{-kt} \to 0$, so the temperatures of both bars will exponentially approach the same surrounding temperature $T_0$.

The graph in option (D) correctly represents two non-intersecting exponential decay curves that approach the same horizontal asymptote.

Answer:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The apparent coefficient of expansion of a liquid, when heated in a copper vessel is $C$ and when heated in silver vessel is S . If A is linear coefficient of expansion of copper, then linear coefficient of expansion of silver is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {} \mathrm{C}=\gamma_L-\gamma_C \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=\gamma_L-\mathrm{3A} \Rightarrow \gamma_L=\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{3A} \\ & \mathrm{~S}=\gamma_L-\gamma_S \Rightarrow \gamma_S=\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{3A}-\mathrm{S} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha_{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{3A}-\mathrm{S}}{3}\end{aligned}$
