questions.q: $300 \mathrm{gm}$ of water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is added to $100 \mathrm{gm}$ of ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The final temperature of the mixture is
questions.solution:
Ref. at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ water
$(\Delta \mathrm{H})$ liberated $=300 \times 1 \times 25=7500 \mathrm{cal}$
$(\Delta \mathrm{H})$ required $=\mathrm{m}_{\text {ice }} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$=100 \times 80 \mathrm{cal}=8000 \mathrm{cal}$
as $(\Delta \mathrm{H})_{\text {req. }}>(\Delta \mathrm{H})_{\text {iib }}$ the mixture remains at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore$ Final temperature of mixture will be $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of boiling water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is added into a calorimeter containing $300 \mathrm{~g}$ of cold water at $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, temperature of the mixture becomes $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Then a metallic block of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ at $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is dipped into the mixture in the calorimeter. After reaching thermal equilibrium, the final temperature becomes $19^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the specific heat of the metal in C.G.S. unit?
questions.solution:
Hint: 
Let, heat capacity of calorimeter = ms
$\begin{aligned}
& 100 \times 1 \times 80=300 \times 1 \times 10+\mathrm{ms} \times 10 \\
\therefore \mathrm{ms}=500 \mathrm{Cal} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}$
Again, $(100+300) \times 1 \times 1+500 \times 1=1000 \times S_{b} \times 9$
$\therefore \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.1 \mathrm{Cal} / \mathrm{g}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider an engine that absorbs 130 cal of heat from a hot reservoir and delivers 30 cal heat to a cold reservoir in each cycle. The engine also consumes 2 J energy in each cycle to overcome friction. If the engine works at 90 cycles per minute, what will be the maximum power delivered to the load? [Assume the thermal equivalent of heat is $4.2 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{cal}]$
questions.solution:
Hint: 
work done per cycle $=100 \times 4.2-2=418 \mathrm{~J}$
Total work done $=418 \times 90$
Power $=\frac{418 \times 90}{60}=627 \mathrm{~W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two black bodies $A$ and $B$ have equal surface areas and are maintained at temperatures $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $177^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. What will be the ratio of the thermal energy radiated per second by $A$ to that by $B ?$
questions.solution:
According to the question, Area of both bodies $A$ and $B=A$
Temperature of body $A=27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=27+273 \mathrm{K}$
Temperature of body $B=177^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=177+273 \mathrm{K}$
Now, by Stefan-Boltzmann law, thermal energy radiated per second by a body
$$
Q=\sigma A T^{4}
$$
where, $A=$ Area
$T=$ temperature and $\quad \sigma=$ Stefan-Boltzmam's constant
So, the ratio of thermal energy radiated per second by $A$ to that by $B$ is
$$
\frac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\frac{\sigma A}{\sigma A}\left(\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{4}
$$
Now, $\frac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\left(\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{4}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\left(\frac{273+27}{273+177}\right)^{4} \\
\frac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\left(\frac{300}{450}\right)^{4}
\end{array}
$$
$$
\frac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{4}=\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)
$$
Ratio of thermal energy radiated per second
$$
Q_{1}: Q_{2}=16: 81
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A horizontal fire hose with a nozzle of cross-sectional arca $\frac{5}{\sqrt{21}} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{m}^{2}$ delivers a cubic metre of water in 10 s. What will be the maximum possible increase in the temperature of water while it hits a rigid wall (neglecting the effect of gravity)?
questions.solution:
Given, cross sectional area of nozzle
$$
A=\frac{5}{\sqrt{21}} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{m}^{2}
$$
and rate of heat transfer $Q=\frac{1}{10}=10^{-1} \mathrm{m}^{3} / \mathrm{s}$
Heat transfer $Q=A V$ $\therefore V=\frac{Q}{A}=\frac{10^{-1} \mathrm{m}^{3} / \mathrm{s}}{\frac{5}{\sqrt{21}} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{m}^{2}}=2 \sqrt{21} \times 10 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$
When it hits a rigid wall then maximum possible increase in temperature of water can be expressed as
$$
\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=m s \Delta T
$$
where, $m=$ mass, $s=$ specific heat of water
$$
=1 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}=4.2 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{J} / \mathrm{kg}
$$
and $\Delta T=$ increase in temperature
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { From Eq. }(i), \Delta T=& \frac{1}{2} \frac{v^{2}}{s} \\
&=\frac{\left(2 \sqrt{21} \times 10^{1}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 4.2 \times 10^{3}} \\
&=\frac{84 \times 10^{2}}{8.4 \times 10^{3}} \\
&=1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The water equivalent of a calorimeter is $10 \mathrm{g}$ and it contains $50 \mathrm{g}$ of water at $15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Some amount of ice, initially at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is dropped in it and half of the ice melts till equilibrium is reached. What was the initial amount of ice that was dropped (when specific heat of ice $=0.5 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{gm}^{-1}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1},$ specific heat of water $=1.0$
cal $\mathrm{gm}^{-1 \circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$ and latent heat of melting of ice $=80$ cal $\mathrm{gm}^{-1}$ )?
questions.solution:
Let the mass of ice $=m$
Applying calorimetry principle, heat given $=$ heat taken $\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) s_{1}\left(t_{1}-t\right)=\frac{m L}{2}+m s_{2}\left(t-t_{2}\right)$
Putting values, we get $(10+50) \times 1 \times(15-0)=\frac{m}{2} \times 80+m \times 0.5[0-(-10)]$
$\Rightarrow 60 \times 15=40 m+\frac{m}{2} \times 10=45 m$
$\therefore \quad m=\frac{60 \times 15}{45}=20 \mathrm{g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid rectangular sheet has two different coefficients of linear expansion $\alpha_{1}$ and $\alpha_{2}$ along its length and breadth respectively. The coefficient of surface expansion is (for $\left.\alpha_{1} t< < 1, \alpha_{2} t< < 1\right)$
questions.solution: The coefficient of linear expansion along its length $=\alpha_{1}$ The coefficient of linear expansion along its breadth $=\alpha_{2}$ Increase in length, $$ L_{i}=l_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta t\right) $$ Increase in breadth, $$ B_{t}=b_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{2} \Delta t_{2}\right) $$ Let coefficient of surface expansion is $\beta$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Area } &=\text { length } \times \text { breadth } \\ &=L_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta t\right) \times b_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{2} \Delta t\right) \\ &=L_{0} b_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta t\right)\left(1+\alpha_{2} \Delta t\right) \\ &=S_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta t+\alpha_{2} \Delta t+\ldots\right) \\ \text { where, } S_{0} &=l_{0} \cdot b_{0} \end{aligned} $$ $=$ Initial area of surface In state of expansion, $$ \begin{aligned} S_{t} &=L_{r} \times B_{r} \\ &=L_{0} b_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta t\right)\left(1+\alpha_{2} \Delta t\right) \\ &=S_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta t+\alpha_{2} \Delta t+\ldots\right) \\ S_{t} &=S_{0}(1+\beta \Delta t) \\ \therefore S_{0}(1+\beta \Delta t) &=S_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta t+\alpha_{2} \Delta t+\ldots\right) \\ \beta \cdot \Delta t &=\alpha_{1} \Delta t+\alpha_{2} \Delta t \\ \beta &=\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the temperature of the Sun gets doubled, the rate of energy received on the Earth will increase by a factor of
questions.solution:
Suppose temperature of $\mathrm{Sun}=T$
When it is doubled, $T^{\prime}=2 T$
From law of radiation (Stefan's law)
$$
E \propto T^{4}=\sigma T^{4}
$$
$\sigma=$ Stefan's constant.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
E_{1}=T^{4} \\
E_{2} \approx(2 \Pi)^{4} \\
\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\frac{T^{4}}{2^{4} \cdot T^{4}} \\
E_{2}=16 E_{1}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of the water of a pond is $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ while that of the surrounding atmosphere is $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the density of ice is $\rho$ coefficient of thermal conductivity is $k$ and latent heat of melting is $L,$ then the thickness $Z$ of ice layer formed increases as a function of time $t$ is
questions.solution: Temperature of water $=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Temperature of surrounding atmosphere $=-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Density of ice $=\rho$ Coefficient of thermal conductivity $=k$ latent heat of melting $=L$ The rate of heat flow $H=\frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{k A\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)}{x}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} =\frac{k f(0-(-20)]}{x} \\ =\frac{k A \times 20}{x} \\ \frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{d m}{d t} \cdot L \quad(\because m L=Q) \\ d m=\rho A \cdot d x \\ \frac{d Q}{d t}=\rho A \cdot \frac{d x}{d t} L=\frac{k A \cdot 20}{x} \\ \int_{0}^{Z} x \cdot d x=\frac{20 k}{\rho L} \int_{0}^{t} d t \Rightarrow Z^{2}=\frac{40 k}{\rho L} t \end{array} $$ So, thickness $Z$ increases as a function of time $t$ according to the above equation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three bodies of the same material and having masses $m, m$ and $3 m$ are at temperatures $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \quad 50^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \quad$ and $\quad 60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. If the bodies are brought in thermal contact, the final temperature will be
questions.solution:
Let the final temperature after the masses in thermal contact is $\theta$, then from the principle of calonimetry.
Heat lost $=$ Heat gained
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow 3 m s(60-\theta) &=m s(\theta-50)+m s(\theta-40) \\ \Rightarrow \quad 3(60-\theta) &=\theta-50+\theta-40 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 180-3 \theta &=2 \theta-90 \\ \theta &=\frac{270}{5}=54^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid maintained at $t_{1}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is kept in an evacuated chamber at temperature $t_{2}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\left(t_{2} \gg t_{1}\right) .$ The rate of heat absorbed by the body is proportional to
questions.solution:
As we know that, the rate of cooling is
$$
\frac{d \theta}{d t}=b A\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right)
$$
where, $b A=$ constant $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d \theta}{d t} \propto \theta-\theta_{0}$
Also $-\frac{d T}{d t} \propto T-T_{0}$
where, $T=$ temperature and $\quad t=$ time
So, the rate of heat absorbed by cooled body is proportional to $t_{2}-t_{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A scientist proposes a new temperature scale in which the ice point is $25 \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{X}$ is the new unit of temperature) and the steam point is $305 \mathrm{X}$. The specific heat capacity of water in this new scale is $\left(\operatorname{in} J \mathrm{kg}^{-1} \mathrm{X}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
Given, $305 \times-25 X=100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
(: $\mathrm{X}$ is the new unit of temperature
$$
(305-25) \mathrm{X}=100^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \Rightarrow 280 \mathrm{X}=100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
$$
$\therefore \quad 1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=2.8 \mathrm{x}$
The specinc heat capacity of water
$\begin{aligned}=4200 \frac{j 0 u l e}{k g \cdot^{\circ} C} &=4200 \times \frac{j \text { oule }}{k g \times 2.8 \times} \\ &=1500 J / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{X} \\ &=1.5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{kg}^{-1} \mathrm{X}^{-1} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Same quantity of ice is filled in each of the two metal containers $P$ and $Q$ having the same size, shape and wall thickness but made of different materials. The containers are kept in identical surroundings. The ice in $P$ melts completely in time $t_{1}$ whereas in $Q$ takes a time $t_{2}$. The ratio of thermal conductivities of the materials of $P$ and $Q$ is
questions.solution:
We know that Relation between temperature gradient (TG) and thermal conductivity $(K)$
So,
$$
\frac{d \theta}{d t}=-K A \frac{d \theta}{d x}=-K A \times(\mathrm{TG})
$$
Le. $\mathrm{TG} \propto \frac{1}{K}\left(\frac{d \theta}{d t}=\right.$ constant $)$
or $K \times \frac{1}{T}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
K_{1} \times \frac{1}{t_{1}} \\
K_{2} \times \frac{1}{t_{2}}
\end{array}
$$
From the Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\frac{1 / t_{1}}{1 / t_{2}}
$$
$$
K_{1}: K_{2}=t_{2}: t_{1}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $A$ 10 W electric heater is used to heat a container filled with $0.5 \mathrm{kg}$ of water. It is found that the temperature of water and the container rises by $3^{\circ} \mathrm{K}$ in $15 \mathrm{min}$. The container is then emptied, dried and filled with $2 \mathrm{kg}$ of oil. The same heater now raises the temperature of container-oil system by $2^{\circ} \mathrm{K}$ in $20 \mathrm{min}$. Assuming that there is no heat loss in the process and the specific heat of water is $4200 \mathrm{d} \mathrm{kg}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1}$, the specific heat of oil in the same unit is equal to
questions.solution:
$m_{1} s_{1} \Delta t+m_{2} s_{2} \Delta t=$ Work done 

$m_{1} s_{1} \Delta t+m_{2} s_{2} \Delta t=P_{1} t_{1}$
where $m_{1}=0.5 \mathrm{kg}$
Specific heat $s_{1}=4200 \mathrm{J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Delta t=\Delta t_{1}=\Delta t_{2}=3 \mathrm{K} \\
P_{1}=P_{2}=10 \mathrm{W} \\
t_{1}=15 \times 60=900 \mathrm{s}
\end{array}
$$
$s_{2}=$ Specific heat capacity of container
$$
0.5 \times 4200 \times(3-0)+m_{2} \mathrm{s}_{2} \times(3-0)
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=10 \times 15 \times 60 \\
2100 \times 3+m_{2} s_{2} \times 3 &=9000 \\
m_{2} s_{2} &=\frac{9000-6300}{3} \\
m_{2} s_{2} &=900
\end{aligned}
$$
Similarly, in case of oil
$$
m_{1} s_{0} \Delta t+m_{2} s_{2} \Delta t=P_{2} t_{2}
$$
where $s_{0}=$ specific heat capacity of oil
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1} &=P_{2}=10 \mathrm{W} \\
2 \times s_{0} \times 2+900 \times 2 &=10 \times 20 \times 60 \\
4 s_{0}+1800 &=12000 \\
4 s_{0} &=12000-1800 \\
s_{0} &=\frac{10200}{4}=2550 \\
&=2.55 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{kg}^{-1} \mathrm{k}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The specific heat $C$ of a solid at low temperature shows temperature dependence according to the relation $C=D T^{3}$, where $D$ is a constant and $T$ is the temperature is kelvin. A piece of this solid of mass $m \mathrm{kg}$ is taken and its temperature is raised from $20 \mathrm{K}$ to $30 \mathrm{K}$. The amount of heat required in the process in energy units is
questions.solution:
Amount of heat required, $Q=\int d Q$ $=\int_{T_{1}=20}^{T_{2}=30} \mathrm{mcdT}$
Given, $C=D T^{3}$
$\therefore \quad Q=\int_{20}^{30} m D T^{3} d T=m D \int_{20}^{30} T^{3} d T$
$$
=m D \frac{1}{4}\left[(30)^{2}-(20)^{2}\right]=\frac{65}{4} \times 10^{4} m D
$$

questions.q: \(1.56 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~J}\) of heat is conducted through a \(2 \mathrm{~m}^2\) wall of \(12 \mathrm{~cm}\) thick in one hour. Temperature difference between the two sides of the wall is \(20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\). The thermal conductivity of the material of the wall is (in \(\mathrm{W} \mathrm{m}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\) )
questions.solution:
Hints: \(\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{KA} \Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{x}}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1.56 \times 10^5}{3600}=\frac{\mathrm{K} \times 2 \times 20}{12 \times 10^{-2}} \\
& \mathrm{~K}=\frac{1.56 \times 10^5 \times 12 \times 10^{-2}}{3600 \times 2 \times 20} \\
& =\frac{1.56}{12}=0.13
\end{aligned}\)

$\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=480 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two substance \(A\) and \(B\) of same mass are heated at constant rate. The variation of temperature \(\theta\) of the substance with time \(\mathrm{t}\) is shown in the figure. Choose the correct statement
options: Specific heat of \(A\) is greater than that of \(B\)
Specific heat of \(B\) is greater than that of \(A\)
None of the above is true
questions.solution:
Hint :
\(\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{mC} \Delta \theta \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dH}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{mC} \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \quad \frac{\mathrm{dH}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{a} \text { constant } \\ & \therefore \frac{d \theta}{d t} \propto \frac{1}{C} \\ & \text { i.e. slope } \propto \frac{1}{C} \quad \therefore C_B < C_A \\ & \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A given quantity of gas is taken from \(A\) to \(C\) in two ways; \(a\) ) directly from \(A \rightarrow C\) along a straight line and \(b\) ) in two steps, from \(A \rightarrow B\) and then from \(B \rightarrow C\). Work done and heat absorbed along the direct path \(A \rightarrow C\) is \(200 \mathrm{~J}\) and \(280 \mathrm{~J}\) respectively
questions.solution:
Hint : \(\Delta W=200 \mathrm{~J} \quad \Delta Q=280 \mathrm{~J} \quad\) for path \(A C\)
\(\therefore \quad \Delta U=\Delta Q-\Delta W=280-200=80 \mathrm{~J}\)
\((\Delta U)\) is same for both paths.
\(\therefore \Delta Q=\Delta W+\Delta U=80+80=160 \mathrm{~J}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a thermodynamic process where internal energy $U=A P^2 \mathrm{~V}(\mathrm{~A}=$ constant). If the process is performed adiabatically, then
questions.solution:
$U=A P^2 V$
$\frac{d U}{d V}=A P^2+2 P A V \frac{d P}{d V}$ [For adiabatic process $\left.d Q=0, d U=-d w=-P d V\right]$
$-\frac{P d V}{d V}=A P^2+2 P A V \frac{d P}{d V}$
$-1=A P+2 A V \frac{d P}{d V} \Rightarrow-1-A P=2 A V \frac{d P}{d V}$
$-\int \frac{d V}{V}=\int \frac{2 A d P}{A P+1}$
$-\ell n V+C=\frac{2 A}{A} \ln (A P+1)$
$C=\ell n(A P+1)^2+\ell n V \Rightarrow C=(A P+1)^2 V$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of a diatomic ideal gas undergoes a process shown in $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V}$ diagram. The total heat given to the gas $(\ln 2=0.7)$ is
questions.solution:
For a Diatomic gas the total heat given is $\Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{AB}}+\Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{BC}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{AB}}(\text { Isochoric Process })=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{V}} \Delta \mathrm{T} \\
&=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\gamma-1}=\frac{2 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0-\mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0}{\frac{7}{5}-1}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0}{\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)}=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0=2.5 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0
\end{aligned}
$$
$\Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{BC}}$ (Isothermal Process)
$$
Q_{B C}=n R T \ell n \frac{V_f}{V_i} \quad=P V \ell n \frac{V_f}{V_i}
$$
$$
=2 \mathrm{P}_0 \times \mathrm{V}_0 \ln \left(\frac{2 \mathrm{~V}_0}{\mathrm{~V}_0}\right)=2 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0 \ell n 2=1.4 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\text {total }}=3.9 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Certain amount of an ideal gas is taken from its initial state 1 to final state 4 through the paths $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4$ as shown in figure. $A B, C D, E F$ are all isotherms. If $v_p$ is the most probable speed of the molecules, then
questions.solution: $v_p \propto \sqrt{T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of an ideal monoatomic gas expands along the polytrope $PV^3=$ constant from $V_1$ to $V_2$ at a constant pressure $P_1$. The temperature during the process is such that molar specific heat $C_V=\frac{3 R}{2}$. The total heat absorbed during the process can be expressed as
questions.solution:
Assuming $P_1$ as initial pressure.
$\mathrm{PV}^3=$ constant $n=3$
$$
\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}+\frac{\mathrm{R}}{1-\mathrm{n}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{R}}{1-3}=\mathrm{R}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&T_i=\frac{P_1 V_1}{R} \\
&P_2 V_2{ }^3=P_1 V_1^3 \\
&P_2=P_1 \times\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^3
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
T_2=\frac{P_2 V_2}{R}=\frac{P_1}{R} \times\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^3 \times V_2=\frac{P_1 V_1^3}{R V_2^2}
$$
$$
\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{nC} \Delta \mathrm{T}
$$
$$
=1 \times R \times\left(\frac{P_1 V_1^3}{R V_2^2}-\frac{P_1 V_1}{R}\right)
$$
$$
\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_1^2}{\mathrm{~V}_2^2}-1\right)
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given figure, 1 represents isobaric, 2 represents isothermal and 3 represents adiabatic processes of an ideal gas. If $\Delta \mathrm{U}_{1}, \Delta \mathrm{U}_{2}, \Delta \mathrm{U}_{3}$ be the changes in internal energy in these processes respectively, then
questions.solution: $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{1}=\mathrm{T}_{0}$ $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{2}=0$ $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{3}=-\mathrm{ve}$ $\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{U}_{1}>\Delta \mathrm{U}_{2}>\Delta \mathrm{U}_{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the P-V diagram for 1 mole of an ideal monatomic gas shown in the figure. Which of the following statements is/are true?
options: The change in internal energy for the whole process is zero
Heat is rejected during the process
Change in internal energy for process $A \rightarrow B$ is $-\frac{3}{2} P_{0} V_{0}$
Work done by the gas during the entire process is $2 \mathrm{P}_{0} \mathrm{~V}_{0}$
questions.solution: $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{n} \mathrm{Cv} \Delta \mathrm{T} \\ \Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{nR}}-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{nR}}=0 \end{array} $$ Hence $\Delta \mathrm{U}=0$ (option $\mathrm{A}$ is correct) $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\text { work } \\ \Delta \mathrm{U}=0, \text { work }=(-) \end{array} $$ $\Rightarrow \Delta Q=(-)$; Hence heat is rejected during the process (option B is correct) for process $A B$ $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{nR}}-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{nR}}\right)=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{0} \mathrm{~V}_{0}}{\mathrm{R}}-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{0} 2 \mathrm{~V}_{0}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)=-\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{P}_{0} \mathrm{~V}_{0}$ (option $\mathrm{C}$ is correct) during entire process work done by gas is $-P_{0} V_{0} \quad$ (option $D$ is correct)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas undergoes the cyclic process abca as shown in the given $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V}$ diagram. It rejects $50 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat during $a b$ and absorbs $80 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat during ca. During $b c$, there is no transfer of heat and $40 \mathrm{~J}$ of work is done by the gas. What should be the area of the closed curve abca?
questions.solution:
Hint:
Area of cycle $=\mathrm{W}_{\text {total }}$ $=\Delta Q_{\text {net }}=\Delta Q_{A B}+\Delta Q_{B C}+\Delta Q_{C A}=-50+0+80=30 \mathrm{~J}$ NOTE: In process $\mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{BC}}>0$ and $\Delta \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{BC}}>0$ $\therefore \Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{BC}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{BC}}>0$ Thus $\Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{eC}}=0$ as given in question isn't possible. But we have neglected this technical mistake while answering.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A container $\mathrm{AB}$ in the shape of a rectangular parallelopiped of length $5 \mathrm{~m}$ is divided internally by a movable partition $\mathrm{P}$ as shown in the figure. The left compartment is filled with a given mass of an ideal gas of molar mass 32 while the right compartment is filled with an equal mass of another ideal gas of molar mass 18 at same temperature. What will be the distance of P from the left wall A when equilibrium is established?
questions.solution:
Hint:
At equilibrium $\quad P_{1}=P_{2}, T_{1}=T_{2}$ (assuming wall to be conducting) $\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{\mu_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{\mu_{2}}=R T$ $\frac{(5-x) 32}{m}=\frac{x \times 18}{m}$ So, $x=3.2 \mathrm{~m}$ Hence the distance of the piston from $\mathrm{A}=5-3.2=1.8 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the given diagram. An ideal gas is contained in a chamber (left) of volume $V$ and is at an absolute temperature $T$. It is allowed to rush freely into the right chamber of volume $V$ which is initially vacuum. The whole system is thermally isolated. What will be the final temperature of the equilibrium has been attained?
questions.solution: Since, the whole system is isolated, this means, there is no transfer of heat between the system and the surrounding. Also, the right side container is initially vacuum, so the gas could easily rush there without any resistive force. As the right side container has no temperature. Thus, the temperature of the ideal gas would remain same even if it enters right side chamber. Therefore, final temperature attained at the equilibrium will be $T$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The initial pressure and volume of a given mass of an ideal gas $\left(\right.$ with $\frac{C_{p}}{C_{V}}=\gamma),$ taken in a cylinder fitted with a piston, are $p_{0}$ and $V_{0}$ respectively. At this stage the gas has the same temperature as that of the surrounding medium which is $T_{0}$. It is adiabatically compressed to a volume equal to $\frac{V_{0}}{2}$ Subsequently the gas is allowed to come to thermal equilibrium with the surroundings. What is the heat released to the surrounding?
questions.solution:
The equation of state for an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic process is given as,
$$
T V^{\gamma-1}=\text { constant }
$$
Let the temperature after adiabatic compression as given in the question be $T$ then,
$$
T_{0} V^{\gamma-1}=T\left(\frac{V_{0}}{2}\right)^{\gamma-1}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad T=T_{0} 2^{\gamma-1}$
Now, heat released at volume $\frac{V_{0}}{2}$ to achieve
temperature $T_{0}$ The net heat released can be determined by the
equation.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Q &=\mu C_{V} \Delta T \\
&=\mu \times \frac{R}{\gamma-1} \quad\left(T_{0} 2^{\gamma-1}-T_{0}\right) \\
&=\frac{\mu R T_{0}}{\gamma-1}\left(2^{\gamma-1}-1\right)\left(\because \mu T_{0} R=p_{0} V_{0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Heat released $=\frac{p_{0} V_{0}}{\gamma-1}\left(2^{\gamma-1}-1\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For an ideal gas with initial pressure and volume $p_{i}$ and $V_{i}$ respectively, a reversible isothermal expansion happens, when its volume becomes $V_{0}$. Then, it is compressed to its original volume $V_{i}$ by a reversible adiabatic process. If the final pressure is $p_{f}$ then which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
questions.solution: In $p-V$ diagram, the slope of an adiabatic curve at any point in steeper than that of isothermal curve at that point. Thus, $p_{f}>p_{i}$

questions.q: Which of the following statements(s) is/are true? "Internal energy of an ideal gas ..........."
options: remains constant in an isothermal process.

$\because$ Internal energy of an ideal gas depends only on the temperature of gas.
$$
E_{\text {in }} \propto T
$$

questions.q: 2 moles of an ideal monoatomic gas is carried from a state $\left(p_{0}, V_{0}\right)$ to state $\left(2 p_{0}, 2 V_{0}\right)$ along a straight line path in a $p-V$ diagram. The amount of heat absorbed by the gas in the process is given by

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The perfect gas equation for $4 \mathrm{g}$ of hydrogen gas is
questions.solution:
Mass of gas $=4 \mathrm{g} \quad$ (given)
From pertect gas equation
$$
p V=n R T
$$
where $n=$ number of moles here, $\left(n=\frac{4}{2}=2\right)$
$\therefore \quad p V=2 R T$
It will be the perfect gas equation for $4 \mathrm{g}$ of hydrogen.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The pressure $p$, volume $V$ and temperature $T$ for a certain gas are related by $p=\frac{A T-B T^{2}}{V}$ where $A$ and $B$ are constants. The work done by the gas when the temperature changes from $T_{1}$ to $T_{2}$ while the pressure remains constant, is given by
questions.solution:
Given $P=\frac{A T-B T^{2}}{V}$
$\begin{array}{lc}\Rightarrow & P V=A T-B T^{2} \\ \Rightarrow & P \Delta V=A \Delta T-B T \Delta T\end{array}$
On integrating, we get Work $=\int P d V=A \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} d T-B \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} T d T$
$=A\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)-\frac{B}{2}\left[\left(T_{2}\right)^{2}-\left(T_{1}\right)^{2}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of an ideal monoatomic gas is heated at a constant pressure from $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $100^\circ C$. Then the change in the internal enery of the gas is (Given, $R=8.32 \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1}$ )

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of a van der Waals' gas obeying the equation $\left(p+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right)(V-b)=R T$ undergoes the quasi-static cyclic process which is shown in the $p \cdot V$ diagram. The net heat absorbed by the gas in this process is
questions.solution: For the cyclic process Heat absorbed = Work done $$ \begin{array}{l} =\text { Area }=\frac{1}{2}(\Delta p) \times \Delta V \\ =\frac{1}{2}\left(p_{1}-p_{2}\right) \times\left(V_{1}-V_{2}\right) \\ =\frac{1}{2}\left(p_{1}-p_{2}\right)\left(V_{1}-V_{2}\right) \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $A$ heating element of resistance $r$ is fitted inside an adiabatic cylinder which carries a frictionless piston of mass $m$ and cross section A as shown in diagram. The cylinder contains one mole diatomic current the element temperatures time $t$ as (a and $\beta$ are constants), while pressure remains constant. The atmospheric pressure above the piston is $P_{0}$. Then
options: the rate of increase in internal energy is $\frac{5}{2} R(\alpha+\beta t)$
the current flowing in the element is $\sqrt{\frac{5}{2 r} R(\alpha+\beta t)}$
the piston moves upwards with constant acceleration
the piston moves upwards with constant speed
questions.solution:
We know that Internal energy, $U=\frac{n f R T}{2}=\frac{5 R}{2}\left(\alpha t+\frac{1}{2} \beta t^{2}\right)$
Differentiate with respect to $t$
$$
\frac{d U}{d t}=\frac{5 R}{2}[\alpha+\beta t]
$$
But, $d Q=n C_{p} d T$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \quad \frac{d Q}{d t}=n C_{p} \times \frac{d T}{d t} \Rightarrow i^{2} r=\frac{7}{2} R \times[\alpha+\beta t] \\
\quad i=\sqrt{\frac{7 R}{2 r}(\alpha+\beta t)} \\
p V=n R T
\end{array}
$$
or $\quad V=\frac{n R T}{p}=\frac{n R}{p}\left(\alpha t+\frac{1}{2} \beta t^{2}\right)$
$X=\frac{n R}{p A}\left(\alpha t+\frac{1}{2} \beta t^{2}\right)$
$$
v=\frac{n R}{p A}(\alpha+\beta t)
$$
and acceleration $=\frac{n R}{p A} \times \beta$
So, the rate of increase in internal energy is $\frac{5}{2} R(\alpha+\beta t)$ and the piston moves upwards with constant acceleration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A frictionless piston-cylinder based enclosure contains some amount of gas at a pressure of $400 \mathrm{kPa}$. Then heat is transferred fo the gas at constant pressure in a quasi-static process. The piston moves up slowly through a height of $10 \mathrm{cm} .$ If the piston has a cross-sectional area of $0.3 \mathrm{m}^{2},$ the work done by the gas in this process is
questions.solution:
$W_{\text {constant pressure }}=p \times \Delta V$ $=400 \times 10^{3} \times 0.3 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}$
$=400 \times 10 \times 3$
$=12000=12 \mathrm{kJ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal mono-atomic gas of given mass is heated at constant pressure. In this process, the fraction of supplied heat energy used for the increase of the internal energy of the gas is
questions.solution:
Fraction $=\frac{\Delta U}{\Delta Q}=\frac{C_{Y}}{C_{p}}$
For monoatomic gas $\frac{C_{p}}{C_{\gamma}}=\gamma=\frac{5}{3}$
So, $\quad \frac{C_{\gamma}}{C_{p}}=\frac{1}{\gamma}=\frac{3}{5}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas is compressed isothermally until its pressure is doubled and then allowed to expand adiabatically to regain its original volume \(\left(\gamma=1.4\right.\) and \(\left.2^{-14}=0.38\right)\). The ratio of the final to initial pressure is
questions.solution:
Hints:
\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{V}^\gamma=\left(2 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2}\right)^\gamma \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}=2\left(\frac{\gamma}{2 \gamma}\right)^\gamma=2(2)^{-\gamma} \\ & =2 \times 0.38=0.76 \\ & \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Density and volume of a body are given as $(20 \pm 4)$ gm/cm$^3$ and $(10 \pm 1)$ cm$^3$ respectively. The absolute error in measurement of mass is
questions.solution:
Mass $M = \rho \times V$

Given $\rho = 20$ gm/cm$^3$, $\Delta \rho = 4$ gm/cm$^3$

$V = 10$ cm$^3$, $\Delta V = 1$ cm$^3$

$M = 20 \times 10 = 200$ gm

The maximum fractional error in mass is given by

$\dfrac{\Delta M}{M} = \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{\Delta V}{V}$

$\dfrac{\Delta M}{200} = \dfrac{4}{20} + \dfrac{1}{10}$

$\dfrac{\Delta M}{200} = \dfrac{4 + 2}{20} = \dfrac{6}{20}$

$\Delta M = 200 \times \dfrac{6}{20} = 60$ gm

The absolute error in measurement of mass is $60$ gm.

Answer: $60$ gm

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity $\nu$ of a particle at time $t$ is given by $\nu = at + \dfrac{b}{t + c}$, where $a, b$ and $c$ are constants. The dimension of $a, b$ and $c$ are, respectively
questions.solution:
According to the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, each term in the equation must have the same dimensions.

The dimension of velocity $\nu$ is $[LT^{-1}]$.

For the term $at$, we have $[a][T] = [LT^{-1}]$, which gives $[a] = [LT^{-2}]$.

In the term $\dfrac{b}{t + c}$, the quantity $c$ is added to time $t$, so $c$ must have the dimension of time. Thus, $[c] = [T]$.

The dimension of the entire term $\dfrac{b}{t + c}$ must be equal to the dimension of velocity. Therefore, $\dfrac{[b]}{[T]} = [LT^{-1}]$, which gives $[b] = [L]$.

The dimensions of $a, b$, and $c$ are $LT^{-2}, L$, and $T$ respectively.

Answer: $LT^{-2}, L, T$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the dimensions of length are expressed as $\mathrm{G}^{\times} \mathrm{C}^y \mathrm{~h}^{\mathrm{z}}$, where $\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{C}$ and h are the universal gravitational constant, speed of light and Planck's constant respectively, then
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { } L=\left[M^{-1} L^3 T^{-2}\right]^x\left[L T^{-1}\right]^y\left[M L^2 T^{-1}\right]^z \\ M^0 L T^0=M^{-x+z} L^{3 x+y+2 z} T^{-2 x-y-2} & \\ -x+z=0 ; \quad 3 x+y+2 z=1 ; \qquad 2 x+y+z=0
\\ x=z ; \quad \qquad 2 z=1 \qquad \qquad \qquad 3x+y=0 \\ \qquad \qquad \qquad z=1 / 2 \\  \qquad \qquad \qquad x=\frac{1}{2} \\ \qquad \qquad \qquad \text { and } y=-\frac{3}{2}\end{array}$

questions.q: The Power $(P)$ radiated from an accelerated charged particle is given by $P \propto \frac{(q a)^m}{c^n}$ where $q$ is the charge, $a$ is the acceleration of the particle and c is speed of light in vacuum. From dimensional analysis, the value of m and n respectively, are

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Which of the following quantity has the dimension of length?
(h is Planck's constant, m is the mass of electron and c is the velocity of light)
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Hint : }[\mathrm{h}]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right] \\
& {[\mathrm{c}]=\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]} \\
& {[\mathrm{m}]=\left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^0 \mathrm{~T}^0\right]} \\
& \text { Dimension of length } \\
& {[\mathrm{L}]=\mathrm{h}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{c}^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{m}^z} \\
& {\left[\mathrm{~L}^1\right]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]^{\mathrm{y}}\left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^0 \mathrm{~T}^0\right]^{\mathrm{z}}}
\end{aligned}
$$
Dimensional analysis on mass
$$
0=x+z \Rightarrow x=-z \Rightarrow z=-1
$$

Dimensional analysis on length
$$
1=2 x+y \Rightarrow 2 x-x=1 \Rightarrow x=1
$$

Dimensional analysis on time
$$
0=-x-y \Rightarrow-x=+y \Rightarrow y=-1
$$

Therefore final formula is $\frac{h}{\mathrm{mc}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the energy, $E=G^p h^q c^r$, where $G$ is the universal gravitational constant, $h$ is the Planck's constant and $c$ is the velocity of light, then the values of $p, q$ and $r$ are, respectively
questions.solution:
$$
E=G^p h^q c^r
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]^p\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]^q\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]^r} \\
& =\mathrm{M}^{-p+q} \mathrm{~L}^{3 p+2 q+r} \mathrm{~T}^{-2 p-q-r}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying principle of homogeneity of dimensions, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& -p+q=1 \\
& 3 p+2 q+r=2 \\
& -2 p-q-r=-2
\end{aligned}
$$

Add (iii) and (iv), $p+q=0$
Add (ii) and (v), we get $q=\frac{1}{2}$
From (ii), we get $p=q-1=\frac{1}{2}-1=-\frac{1}{2}$.
Put in (iii), we get $-\frac{3}{2}+1+r=2, \quad r=5 / 2$

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The correct dimensional formula for impulse is given by
questions.solution:
As we know that,
Impulse, $I=$ force $(F) \times$ small time interval
$\begin{array}{ll}
I=m a \times t & {[\because F=m a]} \\
I=[\mathrm{M}]\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-2}\right] \times[\mathrm{T}] \\
I=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-1}\right]
\end{array}$
Hence, the correct dimensional formula for impulse is given by $\left[\mathrm{M} \mathrm{L} \mathrm{T}^{-1} 1 .\right.$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical liquid drop is placed on a horizontal plane. A small disturbance causes the volume of the drop to oscillate. The time period of oscillation ( $T$ ) of the liquid drop depends on radius $(n$ of the drop, density $(\rho)$ and surface tension (5) of the liquid. Which among the following will be a possible expression for $T$ (where, $k$ is a dimensionless constant)?
questions.solution:
According to the question. time period. $T \alpha r^{a} p^{b} s^{c}$
$r=k r^{a} p^{b} s^{c}$
Thus, putting dimension, we get
Equating the dimensions of both sides, we get
$[T]=[\mathrm{L}]^{a}\left[\mathrm{ML}^{-3}]^ \mathrm{b}\left[\mathrm{MT}^{-2} ]^c\right.\right.$
$[T]=[M]^{b+c} \cdot[L]^{2a-3b} [T]^{-2c}$
$b+c=0, a-3 b=0$
and $-2 x=$
$\begin{array}{l}
c=-\frac{1}{2} \\
b=\frac{1}{2} \\
a=3 b=\frac{3}{2}
\end{array}$
Putting these value of $a, b$ and $c$ into Eq. (i), we get
$T=k \cdot r^{\frac{3}{2}} p^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot s^{-\frac{1}{2}}$
$=k \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{s} r^{3}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimension of the universal constant of gravitation, $G$ is
questions.solution:
The dimensions of universal constant $G$, is obtained by Newton's law of gravitation
$F=\frac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
G=\frac{F \times r^{2}}{m_{1} m_{2}} \\
F=\text { Force }
\end{array}$
$r=$ Distance $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are masses
$\begin{aligned}
G &=\frac{\left[M^{1} L^{1} T^{-2}\right]\left[L^{2}\right]}{[M][M]} \\
&=\frac{\left[M^{\prime} L^{2} T^{-2}\right]}{\left[M^{2}\right]}=\left[M^{-1} L^{2} T^{-2}\right]
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In which of the following pairs, the two physical quantities have different dimensions?
options: Moment of inertia and moment of a force
questions.solution:
(a) Planck's constant = Energy
$=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{T}^{-1}\right]$
Angular momentum = Moment of inertia Angular velocity
$=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{2}\right] \times\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right)=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{T}^{-1}\right]$
(b) Impulse = Force x Time
$=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right] \mathrm{IT}=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-1}\right]$
and linear momentum = Mass $\times$ Velocity
$=\left[\mathrm{M}\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-1}\right)\right.$
(c) Moment of inertia = Mass $\times(\text { Distance })^{2}$
$=\left[M L^{2}\right]$
and moment of force = Force x Distance
$=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right] \mathrm{U}=\left[\mathrm{M}^{2} \mathrm{T}^{-2}\right]$
(d) Energy $=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{T}^{-2} \mathrm{l}\right.$ and torque $=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{T}^{-2} 1\right.$
So, (c) option has different dimensions.

questions.q: If $n$ denotes a positive integer, $h$ the Planck constant, $q$ the charge and $B$ the magnetic field, then the quantity $\left[\frac{n h}{2 \pi q B}\right]$ has the
questions.solution:
$\left[\frac{n h}{2 \pi q B}\right]$
where $n$ and $2 \pi$ dimensionless quantity
So, $[h]=[m v r]$
$\begin{aligned}
\mid q B] &=\left[\frac{q v B}{v}\right]=\left[\frac{F}{v}\right] \\
\therefore \quad\left[\frac{m v r}{F / v}\right] &=\frac{[m v r][v]}{[F]} \\
&=\left[\frac{\left[m v^{2} r\right]}{r}\right]=\left[r^{2}\right\}=\text { Area }
\end{aligned}$

questions.q: The equation of state of a gas is given by $\left(p+\frac{a}{v^{3}}\right)\left(V-b^{2}\right)=c T,$ where $p, V, T$ are pressure, volume and temperature respectively and $a, b, c$ are constants. The dimensions of $a$ and $b$ are respectively

$[P]=\left[\frac{a}{V^{3}}\right] \Rightarrow\left[M L^{-1} T^{-2}\right]=\frac{a}{\left[L^{3}\right]^{3}}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad a &=\left[M L^{8} T^{-2}\right] \\ &[V]=\left[b^{2}\right] \Rightarrow\left[L^{3}\right]=b^{2} \Rightarrow b=\left[L^{3 / 2}\right] \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Up to what distance, the ray optics is sufficiently valid when the aperture is $6$ mm wide and the wavelength is $6000$ Å?
questions.solution:
The distance up to which ray optics is a good approximation is given by the Fresnel distance $Z_F$.

The formula for Fresnel distance is:

$Z_F = \dfrac{a^2}{\lambda}$

Given:

Aperture width, $a = 6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

Wavelength, $\lambda = 6000 \text{ \AA} = 6000 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} = 6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$Z_F = \dfrac{(6 \times 10^{-3})^2}{6 \times 10^{-7}}$

$Z_F = \dfrac{36 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-7}}$

$Z_F = 6 \times 10^1 = 60 \text{ m}$

Thus, ray optics is valid up to a distance of $60 \text{ m}$.

Answer: $60$ m

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two points of monochromatic and coherent sources of light of wavelength $\lambda$ each, are placed as shown in figure. The initial phase difference between the sources is zero, $(D >> d)$. Mark the correct statement(s)
options: If $d = \dfrac{7\lambda}{2}, O$ will be a minima
If $d = \lambda$, only one maxima can be observed on the screen
If $d = 4.8\lambda$, then total $5$ minima would be there on the screen
If $d = \lambda$, the intensity at $O$ would be minimum
questions.solution:
Let $O$ be the origin on the screen. For any point $P$ on the screen at a distance $r$ from $O$, let the angle subtended by $P$ at the sources with respect to the central axis be $\theta$.

The path difference $\Delta x$ between the light waves reaching point $P$ from $S_1$ and $S_2$ is given by:
$\Delta x = S_1P - S_2P$

Since $D \gg d$, we can use the far-field approximation:
$\Delta x \approx d \cos \theta$

At point $O$, $r = 0$, which means $\theta = 0^{\circ}$. Thus, the path difference at $O$ is:
$\Delta x_{O} = d \cos(0^{\circ}) = d$

As we move away from $O$ towards infinity on the screen, $\theta$ increases from $0$ to $\pi/2$, and the path difference $\Delta x$ continuously decreases from $d$ to $0$. Therefore, the path difference on the screen lies in the range $(0, d]$.

Evaluating each option:

For option (A):
If $d = \dfrac{7\lambda}{2} = 3.5\lambda$, the path difference at $O$ is $\Delta x = 3.5\lambda$. Since this is an odd multiple of $\lambda/2$, destructive interference occurs, making $O$ a minima. This statement is correct.

For option (B):
If $d = \lambda$, the path difference on the screen is in the range $(0, \lambda]$. The condition for maxima is $\Delta x = n\lambda$. The only integer multiple of $\lambda$ in this range is for $n = 1$, which gives $\Delta x = \lambda$ at point $O$. The maximum for $n = 0$ ($\Delta x = 0$) is formed at infinity, which is not observed on the screen. Thus, only one maximum is observed on the screen. This statement is correct.

For option (C):
If $d = 4.8\lambda$, the path difference on the screen is in the range $(0, 4.8\lambda]$. The condition for minima is $\Delta x = (n - 0.5)\lambda$. The possible values for minima in this range are $0.5\lambda, 1.5\lambda, 2.5\lambda, 3.5\lambda$, and $4.5\lambda$. These $5$ distinct path differences correspond to $5$ circular dark fringes (minima) on the screen. This statement is correct.

For option (D):
If $d = \lambda$, the path difference at $O$ is $\Delta x = \lambda$. Since this is an integer multiple of $\lambda$, constructive interference occurs, making the intensity at $O$ a maximum, not a minimum. This statement is incorrect.

Answer: If $d = \dfrac{7\lambda}{2}, O$ will be a minima; If $d = \lambda$, only one maxima can be observed on the screen; If $d = 4.8\lambda$, then total $5$ minima would be there on the screen

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A single slit diffraction pattern is obtained using a beam of red light. If red light is replaced by blue light then

fringes will become broader and will be further apart
there is no change in the diffraction pattern

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light of wavelength $6000 Ã…$ is incident on a thin glass plate of $\mu = 1.5$ such that the angle of refraction into the plate is $60^{\circ}$. Calculate the smallest thickness of the plate which will make dark fringe by reflected beam interference.
questions.solution:
For reflected light, condition of destructive interference,
\(2 \mu t \cos r-\frac{\lambda}{2}=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda}{2}\)
\(\Rightarrow 2 \mu \mathrm{t} \cos \mathrm{r}=(2 \mathrm{n}+1) \frac{\lambda}{2}+\frac{\lambda}{2}\)
For minimum thickness of the plate, \(\mathrm{n}=0\),
So,
\(2 \mu t \cos r=\lambda\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 2 \times 1.5 \times \mathrm{t} \times \cos 60^{\circ}=6000 Ã… \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{6000}{1.5}=4000 Ã…
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a single-slit diffraction experiment, the slit is illuminated by light of two wavelength $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$. It is observed that the 2 nd order diffraction minimum for $\lambda_1$ coincides with the 3 rd diffraction minimum for $\lambda_2$. Then
questions.solution:
Hint: $d \sin \theta=\mathrm{n} \lambda$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{n}_1 \lambda_1=\mathrm{n}_2 \lambda_2 \\
& \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_1}=3 / 2
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An interference pattern is obtained with two coherent sources of intensity ratio \(\mathrm{n}: 1\). The ratio \(\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\text {Max }}-\mathrm{I}_{\text {Min }}}{\mathrm{I}_{\text {Max }}+\mathrm{I}_{\text {Min }}}\) will be maximum if
questions.solution:
Hint: \(\frac{I_1}{I_2}=n\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{I}_1=\mathrm{nI}_2 \\
& \mathrm{I}_{\max }=\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_1}+\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_2}\right)^2=\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{nI}} 2+\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_2}\right)^2=(\sqrt{\mathrm{n}}+1)^2\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_2}\right)^2
\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& I_{\min }=\left(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2=\left(\sqrt{n I_2}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2=(\sqrt{n}-1)^2\left(\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2 \\
& \frac{I_{\max }-I_{\min }}{I_{\max }+I_{\min }}=\frac{(\sqrt{n}+1)^2\left(I_2\right)-(\sqrt{n}-1)^2 I_2}{(\sqrt{n}+1)^2 I_2+(\sqrt{n}-1)^2 I_2}=\frac{(\sqrt{n}+1)^2-(\sqrt{n}-1)^2}{(\sqrt{n}+1)^2+(\sqrt{n}-1)^2} \\
& =\frac{(n+1+2 \sqrt{n})-(n+1-2 \sqrt{n})}{n+1+2 \sqrt{n}+n+1-2 \sqrt{n}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{n}}{2(n+1)}=\frac{2 \sqrt{n}}{n+1}
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore\) decreases with increasing \(\mathrm{n}\).
\(\therefore\) It will be maximum if \(n=1\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Young's double slit experiment, the intensity of light at a point on the screen where the path difference between the interfering waves is $\lambda$, ( $\lambda$ being the wavelength of light used) is $\mathrm{I}$. The intensity at a point where the path difference is $\frac{\lambda}{4}$ will be (assume two waves have same amplitude)
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{I}^{\prime}=\mathrm{I} \cos ^2 \phi / 2$
$\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta \mathrm{x}, \quad \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\frac{\phi}{2}=\frac{\lambda}{4}, \quad \mathrm{I}^{\prime}=\mathrm{I}^2 \cos ^2 \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment with a monochromatic light, maximum intensity is 4 times the minimum intensity in the interference pattern. What is the ratio of the intensities of the two interfering waves?
questions.solution:
$\left(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2=4\left(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2$
$\sqrt{l_1}+\sqrt{I_2}=2\left(\sqrt{l_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right), 3 \sqrt{I_2}=\sqrt{I_1}, \frac{l_2}{I_1}=\frac{1}{9}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Frounhofer diffraction experiment, a single slit of width $0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ is illuminated by a monochromatic light of wavelength $600 \mathrm{~nm}$. The diffraction pattern is observed on a screen at a distance of $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the slit. What will be the linear separation of the first order minima?
questions.solution:
Hint:
$\mathrm{d}=0.5 \mathrm{~mm} .$
$\lambda=600 \mathrm{~nm} .$ Width of central maxima $=\frac{2 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}$
$\mathrm{D}=50 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\frac{2 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{2 \times 600 \times 50 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-9}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}=1.2 \mathrm{~mm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's experiment for the interference of light, the separation between the slits is $d$ and the distance of the screen from the slits is $D$. If $D$ is increased by $0.5 \%$ and $d$ is decreased by $0.3 \%$ then for the light of a given wavelength, which one of the following is true? "The fringe width.........
questions.solution:
As we know that, fringe width, $\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}$
where, 
$\lambda=$ wavelength of light 
$D=$ Distance of the screen from the slits 
$d=$ separation between the slits 
Now, $\frac{\Delta \beta}{\beta} \times 100=\frac{\Delta D}{D} \times 100-\frac{\Delta d}{d} \times 100$
According to the question.
$$
\frac{\Delta \beta}{\beta} \times 100=0.5-(-0.3)=0.8 \%
$$
Hence, the fringe width increases by $0.2 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coherent monochromatic beams of intensities $I$ and $4 I$ respectively are superposed. The maximum and minimum intensitien in the reaulting pattern are
questions.solution:
We know that The maximum intensities
$$
I_{\max }=\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}
$$
The minimum intensities
$$
I_{\min }=\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}-\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}
$$
So, the ratio of the maximum and minimum of intensities is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }} &=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}-\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}} \\
\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }} &=\frac{(\sqrt{4 I}+\sqrt{I})^{2}}{(\sqrt{4 I}-\sqrt{I})^{2}} \\
&=\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{I}}{\sqrt{I}}\right)^{2} \\
=& \frac{9}{1}=9: 1
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are the coherent point sources of light located in the $x y$ -plane at points (0,0) and $(0,3 \lambda)$ respectively. Here $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light. At which one of the following points (given as coordinates), the intensity of interference will be maximum?
questions.solution: The given situation can be show as The intensity will be maximum at those given points where the path difference between the two interfering waves is an integral multiple of wavelength $i . e ., \Delta x=n \lambda$ For the the given points, the intensily will be maximum for $(4 \lambda, 0)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment the two slits are d distance apart. Interference pattern is observed on a screen at a distance D from the slits. A dark fringe is observed on the screen directly opposite to one of the slits. The wavelength of light is
questions.solution:
Hints:
\(\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}\) Dark fringe \(\begin{aligned} & (2 n-1) \frac{D \lambda}{2 d}=\frac{d}{2} \\ & \lambda=\frac{d^2}{(2 n-1) D}=\frac{d^2}{D} \quad[\text { for } n=1] \end{aligned}\)

questions.q: The light beams of intensities in the ratio of $9: 1$ are allowed to interfere. What will be the ratio of the intensities of maxima and minima?
questions.solution:
Hints: $\frac{\mathrm{A}_1}{\mathrm{~A}_2}=\frac{3}{1}$
$$
\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{16}{4}=\frac{4}{1}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical light waves, propagating in the same direction, have a phase difference $\delta$. After they superpose the intensity of the resulting wave will be proportional to
questions.solution: $I=4 I_0 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) \Rightarrow I \propto \cos ^2\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equation of a transverse wave is $y = y_0\sin 2\pi\left(ft - \dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)$. If the maximum particle velocity be four times that of wave velocity then
questions.solution:
The given equation of the transverse wave is $y = y_0\sin 2\pi\left(ft - \dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)$.

The particle velocity is given by $v_p = \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial t} = y_0(2\pi f)\cos 2\pi\left(ft - \dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)$.

The maximum particle velocity is $v_{p,\text{max}} = 2\pi f y_0$.

The wave velocity is $v_w = f\lambda$.

According to the given condition, the maximum particle velocity is four times the wave velocity:

$v_{p,\text{max}} = 4v_w$

Substituting the values, we get:

$2\pi f y_0 = 4f\lambda$

$\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi y_0}{4} = \dfrac{\pi y_0}{2}$

Answer: $\lambda = \dfrac{\pi y_0}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equation of stationary wave along a stretched string is given by $y=5 \sin \frac{\pi x}{3} \cos 40 \pi t$. Here x and y are in cm and t in second. The separation between two adjacent nodes is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{y}=5 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} \mathrm{x}\right) \cos (40 \pi \mathrm{t}), \omega=40 \pi \mathrm{k}=3 \mathrm{~cm}, \lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{k}}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
Separation between nodes $=\frac{\lambda}{2}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wave disturbance in a medium is described by $y(x, t)=0.02 \cos \left(50 \pi t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \cos (10 \pi x)$ where $x, y$ are in meters and $t$ is in second. Which statement(s) is/are correct?
options: The speed of the wave is $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$
The wavelength of the wave is 0.2 m
questions.solution:
$\cos 10 \pi x=0 \rightarrow$ Node
$\begin{aligned}
& 10 \pi \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{\pi}{2} \\
& 10 \pi \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{1}{2} \\
& \mathrm{x}=0, \frac{1}{20} \mathrm{~m}, \frac{3}{20} \mathrm{~m} \\
& \cos \pi \mathrm{x}= \pm 1 \rightarrow \text { Antinode } \\
& 10 \pi \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{n} \pi, \mathrm{x}=0, \frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~m}, \frac{2}{10} \mathrm{~m}, \frac{3}{10} \mathrm{~m} \\
& \text { wave speed }(\mathrm{v})=\frac{\omega}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{50 \pi}{10 \pi}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{k}=10 \pi, \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=10 \pi, \lambda=\frac{2}{10}=0.2 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The displacement of a plane progressive wave in a medium, travelling towards positive \(x\)-axis with velocity \(4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\) at \(t=0\) is given by \(y=3 \sin 2 \pi\left(-\frac{x}{3}\right)\). Then the expression for the displacement at a later time \(t=4\) sec will be
questions.solution:
Hint : Let \(y=3 \sin [\omega t-\mathrm{kx}]\)
\(\frac{\text { at } t=0}{y=3 \sin (-k x)}\)
\(k=\frac{2 \pi}{3}\)
\(v=\frac{\omega}{k}\)
\(4=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi / 3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega=\frac{8 \pi}{3}\)
\(\begin{gathered}\therefore \quad y=3 \sin \left[\frac{8 \pi}{3} t-\frac{2 \pi}{3} x\right] \\ \therefore t=4 \\ y=3 \sin \left[\frac{8 \pi}{3} \times 4-\frac{2 \pi}{3} x\right] \\ =3 \sin 2 \pi\left[\frac{-x+16}{3}\right] \\ y=3 \sin \left[2 \pi\left(-\frac{x-16}{3}\right)\right]\end{gathered}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The intensity of light emerging from one of the slits in a Young's double slit experiment is found to be $1.5$ limes the intensity of light emerging from the other slit. What will be the approximate ratio of intensity of an interference maximum to that of an interference minimum
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$I_{1}=1.51_{2}$
$\frac{\left.\right|_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{3}{2} \cdot \quad \frac{l_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{L_{1}}+\sqrt{L_{2}}}{\sqrt{I_{1}}-\sqrt{L_{2}}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}=98$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A uniform string of length $L$ and mass $M$ is fixed at both ends while it is subject to a
tension $T$. It can vibrate at frequencies $(v)$ given by the formula (where $n=1,2,3, \ldots)$
questions.solution:
The formula of frequency of 3 vibrations produced in tensed wire is
$$
v=\frac{n}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}
$$
$m=$ Mass per unit length of the wire $=\frac{M}{L}$
$M=$ Mass of wire
$L=$ Length of wire $n=$ Number of loops produced in wire $v=\frac{n}{2 L} \sqrt{\frac{T}{M / L}}=\frac{n}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{M L}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two monochromatic coherent light beams $A$ and $B$ have intensities $L$ and $\frac{L}{4},$ respectively. If these beams are superposed, the maximum and minimum intensities will be
questions.solution:
For maximum intensity,
$$
I_{\max }=I_{1}+I_{2}+2 \sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \delta
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &=0 \\
I_{\max } &=L+\frac{L}{4}+2 \sqrt{L \times \frac{L}{4}} \cdot \cos 0 \\
&=\frac{5 L}{4}+\frac{2 L}{2} \\
&=\frac{5 L+4 L}{4} \\
&=\frac{9 L}{4} \\
I_{\min } &=I_{1}+I_{2}-2 \sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \theta \\
\theta &=0^{\circ} \\
&=L+\frac{L}{4}-2 \sqrt{L \times \frac{L}{4}} \times 1 \\
&=\frac{5 L}{4}-L=\frac{L}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$
Thus, maximum and minimum intensities will be $\left(\frac{9 L}{4}, \frac{L}{4}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the pressure, temperature and density of an ideal gas are denoted by $p, T$ and $\rho$, respectively, the velocity of sound in the gas is
questions.solution:
$v=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$
(i) Velocity of sound is proportional to $\sqrt{T}$
$\therefore$
$v \propto \sqrt{T}$
(ii) When $\rho$ is constant, the velocity of sound is proportional to root of pressure $P$
$\therefore$
$$
v \propto \sqrt{P}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The velocity of sound in air at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atm pressure is $344.2 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. At $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $2 \mathrm{atm}$
pressure, the velocity of sound in air is approximately
questions.solution:
$\quad T_{1}=273+20=293 \mathrm{K}$
$$
T_{2}=273+40=313 \mathrm{K}
$$
The velocity of sound wave in gases or air is given by
$$
v=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_{1}}{M}}
$$
where, $\gamma=$ Ratio of $C_{p} / C_{V}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}R=\text { gas constant } \\ \qquad \begin{array}{l}v_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_{1}}{M}} \\ v_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_{2}}{M}}\end{array} \\ \frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{293}{313}}\end{array}
$$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Given, } v_{1}=344.2 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} & \\ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad v_{2} &=v_{1} \sqrt{\frac{313}{293}} \\ &=344.2 \times \sqrt{1068} \\ &=344.2 \times 103 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} \\ &=35575 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} \\ &=356 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For air at room temperature, the atmospheric pressure is $1.0 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and density of ait is $1.2 \mathrm{kgm}^{-3}$. For a tube of length $1.0 \mathrm{m}$, closed at one end, the lowest frequency $\begin{array}{lllllll}\text { generated is } & 84 & \text { Hz. } & \text { The } & \text { value } & \text { of } \boldsymbol{\gamma}\end{array}$ (ratio of two specific heats) for air is
questions.solution: The tube is closed at one end. So, it will act like a closed organ pipe. The lowest frequency produced in the tube $$ \begin{array}{l} l=\frac{\lambda}{4}, \lambda=4 \\ l=1 \mathrm{m} \\ f=84 \mathrm{Hz} \\ V=n \lambda=84 \times 4 \end{array} $$ When wave propagate in air, the velocity Here, $$ \begin{array}{l} v=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma p}{\rho}} \\ p=1.0 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}^{2} \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \rho &=1.2 \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3} \\ V^{2} &=\frac{\gamma P}{\rho} \\ \gamma &=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2} \cdot \rho}{\mathrm{P}} \\ &=\frac{(84 \times 4)^{2} \times 1.2}{1.0 \times 10^{5}} \\ &=\frac{135472.2}{10^{5}}=1.354 \\ &=1.4 \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $A$ train is moving with a uniform speed $c^{2}$ $33 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and an observer is approaching the train with the same speed. If the train blows a whistle of frequency $1000 \mathrm{Hz}$ and the velocity of sound is $333 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s},$ then the apparent frequency of the sound that the observer hears is
questions.solution:
We have,
Apparent frequency,
$\mathrm{v}  =\left(\frac{v+u_{0}}{v-u_{s}}\right)\mathrm{v}_{0}=\left(\frac{333+33}{333-33}\right) \times 1000 \\=\frac{366}{300} \times 1000=1220 \mathrm{Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The length of an open organ pipe is twice the length of another closed organ pipe. The fundamental frequency of the open pipe $100 \mathrm{Hz}$. The frequency of the third harmon of the closed pipe is
questions.solution:
Let the length of closed organ pipe 1 be. Then,
$$
v_{n}=(2 n-1) \frac{v}{4 l}
$$
Its third harmonics (put $n=2$ ) in above question.
$$
v_{3}=\frac{3}{4} \frac{v}{1}
$$
Now, the length of open organ pipe is 21 , then
$$
v_{n}=n \frac{v}{2(2 l)}=\frac{n v}{4 l}
$$
Its fundamental frequency,
$$
v_{0}=\frac{v}{2 l}=100
$$
$$
v_{3}=\frac{3 v}{4 l}=3 \times 100=300 \mathrm{Hz}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car is moving with a speed of $72 \mathrm{km}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ towards a roadside source that emits sound at a frequency of $850 \mathrm{Hz}$. The car driver listens to the sound while approaching the source and again while moving away from the source after crossing it. If the velocity of sound is $340 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$, the difference of the two frequencies, the driver hears is
questions.solution:
By Doppler's Effect When observer is moving with velocity $v_{0}$ towards a source at rest then approach frequency
$$
N_{\text {Approach }}=N\left(\frac{v+v_{0}}{v}\right)
$$
where $N=850 \mathrm{Hz}, v=340 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}, v_{0}=72 \mathrm{kmh}^{-1}$
$$
=20 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}=850\left(\frac{340+20}{340}\right)
$$
Similarly. when observer is moving away from source, then
$$
N_{\text {Soparation }}=N\left(\frac{v-v_{0}}{v}\right)=850\left(\frac{340-20}{340}\right)
$$
The different of the two frequency.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
N_{\text {Approuch }}-N_{\text {Soparation }} \\
\qquad \begin{aligned}
&=850\left(\frac{360}{340}\right)-850\left(\frac{320}{340}\right) \\
&=\frac{850}{340} \times 40=\frac{850}{8.5}=100 \mathrm{Hz}
\end{aligned}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A travelling acoustic wave frequency $500 \mathrm{Hz}$ is moving along the positive $x$ -direction with a velocity of $300 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$. The phase difference between two points $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$ is $60^{\circ} .$ Then the minimum separation between the two points is
questions.solution:
By using the relation, $v=v \lambda$
We have, $\quad \lambda=\frac{V}{v}=\frac{300}{500}=\frac{3}{5} \mathrm{m}$
The phase difference, $\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(\Delta x)$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{2 \pi \times 5}{3}(\Delta x) \Rightarrow \Delta x=\frac{1}{10} m=10 \mathrm{cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The fundamental frequency of a closed pipe is equal to the frequency of the second harmonic of an open pipe. The ratio of their lengths is
questions.solution:
Let the length of closed pipe is $L_{1}$ and that of pipe is $L_{2}$. Fundamental frequency of closed pipe, $v_{1}=\frac{v}{4 L_{1}}$
and frequency of second harmonic of open pipe.
$$
v_{2}=2 \times \frac{v}{2 L_{2}}
$$
Given,
$$
v_{1}=v_{2}
$$
$i, \theta$
$$
\frac{v}{4 L_{1}}=\frac{2 v}{2 L_{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{L_{1}}{L_{2}}=\frac{1}{4}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car moving at a velocity of $17 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ towards an approaching bus that blows a horn at a frequency of $640 \mathrm{Hz}$ on a straight track. The frequency of this horn appears to be $680 \mathrm{Hz}$ to the car driver. If the velocity of sound in air is $340 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$, then the velocity of the approaching bus is
questions.solution: Given, velocity of sound, $v=340 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ Velocity of listner, $v_{L}=17 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ Velocity of source $=v_{s}$ Frequency of hom emitted $$ v=640 \mathrm{Hz} $$ The apparent frequency $$ \begin{aligned} v^{\prime} &=v \frac{\left(v+v_{l}\right)}{v-v_{s}} \\ 680 &=640\left(\frac{340+17}{340-v_{s}}\right) \end{aligned} $$ On solving we get $v_{s}=4 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A longitudinal wave is represented by $x=x_0 \sin 2 \pi(n t-x / \lambda)$. The maximum particle velocity will be four times the wave velocity if:
questions.solution:
Hints: Maximum particle velocity $\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}\right)=\mathrm{A} \omega=2 \pi n x_0$
Wave velocity $\left(\mathrm{V}_\omega\right)=n \lambda$
Here, $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}=4 \mathrm{~V}_\omega$
$$
2 \pi n x_0=4 n \lambda
$$
$$
\lambda=\frac{\pi}{2} x_0
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body initially at rest and sliding along a frictionless track from a height '$h$' (as shown in figure) just completes a vertical circle of diameter $AB = d$. The height '$h$' is equal to
questions.solution:
For a body to just complete a vertical circle of radius $R$, the minimum velocity required at the lowest point is $v = \sqrt{5gR}$.

Given the diameter of the circle is $d$, the radius is $R = \dfrac{d}{2}$.

Therefore, the velocity at the lowest point must be:
$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{5gd}{2}}$

By the principle of conservation of mechanical energy, the potential energy of the body at height $h$ is converted into kinetic energy at the lowest point (assuming the lowest point is the reference level for zero potential energy).

$mgh = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$

Substituting the value of $v^2$:
$mgh = \dfrac{1}{2}m \left(\dfrac{5gd}{2}\right)$

$h = \dfrac{5d}{4}$

Answer: $\dfrac{5}{4}d$

questions.q: The velocity-time graph for a body of mass 10 kg is shown in the figure. Work done on the body in the first two seconds of motion is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A train is moving along the tracks at a constant speed \(\mathrm{u}\). A girl on the train throws a ball of mass \(m\) straight ahead along the direction of motion of the train with speed \(v\) with respect to herself. Then
options: Kinetic energy of the ball as measured by the girl on the train is \(\mathrm{mv}^2 / 2\)
Work done by the girl in throwing the ball is \(\mathrm{mv}^2 / 2\)
Work done by the train is mvu
The gain in kinetic energy of the ball as measured by a person standing by the rail track is \(\mathrm{mv}^2 / 2\)
questions.solution:
Hint : w.r.t. the girl \(E_k=\frac{1}{2} m v^2\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{k}}=1 / 2 \mathrm{mv}^2\)
Work by the train \(=\left\{\frac{1}{2}(v+u)^2-\frac{1}{2} m u^2\right\}-\frac{1}{2} m v^2\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1}{2} m\left(v^2+u^2+2 v u\right)-\frac{1}{2} m\left(v^2+u^2\right) \\
& =m v u
\end{aligned}\)
Gain in \(E_k=\frac{1}{2} m(v+u)^2-\frac{1}{2} m u^2=\frac{1}{2} m v^2+m v u\)
measured from rail track

So, Force is slope at $x=10 m \quad F=-\frac{20}{4}=-5 \hat{i}$

questions.q: The bob of a swinging seconds pendulum (one whose time period is $2 \mathrm{~s}$ ) has a small speed $\mathrm{v}_{0}$ at its lowest point. Its height from this lowest point $2.25 \mathrm{~s}$ after passing through it is given by
questions.solution:
Hint: 
$T=2$ sec.
at $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{T} / 8$
$\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{T} / 8 \quad \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{A} \omega \cos \omega \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{v}_{0} / \sqrt{2}$
By Mechanical energy conservation:
$\left(\frac{v_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}=v_{0}^{2}-2 g h \quad h=\frac{v_{0}^{2}}{4 g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body starts from rest, under the action of an engine working at a constant power and moves along a straight line. The displacement s is given as a function of time ( $f$ as
options: $s=a t+b t^{2}, a$ and $b$ are constants
questions.solution:
Given, Power $(P)=$ constant
Kinetic Energy (KE) $=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
We know that, $P=\frac{K E}{\Delta t} \Rightarrow P=\frac{m v^{2}}{\Delta t}$
$\because P=$ constant. 
Hence, velocity of the body $v \propto \sqrt{t}$...(i)
As, Velocity $v=\frac{d s}{d t}$...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
So, $\quad \frac{d s}{d t} \propto \sqrt{t}$
Integrating the above cquation w.r.t. time $(t)$
$$
\int \frac{d s}{d t} \propto \int \sqrt{t}
$$
we get, displacement of the body $s \propto t^{3 / 2}$
Displacement $s=a t^{3 / 2}$, where $a$ is constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bullet of mass $m$ travelling with a speed $v$ hits a block of mass $M$ initially at rest and gets embedded in it. The combined system is free to move and there is no other force acting on the system. The heat generated in the process will be
questions.solution:
Mass of bullet $=m_{1}=m$
Initial speed of bullet $=u_{1}=v$
Mass of block $=m_{2}=M$ 
Initial speed of block $=u_{2}=0$
Let the common velocity of the bodies after collision $=V$ According to conservation of linear momentum
$$
\begin{aligned}
m \times v+M \times 0 &=(m+M) V \\
V &=\frac{m v}{(m+M)}
\end{aligned}
$$
: Heat generated = loss in $\mathrm{KE}$
$=$ Initial KE - Final KE
$=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\frac{1}{2}(m+M) v^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\frac{1}{2}(m+M)\left(\frac{m v}{m+M}\right)^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{m^{2} v^{2}}{(m+M)}$
$=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left(1-\frac{m}{m+M}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left(\frac{m+M-m}{m+M}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m M}{(m+M)} v^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $M$ and charge $q$ is released from rest in a region of uniform electric field magnitude $E$. After a time $t$, the distance travelled by the charge is $S$ and the kinetic energy attained by the particle is $T$. Then, the ratio $T / S$
options: varies linearly with the mass $M$ of the particle
is independent of the magnitude of the electric field E
questions.solution:
Given, mass of the particle $=M$.
Charge on the particle $=q$
Electric field $=E$
Initial velocity, $u=0$
$\therefore$ Acceleration, $a=\frac{F}{M}=\frac{q E}{M}$
:.Distance travelled in electric field, $S=u t+\frac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
$$
S=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{q E}{M}\right) t^{2}
$$
Also, kinetic energy $T=\frac{1}{2} M\left(\frac{q E t}{M}\right)^{2}$
So, $\quad \frac{T}{S}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} M\left(\frac{q E t}{M}\right)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{q E}{M}\right) t^{2}}=q E$
$\Rightarrow$ Ratio of $\frac{T}{S}$ remains constant with time t.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $M$ and charge $q$, initially at rest, is accelerated by a uniform electric field $E$ through a distance $D$ and is then allowed to approach a fixed static charge $Q$ of the same sign. The distance of the closest approach of the charge $q$ will then be
questions.solution:
At the distance of closest approach, the entire $\mathrm{KE}$ of a particle is converted into electric potential energy. i.e.. $\mathrm{KE}=\mathrm{PE}$
Here, KE of the particle = work done in moving the particle in uniform electric field (E) through a distance (D)
$$
=q E \times D
$$
PE of the particle = potential $\times$ charge
$$
=\frac{q}{4 \pi c_{0} \gamma_{0}} \times Q
$$
$\therefore \quad q E D=\frac{q Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \gamma_{0}} \Rightarrow r_{0}=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} E D}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is acted upon by a constant power. Then, which of the following physical quantity remains constant?
options: Rate of change of kinetic energy
questions.solution:
We have Power $=$ Rate of doing work $=\frac{d W}{d t}$
or, $\quad P=\frac{d W}{d t}=$ rate of change in $\mathrm{KE}$
i.e., $\quad P=\frac{d W}{d t}=\frac{d(\mathrm{KE})}{d t}=$ constant

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a spring is stretched by $10 \mathrm{cm}$, the potential energy stored is $E$. When the spring is stretched by $10 \mathrm{cm}$ more, the potential energy stored in the spring becomes
questions.solution:
For 1st situation,
$$
E=\frac{1}{2} k\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}
$$
For 2nd situation,
$$
E^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} k\left(20 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}
$$
On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
E^{\prime}=4 E
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If momentum is increased by $20 \%$, then kinetic energy increases by
questions.solution:
Hints: $\mathrm{K}=\frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{2 m}$
Here $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}=1.2 \mathrm{P}$
Hence, $K^{\prime}=\frac{(1.2 \mathrm{P})^2}{2 m}$
$$
\mathrm{K}^{\prime}=1.44 \frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{2 m}
$$
$\mathrm{K}^{\prime}=1.44 \mathrm{~K}$ or Percentage increase in $\mathrm{K}=44 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force $\mathrm{F}$ acting on a particle moving in a straight line is shown below. What is the work done by the force on the particle in the $1^{\text {st }}$ meter of the trajectory?
questions.solution: Work done in 1 meter $=$ area of shaded curve $=1 / 2 \times 1 \times 5=2.5 \mathrm{~J}$
